name: Alternating Floor Press
description:
**By pressing one kettlebell at a time from the floor, you force each side of your chest to work independently — exposing and fixing strength imbalances you didn't know you had.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The floor limits how far your elbows drop, protecting your shoulders while still giving your chest a solid workout. Alternating arms means your core has to work overtime to keep you stable, and each side gets a brief rest while the other works — letting you squeeze out more quality reps.

**Pro tip:** Press the non-working kettlebell toward the ceiling and keep it locked out the entire time — this keeps tension on that side and prevents your body from rolling.

**Biomechanics:** This exercise is toughest in the middle of the press and eases up at the top and bottom. Because the floor stops your elbows from going too deep, your chest gets loaded in a safe stretched position with solid growth potential.
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor with a kettlebell in each hand, knees bent and feet flat.
Hold both kettlebells above your chest with arms extended, palms facing each other.
Lower one kettlebell slowly until your upper arm rests on the floor, keeping your elbow at roughly 45 degrees from your body.
Press that kettlebell back up to the starting position.
Repeat on the opposite arm, alternating sides for each rep.
After your final rep, carefully set both kettlebells down to your sides.
tips: Let your upper arms touch the floor briefly at the bottom — this is your range limiter
Keep your legs flat or bent depending on your low back comfort
Press straight up, not back toward your face
Retract your scapulae even without a bench for support
coaching_cues: Arms touch the floor, then press

name: Archer Push-Up
description:
**One arm does the heavy lifting while the other just assists — making this the closest thing to a one-arm push-up most people can actually do with good form.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This variation loads one side of your chest far more than a regular push-up, which is great for building strength and size without any equipment. It's also a fantastic stepping stone toward full one-arm push-ups and helps even out left-to-right imbalances.

**Pro tip:** Keep the assisting arm as straight as possible and only use it for balance — the more you bend it, the more you're turning this into a regular wide push-up.

**Biomechanics:** The working arm experiences the hardest challenge in the middle of the movement, with some relief at the top and bottom. Your chest is deeply stretched at the bottom position, which is great for muscle growth — and because it's bodyweight, you can do these anywhere.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with your hands placed much wider than shoulder-width apart, fingers pointing outward.
Shift your weight toward your right hand and bend your right elbow to lower your chest toward your right hand.
Keep your left arm straight as your body moves to the right side.
Push through your right hand to return to the starting position.
Repeat on the left side by shifting your weight and lowering toward your left hand.
Alternate sides for the desired number of reps.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension
Keep the extended arm straight as a lever — it should not bend

name: Around The Worlds
description:
**Imagine making a giant snow angel with dumbbells — this sweeping arc gives your chest a massive stretch that most pressing movements can't match.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The huge range of motion in this exercise stretches your chest fibers from a fully open position all the way to overhead, hitting angles that flat presses miss entirely. It's an excellent finisher that floods your chest with blood and creates a deep stretch under load.

**Pro tip:** Use lighter weight than you think you need — the long lever arm (straight arms + dumbbells far from your body) makes even light dumbbells feel brutally heavy at the widest point.

**Biomechanics:** This exercise is hardest when the dumbbells are out to your sides at chest level, and gets easier as they move overhead or back down to your hips. The deep stretched position at the sides is where your chest works hardest, and that loaded stretch is a powerful driver of muscle growth.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your hips with arms nearly straight and palms facing up.
Keeping your arms slightly bent, sweep both dumbbells out and away from your body in a wide arc toward your head.
Continue the arc until the dumbbells meet above your head.
Reverse the path, sweeping the dumbbells back down in the same wide arc to the starting position at your hips.
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain

name: Barbell Bench Press
description:
**The undisputed king of chest exercises — there's a reason every gym-goer eventually asks "how much do you bench?"**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The barbell bench press lets you move more weight than almost any other chest exercise, making it the go-to for building raw pressing strength and overall chest size. Both arms work together on a fixed bar, so you can load up heavy and push your limits safely with a spotter.

**Pro tip:** Plant your feet firmly on the floor and drive through your legs as you press — this "leg drive" creates a stable base and can add 10-15% to your lift.

**Biomechanics:** The hardest point is in the middle of the press, with some relief at the top lockout and the bottom. Your chest gets a good stretch at the bottom when the barbell touches your chest, and the ability to load this exercise heavy makes it a cornerstone for building size and strength.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your eyes under the barbell, feet flat on the floor.
Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder-width and unrack it with straight arms.
Lower the barbell in a controlled arc to your mid-chest, keeping your elbows at about 45 degrees.
Press the barbell back up to full arm extension, driving through your chest and triceps.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, then rerack the barbell by guiding it back into the hooks.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Barbell Bench Press - Medium Grip
description:
**A slightly narrower grip shifts more work onto your triceps and inner chest, making this a smart variation when your wide-grip bench starts to plateau.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The medium grip keeps your elbows tucked closer to your body, which is generally easier on your shoulders than a wide grip. You'll feel your triceps working harder, and many lifters find they can actually bench more total weight with this grip because the triceps can contribute more force.

**Pro tip:** Grip the barbell so your forearms are perfectly vertical when the bar is on your chest — that's your ideal medium grip width for maximum power transfer.

**Biomechanics:** Like the standard bench, this is toughest in the middle of the press. The narrower grip slightly reduces the chest stretch at the bottom but increases the range of motion, meaning your arms travel further on each rep — more work per rep equals more growth potential over time.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your eyes under the barbell, feet flat on the floor.
Grip the barbell at shoulder width — narrower than a standard bench press grip — and unrack it.
Lower the barbell to your mid-to-lower chest, keeping your elbows closer to your sides at about 30-40 degrees.
Press the barbell back up to full lockout, squeezing your chest and triceps at the top.
Repeat for the desired reps, then carefully rerack the barbell.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Barbell Guillotine Bench Press
description:
**Lowering the barbell to your neck instead of your chest turns the bench press into a chest-isolation monster — but it demands respect and lighter weight.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

By bringing the barbell higher toward your neck, your arms flare out more and your chest does a much larger share of the work compared to a standard bench press. This variation was a favorite of old-school bodybuilders for building a wide, full chest. Use a spotter — the bar path is less forgiving.

**Pro tip:** Never go heavy on these. Use a weight you can control for 8-12 reps and always have a spotter. The payoff is chest activation, not ego numbers.

**Biomechanics:** The hardest point is in the middle of the press, with your chest under a deep stretch at the bottom. The high bar path puts your chest in a more favorable line of pull, which means your chest does more work per rep — great for growth, but the shoulder position demands careful weight selection.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the barbell with a wider-than-shoulder-width grip.
Unrack the barbell and position it above your upper chest and neck area.
Lower the barbell slowly toward your neck/upper chest, allowing your elbows to flare out to the sides.
Lightly touch your upper chest just below the collarbone, then press the barbell back up.
Keep the movement controlled throughout — never bounce the bar.
Rerack the barbell carefully after completing your set.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Barbell Incline Bench Press - Medium Grip
description:
**Angling the bench up shifts the spotlight to your upper chest — the area that gives your chest that full, shelf-like look from the side.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The incline targets the upper portion of your chest that flat pressing often neglects. A medium grip balances the workload between chest, shoulders, and triceps, and tends to be easier on the shoulder joints than a wide grip. If your upper chest is lagging, this should be a staple.

**Pro tip:** Set the bench to 30-45 degrees — any steeper and your shoulders take over. Most people go too steep; a lower incline keeps the chest as the primary mover.

**Biomechanics:** This is toughest in the middle of the press and gets easier at lockout. The incline angle means your upper chest is stretched under load at the bottom, which is excellent for building that area. Expect to use about 15-20% less weight than your flat bench.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45 degree incline inside a power rack or under a barbell.
Lie back on the bench, grip the barbell at shoulder width, and unrack it with straight arms.
Lower the barbell to your upper chest just below the collarbone, elbows at about 45 degrees.
Press the barbell back up to full extension, focusing on driving through your upper chest.
Repeat for the desired reps, then rerack the barbell.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Behind Head Chest Stretch
description:
**Clasping your hands behind your head and pulling your elbows back opens up a tight chest in seconds — perfect for desk workers and heavy pressers alike.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Sitting at a desk or doing lots of pressing can make your chest muscles short and tight, pulling your shoulders forward. This simple stretch counteracts that by opening up the chest and front of the shoulders. Do it between pressing sets or at the end of a workout to maintain flexibility.

**Pro tip:** Breathe out as you pull your elbows back and try to squeeze your shoulder blades together — you'll get a noticeably deeper stretch than just passively holding the position.
instructions: Stand or sit upright with good posture.
Interlace your fingers behind your head with your elbows pointing forward.
Slowly pull your elbows back and apart, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Hold the stretch for 15-30 seconds, breathing deeply and relaxing into it.
Release and repeat 2-3 times.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds
Breathe deeply throughout
Do not bounce

name: Bench Press - With Bands
description:
**Looping resistance bands over the barbell makes the lockout brutally hard — teaching your body to accelerate through the entire press instead of decelerating at the top.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Bands add increasing resistance as you push up, which means you can't coast through the easy part of the lift. This trains explosive power and helps break through sticking points. It's a favorite tool of powerlifters, but anyone who's plateaued on bench press can benefit.

**Pro tip:** Start with light bands — they add more resistance than you'd expect. The band tension at the top should add roughly 20-25% of the barbell weight, not more.

**Biomechanics:** Unlike a regular bench press, this version gets progressively harder as you push up because the bands stretch further. The bottom is the lightest point, and the top is the heaviest. This teaches you to push fast from the start and builds lockout strength that carries over to your regular bench.
instructions: Loop resistance bands around the barbell and anchor them to the base of the bench or heavy dumbbells on the floor.
Lie on the bench, grip the barbell at your normal bench press width, and unrack it.
Lower the barbell to your chest in a controlled motion — the bands will reduce tension at the bottom.
Explode upward, pressing the barbell to full lockout against the increasing band resistance.
Repeat for the desired reps, focusing on pressing as fast as possible on every rep.
Rerack the barbell carefully.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Anchor the band securely and check for wear before each set

name: Bent-Arm Dumbbell Pullover
description:
**This old-school exercise stretches your chest and lats simultaneously by sweeping a dumbbell behind your head — a unique angle no press or fly can replicate.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Lats, shoulders, triceps.

The pullover works your chest through a completely different plane of motion than pressing or flying. As the dumbbell goes behind your head, your chest gets an enormous stretch under load. It also hits your lats, making it one of the few exercises that bridges chest day and back day.

**Pro tip:** Don't let the dumbbell go so far back that your lower back arches off the bench — stop when you feel a strong stretch in your chest and lats, not when your spine starts to hyperextend.

**Biomechanics:** This exercise is hardest when the dumbbell is behind your head and gets easier as you pull it back over your chest. That deep stretched position at the bottom is where your chest is loaded the most, making this excellent for muscle growth — especially the upper and inner chest.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your feet on the floor, holding one dumbbell with both hands above your chest, arms slightly bent.
Keeping the bend in your elbows fixed, slowly lower the dumbbell in an arc behind your head.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch in your chest and lats — your upper arms should be roughly in line with your ears.
Pull the dumbbell back over your chest in the same arc, squeezing your chest at the top.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping the elbow angle constant throughout.
tips: Keep your hips low and ribcage elevated to maximize the stretch overhead
Do not let the weight pull your lower back into excessive extension
Focus on pulling with the chest, not the lats — think about squeezing your pecs
Use a controlled tempo, especially in the stretched position

name: Bodyweight Flyes
description:
**Using two barbells (or sliders) on the floor, you spread your arms apart like a fly — turning a simple bodyweight move into a chest-shredding isolation exercise.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

This exercise mimics a dumbbell fly but uses your own bodyweight as resistance. Because you're controlling your entire body through the movement, your core works hard to keep you stable. It's surprisingly difficult and gives your chest a deep stretch at the bottom that rivals any cable fly.

**Pro tip:** Start on your knees if you can't control the full movement from your toes — collapsing at the bottom is a fast track to a shoulder injury.

**Biomechanics:** The hardest point is at the bottom when your arms are spread wide and your chest is fully stretched. It gets easier as you bring your hands back together. That deep loaded stretch makes this a potent chest builder despite using no external weight.
instructions: Place two barbells (or ab wheel rollers) parallel on the floor, shoulder-width apart.
Get into a push-up position with one hand on each barbell.
Slowly roll the barbells apart by spreading your arms out to the sides, lowering your chest toward the floor.
Go as wide as you can while maintaining control, feeling a deep stretch in your chest.
Squeeze your chest to pull the barbells back together, returning to the starting push-up position.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping your core tight throughout.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain

name: Butterfly
description:
**The pec deck machine locks you into the perfect path so you can focus entirely on squeezing your chest — no stabilization, no wasted effort, just pure chest work.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The machine handles all the balancing for you, which means every ounce of effort goes directly into your chest. This makes it ideal for beginners learning to feel their chest work, and for advanced lifters who want to torch their chest at the end of a workout without worrying about form breakdown.

**Pro tip:** Don't slam the pads together at the front — slow down and hold the squeeze for a full second at the point where the pads meet. That's where the peak contraction happens.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance stays fairly constant throughout the movement thanks to the machine's cam system. Your chest is loaded in the mid-range rather than the stretched position, which makes this easier on your shoulders but means you should pair it with a stretch-focused exercise for complete chest development.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles are at chest level, then sit with your back flat against the pad.
Place your forearms against the pads (or grip the handles) with your elbows at about 90 degrees.
Squeeze your chest to bring the pads together in front of you.
Hold the squeeze for a moment at the center, then slowly let the pads return to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling the return on every rep.
tips: Adjust the seat so your upper arms are parallel to the floor at the start
Do not let the weight stack touch down between reps — maintain tension
Focus on the squeeze at the midline rather than the stretch
Keep your back flat against the pad throughout

name: Cable Chest Fly Standing
description:
**Cables keep constant tension on your chest through the entire arc — unlike dumbbells, there's no resting at the top, so your chest never gets a break.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Standing cable flyes are one of the best chest isolation exercises because the cable pulls sideways throughout the movement, keeping your chest under tension from start to finish. You can also easily adjust the cable height to target upper, middle, or lower chest. It's a versatile staple that belongs in every chest workout.

**Pro tip:** Step one foot forward in a staggered stance and lean slightly into the movement — this prevents the cables from pulling you backward and lets you focus on your chest instead of fighting for balance.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance is fairly even throughout the movement, with a strong stretch when your arms are wide open. Your chest is loaded in the stretched position, which is a powerful trigger for muscle growth. The constant cable tension means no dead spots in the movement.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about shoulder height and attach single handles.
Stand in the center, grab a handle in each hand, and step forward into a staggered stance.
Start with your arms out to the sides, elbows slightly bent, chest up.
Sweep your arms together in a wide arc in front of your chest, squeezing at the center.
Slowly reverse the arc, letting your arms open back to the starting position with control.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining the slight bend in your elbows throughout.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the movement
Squeeze the chest at the midline — imagine hugging a tree
Control the return to the stretched position rather than letting cables pull you back
Adjust pulley height to target different pec fiber angles
coaching_cues: Elbows locked, chest does the work

name: Cable Chest Press
description:
**Pressing with cables instead of a barbell forces each arm to work independently and keeps tension on your chest even at full lockout — where a barbell would let you rest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The cable chest press combines the pressing pattern of a bench press with the constant tension of cables. Each arm works independently, so your stronger side can't compensate for the weaker one. It's also much easier on your shoulders than barbell pressing because you can adjust the angle freely.

**Pro tip:** Angle your hands slightly inward as you press so your hands nearly touch at lockout — this adds a squeezing component that hits the inner chest harder than pressing straight forward.

**Biomechanics:** The cable provides steady resistance throughout the entire press, with no easy lockout point. Your chest stays loaded in the mid-range, which means consistent tension from start to finish. This makes it a great complement to free-weight pressing where the lockout is often the easiest part.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to chest height and attach single handles.
Grab a handle in each hand, step forward, and stagger your feet for balance.
Start with your hands at chest level, elbows bent and behind you.
Press both handles forward and slightly inward until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly bend your elbows to return the handles to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping your core braced throughout.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Set the pulleys to chest height for a flat press angle
Step forward to create tension at the start position

name: Cable Crossover
description:
**Crossing your hands past the midline of your body at the bottom of each rep hits the inner chest in a way that standard flyes simply can't reach.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The crossover takes the cable fly one step further by letting your hands cross over each other, which means your chest keeps working even past the point where your hands meet. This extra range of motion at the end is what makes crossovers uniquely effective for building chest definition and that inner chest line.

**Pro tip:** Alternate which hand crosses on top each rep — this ensures both sides of your inner chest get equal work at the deepest point of contraction.

**Biomechanics:** The cable provides consistent resistance throughout the movement, and your chest stays loaded in the mid-range. Because your hands can cross the midline, you get a more complete contraction than any dumbbell fly allows. Pair this with a stretch-focused exercise for full chest development.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the highest position and attach single handles.
Stand centered between the cables, grab a handle in each hand, and step forward with one foot.
Start with your arms out wide and slightly above shoulder height, elbows slightly bent.
Sweep your arms downward and together in a wide arc, crossing your hands at the bottom.
Slowly reverse the arc, letting your arms rise back to the wide starting position.
Repeat for the desired reps, alternating which hand crosses on top.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and locked in position throughout
Step forward with one foot for balance and to pre-stretch the chest
Squeeze the cables together at the midline for a full contraction
Control the eccentric — do not let the cables snap your arms back

name: Cable Iron Cross
description:
**Holding your arms straight out to the sides against cable resistance turns you into a human crucifix — and your chest will be screaming within seconds.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The iron cross is a cable fly performed with nearly straight arms held at shoulder height, creating an enormous lever arm that makes even light weight feel heavy. It's an advanced isolation move that builds serious chest endurance and control. The constant tension from the cables means there's nowhere to hide.

**Pro tip:** Start with much lighter weight than you'd use for regular cable flyes — the straight-arm position multiplies the effective load dramatically. If you can't hold the bottom position for 2 seconds, go lighter.

**Biomechanics:** The cables provide even resistance throughout the movement, keeping your chest loaded in the mid-range. The long lever arm (straight arms) means your chest has to generate a lot of force even with light weight. This makes it excellent for building muscular endurance and control.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to shoulder height and attach single handles.
Stand centered between the cables and grab a handle in each hand.
Step forward slightly and extend your arms straight out to the sides at shoulder height, palms facing forward.
With arms nearly straight, slowly bring your hands together in front of your chest.
Hold the squeeze briefly, then slowly return your arms to the wide starting position.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping your arms at shoulder height throughout.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and locked in position throughout
Step forward with one foot for balance and to pre-stretch the chest
Squeeze the cables together at the midline for a full contraction
Control the eccentric — do not let the cables snap your arms back

name: Chain Press
description:
**Interlocking your fingers and pressing your palms together as hard as you can creates a surprising amount of chest activation — no equipment needed, anywhere, anytime.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The chain press (also called a Svend press or palm press) uses isometric tension between your own hands to activate your chest. It's not a mass builder, but it's excellent for warming up, learning to feel your chest engage, or getting a quick pump when you have zero equipment. The harder you press your palms together, the harder your chest works.

**Pro tip:** Press your palms together with maximum force and push your hands away from your chest slowly — the combination of the isometric squeeze and the pressing motion creates a burn that's much more intense than it looks.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance comes from your own effort, so it's toughest in the middle of the press where you can generate the most inward force. Your chest is loaded in a stretched position at the start and stays engaged throughout. Growth potential is moderate since you can't progressively overload easily.
instructions: Stand upright with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Press your palms together at chest height with fingers interlocked or a plate between them.
While maintaining maximum inward pressure between your hands, slowly press your hands forward away from your chest.
Extend your arms until they're nearly straight, keeping the palms pressed hard together.
Slowly bring your hands back to your chest, maintaining the inward pressure the entire time.
Repeat for the desired reps — focus on squeezing as hard as possible throughout.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Chest And Front Of Shoulder Stretch
description:
**Grabbing a doorframe and leaning forward opens up your entire chest and the front of your shoulders — undoing hours of hunching over a keyboard in just 30 seconds.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

This stretch targets the chest and front shoulder muscles that get chronically tight from sitting, driving, and pressing exercises. Regular stretching here improves your posture, reduces shoulder pain, and can actually improve your bench press by letting you get into a better starting position.

**Pro tip:** Try the stretch at three different arm heights — low, shoulder level, and overhead — to hit different parts of your chest. Each angle stretches a slightly different area.
instructions: Stand in a doorway and place your forearm against the doorframe with your elbow at shoulder height.
Step forward with the foot on the same side until you feel a stretch across your chest and front shoulder.
Hold for 20-30 seconds, breathing deeply and relaxing into the stretch.
Repeat on the other side.
For a deeper stretch, try placing your arm higher or lower on the doorframe.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds
Breathe deeply throughout
Do not bounce

name: Chest Dips
description:
**Leaning forward on the dip bars shifts the work from your triceps to your chest — making this one of the best bodyweight exercises for building a thick lower chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Chest dips load your chest with your entire bodyweight (or more, with a belt), and the deep bottom position gives your chest an incredible stretch under heavy load. The forward lean is what separates chest dips from tricep dips — without it, your triceps do most of the work.

**Pro tip:** Look down at the floor and lean your torso forward about 30 degrees throughout the movement. If you're staying upright, you're doing tricep dips, not chest dips.

**Biomechanics:** This exercise is hardest at the bottom when your chest is deeply stretched and gets easier as you push up. That deep stretch under your full bodyweight is a powerful growth stimulus for the lower and outer chest. Once bodyweight gets easy, add weight with a dip belt.
instructions: Grip the parallel dip bars and lift yourself to the starting position with arms straight.
Lean your torso forward about 30 degrees and cross your ankles behind you.
Lower yourself by bending your elbows until you feel a deep stretch in your chest — aim for your upper arms to be at least parallel to the floor.
Press yourself back up to the starting position, keeping the forward lean throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding swinging or kipping.
tips: Lean forward approximately 30 degrees to shift emphasis from triceps to chest
Lower until your upper arms are at least parallel to the floor for full pec stretch
Keep elbows slightly flared rather than tucked to increase pec involvement
Control the descent — do not drop into the bottom position
If bodyweight is too easy, add load with a belt rather than increasing speed

name: Chest Push (multiple response)
description:
**An explosive medicine ball chest pass done in rapid succession — building power and speed that translates directly to sports like basketball, football, and boxing.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Unlike slow, controlled bench pressing, this exercise trains your chest to produce force as fast as possible. The multiple-response version means you catch and immediately throw again, training the stretch-shortening cycle — your muscles learn to absorb force and redirect it instantly. It's athletic training, not bodybuilding.

**Pro tip:** Catch the ball with soft elbows and redirect it immediately — don't pause between reps. The faster the turnaround from catch to throw, the more power you're building.
instructions: Stand facing a wall or partner at about arm's length, holding a medicine ball at chest height.
Explosively push the ball forward from your chest, fully extending your arms.
Catch the rebound (or return pass) with both hands at chest level.
Immediately redirect the ball into the next throw without pausing.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining maximum speed on every throw.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: Chest Push (single response)
description:
**One explosive chest pass with everything you've got — this is about maximum power output in a single effort, like a shot put for your chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The single-response chest push trains peak power — your ability to generate the most force possible in one explosive effort. Unlike the multiple-response version, you reset between each throw, which lets you put 100% effort into every single rep. It's a staple in athletic performance training.

**Pro tip:** Reset your stance completely between each throw. Take a breath, set your feet, and explode — treating each rep like a max-effort attempt produces better power gains than rushing through them.
instructions: Stand facing a wall or partner, holding a medicine ball at chest height with both hands.
Set your feet shoulder-width apart and brace your core.
Explosively push the ball forward from your chest with maximum force, fully extending your arms.
Retrieve the ball, reset your stance completely, and prepare for the next rep.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, giving maximum effort on each throw.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: Chest Push from 3 point stance
description:
**Starting from a three-point football stance adds a full-body explosion to the chest pass — you're driving from the ground up, not just pushing with your arms.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This variation integrates your legs and hips into the chest push, training the entire kinetic chain to work together. It mimics the explosive movement patterns in football, rugby, and combat sports where you need to generate force from a low position. It's as much a total-body power exercise as it is a chest exercise.

**Pro tip:** Drive hard off your back foot as you rise — the power should start from your legs and transfer through your hips and core before your arms even start pushing the ball.
instructions: Get into a three-point stance: feet staggered, one hand on the ground, medicine ball held at chest level with the other hand (or both hands with ball on the ground).
Explode upward and forward out of the stance, driving through your legs.
As you rise, push the medicine ball explosively from your chest toward a wall or partner.
Retrieve the ball, reset into the three-point stance, and repeat.
Alternate your lead foot between sets for balanced development.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: Chest Push with Run Release
description:
**You throw the ball and immediately sprint after it — combining explosive chest power with acceleration for a drill that's pure athletic chaos in the best way.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This drill bridges the gap between strength training and sport performance by chaining an explosive push with an immediate sprint. It trains your body to transition from upper-body power to full-speed running — a pattern that shows up constantly in field sports. It's conditioning and power training rolled into one.

**Pro tip:** Don't wait to see where the ball goes — start sprinting the instant the ball leaves your hands. The goal is seamless transition from push to sprint, not push then sprint.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest height.
Explosively push the ball forward from your chest with maximum force.
Immediately sprint forward after the ball as soon as it leaves your hands.
Retrieve the ball, walk back to the starting position, and reset.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on an instant transition from push to sprint.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: Chest Stretch on Stability Ball
description:
**Draping your body over a stability ball opens up your chest, shoulders, and abs in one relaxing stretch — gravity does the work while you just breathe and let go.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The curved surface of the stability ball lets your spine extend naturally, opening up the entire front of your body. This is especially effective after a heavy chest workout or a long day of sitting. The unstable surface also gently engages your core, making it a stretch that doubles as light stabilization work.

**Pro tip:** Walk your feet out further to increase the stretch — the more of your back that drapes over the ball, the deeper the opening across your chest and shoulders.
instructions: Sit on a stability ball, then slowly walk your feet forward until your upper back and head are resting on the ball.
Let your arms fall open to the sides with palms facing up, allowing gravity to pull your chest open.
Relax your entire body and breathe deeply, letting the ball support your spine.
Hold for 20-30 seconds, sinking deeper into the stretch with each exhale.
To finish, walk your feet back and sit up slowly.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds
Breathe deeply throughout
Do not bounce

name: Clap Push-Up
description:
**Launching yourself off the ground hard enough to clap mid-air turns the humble push-up into an explosive power exercise that builds fast-twitch chest fibers.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Clap push-ups train your chest to produce force rapidly — the kind of explosive power that makes you faster and more athletic. You need to push hard enough to get airborne, which recruits muscle fibers that slow push-ups never touch. They're also a great ego boost when you nail them cleanly.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the ground away as hard and fast as possible — the clap is just proof you pushed hard enough. If you can't get enough air for a clean clap, work on explosive regular push-ups first.

**Biomechanics:** The hardest point is the middle of the push where you're accelerating your body upward. Your chest is loaded in a stretched position at the bottom, and the explosive nature of the movement recruits more muscle fibers than a slow push-up. Great for building power, though the impact on landing means joint health matters.
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart and body in a straight line.
Lower your chest to the floor in a controlled motion.
Explode upward as hard as possible, pushing your hands off the ground.
While airborne, quickly clap your hands together in front of your chest.
Land with soft elbows back in the push-up position, immediately lowering into the next rep.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a tight core throughout.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension
Land with soft elbows to absorb impact and protect your joints

name: Clock Push-Up
description:
**Move your hands around an imaginary clock face between reps, and suddenly a basic push-up becomes a full-chest assault from every angle.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

By shifting hand position after each rep, you hit your chest from multiple angles in a single set. This builds well-rounded chest development and challenges your stabilizer muscles far more than standard push-ups. It's also a great way to build endurance and coordination without any equipment.

**Pro tip:** Place a small object on the floor as your "clock center" and physically move one hand to each hour position — don't just guess. Precise placement keeps the angles honest.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance is hardest in the middle of each rep and lighter at the top and bottom. Because your hands shift positions, different parts of your chest get loaded in the stretched position throughout the set. Great for building chest size and shoulder stability over time.
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart.
Perform one push-up, then walk one hand to a new 'clock' position (e.g., from 12 o'clock toward 1 o'clock).
Perform another push-up at the new hand position, keeping your core tight and body straight.
Continue rotating your hand placement around the clock after each rep.
Complete a full rotation (all 12 positions) or work halfway and switch the moving hand.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Cross Over - With Bands
description:
**Anchor a band behind you and bring your hands together in a hugging motion — the harder you squeeze, the harder the band pulls back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Bands get heavier the more you stretch them, so this exercise is toughest right when your chest is fully squeezed together. That constant increasing tension makes your chest work overtime at the finish of every rep. It's portable, joint-friendly, and surprisingly effective for building definition.

**Pro tip:** Cross your hands past each other at the end of each rep — don't just touch them. That extra inch of crossover gives your inner chest a much harder contraction.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance increases as you bring your hands together, meaning the exercise gets hardest at the squeeze. The mid-range loading keeps tension on the chest without overstressing the shoulder joint. Excellent for adding volume and chest detail without heavy weights.
instructions: Anchor a resistance band at chest height behind you (door anchor, pole, or rack).
Grab one end in each hand and step forward until there's light tension with arms wide.
With a slight bend in your elbows, sweep both hands forward and together in an arc.
Cross your hands slightly past midline and squeeze your chest hard for one second.
Slowly return to the starting position with arms wide, controlling the band's pull.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and locked in position throughout
Step forward with one foot for balance and to pre-stretch the chest
Squeeze the cables together at the midline for a full contraction
Control the eccentric — do not let the cables snap your arms back

name: Decline Barbell Bench Press
description:
**Tilt the bench downward and suddenly your lower chest does the lion's share of the pressing — a classic move for building that full, rounded chest look.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The decline angle shifts the workload toward the lower portion of your chest, an area that flat pressing often under-develops. Most people can also handle slightly more weight on decline than flat bench, which means more total load on the chest. It's a staple for anyone chasing a complete chest.

**Pro tip:** Set the bench to only 15–20 degrees of decline — any steeper and your shoulders take over. A slight angle is all you need.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance is toughest in the middle of the press and lighter at lockout and at the bottom. The declined position puts your chest in a strong stretched position at the bottom of each rep. Solid for building lower chest thickness when paired with proper progressive overload.
instructions: Set a decline bench to 15–20 degrees and secure your legs under the pads.
Unrack the barbell with a grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Lower the bar slowly to your lower chest, keeping your elbows at roughly 45 degrees.
Press the bar back up to full arm extension, squeezing your chest at the top.
Re-rack the bar carefully by guiding it back into the hooks.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom
coaching_cues: Chest up even on the decline

name: Decline Cable Fly
description:
**Set the cables high and the bench low, and you get a fly that keeps relentless tension on your lower chest from the first inch to the last.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Unlike dumbbell flyes where gravity reduces tension at the top, cables keep pulling on your chest throughout the entire rep. The decline angle directs that constant tension straight into the lower chest. This makes it one of the best isolation moves for building a full, defined chest from top to bottom.

**Pro tip:** Don't let the handles drift above your chest line — keep the arc aimed at your lower chest. Think about bringing your pinkies together, not your thumbs.

**Biomechanics:** The cable provides steady resistance through the full range of motion, with a strong stretch at the bottom when your arms are wide. The decline angle enhances the stretch on the lower chest fibers. High potential for chest growth due to the deep stretch under constant load.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the highest position and place a decline bench between them.
Lie on the decline bench, secure your legs, and grab a handle in each hand.
Start with arms extended above your lower chest, palms facing each other, slight elbow bend.
Open your arms wide in a controlled arc until you feel a deep stretch across your chest.
Sweep your hands back together in the same arc, squeezing your chest at the top.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the movement
Squeeze the chest at the midline — imagine hugging a tree
Control the return to the stretched position rather than letting cables pull you back
Adjust pulley height to target different pec fiber angles
coaching_cues: Elbows locked, chest does the work

name: Decline Dumbbell Bench Press
description:
**Dumbbells on a decline bench let each arm work independently, exposing and fixing strength imbalances while hammering your lower chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Using dumbbells instead of a barbell means each side of your chest has to pull its own weight — no hiding a weaker arm. The decline angle targets the lower chest, and dumbbells allow a deeper range of motion than a barbell since there's no bar stopping at your chest. More range plus independent arms equals better overall chest development.

**Pro tip:** At the bottom of each rep, let the dumbbells sink an inch lower than where a barbell would stop. That extra stretch is the whole reason you're using dumbbells here.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance peaks in the middle of the press and is lighter at the top and bottom. The stretched position at the bottom is where your chest is loaded the hardest, and dumbbells let you go deeper into that stretch. High growth potential thanks to the combination of deep stretch and independent loading.
instructions: Set a decline bench to 15–20 degrees and secure your legs under the pads.
Sit on the bench with a dumbbell on each knee, then kick them up as you lie back.
Hold the dumbbells above your lower chest with palms facing forward.
Lower both dumbbells slowly until your upper arms are just below parallel to the floor.
Press the dumbbells back up and together, squeezing your chest at the top.
When finished, bring the dumbbells to your knees and sit up carefully.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press
coaching_cues: Chest up even on the decline

name: Decline Dumbbell Fly
description:
**Open your arms wide on a decline bench with dumbbells and feel your lower chest stretch like a rubber band before snapping back together.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

This isolates your chest by removing triceps from the equation — it's all chest doing the work. The decline angle focuses the stretch and contraction on the lower chest, and dumbbells allow you to go deeper than cables or machines. If your lower chest is lagging, this is one of the most direct ways to target it.

**Pro tip:** Stop lowering when you feel a strong stretch — not when your arms hit some arbitrary depth. Everyone's shoulder mobility is different, so let the stretch be your guide.

**Biomechanics:** The exercise is hardest at the bottom when your arms are wide and your chest is fully stretched. Tension decreases as you bring the dumbbells together at the top. The deep stretch under load gives this exercise high growth potential for the lower chest.
instructions: Set a decline bench to 15–20 degrees and secure your legs under the pads.
Lie back with a dumbbell in each hand, arms extended above your lower chest, palms facing each other.
With a slight bend in your elbows, slowly open your arms out to the sides in a wide arc.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch across your chest, then reverse the motion.
Bring the dumbbells back together above your lower chest, squeezing at the top.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain
Secure your legs and use moderate weight — decline flyes are harder to control

name: Decline Dumbbell Flyes
description:
**The classic chest-opening movement performed on a decline — gravity pulls the dumbbells outward, creating an intense stretch that flat flyes simply can't match.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Flyes on a decline put your lower chest under a deep stretch at the bottom of every rep. Because your torso is angled downward, the dumbbells want to pull your arms apart even more than on a flat bench. This extra stretch stimulus is what drives muscle growth in the lower chest region.

**Pro tip:** Keep your elbows locked at the same slight bend throughout — if they straighten or bend more during the rep, you're using momentum instead of your chest.

**Biomechanics:** Maximum resistance occurs at the bottom of the arc where your chest is fully lengthened. As you bring the weights together, tension decreases. The combination of decline angle and dumbbell freedom creates a very deep loaded stretch, giving this variation high growth potential.
instructions: Secure yourself on a decline bench set to 15–20 degrees with legs locked under the pads.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand above your lower chest, arms nearly straight with a slight elbow bend.
Lower the dumbbells outward in a wide arc, keeping the elbow angle fixed.
Descend until you feel a strong stretch in your lower chest — don't force extra depth.
Reverse the arc and bring the dumbbells back together, squeezing your chest at the top.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain
Secure your legs and use moderate weight — decline flyes are harder to control

name: Decline Dumbbell Pullover
description:
**Reach a dumbbell back behind your head on a decline bench and feel your chest and lats stretch simultaneously — it's like a fly and a pullover had a baby.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Lats, triceps.

The pullover stretches your chest in a direction that no press or fly can replicate — overhead and behind you. Adding a decline angle increases how far you can reach back, deepening the stretch on your chest. This makes it a unique chest builder that also hits your lats as a bonus.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips pressed firmly into the bench as you lower the weight behind your head. If your hips rise, you're losing the decline advantage and reducing chest stretch.

**Biomechanics:** The exercise gets harder as the dumbbell moves further behind your head, loading your chest in a deep stretch. Tension decreases as you pull the weight back over your chest. The extended range from the decline position gives this exercise high growth potential for the upper chest and serratus area.
instructions: Secure yourself on a decline bench with legs locked under the pads.
Hold one dumbbell with both hands (diamond grip on the inner plate) above your chest, arms slightly bent.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back and behind your head in an arc, keeping your elbows slightly bent.
Stretch until your arms are roughly in line with your ears or you feel a strong chest stretch.
Pull the dumbbell back over your chest using your chest and lats, not your arms.
Squeeze at the top before starting the next rep.
tips: Keep your hips low and ribcage elevated to maximize the stretch overhead
Do not let the weight pull your lower back into excessive extension
Focus on pulling with the chest, not the lats — think about squeezing your pecs
Use a controlled tempo, especially in the stretched position

name: Decline Push-Up
description:
**Elevate your feet on a bench or box and your bodyweight push-up instantly becomes a targeted upper-chest and shoulder builder.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Raising your feet shifts more of your bodyweight onto your hands and angles the push toward your upper chest and front shoulders. The higher your feet, the harder it gets — making this a simple way to progress push-ups without adding weight. It's one of the best bodyweight exercises for building the upper portion of your chest.

**Pro tip:** Place your feet on a stable surface — not a wobbly ball. You want your energy going into pressing, not balancing. A bench or sturdy box works perfectly.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance is highest in the middle of the rep and lighter at the top and bottom. The elevated feet increase the percentage of bodyweight you're pressing and shift the angle to favor the upper chest. Moderate growth potential — excellent as a warm-up, finisher, or for those training without equipment.
instructions: Place your feet on a bench, box, or sturdy elevated surface behind you.
Set your hands on the floor slightly wider than shoulder width, arms straight.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — don't let your hips sag.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows to about 90 degrees.
Push back up to full arm extension, squeezing your chest at the top.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Decline Smith Press
description:
**The Smith machine's fixed bar path on a decline bench lets you focus purely on pushing heavy weight without worrying about balance — pure lower-chest overload.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The Smith machine removes the need to stabilize the bar, so every ounce of effort goes into pressing. On a decline, this means you can safely push heavier loads into your lower chest without a spotter. It's ideal for training to failure or doing drop sets where a free barbell would be risky.

**Pro tip:** Position the bench so the bar path travels to your lower chest — not your neck or belly. Slide the bench forward or back until the bar naturally touches the bottom of your chest.

**Biomechanics:** The fixed path provides consistent resistance throughout the press, without the bar drifting forward or back. This keeps tension steady on the chest rather than shifting to stabilizers. Good growth potential, especially useful for safely pushing intensity techniques like drop sets and forced reps.
instructions: Position a decline bench inside the Smith machine so the bar aligns with your lower chest.
Secure your legs under the pads and lie back on the bench.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width and rotate it to unrack.
Lower the bar slowly to your lower chest, keeping elbows at about 45 degrees.
Press the bar back up to full extension and squeeze your chest.
Rotate the bar to re-rack when your set is complete.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively
coaching_cues: Chest up even on the decline

name: Dips - Chest Version
description:
**Lean forward on the dip bars and your chest takes over what's normally a triceps exercise — one of the most effective bodyweight chest builders ever invented.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The forward lean is what makes this a chest exercise instead of a triceps exercise. By tilting your torso, you put your chest in a deep stretch at the bottom of every rep under your full bodyweight. Most people can eventually add weight with a belt, making this a movement you'll never outgrow.

**Pro tip:** Lean your torso forward about 30 degrees and keep your elbows flared slightly outward. If you stay upright, your triceps do most of the work and your chest misses out.

**Biomechanics:** The exercise gets easier as you push up, meaning the hardest part is the deep stretch at the bottom. This loaded stretch position is excellent for chest growth. High growth potential — the deep stretch under heavy load makes this one of the best bodyweight chest exercises for building size.
instructions: Grip the parallel dip bars and lift yourself to straight arms.
Lean your torso forward about 30 degrees — don't stay upright.
Lower yourself slowly by bending your elbows until you feel a deep stretch in your chest.
Keep your elbows slightly flared out, not tucked tight to your body.
Press back up to straight arms, focusing on squeezing your chest, not just extending your arms.
tips: Lean forward approximately 30 degrees to shift emphasis from triceps to chest
Lower until your upper arms are at least parallel to the floor for full pec stretch
Keep elbows slightly flared rather than tucked to increase pec involvement
Control the descent — do not drop into the bottom position
If bodyweight is too easy, add load with a belt rather than increasing speed

name: Drop Push
description:
**Start with your hands on raised surfaces, drop to the floor, and explode back up — a plyometric push-up variation that builds explosive chest power.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The drop creates a rapid stretch in your chest that your muscles must instantly reverse, training explosive power. This type of reactive strength carries over to sports and makes your regular push-ups and bench press feel easier. It's an advanced bodyweight move that builds power without any equipment.

**Pro tip:** Start with a very low drop height (a couple of inches) and only increase once you can land softly and push back up without your form breaking down. Height means nothing if your wrists are taking a beating.

**Biomechanics:** The rapid drop loads your chest quickly in the stretched position, and you must immediately reverse it. Tension is brief but intense, training your muscles to produce force fast. Lower growth potential for size, but excellent for building power and speed in pressing movements.
instructions: Place two low platforms (weight plates or low boxes) shoulder-width apart.
Start in a push-up position with one hand on each platform.
Remove your hands from the platforms and drop to the floor between them, catching yourself in a push-up position.
Immediately push explosively off the floor to land back on the platforms.
Reset your hand position and repeat for the desired reps.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: Dumbbell Around The World
description:
**Sweep the dumbbells in a giant circle from your hips to overhead and back — your chest stays under tension through an enormous range of motion that no other fly can match.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

This exercise takes your chest through its full arc of motion — from stretched at the bottom, through the mid-range, all the way overhead, and back. That massive range of motion means more time under tension and more muscle fibers recruited than a standard fly. It's a unique movement that builds chest size and shoulder mobility simultaneously.

**Pro tip:** Use significantly lighter dumbbells than your normal fly weight. The extended arc and overhead position put your shoulders in a vulnerable spot — control beats load on this one.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance shifts throughout the circular path — hardest when the dumbbells are out to the sides and lightest when they're directly above or below you. The stretched position at the sides loads the chest deeply. High growth potential due to the extreme range of motion and prolonged time under tension.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench with a light dumbbell in each hand at your sides, palms facing up.
With nearly straight arms, sweep both dumbbells in a wide arc down toward your hips.
Continue the arc overhead past your head, keeping arms wide and elbows slightly bent.
Reverse the path, sweeping the dumbbells back down and around to the starting position.
Move slowly and controlled — this is not a momentum exercise.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain

name: Dumbbell Bench Press
description:
**The dumbbell bench press gives you everything the barbell version does, plus a deeper stretch and the freedom to find your most natural pressing angle.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Dumbbells let you lower the weight past where a barbell would stop at your chest, giving your pecs a deeper stretch. Each arm also works independently, so your stronger side can't compensate for the weaker one. It's arguably the single best dumbbell exercise for overall chest development.

**Pro tip:** At the bottom of each rep, rotate your hands slightly so your pinkies are a touch higher than your thumbs. This small rotation increases chest activation without stressing your shoulders.

**Biomechanics:** The press is hardest in the middle of the rep and lighter at the top and bottom. Dumbbells allow a deeper stretch at the bottom compared to a barbell, which is where the most growth stimulus occurs. High growth potential — the combination of heavy loading, deep stretch, and independent arms makes this a top-tier chest builder.
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell on each knee.
Kick the dumbbells up one at a time as you lie back, pressing them above your chest.
Lower both dumbbells slowly until your upper arms dip just below parallel.
Press the dumbbells back up and slightly together, squeezing your chest at the top.
When finished, bring the dumbbells back to your knees and sit up.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press

name: Dumbbell Bench Press with Neutral Grip
description:
**Turn your palms to face each other and the bench press becomes dramatically easier on your shoulders while still crushing your chest and triceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The neutral (palms-facing) grip externally rotates your shoulders into a more natural position, reducing stress on the shoulder joint. This means you can press heavy and go deep without the shoulder pinch that some people feel with a standard grip. It also shifts slightly more work to the inner chest and triceps.

**Pro tip:** Keep the dumbbells directly above your chest — not above your face. A common mistake is letting neutral-grip dumbbells drift toward your head, which turns it into a shoulder press.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance peaks in the middle of the press, similar to a standard dumbbell bench. The neutral grip allows most people to achieve a slightly deeper stretch at the bottom without shoulder discomfort. High growth potential — all the benefits of dumbbell pressing with a more joint-friendly grip.
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell on each knee.
Lie back and press the dumbbells up with palms facing each other (neutral grip).
Lower the dumbbells slowly, keeping your palms facing each other throughout.
Descend until your upper arms are just below parallel and you feel a chest stretch.
Press back up to full extension, maintaining the neutral grip, and squeeze your chest.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press

name: Dumbbell Fly Flat
description:
**Open your arms wide with dumbbells on a flat bench and your chest does all the work — no triceps help, no cheating, just pure chest isolation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The flat fly removes your triceps from the movement, making your chest the sole driver. This direct isolation is what makes flyes so effective for building chest width and definition. The deep stretch at the bottom with dumbbells is hard to replicate with any other equipment.

**Pro tip:** Imagine you're hugging a large tree — that's the arm path. Your elbows should stay at the same slight bend from start to finish. If they straighten, you've turned it into a press.

**Biomechanics:** Maximum resistance occurs at the bottom when your arms are wide open and your chest is fully stretched. Tension drops significantly as you bring the dumbbells together at the top. High growth potential due to the deep loaded stretch, which is a powerful driver of muscle growth.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand, arms extended above your chest.
Turn your palms to face each other with a slight bend in your elbows.
Lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the elbow bend constant.
Descend until you feel a strong stretch across your chest.
Reverse the arc and bring the dumbbells back together above your chest, squeezing at the top.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain

name: Dumbbell Flyes
description:
**There's a reason flyes have been a chest-day staple for decades — nothing else stretches your pecs under load quite like opening your arms wide with dumbbells.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Flyes work your chest through a huge range of motion with zero triceps involvement. The deep stretch at the bottom of each rep is where the real growth happens — your chest fibers are lengthened under load, which is one of the strongest signals for muscle growth. Simple, effective, and timeless.

**Pro tip:** Lower the dumbbells only until your hands are level with your chest — not below the bench. Going too deep shifts stress from your chest to your shoulder joint.

**Biomechanics:** The bottom of the fly is where resistance and stretch are both at their peak, making it the most productive part of the rep. Tension fades as the dumbbells come together overhead. High growth potential — the loaded stretch makes flyes one of the best chest isolation exercises for hypertrophy.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand above your chest, palms facing inward.
Maintain a slight, fixed bend in your elbows throughout the movement.
Open your arms and lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a controlled arc.
Stop when you feel a deep stretch in your chest — roughly when your hands are at chest level.
Squeeze your chest to bring the dumbbells back together above you in the same arc.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain

name: Dumbbell Hex Press
description:
**Press two dumbbells together so hard they never separate, and your inner chest lights up in a way that standard presses completely miss.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The constant inward squeeze between the dumbbells activates the inner portion of your chest throughout the entire rep. Most pressing movements lose chest tension at the top, but the hex press keeps it because you're still squeezing the weights together at lockout. It's a targeted way to build that chest separation line down the middle.

**Pro tip:** Use hexagonal (flat-sided) dumbbells so they sit flush against each other. Round dumbbells roll and make the squeeze inconsistent.

**Biomechanics:** Because you're squeezing inward the entire time, your chest stays contracted even at the top of the press where normal presses lose tension. The shortened position loading means the exercise is hardest when your chest is squeezed, not stretched. Lower stretch-based growth potential, but excellent for building inner chest definition and mind-muscle connection.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with two hex dumbbells pressed together above your chest, palms facing each other.
Push the dumbbells firmly against each other — maintain this inward pressure throughout.
Lower the dumbbells together to your chest, keeping them pressed against each other.
Press back up to full extension while still squeezing the dumbbells together.
Focus on the inward squeeze — that's what makes this exercise work.
tips: Squeeze the weight as hard as possible throughout the entire set
Press forward at chest height, not upward
Focus on the isometric adduction — this is what activates the inner chest
Keep shoulders down and back, do not round forward

name: Dumbbell Pullover
description:
**Reach a dumbbell behind your head while lying on a bench and feel your chest and lats stretch in a way that no press or fly can replicate.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Lats, triceps.

The pullover stretches your chest in the overhead direction — a plane of motion that presses and flyes completely ignore. This unique stretch angle recruits chest fibers that other exercises miss, making it an excellent complement to your pressing movements. Your lats get a solid workout too, making it a two-for-one upper body builder.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips low and don't let your back arch excessively as the weight goes behind your head. A slight arch is fine, but if your ribs flare up dramatically, you've lost the chest stretch.

**Biomechanics:** The exercise gets harder as the dumbbell moves further behind your head, loading your chest in a deep overhead stretch. Tension decreases as you pull the weight back over your chest. High growth potential — the unique stretch angle stimulates chest fibers that horizontal pressing can't reach.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench with your head near the edge, holding one dumbbell with both hands above your chest.
Grip the dumbbell by cupping the inner plate with both palms, arms slightly bent.
Lower the dumbbell slowly behind and below your head in an arc, keeping your elbows slightly bent.
Stretch until you feel a strong pull across your chest and lats.
Pull the dumbbell back over your chest using your chest muscles, not your arms.
tips: Keep your hips low and ribcage elevated to maximize the stretch overhead
Do not let the weight pull your lower back into excessive extension
Focus on pulling with the chest, not the lats — think about squeezing your pecs
Use a controlled tempo, especially in the stretched position

name: Dumbbell Squeeze Press
description:
**Mash two dumbbells together and press — the constant inward force turns an ordinary press into an inner-chest isolation exercise disguised as a compound movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Like the hex press, the squeeze press keeps your chest contracted throughout the entire rep by forcing you to push the dumbbells together. The difference is the pressing motion adds triceps and shoulders into the mix, letting you use more weight. It's a hybrid that gives you the benefits of both a press and an isometric chest squeeze.

**Pro tip:** Actively try to crush the dumbbells together as hard as you can — don't just let them touch. The harder you squeeze inward, the more your inner chest works.

**Biomechanics:** The inward squeezing force keeps your chest engaged at the top of the press where it would normally relax. This shortened-position loading is great for building the inner chest but provides less stretch stimulus. Lower stretch-based growth potential, but the constant tension makes it effective for chest activation and pump work.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding two dumbbells together above your chest, palms facing each other.
Press the dumbbells firmly against each other with constant inward force.
Lower the dumbbells to your chest while maintaining the squeeze between them.
Press back up to full arm extension, still crushing the dumbbells together.
Keep the inward pressure constant — if the dumbbells separate, reset and squeeze harder.
tips: Squeeze the weight as hard as possible throughout the entire set
Press forward at chest height, not upward
Focus on the isometric adduction — this is what activates the inner chest
Keep shoulders down and back, do not round forward

name: Dynamic Chest Stretch
description:
**Swing your arms through progressively wider arcs to open up a tight chest — the perfect warm-up that primes your pecs for heavy pressing without any equipment.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

This dynamic stretch increases blood flow to your chest and shoulders while gradually increasing your range of motion. Unlike static stretching, the movement-based approach warms up the muscles and joints simultaneously, reducing injury risk before heavy pressing. It's quick, effective, and should be part of every chest day warm-up.

**Pro tip:** Start with small arm circles and gradually increase the range over 10–15 reps. Don't go full range on the first rep — let your body open up naturally.

**Biomechanics:** There's no significant external resistance — the stretch comes from your own body moving through its range of motion. The loading is minimal and evenly distributed. No direct growth potential, but it prepares your chest and shoulders for the exercises that do build muscle.
instructions: Stand tall with your arms at your sides and feet shoulder-width apart.
Swing both arms forward and across your body, then back and wide behind you.
Gradually increase the range of each swing, opening your chest wider each time.
Perform 10–15 controlled swings, breathing naturally throughout.
Stop when your chest feels warm and your range of motion has fully opened up.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds
Breathe deeply throughout
Do not bounce
