name: Elbows Back
description:
**Pull your elbows straight back behind you and feel your chest open up instantly — the simplest chest stretch you can do anywhere, anytime.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

This stretch counteracts the forward-rounded posture that comes from sitting at a desk or doing too much pressing without stretching. By pulling your elbows behind your body, you lengthen the chest muscles and improve shoulder positioning. It takes five seconds and makes a noticeable difference in how your chest feels before training.

**Pro tip:** Squeeze your shoulder blades together as you pull your elbows back — this deepens the chest stretch and also activates your upper back. Hold for 2–3 seconds at the furthest point.

**Biomechanics:** No external resistance — the stretch is created by your own arm position and gravity. The chest is loaded in a lengthened position passively. No direct growth potential, but regular use improves chest flexibility and pressing range of motion over time.
instructions: Stand tall with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Bend your elbows to 90 degrees and raise them to shoulder height, like a goalpost position.
Pull both elbows straight back behind you, squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Hold the stretched position for 2–3 seconds, feeling the opening across your chest.
Release and repeat for 8–10 reps.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds
Breathe deeply throughout
Do not bounce

name: Extended Range One-Arm Kettlebell Floor Press
description:
**Press a kettlebell from the floor with one arm while the bell hangs below your wrist — the offset weight and single-arm challenge make this a uniquely demanding chest and core exercise.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The kettlebell hangs below your hand, pulling your wrist into extension and forcing your stabilizer muscles to work overtime. Pressing one arm at a time means your core has to fight rotation, turning a chest exercise into a full-body stability challenge. The floor limits your range of motion just enough to protect your shoulders while still giving your chest a solid stretch.

**Pro tip:** Let the kettlebell rest against the outside of your forearm at the bottom — this is the "extended range" part. It lets you sink slightly deeper than a standard floor press for more chest stretch.

**Biomechanics:** The floor stops your elbow from going too far back, protecting your shoulder while still allowing a meaningful chest stretch. The offset kettlebell load makes the pressing arc less predictable, recruiting more stabilizer muscles. High growth potential due to the deep stretch and unilateral loading.
instructions: Lie on the floor with a kettlebell in one hand, arm extended above your chest, bell hanging to the outside of your wrist.
Bend your knees and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Lower the kettlebell slowly until your elbow touches the floor and the bell sinks slightly past your wrist.
Press the kettlebell back up to full extension, keeping your core braced to prevent rotation.
Complete all reps on one side before switching arms.
tips: Let your upper arms touch the floor briefly at the bottom — this is your range limiter
Keep your legs flat or bent depending on your low back comfort
Press straight up, not back toward your face
Retract your scapulae even without a bench for support
coaching_cues: Arms touch the floor, then press

name: Flat Bench Cable Flyes
description:
**Cables keep tension on your chest from the first inch of the fly to the last — unlike dumbbells, there's no rest at the top, so your chest never gets a break.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The cable's constant pull means your chest is working hard through the entire range of motion, including at the top where dumbbell flyes lose all tension. This makes cable flyes superior for building a strong mind-muscle connection and accumulating more total work per rep. Lying on a flat bench keeps the focus on the middle chest.

**Pro tip:** Set the pulleys to the lowest position so the cables pull outward and slightly downward — this creates the deepest stretch at the bottom of the fly. High pulleys change the angle entirely.

**Biomechanics:** Unlike dumbbell flyes where tension drops at the top, cables maintain resistance throughout the full arc. The stretched position at the bottom still provides the strongest stimulus, but the constant tension at the top adds extra growth stimulus. High growth potential — arguably the best fly variation for overall chest development.
instructions: Place a flat bench between two low cable pulleys.
Grab a handle in each hand and lie back on the bench.
Start with arms extended above your chest, palms facing each other, slight elbow bend.
Open your arms in a wide arc until you feel a deep stretch across your chest.
Sweep your arms back together, squeezing your chest hard at the top for one second.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the movement
Squeeze the chest at the midline — imagine hugging a tree
Control the return to the stretched position rather than letting cables pull you back
Adjust pulley height to target different pec fiber angles
coaching_cues: Elbows locked, chest does the work

name: Floor Press
description:
**Take the bench away and press a barbell from the floor — the ground stops your elbows at the perfect depth, making this a shoulder-friendly pressing powerhouse.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The floor limits how far your elbows can travel back, which protects your shoulders while still giving your chest a solid workout. Each rep starts from a dead stop on the floor, eliminating the bounce and stretch reflex that people use to cheat on bench press. This builds raw pressing strength from the most difficult part of the range.

**Pro tip:** Pause for a full second with your elbows on the floor before each rep. This kills all momentum and forces your chest and triceps to generate force from a dead stop — no cheating.

**Biomechanics:** The floor cuts off the bottom portion of the range of motion, so the chest is loaded in a moderate stretch rather than a deep stretch. Resistance is highest in the middle of the press. Moderate growth potential for the chest — the limited range reduces stretch stimulus, but the dead-stop start builds excellent pressing strength.
instructions: Lie on the floor under a barbell in a rack set to a low height, or have a partner hand you the bar.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width and unrack it above your chest.
Lower the bar until both elbows rest on the floor — pause for one second.
Press the bar back up to full arm extension, squeezing your chest at the top.
Re-rack the bar carefully when your set is complete.
tips: Let your upper arms touch the floor briefly at the bottom — this is your range limiter
Keep your legs flat or bent depending on your low back comfort
Press straight up, not back toward your face
Retract your scapulae even without a bench for support
coaching_cues: Arms touch the floor, then press

name: Forward Drag with Press
description:
**Crawling forward while pressing your body off the ground turns a simple push-up into a full-body locomotive challenge that torches your chest and legs simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps, quads, glutes.

This exercise combines a pressing motion with forward movement, forcing your chest to work while your legs drive you ahead. The crawling element adds a coordination and conditioning layer you won't get from stationary pressing.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips low and take small, controlled steps forward — rushing the drag kills chest engagement.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance peaks in the middle of each press as you support your moving bodyweight. Loading is highest when your chest is stretched near the ground, tapering off as you extend your arms and shift forward.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart and core braced.
Lower your chest toward the ground while simultaneously dragging one foot forward.
Press up explosively while stepping the opposite foot forward to advance your body.
Continue alternating legs with each press, maintaining a low, controlled crawl.
Aim for 6-10 forward press-and-drag cycles per set.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: Front Raise And Pullover
description:
**This barbell hybrid sweeps the weight from in front of your hips all the way overhead and behind you, stretching your chest through an enormous range of motion few exercises can match.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Lats, shoulders, triceps.

By combining a front raise with a pullover arc, you load the chest in a deep stretch position that drives serious muscle growth. The barbell keeps both arms locked together, letting you use meaningful weight safely.

**Pro tip:** Slow the pullover phase to a 3-second descent behind your head — that's where the growth stimulus lives.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance builds as the barbell moves away from your body during the raise, then peaks when your arms are extended overhead in the stretched position. The ascending strength curve means the hardest part is the deep stretch, which is ideal for chest development.
instructions: Lie face-up on a flat bench holding a barbell with a shoulder-width grip at your hips.
With slightly bent elbows, raise the barbell in an arc from your hips up and over your head.
Continue lowering the barbell behind your head until you feel a deep chest and lat stretch.
Reverse the arc, pulling the barbell back over and down to your hips in one smooth motion.
Keep your elbows at a consistent slight bend throughout the entire movement.
tips: Keep your hips low and ribcage elevated to maximize the stretch overhead
Do not let the weight pull your lower back into excessive extension
Focus on pulling with the chest, not the lats — think about squeezing your pecs
Use a controlled tempo, especially in the stretched position

name: Hammer Grip Incline DB Bench Press
description:
**Turning your palms to face each other on an incline bench quietly shifts the workload onto your inner and upper chest fibers in a way standard grip pressing misses.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The neutral (palms-in) grip reduces shoulder stress while letting you press through a deep range of motion on the incline. This makes it a joint-friendly way to hammer the upper chest with heavy dumbbells.

**Pro tip:** At the bottom, let the dumbbells sink an inch deeper than feels natural — the hammer grip makes this safe and that extra stretch is where growth happens.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance is highest in the mid-range of the press where your chest has the best leverage. The stretched position at the bottom is heavily loaded because the dumbbells are far from your body, making this excellent for building the upper chest.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45 degree incline and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand.
Hold the dumbbells with palms facing each other (neutral grip) at chest level.
Press both dumbbells up until your arms are extended, keeping the palms-in grip throughout.
Lower the dumbbells slowly to the sides of your chest, feeling a deep stretch.
Pause briefly at the bottom before pressing back up for the next rep.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press

name: Heavy Bag Thrust
description:
**Shoving a heavy bag with explosive chest-driven force builds real-world pushing power that no bench press can replicate.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This exercise trains your chest to produce force rapidly against an unstable target. The heavy bag absorbs and redirects energy, forcing your stabilizer muscles to work overtime alongside the main pressing muscles.

**Pro tip:** Reset your stance between each thrust — planting your feet fresh each time generates far more power than rapid-fire pushes.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance peaks in the middle of the thrust as you accelerate the bag, then drops off as the bag swings away. Because you're pushing a free-moving object, the load is highest when your arms are partially extended and your chest is in its strongest position.
instructions: Stand facing a heavy bag at arm's length with feet staggered for stability.
Place both hands on the bag at chest height with elbows bent.
Drive the bag forward explosively by extending your arms and engaging your chest.
Let the bag swing back, absorb it with soft arms, and reset your position.
Repeat for 8-12 powerful thrusts per set, prioritizing force over speed.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: High Cable Crossover
description:
**Pulling two high cables downward in a hugging arc delivers constant, punishing tension to your chest from the very first inch to the last — no dead spots anywhere.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Because the cables pull from above, your chest works hardest in the stretched position with arms wide open. This makes it one of the best isolation moves for chest growth, with very little fatigue to your triceps or joints.

**Pro tip:** Cross your hands slightly past each other at the bottom squeeze — that extra two inches of range adds meaningful inner chest activation.

**Biomechanics:** The cable's angle creates a descending resistance curve, meaning the exercise is hardest when your arms are spread wide and your chest is fully stretched. Tension stays smooth and continuous throughout, unlike dumbbells which lose resistance at the top.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the highest position and attach single handles.
Stand centered between the cables with a slight forward lean and one foot ahead for balance.
With a slight bend in your elbows, sweep both handles downward and inward in a wide arc.
Bring your hands together (or slightly crossed) at hip level, squeezing your chest hard.
Slowly reverse the arc back to the starting position with arms wide, feeling the stretch.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and locked in position throughout
Step forward with one foot for balance and to pre-stretch the chest
Squeeze the cables together at the midline for a full contraction
Control the eccentric — do not let the cables snap your arms back
Pull downward and inward — think about touching your hip pockets

name: Incline Barbell Bench Press
description:
**The incline barbell bench is the undisputed king of upper chest builders — angling the bench shifts the battle line squarely onto the fibers most people neglect.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The incline angle forces your upper chest to do the heavy lifting instead of letting your lower chest and front shoulders dominate. The barbell lets you load heavy, making this a cornerstone for building a full, balanced chest.

**Pro tip:** Set the bench to 30 degrees, not 45 — steeper angles turn this into a shoulder press in disguise.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance peaks in the mid-range where the barbell is farthest from your shoulder joint. The bottom stretched position is heavily loaded, but the lockout gets easier as your bones stack under the weight.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to roughly 30 degrees and position yourself under a racked barbell.
Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder width and unrack it with arms extended.
Lower the bar slowly to your upper chest, just below the collarbone.
Press the bar back up to full arm extension, driving through your chest.
Keep your shoulder blades pinched together and feet flat on the floor throughout.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Incline Cable Chest Press
description:
**Pressing cables on an incline gives your upper chest the non-stop tension that barbells and dumbbells simply cannot — there is zero rest at any point in the rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Cables maintain resistance through the entire pressing motion, eliminating the easy lockout you get with free weights. This means more time under tension for your upper chest on every single rep.

**Pro tip:** Set the pulleys slightly below shoulder height when seated — this angle maximizes the chest-loading vector on the incline.

**Biomechanics:** The flat resistance curve means the load stays remarkably consistent from the stretched bottom position through to lockout. Unlike a barbell press where the top is easy, cables keep your chest working hard at every point in the range.
instructions: Place an incline bench between two low-to-mid cable pulleys and attach single handles.
Sit back on the bench and grab a handle in each hand at chest level.
Press both handles forward and upward until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly return the handles to chest level, keeping tension in the cables throughout.
Avoid letting the weight stack touch down between reps to maintain constant tension.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Set the pulleys to chest height for a flat press angle
Step forward to create tension at the start position

name: Incline Cable Fly
description:
**Sweeping cables upward on an incline creates a relentless stretch-and-squeeze cycle on your upper chest that free-weight flyes can only dream of matching.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The cable's constant pull means your chest never gets a break, especially in the wide-open stretched position where muscle growth is triggered most. The incline angle directs all that tension to the upper chest specifically.

**Pro tip:** Think about pushing your pinkies together at the top rather than your palms — this subtle cue increases upper chest contraction dramatically.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance is highest when your arms are spread wide and your chest is fully stretched, then tapers slightly as you bring the handles together. This bell-shaped loading pattern is ideal because it maximizes tension exactly where your chest is most vulnerable to growth stimulus.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the low position and place an incline bench centered between them.
Lie back on the bench and take a handle in each hand with arms extended wide.
With a slight elbow bend, sweep both handles upward and inward in a wide hugging arc.
Squeeze your chest at the top when the handles meet above your upper chest.
Lower slowly back to the wide starting position, feeling a deep stretch across your chest.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the movement
Squeeze the chest at the midline — imagine hugging a tree
Control the return to the stretched position rather than letting cables pull you back
Adjust pulley height to target different pec fiber angles
coaching_cues: Elbows locked, chest does the work

name: Incline Cable Flye
description:
**This cable flye variation on an incline bench keeps your upper chest fibers under siege from the first inch of stretch to the final squeeze — no momentum, no escape.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Isolating the chest with cables on an incline removes tricep involvement entirely, so every ounce of effort goes straight into the upper chest. The constant cable tension makes this far superior to dumbbell flyes for sustained muscle stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Pause for a full second in the stretched position with arms wide — resisting the pull in that vulnerable position is where the real growth happens.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve loads your chest most heavily in the mid-range and stretched positions. Because cables pull horizontally, tension remains high even at the bottom where dumbbell flyes would lose all resistance to gravity.
instructions: Position an incline bench between two low cable pulleys and attach D-handles.
Lie back, grab the handles, and start with arms extended above your chest, palms facing in.
Open your arms wide in a controlled arc, lowering until you feel a deep chest stretch.
Pause briefly in the stretched position, then sweep the handles back together overhead.
Maintain a consistent slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the movement
Squeeze the chest at the midline — imagine hugging a tree
Control the return to the stretched position rather than letting cables pull you back
Adjust pulley height to target different pec fiber angles
coaching_cues: Elbows locked, chest does the work

name: Incline Dumbbell Bench Press
description:
**Each arm fights for itself on the incline dumbbell press, exposing and correcting the strength imbalances that barbell pressing hides.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Dumbbells allow a deeper range of motion than a barbell on the incline, stretching the upper chest further at the bottom. The independent arms also recruit more stabilizer muscles, building functional pressing strength.

**Pro tip:** Rotate the dumbbells very slightly inward as you press up — this follows the natural fiber direction of the upper chest and improves the contraction.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve means the movement is hardest in the middle of the press. The bottom position delivers a deep stretch under load because the dumbbells can sink below the level a barbell would stop at, making this excellent for upper chest growth.
instructions: Set a bench to 30-45 degrees and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Kick the dumbbells up to shoulder level and position them at the sides of your upper chest.
Press both dumbbells upward until your arms are fully extended above your upper chest.
Lower the dumbbells slowly, letting them sink deep to get a full stretch at the bottom.
Keep your shoulder blades retracted and feet planted firmly throughout every rep.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press

name: Incline Dumbbell Bench With Palms Facing In
description:
**Flipping your palms to face each other on the incline press is a small grip change that makes a big difference — your shoulders stay happier and your upper chest works harder.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The neutral grip reduces the rotational stress on your shoulder joints while still allowing a deep stretch at the bottom. This makes it a go-to variation for lifters who get shoulder pain from standard incline pressing.

**Pro tip:** Keep the dumbbells directly above your elbows throughout — letting them drift outward turns this into a shoulder exercise.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance peaks in the mid-range of the press and is high in the stretched bottom position. The palms-in grip slightly changes which chest fibers are emphasized, favoring the inner and upper portions compared to a standard pronated grip.
instructions: Set a bench to 30-45 degrees and sit back holding a dumbbell in each hand.
Position the dumbbells at chest level with palms facing each other (neutral grip).
Press the dumbbells straight up, maintaining the palms-in position throughout.
Lower slowly until the dumbbells are level with your upper chest, feeling a deep stretch.
Press back up without rotating your wrists — the neutral grip stays locked the entire set.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press

name: Incline Dumbbell Flyes
description:
**Opening your arms wide against gravity on an incline stretches the upper chest under load like almost nothing else — it's the deep stretch that makes this move a growth machine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Incline dumbbell flyes isolate the chest by removing tricep involvement from the equation. The incline angle targets the upper chest specifically, and the free-weight stretch at the bottom creates a powerful growth signal.

**Pro tip:** Stop the dumbbells when your arms are level with your torso — going deeper with heavy weight risks shoulder injury without adding chest stimulus.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve means the dumbbells are heaviest to control in the mid-range and stretched positions. At the top, gravity pulls straight down through your arms so chest tension drops significantly — don't waste time squeezing at the top.
instructions: Set a bench to 30-45 degrees and lie back with a dumbbell in each hand, arms extended above your chest.
With a slight bend in your elbows, lower both dumbbells outward in a wide arc.
Descend until your arms are roughly parallel with the floor and you feel a strong chest stretch.
Sweep the dumbbells back up in the same arc, bringing them together above your upper chest.
Keep the elbow angle fixed — this is an arc motion, not a pressing motion.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain
Set the bench to 30-45 degrees to bias the upper pec fibers

name: Incline Dumbbell Flyes - With A Twist
description:
**Adding a wrist rotation at the top of each fly rep recruits chest fibers that a standard fly completely ignores — it's a small twist with outsized results.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The twist at the peak contraction forces your chest to work through rotation as well as the standard hugging motion. This hits the chest from a different angle and creates a more complete contraction than regular flyes.

**Pro tip:** Start with palms facing each other at the bottom, then rotate to palms-down at the top — this rotation direction follows the chest's natural fiber path.

**Biomechanics:** Like standard incline flyes, resistance peaks in the stretched and mid-range positions. The twist adds a rotational force demand at the top where standard flyes normally lose tension, effectively filling in the gap in the resistance curve.
instructions: Set a bench to 30-45 degrees and lie back holding dumbbells above your chest with palms facing in.
Lower the dumbbells in a wide arc until your arms are parallel with the floor.
Sweep the dumbbells back up, and as they approach the top, rotate your wrists so palms face your feet.
Squeeze your chest hard at the top with the rotated grip, then rotate back to palms-in as you lower.
Use lighter weight than standard flyes — the rotation demands more control.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain
Set the bench to 30-45 degrees to bias the upper pec fibers

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
description:
**The incline dumbbell press is the Swiss Army knife of upper chest training — heavy enough to build strength, deep enough to build size, and free enough to protect your joints.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This staple movement combines the benefits of incline pressing with the freedom of dumbbells. Each arm works independently through a full range of motion, making it both a mass builder and a balance corrector.

**Pro tip:** At the top of each rep, think about pushing the dumbbells together (even if they don't touch) — this intent fires more upper-inner chest fibers.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve loads the chest hardest in the mid-range. The stretched position at the bottom is where the dumbbells can travel deeper than a barbell allows, creating a strong growth stimulus for the upper chest.
instructions: Adjust a bench to a 30-45 degree incline and sit with dumbbells resting on your thighs.
Use your knees to kick the dumbbells to shoulder height as you lean back into position.
Press the dumbbells up and slightly inward until your arms are extended above your upper chest.
Lower under control to the sides of your chest, allowing a full stretch at the bottom.
Drive back up powerfully, exhaling as you press.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press

name: Incline Dumbbell Squeeze Press
description:
**Pressing dumbbells together so hard they could crack a walnut while pushing them overhead turns a standard press into an inner chest annihilator.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

By squeezing the dumbbells against each other throughout the press, you create constant inner chest activation that normal pressing can't achieve. The incline angle shifts this focus to the upper-inner chest — the hardest area to develop.

**Pro tip:** Use hexagonal dumbbells if available — the flat surfaces lock together and let you squeeze harder without the dumbbells slipping.

**Biomechanics:** Unlike standard presses, the resistance here is highest in the shortened (top) position because the inward squeezing force demands peak chest contraction at lockout. This complements stretch-focused exercises perfectly in a chest program.
instructions: Set a bench to 30-45 degrees and lie back with two dumbbells pressed firmly together above your chest.
Maintain maximum inward pressure — actively try to crush the dumbbells into each other.
Lower the dumbbells together to your chest while keeping them pressed against one another.
Press back up to full extension, never releasing the inward squeezing force.
Use moderate weight — the squeeze is the stimulus, not the load.
tips: Squeeze the weight as hard as possible throughout the entire set
Press forward at chest height, not upward
Focus on the isometric adduction — this is what activates the inner chest
Keep shoulders down and back, do not round forward

name: Incline Push-Up
description:
**Placing your hands on a raised surface makes the push-up easier on paper but sneakily shifts the emphasis to your lower chest — the perfect entry point for beginners building pressing strength.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The elevated hand position reduces the percentage of bodyweight you're pressing, making this accessible for all fitness levels. It also changes the pressing angle to target the lower and mid chest more than a floor push-up.

**Pro tip:** Use a surface that's about hip height to start, then progressively lower it over weeks — this is how you build toward full push-ups.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance peaks in the mid-range when your chest is roughly parallel with your hands. The stretched position at the bottom is well-loaded, and the higher the surface, the less total bodyweight resistance you face.
instructions: Place your hands shoulder-width apart on a sturdy elevated surface like a bench or box.
Walk your feet back until your body forms a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward the surface by bending your elbows to about 90 degrees.
Push back up to full arm extension, keeping your core tight throughout.
Maintain a rigid body line — don't let your hips sag or pike up.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Incline Push-Up Depth Jump
description:
**Launching yourself off an elevated surface and catching your fall builds explosive chest power that slow, grinding reps simply cannot develop.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The depth jump element forces your chest to absorb and redirect force rapidly, training the fast-twitch muscle fibers responsible for power. This is a plyometric movement that bridges the gap between strength training and athletic performance.

**Pro tip:** Start with a low surface (12-18 inches) and master the landing before adding height — sloppy landings wreck your wrists.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance spikes dramatically during the landing/catch phase as your chest decelerates your bodyweight. The mid-range is where peak force occurs, making this a powerful stimulus for fast-twitch chest fibers that respond to explosive loading.
instructions: Set up two elevated surfaces (boxes or steps) slightly wider than shoulder width.
Start in an incline push-up position with hands on one surface.
Lower into a push-up, then explode upward, launching your hands off the surface.
Land with hands on the two separate surfaces, immediately absorbing the impact by lowering into the next rep.
Reset and repeat, focusing on explosive power and controlled landings.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Incline Push-Up Medium
description:
**The medium-height incline push-up sits in the sweet spot between too easy and too hard — it's the Goldilocks variation that builds real chest strength without crushing beginners.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Using a medium-height surface (roughly knee to hip height) provides enough resistance to challenge your chest meaningfully while keeping the movement accessible. It's the ideal progression step between high incline and floor push-ups.

**Pro tip:** Place your hands on the edge of the surface rather than flat on top — this allows your chest to dip slightly below hand level for extra range of motion.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance is bell-shaped, peaking in the mid-range of the push-up. The moderate incline means you're pressing roughly 50-60% of your bodyweight, which is enough to drive strength gains for most people.
instructions: Find a sturdy surface at roughly knee-to-hip height — a bench, sturdy chair, or low wall works well.
Place your hands shoulder-width apart on the edge and walk your feet back to form a straight body line.
Lower your chest to the surface with control, keeping elbows at roughly 45 degrees from your body.
Press back up to full extension, squeezing your chest at the top.
Keep your core engaged and body rigid from head to heels throughout.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Incline Push-Up Reverse Grip
description:
**Flipping your hands so your fingers point toward your feet during an incline push-up creates a surprisingly intense lower chest and bicep activation that feels completely different from any standard push-up.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The reverse grip changes the angle of force through your arms, shifting emphasis toward the lower chest fibers. It also places your biceps in a position where they assist the movement, making this a unique upper-body combination.

**Pro tip:** Warm up your wrists thoroughly before this one — the reverse grip puts them in an unusual position that can feel stiff at first.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve still peaks in the mid-range, but the reversed hand position changes which chest fibers are loaded most. The stretched position at the bottom engages the lower chest more aggressively than a standard grip incline push-up.
instructions: Place your hands on an elevated surface with fingers pointing toward your feet (reverse grip).
Walk your feet back until your body forms a straight line.
Lower your chest toward the surface, keeping your elbows tucked closer to your sides than usual.
Press back up to full extension, focusing on squeezing through your lower chest.
If your wrists feel strained, use a slightly higher surface to reduce the angle.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Incline Push-Up Wide
description:
**Spreading your hands extra wide on an elevated surface turns the incline push-up into a chest-dominant move that minimizes tricep involvement and maximizes the stretch across your pecs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The wide hand placement increases the distance your chest must stretch at the bottom of each rep. This wider arc means your chest does more of the work and your triceps do less, making it a better chest isolation variation.

**Pro tip:** Don't go so wide that your elbows flare to 90 degrees — aim for hands about 1.5x shoulder width to protect your shoulders.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve peaks in the mid-range. The wider grip increases the moment arm on your chest, meaning the same bodyweight creates more chest-specific loading. The stretched position at the bottom is particularly effective for stimulating growth.
instructions: Place your hands on an elevated surface about 1.5 times shoulder-width apart.
Walk your feet back to create a straight body line from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward the surface, allowing your elbows to flare out naturally.
Descend until you feel a strong stretch across your chest, then press back up.
Keep your core braced and avoid letting your lower back arch during the movement.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Incline Push-Ups
description:
**The classic incline push-up is the most scalable chest exercise in existence — adjust the height and you have a progression ladder from rehab to advanced training.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This foundational bodyweight movement teaches proper pressing mechanics while building chest strength at any level. The incline reduces load compared to floor push-ups, making it perfect for high-rep chest work or as a warm-up before heavy pressing.

**Pro tip:** Squeeze your glutes hard throughout the set — this locks your pelvis in place and forces your chest to do all the work instead of compensating with hip movement.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance follows a bell-shaped curve, peaking when your elbows are at 90 degrees. The stretched position at the bottom is well-loaded, and you can increase difficulty simply by choosing a lower surface over time.
instructions: Place both hands on a stable elevated surface at shoulder width.
Step your feet back until your body is in a straight plank position.
Bend your elbows to lower your chest toward the surface in a controlled descent.
Lightly touch or come close to the surface, then press back up explosively.
Breathe in on the way down, out on the way up, and keep your body rigid throughout.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Incline Smith Machine Press
description:
**The Smith machine's fixed bar path lets you focus purely on pushing maximum weight on the incline without worrying about balance — it's a controlled overload machine for the upper chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The guided bar path removes the stabilization demand, letting you safely push heavier loads or train closer to failure without a spotter. The flat resistance profile means the weight feels consistent from bottom to top.

**Pro tip:** Position the bench so the bar travels to your upper chest, not your chin — most people set up too far forward and turn this into a shoulder press.

**Biomechanics:** The Smith machine creates a flat resistance curve because the fixed path eliminates the stabilization cost. This means the load feels even throughout the range, unlike free-weight presses where the lockout is easier. You can safely push to failure without worrying about getting pinned.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to 30-45 degrees inside a Smith machine.
Position the bench so the bar path aligns with your upper chest when lying back.
Unrack the bar by rotating the hooks, and lower it slowly to your upper chest.
Press the bar up to full extension, focusing on driving through your chest.
Re-rack by rotating the hooks at the top — use the safety stops as a backup.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively

name: Isometric Chest Squeezes
description:
**Pressing your palms together as hard as possible with zero movement creates a surprisingly brutal chest contraction you can do literally anywhere — no equipment, no excuses.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Isometric squeezes activate your chest fibers without any joint movement, making them perfect for warming up, rehabbing injuries, or adding extra chest volume without additional fatigue. The mind-muscle connection this builds transfers directly to your pressing exercises.

**Pro tip:** Hold each squeeze for 10-15 seconds and focus on ramping up the force gradually rather than slamming to maximum instantly — this protects your joints and builds a stronger contraction.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance is entirely self-generated and stays flat throughout the hold since there's no movement. Loading occurs in the shortened position where your chest is fully contracted, which complements stretch-focused exercises in your program.
instructions: Stand or sit upright and press your palms together in front of your chest at sternum height.
Push your hands into each other as hard as possible, engaging your chest fully.
Hold the maximum contraction for 10-15 seconds while breathing steadily.
Release slowly, rest for a few seconds, then repeat.
Perform 4-6 holds per set, varying the hand height (low, mid, high) to hit different chest angles.
tips: Press palms together as hard as possible
Hold each contraction for 5-10 seconds
Breathe normally — do not hold your breath
Keep shoulders down and relaxed

name: Isometric Wipers
description:
**Holding a push-up position while sweeping your hands in and out like windshield wipers creates a chest-burning isometric challenge that builds endurance and stability simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This movement combines an isometric hold with lateral hand movement, forcing your chest to stabilize your body while also working through a partial range of motion. It builds the kind of chest endurance and control that translates to better performance in all pressing movements.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips completely still — if they're rocking side to side, your hands are moving too far and your core is compensating.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance is flat and self-generated through the isometric hold, with the chest working hardest in the shortened position as you maintain the plank. The lateral wiping motion adds a small dynamic component that increases inner and outer chest activation beyond a standard hold.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with arms extended and hands shoulder-width apart.
While holding your body rigid, slide one hand outward and the other inward along the floor.
Reverse the motion, sweeping hands back through center and to the opposite positions.
Continue alternating in a controlled windshield-wiper pattern.
Keep your core locked and hips level — the movement comes only from your arms.
tips: Press palms together as hard as possible
Hold each contraction for 5-10 seconds
Breathe normally — do not hold your breath
Keep shoulders down and relaxed

name: Landmine Chest Press
description:
**Pressing the end of a barbell anchored in a corner creates a unique arcing path that's gentler on your shoulders than any flat press while still hammering your chest with serious weight.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The landmine's natural arc pushes the weight both forward and upward, closely matching how your chest fibers actually pull. This makes it one of the most shoulder-friendly pressing variations available, especially for lifters with impingement issues.

**Pro tip:** Stand with a slight forward lean and stagger your feet — this turns the landmine press into a standing chest exercise that also challenges your core stability.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve means the press is hardest in the mid-range. The bottom stretched position is well-loaded as the barbell end is far from your body. As you press to lockout, the arc reduces the effective load slightly, making this forgiving at the top.
instructions: Wedge one end of a barbell into a corner or landmine attachment on the floor.
Stand facing the free end and grip it with both hands at chest height.
Lean slightly forward with feet staggered for stability.
Press the barbell end forward and upward until your arms are extended.
Lower it back to your chest under control, keeping your core braced throughout.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Leg-Over Floor Press
description:
**By crossing one leg over the other while pressing a kettlebell from the floor, you lock your hips in place and force your chest to do all the heavy lifting without any back arch cheating.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The leg-over position pins your lower back flat, which removes momentum and makes your chest work through a honest range of motion. The kettlebell's offset center of mass also demands extra stabilization from your pressing muscles.

**Pro tip:** Cross the leg opposite to your pressing arm — this creates the strongest anti-rotation brace and keeps your torso locked down.

**Biomechanics:** The floor limits how far your elbow drops, so the hardest part of the press is in the middle of the movement where your chest is most active. The kettlebell hanging below your wrist adds a constant pull that keeps tension on your chest even at the top.
instructions: Lie on the floor with a kettlebell in one hand at chest level, arm bent.
Cross the opposite leg over your pressing-side knee to pin your hips flat.
Press the kettlebell straight up until your arm is fully extended, keeping your wrist stacked over your shoulder.
Lower slowly until your upper arm touches the floor, pause briefly.
Repeat for all reps, then switch sides.
tips: Let your upper arms touch the floor briefly at the bottom — this is your range limiter
Keep your legs flat or bent depending on your low back comfort
Press straight up, not back toward your face
Retract your scapulae even without a bench for support
coaching_cues: Arms touch the floor, then press

name: Leverage Chest Press
description:
**The leverage chest press machine lets you chase true chest failure without worrying about a barbell pinning you to the bench — pure pushing power, zero risk.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Because the machine handles all the balancing, every ounce of effort goes directly into pushing. This makes it ideal for building chest size, especially when you push sets to the limit where free weights would be dangerous.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat height so the handles line up with your mid-chest — too high shifts work to your shoulders, too low strains them.

**Biomechanics:** The lever arm provides a fairly even resistance throughout the push, so you won't hit a dramatic sticking point. Loading stays consistent from start to finish, which means your chest is working hard across the entire rep.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles align with the middle of your chest.
Sit back firmly with your shoulder blades pressed into the pad.
Grip the handles and press forward until your arms are nearly straight — don't lock your elbows.
Slowly return the handles until you feel a stretch across your chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively

name: Leverage Decline Chest Press
description:
**Pressing on a downward angle targets the thick lower shelf of your chest that gives your pecs that full, finished look from every angle.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest (lower). Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The decline angle shifts the workload to the lower chest fibers that are often underdeveloped. Using a machine means you can load heavy and push hard without needing a spotter, making it a reliable mass builder for the lower pecs.

**Pro tip:** Focus on driving through the heel of your palm rather than gripping hard — this keeps the tension in your chest instead of your forearms.

**Biomechanics:** The downward pressing path keeps resistance steady throughout the movement with no weak spots. Because the angle reduces shoulder involvement, your chest handles a larger share of the load from start to finish.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are level with your lower chest.
Sit back and plant your feet flat on the floor or foot platform.
Grip the handles and press forward and slightly downward until your arms are extended.
Control the weight back until your chest gets a full stretch.
Repeat without letting the weight stack touch down between reps.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively
coaching_cues: Chest up even on the decline

name: Leverage Incline Chest Press
description:
**Angled upward on a machine track, this press hammers the upper chest fibers that create that powerful collarbone-to-shoulder shelf.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest (upper). Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The incline angle recruits the upper portion of the chest that flat pressing often misses. The machine's fixed path lets you focus entirely on squeezing your upper pecs without worrying about balancing the weight.

**Pro tip:** Lean your head back into the pad and think about pushing your hands together as you press — this mental cue fires the upper chest harder.

**Biomechanics:** The lever system delivers consistent resistance through the entire pressing arc, so there's no easy point where you can coast. The upward angle means your upper chest fibers are the primary movers throughout, with steady loading from bottom to top.
instructions: Set the seat so the handles are at upper-chest or collarbone height.
Sit with your back flat against the pad and feet firmly planted.
Grip the handles and press upward and forward until your arms are nearly locked out.
Lower under control until you feel a deep stretch across your upper chest.
Repeat for all reps, keeping your back against the pad throughout.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively

name: Low Cable Crossover
description:
**Sweeping cables upward from below your waist creates a unique line of pull that lights up your upper and inner chest like no dumbbell fly can match.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest (upper/inner). Secondary: Shoulders.

The low-to-high arc targets chest fibers that run toward your collarbone, filling in the upper chest. Unlike dumbbells, the cable keeps constant tension on your muscles — there's no resting point at the top or bottom.

**Pro tip:** Bring your hands together at chin height and squeeze for a one-second hold — this peak contraction is where the inner chest gets its best stimulus.

**Biomechanics:** The cable provides even resistance throughout the sweeping arc, so your chest never gets a break during the rep. Because the pull comes from below, the resistance perfectly matches the upward fiber direction of the upper chest.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
Stand centered between the cables, one foot slightly forward for balance.
With a slight bend in your elbows, sweep both handles upward and inward in a wide arc.
Bring your hands together at about chin height, squeezing your chest hard.
Slowly reverse the arc back to the starting position with arms wide.
Repeat without swinging or using momentum.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and locked in position throughout
Step forward with one foot for balance and to pre-stretch the chest
Squeeze the cables together at the midline for a full contraction
Control the eccentric — do not let the cables snap your arms back
Drive upward and inward — finish at eye level

name: Machine Bench Press
description:
**The machine bench press strips away every distraction — no balancing, no spotter needed — so you can pour everything into crushing your chest with heavy weight.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This is the simplest way to overload your chest safely. The fixed path means your stabilizer muscles don't limit how much weight you can push, so your chest gets maximal tension every rep.

**Pro tip:** Pause for a full second at the bottom of each rep — this eliminates the bounce and forces your chest to restart the press from a dead stop.

**Biomechanics:** The machine delivers even resistance from bottom to top with no sticking point. Because the path is fixed, loading stays consistent through the full range, making every inch of the press productive for chest growth.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest height.
Sit back with your shoulder blades squeezed together against the pad.
Grip the handles at chest width and press forward until your arms are nearly straight.
Return slowly until the weight stack almost touches, feeling the stretch.
Repeat for all reps without fully resting the weight between reps.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively

name: Machine Chest Press
description:
**Sitting upright and pressing forward on a chest press machine mimics the motion of pushing open a heavy door — simple, effective, and brutally direct for your pecs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The seated position and guided handles let beginners learn the pressing pattern safely and let advanced lifters train to absolute failure. It's one of the most time-efficient chest builders in any gym.

**Pro tip:** Experiment with grip width if the machine allows it — a wider grip emphasizes the outer chest, while a narrower grip shifts more work to the triceps.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance is smooth and consistent throughout the pressing motion with no dead zones. The mid-range loading means your chest works hardest in the middle of the rep where it can produce the most force.
instructions: Set the seat height so the handles are level with your chest.
Press your back into the pad and grip the handles firmly.
Push the handles forward until your arms are extended but not locked.
Slowly bring the handles back until you feel a comfortable stretch in your chest.
Repeat with controlled speed on both the push and return.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively

name: Machine Incline Press
description:
**Pressing upward on a fixed incline track isolates the stubborn upper chest fibers that separate a good chest from a great one.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest (upper). Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The machine removes the need to stabilize the weight, so you can focus entirely on feeling your upper chest contract. This makes it perfect for high-rep finishing sets where fatigue would make free weights sloppy.

**Pro tip:** Don't grip the handles too tightly — a relaxed grip keeps the tension in your chest instead of shifting it to your arms.

**Biomechanics:** The incline lever arm provides flat, even resistance through the entire press. There's no point in the rep where gravity lets you rest, so your upper chest fibers stay loaded from the first inch to the last.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles align with your upper chest near your collarbones.
Sit back firmly and plant your feet on the floor.
Press the handles upward and forward until your arms are almost fully extended.
Lower the handles with control until the weight stack nearly touches.
Repeat, keeping your upper back pressed into the pad throughout.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively

name: Medicine Ball Chest Pass
description:
**Explosively launching a medicine ball from your chest trains your pecs to produce power and speed — the kind of raw force that no slow bench press can build.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

This exercise teaches your chest muscles to fire fast and hard, which translates to athletic performance and real-world pushing strength. It's also a great warm-up to activate your chest before heavier pressing work.

**Pro tip:** Release the ball at chest height, not above your head — throwing upward turns it into a shoulder exercise and wastes the chest training effect.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance is highest at the start when the ball is against your chest and decreases as you release it. This bell-shaped loading pattern means the stretch position gets the most work, training your chest through its strongest range.
instructions: Stand facing a wall or partner, holding a medicine ball at chest height with both hands.
Bend your elbows and bring the ball to your chest.
Explosively push the ball forward by extending your arms as fast as possible.
Catch the rebound or receive the return pass and immediately load up for the next rep.
Repeat for the desired reps, focusing on speed and power each throw.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: Neck Press
description:
**Lowering a barbell to your neck instead of your nipple line dramatically increases the stretch on your upper chest — a old-school bodybuilding secret for pec thickness.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest (upper). Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The higher bar path puts your chest fibers in a deeper stretch at the bottom, which is a powerful trigger for muscle growth. This exercise requires lighter weight and strict control, but the upper chest activation is hard to beat.

**Pro tip:** Use a weight that's about 60% of your normal bench press and keep your elbows flared wide — this is a feel exercise, not a strength exercise.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve means the hardest point is in the middle of the press. The stretched starting position at your neck loads the chest fibers at their longest length, which research shows is especially effective for growth.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and unrack a barbell with a wider-than-shoulder grip.
Lower the bar slowly toward your upper chest, just below your neck — not your throat.
Keep your elbows flared out to the sides at roughly 90 degrees from your body.
Press the bar back up to full lockout in a slight arc toward your face.
Repeat with light to moderate weight, prioritizing control over load.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: One Arm Dumbbell Bench Press
description:
**Pressing a dumbbell with one arm while the other side stays empty forces your core to fight rotation, turning a chest exercise into a full-body stability challenge.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The unilateral load means your core has to brace hard to keep you from rolling off the bench. This fixes left-right strength imbalances while giving each side of your chest focused, undivided attention.

**Pro tip:** Grip the edge of the bench with your free hand for the first few sets until you learn the balance — then progress to holding nothing for maximum core demand.

**Biomechanics:** The dumbbell creates a bell-shaped resistance curve where the middle of the press is hardest. The stretched position at the bottom loads your chest at its longest length, which is ideal for stimulating growth in each pec independently.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding one dumbbell at chest height, other hand free or gripping the bench.
Brace your core hard to prevent your body from tilting toward the loaded side.
Press the dumbbell straight up until your arm is fully extended.
Lower slowly until the dumbbell is beside your chest and you feel a deep stretch.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other arm.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press

name: One-Arm Flat Bench Dumbbell Flye
description:
**Performing a fly with just one dumbbell lets you reach across your body further than both arms ever could, unlocking a chest squeeze that bilateral flyes simply can't deliver.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

The single-arm setup allows a greater range of motion at the top, where your working arm can cross past the midline of your body. This extra range hits the inner chest fibers that are hard to reach with standard two-arm flyes.

**Pro tip:** At the top, actively reach the dumbbell an inch past your centerline — that extra inch is where the inner chest contraction peaks.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance is highest when your arm is out wide at the bottom, creating a deep stretch on the chest. The load decreases as you bring the dumbbell up, so the stretched position — where growth stimulus is greatest — gets the most tension.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding one dumbbell above your chest with a slight elbow bend.
Slowly lower the dumbbell out to the side in a wide arc until you feel a deep chest stretch.
Reverse the arc, sweeping the dumbbell back up and slightly past your body's centerline.
Squeeze your chest hard at the top for a one-second hold.
Complete all reps on one side, then switch arms.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout — do not turn this into a press
Lower the dumbbells until you feel a deep stretch across the chest
Control the eccentric phase for at least 2 seconds to maximize stretch stimulus
Avoid going so deep that you feel shoulder joint pain

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Floor Press
description:
**Pressing a kettlebell from the floor with one arm combines the joint-friendly range limit of a floor press with the offset load of a kettlebell for a shoulder-safe chest builder.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The floor stops your elbow before your shoulder reaches a vulnerable position, making this ideal for lifters with cranky shoulders. The kettlebell's weight hanging below your wrist also forces your grip and stabilizers to work overtime.

**Pro tip:** Rotate the kettlebell slightly outward at the top so the bell rests on the outside of your forearm — this keeps your wrist neutral and your pressing groove smooth.

**Biomechanics:** The floor cuts off the bottom of the range, so the hardest part of the press is in the mid-range where your chest and triceps are strongest. The kettlebell's offset mass adds a constant rotational pull that keeps your chest engaged even at lockout.
instructions: Lie on the floor with knees bent, holding a kettlebell in one hand at shoulder level.
Keep your elbow at roughly 45 degrees from your body.
Press the kettlebell straight up until your arm is fully extended.
Lower until your upper arm rests on the floor, pause for a beat.
Repeat for all reps, then switch to the other arm.
tips: Let your upper arms touch the floor briefly at the bottom — this is your range limiter
Keep your legs flat or bent depending on your low back comfort
Press straight up, not back toward your face
Retract your scapulae even without a bench for support
coaching_cues: Arms touch the floor, then press

name: Pec Deck Fly
description:
**The pec deck is the closest thing to a chest isolation machine that exists — it removes your triceps from the equation entirely and makes your pecs do every bit of the work.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Because your elbows are braced against pads, you can't cheat with arm strength. This pure isolation makes the pec deck one of the best exercises for feeling and fatiguing your chest, especially at the end of a workout.

**Pro tip:** Don't let the pads come all the way back between reps — stop when you feel a stretch but before your shoulders roll forward, which protects the joint.

**Biomechanics:** The machine provides flat, even resistance through the entire squeezing arc. There's no dead spot at the top or bottom, so your chest stays under constant tension for the full rep — ideal for accumulating volume safely.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your upper arms are parallel to the floor when gripping the pads.
Sit with your back flat against the pad and place your forearms against the arm pads.
Squeeze the pads together in front of your chest by contracting your pecs.
Hold the squeezed position for a brief moment.
Slowly return to the start until you feel a comfortable stretch, then repeat.
tips: Adjust the seat so your upper arms are parallel to the floor at the start
Do not let the weight stack touch down between reps — maintain tension
Focus on the squeeze at the midline rather than the stretch
Keep your back flat against the pad throughout

name: Plate Press
description:
**Squeezing a weight plate between your palms and pressing it forward creates constant inward tension that fries your inner chest fibers like nothing else.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest (inner). Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The key here is the squeeze — you have to actively press your hands together the entire time, which keeps your inner chest under non-stop tension. It's a simple exercise that requires zero equipment beyond a single plate.

**Pro tip:** Use a 25-pound plate to start and focus on squeezing as hard as possible — the inward force matters more than the weight of the plate.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance curve is bell-shaped, with the hardest point in the middle of the press. Because the load is in a shortened chest position, this exercise is best for training the squeeze and contraction rather than the stretch.
instructions: Stand or sit upright holding a weight plate between both palms at chest height.
Squeeze your hands together as hard as you can — this is the key to the exercise.
Press the plate forward until your arms are fully extended, maintaining the squeeze.
Slowly bring the plate back to your chest without relaxing the inward pressure.
Repeat for all reps, keeping maximum squeeze pressure throughout.
tips: Squeeze the weight as hard as possible throughout the entire set
Press forward at chest height, not upward
Focus on the isometric adduction — this is what activates the inner chest
Keep shoulders down and back, do not round forward

name: Plyo Kettlebell Pushups
description:
**Exploding off the floor and landing on kettlebell handles turns a basic push-up into a chest power exercise that builds explosive strength and iron wrists.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The explosive push develops fast-twitch muscle fibers in your chest, while landing on the kettlebells demands wrist stability and grip strength. This combination builds functional pushing power that carries over to sports and daily life.

**Pro tip:** Start with the kettlebells on a non-slip surface and use flat-bottomed kettlebells — round-bottomed bells will roll and wreck your wrists.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve means the hardest work happens in the middle of the push. The stretched position at the bottom loads your chest deeply, and the explosive intent recruits more muscle fibers than a slow push-up ever could.
instructions: Place two kettlebells on the floor slightly wider than shoulder width, handles parallel.
Start in a push-up position with hands on the floor between the kettlebells.
Lower into a push-up, then explode upward with enough force to leave the ground.
Land with your hands on the kettlebell handles, absorbing the impact with bent elbows.
Lower into a push-up on the kettlebells, then explode off and land back on the floor.
Repeat, alternating between floor and kettlebell positions.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension
Land with soft elbows to absorb impact and protect your joints

name: Plyo Push-up
description:
**Launching your entire upper body off the ground with each rep teaches your chest to produce maximum force in minimum time — the definition of explosive power.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Plyo push-ups recruit fast-twitch muscle fibers that regular push-ups barely touch. This makes them excellent for athletes who need pushing speed, and they also serve as a high-intensity finisher for chest workouts.

**Pro tip:** Focus on the push, not the clap — getting your hands as high off the ground as possible matters more than any mid-air tricks.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance peaks in the middle of the push where your chest generates the most force. The stretched position at the bottom provides a powerful loading stimulus, and the explosive intent ensures you're recruiting the maximum number of muscle fibers each rep.
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart.
Lower your chest to the floor with control.
Explode upward as hard as possible so your hands leave the ground.
Land softly with slightly bent elbows to absorb the impact.
Immediately descend into the next rep without pausing.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension
Land with soft elbows to absorb impact and protect your joints

name: Push Up to Side Plank
description:
**Adding a side plank rotation after each push-up transforms a chest exercise into a full-torso sculptor that hits your pecs, obliques, and shoulders in one fluid movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Abs, shoulders, triceps.

The push-up builds your chest while the side plank rotation fires your obliques and challenges your shoulder stability. This combination makes it one of the most efficient bodyweight exercises for building a strong, functional upper body.

**Pro tip:** Rotate until your hips are fully stacked and your top arm points straight at the ceiling — half rotations cheat your core out of the best part of the exercise.

**Biomechanics:** The push-up portion follows a bell-shaped resistance curve with peak difficulty in the middle. The side plank adds an anti-rotation demand that loads your core isometrically, making this exercise work two movement patterns in one rep.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands shoulder-width apart and body in a straight line.
Perform a full push-up, lowering your chest to the floor and pressing back up.
At the top, rotate your entire body to one side, stacking your feet and raising your top arm to the ceiling.
Hold the side plank for a brief moment, then rotate back to the push-up position.
Perform another push-up and rotate to the opposite side.
Continue alternating sides with each rep.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Push-Up Wide
description:
**Spreading your hands wider than normal shifts the workload outward to the outer edges of your chest, building that wide, shelf-like pec shape.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest (outer). Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The wider hand position increases the stretch on your chest at the bottom and reduces how much your triceps can help. This means your pecs handle a bigger share of your bodyweight through a larger range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Point your fingers slightly outward at about 45 degrees — this aligns your wrists naturally and prevents the inner elbow strain that straight-ahead fingers can cause.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve peaks in the middle of the movement. The wide hand position increases the moment arm on your chest, meaning your pecs work harder per rep compared to a standard-width push-up, especially in the stretched bottom position.
instructions: Place your hands on the floor about 1.5 times shoulder width apart, fingers angled slightly out.
Set up in a plank position with your body forming a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows out to the sides.
Descend until your chest is just above the floor or as low as comfortable.
Press back up to the starting position by squeezing your chest.
Repeat with a controlled tempo.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Push-Ups
description:
**The push-up is the most battle-tested chest exercise in human history — no equipment, no excuses, just your body against gravity building real pressing strength.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Push-ups train your chest, shoulders, and triceps while also demanding core stability to maintain a rigid body position. They can be scaled from beginner to advanced by adjusting tempo, range of motion, or adding pauses.

**Pro tip:** Screw your hands into the floor by externally rotating your shoulders — this creates a stable shelf for your shoulder blades and protects the joint.

**Biomechanics:** Resistance is bell-shaped, peaking in the middle of the movement where your chest is most active. The bottom position provides a deep stretch under load, which is a strong stimulus for chest muscle growth even with just bodyweight.
instructions: Place your hands on the floor slightly wider than shoulder width.
Extend your legs behind you so your body forms a straight line from head to heels.
