Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows to about 90 degrees.
Press back up until your arms are fully extended.
Repeat, keeping your core tight and body straight throughout.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Push-Ups With Feet Elevated
description:
**Elevating your feet onto a bench shifts more of your bodyweight onto your hands and angles the press upward, turning a standard push-up into a serious upper chest builder.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest (upper). Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The decline angle created by raised feet mimics an incline press, targeting the upper chest fibers. The higher your feet, the more weight your arms support and the more your upper pecs and shoulders have to work.

**Pro tip:** Start with a low surface like a step and gradually increase height — jumping straight to a tall bench can overload your shoulders before your chest is ready.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve still peaks mid-rep, but the elevated angle increases the percentage of bodyweight your upper body must handle. The stretched bottom position loads the upper chest fibers at their longest length, amplifying the growth stimulus.
instructions: Place your feet on a bench, box, or sturdy elevated surface behind you.
Set your hands on the floor slightly wider than shoulder width.
Maintain a straight line from your heels to your head — don't let your hips sag.
Lower your chest toward the floor until your upper arms are parallel to the ground.
Press back up to full arm extension and repeat.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Push-Ups With Feet On An Exercise Ball
description:
**Placing your feet on an unstable exercise ball turns every push-up into a balance challenge that forces your core and chest to work twice as hard just to stay steady.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

The unstable surface demands constant micro-adjustments from your entire body, dramatically increasing core activation compared to regular push-ups. Your chest still does the pressing work, but now your stabilizer muscles are firing on all cylinders.

**Pro tip:** Place your shins — not your toes — on the ball for your first attempts. This gives a wider base of support while you build the balance needed for toes-on-ball progression.

**Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve is the same as a standard push-up, but the instability means your muscles must produce force while also correcting for wobble. This recruits more total muscle fibers per rep, making each push-up more demanding than it looks.
instructions: Place your shins or feet on top of an exercise ball and walk your hands out to a push-up position.
Stabilize your body — squeeze your glutes and brace your core to minimize wobble.
Lower your chest toward the floor with controlled speed.
Press back up to full extension while keeping the ball as still as possible.
Repeat, resetting your balance at the top of each rep if needed.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Pushups
description:
**The classic pushup remains the gold standard of bodyweight chest training — a movement so effective that militaries worldwide have used it as a fitness benchmark for over a century.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Pushups build pressing strength, shoulder stability, and core endurance simultaneously. They require zero equipment and can be performed anywhere, making them the most accessible and versatile chest exercise available.

**Pro tip:** Keep your elbows at a 45-degree angle from your body — flaring them to 90 degrees stresses your shoulders, while tucking them tight shifts work to your triceps.

**Biomechanics:** The resistance curve is bell-shaped, with the greatest challenge in the middle of the rep. Your chest is loaded most in the stretched bottom position where your pecs are at their longest, providing a strong growth stimulus from bodyweight alone.
instructions: Start face-down with your hands just outside shoulder width on the floor.
Push up to a straight-arm plank position with your body rigid from head to toe.
Bend your elbows at 45 degrees to lower your chest to the floor.
Touch your chest lightly to the ground, then press back up powerfully.
Repeat without letting your hips drop or pike up.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Pushups (Close and Wide Hand Positions)
description:
**Alternating between close and wide hand positions within the same set hits your chest from two different angles, covering both inner and outer pec fibers in one brutal sequence.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders, triceps.

Close hands emphasize the inner chest and triceps, while wide hands target the outer chest with a bigger stretch. Combining both in one exercise gives your pecs a more complete stimulus than either position alone.

**Pro tip:** Do all your close-grip reps first while you're fresh — they're harder on the triceps, and fatigued triceps will limit your chest work on the wide reps.

**Biomechanics:** Both positions follow a bell-shaped resistance curve, but the loading shifts. Close grip increases the range of motion and triceps demand, while wide grip increases the chest stretch at the bottom. Together, they cover the full spectrum of chest fiber recruitment.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands close together, thumbs nearly touching.
Perform a set of push-ups in this close position, lowering your chest between your hands.
Without resting, walk your hands out to about 1.5 times shoulder width.
Perform a set of push-ups in the wide position, lowering your chest to the floor.
Continue alternating between close and wide positions for the desired total reps.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension

name: Resistance Band Chest Fly
description:
**A resistance band fly gets progressively harder as you squeeze your hands together, delivering peak tension exactly where your chest is strongest — at full contraction.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: Chest. Secondary: Shoulders.

Unlike dumbbell flyes where tension drops at the top, the band pulls harder the more you stretch it. This means your chest gets maximum resistance at the squeezed position, which is excellent for building that inner chest definition.

**Pro tip:** Anchor the band at chest height behind you — too high turns it into a downward press, too low makes it an upward sweep, and neither targets your mid-chest optimally.

**Biomechanics:** The ascending resistance curve means the band is lightest when your arms are wide and heaviest when your hands are together. This is the opposite of a dumbbell fly, making bands an excellent complement to free-weight chest work by training the range that dumbbells miss.
instructions: Anchor a resistance band at chest height behind you — around a pole, door anchor, or rack.
Grab one end in each hand and step forward until there's light tension with arms wide.
With a slight bend in your elbows, sweep both hands forward and together in a hugging arc.
Squeeze your chest hard when your hands meet in front of you.
Slowly reverse the arc back to the wide starting position, controlling the band's pull.
Repeat without letting the band snap your arms back.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent throughout
Anchor the band at chest height behind you
Squeeze at the midline for peak contraction
Control the return to avoid band snap-back

name: Resistance Band Push-Up
description: **Turn a classic bodyweight move into a chest-scorching challenge with one simple loop of rubber.** **Muscles worked:** chest, triceps, shoulders. The band adds increasing resistance as you push up, making the top of each rep brutally hard where normal push-ups feel easiest. This forces your chest to work overtime through the entire range of motion. **Pro tip:** Double-loop the band around your hands if a single wrap feels too easy — small changes in band tension make a big difference. **Biomechanics:** The ascending resistance from the band matches your increasing leverage advantage at the top, eliminating the "easy zone" and keeping tension on the chest throughout.
instructions: Loop a resistance band across your upper back and secure each end under your palms on the floor.
Set up in a push-up position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, body in a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward the floor slowly, keeping elbows at roughly 45 degrees from your body.
Press back up powerfully against the band's pull, squeezing your chest hard at the top.
Keep your core tight and hips level throughout — don't let your lower back sag.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension
Loop the band across your upper back and anchor under your palms

name: Seated Cable Fly
description: **Sit down, spread wide, and let the cables do what dumbbells can't — punish your chest from start to finish.** **Muscles worked:** chest, shoulders. Because you're seated, your body stays locked in place so every ounce of effort goes straight into your chest. The cables keep constant pull on the muscle even at the deepest stretch, where real growth happens. **Pro tip:** Let your arms open wide enough to feel a deep stretch before squeezing back — don't cut the range short. **Biomechanics:** The cable's line of pull maintains steady resistance across the entire arc, unlike dumbbells which lose tension at the top.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to shoulder height and sit on a bench positioned between them.
Grab a handle in each hand and sit upright with a slight forward lean, arms extended out to the sides with a soft bend in the elbows.
Slowly open your arms wide until you feel a strong stretch across your chest.
Squeeze your arms together in a hugging motion, bringing the handles in front of your chest.
Pause briefly with the handles close together, flexing your chest, then control the return to the stretched position.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the movement
Squeeze the chest at the midline — imagine hugging a tree
Control the return to the stretched position rather than letting cables pull you back
Adjust pulley height to target different pec fiber angles
coaching_cues: Elbows locked, chest does the work

name: Single Arm Dumbbell Bench Press
description: **One arm. One dumbbell. Twice the core demand and zero places for your weak side to hide.** **Muscles worked:** chest, triceps, shoulders, abs. Pressing with one arm forces your core to fight rotation while your chest does the heavy lifting. This exposes and fixes strength imbalances between sides that barbell pressing can mask. **Pro tip:** Brace your free hand on your stomach or the bench edge to help stabilize until you build enough core strength to go hands-free. **Biomechanics:** The unilateral load creates a strong anti-rotation demand on the torso, recruiting deep core muscles while the working-side chest handles the full pressing load through a deep stretch at the bottom.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding one dumbbell at chest level, arm bent at 90 degrees.
Plant both feet firmly on the floor and squeeze your glutes to anchor your hips.
Press the dumbbell straight up over your chest, fully extending your arm without locking the elbow.
Lower the dumbbell slowly until your upper arm dips just below bench level for a full stretch.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other arm.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Allow the dumbbells to travel slightly deeper than a barbell would permit
Keep wrists neutral and stacked over elbows throughout the press

name: Single-Arm Cable Crossover
description: **Isolate one side of your chest with laser focus and feel every fiber squeeze shut like a vice.** **Muscles worked:** chest, shoulders. Working one arm at a time lets you concentrate entirely on the mind-muscle connection and correct any left-right imbalances. The cable provides smooth, even resistance throughout the sweeping motion. **Pro tip:** Angle your body slightly away from the cable machine to increase the stretch at the start of each rep. **Biomechanics:** The single-arm setup allows a greater range of motion across the midline of the body, increasing chest fiber recruitment compared to the bilateral version.
instructions: Set a single cable pulley to about head height and stand sideways to the machine.
Grab the handle with the arm closest to the machine, stepping forward to create tension on the cable.
With a slight bend in your elbow, sweep your arm down and across your body toward the opposite hip.
Squeeze your chest hard at the end of the motion, then slowly return to the starting position.
Finish all reps on one side, then switch to the other arm.
tips: Keep elbows slightly bent and locked in position throughout
Step forward with one foot for balance and to pre-stretch the chest
Squeeze the cables together at the midline for a full contraction
Control the eccentric — do not let the cables snap your arms back

name: Single-Arm Push-Up
description: **The ultimate bodyweight flex — one hand on the ground, total chest and core domination.** **Muscles worked:** chest, shoulders, triceps, abs. This advanced push-up variation demands serious strength and balance. Your chest, shoulder, and core must all fire together to keep you stable while pressing your entire bodyweight with one arm. **Pro tip:** Widen your feet for more stability as you build up strength — the wider the base, the easier the balance. **Biomechanics:** The offset loading dramatically increases demand on the working-side chest and the entire core to prevent rotation, making this a top-tier strength builder.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with feet spread wider than shoulder-width for balance.
Place one hand flat on the floor beneath your chest and tuck the other hand behind your lower back.
Lower your chest toward the floor in a controlled motion, keeping your hips as level as possible.
Press back up explosively, driving through your palm until your arm is fully extended.
Keep your core braced tight throughout to prevent your body from twisting.
tips: Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips
Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin
Protract your scapulae at the top to fully shorten the pec
Engage your core throughout to prevent lumbar extension
Widen your feet for balance and rotate your torso slightly toward the working arm

name: Smith Machine Bench Press
description: **Lock into a fixed path and push heavier weight without worrying about a wobbly bar path.** **Muscles worked:** chest, shoulders, triceps. The guided bar lets you focus purely on pressing power without needing a spotter. This makes it a great option for safely pushing close to failure and building raw chest strength. **Pro tip:** Position the bench so the bar touches your mid-chest at the bottom — too high hits your shoulders, too low strains them. **Biomechanics:** The fixed vertical path removes the need for horizontal stabilization, allowing you to load more weight on the chest but reducing the involvement of smaller stabilizer muscles.
instructions: Position a flat bench centered under the Smith machine bar.
Lie back and grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width, then unhook it by rotating your wrists.
Lower the bar slowly to your mid-chest, keeping your elbows at about 45 degrees.
Press the bar back up until your arms are fully extended but not locked out.
Re-hook the bar at the top after your final rep by twisting it back into the safety catches.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Adjust the seat height so handles align with mid-chest
Focus on squeezing the chest at the top rather than locking out aggressively

name: Smith Machine Decline Press
description: **Target the thick lower shelf of your chest with heavy, guided reps and zero spotter anxiety.** **Muscles worked:** chest, shoulders, triceps. The decline angle shifts emphasis to the lower chest fibers, and the machine's fixed track lets you load up aggressively. This is a go-to for building that defined lower chest line. **Pro tip:** Set the safety stops just above your chest so you can bail safely if you hit failure mid-rep. **Biomechanics:** The downward pressing angle combined with the fixed bar path maximizes lower chest activation while the machine handles balance, letting you focus on pure pressing force.
instructions: Set a decline bench under the Smith machine with the bar aligned over your lower chest.
Secure your legs under the foot pads, lie back, and grip the bar just outside shoulder-width.
Unhook the bar and lower it under control to your lower chest.
Drive the bar back up powerfully, squeezing your chest at the top.

The pullover takes your chest through a massive stretch behind your head that no fly or press can replicate. description: **Hang from straps and turn a basic push-up into an unstable, muscle-shaking chest builder.** **Muscles worked:** chest, shoulders, triceps, abs. Suspension straps force every stabilizer muscle in your upper body to fire just to keep you from wobbling. Your chest works harder through a deeper range of motion than floor push-ups allow. **Pro tip:** The lower you set the handles, the harder the exercise — start at waist height and work your way down as you get stronger. **Biomechanics:** The unstable handles increase the demand on chest stabilizers and core muscles, while allowing a deeper stretch at the bottom than floor-based push-ups.

This simple hand placement change makes a huge difference in chest activation. **Pro tip:** Point your fingers slightly outward to keep your wrists comfortable in the wider position. **Biomechanics:** The wider hand placement increases the moment arm at the shoulder, placing greater demand on the chest while reducing triceps contribution compared to a standard-width push-up.

Lower your chest to the floor, not just your chin

The atlas stone trainer mimics the classic strongman lift using bodyweight technique drills. name: Atlas Stones

There's no machine, no handle, no easy grip — just you and a heavy sphere testing your entire body. **Pro tip:** Use tacky or chalk on your forearms and hands for grip — the stone's smooth surface is the biggest challenge. **Biomechanics:** The lift requires a two-phase pull — a lap phase using hip flexion and arm strength, followed by explosive hip extension to load the stone.

The thicker axle bar makes gripping dramatically harder, which forces your forearms and hands to work overtime while your posterior chain handles the heavy pulling. It's a deadlift that builds iron grip strength as a bonus. **Pro tip:** Use a double-overhand grip as long as possible to maximize grip development — switch to mixed grip only when you truly can't hold on. **Biomechanics:** The larger bar diameter reduces the mechanical advantage of the fingers, increasing forearm activation significantly while the pulling mechanics remain similar to a conventional deadlift.

name: Band Assisted Pull-Up
description: **Get the full pull-up experience with a boost from a band that makes every rep achievable.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. The band provides the most help at the bottom where you're weakest and the least help at the top where you're strongest. This makes it the perfect tool for building toward unassisted pull-ups while still training the full movement. **Pro tip:** Use progressively thinner bands over time — the goal is to need less and less assistance until you're flying solo. **Biomechanics:** The band's elastic assistance is greatest at full stretch (bottom position), which compensates for the weakest point in the pull-up strength curve and allows full range-of-motion training.

description: **A tiny movement with a band that bulletproofs your shoulders and fixes your posture in minutes a day.** **Muscles worked:** rear shoulders, traps. This simple exercise strengthens the muscles between your shoulder blades that get weak from sitting and slouching. A few sets daily can dramatically improve shoulder health and posture. **Pro tip:** Hold the end position for a one-second squeeze on every rep to maximize the benefit. **Biomechanics:** The horizontal pulling motion targets the rear shoulders and mid-traps in their shortened position, reinforcing the muscles responsible for pulling the shoulders back and maintaining upright posture.

The deadlift is the most fundamental strength exercise in existence. It trains your entire backside, builds unbreakable grip strength, and teaches your body to produce maximum force safely. **Pro tip:** Think about pushing the floor away with your feet rather than pulling the bar up — this cue keeps your form tight. **Biomechanics:** The hip-hinge pattern loads the entire posterior chain, with glutes and hamstrings as prime movers and back muscles stabilizing spinal position under load.

Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift

Keeping your palms facing each other throughout the row emphasizes the lower lats and reduces wrist strain. instructions: Grab a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms-in) grip and stand with feet hip-width apart.

Squeeze your shoulder blades at the top for a brief hold.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the start and repeat.

This grip biases the upper lat fibers — pull to the lower ribs
Maintain the hip hinge throughout
Slightly lighter loads than pronated grip due to arm path

name: Bent-Arm Barbell Pullover
description: **A deep-stretch chest move that doubles as a rib cage expander.** **Muscles worked:** chest, shoulders, triceps, lats. Bending your arms more than a straight-arm pullover lets you use heavier weight and puts extra emphasis on the chest. The deep overhead stretch is unmatched for opening up tight shoulders too. **Pro tip:** Keep your hips slightly lower than the bench to increase the stretch — but don't overdo it or your lower back will complain. **Biomechanics:** The bent-arm position shortens the lever arm, allowing heavier loads while still achieving a deep stretch at the bottom.

Pull the barbell back over your chest by squeezing your chest and lats.
Keep your elbows at a consistent bend throughout the entire movement.

Do not let the weight pull your lower back into excessive extension
Focus on pulling with the chest, not the lats — think about squeezing your pecs
Use a controlled tempo, especially in the stretched position

name: Block Pull
description: **A shortened deadlift that lets you overload the lockout like nothing else.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, erectors, lats, forearms, traps. By pulling from elevated blocks, you reduce the range of motion and can handle heavier weight than a full deadlift. This builds serious strength in the top half of the pull and thickens your entire posterior chain. **Pro tip:** Set the blocks so the bar starts just below your knees — this is the sweet spot for most people. **Biomechanics:** The reduced range of motion shifts the hardest work to the hip extension phase, making glutes and erectors the primary drivers.
instructions: Place the barbell on blocks or stacked plates so it sits between mid-shin and just below your knees.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, hinge down, and grip the bar just outside your legs.
Brace your core, flatten your back, and drive through your feet to stand up with the bar.
Lock out at the top by squeezing your glutes and standing tall.
Lower the bar back to the blocks under control and reset before the next rep.

Squeeze the glutes at the point of peak contraction
Maintain a neutral spine throughout

name: Bodyweight Mid Row
description: **No weights needed — just a bar and your own body to sculpt a stronger back.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, traps, erectors. This inverted row variation is perfect for beginners building pulling strength or advanced lifters chasing high-rep back work. You control the difficulty by adjusting your body angle. **Pro tip:** The more horizontal your body, the harder it gets — start at a steeper angle and progress over time. **Biomechanics:** Pulling your bodyweight in a horizontal line mimics a barbell row pattern, training the same muscles without spinal loading.
instructions: Set a bar in a rack or Smith machine at about waist height.
Grab the bar with an overhand grip, walk your feet forward, and hang underneath with your body in a straight line.
Pull your chest up to the bar by driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower yourself back down with control until your arms are fully straight, then repeat.
tips: Adjust the bar height to control difficulty
Pull your chest to the bar
Keep your body in a straight line
Great for warm-ups or high-rep lat work

name: Cable Incline Pushdown
description: **A lat isolation gem that most people walk right past.** **Muscles worked:** lats. This cable movement targets your lats without any bicep involvement, making it one of the purest lat exercises you can do. The constant cable tension means there's no rest point during the set. **Pro tip:** Use a slight forward lean and think about pushing the handle toward your hips, not pulling it down. **Biomechanics:** By working in the shortened position, this exercise complements stretch-focused lat movements like pullovers for complete development.

Keep arms straight — this isolates shoulder extension
The lats are loaded at mid-range and shortened positions

name: Cable Pullover
description: **Constant tension from start to finish — your chest and lats will have nowhere to hide.** **Muscles worked:** chest, triceps, lats. Unlike dumbbell or barbell pullovers where tension drops at the top, the cable keeps your muscles loaded through every inch. This makes it a superior choice for muscle growth. **Pro tip:** Stand far enough from the machine that you feel a stretch overhead — if there's no stretch, you're too close. **Biomechanics:** The cable's line of pull maintains resistance in the stretched overhead position, maximizing the growth stimulus where it matters most.

Do not let the weight pull your lower back into excessive extension
Focus on pulling with the chest, not the lats — think about squeezing your pecs

name: Cat Stretch
description: **The simplest way to wake up a stiff, cranky spine.** **Muscles worked:** erectors, abs. This gentle movement alternates between rounding and arching your back, releasing tension built up from sitting or sleeping. It's a go-to warm-up that feels good immediately. **Pro tip:** Breathe out as you round up and breathe in as you arch down — syncing your breath makes the stretch deeper and more relaxing.

Round your back up toward the ceiling like an angry cat, tucking your chin to your chest.

Exhale fully during the flexion phase
Inhale during extension

name: Catch and Overhead Throw
description: **Explosive power meets full-body coordination in one dynamic move.** **Muscles worked:** lats, abs, chest, shoulders. This is a power exercise, not for hypertrophy
Use a medicine ball
Focus on explosive power

name: Chair Lower Back Stretch
description: **Office-friendly relief for a lower back that's been sitting all day.** **Muscles worked:** erectors. All you need is a chair to release tightness in your lower back. This seated stretch is gentle enough to do multiple times a day and requires zero warm-up. **Pro tip:** Let gravity do the work — don't force yourself deeper. Round your back to stretch the erectors

name: Chest Supported T-Bar Row
description: **Take your lower back out of the equation and let your lats do all the talking.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. name: Chest-Supported Row
description: **Cheat-proof rowing that forces your back to grow.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. Lying face-down on an incline bench with dumbbells removes all momentum and body English from the equation. Your lats and mid-back have no choice but to do the work. **Pro tip:** Try a slight pause at the bottom of each rep in the stretched position — this eliminates the bounce and makes each rep count more. **Biomechanics:** The incline support neutralizes spinal loading, making this a joint-friendly option you can push hard without worrying about your lower back.

Pull toward the hips for lat bias

name: Child's Pose

It's the perfect way to decompress between hard sets or wind down after a workout. **Pro tip:** Spread your knees wider to get a deeper stretch through your hips and lower back — keep your big toes touching.

Reach arms as far forward as possible
Breathe deeply into the lower back

name: Chin-Up
description: **The bodyweight bicep and back builder that never goes out of style.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. With your palms facing you, the chin-up recruits your biceps more than a pull-up while still hammering your lats. It's one of the best upper body exercises you can do with zero equipment beyond a bar. **Pro tip:** Start each rep from a dead hang with straight arms — partial reps shortchange your results. **Biomechanics:** The supinated grip places the biceps in a mechanically stronger position, which is why most people can do more chin-ups than pull-ups.

The underhand grip allows elbows to travel closer to the body
Lats contribute most from mid-range, not the dead hang
Lean back slightly for better lat leverage

name: Chin-Ups
description: **Pound for pound, one of the most effective exercises ever invented.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. This classic pull trains your entire upper back and arms using nothing but your body weight. Progressive overload is simple — add reps, slow down the tempo, or strap on extra weight. **Pro tip:** If you can't do a full rep yet, use a resistance band looped over the bar and under your feet to assist. **Biomechanics:** The underhand grip allows greater elbow flexion range, increasing bicep involvement compared to overhand variations.

Pull chin over the bar

Shoulder-width grip is optimal

description: **A narrow grip that zeroes in on your lower lats for that coveted V-taper.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. Pulling to the front with a close grip emphasizes the lower portion of your lats, helping create width that shows even in a t-shirt. The cable provides smooth, consistent resistance. **Pro tip:** Lean back just slightly — about 10 degrees — to align the pull with your lat fibers. Sitting bolt upright limits the range. **Biomechanics:** The close grip increases the range of motion at the shoulder, allowing a longer stretch at the top and fuller contraction at the bottom.

Pull to the upper chest
The close grip allows slightly more shoulder extension ROM
Lean back 10-15 degrees

name: Close-Grip Lat Pulldown
description: **Narrow your grip to widen your back — sounds backwards, but it works.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. This pulldown variation uses a close neutral or underhand grip to increase the pulling range and shift more work to the lower lats. It's a reliable mass builder for anyone at any level. **Pro tip:** Don't yank the weight down — use a two-second pull and a three-second return for maximum muscle tension. **Biomechanics:** The narrower grip allows greater shoulder extension, which lengthens the lat through a bigger range of motion per rep.

Pull the bar to the upper chest
Slight lean back improves the pulling angle for lats

name: Commando Pull-Up
description: **A side-to-side pull-up that hits your back from an angle nothing else can.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. Standing perpendicular to the bar and alternating which side of the bar your head passes adds a rotational challenge that standard pull-ups miss. It builds grip strength and core stability alongside your back. **Pro tip:** Alternate which side your head goes to each rep to keep the work balanced between left and right. **Biomechanics:** The offset hand position creates an asymmetric load, forcing each lat to work slightly differently and building functional pulling strength.

On the next rep, pull your head to the opposite side of the bar.

description: **The single most productive exercise you can do with a barbell — period.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, erectors, lats, forearms, traps, quads. Picking a heavy barbell off the floor trains nearly every muscle in your body in one movement. It builds raw strength, thick muscle, and real-world power like nothing else. **Pro tip:** Push the floor away with your feet rather than pulling the bar up — this mental cue keeps your back safer and your legs more engaged. **Biomechanics:** The ascending strength curve means the lift gets easier as you stand up, which is why lockout strength develops quickly with this exercise.

Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift

name: Crossover Reverse Lunge
description: **A lunge with a twist that lights up your inner thighs and challenges your balance.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs. By stepping back and across your body, you add an adductor stretch and rotational stability demand that regular lunges can't match. It's a sneaky-hard leg exercise that also improves hip mobility. **Pro tip:** Start with bodyweight only until the crossover pattern feels natural — adding load too soon turns this into a balance disaster. **Biomechanics:** The crossover step places the hip in adduction under load, creating a unique stretch on the glutes and inner thigh that drives growth in often-neglected muscles.

description: **Borrowed from ballet, this graceful stretch unlocks a stiff lower back like nothing else.** **Muscles worked:** erectors, glutes, hamstrings. Hold each position and breathe deeply to let gravity do the work. **Pro tip:** Keep your breathing slow and steady — exhale as you fold deeper into the stretch.

Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift

name: Deadlift with Chains

This barbell deadlift uses chains draped over the bar so the load increases as you stand up. It builds explosive power off the floor and teaches you to grind through lockout. **Pro tip:** Let the chains pile on the floor between reps so the weight resets completely each time. **Biomechanics:** As chain links lift off the ground, the load progressively increases, matching your improving leverage and training maximal force production at every joint angle.

Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift

name: Dumbbell Incline Row
description: **Chest-supported and cheat-proof — your lats have nowhere to hide.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms. Lying face-down on an incline bench removes momentum and lower-back stress, letting you isolate the pulling muscles. This is one of the cleanest rowing variations for building a thick back. **Pro tip:** Let the dumbbells hang with a full stretch at the bottom before each pull. **Biomechanics:** The incline angle keeps your torso fixed, eliminating body English and ensuring the lats and mid-back do all the work through a full range of motion.

name: Dumbbell Row
description: **The single-arm king of back builders — heavy, simple, and brutally effective.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, traps, erectors. Using one dumbbell at a time lets you load each side of your back independently, fixing imbalances and allowing heavier weights. A bench provides support for your free hand. **Pro tip:** Think about pulling your elbow toward your hip pocket rather than just lifting the weight. **Biomechanics:** The unilateral loading creates an anti-rotation demand on the core while the free range of the dumbbell allows a natural pulling arc that suits individual shoulder mechanics.

name: Dynamic Back Stretch

Use as part of a warm-up

name: Elevated Cable Rows
description: **Sitting higher changes the pull angle and lights up fibers your regular rows miss.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, traps. By sitting on a raised surface or adjusting the cable height, you change the line of pull to target different portions of the back. The constant cable tension keeps muscles working through the entire rep. **Pro tip:** Experiment with seat height to find the angle that gives you the strongest squeeze in your mid-back. **Biomechanics:** The elevated position creates a slightly downward pulling angle, shifting emphasis toward the lower lats and teres major compared to a standard seated row.

Pull the handle toward your midsection by driving your elbows straight back.

name: Face Pull
description: **The shoulder-saver that belongs in every program, no exceptions.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders (rear delts), traps. This cable exercise targets the often-neglected rear deltoids and upper back, counterbalancing all the pressing most people do. name: Full Range-Of-Motion Lat Pulldown

Keep elbows tight to the body

name: Reverse Grip Bent-Over Rows
description: **The underhand secret weapon for a back that looks as strong as it is.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, traps, lower back. By flipping to a palms-up grip on this barbell row, you recruit your biceps more heavily and target the lower lats with greater emphasis. This variation is excellent for building pulling strength that transfers to deadlifts and pull-ups. **Pro tip:** Brace your core like someone's about to push you — this protects your lower back and keeps the work in your upper body. **Biomechanics:** The supinated grip increases elbow flexor involvement, letting you pull through a slightly longer range of motion and hit the lower lats harder than a standard overhand row.
instructions: Grip a loaded barbell with palms facing up, hands about shoulder-width apart.
Bend at the hips until your upper body is at roughly a 45-degree angle.
With the bar hanging below you, row it toward your belly button by driving your elbows behind you.
Hold the top position for a brief squeeze.
Lower the bar slowly until your arms are fully extended and repeat.
tips: Underhand grip allows elbows to travel further behind the torso
Pull toward the navel for maximum lat bias
Maintain the hip hinge — do not stand up during the pull
Moderate loads work best as biceps are the weak link

name: Reverse Grip Lat Pulldown
description: **Turn your palms toward you and discover a whole new angle on the pulldown.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, traps. This cable pulldown uses an underhand grip to place extra demand on your biceps and lower lats. It's a great alternative when standard pulldowns start feeling stale, and it often allows a longer range of motion. **Pro tip:** Lean back just slightly — about 10 degrees — to create a better line of pull for your lats. **Biomechanics:** The underhand grip shifts the emphasis to the lower fibers of your lats and keeps your biceps in a mechanically strong position, often allowing you to feel a deeper contraction at the bottom.
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown machine and grab the bar with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Secure your thighs under the knee pad and sit tall.
Pull the bar down toward your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back.
Squeeze your back hard at the bottom of the movement.
Slowly let the bar rise back up until your arms are fully extended overhead.
tips: Supinated grip increases biceps demand — they may fatigue first
Pull to the upper chest
The underhand grip allows elbows to travel closer to the body
Lean back slightly for better lat angle

name: Rhomboids-SMR
description: **Roll out the knots hiding between your shoulder blades — your posture will thank you.** **Muscles worked:** lats (upper back area), traps. This self-massage technique uses your bodyweight against a foam roller or ball to release tension in the muscles between your shoulder blades. It's ideal for anyone who sits at a desk, drives a lot, or does heavy pulling work. **Pro tip:** Cross your arms over your chest to spread your shoulder blades apart — this exposes the muscles you're trying to release.
instructions: Lie on your back with a foam roller positioned horizontally across your upper back, just below your shoulder blades.
Cross your arms over your chest and lift your hips off the floor.
Slowly roll up and down between your mid-back and the base of your neck.
When you find a tender spot, pause and hold pressure on it for 15-30 seconds.
Continue rolling for 1-2 minutes, breathing deeply throughout.
tips: Use a foam roller or lacrosse ball between the shoulder blades
Cross your arms to protract the scapulae and expose the rhomboids
Roll slowly and pause on tender spots
Useful for upper back tightness

name: Rocky Pull-Ups/Pulldowns
description: **Alternate in front and behind your head like Rocky training in the gym — dramatic results included.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, shoulders, traps. This bodyweight variation alternates pulling your chin in front of and behind the bar on consecutive reps. The shifting angle hits your back from multiple directions in a single set, building well-rounded upper back development. **Pro tip:** Only go behind the bar as far as your shoulder mobility comfortably allows — forcing it risks injury. **Biomechanics:** The front pull emphasizes your lower lats and biceps, while the behind-the-neck variation shifts more work to your upper back and rear shoulders, covering more muscle in fewer sets.
instructions: Grab a pull-up bar with a wide overhand grip.
Pull yourself up and bring your chin over the front of the bar.
Lower back to a full hang.
On the next rep, pull up and guide your head forward so the bar passes behind your neck.
Lower back down and continue alternating front and back each rep.
tips: The front pull-up portion is the effective lat stimulus
Behind-the-neck adds shoulder risk
Use moderate rep ranges
Standard pull-ups are equally effective and safer

name: Rope Climb
description: **Grip it, climb it, conquer it — few exercises test total upper-body grit like this one.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, shoulders, traps, abs. Rope climbing is a primal bodyweight movement that demands pulling strength, grip endurance, and coordination. Every inch you climb forces your back, arms, and core to work together under your full body weight. **Pro tip:** Use your feet to clamp the rope and take pressure off your arms — this lets you climb longer before your grip gives out. **Biomechanics:** Each pull shortens your lats and biceps against your full bodyweight, with your grip and forearms working continuously to prevent you from sliding down.
instructions: Stand at the base of a hanging rope and reach up as high as you can with both hands.
Clamp the rope between your feet by wrapping it around one foot and stepping on it with the other.
Pull yourself up with your arms while pushing up with your legs.
Reach one hand higher, re-clamp your feet, and repeat the pull.
To descend, loosen your foot clamp slightly and lower yourself hand-over-hand with control.
tips: Use your legs to assist — arms-only is extremely fatiguing
The lat stimulus comes from repeated mid-range pulls
Grip will often be the limiter
Better for conditioning than pure lat hypertrophy

name: Rope Face Pull
description: **The shoulder-saver that belongs in every program but almost nobody does enough of.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders (rear), traps, biceps, forearms. This cable exercise using a rope attachment targets the often-neglected rear shoulders and upper back. It's one of the best movements for balancing out all the pressing most people do, keeping shoulders healthy and posture upright. **Pro tip:** Pull the rope apart at the end of each rep — this extra rotation fires up the small stabilizer muscles in your shoulders. **Biomechanics:** The high cable angle and rope separation create external rotation at the shoulder, strengthening the rotator cuff muscles alongside the rear delts and mid-traps.
instructions: Set a cable machine with a rope attachment at about face height.
Grab both ends of the rope with an overhand grip and step back until there's tension.
Pull the rope toward your face, splitting the ends apart as they reach your ears.
Squeeze your upper back and hold for a beat with your hands beside your temples.
Slowly return the rope to the starting position and repeat.
tips: Pull to your forehead or temples, not your chest
Externally rotate at the end — hands should finish above elbows
Use a rope attachment for the best range of motion
This is as much a shoulder health exercise as a hypertrophy exercise

name: Rope Straight-Arm Pulldown
description: **Isolate your lats without your biceps stealing the show.** **Muscles worked:** lats, triceps, abs. This cable exercise keeps your arms straight throughout, making it one of the purest lat isolation movements available. Using a rope attachment allows a natural hand position and a longer squeeze at the bottom. It's perfect as a warm-up or finisher on back day. **Pro tip:** Push the rope ends past your thighs at the bottom and hold for a two-count — this peak contraction is where the magic happens. **Biomechanics:** With straight arms, your biceps can't contribute, so your lats handle nearly all the pulling force. The shortened position at the bottom creates intense peak contraction.
instructions: Attach a rope to a high cable pulley and face the machine.
Grab both ends of the rope with straight arms, hands at about shoulder height.
With a slight lean forward, pull the rope down in an arc toward your thighs, keeping your arms straight.
Squeeze your lats hard at the bottom, pressing the rope ends apart.
Slowly let the rope return overhead with control, feeling the stretch in your lats.
tips: Split the rope at the bottom for extra ROM
Keep arms straight throughout
The rope allows a more natural hand path
Low fatigue — excellent for extra lat volume

name: Seal Row
description: **Lie face-down and row — zero cheating, zero ego, pure back gains.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, traps. This barbell row is performed lying chest-down on an elevated bench, completely eliminating momentum and lower back involvement. Every ounce of effort goes straight into your back muscles. It's one of the most honest rowing variations you can do. **Pro tip:** Let the bar hang at full arm extension between reps — the stretch at the bottom is where your lats get the most benefit. **Biomechanics:** The prone position removes your lower back and legs from the equation, isolating your lats and upper back through the full range of motion with consistent tension.
instructions: Set up a flat bench on blocks or a rack so there's room for your arms to hang straight down with a barbell below.
Lie face-down on the bench with your chest supported and grab the barbell with an overhand grip.
Let the bar hang at full arm extension.
Row the barbell up into the underside of the bench by driving your elbows toward the ceiling.
Squeeze your back at the top, then lower the bar slowly back to a full stretch.
tips: Elevate the bench on blocks or plates so the barbell can hang freely
Pull to the lower chest or upper abdomen
No body English possible — every rep is strict
Use straps to ensure grip does not limit lat loading

name: Seated Cable Row
description: **Sit down, strap in, and build a back that fills out every shirt you own.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, traps, lower back. This cable machine staple provides smooth, constant tension throughout the entire rowing motion. It's beginner-friendly yet effective enough for advanced lifters, making it a true workhorse back exercise. **Pro tip:** Let your shoulders reach forward slightly at the start of each rep to get a full stretch — then pull back with authority. **Biomechanics:** The cable's constant tension means there's no dead spot in the movement, keeping your lats and mid-back loaded from full stretch to full contraction.
instructions: Sit at a cable row station with your feet on the footplates and knees slightly bent.
Grab the handle with both hands and sit upright with arms extended.
Pull the handle toward your lower chest by driving your elbows straight back.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of the pull.
Slowly extend your arms back to the starting position, feeling the stretch in your lats.
tips: Sit upright — do not lean excessively forward or back
Pull the handle to the lower abdomen for lat emphasis
The cable provides constant tension — no dead spots
Avoid using momentum by swinging the torso
coaching_cues: Sit tall, pull to the belly

name: Seated Cable Rows
description: **Constant tension from start to finish — your back never gets a break.** **Muscles worked:** lats, biceps, forearms, traps, lower back. This cable rowing variation keeps your muscles under load throughout the entire rep, unlike free-weight rows where gravity creates easy spots. It's a reliable back-builder that works for every experience level. **Pro tip:** Experiment with different handles — a wide bar hits your upper back more, while a close-grip V-bar targets your lower lats. **Biomechanics:** The pulley system delivers uniform resistance across the full range of motion, ensuring your lats and mid-back are challenged equally at every point in the row.
instructions: Take a seat at the cable row machine and plant your feet firmly on the foot platform.
Reach forward to grab the attachment with both hands, keeping a tall spine.
Row the handle into your midsection by pulling your elbows past your torso.
Hold the contracted position for a one-count, squeezing your back.
Release the handle forward slowly until your arms are straight and you feel a stretch across your back.
tips: Maintain an upright torso throughout
Pull to the lower abdomen
Control both the concentric and eccentric
Use a V-handle for upper lat emphasis or wide handle for lower lats

name: Seated Good Mornings
description: **Take your legs out of the equation and make your lower back do all the talking.** **Muscles worked:** lower back, glutes, hamstrings. This barbell variation is performed seated on a bench, which removes your legs from the movement and isolates your spinal erectors. It's a focused way to build lower back strength and improve your hinge pattern for deadlifts and squats. **Pro tip:** Start with just the empty bar — this exercise humbles even strong lifters because your legs can't help. **Biomechanics:** Sitting eliminates hip extensor assistance from your glutes and hamstrings, placing nearly all the load on your lower back muscles as they work to extend your spine against the barbell.
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a barbell across your upper back, feet flat on the floor.
Brace your core and sit tall with your chest up.
Slowly hinge forward at the waist, lowering your chest toward your thighs.
Go as low as your flexibility allows while keeping your back flat — not rounded.
Drive back up to the upright seated position by squeezing your lower back muscles.
tips: Sit on a bench with feet firmly planted
Round forward under control, then extend back to neutral
Use light to moderate loads — the erectors are isolated and vulnerable
Don't hyperextend at the top

name: Seated One-arm Cable Pulley Rows
description:
**A unilateral cable row performed from a seated position that isolates each side of the back independently.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps, erectors.

The single-arm setup allows you to rotate slightly through the torso, increasing the range of motion and creating a deeper contraction in the working lat. This exercise is outstanding for correcting left-to-right imbalances and maintaining constant tension throughout the pull.

**Pro tip:** Slight torso rotation toward the working side is acceptable
instructions: Sit on the bench or pad facing the cable stack with feet braced.
Grasp a single handle attachment with one hand using a neutral grip.
Begin with your arm fully extended and a slight forward lean to pre-stretch the lat.
Pull the handle toward your lower ribcage by driving the elbow back and slightly down, squeezing the shoulder blade toward your spine at peak contraction.
Control the return to full extension without letting the weight stack slam.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Slight torso rotation toward the working side is acceptable
Pull to the hip on the working side
The unilateral setup helps identify and correct imbalances
Keep the non-working hand on your thigh for stability

name: Shotgun Row
description:
**A dynamic single-arm cable row where you stand perpendicular to the cable machine and pull the handle across your body in a motion resembling cocking a shotgun.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps.

The angled pulling path and rotational element create a unique stimulus for the lats and mid-back that standard rows cannot replicate. The staggered stance and cross-body mechanics also engage the core as a stabilizer.

**Pro tip:** Split stance with the opposite foot forward
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about chest height.
Stand sideways to the machine with your inside foot forward in a staggered stance.
Reach across your body to grasp the handle with your outside hand, arm fully extended toward the pulley.
Pull the handle forcefully toward your outside hip by driving the elbow back and rotating your torso slightly away from the machine.
Squeeze the lat hard at the end of the pull, then return under control to the stretched position.
Finish all reps before switching sides.
tips: Split stance with the opposite foot forward
Pull the handle toward the hip with a slight rotation
The cable provides constant tension throughout
Focus on the lat squeeze at mid-range

name: Side To Side Chins
description:
**An advanced chin-up variation where you pull yourself up and shift laterally toward one hand at the top of each rep, alternating sides.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, shoulders, traps.

This movement loads one lat significantly more than the other at peak contraction, serving as a bridge exercise toward one-arm chin-ups. It demands considerable grip strength, shoulder stability, and lat power to execute with control.

**Pro tip:** Pull toward one hand, then the other on alternating reps
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with a grip slightly wider than shoulder width, palms facing you.
Initiate the pull by engaging both lats and driving your elbows down.
As you approach the top, shift your body laterally so your chin travels toward your right hand, loading the right lat more heavily.
Lower yourself back to a full dead hang under control.
On the next rep, pull up and shift toward your left hand.
Continue alternating sides for the prescribed reps.
tips: Pull toward one hand, then the other on alternating reps
The shifting load increases unilateral lat demand
Requires good baseline pull-up strength
Control the lateral shift

name: Side-Lying Floor Stretch
description:
**A passive lat stretch performed while lying on your side on the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: shoulders.

By extending the bottom arm overhead and letting gravity pull the ribcage toward the ground, you achieve a deep, sustained stretch through the entire lat and teres major. This is a recovery and mobility tool rather than a hypertrophy exercise, ideal for improving overhead range of motion and reducing lat tightness after heavy pulling sessions.

**Pro tip:** Lie on your side with the bottom arm extended overhead
instructions: Lie on your right side on the floor with your legs stacked and slightly bent for stability.
Extend your right arm fully overhead along the floor, palm facing up.
Place your left hand on the floor in front of your chest for light support.
Allow your ribcage to sink toward the floor, feeling a deep stretch along the right lat and side of the torso.
Hold for 30 to 60 seconds while breathing deeply, then switch sides.
tips: Lie on your side with the bottom arm extended overhead
Let gravity pull you into the stretch
Hold for 30-60 seconds per side
Very gentle — no forcing

name: Single Arm Lat Pulldown
description:
**A unilateral pulldown performed one arm at a time on a cable machine, allowing each lat to work independently through a full range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps.

The single-arm setup permits a slight lean and rotation that can enhance the stretch at the top and the peak contraction at the bottom. This variation is excellent for identifying and correcting strength asymmetries between sides.

**Pro tip:** Slight lean toward the working arm
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown station and grasp a single handle attachment with one hand.
Position your thighs snugly under the knee pad.
Start with your arm fully extended overhead, allowing a slight lean toward the working side to maximize the lat stretch.
Pull the handle down toward the side of your chest by driving your elbow down and back, focusing on the lat doing the work rather than the bicep.
Squeeze at the bottom, then return slowly to full extension.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Slight lean toward the working arm
Pull to the working-side shoulder
Focus on the mid-range contraction
Use moderate loads for best mind-muscle connection

name: Single-Arm Cable Row
description:
**A standing or seated unilateral cable row that provides constant tension on the lat through the entire pulling arc.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps, erectors.

Working one arm at a time allows for greater range of motion as you can rotate the torso slightly to achieve a fuller stretch and contraction. The cable's fixed resistance path makes this movement joint-friendly while delivering an outstanding effort-to-results ratio for back development.

**Pro tip:** Stand or sit with a slight stagger for balance
instructions: Set a cable pulley to roughly navel height.
Stand or sit facing the machine and grasp a single handle with one hand, arm fully extended.
Brace your core and keep your torso relatively upright with only a slight forward lean.
Row the handle toward your hip by retracting the shoulder blade and driving the elbow past your torso.
Hold the peak contraction for a beat, then extend the arm back to the start under control, allowing the shoulder to protract slightly for a full stretch.
Complete all reps before switching arms.
tips: Stand or sit with a slight stagger for balance
Pull toward the hip on the working side
The cable angle can be adjusted — horizontal is best for lats
Control the eccentric to maintain constant tension

name: Sled Row
description:
**A horizontal pulling movement performed by rowing your body toward a fixed anchor point while your feet remain on the ground or a sled.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps, erectors.

Similar to an inverted row but with the added challenge of pulling your entire bodyweight along a surface. This exercise builds functional pulling strength and lat thickness while also demanding significant core stability to maintain a rigid body line throughout the movement.

**Pro tip:** Concentric only — very low muscle damage
instructions: Lie face-up on a sled or smooth surface beneath a sturdy low bar or set of handles.
Grasp the handles with both hands, arms fully extended, body in a straight line from head to heels.
Brace your core and pull your chest up toward the handles by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower yourself back to full arm extension in a controlled manner.
Keep your hips elevated and body rigid throughout the set.
tips: Concentric only — very low muscle damage
Great for recovery days or extra lat volume
Pull the sled with a rowing motion
The lack of eccentric means less soreness

name: Smith Machine Bent Over Row
description:
**A bent-over row performed on the Smith machine, which locks the bar into a fixed vertical path.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps, erectors.

This removes the need to stabilize the bar horizontally, allowing you to focus purely on pulling force and lat engagement. The guided track makes it easier to maintain a consistent torso angle and is particularly useful for lifters who struggle with lower back fatigue limiting their free-weight row performance.

**Pro tip:** The fixed path allows you to focus purely on pulling
instructions: Stand inside the Smith machine with the bar set at about mid-thigh height.
Grasp the bar with an overhand or underhand grip at shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to the floor, knees slightly bent.
Unhook the bar and let it hang at arm's length.
Row the bar toward your lower chest or upper abdomen by pulling the elbows up and back.
Squeeze the lats at the top, then lower the bar under control to full extension.
tips: The fixed path allows you to focus purely on pulling
Hinge at the hips and pull to the lower abdomen
Less stabilization demand than free barbell rows
Good option if lower back fatigue is a concern

name: Smith Machine Row
description:
**A rowing variation using the Smith machine's guided bar path to provide a stable, predictable pulling movement for the lats and upper back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps.

The fixed track eliminates lateral bar drift and reduces the stabilization demand on the lower back, letting you load the lats more aggressively. This makes it a solid choice for higher-rep back work or for lifters rehabbing lower back issues who still want to train heavy rows.

**Pro tip:** The guided path lets you focus on the lat squeeze
instructions: Position yourself inside the Smith machine and set the bar to an appropriate starting height.
Take an overhand or neutral grip at roughly shoulder width.
Hinge forward at the hips with a flat back, knees soft, until your torso is between 30 and 60 degrees from the floor.
Unrack the bar and let your arms hang straight.
Pull the bar into your midsection by driving the elbows back, focusing on squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower with control and repeat for the target rep count.
tips: The guided path lets you focus on the lat squeeze
Pull to the lower abdomen
Hinge at the hips to the appropriate angle
Good for higher rep lat work with less fatigue

name: Snatch Grip Deadlift
description:
**A deadlift variation performed with an extra-wide grip that mimics the hand placement used in the Olympic snatch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, lats, forearms, traps, quads.

The wider grip forces you into a deeper starting position with greater hip flexion and a more horizontal torso, dramatically increasing the range of motion and the demand on the upper back, traps, and lats compared to a conventional deadlift. It is an extremely taxing full-body movement best used for strength and athletic development rather than targeted hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot.
Take a very wide overhand grip with index fingers near or on the snatch rings of the bar.
Push your hips back and bend your knees to lower into the start position with your chest up, back flat, and shoulders over or slightly in front of the bar.
Brace hard, then drive through the floor by extending the knees and hips simultaneously.
Keep the bar close to your body as you stand up to full lockout with hips and knees extended.
Reverse the movement under control to return the bar to the floor.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Spinal Stretch
description:
**A gentle mobility exercise that elongates the spinal erectors and surrounding musculature through sustained flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: erectors. Secondary: lats.

By rounding the spine in a controlled manner and holding the stretched position, you decompress the vertebral segments and relieve tension accumulated from heavy lifting or prolonged sitting. This is a recovery tool, not a muscle-building exercise, and is best used as part of a warm-up or cool-down routine.

**Pro tip:** Move slowly through the range
instructions: Sit on the floor with your legs extended straight in front of you, feet together.
Slowly round your spine forward, reaching your hands toward your toes or shins.
Let your head drop naturally and focus on creating a long C-curve through your entire spine.
Breathe deeply and allow gravity to deepen the stretch with each exhale.
Hold for 30 to 60 seconds.
To exit, slowly reverse the movement by restacking your vertebrae from the lower back upward.
tips: Move slowly through the range
Don't force end-range positions
Hold stretches for 30-60 seconds

name: Standing Pelvic Tilt
description:
**A subtle but effective core activation drill that teaches you to control the position of your pelvis through abdominal and glute engagement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: glutes, erectors.

By alternating between anterior and posterior pelvic tilt while standing, you develop the nerve-to-muscle awareness needed to maintain a neutral spine during heavy compound lifts. This exercise is primarily corrective and balance and coordination rather than hypertrophy-focused.

**Pro tip:** Move slowly through the full range
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly soft, and arms relaxed at your sides.
Begin by tilting your pelvis posteriorly — tuck your tailbone under by contracting your lower abs and squeezing your glutes, flattening the curve in your lower back.
Hold for two to three seconds.
Then reverse by tilting your pelvis anteriorly, allowing your lower back to arch slightly.
Alternate between these two positions in a slow, controlled rhythm for the prescribed number of reps, focusing on isolating the pelvic movement from the rest of your body.
tips: Move slowly through the full range
Breathe deeply into the stretch
Hold end positions for 15-30 seconds

name: Stiff Leg Barbell Good Morning
description:
**A hip hinge movement with the barbell on your upper back and legs kept nearly straight, placing an intense stretch on the hamstrings at the bottom of each rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors.

The combination of a loaded stretch position and the long moment arm created by the bar's placement makes this exercise exceptionally potent for hamstring hypertrophy through stretch-mediated growth. It also heavily taxes the spinal erectors as they work isometrically to maintain a flat back.

**Pro tip:** Push hips back, not down
instructions: Set a barbell across your upper traps as you would for a back squat.
Stand with feet hip-width apart and knees locked out or with only the slightest bend.
Hinge at the hips by pushing them straight back, lowering your torso toward the floor while keeping your back flat and chest proud.
Descend until you feel a strong stretch in the hamstrings — typically when your torso is near parallel to the floor.
Drive your hips forward to return to the upright position, squeezing the glutes at the top.
Keep the movement slow and controlled, especially during the eccentric.
tips: Push hips back, not down
Maintain a neutral spine throughout
Stop descent when you feel a strong hamstring stretch, not when your back rounds
Use a moderate load — this is high-fatigue due to spinal loading

name: Straight Bar Bench Mid Rows
description:
**A chest-supported barbell row performed lying face-down on an elevated bench, which completely eliminates momentum and lower back involvement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps.

The bench support forces the lats and mid-back to do all the work without any cheating, making it one of the most honest back exercises available. The straight bar allows for heavy loading while the prone position keeps the stimulus squarely on the target muscles.

**Pro tip:** Keep chest pressed into the bench
instructions: Set an incline bench to roughly 30 to 45 degrees.
Lie face-down on the bench with your chin just above the top edge and feet planted on the floor behind you.
Reach down and grasp a barbell on the floor with an overhand grip at shoulder width.
Row the bar straight up toward the underside of the bench by driving your elbows up and back, squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower the bar under control until your arms are fully extended.
Keep your chest pressed into the pad throughout to prevent momentum.
tips: Keep chest pressed into the bench
Pull the bar toward the lower chest
Strict form — no momentum
Focus on the mid-range squeeze

name: Straight-Arm Pulldown
description:
**A cable isolation exercise that targets the lats through shoulder extension with the arms kept straight throughout.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: triceps, abs.

By removing elbow flexion from the equation, you eliminate bicep involvement and force the lats to work in near-complete isolation. The movement is particularly effective at training the shortened position of the lats and serves as an excellent activation drill before heavy pulling work or as a finishing exercise to accumulate volume without taxing the biceps.

**Pro tip:** Keep arms straight — any elbow bend shifts load to biceps
instructions: Stand facing a high cable pulley and grasp a straight bar or rope attachment with both hands at shoulder width.
Step back slightly so the weight stack is lifted with your arms extended overhead at roughly a 45-degree angle.
With a slight bend in the elbows that remains fixed throughout, sweep the bar downward in an arc by driving through the lats until your hands reach your thighs.
Squeeze the lats hard at the bottom, then return the bar overhead under control, feeling the stretch through the lats at the top.
tips: Keep arms straight — any elbow bend shifts load to biceps
The lat stimulus is at mid-range to shortened, not stretched
Use a rope or straight bar attachment
Excellent as a pre-exhaust or finisher

name: Sumo Deadlift
description:
**A deadlift variation performed with a wide stance and toes pointed outward, which shortens the range of motion and shifts emphasis toward the glutes, adductors, and quads compared to the conventional pull. The more upright torso position reduces shear force on the lower back, making it a preferred deadlift style for many lifters with longer torsos or those seeking to minimize spinal loading. It remains a full-body strength exercise that allows very heavy loads.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, quads, adductors, forearms, traps, lats.



**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Stand with feet significantly wider than shoulder width, toes angled out at 30 to 45 degrees.
Position the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to grip the bar with a shoulder-width or narrower grip, arms hanging straight between your knees.
Drop your hips, lift your chest, and brace your core.
Initiate the pull by driving your feet outward into the floor and extending the knees and hips simultaneously.
Keep the bar tight to your body as you stand to full lockout.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Superman
description:
**A prone bodyweight exercise where you simultaneously lift your arms and legs off the floor by contracting the spinal erectors, glutes, and hamstrings.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: erectors. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings.

The movement trains back extension against gravity and is one of the simplest ways to strengthen the posterior chain without any equipment. It is particularly useful for beginners building foundational back strength or as a warm-up activation drill before heavier posterior chain work.

**Pro tip:** Lift arms and legs simultaneously
instructions: Lie face-down on the floor with your arms extended straight overhead and legs straight behind you.
Simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the floor by contracting your lower back, glutes, and hamstrings.

Lower everything back to the floor under control.
Avoid jerking or using momentum — the movement should be smooth and deliberate.
Repeat for the prescribed reps.

Don't hyperextend — just come to a slight arch
Hold the top position for 1-2 seconds
Keep neck neutral — look at the floor

name: Suspended Row
description:
**An inverted row performed using suspension straps such as gymnastic rings or a TRX system.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps, erectors, abs.

The unstable handles force greater stabilizer recruitment through the shoulders, core, and grip compared to a fixed-bar inverted row. Difficulty is easily adjusted by changing your foot position — the more horizontal your body, the harder the exercise. This makes it a versatile pulling movement suitable for all experience levels.

**Pro tip:** Walk feet forward to increase difficulty
instructions: Set suspension straps to roughly waist height.
Grasp the handles with a neutral grip and walk your feet forward until your body forms a straight line at an angle beneath the anchor point.

Pull your chest up toward the handles by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Keep your body rigid from head to heels throughout the movement.
Lower yourself back to full arm extension under control.

Pull handles to the sides of your ribcage
The instability adds a stabilization component
Keep core braced throughout

name: T-Bar Row
description:
**A classic barbell rowing movement where one end of the bar is anchored to the floor and you straddle the opposite loaded end, pulling it toward your chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps, erectors.

The arc of the barbell creates a natural bell-shaped resistance curve and the close grip typically used biases the movement toward the mid-back and lats. The T-bar row allows heavy loading and has been a staple back-thickness builder for decades.

**Pro tip:** Close grip biases upper lat fibers
instructions: Wedge one end of a barbell into a corner or landmine attachment.
Load the opposite end with plates.
Straddle the bar facing the loaded end and place a close-grip row handle under the bar against the plates.
Hinge at the hips with a flat back until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to the floor.
Grasp the handle with both hands, arms extended.
Row the bar toward your chest by driving the elbows up and back, squeezing the lats and mid-back at the top.

Pull to the chest
The fixed pivot creates a natural arc
Significant lower back demand — brace hard

name: T-Bar Row with Handle
description:
**A T-bar row variation performed on a dedicated platform that includes built-in handles, typically offering both close and wide grip options.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps, erectors.

The platform provides a chest pad or foot plates for stability, and the integrated handles allow for a more ergonomic grip angle than improvising with a barbell and V-handle. This setup makes it easier to maintain proper form under heavy loads and focus on back contraction.

**Pro tip:** The handle provides a comfortable neutral grip
instructions: Stand on the foot platform of the T-bar row station and select your preferred handle width.
Hinge at the hips until your torso is at roughly 45 degrees, keeping your back flat and core braced.
Grasp the handles with arms fully extended.
Pull the weight toward your lower chest by driving the elbows back and retracting the shoulder blades.
Hold the contraction briefly at the top, then lower the weight under control to a full stretch at the bottom.
Maintain a stable torso angle throughout — avoid rising up to cheat the weight.

Pull to the chest or upper abdomen
Brace your core — significant spinal loading
The arc path naturally loads mid-range

name: Trap Bar Deadlift
description:
**A deadlift performed inside a hexagonal trap bar, which places the load at your sides rather than in front of your body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, erectors, forearms, traps, lats.

This shifts the center of gravity closer to your midline, reducing shear force on the lumbar spine and allowing a more upright torso. The neutral grip handles are easier on the shoulders and biceps tendons, and the movement pattern falls somewhere between a conventional deadlift and a squat, making it an excellent general-purpose lower body strength builder.

**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Step inside the trap bar and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to grasp the handles at your sides with a neutral grip.
Set your back flat, chest up, and brace your core.
Drive through the floor by extending the knees and hips together, standing up tall to full lockout.
Keep your weight centered over mid-foot throughout the lift.
Reverse the movement by pushing the hips back and bending the knees to lower the bar to the floor under control.
