name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Row
description:
**A bilateral bent-over row using two kettlebells, one in each hand.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps, erectors.

The offset center of mass of the kettlebells increases grip and forearm demand compared to dumbbells, and the neutral grip is generally more shoulder-friendly. Rowing both arms simultaneously allows for heavier total loading than single-arm variations while the free-standing position engages the erectors and core as stabilizers throughout the set.

**Pro tip:** Hinge at the hips to 45-60 degrees

Hinge at the hips with a flat back and soft knees, and grasp one kettlebell in each hand with a neutral grip.

name: Underhand Cable Pulldowns
description:
**A lat pulldown variation performed with a supinated underhand grip on a straight or EZ bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps.

The supinated hand position places the biceps in a stronger line of pull, which can allow you to handle slightly more weight and achieve a deeper contraction at the bottom of the movement. This grip also tends to bias the lower lats more than an overhand grip, making it a useful variation for building lat width and thickness in the lower portion of the muscle.

name: Upper Back Stretch
description:
**A static stretch targeting the lats, rhomboids, and mid-traps by protracting the shoulder blades and rounding the upper back forward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: traps.

This movement helps counteract the tightness that develops from heavy rowing and pulling work, improving thoracic mobility and shoulder health. It is a recovery and flexibility tool rather than a strength exercise, best used during cool-downs or between pulling sets to maintain range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Clasp hands in front and round your upper back

You should feel a deep stretch across the upper back, between the shoulder blades, and through the lats.

name: V-Bar Lat Pulldown

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps.

The narrow, neutral grip shifts emphasis slightly toward the lower lats and allows for a longer range of motion compared to wide-grip pulldowns. The V-bar also tends to be easier on the wrists and elbows, making it a comfortable option for high-volume lat training and a solid alternative when wide-grip variations cause joint discomfort.

name: V-Bar Pulldown

name: Weighted Ball Hyperextension

**The standard pull-up loaded with additional weight via a dip belt, weighted vest, or dumbbell between the feet.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, forearms, traps.

name: Barbell Incline Shoulder Raise
description:
**A front raise variation performed while seated on an incline bench, which changes the resistance angle and increases the range of motion for the anterior deltoid.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, chest.

The incline position reduces the ability to cheat with momentum, making each rep more strict and effective.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands
instructions: Sit on an incline bench set to about 45 degrees, holding a barbell with an overhand grip resting on your thighs.
With arms nearly straight and a slight elbow bend, raise the barbell in front of you until your arms are roughly parallel with the floor or in line with the bench angle.
Lower the bar slowly back to the starting position.
The incline will naturally limit how much weight you can use—prioritize form and control over load.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands
A slight forward lean can improve the resistance profile
Don't swing — if you need momentum, the weight is too heavy
Stop at or just above shoulder height

name: Barbell Rear Delt Row
description:
**A bent-over barbell row performed with a wide grip and elbows flared out to specifically target the rear deltoids and upper back rather than the lats.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: biceps, traps, forearms.

The stretched loading position at the bottom provides a deep stretch on the posterior delts, and the compound nature allows meaningful loading.

**Pro tip:** Keep elbows high and wide — this is what differentiates it from a back row
instructions: Hold a barbell with a wide overhand grip (wider than shoulder width).
Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor.
Row the barbell toward your upper chest by driving your elbows out to the sides at roughly 90 degrees from your torso.
Squeeze your rear delts and upper back at the top.
Lower the bar under control to a full arm extension.
Keep your back flat and core braced throughout.
tips: Keep elbows high and wide — this is what differentiates it from a back row
Pull to your upper chest or face level
If your elbows drop, the emphasis shifts from rear delts to lats
Lighter weight with strict form beats heavy weight with body English

name: Barbell Shoulder Press
description:
**The foundational barbell overhead press, performed standing or seated, pressing a barbell from the front rack position to full lockout overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

This is one of the most effective compound movements for building overall shoulder mass and pressing strength, heavily loading the anterior and lateral deltoids along with the triceps.

**Pro tip:** Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
instructions: Unrack a barbell at collarbone height with a grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, core braced, and glutes tight.
Press the bar straight up, moving your head slightly back to clear your chin, then forward once the bar passes.
Lock out with the bar directly over your mid-foot and ears between your arms.
Lower the bar under control back to your collarbone.
Avoid excessive back arching by keeping your ribs down and core engaged.
tips: Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: Barbell Shrug Behind The Back
description:
**A barbell shrug performed with the bar held behind the body, which shifts emphasis toward the mid and lower traps and encourages scapular retraction.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms, shoulders.

This variation also promotes better posture mechanics compared to front shrugs by pulling the shoulders back during the shrugging motion.

**Pro tip:** Keep the bar close to your body throughout
instructions: Stand in front of a barbell on a rack set at glute height, or deadlift it and rotate it behind you.
Hold the bar behind your back with an overhand grip, arms fully extended.
Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears while slightly squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Hold the top position for a one-second count, then lower under control.
Avoid rolling your shoulders or bending your elbows.
A Smith machine can make this variation more comfortable.
tips: Keep the bar close to your body throughout
Shrug straight up without leaning forward
Use a smith machine if balance is an issue

name: Battling Ropes
description:
**A high-intensity conditioning exercise using heavy ropes anchored at one end, creating waves through alternating or simultaneous arm movements.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: forearms, abs, chest.

Battling ropes primarily challenge shoulder endurance, grip strength, and cardiovascular capacity. They are a conditioning tool rather than a hypertrophy exercise.

**Pro tip:** These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
instructions: Stand facing the anchor point with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, and hips hinged.
Hold one end of the rope in each hand.
Create waves by rapidly alternating your arms up and down, or slam both arms simultaneously for power waves.
Maintain an athletic stance with your core braced throughout.
Perform for timed intervals (20-40 seconds) rather than rep counts.
Focus on generating waves all the way to the anchor point by using full arm range of motion.
tips: These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
The shoulders will fatigue as part of the full-body effort
Keep form consistent even as fatigue accumulates
Useful for work capacity and general fitness

name: Behind-the-Neck Press
description:
**An overhead barbell press performed by lowering the bar behind the head to the base of the neck.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

This variation places the shoulder joint in greater external rotation and abduction, shifting emphasis toward the lateral and posterior deltoid heads compared to a standard military press. Requires adequate shoulder mobility to execute safely.

**Pro tip:** Only perform this if you have adequate shoulder external rotation mobility
instructions: Unrack a barbell from a squat rack at upper-chest height and position it across your upper traps behind your neck.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, core braced, and grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder width.
Press the bar straight overhead until your elbows are fully extended, keeping your head slightly forward to clear the bar path.
Lower the bar under control back to the base of your neck, stopping when your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Avoid excessive forward head drift or rib flare throughout the movement.
tips: Only perform this if you have adequate shoulder external rotation mobility
Use a wider grip than your standard press
Don't go excessively deep — just below ear level is sufficient
Start light and assess your shoulder tolerance

name: Bent Over Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise With Head On Bench
description:
**A rear deltoid fly performed in a bent-over position with the forehead resting on an incline bench.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

Bracing the head against the bench eliminates momentum and lower-back involvement, allowing you to isolate the posterior deltoids more effectively than a freestanding bent-over raise.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Set an incline bench to roughly 30-45 degrees and stand behind it, hinging at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor.
Rest your forehead gently on the top edge of the bench and hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging straight down, palms facing each other.
With a slight bend in your elbows, raise both dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly in line with your torso.
Squeeze the rear delts briefly at the top, then lower the weights slowly back to the starting position.
Keep your torso stationary throughout - the bench contact ensures you are not using body English.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Bent Over Low-Pulley Side Lateral
description:
**A rear deltoid raise performed bent over using a low cable pulley.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The cable provides continuous tension throughout the entire range of motion - including the stretched position at the bottom - making it superior to dumbbell variants for maintaining load on the posterior deltoid where it matters most for growth.

**Pro tip:** Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the lowest position and stand sideways to the machine, then hinge forward at the hips until your torso is close to parallel with the floor.
Reach across your body with the far hand and grasp the handle attached to the low pulley.
With a slight elbow bend, sweep the handle outward and upward in a wide arc until your arm is roughly level with your torso.
Hold the peak contraction for a beat, then resist the cable as you return to the start.
Complete all reps on one side before switching. Keep your torso stable and avoid rotating to generate momentum.
tips: Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
Constant tension means no rest at the bottom — every rep counts fully
Single-arm allows you to lean slightly for an even better stretch
This is one of the best lateral deltoid exercises from a stimulus-to-fatigue perspective

name: Bottoms Up Kettlebell Press
description:
**A single-arm overhead press performed with the kettlebell held upside down so the bell faces the ceiling.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, forearms.

The inherently unstable load demands intense grip activation and shoulder stabilizer recruitment, making it an excellent tool for building pressing stability, rotator cuff strength, and grip endurance alongside deltoid development.

**Pro tip:** Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position, then flip it so the bottom of the bell points straight up and your wrist is directly beneath it.
Squeeze the handle as hard as possible to maintain balance - your grip is the primary stabilizer.
Brace your core and press the kettlebell overhead, keeping your wrist stacked and the bell balanced throughout.
Lock out at the top with your bicep near your ear, pause briefly, then lower under strict control back to the starting position.
Perform all reps on one arm before switching. Use lighter loads than a standard press - stability is the limiting factor.
tips: Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
The bell sits on the back of your forearm — don't fight it, work with it
Press in a groove that feels natural for your shoulder
Grip hard to improve rotator cuff co-contraction

name: Bradford/Rocky Presses
description:
**A continuous-tension overhead press that alternates between lowering the barbell to the front of the shoulders and behind the neck without fully locking out at the top.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

By eliminating the lockout, the deltoids remain under constant load, and the front-to-back oscillation hits both the anterior and lateral deltoid heads within a single set.

**Pro tip:** Only perform this if you have adequate shoulder external rotation mobility
instructions: Unrack a barbell at the front rack position with a grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Press the bar upward just high enough to clear the top of your head - do not lock out your elbows.
Immediately lower the bar behind your neck to the base of your traps.
Press back up just over the top of your head again, then lower to the front rack position.
Continue alternating front and back without pausing at the top. Use a controlled tempo and lighter weight than a standard press.
tips: Only perform this if you have adequate shoulder external rotation mobility
Use a wider grip than your standard press
Don't go excessively deep — just below ear level is sufficient
Start light and assess your shoulder tolerance

name: Cable External Rotation
description:
**A rotator cuff exercise performed by externally rotating the shoulder against cable resistance with the elbow pinned at 90 degrees.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: none.

The cable delivers consistent tension across the full arc of rotation, making it a staple prehab and rehab movement for strengthening the infraspinatus and teres minor.

**Pro tip:** Use light weight — these are small muscles that don't need heavy loading
instructions: Set a cable pulley to elbow height and stand sideways to the machine with your working arm on the far side.
Grasp the handle with the outside hand, tuck your elbow tight against your ribs, and bend it to 90 degrees.
Keeping your elbow glued to your side, rotate your forearm outward away from your body as far as your mobility allows.
Hold the end position for a one-count, then slowly return to the start under control.
Use light weight and higher reps - this is a stability and health exercise, not a strength movement.
tips: Use light weight — these are small muscles that don't need heavy loading
Keep your elbow pinned to your side at 90 degrees
Slow, controlled reps are more effective than fast ones
Do these regularly if you press heavy — they protect your shoulders

name: Cable Front Raise
description:
**A front deltoid isolation exercise performed by raising a cable handle from thigh level to eye height in front of the body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest, traps.

The low-pulley cable maintains meaningful tension at the bottom of the rep where a dumbbell front raise goes slack, providing a fuller stimulus across the anterior deltoid's range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
instructions: Attach a straight bar or single handle to a low cable pulley and stand facing away from the machine.
Hold the handle at arm's length in front of your thighs with a pronated or neutral grip.
With a slight bend in your elbows, raise the handle forward and upward until your arm reaches approximately eye level.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower the weight in a controlled two-to-three-second negative.
Avoid swinging your torso or shrugging your traps to complete the rep.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Cable Internal Rotation
description:
**A rotator cuff exercise targeting the subscapularis by internally rotating the shoulder against cable resistance with the elbow fixed at 90 degrees.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: none.

Paired with external rotation work, it helps maintain balanced rotator cuff strength and supports long-term shoulder health for pressing athletes.

**Pro tip:** Use light weight — these are small muscles that don't need heavy loading
instructions: Set a cable pulley to elbow height and stand sideways to the machine with your working arm on the near side.
Grasp the handle, pin your elbow to your ribs, and bend it to 90 degrees so your forearm points toward the cable.
Rotate your forearm inward across your body, pulling the cable toward your midsection.
Squeeze briefly at the end range, then return slowly to the start.
Keep the movement strictly at the shoulder joint - no torso rotation or elbow drift.
tips: Use light weight — these are small muscles that don't need heavy loading
Keep your elbow pinned to your side at 90 degrees
Slow, controlled reps are more effective than fast ones
Do these regularly if you press heavy — they protect your shoulders

name: Cable Lateral Raise
description:
**A lateral deltoid isolation movement using a low cable pulley that provides constant tension from the very bottom of the rep through the top.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

Unlike dumbbell lateral raises where the first 20-30 degrees are essentially unloaded, the cable's line of pull ensures the medial deltoid works hard in the stretched position, which may enhance hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
instructions: Stand sideways to a low cable pulley and grasp the handle with the far hand, allowing the cable to cross in front of your body.
Start with your working arm hanging at your side, cable taut, with a slight elbow bend.
Raise your arm directly out to the side until it reaches roughly shoulder height, leading with the elbow.
Control the descent back to the starting position over two to three seconds - do not let the weight stack crash.
Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning away to cheat the weight up.
tips: Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
Constant tension means no rest at the bottom — every rep counts fully
Single-arm allows you to lean slightly for an even better stretch
This is one of the best lateral deltoid exercises from a stimulus-to-fatigue perspective

name: Cable Lateral Raise Single Arm
description:
**A unilateral cable lateral raise performed one arm at a time, allowing you to focus entirely on the working deltoid and correct any side-to-side imbalances.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The single-arm setup also lets you lean slightly away from the machine to increase the range of motion and load the lateral deltoid more aggressively in the stretched position.

**Pro tip:** Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
instructions: Stand next to a low cable pulley, grasp a sturdy part of the machine with your inside hand for support, and hold the handle with your outside hand.
Lean slightly away from the machine to increase the stretch on the working deltoid at the bottom.
Raise your arm out to the side in a smooth arc until it reaches shoulder height, maintaining a slight elbow bend.
Lower under control, feeling the cable pull your arm back across your body into a deep stretch.
Complete all reps on one side before switching. Avoid jerking or using momentum.
tips: Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
Constant tension means no rest at the bottom — every rep counts fully
Single-arm allows you to lean slightly for an even better stretch
This is one of the best lateral deltoid exercises from a stimulus-to-fatigue perspective

name: Cable Rear Delt Fly
description:
**A posterior deltoid isolation exercise performed by pulling two cable handles apart at shoulder height with the arms nearly straight.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The cables cross in front of the body, providing peak tension in the stretched position and continuous resistance throughout the entire range - making it one of the most effective rear delt movements available.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to shoulder height on a dual cable machine. Grab the left handle with your right hand and the right handle with your left hand so the cables cross.
Step back to create tension, stand tall, and extend your arms in front of you at shoulder height with a slight elbow bend.
Pull both handles apart and outward in a wide arc, squeezing your rear delts as your arms reach a T-position.
Hold the peak contraction for a beat, then slowly allow the cables to bring your hands back together.
Keep your shoulders down and avoid shrugging your traps to take over the movement.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Cable Reverse Fly
description:
**A variation of the rear delt fly using cables set at various heights to target the posterior deltoid from different angles.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The constant cable tension ensures the rear delts are loaded even at full arm extension in front of the body, providing a superior stretch stimulus compared to dumbbell reverse flies.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Set cable pulleys to the desired height (upper, mid, or low) and cross-grip the handles - right hand takes the left cable and vice versa.
Stand centered between the pulleys with arms extended in front of you, cables crossed, and a slight bend in your elbows.
Pull the handles apart by driving your elbows back and out, focusing on squeezing the rear delts.
Open your arms until they are roughly in line with your torso, hold briefly, then return under control.
Maintain a proud chest and avoid rounding your upper back during the movement.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Cable Rope Rear-Delt Rows
description:
**A face-pull-style rowing movement using a rope attachment that targets the rear deltoids and mid-traps simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, biceps, forearms.

By pulling toward the face with the elbows flared high, the emphasis shifts away from the lats and onto the posterior shoulder complex, making it a hybrid between a row and a rear delt fly.

**Pro tip:** Keep elbows high and wide — this is what differentiates it from a back row
instructions: Attach a rope to a cable pulley set at upper-chest to face height.
Grasp both ends of the rope with a neutral grip, step back to create tension, and stand with feet staggered for balance.
Pull the rope toward your face by driving your elbows high and wide, splitting the rope ends apart as they approach your ears.
Squeeze your rear delts and mid-traps hard at the end position, holding for a one-count.
Extend your arms back to the start slowly, maintaining tension on the posterior delts throughout.
tips: Keep elbows high and wide — this is what differentiates it from a back row
Pull to your upper chest or face level
If your elbows drop, the emphasis shifts from rear delts to lats
Lighter weight with strict form beats heavy weight with body English

name: Cable Seated Lateral Raise
description:
**A lateral raise performed seated next to a low cable pulley, which eliminates lower-body momentum and forces the lateral deltoid to do all the work.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The seated position also standardizes your torso angle rep to rep, improving consistency and making progressive overload easier to track.

**Pro tip:** Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
instructions: Place a flat bench sideways next to a low cable pulley and sit with the cable running behind or in front of your body.
Grasp the handle with the far hand, arm hanging at your side, cable taut.
Raise your arm out to the side until it reaches shoulder height, leading with the elbow and maintaining a slight bend.
Lower slowly back to the start, resisting the pull of the cable the entire way down.
Stay seated and upright throughout - no leaning or rocking to generate momentum.
tips: Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
Constant tension means no rest at the bottom — every rep counts fully
Single-arm allows you to lean slightly for an even better stretch
This is one of the best lateral deltoid exercises from a stimulus-to-fatigue perspective

name: Cable Shoulder Press
description:
**An overhead pressing movement performed using cable pulleys instead of free weights.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

The cable's line of pull provides more consistent resistance throughout the press compared to a barbell or dumbbell, where the difficulty varies with leverage. This makes it a useful variation for maintaining tension on the deltoids at every point in the range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Set the pulleys at shoulder height for a pressing angle
instructions: Set both pulleys to the lowest position on a dual cable machine and grasp a handle in each hand.
Bring the handles to shoulder height with your palms facing forward, elbows bent at roughly 90 degrees.
Press both handles overhead until your arms are fully extended, converging slightly at the top.
Lower under control back to the starting position, feeling the cables resist the descent.
Keep your core braced and avoid excessive arching of the lower back.
tips: Set the pulleys at shoulder height for a pressing angle
The constant tension means no rest at any point in the ROM
Control the eccentric — cables reward slow negatives
Adjust cable angle to bias anterior vs lateral deltoid

name: Cable Shrugs
description:
**A trap-focused shrugging movement performed with a cable machine, which provides smooth, constant tension throughout the entire range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms, shoulders.

Unlike barbell or dumbbell shrugs where the resistance profile is purely gravitational, the cable allows you to angle the line of pull slightly forward or backward to bias different portions of the trapezius.

**Pro tip:** Stand far enough from the cable to maintain tension at the bottom
instructions: Attach a straight bar or two single handles to a low cable pulley and stand facing the machine.
Grasp the attachment with both hands at arm's length, shoulders relaxed and depressed.
Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible, squeezing the upper traps at the top.
Hold the peak contraction for one to two seconds, then lower your shoulders slowly back to the start.
Avoid bending your elbows or rolling your shoulders - the movement should be a pure vertical shrug.
tips: Stand far enough from the cable to maintain tension at the bottom
Shrug straight up with control
Pause at the top contraction

name: Cable Upright Row
description:
**An upright rowing movement performed with a cable that targets the upper traps and lateral deltoids.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: biceps, forearms, shoulders.

The cable maintains tension at the bottom of the rep where a barbell version goes slack, and the smooth resistance curve is generally more shoulder-friendly than its barbell counterpart.

**Pro tip:** Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
instructions: Attach a straight bar or EZ-bar to a low cable pulley and stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart.
Grasp the bar with an overhand grip, hands slightly narrower than shoulder width.
Pull the bar upward along the front of your body by driving your elbows up and out, raising them above your hands.
Lift until your elbows reach roughly shoulder height, then pause briefly.
Lower the bar under control. Use a grip width that allows pain-free movement - wider grips reduce shoulder impingement risk.
tips: Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
Pull to chest height, not chin height
Lead with your elbows, not your hands

name: Calf-Machine Shoulder Shrug
description:
**A shrug performed using a standing calf raise machine by positioning the shoulder pads on top of the shoulders and shrugging upward against the resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: shoulders.

This setup frees the grip entirely, eliminating forearm fatigue as a limiting factor and allowing you to focus purely on upper trap contraction with heavier loads.

**Pro tip:** Focus entirely on the shrug — the machine handles stability
instructions: Step under a standing calf raise machine and position the shoulder pads on top of your shoulders (not on your upper back).
Stand upright with your knees slightly bent and your shoulders fully depressed to start.
Shrug your shoulders straight up into the pads as high as possible, driving the weight upward.
Hold the top position for a one-to-two-second squeeze, then lower slowly.
Since grip is not a factor, focus on maximizing range of motion and time under tension.
tips: Focus entirely on the shrug — the machine handles stability
Use the full range from depressed to fully elevated
Control the eccentric to maximize time under tension

name: Car Drivers
description:
**A shoulder endurance exercise performed by holding a weight plate at arm's length in front of the body and rotating it side to side like turning a steering wheel.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: forearms.

This movement challenges the anterior and lateral deltoids isometrically while the rotation component works the forearms and rotator cuff dynamically.

**Pro tip:** Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
instructions: Hold a weight plate with both hands at the 9-and-3 o'clock positions, arms extended straight in front of you at shoulder height.
Brace your core and keep your arms locked out with a slight elbow bend.
Rotate the plate clockwise as far as comfortable, then immediately rotate counterclockwise to the same degree - like turning a car steering wheel.
Continue alternating rotations for the prescribed reps or time, keeping the plate at shoulder height throughout.
Use a lighter plate than you think - the isometric hold combined with rotation creates significant fatigue quickly.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Chair Upper Body Stretch
description:
**A passive shoulder and chest stretch performed by placing both hands on a chair or elevated surface behind you and gently sinking the torso downward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest.

This opens up the anterior shoulder capsule and pectoral muscles, making it useful as a warm-up or cool-down for pressing-heavy sessions.

**Pro tip:** Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
instructions: Stand facing away from a sturdy chair or bench and place both palms on the seat behind you, fingers pointing backward.
Walk your feet forward slightly so your arms are extended behind your body.
Slowly bend your knees and lower your hips toward the floor, allowing your shoulders and chest to stretch as your torso drops.
Hold the stretched position for 20-30 seconds, breathing deeply and relaxing into the stretch.
Rise back up gently. Repeat two to three times, sinking slightly deeper each round.
tips: Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
Move through the range slowly and with control
Never force range of motion — work at the edge of comfort
Consistency matters more than intensity for mobility work

name: Chin To Chest Stretch
description:
**A simple neck and upper trap stretch performed by tucking the chin toward the chest and gently pulling the head forward with the hands.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: erectors.

This lengthens the upper trapezius and posterior cervical muscles, providing relief from tension headaches and the postural tightness common in desk workers.

**Pro tip:** Hold stretches for 20-30 seconds
instructions: Sit or stand with an upright posture, shoulders relaxed and down.
Interlace your fingers and place both hands on the back of your head, just above the base of the skull.
Gently tuck your chin and use your hands to guide your head forward and downward toward your chest.
Apply only light pressure - you should feel a comfortable stretch along the back of your neck and upper traps.
Hold for 20-30 seconds, breathing steadily. Release slowly and repeat two to three times.
tips: Hold stretches for 20-30 seconds
For SMR, apply gentle pressure on tender spots
Perform before or after trap training

name: Circus Bell
description:
**A strongman event where an oversized, thick-handled dumbbell is cleaned from the floor and pressed overhead with one arm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps, forearms, glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs.

The circus bell demands total-body coordination - legs drive the clean, the core stabilizes the asymmetric load, and the shoulder and triceps lock it out overhead. It is as much a skill event as a strength event.

**Pro tip:** Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
instructions: Straddle the circus bell on the floor with feet wider than shoulder width, hinge down, and grip the thick handle with one hand.
Clean the bell to your shoulder using a powerful hip extension, catching it in the rack position against your deltoid and upper chest.
Dip slightly at the knees, then drive upward explosively to press or push-press the bell to full lockout overhead.
Stabilize at the top with your arm fully extended, hips and knees locked, then lower the bell back to the shoulder under control.
The thick handle is the main challenge - chalk your hand generously and squeeze the handle as hard as possible throughout.
tips: Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: Clean and Jerk
description:
**One of the two competition Olympic lifts, consisting of a clean (pulling the barbell from the floor to the shoulders) followed by a jerk (driving the barbell from the shoulders to overhead lockout).**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: quads, glutes, hamstrings, traps, erectors, triceps, abs, forearms, calves.

It is the ultimate expression of full-body power and coordination, recruiting virtually every major muscle group in a single explosive sequence.

**Pro tip:** Master each component separately before combining them
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, shins touching the bar, and grip it just outside your knees with a hook grip.
Initiate the clean by driving through the floor with your legs, keeping the bar close to your body, then explosively extend your hips and shrug to accelerate the bar upward.
Pull yourself under the bar and catch it in a front squat position across your front delts, then stand up to complete the clean.
Reset your feet to a pressing stance, dip at the knees, and drive the bar overhead aggressively, splitting or pushing your feet apart to catch the bar at lockout.
Recover to a standing position with the bar locked out overhead, feet together, then lower the bar back to the shoulders or floor.
tips: Master each component separately before combining them
The clean positions the bar for the press — don't rush the transition
These are power and conditioning movements, not hypertrophy exercises

name: Clean Shrug
description:
**An explosive shrugging movement performed from the hang or floor position using the same mechanics as the second pull of a clean.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms, shoulders, erectors, glutes, quads, hamstrings.

The violent hip extension combined with the aggressive trap shrug allows you to handle significantly more weight than a standard shrug, overloading the upper traps with a powerful, dynamic stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Use hip drive to initiate — this is not a strict shrug
instructions: Set up as you would for a clean - feet hip-width, grip just outside the knees, back flat, hips loaded.
Pull the bar to the hang position (mid-thigh) by extending your knees and hips.
From the hang, explosively extend your hips and knees while simultaneously shrugging your shoulders as high and as fast as possible.
Allow the bar to travel upward from the momentum, keeping your arms straight - this is a shrug, not an upright row.
Lower the bar back to the hang position under control and reset for the next rep.
tips: Use hip drive to initiate — this is not a strict shrug
The explosive nature allows heavier loads than strict shrugs
Keep the bar close to your body

name: Crucifix
description:
**A strongman event and shoulder endurance exercise where weights are held at arm's length out to the sides in a T-position for as long as possible.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: forearms, traps.

The crucifix hold is a brutal isometric challenge for the lateral deltoids, upper traps, and grip, testing muscular endurance and mental toughness in equal measure.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands
instructions: Stand upright and hold a dumbbell or implement in each hand with arms extended straight out to the sides at shoulder height, palms facing down.
Lock a slight bend in your elbows and brace your core to maintain an upright torso.
Hold this T-position for the prescribed time, fighting the urge to let your arms drop.
Keep your shoulders down and away from your ears - shrugging shifts the load off the delts and onto the traps.
When you can no longer maintain shoulder height, the set is over. Track your hold time for progressive overload.

A slight forward lean can improve the resistance profile
Don't swing — if you need momentum, the weight is too heavy
Stop at or just above shoulder height

name: Cuban Press
description: **The shoulder trifecta that packs rotation, stability, and pressing into one seamless flow.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, traps, biceps, forearms. The Cuban Press combines an upright row, external rotation, and overhead press into a single fluid movement using dumbbells. It builds shoulder health while strengthening the rotator cuff and deltoids together. **Pro tip:** Use lighter weight than you think — the external rotation portion is the weakest link and controls the load for the entire movement. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve means the mid-range of each phase is hardest, making this excellent for balanced shoulder development across multiple planes of motion.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing your body, arms hanging at your sides.
Pull both dumbbells up along your torso until your upper arms are parallel to the floor, elbows bent at 90 degrees.
Rotate your forearms upward until the dumbbells point toward the ceiling, keeping your elbows at shoulder height.
Press the dumbbells overhead until your arms are fully extended.
Reverse the entire sequence — lower to the rotated position, rotate forearms down, then lower the dumbbells back to the start.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping each phase controlled and smooth.

Allow a natural arc as you press — don't force a straight line
Control the eccentric to maintain tension on the deltoids

name: Double Kettlebell Jerk
description: **An explosive full-body launch that turns two kettlebells into overhead rockets.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, quads, glutes, calves, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms. This powerful overhead lift uses a quick dip-and-drive from the legs to propel two kettlebells from your shoulders to locked out overhead. description: **A unilateral overhead press that builds pressing power and bulletproof core stability simultaneously.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, abs. Working unilaterally helps you spot and correct strength imbalances. **Pro tip:** Pull your elbow high and slightly back rather than straight up — this shifts more work to the traps and reduces shoulder impingement risk. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve peaks when your elbow reaches shoulder height, making the mid-range the most demanding portion of the pull.

description: **The shoulder-friendly raise that works with your body's natural movement plane, not against it.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, traps. description: **A zero-equipment warm-up that wakes up stiff shoulders and gets synovial fluid flowing.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders.
