The front dumbbell raise lifts the weight directly in front of you to target the front head of the deltoid. It's a simple, effective movement for adding definition and strength to the front of your shoulders. **Pro tip:** Alternate arms instead of lifting both at once — this lets you use slightly heavier weight and gives each shoulder a brief rest between reps. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve means the movement is easiest at the bottom and top, with peak difficulty when your arm is parallel to the floor, making controlled reps through that zone most valuable.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, palms facing your thighs.
Raise one dumbbell straight out in front of you, keeping a slight bend in your elbow.
Lift until your arm is parallel to the floor or slightly above.
Lower the dumbbell back to your side under control.
Repeat with the other arm, alternating sides, or raise both simultaneously.
Keep your torso upright and avoid rocking back and forth.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Front Incline Dumbbell Raise
description: **An incline-bench twist on the classic front raise that eliminates cheating and extends the range of motion.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, chest. Performing front raises while seated on an incline bench lets your arms hang behind your body at the start, creating a longer range of motion and a deeper stretch on the front deltoid. The bench also prevents momentum. **Pro tip:** Set the bench to about 45-60 degrees — too upright and you lose the extended range, too flat and it becomes awkward. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve is shifted by the incline angle, placing the front delt under tension earlier in the range and providing a stretch-position challenge that a standing version cannot match.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 45-60 degrees and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging straight down.
With palms facing each other or facing down, raise one or both dumbbells in front of you.
Lift until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower the dumbbells back to the hanging position slowly.
Keep your back pressed against the bench throughout to prevent cheating.
Repeat for the desired number of reps.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Front Plate Raise
description: **A grip-and-shoulder double threat that turns a single weight plate into a front-delt finisher.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, chest. The front plate raise has you gripping a weight plate with both hands and raising it in front of you. The wide grip and plate shape force your hands and forearms to work harder than dumbbells, and the fixed hand position keeps both shoulders working evenly. **Pro tip:** Grip the plate at the 3 and 9 o'clock positions and squeeze it hard throughout — this engages your chest more and keeps the plate from wobbling. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve peaks when the plate reaches shoulder height, and the fixed bilateral grip ensures both shoulders share the load equally, preventing one side from dominating.
instructions: Stand upright holding a weight plate with both hands at the sides, arms extended in front of your thighs.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and your core braced.
Raise the plate straight out in front of you until it reaches shoulder or eye height.
Pause briefly at the top, squeezing your front delts.
Lower the plate back to the starting position slowly and with control.
Repeat without swinging or leaning back to assist the lift.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Front Two-Dumbbell Raise
description:
**A bilateral front raise variation using two dumbbells simultaneously to target the anterior deltoids.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest.

Lifting both weights at once demands greater core stabilization and ensures symmetrical loading across both shoulders throughout the movement.

**Pro tip:** Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing your thighs.
Brace your core and keep a slight bend in your elbows.
Raise both dumbbells forward and upward in a controlled arc until your arms reach shoulder height.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower the weights back down under control.
Avoid swinging or using momentum from your hips.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Handstand Push-Up
description:
**An advanced bodyweight pressing movement performed in an inverted position against a wall.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps, abs.

This exercise loads the shoulders with nearly your full body weight, making it one of the most demanding overhead pressing variations available without equipment.

**Pro tip:** Wall-assisted is fine — the deltoid stimulus is the same
instructions: Kick up into a handstand position with your back facing a wall and hands placed shoulder-width apart on the floor.
Keep your body tight and core engaged.
Bend your elbows to lower the top of your head toward the ground in a controlled manner.
Once your head lightly touches the floor, press forcefully through your palms to return to the starting position.
Maintain a straight body line throughout.
tips: Wall-assisted is fine — the deltoid stimulus is the same
The bottom is the hardest part, which is where the deltoids are most stretched
Keep your core tight to prevent arching
Pike push-ups are a valid regression with similar mechanics

name: Handstand Push-Ups
description:
**A challenging calisthenics staple that uses full bodyweight as resistance in an overhead press pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps, abs.

Performed inverted against a wall, this movement builds raw pressing strength in the deltoids and triceps while demanding significant balance and midline stability.

**Pro tip:** Wall-assisted is fine — the deltoid stimulus is the same
instructions: Place your hands on the floor about shoulder-width apart and kick up into a handstand against a wall.
Squeeze your glutes and abs to maintain rigidity.
Lower yourself by bending at the elbows until the crown of your head nears the floor.
Drive through your hands to press back up to full arm extension.
Perform each rep with deliberate control, avoiding any kipping or jerking.
tips: Wall-assisted is fine — the deltoid stimulus is the same
The bottom is the hardest part, which is where the deltoids are most stretched
Keep your core tight to prevent arching
Pike push-ups are a valid regression with similar mechanics

name: Internal Rotation with Band
description:
**A rotator cuff strengthening exercise using a resistance band to train the subscapularis through internal rotation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: none.

This prehab staple helps maintain shoulder joint health and is commonly used in warm-up routines or rehabilitation programs.

**Pro tip:** Use light weight — these are small muscles that don't need heavy loading
instructions: Anchor a resistance band at elbow height to a sturdy object.
Stand sideways to the anchor point and grasp the band with the hand closest to it.
Pin your elbow to your side at a 90-degree angle.
Rotate your forearm inward across your body against the band's resistance.
Return slowly to the starting position.
Keep your elbow fixed at your side throughout the entire movement.
tips: Use light weight — these are small muscles that don't need heavy loading
Keep your elbow pinned to your side at 90 degrees
Slow, controlled reps are more effective than fast ones
Do these regularly if you press heavy — they protect your shoulders

name: Iron Cross
description:
**A full-body dumbbell exercise that combines a squat with a lateral raise, creating a cross-shaped arm position at the top.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest, traps, abs, glutes, hamstrings, quads.

This movement challenges shoulder endurance and total-body coordination while building the medial deltoids under sustained tension.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands
instructions: Stand holding a light dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Descend into a squat while keeping the dumbbells by your legs.
As you drive up out of the squat, simultaneously raise both arms out to the sides until they form a T-shape at shoulder height.
Hold the top position momentarily, then lower the dumbbells as you descend into the next squat.
Use a weight that allows strict form throughout all reps.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands
A slight forward lean can improve the resistance profile
Don't swing — if you need momentum, the weight is too heavy
Stop at or just above shoulder height

name: Isometric Neck Exercise - Front And Back
description:
**A static neck strengthening exercise that targets the anterior and posterior cervical muscles through self-resisted isometric holds.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: none.

This movement builds neck stability and resilience without requiring any equipment or spinal movement.

**Pro tip:** Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
instructions: Stand or sit tall with a neutral spine.
Place both hands on your forehead and push your head forward against your hands while resisting all movement with your arms.
Hold for the prescribed duration.
Then place both hands on the back of your head and push backward against your hands, again resisting all motion.
Maintain steady breathing and apply force gradually rather than explosively.
tips: Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
Move through the full range of motion slowly
Never jerk or use momentum on neck exercises

name: Isometric Neck Exercise - Sides
description:
**A lateral isometric neck exercise that strengthens the muscles along the sides of the neck, including the upper trapezius and sternocleidomastoid.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: none.

Self-applied hand resistance allows precise intensity control for safe, progressive neck training.

**Pro tip:** Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
instructions: Sit or stand with an upright posture and neutral head position.
Place your right hand against the right side of your head above the ear.
Press your head sideways into your hand while your hand resists all movement.
Hold for the prescribed time, then switch to the left side.
Apply force progressively and keep your shoulders level throughout each hold.
tips: Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
Move through the full range of motion slowly
Never jerk or use momentum on neck exercises

name: Jerk Balance
description:
**An Olympic weightlifting accessory drill that trains the receiving position of the split jerk.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, traps, erectors, forearms.

Starting with the barbell in the front rack, you dip and drive the bar overhead while stepping your front foot forward into a split stance, developing timing, footwork, and lockout confidence.

**Pro tip:** This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
instructions: Begin with a barbell in the front rack position, feet hip-width apart.
Perform a short, sharp dip by bending your knees slightly, then drive explosively upward through your legs.
As the bar travels overhead, step your front foot forward into a split position while pressing the bar to full lockout.
Your rear foot stays planted.
Stabilize overhead, then recover by stepping your front foot back.
Reset and repeat.
tips: This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
The legs do most of the work — the arms guide and stabilize
Practice the footwork separately before adding heavy loads
Not recommended as a primary deltoid hypertrophy exercise

name: Kettlebell Arnold Press
description:
**A kettlebell adaptation of the classic Arnold press that rotates the wrists from a palms-in position to palms-forward as you press overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

The offset center of mass of the kettlebell increases grip and stabilizer demands compared to the dumbbell version.

**Pro tip:** Start with palms facing you at chin height — this is the extra ROM that makes it valuable
instructions: Stand or sit holding a kettlebell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing you.
Initiate the press by rotating your wrists outward while simultaneously driving the kettlebells overhead.
At the top, your palms should face forward with arms fully extended.
Reverse the motion by lowering the kettlebells while rotating your palms back toward you.
Control the eccentric portion to maximize time under tension.
tips: Start with palms facing you at chin height — this is the extra ROM that makes it valuable
Rotate smoothly as you press — the transition should be seamless, not jerky
The wider ROM means you'll use less weight than a standard press — that's expected
Focus on the stretch at the bottom position

name: Kettlebell Halo
description:
**A shoulder mobility and stability exercise where you trace a circular path around your head with a kettlebell.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, traps, forearms.

This movement promotes healthy range of motion in the shoulder girdle while engaging the core and upper traps to control the weight's trajectory.

**Pro tip:** Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the horns at chest height, bottom facing up.
Slowly move the kettlebell around your head in a circular path, passing it behind your neck and back to the front.
Keep the circle tight and close to your head.
Your torso should remain still with your core braced.
Complete the prescribed reps in one direction, then reverse the direction for an equal number of reps.
tips: Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
Move through the range slowly and with control
Never force range of motion — work at the edge of comfort
Consistency matters more than intensity for mobility work

name: Kettlebell Pirate Ships
description:
**A dynamic kettlebell shoulder exercise where you swing the bell side to side in front of your body like a pendulum.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, forearms.

This lateral swinging pattern challenges the medial deltoids, grip strength, and anti-rotational core stability in a unique way.

**Pro tip:** The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width apart.
Hold a kettlebell by the handle with both hands, arms extended in front of you.
Swing the kettlebell to one side by shifting your weight and rotating slightly, then reverse the motion and swing it to the opposite side.
Keep your arms relatively straight and control the arc with your shoulders.
Brace your core throughout to resist excessive trunk rotation.
tips: The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized
Scaption (30° forward of lateral) may be more shoulder-friendly than pure lateral raises
Y-raises emphasize the junction between lateral and posterior deltoid
Use lighter loads — these are precision movements

name: Kettlebell Press
description:
**A unilateral overhead press performed with a kettlebell in the rack position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps, abs.

The kettlebell's offset load forces the forearm and shoulder stabilizers to work harder than a dumbbell press, building functional pressing strength and joint resilience.

**Pro tip:** Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position at your shoulder with the bell resting on the back of your forearm.
Stand tall with your feet hip-width apart and core braced.
Press the kettlebell overhead by driving your arm straight up until your elbow locks out.
The kettlebell should settle behind your wrist at the top.
Lower it back to the rack position under control.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
The bell sits on the back of your forearm — don't fight it, work with it
Press in a groove that feels natural for your shoulder
Grip hard to improve rotator cuff co-contraction

name: Kettlebell Seated Press
description:
**A seated variation of the kettlebell overhead press that eliminates lower body assistance, isolating the deltoids and triceps more directly.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

Sitting removes the ability to generate momentum through the legs or hips, demanding pure upper-body pressing strength.

**Pro tip:** Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
instructions: Sit on a bench or the floor with your legs extended or crossed.
Clean a kettlebell to the rack position at your shoulder.
Brace your core firmly to maintain an upright torso.
Press the kettlebell directly overhead to full lockout.
Lower it back to the rack position with control.
Avoid leaning back excessively to compensate for the load.
tips: Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
The bell sits on the back of your forearm — don't fight it, work with it
Press in a groove that feels natural for your shoulder
Grip hard to improve rotator cuff co-contraction

name: Kettlebell Seesaw Press
description:
**An alternating kettlebell press where one arm drives up as the other lowers, creating a continuous seesaw rhythm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, abs, traps.

This pattern increases time under tension for the shoulders and demands heightened core engagement to resist lateral tilting.

**Pro tip:** Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, a kettlebell racked at each shoulder.
Press one kettlebell overhead to lockout.
As you begin lowering that kettlebell back to the rack, simultaneously press the opposite kettlebell up.
The two bells should pass each other at roughly the midpoint.
Maintain a rigid torso and avoid side-bending.
Continue alternating in a smooth, rhythmic fashion for the prescribed reps per arm.
tips: Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
The bell sits on the back of your forearm — don't fight it, work with it
Press in a groove that feels natural for your shoulder
Grip hard to improve rotator cuff co-contraction

name: Kettlebell Sumo High Pull
description:
**A powerful full-body pulling movement that starts from a wide sumo stance and finishes with the kettlebell pulled to chin height.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, glutes, hamstrings, quads, forearms, erectors.

This exercise bridges the gap between lower-body hip drive and upper-body pulling, training explosive power through the entire posterior chain and shoulders.

**Pro tip:** Use a wider grip to reduce internal rotation stress on the shoulder
instructions: Stand over a kettlebell with a wide sumo stance, toes pointed outward.
Hinge at the hips and grip the kettlebell handle with both hands.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips explosively.
As the kettlebell rises, pull it upward by bending your elbows high and wide until the handle reaches chin height.
Lower the kettlebell back between your legs with control and repeat.
Let the hip drive do most of the work.
tips: Use a wider grip to reduce internal rotation stress on the shoulder
Pull to chest height, not chin height — going too high increases impingement risk
Lead with your elbows, not your hands
If this bothers your shoulders, cable lateral raises are a better alternative

name: Kettlebell Thruster
description:
**A high-output combination exercise that merges a front squat with an overhead press in one fluid motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, abs.

The leg drive from the squat transfers directly into the press, allowing heavier loads overhead while taxing the cardiovascular system significantly.

**Pro tip:** The squat drives the press — use the momentum from standing up
instructions: Rack one or two kettlebells at shoulder height.
Drop into a full front squat, keeping your elbows up and torso upright.
Drive out of the bottom explosively and use the upward momentum to press the kettlebells overhead to full lockout.
Lower the kettlebells back to the rack as you descend into the next squat.
Maintain a continuous rhythm without pausing between the squat and press phases.
tips: The squat drives the press — use the momentum from standing up
Keep the bar or dumbbells in a front rack position during the squat
These are metabolically brutal — expect cardiovascular limitation before deltoid failure
Better for conditioning than isolated deltoid hypertrophy

name: Kettlebell Turkish Get-Up (Lunge style)
description:
**A multi-phase ground-to-standing exercise performed while keeping a kettlebell locked out overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, glutes, quads, hamstrings, triceps, traps.

The lunge-style variation transitions through a kneeling lunge to stand, emphasizing single-leg strength and hip mobility alongside total-body stability and shoulder endurance.

**Pro tip:** Keep your eyes on the weight throughout the entire movement
instructions: Lie on your back with a kettlebell pressed to full lockout in one hand.
Bend the knee on the same side and plant that foot flat.
Roll onto your opposite elbow, then up to your hand.
Bridge your hips high, sweep your free leg underneath into a kneeling lunge position.
From the lunge, stand up while keeping the kettlebell locked overhead with eyes on the bell.
Reverse each step to return to the floor.
tips: Keep your eyes on the weight throughout the entire movement
Each position in the get-up is a checkpoint — pause and stabilize
The shoulder works isometrically — it stabilizes, not presses
Start light and master the pattern before adding load

name: Kettlebell Turkish Get-Up (Squat style)
description:
**A Turkish get-up variation that uses a squat rather than a lunge to transition from the ground to standing.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, glutes, quads, hamstrings, calves, triceps, traps.

This version demands greater hip and ankle mobility and shifts more emphasis onto bilateral leg strength during the standing phase.

**Pro tip:** Keep your eyes on the weight throughout the entire movement
instructions: Begin lying on your back with a kettlebell locked out overhead in one hand.
Bend the same-side knee and plant your foot.
Roll to your elbow, then to your hand, and bridge your hips up.
Sweep your free leg underneath your body and position both feet to squat.
Rise from the squat to a full standing position with the kettlebell overhead.
Reverse the sequence step by step to return to the floor.
tips: Keep your eyes on the weight throughout the entire movement
Each position in the get-up is a checkpoint — pause and stabilize
The shoulder works isometrically — it stabilizes, not presses
Start light and master the pattern before adding load

name: Kneeling Arm Drill
description:
**A bodyweight coordination drill performed from a kneeling position that cycles the arms through various overhead and lateral patterns.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs.

Primarily used as a warm-up or movement prep exercise, it activates the deltoids and reinforces core stability without external load.

**Pro tip:** These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
instructions: Kneel on both knees with your torso upright and core engaged.
Extend your arms out to the sides at shoulder height.
Cycle through arm movements — circles forward, circles backward, overhead reaches, and cross-body patterns — while maintaining a stable kneeling base.
Keep your hips stacked over your knees and avoid swaying.
Perform each pattern for the prescribed duration or rep count.
tips: These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
The shoulders will fatigue as part of the full-body effort
Keep form consistent even as fatigue accumulates
Useful for work capacity and general fitness

name: Landmine Linear Jammer
description:
**An explosive full-body pressing exercise using a landmine setup where you drive the barbell forward and upward from a quarter-squat position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, chest, quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs.

This movement trains triple extension through the ankles, knees, and hips while finishing with a powerful shoulder press.

**Pro tip:** Stand at the end of the barbell, not beside it
instructions: Stand facing the end of a barbell anchored in a landmine attachment.
Hold the end of the bar at chest height with both hands in a staggered or parallel stance.
Drop into a quarter squat, then explode upward through your legs and press the bar forward and up to full arm extension.
Absorb the bar back to your chest as you decelerate into the next rep.
Generate power from the legs and transfer it through the press.
tips: Stand at the end of the barbell, not beside it
The arc path is natural — don't fight it
Great option if strict overhead pressing bothers your shoulders
Can be done single-arm for added core demand

name: Landmine Press
description:
**A shoulder-friendly pressing variation using a barbell anchored in a landmine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, chest, abs.

The angled pressing path reduces stress on the shoulder joint compared to strict vertical pressing, making it an excellent option for those with overhead mobility limitations or shoulder discomfort.

**Pro tip:** Stand at the end of the barbell, not beside it
instructions: Stand facing the end of a barbell secured in a landmine base.
Grip the end of the bar with one hand at shoulder height.
Brace your core and press the bar up and away from you along the natural arc of the landmine.
Lock out your arm fully at the top.
Lower the bar back to shoulder height with control.
Keep your torso stable and avoid excessive leaning.
tips: Stand at the end of the barbell, not beside it
The arc path is natural — don't fight it
Great option if strict overhead pressing bothers your shoulders
Can be done single-arm for added core demand

name: Lateral Raise - With Bands
description:
**A lateral raise performed with resistance bands that provides increasing tension as the arms rise.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The ascending resistance profile makes the top of the movement — where the medial deltoid is most active — significantly more challenging than the bottom, creating a unique stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Stand on the band center for even resistance
instructions: Stand on the center of a resistance band with both feet.
Hold one end of the band in each hand with arms at your sides.
Raise both arms out to the sides with a slight bend in your elbows until they reach shoulder height.
Squeeze at the top where band tension is greatest, then lower your arms back down slowly.
Keep your wrists neutral and avoid shrugging your shoulders upward.
tips: Stand on the band center for even resistance
The bottom portion will feel very light — that's the nature of bands
The top is where the real work happens
Good for warm-ups, pump work, or when no other equipment is available

name: Leverage Shoulder Press
description:
**A machine-based overhead press that guides the weight along a fixed path, allowing you to focus entirely on pushing effort without balancing the load.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

The leverage system provides consistent resistance throughout the range of motion, making it ideal for controlled hypertrophy work or training to failure safely.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles align with your shoulders.
Sit with your back firmly against the pad and grasp the handles with an overhand grip.
Press the handles overhead until your arms are fully extended.
Lower the weight back to the starting position under control without letting the plates slam.
Keep your back pressed into the pad and feet flat on the floor throughout.
tips: Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
The fixed path lets you push harder without worrying about balance
Good option for training close to failure safely
Focus on squeezing the deltoids rather than just moving the weight

name: Leverage Shrug
description:
**A machine shrug that isolates the upper trapezius through a guided vertical path.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms, shoulders.

By removing the need to balance a free weight, this exercise allows you to load the traps heavily and focus purely on the contraction, making it one of the most efficient trap-building movements available.

**Pro tip:** Focus entirely on the shrug — the machine handles stability
instructions: Stand inside the leverage shrug machine and grip the handles at your sides.
Stand tall with your shoulders relaxed downward.
Elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible, squeezing your traps hard at the top.
Hold the peak contraction briefly, then lower your shoulders back down in a controlled manner.
Avoid bending your elbows or rolling your shoulders.
tips: Focus entirely on the shrug — the machine handles stability
Use the full range from depressed to fully elevated
Control the eccentric to maximize time under tension

name: Log Lift
description:
**A strongman competition staple that involves cleaning and pressing a log implement overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, chest, traps, abs, glutes, hamstrings, quads, erectors, forearms.

The neutral grip handles and thick diameter of the log create a unique pressing challenge that demands raw strength, explosive power, and total-body coordination from floor to lockout.

**Pro tip:** Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
instructions: Straddle the log with feet hip-width apart.
Bend down and grip the neutral handles.
Deadlift the log to your lap by extending your hips and knees.
Roll the log up your torso into the rack position at your chest, tucking your elbows underneath.
From the rack, dip slightly at the knees and drive the log overhead using leg drive combined with a strong press.
Lock out fully overhead, then lower the log back to your chest and down to the ground.
tips: Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: Low Pulley Row To Neck
description:
**A cable rowing variation that targets the rear deltoids and upper traps by pulling a low pulley attachment toward the neck.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, biceps, forearms.

The upward rowing angle shifts emphasis away from the lats and onto the posterior shoulder complex, making it an effective compound movement for building thickness across the upper back and rear delts.

**Pro tip:** Keep elbows high and wide — this is what differentiates it from a back row
instructions: Attach a straight bar or rope handle to a low cable pulley and sit on the bench or floor facing the machine with feet braced.
Grasp the handle with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with your chest lifted and a slight backward lean.
Pull the handle toward your upper chest and neck area, driving your elbows high and wide.
Squeeze your rear deltoids and upper traps hard at the top of the movement.
Slowly return the handle to the starting position, allowing a full stretch through the shoulders.
Maintain an upright torso throughout and avoid excessive body momentum.
tips: Keep elbows high and wide — this is what differentiates it from a back row
Pull to your upper chest or face level
If your elbows drop, the emphasis shifts from rear delts to lats
Lighter weight with strict form beats heavy weight with body English

name: Lu Raise
description:
**Named after Olympic weightlifter Lu Xiaojun, this hybrid raise combines a lateral raise with an external rotation at the top.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

It trains the lateral and rear deltoids along with the rotator cuff in a single fluid motion, promoting both shoulder size and joint health.

**Pro tip:** The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized
instructions: Stand upright holding a light dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip.
Raise the dumbbells out to your sides until your upper arms are parallel to the floor, as in a standard lateral raise.
At the top position, externally rotate your forearms so the dumbbells point toward the ceiling, forming an 'L' shape with each arm.
Hold the externally rotated position briefly, feeling the contraction in your lateral delts and rotator cuff.
Reverse the rotation, then lower the dumbbells back to your sides under control.
Use light weight and prioritize smooth, controlled movement throughout the entire range.
tips: The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized
Scaption (30° forward of lateral) may be more shoulder-friendly than pure lateral raises
Y-raises emphasize the junction between lateral and posterior deltoid
Use lighter loads — these are precision movements

name: Lying Face Down Plate Neck Resistance
description:
**A neck extension exercise performed face down on a bench with a weight plate resting on the back of the head.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: erectors.

It directly strengthens the posterior neck muscles and upper traps, which are often neglected in standard training programs. Consistent neck training supports posture and reduces injury risk in contact activities.

**Pro tip:** Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
instructions: Lie face down on a flat bench with your head hanging just past the edge.
Place a folded towel on the back of your head for padding, then position a light weight plate on top, holding it in place with both hands.
Begin with your neck in a flexed position, chin tucked toward your chest.
Slowly extend your neck upward, raising your head against the resistance of the plate.
Pause briefly at the top when your head is in line with your spine.
Lower your head back to the starting position under control.
Use very conservative weight and high repetitions to protect the cervical spine.
tips: Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
Move through the full range of motion slowly
Never jerk or use momentum on neck exercises

name: Lying Face Up Plate Neck Resistance
description:
**A neck flexion exercise performed lying face up on a bench with a weight plate held on the forehead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: none.

It targets the sternocleidomastoid and anterior neck muscles, building balanced neck strength when paired with neck extension work. Strong anterior neck muscles are critical for head stability and injury prevention.

**Pro tip:** Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
instructions: Lie face up on a flat bench with your head extending just past the edge.
Place a folded towel on your forehead for comfort, then rest a light weight plate on top, securing it with both hands.
Start with your head hanging back in a slightly extended position.
Flex your neck forward, curling your chin toward your upper chest against the plate's resistance.
Squeeze at the top for a brief pause.
Slowly lower your head back to the starting position.
Keep the movement smooth and deliberate—never jerk or use momentum with neck exercises.
tips: Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
Move through the full range of motion slowly
Never jerk or use momentum on neck exercises

name: Lying One-Arm Lateral Raise
description:
**A unilateral lateral raise performed while lying on your side on an incline or flat bench.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The side-lying position changes the resistance curve so that tension peaks when the delt is in a shortened position, and it eliminates momentum and body English that often compromise standing lateral raises.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands
instructions: Lie on your side on a flat or slightly inclined bench, with your bottom arm stabilizing your body.
Hold a light dumbbell in your top hand, resting it against your upper thigh with your arm slightly bent.
Raise the dumbbell upward in an arc until your arm is roughly perpendicular to the floor.
Focus on leading with your elbow and contracting the lateral deltoid at the top.
Lower the dumbbell slowly back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other.
Avoid swinging or rotating your torso to generate momentum.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands
A slight forward lean can improve the resistance profile
Don't swing — if you need momentum, the weight is too heavy
Stop at or just above shoulder height

name: Lying Rear Delt Raise
description:
**A prone dumbbell raise that isolates the rear deltoids by removing lower body momentum from the equation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, erectors.

Lying chest-down on an incline bench places the shoulders in a stretched position at the bottom, maximizing the range of motion and ensuring constant tension on the posterior delts throughout the lift.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Set an incline bench to approximately 30-45 degrees and lie face down with your chest supported against the pad.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging straight down, palms facing each other.
With a slight bend in your elbows, raise both dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc.
Lift until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor, squeezing your rear delts at the top.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the hanging position, feeling a deep stretch in the posterior shoulders.
Keep your forehead resting on the bench and avoid lifting your chest off the pad.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Machine Lateral Raise
description:
**A machine-based lateral raise that provides consistent resistance throughout the entire range of motion thanks to its cam-based design.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The fixed movement path eliminates stabilization demands, allowing you to focus purely on contracting the lateral deltoid with minimal fatigue to surrounding structures.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the pad height so it contacts your outer arm just above the elbow
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the pivot point of the machine aligns with your shoulder joints.
Sit with your back flat against the pad and position your upper arms against the arm pads.
Grasp the handles lightly—avoid gripping too hard, which shifts tension to the forearms.
Raise your arms out to the sides by driving through your elbows until your upper arms reach shoulder height.
Hold the top position for a one-second squeeze.
Lower the weight under control, stopping just short of the stack touching to maintain tension.
Keep your torso still and avoid shrugging your shoulders upward.
tips: Adjust the pad height so it contacts your outer arm just above the elbow
The machine handles balance — you just focus on abduction force
Great for training to failure safely
One of the best options for lateral deltoid isolation

name: Machine Reverse Fly
description:
**A rear deltoid isolation exercise performed on a pec deck or dedicated reverse fly machine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, erectors.

The machine provides a smooth, consistent resistance profile that keeps the posterior deltoids under tension from a deep stretch to full contraction. It is one of the most joint-friendly ways to build rear delt size.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Adjust the machine handles to the rear-most position and set the seat so your shoulders align with the handles.
Sit facing the pad with your chest pressed firmly against it.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or pronated grip, arms extended in front of you.
Open your arms in a wide arc, squeezing your shoulder blades together as you pull the handles back.
Continue until your arms are roughly in line with your torso, feeling a strong contraction in the rear delts.
Return to the start position slowly, allowing a full stretch across the back of the shoulders.
Avoid using excessive weight that forces you to lean back or use momentum.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Machine Shoulder (Military) Press
description:
**A fixed-path overhead pressing movement that mimics the barbell military press while providing the stability and safety of a machine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

The guided track allows you to push closer to failure without needing a spotter, making it an efficient mass-builder for the anterior and lateral deltoids.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are at approximately ear level when seated.
Sit with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip at roughly shoulder width.
Press the handles overhead by extending your arms, stopping just short of full elbow lockout.
Lower the weight under control until your upper arms drop slightly below parallel.
Drive back up explosively while keeping your lower back pressed into the pad.
Exhale on the press and inhale on the descent.
tips: Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
The fixed path lets you push harder without worrying about balance
Good option for training close to failure safely
Focus on squeezing the deltoids rather than just moving the weight

name: Machine Shoulder Press
description:
**A seated overhead press performed on a converging or vertical press machine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

The machine stabilizes the load path, reducing the balance demands of free-weight pressing and letting you direct maximum effort into the deltoids and triceps. Ideal for high-volume shoulder work or training to failure safely.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
instructions: Set the seat height so the handles start at shoulder level or just below.
Sit back against the support pad with a natural arch in your lower back.
Grip the handles firmly with palms facing forward.
Press upward until your arms are nearly fully extended overhead.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower the handles back to the starting position in a controlled manner.
Stop the descent when you feel a comfortable stretch in the front delts—do not let the weight stack slam.
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect your spine.
tips: Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
The fixed path lets you push harder without worrying about balance
Good option for training close to failure safely
Focus on squeezing the deltoids rather than just moving the weight

name: Medicine Ball Scoop Throw
description:
**An explosive total-body power exercise where you scoop a medicine ball from between your legs and launch it overhead or forward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, quads, glutes, hamstrings.

It trains rapid force production through the hips, core, and shoulders in a coordinated chain. Primarily used for athletic performance rather than hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball with both hands in front of your hips.
Hinge at the hips and bend your knees to lower the ball between your legs.
Explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles while simultaneously swinging the ball upward.
Release the ball at the top of the arc, throwing it as high or as far forward as the drill requires.
Follow through with your arms and rise onto your toes.
Retrieve the ball, reset your stance, and repeat.
Focus on generating power from the lower body rather than muscling the ball with your arms.

The shoulders will fatigue as part of the full-body effort
Keep form consistent even as fatigue accumulates
Useful for work capacity and general fitness

name: Neck-SMR
description:
**A self-muscle and tissue release technique for the neck and upper trap region using a foam roller, lacrosse ball, or similar tool.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: none.

It helps alleviate tension, improve tissue quality, and restore range of motion in the cervical and upper thoracic area. Best used as part of a warm-up or cool-down routine.

**Pro tip:** Hold stretches for 20-30 seconds
instructions: Lie on your back and place a foam roller or lacrosse ball beneath the base of your skull where the neck meets the traps.
Allow your head to relax and let gravity press the tissue into the tool.
Slowly turn your head side to side, pausing on any tender spots for 20-30 seconds.
Gradually reposition the ball or roller along the upper traps and sides of the neck.
Breathe deeply and consciously relax the muscles being worked.
Spend 1-2 minutes per side, avoiding direct pressure on the spine itself.
Stop immediately if you feel any sharp pain, numbness, or tingling.
tips: Hold stretches for 20-30 seconds
For SMR, apply gentle pressure on tender spots
Perform before or after trap training

name: One-Arm Incline Lateral Raise
description:
**A lateral raise performed while leaning against an incline bench, which shifts the resistance curve to load the lateral deltoid more heavily in the stretched position at the bottom of the rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

This variation increases the effective range of motion compared to standing raises and reduces the ability to cheat with momentum.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 55-65 degrees and stand beside it, leaning your non-working side into the pad.
Hold a dumbbell in your outside hand with your arm hanging straight down.
With a slight bend in your elbow, raise the dumbbell out to your side in a smooth arc.
Lift until your arm is approximately parallel to the floor or slightly above.
Pause at the top and focus on the lateral delt contraction.
Lower the weight slowly, letting the dumbbell travel past your body to get a full stretch at the bottom.
Complete all reps on one side, then switch.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands
A slight forward lean can improve the resistance profile
Don't swing — if you need momentum, the weight is too heavy
Stop at or just above shoulder height

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Clean and Jerk
description:
**A full-body kettlebell lift that combines a single-arm clean to the rack position with an overhead jerk.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps, glutes, hamstrings, quads, forearms, erectors.

It demands coordination, power, and shoulder stability across multiple joints. While not optimal for hypertrophy, it builds functional strength, cardiovascular endurance, and explosive athleticism.

**Pro tip:** Master each component separately before combining them
instructions: Stand over a kettlebell with feet hip-width apart and hinge down to grip it with one hand.
Clean the kettlebell to the rack position by driving through your hips and pulling the bell close to your body, catching it at your shoulder with your elbow tucked.
Dip your knees slightly, then explosively extend your legs to drive the kettlebell overhead.
Punch your hand through at the top and lock out your arm with the kettlebell balanced over your shoulder.
Lower the kettlebell back to the rack position under control.
From the rack, guide the bell back down between your legs to begin the next rep.
Keep your core braced and your wrist neutral throughout the movement.
tips: Master each component separately before combining them
The clean positions the bar for the press — don't rush the transition
These are power and conditioning movements, not hypertrophy exercises
Fatigue accumulates fast — keep reps low

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Jerk
description:
**A ballistic overhead lift starting from the kettlebell rack position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, calves, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

The jerk uses a quick dip-and-drive of the legs to launch the bell overhead, followed by a second dip to catch it at lockout. It develops overhead power, shoulder stability, and total-body coordination with less shoulder fatigue than strict pressing.

**Pro tip:** This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position—bell resting against your forearm, elbow tight to your ribs.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly soft.
Perform a short, sharp dip by bending your knees a few inches.
Immediately drive upward through your legs, using the momentum to propel the kettlebell overhead.
As the bell rises, drop under it by bending your knees again and punch your arm to full lockout.
Stand up to complete the lift with the kettlebell stable overhead.
Lower the bell back to the rack position and reset before the next rep.
tips: This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
The legs do most of the work — the arms guide and stabilize
Practice the footwork separately before adding heavy loads
Not recommended as a primary deltoid hypertrophy exercise

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Military Press To The Side
description:
**A strict single-arm kettlebell press performed with a slight lateral lean away from the working arm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, abs, traps.

This offset pressing angle increases demand on the lateral deltoid and obliques while building unilateral overhead strength. The kettlebell's offset center of mass also challenges grip and wrist stability.

**Pro tip:** Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position on one side, elbow tucked against your ribs.
Brace your core and create a slight lateral lean away from the kettlebell side.
Press the kettlebell overhead in a path that angles slightly to the side rather than straight up.
Lock out your arm fully with the bicep near your ear.
Lower the kettlebell back to the rack position under control.
Maintain the lateral brace through your obliques for the entire set.
Complete all reps on one side before switching arms.

The bell sits on the back of your forearm — don't fight it, work with it
Press in a groove that feels natural for your shoulder
Grip hard to improve rotator cuff co-contraction

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Para Press
description:
**A single-arm kettlebell overhead press performed with a neutral or parallel grip, keeping the palm facing inward throughout the movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

This grip orientation is generally more shoulder-friendly and shifts emphasis slightly toward the anterior deltoid while still heavily involving the lateral head and triceps.

**Pro tip:** Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position with a neutral grip—palm facing your midline.
Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and your free hand at your side or on your hip.
Press the kettlebell straight overhead while maintaining the neutral wrist position.
Fully extend your arm at the top, keeping the bell balanced over your shoulder joint.
Lower the kettlebell back to the rack in a controlled manner.
Avoid flaring your elbow outward—keep it tracking forward.
Perform all prescribed reps, then switch to the opposite arm.
tips: Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
The bell sits on the back of your forearm — don't fight it, work with it
Press in a groove that feels natural for your shoulder
Grip hard to improve rotator cuff co-contraction

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Push Press
description:
**A single-arm overhead press that uses a brief leg dip to generate initial momentum, allowing you to handle heavier loads than a strict press.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, calves.

The push press bridges the gap between strict pressing and jerking, building both overhead strength and power while training the shoulders through a full range of motion.

**Pro tip:** The dip should be short and sharp — just enough to generate momentum
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position with your elbow tight to your body.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent.
Dip your knees quickly by dropping two to three inches, keeping your torso vertical.
Immediately drive upward through your legs and use that momentum to press the kettlebell overhead.
Lock out your arm at the top with the bell stable over your shoulder.
Lower the kettlebell back to the rack position under control—do not drop it.
Reset your stance and repeat, maintaining a rhythmic dip-drive tempo.

Catch the bar at lockout with straight arms
The leg drive handles the bottom — your delts finish the top
Better for strength and power than pure hypertrophy

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Snatch
description:
**A dynamic full-body kettlebell movement that takes the bell from between the legs to overhead lockout in one explosive motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: traps, hamstrings, erectors, triceps, forearms, quads.

The snatch is a hip-dominant power exercise that builds posterior chain explosiveness, grip endurance, and shoulder stability. It is a staple of kettlebell sport and conditioning programs.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Stand over a kettlebell with feet slightly wider than hip-width and hinge to grip it with one hand.
Hike the kettlebell back between your legs, loading your hips and hamstrings.
Explosively snap your hips forward, driving the kettlebell upward close to your body.
As the bell passes chest height, punch your hand through the handle so the kettlebell rolls smoothly onto the back of your forearm at lockout.
Finish with your arm fully extended overhead, bicep by your ear.
Pull the kettlebell back down by rotating your hand over the top and guiding it between your legs for the next rep.
Keep your core tight and avoid hyperextending your lower back at the top.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Split Jerk
description:
**An advanced kettlebell jerk variation where you split your feet into a lunge stance as you drive the bell overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

The split catch position allows you to get under heavier loads and develops unilateral leg stability alongside overhead pressing power. It closely mirrors the footwork used in Olympic weightlifting jerks.

**Pro tip:** This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position on one side.
Dip your knees with your torso upright, then explosively drive through your legs.
As the kettlebell launches upward, split your feet—front foot forward, back foot behind—dropping into a partial lunge.
Catch the kettlebell at full arm lockout while in the split stance.
Recover by stepping your front foot back, then your rear foot forward, returning to a standing position with the bell overhead.
Lower the kettlebell to the rack and reset.
Alternate your lead leg each set or as prescribed to maintain balance.

The legs do most of the work — the arms guide and stabilize

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Split Snatch
description:
**A kettlebell snatch variation that incorporates a split-stance catch, combining the explosive hip drive of a standard snatch with the footwork of a split jerk.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: traps, hamstrings, quads, erectors, forearms.

This advanced movement develops total-body power, coordination, and unilateral stability. It is primarily used for athletic development and kettlebell sport training.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Stand over a kettlebell and grip it with one hand, hinging at the hips.
Hike the bell between your legs to load the posterior chain.
Explosively extend your hips to drive the kettlebell upward.
As the bell rises past chest height, split your feet into a lunge position while punching your hand through the handle to catch the bell overhead.

Lower the kettlebell back between your legs and repeat.

Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: One-Arm Side Laterals
description:
**A unilateral dumbbell lateral raise performed one arm at a time, allowing you to focus entirely on the working deltoid.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

Training each side independently helps identify and correct strength imbalances while enabling a slightly greater range of motion and mind-muscle connection than bilateral raises.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in one hand at your side, palm facing your thigh.
Place your free hand on your hip or hold a sturdy object for balance.
With a slight bend in your elbow, raise the dumbbell out to your side in a controlled arc.
Lift until your arm reaches shoulder height, leading with your elbow rather than your hand.
Hold the top position for a brief squeeze.
Lower the dumbbell back to your side slowly, resisting gravity on the way down.

A slight forward lean can improve the resistance profile
Don't swing — if you need momentum, the weight is too heavy
Stop at or just above shoulder height

name: Overhead Press
description:
**The barbell overhead press is a foundational compound lift for building shoulder mass and upper-body pressing strength.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps, abs.

Performed standing, it demands core stability and full-body tension to drive the bar from the front rack to overhead lockout. It is one of the most effective movements for developing the anterior and lateral deltoids.

**Pro tip:** Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
instructions: Set a barbell in a rack at upper-chest height and grip it just outside shoulder width with an overhand grip.
Unrack the bar and step back, positioning it across your front deltoids and upper chest with elbows slightly in front of the bar.
Brace your core, squeeze your glutes, and press the bar straight overhead.
Move your head slightly back as the bar passes your face, then push it forward once the bar clears your forehead.
Lock out your arms fully with the bar balanced over the middle of your feet.
Lower the bar back to the front rack position under control.
Avoid excessive back arching—if you need to lean back significantly, reduce the weight.

Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: Pike Push-Up
description:
**A bodyweight pressing exercise performed in an inverted V position that shifts the load onto the shoulders rather than the chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, chest.

The pike push-up serves as a progression toward handstand push-ups and is an effective shoulder builder when equipment is unavailable. Elevating the feet increases difficulty further.

**Pro tip:** Wall-assisted is fine — the deltoid stimulus is the same
instructions: Start in a standard push-up position, then walk your hands back and push your hips high into the air, forming an inverted V shape.
Position your hands shoulder-width apart with fingers pointing forward.
Keep your legs as straight as possible and your head between your arms.
Bend your elbows to lower the top of your head toward the floor between your hands.
Press back up to the starting pike position by extending your arms.
Maintain the high-hip position throughout—do not let your hips sag.
To increase difficulty, elevate your feet on a bench or box.

The bottom is the hardest part, which is where the deltoids are most stretched
Keep your core tight to prevent arching

name: Plate Front Raise
description:
**A front deltoid isolation exercise performed by raising a weight plate from thigh level to shoulder height or above.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest.

Gripping the plate with both hands at the sides forces a neutral hand position and engages the forearms, while the wide grip subtly activates the upper chest. It is a simple yet effective anterior delt builder.

**Pro tip:** Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
instructions: Stand upright holding a weight plate with both hands at the 3 and 9 o'clock positions, arms extended in front of your thighs.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement.
Raise the plate in front of you in a smooth arc until it reaches shoulder height or slightly above.
Pause at the top and squeeze the front deltoids.
Lower the plate back to the starting position under control.
Avoid swinging your torso or using hip momentum to lift the plate.
Keep your wrists firm and the plate level throughout each repetition.

The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most

name: Power Partials
description:
**A partial-range lateral raise variation that targets the lateral deltoids through the strongest portion of the movement arc.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

By limiting the range of motion to the mid-range where mechanical advantage peaks, you can handle heavier loads and accumulate significant tension on the side delts without excessive fatigue from the weakest points of the lift.

**Pro tip:** The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a slight bend in the elbows.
Raise the dumbbells outward to roughly 30 degrees from your torso to begin the working range.
From that starting angle, drive the dumbbells up to approximately shoulder height using controlled force.
Pause briefly at the top of the partial range, squeezing the lateral delts.
Lower back to the 30-degree starting position under control and repeat for the prescribed reps.

Y-raises emphasize the junction between lateral and posterior deltoid
