name: Prone I-Y-T Raise
description:
**A multi-angle shoulder raise performed face-down on an incline bench that systematically works the rear deltoids, lower traps, and mid-traps through three distinct arm paths.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, erectors.

Each letter shape—I, Y, and T—shifts emphasis across the posterior shoulder complex, making this an efficient movement for scapular health and upper-back development.

**Pro tip:** The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized
instructions: Lie face down on an incline bench set to roughly 30–45 degrees, holding a light dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging straight down.
For the I-raise, lift both arms straight overhead in line with your body, thumbs pointing up, then lower with control.
For the Y-raise, lift both arms at roughly a 45-degree angle from your head to form a Y shape, then lower slowly.
For the T-raise, lift both arms directly out to the sides perpendicular to your torso, forming a T shape, then lower.
Perform the prescribed number of reps for each letter before moving to the next, or alternate through all three as one combined rep.
Keep your chest pressed into the bench throughout and use light loads—this movement is about precision, not heavy weight.
tips: The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized
Scaption (30° forward of lateral) may be more shoulder-friendly than pure lateral raises
Y-raises emphasize the junction between lateral and posterior deltoid
Use lighter loads — these are precision movements

name: Push Press
description:
**An explosive overhead pressing movement that uses a brief leg drive to initiate the barbell past the sticking point, allowing you to handle loads well beyond your strict press capacity.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, traps.

The push press develops overhead strength, power output, and full-body coordination while heavily loading the deltoids and triceps through the lockout phase.

**Pro tip:** The dip should be short and sharp — just enough to generate momentum
instructions: Unrack a barbell at the front rack position with the bar resting across your front deltoids and collarbone, hands just outside shoulder width.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, brace your core, and dip your knees to roughly a quarter-squat depth.
Explosively extend your hips and knees to drive the bar upward off your shoulders.
As the bar clears your head, press through with your shoulders and triceps to full lockout overhead.
Lower the bar back to the front rack position under control, absorbing the weight with a slight knee bend.
Reset your stance and repeat, ensuring each dip is quick and shallow rather than turning it into a full squat.
tips: The dip should be short and sharp — just enough to generate momentum
Catch the bar at lockout with straight arms
The leg drive handles the bottom — your delts finish the top
Better for strength and power than pure hypertrophy

name: Push Press - Behind the Neck
description:
**A behind-the-neck variation of the push press that shifts emphasis toward the lateral and rear deltoids by positioning the bar behind the head.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, traps, calves.

The leg drive assists the initial press, enabling supramaximal overhead loading relative to a strict behind-the-neck press. This variation demands adequate shoulder mobility and rewards it with exceptional delt recruitment.

**Pro tip:** The dip should be short and sharp — just enough to generate momentum
instructions: Position a barbell across your upper traps as you would for a back squat, gripping the bar with a moderately wide overhead press grip.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, core braced, and elbows angled slightly downward beneath the bar.
Perform a quick, shallow knee dip and then explosively drive upward through your legs.
Use the momentum to press the bar from behind your neck to full lockout overhead.
Lower the bar back to the rear rack position on your traps, cushioning the descent with a slight knee bend.
Only perform this variation if you have sufficient shoulder external rotation and thoracic mobility to keep the movement pain-free.
tips: The dip should be short and sharp — just enough to generate momentum
Catch the bar at lockout with straight arms
The leg drive handles the bottom — your delts finish the top
Better for strength and power than pure hypertrophy

name: Rack Delivery
description:
**A barbell transition movement that trains the ability to clean or muscle the bar from a hang position into the front rack.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, forearms, biceps.

It develops the upright row pattern, high-pull mechanics, and front rack receiving position, making it a useful skill-transfer drill for Olympic lifting derivatives while building trap and deltoid strength under moderate loads.

**Pro tip:** Master each component separately before combining them
instructions: Stand holding a barbell at hip height with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Initiate the movement by shrugging your traps and pulling your elbows high and wide, drawing the bar up your torso.
As the bar reaches upper-chest height, quickly rotate your elbows under and forward to catch the bar in a front rack position across your front delts.
Pause briefly in the rack position with elbows high and the bar secure on your shoulders.
Reverse the motion by lowering the bar back to the hang position at your hips under control.
Focus on smooth elbow turnover speed rather than heavy loading—this is a technique-driven movement.
tips: Master each component separately before combining them
The clean positions the bar for the press — don't rush the transition
These are power and conditioning movements, not hypertrophy exercises
Fatigue accumulates fast — keep reps low

name: Resistance Band Lateral Raise
description:
**A lateral raise performed with a resistance band that provides increasing tension as the arms rise, creating an ascending resistance profile.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

While the bottom of the movement is relatively unloaded, the peak contraction at the top is intensely challenging, making this a convenient option for high-rep shoulder work and warm-ups when dumbbells are unavailable.

**Pro tip:** Stand on the band center for even resistance
instructions: Stand on the center of a resistance band with both feet, holding one end in each hand at your sides.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and your palms facing inward toward your thighs.
Raise both arms out to the sides in a wide arc until they reach approximately shoulder height.
Hold the top position for a one-count squeeze, feeling the band tension peak on your lateral delts.
Lower your arms back to your sides slowly, resisting the band on the way down.
Use a band tension that allows you to complete all reps with strict form—momentum defeats the purpose here.
tips: Stand on the band center for even resistance
The bottom portion will feel very light — that's the nature of bands
The top is where the real work happens
Good for warm-ups, pump work, or when no other equipment is available

name: Return Push from Stance
description:
**A dynamic bodyweight pressing movement performed from a staggered or athletic stance that combines a push-up-like pressing pattern with a return to standing.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest, triceps, abs.

It emphasizes shoulder endurance, core stability, and upper-body coordination, making it more of a conditioning and movement-quality drill than a pure hypertrophy exercise.

**Pro tip:** These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
instructions: Begin in an athletic stance with one foot slightly ahead of the other, arms at your sides.
Drop your hands to the floor and kick or step your feet back into a push-up position.
Perform a controlled push-up, keeping your elbows at roughly a 45-degree angle from your torso.
Push back up explosively and step or hop your feet forward to return to the staggered stance.
Stand fully upright to complete one rep, then immediately begin the next.
Maintain a tight core throughout and avoid letting your hips sag during the push-up portion.
tips: These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
The shoulders will fatigue as part of the full-body effort
Keep form consistent even as fatigue accumulates
Useful for work capacity and general fitness

name: Reverse Flyes
description:
**A bent-over dumbbell fly that isolates the rear deltoids and mid-traps by sweeping the arms outward against gravity from a stretched starting position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, erectors.

The hinged torso angle places the rear delts under meaningful tension at the bottom of the rep, and the bell-shaped resistance curve ensures strong stimulus through the mid-range where the muscle is most active.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging straight down and palms facing each other.
Maintain a flat back with a slight bend in your knees for stability.
Raise both dumbbells outward in a wide arc, leading with your elbows slightly, until your arms are roughly in line with your shoulders.
Squeeze your rear delts and mid-traps hard at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbells back to the hanging start position under control, feeling the stretch across the back of your shoulders.
Avoid jerking the weights up or using excessive body English—keep the torso angle fixed throughout.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Reverse Flyes With External Rotation
description:
**A reverse fly variation that adds an external rotation component at the top of each rep, recruiting the infraspinatus and teres minor alongside the rear deltoids.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, erectors.

This dual-action movement is excellent for rotator cuff health and posterior shoulder development, making it a staple for prehab programming and balanced shoulder training.

**Pro tip:** Use light weight — these are small muscles that don't need heavy loading
instructions: Hinge forward at the hips with a flat back, holding a light dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging down and palms facing each other.
Raise the dumbbells outward to the sides as in a standard reverse fly.
At the top of the fly, rotate your forearms upward so your thumbs point toward the ceiling, externally rotating at the shoulder joint.
Hold the externally rotated position for a brief pause, feeling the contraction deep in the rear shoulder.
Reverse the rotation, then lower the dumbbells back to the start under control.
Use lighter weight than standard reverse flyes—the external rotation component is the limiting factor and demands precision.
tips: Use light weight — these are small muscles that don't need heavy loading
Keep your elbow pinned to your side at 90 degrees
Slow, controlled reps are more effective than fast ones
Do these regularly if you press heavy — they protect your shoulders

name: Reverse Machine Flyes
description:
**A machine-based rear delt fly that provides a consistent resistance profile throughout the entire range of motion, eliminating the momentum and stability demands of free-weight variations.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The fixed path and stretched loading position make this one of the most efficient rear delt exercises available, delivering excellent stimulus with minimal systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Sit facing the pad of a reverse fly machine with your chest pressed firmly against it and adjust the seat height so the handles align with your shoulders.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or pronated grip, arms extended in front of you at shoulder height.
Drive your arms rearward in a wide arc, squeezing your rear delts as your hands travel as far back as your mobility allows.
Hold the fully contracted position for a one-second squeeze.
Return the handles forward slowly, allowing a deep stretch across the rear delts at the start position.
Keep your chest glued to the pad throughout—lifting off the pad shifts work away from the rear delts.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Reverse Pec Deck Fly
description:
**A rear deltoid isolation exercise performed on the pec deck machine in reverse orientation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, erectors.

By sitting facing the backrest and driving the handles rearward, you target the posterior deltoids with a smooth, guided resistance curve. The stretched position at the start of each rep and the machine's stability make this a top-tier choice for rear delt hypertrophy with minimal joint stress.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Sit facing the back pad of a pec deck machine, adjusting the seat so the handles are at shoulder height when your arms are extended forward.
Grip the handles with palms facing down or inward, depending on the machine design, and press your chest into the pad.
Open your arms in a wide reverse fly arc, driving the handles rearward by contracting your rear deltoids.
Continue until your arms are roughly in line with your torso or slightly behind it.
Reverse the motion slowly, letting the weight stack pull your arms forward into a full stretch across the rear delts.
Maintain constant tension by not letting the weight stack touch down between reps.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Round The World Shoulder Stretch
description:
**A dynamic shoulder mobility drill that takes the arms through a full circumduction pattern—tracing a large circle from front to overhead to behind the body and back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest.

This movement progressively opens the shoulder capsule, stretches the pecs and anterior delts, and improves overhead range of motion, making it an ideal warm-up or cooldown exercise.

**Pro tip:** Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
instructions: Stand tall with your arms at your sides and shoulders relaxed.
Raise both arms forward and overhead in a smooth arc, keeping them as straight as possible.
Continue the arc behind your body as far as your shoulder mobility allows, tracing a full circle.
Bring the arms back around to the starting position to complete one full rotation.
Perform the prescribed number of circles in one direction, then reverse the direction.
Move slowly and breathe deeply—if you feel pinching, reduce the circle size until mobility improves.
tips: Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
Move through the range slowly and with control
Never force range of motion — work at the edge of comfort
Consistency matters more than intensity for mobility work

name: Scapular Pull-Up
description:
**A partial pull-up movement that isolates scapular depression and retraction by moving only through the initial few inches of the pull-up without bending the elbows.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: lats, shoulders.

This exercise strengthens the lower traps, serratus anterior, and scapular stabilizers, building the foundational shoulder blade control needed for heavy pulling movements and healthy overhead mechanics.

**Pro tip:** Start from a dead hang with fully elevated scapulae
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip, arms fully extended and body relaxed in a dead hang.
Without bending your elbows, pull your shoulder blades down and together, lifting your body slightly upward.
You should rise only a few inches—the movement comes entirely from scapular depression, not arm flexion.
Hold the top position for a one-to-two-second squeeze, feeling your lower traps engage.
Relax your scapulae to return to the full dead hang position.
Keep your core lightly braced to prevent excessive swinging throughout the set.
tips: Start from a dead hang with fully elevated scapulae
Depress your scapulae without bending your elbows
The range of motion is small — focus on quality

name: Seated Barbell Military Press
description:
**The classic seated overhead barbell press performed with a strict upright torso on a military bench or upright seat.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

Removing leg drive and limiting torso lean isolates the anterior and lateral deltoids more directly than standing variations, making it a reliable strength builder for the shoulders with strong carryover to all pressing movements.

**Pro tip:** Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
instructions: Sit on an upright bench with back support, unrack a barbell at collarbone height, and hold it with hands just outside shoulder width.
Plant your feet flat on the floor and press your upper back firmly into the bench pad.
Press the barbell overhead in a slight arc, moving your head back just enough to clear the bar path.
Lock out the bar directly over your shoulder joints with arms fully extended.
Lower the bar under control back to your upper chest or collarbone, letting your elbows drift slightly forward.
Avoid excessive back arching—if your lower back lifts off the bench, the weight is too heavy.
tips: Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: Seated Barbell Press
description:
**A seated overhead press using a barbell that allows for heavy loading of the deltoids and triceps while the bench provides spinal support.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

Sitting eliminates lower-body contribution, forcing the shoulders to handle the full workload. This is a foundational pressing movement for building overhead strength and shoulder mass.

**Pro tip:** Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
instructions: Sit on a bench with the backrest set to 90 degrees or just slightly reclined, and unrack the barbell at shoulder height.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over elbows.
Brace your core and press the bar straight up, guiding it in a path that ends directly over your head.
Fully extend your arms at the top without shrugging your shoulders up toward your ears.
Lower the barbell in a controlled manner to your upper chest, pausing briefly before the next rep.
Keep your ribcage down and avoid flaring your elbows excessively wide to protect the shoulder joint.
tips: Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: Seated Bent-Over Rear Delt Raise
description:
**A seated variation of the bent-over rear delt raise that limits lower-body momentum and spinal movement by anchoring your torso over your thighs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, erectors.

The seated position enforces a deeper hip hinge and places the rear deltoids under a pronounced stretch at the bottom of each rep, improving muscle fiber recruitment through the lengthened position.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with your feet together on the floor, holding a dumbbell in each hand.
Lean forward from the hips until your chest is close to or resting on your thighs, letting the dumbbells hang beneath your legs with palms facing each other.
Raise both dumbbells outward and upward in a wide arc, leading with your elbows.
Lift until your arms are roughly parallel with the floor, squeezing the rear delts at the top.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the hanging position, feeling a stretch across the posterior shoulders.
Keep your torso pinned against your thighs throughout—sitting up during the lift means the weight is too heavy.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Seated Cable Shoulder Press
description:
**An overhead press performed seated between two low cable pulleys, providing smooth and constant tension throughout the entire pressing range.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

Unlike barbell or dumbbell presses where gravity creates variable resistance, the cable's line of pull maintains consistent loading on the deltoids from the bottom stretch to full lockout, making it a joint-friendly pressing option with excellent time under tension.

**Pro tip:** Set the pulleys at shoulder height for a pressing angle
instructions: Position a bench between two low cable pulleys and sit with your back supported, grabbing a handle in each hand at shoulder height.
Start with your elbows bent at roughly 90 degrees, handles at ear level, and palms facing forward.
Press both handles overhead simultaneously, extending your arms fully without locking your elbows aggressively.
Squeeze your deltoids at the top for a brief pause.
Lower the handles back to the starting position at ear level, maintaining tension on the cables throughout.
Keep your back flat against the bench and avoid arching excessively as the weight gets challenging.

The constant tension means no rest at any point in the ROM
Control the eccentric — cables reward slow negatives
Adjust cable angle to bias anterior vs lateral deltoid

name: Seated Dumbbell Press
description:
**A staple overhead pressing movement performed seated with dumbbells that allows each arm to work independently, correcting strength imbalances while building substantial deltoid and tricep mass.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

The free-weight path demands greater stabilizer recruitment than machine presses, and the seated position removes leg drive to keep the focus squarely on the shoulders.

**Pro tip:** Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
instructions: Sit on an upright bench with back support, holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward.
Plant your feet firmly on the floor and press your shoulder blades into the bench.
Press both dumbbells overhead in a converging arc until they nearly touch at the top with arms fully extended.
Lower the dumbbells under control back to shoulder height, letting your elbows drop to roughly 90 degrees or slightly below.
Avoid bouncing at the bottom—pause briefly before pressing the next rep.
If you find yourself leaning back excessively, reduce the weight to maintain an upright pressing posture.

Allow a natural arc as you press — don't force a straight line
Control the eccentric to maintain tension on the deltoids
Each arm works independently, exposing and correcting imbalances

name: Seated Front Deltoid
description:
**A seated front raise variation using bodyweight resistance or isometric tension to target the anterior deltoid.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest.

Performing the movement seated eliminates momentum from the lower body and isolates the front delt through shoulder flexion. This is a useful activation drill or high-rep burnout exercise for the front of the shoulder.

**Pro tip:** Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
instructions: Sit upright on a bench or chair with your feet flat on the floor and your arms at your sides.
With palms facing downward or inward, raise one or both arms straight out in front of you to shoulder height.
Keep your elbows straight but not locked, and lead the movement from the front of your shoulder.
Hold the top position for a two-second contraction, focusing on the anterior delt.
Lower your arms back to your sides in a slow, controlled manner.

Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps

name: Seated Head Harness Neck Resistance
description:
**A neck strengthening exercise performed seated using a head harness with attached resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: none.

It targets the upper traps, sternocleidomastoid, and deep cervical muscles through controlled neck flexion and extension patterns. Consistent neck training builds a thicker neck, improves posture, and can reduce the risk of cervical injuries in contact activities.

**Pro tip:** Use light resistance and high reps for neck work
instructions: Sit on a bench and secure a head harness with the appropriate weight attached, letting the load hang in front of you.
Place your hands on your knees for stability and lean your torso slightly forward.
Begin with your chin tucked toward your chest, then extend your neck upward and backward against the resistance until your head is in a neutral or slightly extended position.
Lower your head back toward your chest slowly, controlling the weight on the way down.
Perform all reps in a smooth, deliberate rhythm—never jerk or snap your neck against the load.
Start with very light weight and progress gradually, as the cervical spine is sensitive to overloading.

Move through the full range of motion slowly
Never jerk or use momentum on neck exercises

name: Seated Side Lateral Raise
description:
**A lateral raise performed from a seated position that eliminates hip swing and lower-body momentum, forcing the lateral deltoids to handle the load with strict form.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

Sitting down shortens the kinetic chain and makes cheating nearly impossible, resulting in more honest reps and better isolation of the side delts compared to the standing version.

**Pro tip:** Lead with your elbows, not your hands
instructions: Sit upright on the edge of a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing inward.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and your chest lifted.
Raise both dumbbells outward and upward in a smooth arc until your arms reach shoulder height.
Lead with your elbows rather than your hands—imagine pouring water from a pitcher at the top.
Lower the dumbbells back to your sides under control, resisting gravity on the way down.
Avoid shrugging your traps to lift the weight; if your shoulders creep toward your ears, reduce the load.

A slight forward lean can improve the resistance profile
Don't swing — if you need momentum, the weight is too heavy
Stop at or just above shoulder height

name: See-Saw Press (Alternating Side Press)
description:
**An alternating overhead dumbbell press where one arm presses up as the other lowers, creating a continuous see-saw rhythm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, abs, traps.

This pattern keeps the deltoids under constant tension, challenges core anti-lateral-flexion stability, and allows a slight body lean that can help drive heavier loads overhead compared to strict simultaneous pressing.

**Pro tip:** Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
instructions: Stand or sit holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height in the standard press starting position.
Press one dumbbell overhead to full lockout while simultaneously lowering the other dumbbell back to shoulder height.
As the first arm reaches the top, the second arm should be arriving at the bottom—the two arms move in opposite directions continuously.
Allow a slight lateral lean toward the lowering side to generate a natural pressing rhythm.
Continue alternating without pausing, maintaining a steady cadence throughout the set.
Brace your core firmly to resist excessive side-to-side torso movement—the lean should be subtle, not dramatic.

Allow a natural arc as you press — don't force a straight line
Control the eccentric to maintain tension on the deltoids
Each arm works independently, exposing and correcting imbalances

name: Shoulder Circles
description:
**A simple shoulder mobility and warm-up exercise that moves the shoulder joint through its full circumduction range by tracing circles with the arms.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

This movement lubricates the joint capsule, activates the rotator cuff and deltoid musculature, and prepares the shoulders for heavier pressing or overhead work in the session ahead.

**Pro tip:** Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and extend both arms straight out to the sides at shoulder height.
Begin making small forward circles with both arms simultaneously, keeping your arms straight.
Gradually increase the diameter of the circles over several reps until you are making large, full-range circles.
After the prescribed number of forward circles, reverse direction and perform the same number of backward circles.
Keep your core engaged and avoid swaying your torso as the circles get larger.
This is a warm-up movement—focus on smooth, controlled motion rather than speed.

name: Shoulder Press - With Bands
description:
**An overhead press performed against resistance band tension that increases progressively as the arms extend, creating an ascending strength curve.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, traps.

The band provides minimal resistance at the bottom and maximum resistance at lockout, training explosive pressing power and lockout strength. It is a portable and joint-friendly alternative when barbells or dumbbells are unavailable.

**Pro tip:** Stand on the band with both feet for consistent anchor
instructions: Stand on the center of a resistance band with both feet approximately hip-width apart, holding one end of the band in each hand at shoulder height.
Position your hands just outside your shoulders with palms facing forward and elbows bent.
Press both hands overhead by extending your arms fully, driving against the increasing band tension.
Lock out at the top and hold for a brief squeeze before lowering.
Return your hands to shoulder height under control, resisting the band on the way down.
Choose a band thickness that makes the last few reps of each set genuinely challenging at the top.

name: Shoulder Raise
description:
**A bodyweight shoulder raise that targets the deltoids through arm elevation against gravity without external load.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

This exercise is useful for beginners building foundational shoulder strength, for rehabilitation contexts where external loading is contraindicated, or as a high-rep burnout finisher to flush the delts with blood after heavier pressing work.

**Pro tip:** The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized

name: Shoulder Stretch
description: **The tension eraser your desk-bound shoulders are begging for.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders. This simple bodyweight stretch loosens tight shoulder muscles and improves your range of motion. Hold each position gently and breathe deeply to let the tissue release. It's perfect as a warm-up or cool-down after any upper-body session. **Pro tip:** Never force the stretch past mild discomfort — ease into it over 20-30 seconds for the best results.
instructions: Stand tall with your feet shoulder-width apart and your arms relaxed at your sides.
Bring one arm across your chest at shoulder height and use the opposite hand to gently press it closer to your body.
Hold for 20-30 seconds while breathing slowly and deeply, feeling a gentle pull through the shoulder.
Release and repeat on the other side.
Perform 2-3 rounds per side, gradually increasing the stretch depth with each round.

Move through the range slowly and with control

name: Side Lateral Raise
description: **The move that builds shoulders wide enough to fill a doorframe.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, traps. This dumbbell isolation exercise targets the side head of your shoulder, which is responsible for that broad, capped look. You lift the weights out to your sides in a controlled arc, keeping constant tension on the muscle. **Pro tip:** Use a slight pause at the top to eliminate momentum and maximize the burn. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve means the exercise is hardest at shoulder height and easier at the bottom and top, so control the weight through the entire range.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing inward.
With a slight bend in your elbows, raise both dumbbells out to your sides until your arms are parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly at the top, keeping your wrists level with or slightly below your elbows.
Lower the dumbbells back down slowly, resisting gravity on the way down.
Repeat for your target number of reps without swinging your body.

name: Side Laterals to Front Raise
description: **A two-for-one shoulder sculptor that hits you from every angle.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, traps. This dumbbell combo exercise merges a lateral raise with a front raise in one fluid motion, targeting both the side and front heads of your shoulders. It's a time-efficient way to build well-rounded delts. **Pro tip:** Keep the weight light — the extended time under tension makes even small dumbbells feel heavy by the end of a set. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance profile peaks when your arms are parallel to the floor in both directions, so focus on controlling those midpoints.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Raise the dumbbells out to your sides until your arms are parallel to the floor.
Without lowering them, sweep your arms forward so the dumbbells meet in front of you at shoulder height.
Lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position in a controlled manner.

name: Side Neck Stretch

Gently tilt your head toward your right shoulder, bringing your ear closer to it.

name: Side Wrist Pull
description: **A sneaky stretch that unlocks hidden tension from your wrist to your shoulder.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, forearms. This bodyweight stretch uses a gentle pulling motion across your body to open up the wrist, forearm, and outer shoulder. It's especially useful if you spend a lot of time typing or gripping weights. **Pro tip:** Keep your pulling arm's shoulder relaxed and down — shrugging defeats the purpose of the stretch.

name: Single Arm Dumbbell Press
description: **One arm. One dumbbell. Double the core challenge.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, abs. This compound pressing movement forces your core to work overtime to keep you stable while one arm pushes a dumbbell overhead. It builds shoulder strength and exposes any left-to-right imbalances. **Pro tip:** Brace your core as if someone is about to poke your stomach — this keeps your torso from leaning sideways. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve means the press is hardest at the midpoint of the lift, so drive through that sticking point with steady force.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding one dumbbell at shoulder height with your palm facing forward.
Brace your core tightly to prevent your torso from tilting to one side.
Press the dumbbell straight overhead until your arm is fully extended.
Lower the dumbbell back to shoulder height under control.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other arm.
tips: Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
Allow a natural arc as you press — don't force a straight line
Control the eccentric to maintain tension on the deltoids
Each arm works independently, exposing and correcting imbalances
coaching_cues: Press and converge slightly at top

name: Single Dumbbell Raise
description: **Laser-focused isolation that lets you dial in each shoulder separately.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, traps. Using just one dumbbell at a time, this exercise lets you concentrate fully on the working shoulder without your stronger side compensating. It's excellent for fixing muscle imbalances. **Pro tip:** Hold onto a sturdy surface with your free hand to eliminate body sway and keep all the work in your shoulder. **Biomechanics:** Resistance peaks when your arm is parallel to the floor due to the bell-shaped curve, so slow down through that range for maximum muscle activation.
instructions: Stand upright holding a single dumbbell in one hand at your side, palm facing inward.
With a slight bend in your elbow, raise the dumbbell out to your side until your arm is parallel to the floor.
Pause at the top for a one-second count.
Lower the dumbbell slowly back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side, then switch hands and repeat.
tips: The angle of raise determines which deltoid head is emphasized
Scaption (30° forward of lateral) may be more shoulder-friendly than pure lateral raises
Y-raises emphasize the junction between lateral and posterior deltoid
Use lighter loads — these are precision movements

name: Single-Arm Linear Jammer
description: **An explosive press that bridges the gap between the gym and the playing field.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, chest, triceps, abs. This barbell-based compound movement mimics a powerful punching or pushing motion. You drive one end of a barbell from shoulder height to full extension using your legs and shoulder together. It builds functional, athletic power. **Pro tip:** Initiate the push from your legs and hips — your arm finishes the job, not the other way around. **Biomechanics:** The angled pressing path and bell-shaped resistance curve load the shoulder most at the midpoint, making it a joint-friendly alternative to strict overhead pressing.
instructions: Wedge one end of a barbell into a corner or landmine attachment. Load the free end with weight.
Stand facing the barbell and grip the loaded end at shoulder height with one hand.
Bend your knees slightly, then explosively extend your legs while pressing the barbell forward and upward.
Lock out your arm fully at the top of the movement.
Lower the barbell back to shoulder height under control and repeat for your target reps before switching arms.
tips: Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: Sled Overhead Backward Walk
description: **Walk backward, build forward — a full-body grind that torches everything.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, calves, traps, quads, glutes. This compound bodyweight-driven sled exercise has you holding the sled handles overhead while walking backward. It challenges shoulder stability, leg endurance, and coordination all at once. **Pro tip:** Take short, deliberate steps and keep your arms locked out — losing overhead position under fatigue is the most common mistake. **Biomechanics:** The flat resistance profile means the load stays constant throughout the walk, so pace yourself to maintain form over the full distance.
instructions: Load a sled with an appropriate weight and stand facing it.
Grip the sled handles and press them overhead with arms fully extended.
Begin walking backward in small, controlled steps while keeping the sled moving steadily.
Maintain a tall posture with your arms locked out overhead throughout the walk.
Continue for your target distance or time, then lower the handles carefully.
tips: These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
The shoulders will fatigue as part of the full-body effort
Keep form consistent even as fatigue accumulates
Useful for work capacity and general fitness

name: Sled Reverse Flye
description: **Turn a sled into a rear-delt demolition machine.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders (rear delts), traps. This unique bodyweight-resistance exercise uses sled straps to perform a reverse flye motion while leaning back. It places the rear delts under a deep stretch at the start, which can enhance muscle growth. **Pro tip:** Lean back further to increase difficulty — the more horizontal your body, the harder your rear delts work. **Biomechanics:** The stretched starting position combined with a bell-shaped resistance curve means the rear delts are loaded heavily at their longest length, which is ideal for muscle development.
instructions: Attach handles or straps to a sled or cable anchor point at about chest height.
Grip a handle in each hand and lean back with arms extended in front of you, creating tension in the straps.
With a slight bend in your elbows, pull your arms apart and out to your sides in a wide arc.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end of the movement.
Slowly return to the starting position with arms extended and repeat.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands — think about pulling elbows apart
A slight bend in the elbows reduces biceps involvement
The posterior deltoid is often undertrained relative to the anterior head
Machine and cable variants provide more constant tension

name: Smith Incline Shoulder Raise
description: **A gravity-defying angle that lights up your front delts like nothing else.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, chest, traps. Performed on an incline bench inside a Smith machine, this barbell isolation exercise changes the resistance angle to keep constant tension on the front and side shoulders. The fixed bar path lets you focus purely on the muscle contraction. **Pro tip:** Set the incline to about 45 degrees — too upright turns it into a standard raise, too flat shifts work to your chest. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve peaks at the midpoint of the raise, and the incline angle ensures your shoulders never fully unload at the bottom.
instructions: Set an incline bench inside a Smith machine at roughly 45 degrees and sit back against it.
Grip the barbell with an overhand grip at about shoulder width.
Unrack the bar and hold it with arms extended in front of your chest.
Raise the bar upward in a controlled arc until your arms are overhead or near it.
Lower the bar slowly back to the starting position and repeat.
tips: Lead with your elbows, not your hands
A slight forward lean can improve the resistance profile
Don't swing — if you need momentum, the weight is too heavy
Stop at or just above shoulder height

name: Smith Machine Behind the Back Shrug
description: **Hit your traps from an angle they've never felt before.** **Muscles worked:** traps, shoulders, forearms. This machine-based isolation exercise positions the bar behind your body, which shifts the emphasis to the middle and lower traps compared to a standard shrug. The Smith machine's fixed path keeps you stable so you can load heavier. **Pro tip:** Roll your shoulders slightly back at the top rather than straight up — this engages more of the mid-trap fibers. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve means the shrug is hardest at peak contraction, so hold the top position for a full second to maximize trap activation.
instructions: Stand inside a Smith machine with the bar positioned behind your glutes at arm's length.
Grip the bar with both hands using an overhand grip, slightly wider than hip width.
Unrack the bar and let it hang behind you with arms fully extended.
Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible.
Hold the top position briefly, then lower the bar back down under control and repeat.
tips: Keep the bar close to your body throughout
Shrug straight up without leaning forward
Use a smith machine if balance is an issue

name: Smith Machine One-Arm Upright Row
description: **A guided track that lets you row heavier without worrying about balance.** **Muscles worked:** traps, biceps, forearms, shoulders. This single-arm compound exercise on the Smith machine isolates one side at a time while the fixed bar path handles stability for you. It's great for building thick traps and identifying strength differences between sides. **Pro tip:** Lead with your elbow, not your hand — imagine pulling your elbow toward the ceiling for better trap engagement. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped curve loads the traps most when your elbow reaches shoulder height, so aim to hit that position on every rep.
instructions: Stand next to a Smith machine with the bar at thigh height.
Grip the bar with one hand using an overhand grip, centered on your body.
Unrack the bar and let it hang at arm's length in front of you.
Pull the bar upward by driving your elbow toward the ceiling until it reaches shoulder height.
Lower the bar slowly back to the starting position and complete all reps before switching arms.
tips: Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
Pull to chest height, not chin height
Lead with your elbows, not your hands

name: Smith Machine Overhead Press
description: **Fixed-path pressing that lets you chase heavy weights with confidence.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, chest. This compound Smith machine exercise removes the need to balance the bar, so you can focus entirely on pushing maximum weight overhead. It's a staple for building thick, powerful shoulders. **Pro tip:** Position the bench so the bar travels just in front of your face — pressing too far forward or behind shifts stress away from your shoulders. **Biomechanics:** The flat strength curve provides consistent resistance throughout the press, making it equally challenging at the bottom, middle, and top of the movement.
instructions: Sit on a bench inside a Smith machine with the bar at about chin height.
Grip the bar with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Unrack the bar and lower it to just above your upper chest.
Press the bar straight up until your arms are fully extended overhead.
Lower the bar back down under control and repeat for your target reps.
tips: Position the bench so the bar tracks in front of your face, not behind
The fixed path means you can push closer to failure safely
Adjust your seat position to find a comfortable pressing angle
Don't let the fixed path force your shoulders into an uncomfortable position
coaching_cues: Set up so bar clears your face

name: Smith Machine Overhead Shoulder Press
description: **A shoulder-building workhorse that takes the guesswork out of bar path.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, chest. This machine-based compound press is ideal for lifters who want to push heavy without a spotter. The guided track ensures a consistent path every rep, letting you train close to failure safely. **Pro tip:** Adjust the seat height so the bar starts at chin level — this gives you the full range of motion without straining your rotator cuff at the bottom. **Biomechanics:** The flat resistance profile means there's no easy portion of the lift, so maintain steady pressing speed from bottom to top.
instructions: Sit on the machine's seat with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grip the handles or bar at shoulder width with an overhand grip.
Unrack the weight and lower it to just above your collarbone.
Press the weight straight overhead until your arms lock out.
Lower it back down with control and repeat.
tips: Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
The fixed path lets you push harder without worrying about balance
Good option for training close to failure safely
Focus on squeezing the deltoids rather than just moving the weight

name: Smith Machine Shoulder Press
description: **Guided precision for shoulders that demand progressive overload.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, chest. This Smith machine compound press is perfect for systematically adding weight week after week because the fixed bar path eliminates stabilization demands. It's a go-to for hypertrophy-focused shoulder training. **Pro tip:** Try a standing version occasionally — it recruits more core and can break through pressing plateaus. **Biomechanics:** The flat strength curve delivers uniform resistance, so you won't find a rest point during the rep — keep pressing smoothly through the full range.
instructions: Position a bench inside the Smith machine and sit with your back supported.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder width, palms facing forward.
Unrack the bar and lower it to the top of your chest or chin level.
Drive the bar overhead by extending your arms fully.
Lower the bar back to the starting position under control and repeat.
tips: Position the bench so the bar tracks in front of your face, not behind
The fixed path means you can push closer to failure safely
Adjust your seat position to find a comfortable pressing angle
Don't let the fixed path force your shoulders into an uncomfortable position
coaching_cues: Set up so bar clears your face

name: Smith Machine Upright Row
description: **A trap-thickening classic made safer with a guided bar path.** **Muscles worked:** traps, biceps, forearms, shoulders. This compound machine exercise builds the upper traps and shoulders by pulling the bar straight up along your body. The Smith machine keeps the movement locked in, reducing wrist and shoulder strain compared to free-weight versions. **Pro tip:** Stop the bar at chest height rather than chin height — going too high can pinch your shoulder joint. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve peaks when your elbows reach shoulder level, which is exactly where your traps are working hardest.
instructions: Stand facing a Smith machine with the bar at thigh height.
Grip the bar with an overhand grip, hands about 6-8 inches apart.
Unrack the bar and let it hang at arm's length against your thighs.
Pull the bar straight up along your body by driving your elbows toward the ceiling.
Stop when the bar reaches upper chest height, pause briefly, then lower it back down and repeat.
tips: Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
Pull to chest height, not chin height
Lead with your elbows, not your hands

name: Snatch Shrug
description: **An Olympic-inspired power move that builds traps with explosive speed.** **Muscles worked:** traps, shoulders, forearms, calves, glutes, hamstrings, quads, erectors. This barbell compound exercise uses a wide snatch grip and an explosive shrugging motion to train the upper traps and the entire posterior chain. It develops the kind of power needed for Olympic lifts and athletic performance. **Pro tip:** Think 'jump and shrug' — the power comes from your legs driving into the floor, not from pulling with your arms. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve means the load peaks at the top of the shrug, so explode upward aggressively to overcome that sticking point.
instructions: Stand over a barbell with feet hip-width apart and grip the bar with a wide overhand snatch grip.
Set your back flat, chest up, and hips slightly above your knees.
Explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles as if jumping, then shrug your shoulders powerfully toward your ears.
Keep your arms straight throughout — the shrug comes from your traps, not your biceps.
Lower the bar back to the starting position under control and reset before the next rep.
tips: Initiate with a powerful hip extension before pulling with the traps
This is an explosive movement — do not grind reps
Keep the bar close to your body throughout

name: Standing Alternating Dumbbell Press
description: **Press left, press right — your core won't know what hit it.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, abs. This compound dumbbell exercise alternates arms with each rep, which keeps your core firing constantly to resist rotation. It builds shoulder strength while doubling as a sneaky ab workout. **Pro tip:** Don't rush the alternation — fully lock out one arm before starting the other to get the most anti-rotation benefit. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve makes the midpoint of each press the hardest, and the alternating pattern means your core is under tension for the entire set.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward.
Brace your core and press the right dumbbell overhead until your arm is fully extended.
Lower the right dumbbell back to shoulder height.
Press the left dumbbell overhead in the same manner.
Continue alternating arms for your target number of total reps.
tips: Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
Allow a natural arc as you press — don't force a straight line
Control the eccentric to maintain tension on the deltoids
Each arm works independently, exposing and correcting imbalances
coaching_cues: Press and converge slightly at top

name: Standing Barbell Press Behind Neck
description: **An old-school shoulder builder that demands respect and mobility.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, chest. This barbell compound press lowers the bar behind your head, placing the shoulders in a deep stretch under load. It's highly effective for building all three heads of the shoulder but requires good shoulder flexibility. **Pro tip:** Only lower the bar to ear level — going deeper than your mobility allows puts unnecessary stress on your shoulder joints. **Biomechanics:** The stretched loading position combined with a bell-shaped resistance curve means the shoulders are challenged most at the bottom of the press, making the initial push the hardest part.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and unrack a barbell onto your upper traps behind your neck.
Grip the bar with hands wider than shoulder width, elbows pointing down and slightly back.
Press the bar straight up overhead until your arms are fully extended.
Lower the bar back behind your head to about ear level under control.
Repeat for your target reps, keeping your core tight and torso upright throughout.
tips: Only perform this if you have adequate shoulder external rotation mobility
Use a wider grip than your standard press
Don't go excessively deep — just below ear level is sufficient
Start light and assess your shoulder tolerance

name: Standing Bradford Press
description: **A continuous-tension press that never lets your shoulders rest.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, chest. This barbell compound exercise alternates pressing the bar from the front of your head to behind it without ever locking out at the top. The constant motion keeps your shoulders under tension the entire set, creating an intense burn. **Pro tip:** Use significantly lighter weight than your normal overhead press — the non-stop tension makes this brutally hard even with modest loads. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped curve and stretched position at both the front and back create two challenging points per rep, effectively doubling the time your shoulders spend in the hardest range.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hold a barbell at the front of your shoulders with an overhand grip.
Press the bar up just high enough to clear the top of your head.
Instead of locking out, immediately lower the bar behind your head to about ear level.
Press the bar back up just over your head and lower it to the front starting position.
Continue this front-to-back-to-front rhythm without pausing at the top for your target reps.
tips: Only perform this if you have adequate shoulder external rotation mobility
Use a wider grip than your standard press
Don't go excessively deep — just below ear level is sufficient
Start light and assess your shoulder tolerance

name: Standing Dumbbell Press
description: **The foundational overhead press that builds real-world pushing power.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, triceps, chest. This compound dumbbell exercise is one of the most effective ways to build shoulder size and strength. Standing while pressing forces your entire body to stabilize, making it more functional than seated versions. **Pro tip:** Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs before each rep — a solid base lets you press more weight safely. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve makes the press hardest at about forehead height, so drive through that sticking point with consistent force rather than bouncing the weight.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward.
Brace your core and squeeze your glutes for a stable base.
Press both dumbbells overhead until your arms are fully extended and the weights nearly touch.
Lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height under control.
Repeat for your target number of reps.
tips: Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
Allow a natural arc as you press — don't force a straight line
Control the eccentric to maintain tension on the deltoids
Each arm works independently, exposing and correcting imbalances
coaching_cues: Press and converge slightly at top

name: Standing Dumbbell Straight-Arm Front Delt Raise Above Head
description: **Go beyond parallel for a front delt stretch most lifters never experience.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, traps. This dumbbell isolation exercise takes the standard front raise and extends the range of motion overhead, hitting the front delts through a much larger arc. The extra range recruits upper trap fibers at the top. **Pro tip:** Use a thumbs-up grip to reduce shoulder impingement risk when raising above head height. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve peaks around shoulder height, but continuing overhead adds a secondary loading zone where the traps take over, giving you two muscles for the price of one movement.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs with palms facing your body.
With arms straight and a slight bend in your elbows, raise the dumbbells in front of you.
Continue raising past shoulder height until the dumbbells are directly overhead.
Lower the dumbbells back down to your thighs in a slow, controlled arc.
Repeat for your target reps, keeping your torso still throughout.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Standing Dumbbell Upright Row
description: **A free-weight row that stacks size on your traps and shoulders simultaneously.** **Muscles worked:** traps, biceps, forearms, shoulders. This compound dumbbell exercise pulls the weights up along your body to build thick upper traps and rounded shoulders. Using dumbbells instead of a barbell lets your wrists rotate naturally, which is easier on your joints. **Pro tip:** Let the dumbbells drift slightly away from your body as you row up — this small adjustment reduces shoulder impingement while keeping the traps fully engaged. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve loads the traps most heavily when your elbows reach shoulder height, making that the key position to hit on every rep.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs with palms facing your body.
Pull the dumbbells upward by driving your elbows toward the ceiling.
Raise the dumbbells until your elbows reach shoulder height and the weights are near your upper chest.
Pause briefly at the top, squeezing your traps.
Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control and repeat.
tips: Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
Pull to chest height, not chin height
Lead with your elbows, not your hands

name: Standing Front Barbell Raise Over Head
description: **A full-arc front raise that takes your delts from zero to overhead.** **Muscles worked:** shoulders, traps. This barbell isolation exercise extends the classic front raise all the way overhead, dramatically increasing the range of motion and time under tension for the front delts. The barbell lets you load heavier than dumbbells while keeping both arms synchronized. **Pro tip:** Control the descent — the lowering phase is where most of the muscle-building stimulus happens in this exercise. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve peaks around shoulder height on the way up, but the extended overhead range adds a second challenge zone where your traps must fire to complete the lift.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip at thigh level, hands about shoulder-width apart.
With arms straight and a slight bend in your elbows, raise the barbell in front of you.
Continue the arc past shoulder height until the barbell is directly overhead.
Lower the barbell back down to your thighs in a slow, controlled motion.
Repeat for your target reps, keeping your core braced and avoiding any backward lean.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Standing Low-Pulley Deltoid Raise
description:
**A cable-based lateral raise performed from a low pulley that delivers constant tension through the full range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The low anchor point creates a favorable resistance angle that loads the lateral deltoid from a deep stretch, making it one of the most effective isolation movements for shoulder width development.

**Pro tip:** Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
instructions: Stand sideways to a low cable pulley and grasp the handle with the far hand, allowing the cable to cross in front of your body.
Set your feet shoulder-width apart with a slight bend in your knees and a tall, braced torso.
With a soft bend in your elbow, raise your arm out to the side in a wide arc until your hand reaches roughly ear height.
Pause briefly at the top, focusing on squeezing the lateral deltoid.
Lower the handle under control back to the starting position, feeling the stretch at the bottom.
Complete all prescribed reps on one side before switching to the other.
tips: Position the cable behind your body to load the lateral deltoid at longer lengths
Constant tension means no rest at the bottom — every rep counts fully
Single-arm allows you to lean slightly for an even better stretch
This is one of the best lateral deltoid exercises from a stimulus-to-fatigue perspective

name: Standing Military Press
description:
**The standing military press is a foundational overhead barbell movement that builds raw pressing strength and overall shoulder mass.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, chest, abs.

Performed on your feet, it demands significant core stabilization and coordinates the deltoids, triceps, and upper chest to drive the bar from the front rack to full lockout overhead.

**Pro tip:** Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
instructions: Unrack a barbell at collarbone height, gripping it just outside shoulder width with your elbows slightly in front of the bar.
Step back, set your feet hip-width apart, and brace your core as if preparing to take a punch.
Press the bar straight up, moving your head slightly back to clear your chin, then pushing it forward once the bar passes your forehead.
Lock out fully overhead with the bar stacked directly over your mid-foot, shoulders, and hips.
Lower the bar in a controlled path back to the front rack position at your collarbones.
Reset your brace between reps if needed and repeat for the prescribed count.
tips: Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: Standing Palm-In One-Arm Dumbbell Press
description:
**A unilateral overhead press performed with a neutral (palm-in) grip that shifts emphasis toward the anterior deltoid while reducing shoulder joint stress.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, abs.

Training one arm at a time exposes and corrects strength imbalances and forces the obliques and deep core muscles to work overtime to prevent lateral trunk lean.

**Pro tip:** Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hold a dumbbell at shoulder height with your palm facing inward toward your head.
Brace your core firmly and keep your torso vertical — avoid leaning away from the working arm.
Press the dumbbell straight overhead until your arm is fully extended, maintaining the neutral grip throughout.
Hold the lockout for a brief count, ensuring the weight is balanced directly over your shoulder joint.
Lower the dumbbell back to shoulder height under control, resisting the urge to let it drop quickly.
Finish all reps on one arm before switching sides.
tips: Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
Allow a natural arc as you press — don't force a straight line
Control the eccentric to maintain tension on the deltoids
Each arm works independently, exposing and correcting imbalances

name: Standing Palms-In Dumbbell Press
description:
**A bilateral standing overhead press using a neutral hammer grip on both dumbbells simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, abs.

The palms-in hand position places the shoulder in a more externally rotated posture, which many lifters find more comfortable than a pronated grip, while still delivering potent stimulus to the anterior and lateral deltoids along with the triceps.

**Pro tip:** Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
instructions: Stand upright with a dumbbell in each hand held at shoulder height, palms facing each other.
Position your feet roughly hip-width apart and tighten your glutes and abs to create a stable base.
Press both dumbbells upward simultaneously, keeping your wrists stacked over your elbows throughout the movement.
Extend your arms fully at the top without flaring your ribcage or arching your lower back.
Lower the dumbbells back to shoulder level in a smooth, controlled tempo.
Repeat for the target number of repetitions, resetting your brace as needed.
tips: Start with dumbbells at shoulder height, palms forward
Allow a natural arc as you press — don't force a straight line
Control the eccentric to maintain tension on the deltoids
Each arm works independently, exposing and correcting imbalances

name: Standing Two-Arm Overhead Throw
description:
**An explosive plyometric movement typically performed with a medicine ball that develops upper-body power and coordination.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest, lats, triceps, abs.

The overhead throw pattern recruits the entire shoulder girdle, core, and upper extremities in a rapid stretch-shortening cycle, making it a valuable tool for athletic performance training rather than pure hypertrophy work.

**Pro tip:** These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
instructions: Stand facing a wall or open space with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball with both hands.
Bring the ball overhead and slightly behind your head, extending through your thoracic spine to load the throw.
Explosively drive the ball forward and downward by snapping your hips, contracting your core, and following through with your arms.
Release the ball at roughly forehead height for maximum velocity and distance.
If throwing against a wall, catch the rebound with soft hands and immediately reset for the next rep.
Focus on maximal intent with each throw — this is a power exercise, not an endurance drill.
tips: These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
The shoulders will fatigue as part of the full-body effort
Keep form consistent even as fatigue accumulates
Useful for work capacity and general fitness

name: Straight Raises on Incline Bench
description:
**A front raise variation performed face-down on an incline bench that eliminates momentum and isolates the anterior deltoid through a strict range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps.

The incline angle shifts the resistance profile so the deltoid works hardest in the mid-range, and the bench support prevents any cheating through hip drive or back extension.

**Pro tip:** Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to roughly 30-45 degrees and lie face-down with your chest supported against the pad.
Hold a light barbell with an overhand grip at arm's length, letting it hang straight down toward the floor.
With a slight bend in your elbows, raise the barbell forward and upward in an arc until your arms are roughly parallel with the floor.
Squeeze the front deltoids at the top of the movement and hold for a one-count.
Lower the barbell slowly back to the hanging start position, maintaining tension throughout the negative.
Use a controlled tempo on every rep — the bench removes momentum, so let it do its job.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout
Stop at shoulder height — going higher shifts work to the traps
The anterior delt gets heavy work from all pressing — front raises are optional for most
Control the eccentric for maximum tension

name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Clean
description:
**A ballistic kettlebell movement that trains explosive hip extension while developing grip strength and total-body coordination.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, shoulders, traps, forearms, erectors, quads.

Two kettlebells are swung from between the legs and caught in the front rack position, making it an excellent power and conditioning exercise that bridges the gap between lower-body strength work and athletic movement.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand over two kettlebells placed just in front of your feet with a stance slightly wider than hip width.
Hinge at the hips, grip both kettlebells firmly, and hike them back between your legs to load your posterior chain.
Explosively extend your hips and knees, driving the kettlebells upward close to your body.
As the bells rise to chest height, rotate your hands around them and punch your elbows forward to catch them in the front rack position against your forearms.
Stand tall at the top with the kettlebells resting comfortably on your chest, elbows tucked.
Let the kettlebells drop back between your legs by hinging your hips and repeat the clean in one fluid motion.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Jerk
description:
**A dynamic overhead kettlebell lift that combines a short dip-drive with a fast lockout to get two kettlebells from the rack position to full arm extension overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, calves, abs, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

It is a highly technical, full-body power movement that taxes the legs, shoulders, and cardiovascular system simultaneously, making it a staple in kettlebell sport and metabolic conditioning.

**Pro tip:** This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
instructions: Clean two kettlebells into the front rack position with your elbows tight against your ribcage and the bells resting on your forearms.
Initiate a short, sharp dip by bending your knees a few inches while keeping your torso perfectly vertical.
Immediately reverse direction and drive explosively through your legs, using the momentum to launch the kettlebells upward.
As the bells travel up, quickly dip under them a second time and punch your arms to lockout overhead.
Stand up fully with both kettlebells locked out, arms straight, and biceps near your ears.
Lower the bells back to the rack position by absorbing the weight with a slight knee bend and repeat.
tips: This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
The legs do most of the work — the arms guide and stabilize
Practice the footwork separately before adding heavy loads
Not recommended as a primary deltoid hypertrophy exercise

name: Two-Arm Kettlebell Military Press
description:
**A strict overhead press performed with two kettlebells from the front rack position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, abs.

Unlike the jerk, this variation uses zero leg drive, placing all the pressing demand on the deltoids and triceps. The offset center of mass of the kettlebells also challenges grip and wrist stability, making it a highly functional pressing movement.

**Pro tip:** Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
instructions: Clean two kettlebells into the rack position with the bells resting on the backs of your forearms and your elbows pinned to your sides.
Set your feet hip-width apart, squeeze your glutes, and brace your abs hard to create a rigid base.
Press both kettlebells overhead simultaneously using only upper-body strength — no dipping or leg drive allowed.
Lock out fully with your arms straight and the kettlebells balanced over your shoulders.
Lower the bells back to the rack in a controlled manner, keeping your elbows tracking forward rather than flaring out.
Maintain a tight core throughout the set to protect your lower back from excessive arching.
tips: Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
The bell sits on the back of your forearm — don't fight it, work with it
Press in a groove that feels natural for your shoulder
Grip hard to improve rotator cuff co-contraction

name: Upright Barbell Row
description:
**A vertical pulling movement that targets the lateral deltoids and upper traps by drawing a barbell up along the front of the body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, biceps, forearms.

Grip width significantly influences muscle emphasis — a wider grip shifts more work to the delts while a narrower grip recruits more traps. It is effective for building shoulder width but requires mindful execution to keep the shoulder joint comfortable.

**Pro tip:** Use a wider grip to reduce internal rotation stress on the shoulder
instructions: Stand upright and hold a barbell with an overhand grip, hands spaced roughly shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
Let the bar hang at arm's length against your thighs with your shoulders pulled slightly back.
Pull the barbell straight up by driving your elbows high and out to the sides, keeping the bar close to your body.
Raise the bar until your elbows reach approximately shoulder height — avoid pulling excessively high if it causes discomfort.
Pause briefly at the top, feeling the contraction in your lateral delts and upper traps.
Lower the bar back to the start position under control and repeat without using momentum.
tips: Use a wider grip to reduce internal rotation stress on the shoulder
Pull to chest height, not chin height — going too high increases impingement risk
Lead with your elbows, not your hands
If this bothers your shoulders, cable lateral raises are a better alternative

name: Upright Cable Row
description:
**A cable variation of the upright row that provides smoother, more consistent resistance compared to the barbell version.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, biceps, forearms.

The cable's constant tension eliminates the dead zone at the bottom of the movement and allows for a more natural pulling path, which many lifters find easier on the shoulder joints while still effectively targeting the lateral deltoids and traps.

**Pro tip:** Use a wider grip to reduce internal rotation stress on the shoulder
instructions: Attach a straight bar or EZ-bar handle to a low cable pulley and stand about one step back from the machine.
Grip the handle with an overhand grip at roughly shoulder width and let your arms hang fully extended.
Pull the handle upward by leading with your elbows, keeping them flared out to the sides as the bar travels up your torso.
Continue until your elbows reach shoulder height and you feel a strong contraction in your delts.
Hold the peak position for a beat, then lower the handle back down with a slow, deliberate tempo.
Keep your torso upright throughout — avoid leaning back to cheat the weight up.
tips: Use a wider grip to reduce internal rotation stress on the shoulder
Pull to chest height, not chin height — going too high increases impingement risk
Lead with your elbows, not your hands
If this bothers your shoulders, cable lateral raises are a better alternative

name: Upright Row - With Bands
description:
**A banded upright row that uses elastic resistance to create an ascending load profile — the higher you pull, the harder it gets.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: biceps, forearms, shoulders.

This makes the peak contraction at the top especially demanding for the upper traps and lateral deltoids. Bands are joint-friendly and portable, making this a versatile option for warm-ups, finishers, or travel workouts.

**Pro tip:** Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
instructions: Stand on the center of a resistance band with both feet and grasp each end or the handles with an overhand grip.
Start with your arms hanging in front of your thighs, hands close together, and the band under light tension.
Pull your hands upward by driving your elbows high and wide, keeping the band close to your body.
Raise your elbows to shoulder height or just above, squeezing your traps and delts hard at the top.
Lower your hands back to the starting position slowly, resisting the band's pull on the way down.
Maintain an upright posture and avoid shrugging your shoulders up toward your ears excessively.
tips: Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
Pull to chest height, not chin height
Lead with your elbows, not your hands

name: Upward Stretch
description:
**A simple overhead reaching stretch that improves shoulder mobility and relieves tension in the deltoids and surrounding musculature.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: none.

While not a hypertrophy exercise, it serves as an effective warm-up or cool-down movement that promotes healthy overhead range of motion and can help decompress the shoulder joint between pressing sets.

**Pro tip:** Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
