instructions: Stand tall with your feet hip-width apart and interlace your fingers in front of your body.
Rotate your palms outward and press your hands overhead, straightening your arms fully.
Reach as high as possible, actively pushing your palms toward the ceiling while keeping your ribcage down.
Hold the stretched position for 15-30 seconds, breathing deeply and trying to lengthen your spine with each exhale.
Release your hands and slowly lower your arms back to your sides.
Repeat 2-3 times, attempting to reach slightly higher with each repetition.
tips: Perform these as part of your warm-up or cool-down
Move through the range slowly and with control
Never force range of motion — work at the edge of comfort
Consistency matters more than intensity for mobility work

name: Viking Press
description:
**A machine-based overhead press performed on a landmine or lever apparatus that guides the bar along a fixed arc.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, chest.

The angled pressing path is generally more shoulder-friendly than a straight vertical press and allows for heavier loading with reduced stabilization demands. It is an excellent choice for building pressing strength and deltoid mass with a lower injury risk.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
instructions: Position yourself in the Viking press machine with the handles at shoulder height and your feet firmly planted on the platform.
Grip the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, depending on the machine design, and unrack the weight.
Brace your core and press the handles upward along the machine's guided path until your arms are fully extended.
Squeeze your shoulders at the top lockout position for a brief moment.
Lower the handles back to the starting position in a controlled manner, allowing a full stretch at the bottom.
Drive through each rep with intent, using the machine's stability to focus purely on pressing force.
tips: Adjust the seat so handles start at shoulder height
The fixed path lets you push harder without worrying about balance
Good option for training close to failure safely
Focus on squeezing the deltoids rather than just moving the weight

name: Z Press
description:
**A seated-on-the-floor overhead barbell press that strips away all lower body assistance and back support, making it one of the most demanding tests of pure pressing strength and core stability.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, chest, abs.

Sitting with legs extended eliminates leg drive and spinal extension compensation, forcing the deltoids, triceps, and deep core stabilizers to do all the work.

**Pro tip:** Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
instructions: Sit on the floor inside a power rack with your legs extended straight in front of you and set the J-hooks so the barbell is at shoulder height.
Unrack the barbell to the front rack position with a grip just outside shoulder width.
Brace your core intensely — without a back support or leg drive, your abs are the only thing preventing you from tipping backward.
Press the bar overhead to full lockout, keeping the bar path as vertical as possible.
Lower the bar back to your collarbones with control, maintaining an upright torso throughout.
If you feel yourself leaning back excessively, reduce the weight — the strict posture is the entire point of this exercise.
tips: Grip width just outside shoulders for optimal deltoid leverage
Press in a slight arc, not straight vertical, to match the shoulder's natural mechanics
Brace your core hard — energy leaks reduce pressing force
Lock out fully overhead to complete the range of motion

name: 21s Barbell Curl
description:
**A high-volume biceps curl protocol that divides each set into three seven-rep segments covering the bottom half, top half, and full range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This method maximizes time under tension and creates an intense metabolic pump by forcing the biceps to work through partial ranges before finishing with full curls when the muscle is already fatigued.

**Pro tip:** Use a lighter weight than your normal curl — the accumulated fatigue is substantial
instructions: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand grip at arm's length, feet shoulder-width apart.
Perform 7 reps from the bottom position to the midpoint (forearms parallel to the floor), then hold at the bottom.
Immediately perform 7 reps from the midpoint to the fully contracted top position, squeezing the biceps at the top of each rep.
Without resting, perform 7 full range-of-motion curls from the bottom all the way to the top.
Keep your elbows pinned to your sides throughout all 21 reps — no swinging or using your back.
Use a lighter weight than your normal curl — the accumulated fatigue makes this far harder than it looks.
tips: Use a lighter weight than your normal curl — the accumulated fatigue is substantial
Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout all 21 reps
The bottom-half reps will feel easy — save energy for the full-range reps
Supinate fully to maximize biceps moment arm

name: Alternate Hammer Curl
description:
**An alternating dumbbell curl performed with a neutral (hammer) grip that targets the brachialis and brachioradialis in addition to the biceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The hammer grip places the forearm in a biomechanically strong position, typically allowing heavier loads than supinated curls, and builds the thickness of the upper arm when viewed from the side.

**Pro tip:** Keep your wrist neutral — don't let it rotate into supination
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with your palms facing your thighs (neutral grip).
Keeping your elbow stationary at your side, curl one dumbbell upward while maintaining the neutral wrist position throughout.
Raise the dumbbell until your forearm is roughly vertical and you feel a strong contraction in your biceps and brachialis.
Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control.
Repeat the movement with the opposite arm, alternating sides with each rep.
Avoid rocking your torso or swinging the weights — each rep should be strict and deliberate.
tips: Keep your wrist neutral — don't let it rotate into supination
Control the eccentric for at least 2 seconds
Don't swing the torso to generate momentum
Alternate arms to allow brief recovery between reps

name: Alternate Incline Dumbbell Curl
description:
**An alternating dumbbell curl performed while seated on an incline bench, which positions the arms behind the torso and places the biceps under a greater stretch at the bottom of each rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This extended starting position increases the range of motion and recruits the long head of the biceps more aggressively than flat or standing curl variations.

**Pro tip:** Set the bench to 45-60° incline
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 45-60 degree incline and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging straight down.
Let your arms hang fully extended with your palms facing forward — you should feel a deep stretch in both biceps.
Curl one dumbbell upward by flexing at the elbow, keeping your upper arm stationary against your side.
Squeeze the biceps hard at the top of the curl, then lower the dumbbell back to full extension.
Repeat with the opposite arm, alternating sides for the prescribed number of reps.
Keep your back pressed firmly into the bench throughout — do not lift your shoulders off the pad to generate momentum.
tips: Set the bench to 45-60° incline
Let your arms hang straight down at the start
Supinate as you curl to maximize biceps involvement
Don't let your elbows drift forward — keep them behind your torso

name: Alternating Dumbbell Curl
description:
**The classic alternating dumbbell curl performed standing with a supinated grip.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

Curling one arm at a time allows full mental focus on each biceps contraction and enables a slight supination twist that maximizes biceps activation. It is one of the most versatile and widely used arm-building exercises for all experience levels.

**Pro tip:** Supinate your wrist as you curl to maximize biceps activation
instructions: Stand with a dumbbell in each hand, arms at your sides, palms initially facing your thighs.
Curl one dumbbell upward, rotating your wrist so your palm faces the ceiling (supination) as the weight rises.
Continue curling until the dumbbell is near shoulder height and the biceps is fully contracted.
Lower the dumbbell back down slowly, reversing the wrist rotation on the way down.
As the first arm returns to the start, begin curling the opposite arm in the same fashion.
Keep your elbows locked at your sides and your torso still — the only joint moving should be the elbow.
tips: Supinate your wrist as you curl to maximize biceps activation
Keep your torso upright — no swinging
Lower under control for at least 2 seconds
Start the next rep only after the other arm is fully lowered

name: Band Curl
description:
**A biceps curl performed with a resistance band that provides an ascending resistance profile — the tension increases as the band stretches through the curl.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This makes the exercise hardest at the top where the biceps is fully shortened, delivering a powerful peak contraction and pump that complements free-weight curls which are typically hardest at the midpoint.

**Pro tip:** Stand on the band with both feet for more resistance
instructions: Stand on the center of a resistance band with both feet and grip the ends or handles with an underhand grip.
Start with your arms fully extended at your sides, the band under slight tension.
Curl both hands toward your shoulders by flexing at the elbows, keeping your upper arms pinned to your torso.
Squeeze your biceps forcefully at the top where the band tension is greatest and hold for a one-count.
Lower your hands back to the starting position in a controlled manner, resisting the band's pull.
Adjust your foot spacing on the band to increase or decrease the baseline tension as needed.
tips: Stand on the band with both feet for more resistance
Focus on squeezing hard at the top where resistance peaks
Use a slow eccentric to compensate for the light bottom portion
Pair with a mid-range-loaded exercise for complete stimulus

name: Barbell Curl
description:
**The barbell curl is the quintessential biceps mass builder that allows for heavy bilateral loading through a straightforward curling motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The fixed bar path forces both arms to work equally and the ability to progressively overload with small weight increments makes it a cornerstone exercise for long-term biceps development.

**Pro tip:** Grip the bar at shoulder width with full supination
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and grip a barbell with an underhand grip, hands roughly shoulder-width.
Let the bar hang at arm's length against your thighs with your shoulders pulled back slightly.
Curl the bar upward in a smooth arc by flexing your elbows, keeping your upper arms stationary.
Bring the bar to approximately shoulder height, squeezing the biceps at the peak contraction.
Lower the bar back to the starting position under control — take about two seconds on the descent.
Avoid swinging the bar or using hip thrust to move the weight; if you need momentum, the load is too heavy.
tips: Grip the bar at shoulder width with full supination
Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout
Don't lean back to cheat the weight up
Lower under control — the eccentric is valuable

name: Barbell Curls Lying Against An Incline
description:
**A barbell curl performed while lying chest-down against an incline bench, which locks the upper arms in a position behind the torso and eliminates all body English.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This setup places the biceps under a deep stretch at the bottom of each rep and removes any possibility of cheating, making every rep brutally strict and highly effective for targeted biceps growth.

**Pro tip:** Press your chest firmly into the bench to eliminate cheating
instructions: Set an incline bench to approximately 45 degrees and lie face-down with your chest against the pad, feet on the floor for stability.
Have a partner hand you a barbell or pick it up from the floor with an underhand grip, arms hanging straight down.
Curl the barbell upward by flexing your elbows, keeping your upper arms perpendicular to the floor throughout.
Contract the biceps hard at the top of the movement, then lower the bar slowly back to full arm extension.
Feel the stretch at the bottom of each rep before initiating the next curl.
Use a moderate weight — the strict positioning makes this significantly harder than standing curls.
tips: Press your chest firmly into the bench to eliminate cheating
Let the bar hang straight down at the start
Curl in a smooth arc — don't jerk
Use a lighter weight than standing curls

name: Bayesian Curl
description:
**A cable curl performed facing away from the machine with the arm pulled behind the body, creating a deep stretch on the biceps long head at the bottom of every rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The cable provides constant tension throughout the entire range of motion, and the behind-body arm position makes this one of the most effective exercises for emphasizing the biceps long head and building a prominent peak.

**Pro tip:** Stand facing away from the cable machine
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the lowest position and attach a single handle. Stand facing away from the machine and grasp the handle with one hand.
Step forward until your working arm is pulled slightly behind your torso, placing the biceps on stretch.
Stagger your stance for balance with the same-side foot slightly back.
Curl the handle forward and upward by flexing your elbow, keeping your upper arm fixed behind your body.
Squeeze the biceps at the top of the curl, then slowly extend your arm back to the stretched starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching — focus on feeling the stretch at the bottom of every rep.
tips: Stand facing away from the cable machine
Keep your elbow behind your torso throughout
Curl until your forearm is roughly horizontal
Use a slow eccentric to maximize time under tension

name: Brachialis-SMR
description:
**A self-muscle and tissue release technique targeting the brachialis muscle on the outer portion of the upper arm using a foam roller, lacrosse ball, or similar tool.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: none.

While not a strength exercise, regular SMR work on the brachialis can improve elbow flexion mobility, reduce trigger points, and enhance recovery between arm training sessions.

**Pro tip:** Apply moderate pressure — this area can be tender
instructions: Lie on your side or position your upper arm on a foam roller or lacrosse ball so the tool presses into the outer portion of your upper arm between the biceps and triceps.
Apply moderate pressure by relaxing your body weight onto the tool.
Slowly roll along the length of the brachialis from just above the elbow to the mid-upper arm, spending extra time on any tender spots.
When you find a particularly tight or painful area, pause and hold pressure on that spot for 20-30 seconds.
Gently flex and extend your elbow while maintaining pressure to create a pin-and-stretch effect.
Spend 1-2 minutes per arm, breathing deeply and progressively relaxing into the pressure.
tips: Apply moderate pressure — this area can be tender
Roll slowly along the outer upper arm
Spend extra time on any tender spots
Perform before or after training

name: Cable Concentration Curl
description:
**A single-arm cable curl performed in a concentration curl position that combines the constant tension of a cable with the strict isolation of bracing the elbow against the inner thigh.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This setup eliminates all momentum and secondary muscle involvement, making it a pure biceps exercise with an excellent effort-to-results ratio and a skin-splitting pump.

**Pro tip:** Brace your elbow against your inner thigh
instructions: Attach a single handle to a low cable pulley.
Sit on a bench and lean forward, bracing your elbow against your inner thigh.
Grip the cable handle with an underhand grip, arm fully extended.
Curl the handle toward your shoulder, squeezing your bicep at the top.
Lower the handle back down slowly under control.
Complete all reps, then switch arms.
tips: Brace your elbow against your inner thigh
Curl to full flexion — the cable keeps tension at the top
Use a slow eccentric
Don't rotate your torso to cheat

name: Cable Curl
description:
**A biceps isolation exercise performed on a cable machine that delivers constant tension throughout the entire range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The pulley system eliminates the dead spots found in free-weight curls, keeping the biceps under continuous load from full extension to peak contraction.

**Pro tip:** Stand a step back from the pulley for a consistent line of pull
instructions: Attach a straight bar or EZ-bar handle to a low cable pulley.
Stand facing the machine and grasp the attachment with an underhand grip at shoulder width.
Position your feet hip-width apart, brace your core, and pin your elbows to your sides.
Curl the handle upward by flexing at the elbows, squeezing the biceps hard at the top.
Lower the weight under control back to full arm extension.
Maintain a rigid torso throughout—avoid swinging or using momentum.
tips: Stand a step back from the pulley for a consistent line of pull
Keep elbows at your sides — don't let them drift forward
Squeeze at the top — the cable still has tension there
Control the eccentric fully

name: Cable Hammer Curl
description:
**A neutral-grip cable curl that shifts emphasis toward the brachialis and brachioradialis while still heavily recruiting the biceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The cable's constant resistance profile makes this variation superior to dumbbell hammer curls for maintaining tension at both the top and bottom of the movement.

**Pro tip:** Use a rope attachment for the neutral grip
instructions: Attach a straight bar or dual handles to a low cable pulley.
Grip the attachment with palms facing each other (neutral grip).
Stand upright with elbows locked at your sides and chest lifted.
Curl the weight up by bending at the elbows while keeping your wrists neutral throughout.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower slowly to full extension.
Keep your upper arms stationary—only the forearms should move.
tips: Use a rope attachment for the neutral grip
Keep wrists neutral throughout — don't supinate
Stand far enough from the machine for a clean line of pull
Squeeze at the top where dumbbells would normally unload

name: Cable Hammer Curls - Rope Attachment
description:
**A hammer curl variation using a rope attachment on a low cable pulley, allowing a natural neutral grip and the ability to spread the rope ends apart at peak contraction for an intensified squeeze.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The cable provides unbroken tension that free weights cannot replicate in this movement pattern.

**Pro tip:** Pull the rope ends apart slightly at the top for peak contraction
instructions: Clip a rope attachment to a low cable pulley and stand facing the stack.
Grasp each end of the rope with a neutral (palms-in) grip.
Stand tall with elbows pinned to your ribcage and shoulders pulled back.
Curl the rope upward, and at the top, split the rope ends outward to maximize the contraction.
Hold the peak squeeze for one second, then return to the start under control.
Avoid rocking your torso or letting your elbows drift forward.
tips: Pull the rope ends apart slightly at the top for peak contraction
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Don't lean back — stand upright
Use a controlled 2-second eccentric

name: Cable Preacher Curl
description:
**A preacher curl performed with a cable machine, combining the strict arm-bracing benefits of a preacher bench with the constant tension of a pulley system.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This setup eliminates cheating and keeps the biceps loaded even at the bottom of the rep where barbell preacher curls typically lose resistance.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the cable so the line of pull is roughly perpendicular to your forearm at mid-range
instructions: Position a preacher bench in front of a low cable pulley and attach a straight or EZ-bar handle.
Sit on the bench and drape the backs of your upper arms over the pad.
Grasp the handle with an underhand grip, arms extended but not hyperextended.
Curl the handle toward your shoulders by contracting the biceps.
Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly, feeling the cable pull throughout the descent.
Keep your chest pressed into the pad and avoid lifting your elbows off the surface.
tips: Adjust the cable so the line of pull is roughly perpendicular to your forearm at mid-range
Press your upper arms firmly into the pad
Don't let your shoulders rise off the pad at the top
Control the eccentric — the cable keeps tension on the way down

name: Chin-Up Close Grip
description:
**A bodyweight compound pull performed with hands placed close together in a supinated grip, heavily biasing the biceps over the lats compared to wider chin-up variations.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: lats, forearms.

This exercise allows you to load the biceps with your full bodyweight while also building back thickness and grip strength.

**Pro tip:** Grip the bar with hands 6-8 inches apart, palms facing you
instructions: Grip a pull-up bar with an underhand grip, hands 6–8 inches apart.
Hang at full arm extension with shoulders engaged (not shrugged to your ears).
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows down and back, focusing on bending at the elbow.
Pull until your chin clears the bar, squeezing the biceps at the top.
Lower yourself under control to a full dead hang.
Avoid kipping or swinging—each rep should be strict and controlled.
tips: Grip the bar with hands 6-8 inches apart, palms facing you
Initiate by driving elbows down and back
Pull until your chin clears the bar
Control the descent — don't just drop

name: Close-Grip EZ Bar Curl
description:
**A standing curl using the inner grips of an EZ-bar, which places the hands in a narrow, slightly angled position that preferentially targets the long (outer) head of the biceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The cambered bar reduces wrist strain compared to a straight barbell while the close grip increases the stretch on the outer biceps head.

**Pro tip:** Grip the inner cambers of the EZ bar
instructions: Load an EZ-bar and grasp it on the inner angled grips with an underhand hold.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, arms fully extended, bar resting against your thighs.
Keeping elbows locked at your sides, curl the bar upward in a smooth arc.
Squeeze the biceps firmly at the top of the movement.
Lower the bar slowly back to the starting position without letting it drop.
Maintain an upright posture—do not lean back or swing the weight.
tips: Grip the inner cambers of the EZ bar
Keep elbows tight to your sides
Don't flare your elbows outward as you curl
Lower under control for 2-3 seconds

name: Close-Grip EZ-Bar Curl with Band
description:
**An accommodating-resistance curl that pairs an EZ-bar with a looped resistance band to create an ascending strength curve.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The band adds progressively more resistance as you curl higher, overloading the biceps at peak contraction where they are strongest and standard curls typically become easier.

**Pro tip:** Loop the band under your feet and over the bar
instructions: Stand on the center of a looped resistance band and hook each end over the EZ-bar ends.
Grip the inner handles of the EZ-bar with an underhand grip.
Begin with arms fully extended; the band should have slight tension at the bottom.
Curl the bar upward—feel the resistance increase as the band stretches.
Fight the band's pull on the way down, lowering over 2–3 seconds.
Keep elbows stationary and torso braced throughout every rep.
tips: Loop the band under your feet and over the bar
The top will be significantly harder — choose bar weight accordingly
Keep elbows pinned despite the increasing pull
Focus on controlling the eccentric as band tension drops

name: Close-Grip Standing Barbell Curl
description:
**A classic barbell curl performed with a narrow grip that emphasizes the long head of the biceps and builds impressive arm peak.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

By bringing the hands closer together on a straight bar, you increase the demand on the outer portion of the biceps while still allowing heavy loading.

**Pro tip:** Grip the bar 6-8 inches apart
instructions: Grasp a straight barbell with an underhand grip, hands approximately 6–8 inches apart.
Stand upright with the bar at arm's length against your thighs.
Brace your core and pin your elbows firmly to your sides.
Curl the bar toward your shoulders in a controlled arc.
Hold the peak contraction for a beat, then lower steadily to full extension.
Do not allow your elbows to travel forward or your back to arch.
tips: Grip the bar 6-8 inches apart
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Don't lean back — maintain upright posture
Supinate fully throughout the movement

name: Concentration Curl
description:
**A seated single-arm curl where the working elbow is braced against the inner thigh, completely eliminating momentum and body English.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

Research has shown this exercise produces among the highest biceps EMG activation of any curl variation due to the strict isolation it enforces.

**Pro tip:** Brace your elbow firmly against your inner thigh — don't let it slide
instructions: Sit on a bench with feet flat and legs spread wide.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand and press the back of that upper arm against your inner thigh.
Start with the arm fully extended, palm facing away from the thigh.
Curl the dumbbell toward your shoulder, rotating the wrist slightly outward at the top.
Squeeze the biceps hard at peak contraction, then lower slowly.
Complete all reps on one arm before switching to the other.
tips: Brace your elbow firmly against your inner thigh — don't let it slide
Supinate your wrist fully throughout
Squeeze hard at the top for 1 second
Lower slowly — the isolation makes the eccentric very effective

name: Concentration Curls
description:
**A unilateral biceps exercise performed seated with the elbow anchored against the inner knee, providing a fixed pivot point that isolates the biceps with zero assistance from other muscle groups.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This strict positioning makes it an excellent finishing movement for maximizing the mind-muscle connection.

**Pro tip:** Sit with legs spread and brace your working elbow against your thigh
instructions: Sit on a flat bench and spread your knees apart for stability.
Lean forward slightly and brace the back of your working arm's triceps against the inside of the same-side thigh.
Let the dumbbell hang at full arm extension.
Curl the weight upward by flexing only at the elbow.
Pause at the top and actively squeeze the biceps for one second.
Lower under control and repeat; switch arms after completing the set.
tips: Sit with legs spread and brace your working elbow against your thigh
Use a full range of motion from near-full extension to full flexion
Don't rotate your torso to assist the curl
Focus on the squeeze at the top

name: Cross Body Hammer Curl
description:
**A hammer curl variation where the dumbbell is curled across the body toward the opposite shoulder rather than straight up.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This cross-body path increases brachialis and brachioradialis involvement while also engaging the biceps through a unique angle that standard hammer curls miss.

**Pro tip:** Curl the dumbbell toward the opposite shoulder, not straight up
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral (palms-in) grip.
Keeping your right elbow pinned, curl the right dumbbell across your torso toward your left shoulder.
Squeeze at the top when the dumbbell reaches chest height.
Lower slowly back to the starting position.
Repeat with the left arm curling toward the right shoulder.
Alternate arms each rep, keeping your torso completely still throughout.
tips: Curl the dumbbell toward the opposite shoulder, not straight up
Keep your wrist neutral throughout
Don't rotate your torso — only the forearm moves
Control the eccentric back to the starting position

name: Drag Curl
description:
**An unconventional barbell curl where the elbows travel backward behind the body as the bar is dragged up the torso, keeping the load in the shortened position of the biceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

By removing the front deltoid from the movement, drag curls place intense stress on the biceps peak and are an excellent complement to standard curls.

**Pro tip:** Drag the bar along your body — it should touch your torso throughout
instructions: Hold a barbell with an underhand shoulder-width grip, arms extended in front of your thighs.
Instead of curling in an arc, pull your elbows straight back behind your body.
Drag the bar up along your torso—it should lightly graze your shirt as it rises.
Continue until the bar reaches mid-chest level and the biceps are fully shortened.
Reverse the motion, sliding the bar back down your body.
Use a lighter weight than standard curls—this movement is about contraction quality, not load.
tips: Drag the bar along your body — it should touch your torso throughout
Drive your elbows back as you curl
Use a lighter weight than standard curls
Focus on the biceps squeeze at the top

name: Dumbbell Alternate Bicep Curl
description:
**A standing dumbbell curl performed one arm at a time in an alternating fashion, allowing each biceps to work independently while the other briefly recovers.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This alternating pattern lets you handle slightly heavier loads per arm and helps identify and correct strength imbalances between sides.

**Pro tip:** Supinate fully as you curl each arm
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand, arms at your sides, palms facing forward.
Curl the right dumbbell toward your right shoulder while keeping the left arm stationary.
Squeeze at the top, then lower the right dumbbell under control.
As the right arm returns to the bottom, begin curling the left dumbbell.
Continue alternating arms for the prescribed number of reps per side.
Keep your elbows glued to your ribs and resist any torso sway.
tips: Supinate fully as you curl each arm
Keep your torso still — no rocking
Lower one arm completely before curling the other
Maintain a slight bend at the bottom — don't hyperextend

name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
description:
**The foundational dumbbell curl performed with both arms simultaneously using a supinated grip.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This staple biceps builder allows full supination of the forearm, which maximally activates the biceps brachii, and the independent loading of each arm ensures balanced development.

**Pro tip:** Keep your wrists supinated (palms up) throughout the entire rep
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing forward.
Keep your elbows tight to your sides and your shoulders pulled back.
Curl both dumbbells upward simultaneously, rotating your pinkies slightly outward at the top.
Squeeze the biceps at peak contraction.
Lower both dumbbells in a controlled 2–3 second descent.
Fully extend your arms at the bottom before initiating the next rep.
tips: Keep your wrists supinated (palms up) throughout the entire rep
Don't swing the weight — if you need momentum, it's too heavy
Lower for 2-3 seconds on every rep
Keep elbows at your sides, not drifting forward

name: Dumbbell Curl
description:
**A versatile single- or dual-arm curl using dumbbells that allows a natural wrist rotation through the curling motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The free-weight nature demands stabilizer engagement and permits a full range of motion from complete extension to maximal biceps contraction.

**Pro tip:** Full supination from start to finish
instructions: Grab a pair of dumbbells and stand tall with arms hanging at your sides, palms initially facing your thighs.
Begin curling upward, supinating your wrists (rotating palms up) as the dumbbells pass your hips.
Continue curling until the dumbbells approach shoulder height and the biceps are fully contracted.
Hold the top position briefly.
Reverse the motion, pronating the wrists back to neutral as you lower.
Maintain strict posture—no leaning, swinging, or shoulder shrugging.
tips: Full supination from start to finish
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Control the weight on the way down
Don't use body momentum

name: Dumbbell Prone Incline Curl
description:
**A curl performed lying face-down on an incline bench, which lets the arms hang straight down under gravity and places the biceps in a deep stretched position at the bottom of each rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The prone position makes cheating virtually impossible and biases the early range of motion where the biceps are most lengthened.

**Pro tip:** Lie chest-down on a 45° incline bench
instructions: Set an incline bench to approximately 45 degrees and lie face-down with your chest supported on the pad.
Let your arms hang straight down, holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing forward.
Curl both dumbbells upward, keeping your upper arms perpendicular to the floor.
Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly, emphasizing the stretch at the bottom.
Allow your arms to reach full extension before starting the next rep.
Keep your chest pressed firmly into the bench—no lifting your torso.
tips: Lie chest-down on a 45° incline bench
Let your arms hang straight down to start
Curl in a smooth arc without lifting your chest off the pad
Use a lighter weight than standing curls — there's no momentum here

name: Dumbbell Reverse Curl
description:
**A curl performed with an overhand (pronated) grip that shifts the primary workload from the biceps to the brachioradialis and wrist extensors of the forearm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: biceps.

This exercise is essential for building forearm size and grip strength, and it helps prevent the elbow imbalances that can develop from exclusively supinated curling.

**Pro tip:** Keep palms facing down throughout
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand with an overhand grip (palms facing down).
Position your arms at full extension with the dumbbells resting against your thighs.
Keeping your wrists rigid and elbows at your sides, curl the dumbbells upward.
Raise until your forearms are roughly parallel to the floor or slightly higher.
Lower under control, resisting the urge to let gravity take over.
Use lighter weight than standard curls—forearm muscles fatigue quickly.
tips: Keep palms facing down throughout
Don't let wrists flex — keep them rigid
Control the eccentric for 2 seconds
Use lighter weight than supinated curls

name: EZ Bar Preacher Curl
description:
**A strict biceps curl performed on a preacher bench using an EZ-bar, which locks the upper arms against the angled pad and removes all momentum from the exercise.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The cambered bar reduces wrist and elbow stress while the bench enforces a pure elbow-flexion movement pattern for maximum biceps isolation.

**Pro tip:** Press your upper arms firmly into the pad — no daylight between arm and pad
instructions: Adjust the preacher bench so the top of the pad fits snugly under your armpits when seated.
Grasp an EZ-bar on the angled grips with an underhand hold.
Drape the backs of your upper arms flat against the pad, arms extended but elbows not locked.
Curl the bar upward until your forearms are nearly vertical.
Squeeze the biceps, then lower the bar slowly—take at least 2 seconds on the descent.
Stop just short of full lockout at the bottom to keep tension on the muscle.
tips: Press your upper arms firmly into the pad — no daylight between arm and pad
Use the inner or outer cambers based on comfort
Don't let your shoulders rise at the top
Control the eccentric — the preacher position makes the bottom vulnerable to strain

name: EZ-Bar Curl
description:
**A standing curl using the angled grips of an EZ-bar, which places the wrists in a semi-supinated position that is more joint-friendly than a straight bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This is one of the most popular biceps exercises in any gym because it allows heavy loading with reduced wrist and forearm discomfort.

**Pro tip:** Grip the outer cambers for more biceps emphasis
instructions: Stand and grip an EZ-bar on the outer or inner cambers with an underhand grip, depending on preference.
Hold the bar at arm's length against your thighs, feet shoulder-width apart.
Brace your core and curl the bar upward in a smooth arc, keeping elbows stationary.
Contract the biceps hard at the top of the movement.
Lower the bar under control to full arm extension.
Avoid swinging the weight—if you need momentum, reduce the load.
tips: Grip the outer cambers for more biceps emphasis
Keep elbows at your sides throughout
Don't lean back at the top
Lower under control for 2-3 seconds

name: Flexor Incline Dumbbell Curls
description:
**A dumbbell curl performed while seated on an incline bench, which positions the arms behind the torso and places the biceps under a greater stretch at the bottom of each rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The reclined angle increases the range of motion and prevents the use of body momentum, making each rep more demanding on the biceps.

**Pro tip:** Set the bench to 45-60° incline
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 45–60 degree incline and sit back with your shoulders and back flat against the pad.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand, letting your arms hang straight down toward the floor.
With palms facing forward, curl both dumbbells upward without moving your upper arms.
Squeeze at the top when the dumbbells reach shoulder level.
Lower slowly, feeling the stretch in the biceps as your arms return to full extension.
Do not let your elbows drift forward—keep them pointed at the ground throughout.
tips: Set the bench to 45-60° incline
Let arms hang fully at the start — don't pre-bend
Supinate as you curl
Keep your back pressed into the bench throughout

name: Hammer Curl
description:
**A dumbbell curl performed with a neutral (thumbs-up) grip that recruits the brachialis and brachioradialis alongside the biceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

Because the brachialis sits underneath the biceps, developing it pushes the biceps up and adds visible arm thickness that supinated curls alone cannot achieve.

**Pro tip:** Keep your wrist locked in neutral — no rotation
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing your body (neutral grip).
Keep your elbows pinned to your sides and your chest up.
Curl both dumbbells upward simultaneously, maintaining the neutral wrist position throughout.
Raise until the dumbbells reach shoulder height and the forearms are vertical.
Pause, then lower under control to full arm extension.
Resist the temptation to swing—strict form maximizes brachialis recruitment.
tips: Keep your wrist locked in neutral — no rotation
Don't swing the weight
Lower under control for 2 seconds
Keep elbows at your sides

name: Hammer Curls
description:
**A neutral-grip dumbbell curl that targets the brachialis, brachioradialis, and biceps simultaneously, making it one of the most efficient exercises for overall upper-arm and forearm development.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The thumbs-up hand position allows most lifters to handle heavier loads than with supinated curls.

**Pro tip:** Both arms can curl simultaneously or alternating
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, a dumbbell in each hand, arms at your sides with palms facing inward.
Brace your core and fix your elbows against your torso.
Curl the dumbbells upward together, keeping your thumbs pointed toward the ceiling.
Contract at the top when the weights are near your front delts.
Lower in a controlled manner over 2–3 seconds.
Fully straighten your arms at the bottom before beginning the next rep.
tips: Both arms can curl simultaneously or alternating
Maintain strict neutral wrist position
Don't let elbows drift forward
Use a full range of motion

name: High Cable Curls
description:
**A biceps curl performed with arms raised to shoulder height using high cable pulleys, mimicking a front double biceps pose under load.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This unique angle places constant tension on the biceps peak and is one of the few exercises that trains the biceps in a fully shortened, overhead-arm position.

**Pro tip:** Set the pulleys at head height or slightly above
instructions: Set both pulleys on a cable crossover station to the highest position and attach single handles.
Stand in the center and grasp a handle in each hand with an underhand grip, arms extended out to the sides at shoulder height.
Curl both handles toward your ears by flexing at the elbows.
Squeeze your biceps as if hitting a front double biceps pose.
Slowly extend your arms back to the starting position, maintaining shoulder-height alignment.
Keep your upper arms parallel to the floor throughout—do not let them drop.
tips: Set the pulleys at head height or slightly above
Keep your upper arms parallel to the floor throughout
Curl your hands toward your ears
Squeeze hard at full flexion

name: Incline Cable Curl
description:
**A curl performed on an incline bench positioned in front of a low cable pulley, combining the stretched-arm benefits of incline curls with the constant tension of cable resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This hybrid setup ensures the biceps never get a rest at any point in the range of motion, making it a potent hypertrophy stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Set the bench at 45-60° and position it in front of a low pulley
instructions: Place an incline bench (set to 45–60 degrees) facing away from a low cable pulley.
Attach a straight bar or individual handles and sit back on the bench.
Let your arms hang behind your torso with the cable taut.
Curl the attachment forward and upward, keeping your upper arms stationary.
Squeeze at the top, then resist the cable's pull as you lower back to full stretch.
Keep your back and shoulders pressed into the bench at all times.
tips: Set the bench at 45-60° and position it in front of a low pulley
Let your arms hang at the start
Curl smoothly — the cable provides constant tension
Don't let your elbows drift forward

name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
description:
**A seated curl on an incline bench that positions the arms behind the body, placing the long head of the biceps under a deep stretch at the bottom of each rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This extended starting position increases the effective range of motion and is widely regarded as one of the best exercises for targeting the biceps long head and building arm peak.

**Pro tip:** Set bench to 45-60° incline
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 45-degree incline and sit with your back flat against the pad.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand, letting your arms hang straight down with palms facing forward.
Without moving your upper arms, curl the dumbbells toward your shoulders.
Squeeze the biceps at the top of the movement.
Lower slowly, allowing a full stretch at the bottom—feel the pull through the long head.
Keep your shoulder blades retracted and elbows pointing at the floor throughout the set.
tips: Set bench to 45-60° incline
Let arms hang straight down at the start
Supinate fully as you curl
Keep elbows behind your torso — don't let them drift forward

name: Incline Hammer Curl
description:
**A biceps curl performed on an incline bench with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The reclined angle lets the arms hang behind the torso, increasing the stretch on the long head of the biceps while the hammer grip shifts emphasis toward the brachialis and brachioradialis.

**Pro tip:** Set bench to 45-60° incline
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 45–60 degree incline and sit back with your shoulders and upper back firmly against the pad.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, letting your arms hang straight down at your sides.
Keeping your upper arms stationary and elbows pointed toward the floor, curl both dumbbells upward without rotating your wrists.
Squeeze at the top when your forearms are roughly parallel to the floor, then lower under control back to full arm extension.
Avoid swinging the torso or letting the elbows drift forward throughout the set.
tips: Set bench to 45-60° incline
Keep wrists neutral — thumbs up throughout
Let arms hang fully at the start
Don't let elbows swing forward as you curl

name: Incline Hammer Curls
description:
**An incline bench dumbbell curl using a neutral wrist position throughout the movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The inclined torso places the biceps under a deeper stretch at the bottom, and the hammer grip recruits the brachialis heavily alongside the biceps brachii.

**Pro tip:** Set bench to 45-60°
instructions: Recline on a bench set between 45 and 60 degrees, pressing your back flat against the pad with feet planted on the floor.
Grasp a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing inward and let your arms hang fully extended beside the bench.
Curl the weights upward simultaneously, maintaining the neutral wrist position and keeping your elbows pinned in place.
Pause briefly at the top of the contraction, then lower the dumbbells slowly to a full stretch at the bottom.
Do not allow your shoulders to roll forward or your back to arch away from the bench.
tips: Set bench to 45-60°
Keep both wrists neutral throughout
Curl both arms simultaneously for efficiency
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds

name: Incline Inner Biceps Curl
description:
**A supinated dumbbell curl on an incline bench with the arms rotated outward to target the short (inner) head of the biceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The wide, externally rotated arm path combined with the incline stretch creates a unique stimulus for the inner biceps.

**Pro tip:** Rotate your pinkies outward as you curl
instructions: Set a bench to roughly 55 degrees and sit back with your spine fully supported.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a supinated grip and rotate your arms slightly outward so your pinkies angle away from your body.
Curl the dumbbells upward while keeping the externally rotated arm position, focusing on squeezing the inner portion of the biceps.
Lower the weights in a controlled arc until your arms are fully extended and you feel a deep stretch.
Keep your elbows from drifting inward or forward; the upper arm should remain stationary throughout.
tips: Rotate your pinkies outward as you curl
Keep your back pressed into the bench
Don't let elbows drift forward
Use a lighter weight — the rotation makes this harder

name: Kettlebell Curl
description:
**A standing biceps curl using a kettlebell, where the offset center of mass hangs below the handle and forces the forearms and grip to work harder than a standard dumbbell curl.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The unique loading also creates a slightly different resistance profile through the range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Grip the handle firmly — the offset mass will try to rotate your wrist
instructions: Stand upright holding a kettlebell in one or both hands by the handle with an underhand grip, arms fully extended at your sides.
Brace your core and pin your elbows to your ribcage.
Curl the kettlebell upward by flexing at the elbow, keeping the wrist neutral and firm to control the swinging bell.
Contract the biceps hard at the top, then lower the weight slowly, resisting the pull of gravity the entire way down.
Avoid using momentum from the hips or shoulders; the movement should come entirely from elbow flexion.
tips: Grip the handle firmly — the offset mass will try to rotate your wrist
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Supinate fully throughout
Control the eccentric — the kettlebell's momentum can pull you

name: Lying Cable Curl
description:
**A biceps curl performed while lying on the floor facing a low cable pulley.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The supine position eliminates momentum and body English, while the cable provides constant tension across the entire range of motion for an excellent effort-to-results ratio.

**Pro tip:** Lie on your back with feet toward the cable machine
instructions: Attach a straight or EZ-bar to a low cable pulley and lie face-up on the floor with your feet near the base of the machine.
Reach back and grasp the bar with an underhand grip, arms extended toward the pulley.
Curl the bar toward your forehead by flexing your elbows, keeping your upper arms perpendicular to the floor.
Squeeze the biceps at peak contraction, then slowly extend your arms back to the starting position.
Keep your lower back pressed into the floor and avoid lifting your shoulders off the ground.
tips: Lie on your back with feet toward the cable machine
Keep upper arms perpendicular to the floor
Curl the handle toward your forehead
Don't lift your elbows off the floor

name: Lying Close-Grip Bar Curl On High Pulley
description:
**A supine cable curl performed with a close grip on a bar attached to a high pulley.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

Lying beneath the cable stack with arms extended overhead creates continuous tension on the biceps and removes any ability to cheat with body momentum.

**Pro tip:** Lie on your back under a high pulley
instructions: Attach a short straight bar to a high pulley and lie on the floor or a flat bench positioned beneath the cable stack.
Reach overhead and grasp the bar with a close, supinated grip, arms extended toward the pulley.
Curl the bar down toward your forehead by bending only at the elbows, keeping your upper arms fixed in position.
Hold the peak contraction for a beat, then extend your arms back overhead under control.
Maintain a flat back and avoid flaring your elbows outward during the movement.
tips: Lie on your back under a high pulley
Grip the bar with hands close together
Keep upper arms vertical — only forearms move
Control the eccentric as the cable pulls your arms back

name: Lying High Bench Barbell Curl
description:
**A barbell curl performed face-down on a high bench so the arms hang straight toward the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This prone position places the biceps in a stretched position at the bottom and completely prevents any cheating through hip or back extension.

**Pro tip:** Lie chest-down on a bench high enough for your arms to hang freely
instructions: Lie face-down on a bench high enough that your arms can hang freely without the barbell touching the floor.
Have a partner hand you a barbell or pick it up from the floor with an underhand grip, arms fully extended.
Curl the barbell upward by flexing at the elbows while keeping your chest pressed into the bench.
Squeeze at the top, then lower the bar slowly, emphasizing the stretch at the bottom of each rep.
Keep your upper arms vertical and avoid rocking your torso to generate momentum.
tips: Lie chest-down on a bench high enough for your arms to hang freely
Let the bar hang straight down at the start
Curl in a smooth arc
Use lighter weight than standing curls

name: Lying Supine Dumbbell Curl
description:
**A dumbbell curl performed while lying face-up on a flat bench.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The supine position anchors the shoulders and back, preventing momentum and isolating the biceps through a strict range of motion with dumbbells at your sides.

**Pro tip:** Lie flat on a bench with dumbbells at arm's length
instructions: Lie face-up on a flat bench with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging straight down toward the floor with palms facing forward.
Press your shoulder blades into the bench and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Curl both dumbbells upward by bending at the elbows, keeping your upper arms stationary.
Pause at the top of the contraction, then lower the weights under control to full extension.
Avoid lifting your head or shoulders off the bench and do not swing the dumbbells.
tips: Lie flat on a bench with dumbbells at arm's length
Curl the dumbbells toward your shoulders
Keep your upper arms still — only forearms move
Don't arch your back off the bench

name: Machine Bicep Curl
description:
**A guided biceps curl on a selectorized machine that locks the upper arms in place against a pad.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The cam-based resistance provides consistent tension throughout the range of motion, making it one of the most fatigue-efficient biceps exercises available.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat so your elbows align with the machine's pivot point
instructions: Adjust the seat height so that your upper arms rest flat on the pad and your elbows align with the machine's pivot point.
Grasp the handles with an underhand grip and sit with your chest against the support pad.
Curl the handles toward your shoulders by flexing at the elbows, focusing on contracting the biceps.
Hold the top position briefly, then lower the weight slowly until your arms are nearly fully extended.
Avoid pulling with your shoulders or leaning back away from the chest pad.
tips: Adjust the seat so your elbows align with the machine's pivot point
Use a full range of motion
Don't use momentum — let the machine guide the path
Squeeze at the top for 1 second

name: Machine Curl
description:
**A selectorized biceps curl machine that stabilizes the body and guides the movement path.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The fixed arc of motion and even resistance curve allow you to train the biceps to failure safely without worrying about balance or form breakdown.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat height so elbows align with the pivot
instructions: Set the seat so your elbows line up with the machine's axis of rotation and your upper arms sit comfortably on the pad.
Grip the handles with palms facing up and press your back into the seat.
Curl the weight up smoothly, driving through the biceps rather than pulling with the hands.
Control the negative by lowering the handles slowly until your arms reach full extension without letting the weight stack slam.
Keep your wrists straight and avoid shrugging your shoulders during the movement.
tips: Adjust the seat height so elbows align with the pivot
Use the full range of motion the machine allows
Focus on squeezing at the top
Control the eccentric — don't let the weight stack slam

name: Machine Preacher Curl
description:
**A preacher curl station built into a machine with a cam that matches the biceps' strength curve.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The angled arm pad eliminates shoulder involvement and the machine's resistance profile keeps tension on the muscle even at the top where free-weight preacher curls typically lose load.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat so your armpits rest on the top of the pad
instructions: Adjust the seat so your armpits rest snugly against the top edge of the preacher pad and your elbows align with the pivot.
Grasp the handles with a supinated grip and let your arms extend down the pad.
Curl the handles upward, pressing your triceps firmly into the pad throughout the rep.
Squeeze the biceps at the top, then lower under control to a near-full extension without hyperextending the elbows.
Do not lift your elbows off the pad or lean your torso backward to assist the curl.
tips: Adjust the seat so your armpits rest on the top of the pad
Keep your chest against the pad throughout
Use the full range of motion
Don't let the weight stack crash at the bottom

name: Machine Preacher Curls
description:
**A machine-based preacher curl that braces the upper arms against an angled pad while a cam system delivers smooth, constant resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This setup is ideal for safely pushing close to failure since the fixed path prevents form breakdown.

**Pro tip:** Align your elbows with the machine's axis of rotation
instructions: Set the seat height so the top of the pad sits comfortably under your armpits and your elbows are centered on the machine's rotation axis.
Take an underhand grip on the handles and allow your arms to straighten down the pad.
Curl the weight up by contracting the biceps, keeping your chest against the rear support.
Pause at peak contraction, then slowly return to the start, stopping just short of full lockout to maintain tension.
Avoid jerking the weight or allowing your shoulders to rise off the pad.
tips: Align your elbows with the machine's axis of rotation
Press your upper arms firmly into the pad
Use a controlled tempo throughout
Train close to failure — the machine makes this safe

name: One Arm Dumbbell Preacher Curl
description:
**A unilateral preacher curl using a single dumbbell, allowing you to focus entirely on one arm at a time.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The preacher pad locks the upper arm in place, eliminating momentum, while the single-arm approach helps identify and correct strength imbalances between sides.

**Pro tip:** Press your upper arm firmly into the pad
instructions: Sit or stand at a preacher bench and drape one arm over the pad with a dumbbell held in a supinated grip.
Use your free hand to brace against the bench or your opposite shoulder for stability.
Curl the dumbbell upward in a smooth arc, keeping the back of your upper arm pressed into the pad.
Squeeze the biceps at the top, then lower the dumbbell slowly until your arm is nearly fully extended.
Complete all reps on one side before switching; avoid twisting your torso to assist the lift.
tips: Press your upper arm firmly into the pad
Use your free hand to stabilize yourself
Don't let your shoulder rise off the pad
Control the eccentric carefully — the bottom position is vulnerable

name: Overhead Cable Curl
description:
**A biceps curl performed with arms extended out to the sides at shoulder height using two high cable pulleys.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This position mimics a front double biceps pose and places constant cable tension on the biceps through the entire range, emphasizing the peak contraction.

**Pro tip:** Use a high pulley and face away from the machine
instructions: Stand in the center of a cable crossover station and grasp a high pulley handle in each hand with an underhand grip.
Extend your arms out to the sides at shoulder height so they form a straight line, palms facing the ceiling.
Curl both handles toward your ears by flexing at the elbows, keeping your upper arms parallel to the floor.
Squeeze the biceps hard in the fully contracted position, then slowly extend your arms back to the starting position.
Keep your torso upright and stationary; do not lean forward or let your elbows drop below shoulder level.
tips: Use a high pulley and face away from the machine
Keep your upper arm fixed — only the forearm moves
Curl toward the back of your head
Don't let your elbow drop

name: Preacher Curl
description:
**A classic barbell curl performed over an angled preacher bench that braces the upper arms and removes momentum from the lift.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The fixed arm position isolates the biceps and shifts peak tension toward the mid-range of elbow flexion.

**Pro tip:** Press your armpits against the top of the pad
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench and position your upper arms flat against the angled pad with your armpits resting near the top edge.
Grasp an EZ-curl or straight barbell with an underhand grip at roughly shoulder width.
Curl the bar upward until your forearms are nearly vertical, focusing on a strong biceps contraction.
Lower the bar under control, stopping just before full elbow extension to protect the joint.
Keep your wrists firm and avoid rocking your body backward to lift the weight.
tips: Press your armpits against the top of the pad
Don't let your shoulders rise as you curl
Control the eccentric carefully — the bottom is a vulnerable position
Use an EZ-bar if straight bar causes wrist pain

name: Preacher Hammer Dumbbell Curl
description:
**A preacher bench curl performed with a neutral dumbbell grip, combining the strict arm isolation of the preacher pad with the brachialis and brachioradialis emphasis of a hammer curl.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This variation builds arm thickness alongside biceps size.

**Pro tip:** Keep your wrist neutral — thumbs up throughout
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench and place the back of one or both upper arms against the pad, holding dumbbells with palms facing each other.
Let the dumbbells hang at full arm extension down the pad.
Curl the weights upward while maintaining the neutral grip, pressing your triceps into the pad throughout.
Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly to a full stretch without bouncing at the bottom.
Keep your wrists locked in the neutral position and avoid lifting your elbows off the pad.
tips: Keep your wrist neutral — thumbs up throughout
Press your upper arm into the pad
Don't let your shoulder rise
Control the bottom portion carefully

name: Prone Incline Curl
description:
**A dumbbell curl performed lying face-down (prone) on an incline bench with the arms hanging straight toward the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

This position places the biceps under significant stretch at the bottom and makes it impossible to use body momentum, resulting in strict isolation.

**Pro tip:** Lie chest-down on a 45° incline bench
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 45 degrees and lie face-down with your chest supported on the pad and chin above the top edge.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging straight down, palms facing forward.
Curl the dumbbells upward by flexing at the elbows, keeping your upper arms perpendicular to the floor.
Squeeze the biceps at the top, then lower slowly, emphasizing the deep stretch at the bottom.
Do not swing the weights or let your shoulders roll forward off the bench.
tips: Lie chest-down on a 45° incline bench
Let arms hang straight down at the start
Curl smoothly without lifting your chest
Use lighter weight than standing curls

name: Resistance Band Curl
description:
**A standing biceps curl using a resistance band anchored under the feet.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The ascending resistance profile means tension increases as the band stretches, loading the biceps hardest in the shortened (top) position — a useful complement to free-weight curls that are hardest at mid-range.

**Pro tip:** Stand on the band with both feet for more resistance
instructions: Stand on the center of a resistance band with feet hip-width apart and grasp each end or handle with an underhand grip.
Start with your arms fully extended at your sides, band taut but not overly stretched.
Curl your hands toward your shoulders by bending at the elbows, keeping your upper arms pinned to your sides.
Squeeze hard at the top where band tension is greatest, then lower your hands back down under control.
Maintain an upright posture and avoid leaning back to compensate for the increasing resistance.
tips: Stand on the band with both feet for more resistance
Squeeze hard at the top where resistance is highest
Use a slow eccentric
Can be done anywhere — great for travel

name: Reverse Barbell Preacher Curls
description:
**A preacher curl performed with an overhand (pronated) grip on a barbell, shifting the primary workload from the biceps to the brachioradialis and wrist extensors of the forearm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: biceps.

The preacher pad ensures strict form and prevents cheating.

**Pro tip:** Press upper arms into the pad
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench and place your upper arms against the pad, gripping a barbell with palms facing down at shoulder width.
Start with your arms extended down the pad, maintaining a firm wrist position.
Curl the bar upward by flexing at the elbows, focusing on driving with the top of your forearms rather than your palms.
Pause at the top, then lower the bar slowly to near-full extension.
Keep your wrists straight throughout — do not let them flex or extend under load.
tips: Press upper arms into the pad
Keep palms facing down
Don't let wrists flex under load
Control the bottom portion carefully

name: Reverse Cable Curl
description:
**A standing cable curl with a pronated (overhand) grip that targets the brachioradialis and forearm extensors.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: biceps.

The cable's constant tension throughout the range of motion makes this more effective than the barbell version for sustained forearm loading.

**Pro tip:** Use an overhand grip on a straight bar attachment
instructions: Attach a straight bar to a low cable pulley and stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart.
Grasp the bar with an overhand grip, hands about shoulder-width, and let your arms hang fully extended.
Curl the bar upward by bending at the elbows, keeping your upper arms stationary at your sides.
Squeeze the forearms at the top, then lower the bar slowly back to the start.
Maintain a rigid wrist position and avoid using momentum from the hips or back.
tips: Use an overhand grip on a straight bar attachment
Keep elbows at your sides
Extend wrists slightly to increase forearm demand
Control the eccentric

name: Reverse Curl
description:
**A standing barbell curl with palms facing down, targeting the brachioradialis and forearm extensors instead of the biceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: biceps.

This is a foundational forearm builder that also strengthens grip and contributes to overall arm thickness.

**Pro tip:** Grip the bar with palms facing down
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip at shoulder width, arms fully extended in front of your thighs.
Brace your core and pin your elbows to your sides.
Curl the bar upward in a smooth arc until your forearms approach vertical.
Pause at the top, then lower the bar under control to the starting position.
Use a lighter weight than standard curls and keep your wrists locked — do not let them bend backward.
tips: Grip the bar with palms facing down
Keep wrists rigid — don't let them flex
Keep elbows at your sides
Use lighter weight than standard curls

name: Reverse Plate Curls
description:
**A reverse curl performed by gripping a weight plate with an overhand pinch or flat-hand grip.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: biceps.

The plate's shape challenges grip strength and forearm extensors simultaneously, making it a practical forearm and grip builder with minimal equipment.

**Pro tip:** Grip the plate by its edges with thumbs on top
instructions: Stand upright and hold a weight plate with both hands using an overhand grip on the edges or rim, arms extended in front of your thighs.
Keep your elbows pinned to your sides and your core braced.
Curl the plate upward by flexing at the elbows, maintaining the pronated hand position throughout.
Squeeze the forearms at the top, then lower the plate slowly back to the start.

tips: Grip the plate by its edges with thumbs on top
Keep palms facing down
The plate grip will challenge your fingers
Use a lighter plate than you think

name: Seated Biceps
description:
**A bodyweight biceps exercise performed seated, typically by curling against your own leg resistance or using self-resisted isometric contractions.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: none.

It serves as a warm-up, rehabilitation movement, or a no-equipment option when weights are unavailable.

**Pro tip:** Hook your hands under your thighs or a fixed surface
instructions: Sit on a bench or chair with your feet flat on the floor and your back upright.
Place one hand under the opposite thigh and attempt to curl your arm upward while pressing your leg down to create resistance.
Flex the biceps hard against the self-imposed resistance, holding each contraction for 2–3 seconds.
Perform the desired number of reps, then switch arms.
Focus on maximizing the mind-muscle connection since the resistance is limited compared to weighted exercises.
tips: Hook your hands under your thighs or a fixed surface
Curl against the resistance of your own body
Focus on the squeeze at peak contraction
Use as a warm-up or finisher

name: Seated Close-Grip Concentration Barbell Curl
description:
**A seated barbell curl with a narrow grip and the elbows braced against the inner thighs, mimicking a concentration curl setup.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The close grip and fixed elbow position isolate the biceps and emphasize the peak contraction at the top of each rep.

**Pro tip:** Sit with elbows braced against your inner thighs

Curl the bar upward by flexing at the elbows, keeping your upper arms pressed firmly into your legs.
Squeeze the biceps at the top of the movement, then lower the bar slowly to full extension.

tips: Sit with elbows braced against your inner thighs
Use a narrow grip on the barbell
Curl smoothly to full flexion
Don't rock your torso

name: Seated Dumbbell Curl
description:
**A standard dumbbell curl performed while seated on a bench, which limits the ability to use leg drive or hip swing to cheat the weight up.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: biceps. Secondary: forearms.

The seated position enforces stricter form than standing curls while allowing full supination through the range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Sit upright on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor
instructions: Sit upright on a flat or low-incline bench with your back straight, feet flat on the floor, and a dumbbell in each hand at arm's length with palms facing forward.
Pin your elbows to your sides and curl both dumbbells upward simultaneously by flexing at the elbows.
Rotate your wrists slightly outward (supinate) as you curl to maximize biceps engagement.
Squeeze at the top, then lower the dumbbells under control to full arm extension.

Supinate fully throughout the curl
Don't lean back to cheat
Lower under control for 2-3 seconds

name: Seated Dumbbell Inner Biceps Curl
description: **The seated twist that lights up your inner arm like nothing else.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. This curl is performed seated with your palms rotated outward (supinated) to shift emphasis toward the inner (short) head of the biceps. Sitting down removes momentum so every rep is pure arm work. **Pro tip:** Rotate your pinkies outward at the top of each rep to maximize the squeeze on the short head. **Biomechanics:** The supinated, outward-angled grip increases activation of the short head of the biceps by placing it in a mechanically favorable line of pull throughout the curl.
instructions: Sit on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging at your sides, palms facing forward and angled slightly outward.
Keep your elbows pinned to your sides and curl both dumbbells upward, turning your pinkies outward as you lift.
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top for a one-second hold.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position under control.

Keep your back straight against the bench

Use lighter weight due to the rotation

name: Single Arm Cable Curl
description: **One arm, one cable, zero places to hide weakness.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. Using a single handle on a low cable pulley, you curl one arm at a time, which lets you focus completely on each bicep individually. The cable provides smooth, constant tension from bottom to top. **Pro tip:** Stand slightly offset from the pulley so the cable pulls at a slight angle — this keeps tension on the muscle even at the bottom. **Biomechanics:** The cable's fixed anchor point delivers consistent resistance throughout the entire range of motion, eliminating the dead spots that free weights create at the top and bottom.
instructions: Attach a single handle to a low cable pulley and grab it with one hand, palm facing up.
Stand about one step back from the machine with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Keeping your elbow locked at your side, curl the handle up toward your shoulder.

Lower the handle back down slowly, resisting the pull of the cable.
Complete all reps on one arm before switching to the other.
tips: Stand to the side of a low pulley
Keep your elbow at your side throughout
Curl to full flexion — the cable keeps tension
Match reps and weight on both sides

name: Spider Curl
description: **The gravity-defying curl that punishes your biceps from the very first inch.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. You lie face-down on an incline bench and curl dumbbells with your arms hanging straight down. This position makes the hardest part of the curl happen right at the start, creating an intense stretch and contraction. **Pro tip:** Let your arms hang completely straight at the bottom — don't cut the range short, because the stretched position is where the magic happens. **Biomechanics:** The forward-leaning torso shifts peak resistance to the early portion of the curl, loading the biceps in a lengthened position that maximizes mechanical tension at the stretch.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 45 degrees and lie face-down with your chest against the pad.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with your arms hanging straight down, palms facing forward.
Curl both dumbbells upward by bending at the elbows only — don't swing your shoulders.
Squeeze at the top, then lower the weights slowly back to full arm extension.
Keep your upper arms vertical and motionless throughout every rep.

Let your arms hang straight down
Curl smoothly — no swinging
Use lighter weight than standard curls

name: Standing Barbell Curl 21s
description: **Twenty-one reps of pure bicep punishment split into three brutal zones.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. This classic technique breaks one set into three segments of seven reps: bottom half, top half, then full range. By the final seven reps your arms are flooded with blood and burning intensely. **Pro tip:** Use a lighter barbell than your normal curl weight — you'll need it by rep 15. **Biomechanics:** Segmenting the range of motion forces the biceps to work under sustained tension without rest, and each partial range targets a different portion of the strength curve.
instructions: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart, arms fully extended.
Perform 7 reps curling from the bottom up to the halfway point (elbows at 90 degrees), then lower back down.
Without resting, perform 7 reps from the halfway point up to your shoulders, then back to halfway.
Without resting, perform 7 full-range curls from the bottom all the way to the top.
Keep your elbows pinned at your sides and avoid swinging your body throughout all 21 reps.
tips: Use about 60% of your normal curl weight
Bottom 7: from full extension to 90°
Top 7: from 90° to full flexion
Full 7: complete range of motion

name: Standing Biceps Cable Curl
description: **Constant tension from start to finish — your biceps never get a break.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. Standing in front of a low cable pulley with a straight or EZ-bar attachment, you curl the weight up while the cable keeps pulling against you the entire time. This makes every inch of the rep productive. **Pro tip:** Take one small step back from the pulley to keep the cable path angled and maintain tension at the very bottom of the curl. **Biomechanics:** Unlike a free-weight curl where gravity reduces tension at the top, the cable's horizontal force vector ensures the biceps remain loaded through full elbow flexion.
instructions: Attach a straight bar or EZ-bar to a low cable pulley.
Stand facing the machine, grab the bar with both hands using an underhand grip, and step back slightly.
With your elbows at your sides, curl the bar up toward your chest.
Hold the top position for a brief squeeze.
Lower the bar back down under control until your arms are fully extended.
Repeat without letting the weight stack touch down between reps.
tips: Stand a step back from the pulley
Keep elbows at your sides
Squeeze at the top — tension is still there
Control the eccentric fully

name: Standing Biceps Stretch
description: **The doorway-free arm opener that melts away tightness after a heavy curl session.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, chest, shoulders. Stand and extend your arms behind you with your palms facing down to stretch the front of your arms and chest. This is a simple but effective way to restore flexibility after intense bicep training. **Pro tip:** Interlace your fingers behind your back and gently lift your hands upward to deepen the stretch without forcing it.
