Reach both arms behind you, interlace your fingers with palms facing inward.
Slowly straighten your arms and lift your hands away from your body until you feel a stretch in your biceps and chest.
Hold the stretch for 20 to 30 seconds while breathing deeply.
Release gently and repeat 2 to 3 times.
tips: Extend your arm behind you with palm facing away from your body
Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
Don't force the stretch — ease into it
Breathe normally throughout

name: Standing Concentration Curl
description: **The bent-over isolation move that forces your bicep to do all the work solo.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. While standing in a bent-over position, you brace your elbow against your inner thigh and curl one dumbbell at a time. This locks out any cheating and puts 100% of the effort on the working arm. **Pro tip:** Don't rush the lowering phase — a slow 2-3 second negative dramatically increases the muscle-building stimulus. **Biomechanics:** Bracing the elbow against the thigh eliminates shoulder involvement and momentum, isolating the biceps through a full range of elbow flexion.
instructions: Stand with your feet wider than shoulder-width and bend forward at the hips.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand and press the back of that upper arm against your inner thigh.
Let the dumbbell hang at full arm extension, palm facing away from your leg.
Curl the dumbbell up toward your shoulder, keeping your upper arm pressed against your thigh.
Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly back to the start.
Complete all reps on one side before switching arms.
tips: Bend at the waist and let your arm hang straight down
Curl without swinging your torso
Squeeze at the top for 1 second
Use your free hand on your knee for support

name: Standing Dumbbell Reverse Curl
description: **Flip your grip and unlock the forearm size most lifters completely ignore.** **Muscles worked:** forearms, biceps. By curling with an overhand (palms-down) grip, you shift the workload from the biceps onto the brachioradialis and forearm extensors. This builds thicker forearms and a stronger grip. **Pro tip:** Use a thumbless grip (thumb on the same side as your fingers) to further increase forearm activation. **Biomechanics:** The pronated grip reduces the biceps' mechanical advantage, forcing the brachioradialis and wrist extensors to become the primary movers during elbow flexion.
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with an overhand grip (palms facing your thighs).
Keep your elbows pinned to your sides and curl both dumbbells upward.
Maintain the overhand grip throughout — don't let your wrists rotate.
Squeeze at the top, then lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position.
Keep your wrists straight and firm throughout every rep.

Don't rotate to supination as you curl
Keep elbows at your sides
Lower under control

name: Standing Inner-Biceps Curl
description: **Turn your wrists out and watch the inner head of your biceps come alive.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. This standing curl variation uses an outward-rotated grip to emphasize the short (inner) head of the biceps, helping build that coveted arm thickness when viewed from the front. The standing position lets you use slightly more weight than the seated version. **Pro tip:** Think about pointing your pinkies toward the ceiling at the top of each rep to maximize inner head engagement. **Biomechanics:** Externally rotating the forearm during the curl shifts the line of pull to favor the short head of the biceps, which crosses the shoulder joint on the inner side of the arm.

Angle your hands slightly outward so your pinkies are higher than your thumbs.
Curl both dumbbells up while maintaining that outward rotation.
Squeeze hard at the top for a one-second pause.
Lower the dumbbells under control back to the starting position.

Keep elbows at your sides
Don't lean back
Use lighter weight than standard curls

name: Standing One-Arm Cable Curl
description: **Solo cable work that exposes and fixes every imbalance between your arms.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. Using a single handle on a low pulley, you curl one arm at a time while standing. The cable provides non-stop resistance, and the unilateral approach ensures your stronger arm can't compensate for the weaker one. **Pro tip:** Always start with your weaker arm and match those reps on the stronger side to even out imbalances over time. **Biomechanics:** The single-arm setup combined with cable resistance creates uniform tension across the full range of motion while demanding greater core stabilization to resist rotational forces.
instructions: Attach a single handle to a low cable pulley and grasp it with one hand, palm facing up.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart, one small step back from the stack.
Pin your elbow to your side and curl the handle toward your shoulder.
Squeeze at the top, then lower the handle slowly back to full extension.
Complete all reps on one arm, then switch to the other side.
tips: Stand to the side of a low pulley
Keep your elbow pinned to your side
Curl to full flexion
Match volume and load on both arms

name: Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Curl Over Incline Bench
description: **Drape your arm over a bench and feel every fiber of your bicep working in isolation.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. Standing behind an incline bench, you rest the back of one arm over the pad and curl a dumbbell. The bench acts as a brace that eliminates all cheating and momentum. **Pro tip:** Set the bench to about 55-60 degrees so your arm hangs at a natural angle without straining your shoulder. **Biomechanics:** The incline pad locks the upper arm in place, preventing shoulder flexion and body English, which isolates the elbow flexors and ensures the biceps handle the full load.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 55-60 degrees and stand behind it.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand and drape the back of your upper arm over the top of the bench pad.
Let the dumbbell hang at full arm extension with your palm facing up.
Curl the dumbbell upward, keeping your upper arm pressed against the pad.
Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly back to full extension.
Complete all reps on one arm before switching.

Keep your chest against the bench for stability
Curl smoothly to full flexion
Control the eccentric — the bottom is vulnerable

name: Two-Arm Dumbbell Preacher Curl
description: **Lock your arms to the pad and let your biceps do the talking with nowhere to hide.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. Sitting at a preacher bench with a dumbbell in each hand, you curl against the angled pad that pins your upper arms in place. This removes all momentum and forces strict form on every single rep. **Pro tip:** Don't let your arms go completely limp at the bottom — maintain a slight bend to keep tension on the biceps and protect your elbows. **Biomechanics:** The preacher pad positions the upper arms in front of the body, which shifts peak tension toward the mid-range of the curl and reduces the ability to use body momentum.
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench and place the backs of both upper arms flat against the pad.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing up, arms extended but not locked out.
Curl both dumbbells upward simultaneously, keeping your upper arms pressed into the pad.
Squeeze your biceps at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back down, stopping just short of full extension to maintain tension.

Curl both dumbbells simultaneously
Don't let shoulders rise off the pad
Control the eccentric carefully at the bottom

name: Waiter Curl
description: **Serve up serious bicep peaks by cradling one dumbbell like a platter.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. You hold a single dumbbell vertically with both palms pressing against the underside of the top bell, then curl it up. This unique grip position keeps your biceps under tension and emphasizes the long head for peak development. **Pro tip:** Keep your elbows slightly in front of your body rather than at your sides to increase the range of motion and peak contraction. **Biomechanics:** The palms-up, narrow grip with elbows forward places the long head of the biceps in a shortened position at the top, maximizing peak contraction and emphasizing the muscle's role in forearm supination.

Keep your elbows close together and slightly in front of your torso.
Curl the dumbbell upward toward your chin, keeping both palms pressed firmly against the weight.

Lower the dumbbell slowly back to the starting position with control.

Keep elbows close together and pinned to your sides

name: Wide-Grip Standing Barbell Curl
description: **Widen your grip and shift the spotlight onto the inner head for thicker-looking arms.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. By taking a grip wider than shoulder-width on a barbell, you change the angle of pull to emphasize the short (inner) head of the biceps. This is a simple grip adjustment that produces a noticeably different stimulus. **Pro tip:** Don't go excessively wide — just a few inches outside shoulder-width is enough. Going too wide can strain your wrists. **Biomechanics:** The wider grip externally rotates the shoulders slightly and changes the forearm angle, placing the short head of the biceps in a stronger mechanical position throughout the curl.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and grip a barbell with an underhand grip, hands several inches wider than shoulder-width.
Let the barbell hang at arm's length in front of your thighs.
Keeping your elbows pinned at your sides, curl the barbell up toward your chest.
Squeeze at the top, then lower the barbell slowly back to the starting position.

Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Don't lean back
Lower under control

name: Zottman Curl
description: **The two-for-one curl that builds your biceps on the way up and your forearms on the way down.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. You curl dumbbells up with palms facing up, then rotate to a palms-down grip at the top and lower with that overhand position. This trains both the biceps and the forearm extensors in a single fluid movement. **Pro tip:** Make the lowering phase extra slow (3-4 seconds) — that's where the forearm work happens, so don't waste it. **Biomechanics:** The supinated concentric phase maximizes biceps activation, while the pronated eccentric phase loads the brachioradialis and wrist extensors, training two movement patterns in one exercise.
instructions: Stand with a dumbbell in each hand, arms at your sides, palms facing forward.
Curl both dumbbells up toward your shoulders with the palms-up grip.
At the top, rotate your wrists so your palms face downward (overhand grip).
Lower the dumbbells slowly with this overhand grip back to the starting position.
At the bottom, rotate your palms back to facing forward and repeat.

Rotate to pronated at the top before lowering
Lower slowly with the pronated grip — this is the forearm portion
Use lighter weight than standard curls

name: Zottman Preacher Curl
description: **Combine the preacher pad's strict form with the Zottman's grip rotation for an arm-building double threat.** **Muscles worked:** biceps, forearms. Performed on a preacher bench, this variation adds the signature palm rotation of the Zottman curl to the locked-in position of the preacher pad. Your biceps power the way up, and your forearms fight the way down. **Pro tip:** Use lighter weight than your regular preacher curl — the overhand lowering phase is significantly harder than you expect. **Biomechanics:** The preacher pad eliminates momentum while the grip rotation ensures both the biceps (concentric) and brachioradialis (eccentric) receive targeted loading within a single set.
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench with a dumbbell in each hand, upper arms flat against the pad, palms facing up.
Curl both dumbbells up toward your shoulders, keeping your arms pressed into the pad.
At the top, rotate your wrists so your palms face downward.
Lower the dumbbells slowly with the overhand grip until your arms are nearly extended.

Curl up with palms facing up
Rotate to palms down at the top
Lower slowly with the pronated grip

name: Band Skull Crusher
description: **Resistance that ramps up as you extend — your triceps work hardest exactly where they're strongest.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. Using a resistance band anchored behind you, you extend your arms overhead in a skull-crusher motion. The band gets harder as it stretches, matching your triceps' natural strength curve perfectly. **Pro tip:** Anchor the band low behind you and step forward until there's already some tension at the starting position — no dead zone at the bottom. **Biomechanics:** The ascending resistance profile of the band increases load as the elbow extends, aligning peak resistance with the triceps' strongest mechanical position near full lockout.
instructions: Anchor a resistance band to a low, sturdy point behind you.
Face away from the anchor, hold both ends of the band overhead with your elbows bent and pointing forward.
Extend your arms straight overhead by straightening your elbows against the band's resistance.
Squeeze your triceps at full extension for a brief pause.
Slowly bend your elbows to return to the starting position, controlling the band's pull.
Keep your upper arms stationary and pointing upward throughout.
tips: Anchor the band behind your head
Keep upper arms vertical
Control the eccentric as band tension drops
Focus on locking out fully

name: Bench Dips
description: **All you need is a bench and your own bodyweight to torch your triceps anywhere.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, chest, shoulders. With your hands on a bench behind you and your feet on the floor, you lower and raise your body using your arms. It's one of the most accessible tricep exercises that still delivers serious results. **Pro tip:** Keep your back close to the bench as you dip — drifting forward shifts the load to your shoulders and away from your triceps. **Biomechanics:** The vertical pressing pattern with hands behind the torso places the triceps in a stretched position at the bottom, and the ascending strength curve means the movement gets easier as you lock out.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a sturdy bench and place your hands on the edge beside your hips, fingers pointing forward.
Slide your hips off the bench with your legs extended in front of you (bend knees to make it easier).
Lower your body by bending your elbows to about 90 degrees, keeping your back close to the bench.
Press through your palms to push yourself back up until your arms are straight.
Repeat without locking your elbows aggressively at the top.
tips: Keep your back close to the bench throughout
Don't go too deep — excessive shoulder extension can cause impingement
Press through your palms to lockout
Add weight on your lap for progression

name: Bench Press - Powerlifting
description: **The competition-style press built for moving maximum weight off your chest.** **Muscles worked:** chest, triceps, shoulders, forearms. This powerlifting variation uses a wider grip, an arched back, and a pause on the chest to maximize the weight you can press. It's a full-body effort disguised as an upper-body lift. **Pro tip:** Drive your feet into the floor and squeeze your shoulder blades together before unracking — this creates a stable base for heavier loads. **Biomechanics:** The arch reduces range of motion and positions the shoulders in a safer, retracted position, while leg drive transfers force through the kinetic chain to assist the press off the chest.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with your eyes under the barbell, feet flat on the floor.
Arch your upper back, squeeze your shoulder blades together, and grip the barbell wider than shoulder-width.
Unrack the bar with straight arms and position it over your lower chest.
Lower the barbell to your chest in a controlled manner, pausing briefly with the bar touching your chest.
Press the barbell back up explosively to full lockout, driving your feet into the floor.
Re-rack the bar carefully after completing your reps.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Bench Press with Chains
description: **Add chains to the bar and the weight grows heavier as you press — training lockout power like nothing else.** **Muscles worked:** chest, triceps, shoulders. Chains hang from each end of the barbell and pile on the floor at the bottom, then lift off as you push up. The load increases through the range of motion, overloading the top where you're strongest. **Pro tip:** Ensure the chains are long enough that most links rest on the floor at the bottom — if they're all hanging, you lose the variable resistance effect. **Biomechanics:** The progressive chain loading creates accommodating resistance matching the bench press's ascending strength curve, reducing joint stress at the bottom while overloading lockout.
instructions: Set up on a flat bench with chains draped evenly over each end of the barbell.
Ensure the chains pile on the floor when the bar is at chest level.
Unrack the barbell, holding it at arm's length over your chest.
Lower the bar to your chest as the chains settle onto the floor.
Press the bar back up — the weight increases as chain links lift off the ground.
Complete your reps and re-rack carefully.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Board Press
description: **Shorten the range and overload your lockout to smash through bench press sticking points.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, chest, shoulders, forearms. A board (or stack of boards) is placed on your chest to limit how far the barbell descends. This lets you handle heavier weight and specifically strengthens the top portion of the press where the triceps dominate. **Pro tip:** Use 2-3 boards for most lockout work — a single board barely changes the range, while too many boards turns it into a different exercise entirely. **Biomechanics:** By eliminating the bottom portion of the press, the board press reduces pec stretch and shifts primary demand to the triceps and anterior deltoids, training the specific joint angles where most lifters fail.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and have a partner hold a board (or stacked boards) on your chest.
Grip the barbell with your normal bench press grip and unrack it.
Lower the barbell until it touches the board — do not press through the board into your chest.
Press the barbell back up to full lockout.
Repeat for the desired reps, ensuring the bar touches the board consistently on each rep.
tips: Use 2-3 boards to limit ROM to the top half
Press explosively off the boards
Keep elbows at 45° to your torso
Use a spotter to hold the boards

name: Body Tricep Press
description: **A sneaky bodyweight move that isolates your triceps using nothing but a sturdy surface.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. Place your hands on an elevated surface like a counter or bar, lean forward, and extend your arms to push yourself back. It mimics a skull crusher but uses your body as the resistance. **Pro tip:** The lower the surface, the harder the exercise — start with a counter height and progress to a lower bar as you get stronger. **Biomechanics:** The body angle creates a lever arm that loads the triceps through elbow extension, similar to an overhead tricep extension but with bodyweight as the resistance variable.
instructions: Stand facing a sturdy elevated surface (counter, bar, or railing) and place both hands on it, shoulder-width apart.
Walk your feet back until your body is at an angle, arms extended.
Bend only at the elbows to lower your head toward or below the surface, keeping your body straight.
Press through your hands to extend your elbows and push yourself back to the starting position.
Keep your elbows pointing forward, not flaring out, throughout the movement.
tips: Use a bar or bench at waist height
Keep your body straight — don't bend at the hips
Lower by bending only at the elbows
Press back to full extension

name: Body-Up
description: **The floor-based tricep blaster that looks easy until you actually try it.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, abs, forearms. Lying face-down, you place your palms flat on the floor near your chest and press your entire upper body off the ground using only your triceps — no chest push-up motion involved. It's deceptively challenging. **Pro tip:** Keep your elbows tucked tight to your ribs — if they flare out, your chest takes over and the tricep focus is lost. **Biomechanics:** By initiating the press from a prone position with elbows close to the body, the movement isolates elbow extension while the core must stabilize against spinal extension forces.
instructions: Lie face-down on the floor with your palms flat beside your chest, elbows bent and tucked close to your body.
Instead of a push-up, press through your palms to lift only your upper body by straightening your elbows.
Your hips and legs stay on the ground — only your torso rises.
Lower yourself back down with control until your chest touches the floor.
Keep your core tight and elbows close to your sides throughout.
tips: Start with forearms on a bar or surface
Press up by extending your elbows
Keep your body rigid throughout
Lower under control

name: Bodyweight Tricep Extension
description: **Turn any bar or ledge into a tricep-sculpting station using just your body.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. Grip a bar at about waist height, lean forward with your body straight, and bend at the elbows to lower your head below the bar, then extend back up. It's like a skull crusher standing up. **Pro tip:** The further your feet are from the bar, the more of your bodyweight you'll be pressing — adjust your foot position to match your strength level. **Biomechanics:** The overhead-style elbow extension with a fixed hand position creates a long lever arm that loads the long head of the triceps through a full stretch-to-contraction range.
instructions: Grip a sturdy bar or ledge at about waist height with both hands, shoulder-width apart.
Walk your feet back so your body forms a straight line at an angle to the bar.
Keeping your body rigid, bend at the elbows to lower your forehead toward or below the bar.
Extend your elbows to press yourself back to the starting position.
Keep your elbows pointing forward and your core braced throughout.
tips: The more horizontal your body, the harder it is
Keep your body in a straight line
Lower by bending only at the elbows — not the hips
Press to full lockout

name: Cable Incline Triceps Extension
description: **Lie back on an incline and let the cable stretch your triceps into new growth territory.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. Sitting on an incline bench facing away from a low cable pulley, you extend a rope or bar overhead. The incline plus cable tension creates a deep stretch on the long head of the triceps at the bottom of every rep. **Pro tip:** Let your elbows drift slightly behind your head at the bottom to maximize the stretch — that's the whole point of the incline angle. **Biomechanics:** The incline places the shoulder in flexion, pre-stretching the long head across the shoulder joint, and the cable maintains tension through the full range including the stretched position.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 45 degrees facing away from a low cable pulley.
Attach a rope or bar to the low pulley, sit back, and hold the attachment overhead with arms extended.
Keeping your upper arms stationary, bend your elbows to lower the attachment behind your head.
Feel a deep stretch in your triceps at the bottom.
Extend your elbows to press back overhead to full lockout and squeeze.
tips: Set an incline bench in front of a low pulley
Keep upper arms fixed — only extend at the elbows
Lock out fully at the top
Control the eccentric

name: Cable Lying Triceps Extension
description:
**A supine cable triceps exercise that maintains constant tension throughout the entire range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

Lying on a flat bench with the cable anchored low behind you, this movement isolates the triceps while the cable's pulley system eliminates dead spots found in free-weight skull crushers.

**Pro tip:** Lie on a bench with head toward a low pulley
instructions: Position a flat bench in front of a low cable pulley and attach a straight or EZ-bar handle.
Lie face-up on the bench with your head closest to the cable machine, grasping the handle with an overhand grip.
Extend your arms so they point straight up toward the ceiling — this is your start position.
Keeping your upper arms stationary and elbows pointed upward, bend at the elbows to lower the handle toward your forehead.
Lower until your forearms are roughly parallel to the floor or you feel a deep stretch in the triceps.
Reverse the motion by contracting your triceps to press the handle back to full lockout.
Maintain tight elbows throughout — avoid letting them flare outward.
tips: Lie on a bench with head toward a low pulley
Keep upper arms vertical
Extend to full lockout — cable keeps tension there
Control the eccentric smoothly

name: Cable One Arm Tricep Extension
description:
**A unilateral cable pushdown variation that allows you to address left-right triceps imbalances while benefiting from the cable's smooth, continuous resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

Working one arm at a time also lets you focus entirely on the mind-muscle connection with each rep.

**Pro tip:** Use a single handle on a high pulley
instructions: Attach a single-grip handle to a high cable pulley and stand facing the machine.
Grasp the handle with one hand using an overhand or neutral grip, elbow pinned to your side at roughly 90 degrees.
Brace your core and keep your torso upright — avoid leaning into the movement.
Press the handle downward by extending your elbow until your arm is fully straight.
Squeeze the triceps hard at the bottom for a one-count hold.
Slowly allow the handle to return to the start position under control.
Complete all prescribed reps on one arm before switching to the other.
tips: Use a single handle on a high pulley
Keep your elbow pinned to your side
Extend to full lockout
Match reps and weight on both arms

name: Cable Overhead Extension
description:
**An overhead triceps extension performed facing away from a low or mid-height cable pulley.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: shoulders.

The overhead arm position places the long head of the triceps under a meaningful stretch, while the cable provides even resistance from bottom to top, making it a highly effective mass builder for the back of the arm.

**Pro tip:** Face away from a high or low pulley
instructions: Attach a rope or straight bar to a cable set at low-to-mid height.
Grasp the attachment, turn away from the machine, and stagger your stance for balance.
Lean your torso slightly forward with arms overhead, elbows bent and pointing toward the ceiling.
Without moving your upper arms, extend your elbows to press the attachment forward and upward until your arms are straight.
Hold the peak contraction briefly, then slowly bend your elbows to return to the deep stretch position.
Keep your elbows close to your head throughout — do not let them drift wide.
Maintain a neutral spine; avoid excessive arching of the lower back.
tips: Face away from a high or low pulley
Keep upper arms close to your ears
Extend fully at the top
Don't let your elbows flare out

name: Cable Pushdown
description:
**The quintessential cable triceps exercise and a staple in virtually every arm-training program.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

Standing in front of a high pulley, you press a bar or rope attachment downward by extending the elbows, keeping constant tension on the triceps from stretch to contraction with minimal joint stress.

**Pro tip:** Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout
instructions: Attach a straight bar, V-bar, or rope handle to a high cable pulley.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent.
Grip the attachment with both hands and tuck your elbows firmly against your sides.
Starting with your forearms roughly parallel to the floor, press the handle straight down by extending your elbows.
Lock out completely at the bottom and squeeze the triceps for a brief pause.
Under control, allow the handle to rise back to the starting position — stop when your forearms reach parallel.
Keep your shoulders down and back; the only joints moving should be your elbows.
tips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout
Extend to full lockout on every rep
Don't lean forward excessively to cheat
Control the eccentric — don't let the weight stack pull you

name: Cable Rope Overhead Triceps Extension
description:
**A rope-specific overhead extension that combines the long-head stretch of an overhead position with the ability to spread the rope ends apart at lockout for an intensified peak contraction.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: shoulders.

The cable ensures the triceps are loaded through every degree of the movement.

**Pro tip:** Face away from a low pulley with the rope overhead
instructions: Attach a rope handle to a low cable pulley.
Grasp both ends of the rope, turn away from the machine, and step forward into a staggered stance.
Bring the rope behind your head with elbows bent and pointed forward, upper arms close to your ears.
Extend your elbows to drive the rope upward and forward until your arms are fully straight.
At the top, actively spread the rope ends apart to maximize the triceps squeeze.
Slowly return to the stretched position behind your head, feeling the long head lengthen.
Keep your core braced and avoid using momentum or arching your back to move the weight.
tips: Face away from a low pulley with the rope overhead
Keep upper arms close to your ears
Split the rope apart at full extension for peak contraction
Don't let elbows flare wide

name: Chain Handle Extension
description:
**A bodyweight triceps extension performed using suspended chain handles or similar apparatus.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The movement mimics a skull crusher pattern but uses your own body weight as resistance, creating a bell-shaped resistance curve that peaks in the mid-range of elbow extension.

**Pro tip:** Grip the chains or handles at chest height
instructions: Set chain handles or suspension straps at roughly waist height.
Grasp the handles with an overhand grip and walk your feet back so your body forms an angled plank.
Keep your elbows pointed forward and your upper arms fixed in position.
Lower your body by bending only at the elbows, letting your head travel between or below the handles.
Once you reach a deep stretch in the triceps, reverse the motion by forcefully extending your elbows.
Lock out fully at the top and squeeze the triceps.
Adjust difficulty by walking your feet closer to the anchor (harder) or further away (easier).
tips: Grip the chains or handles at chest height
Lower by bending only at the elbows
Keep your body rigid
Press to full lockout

name: Close Grip Smith Machine Press
description:
**A Smith machine bench press performed with a narrow grip to shift emphasis from the chest onto the triceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

The fixed bar path removes the need for lateral stabilization, allowing you to focus purely on pressing heavy loads through the triceps while the chest and front delts assist.

**Pro tip:** Grip the bar at shoulder width or slightly narrower
instructions: Position a flat bench centered under the Smith machine bar.
Lie back and grip the bar with hands roughly shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
Unrack the bar by rotating the hooks and hold it at arm's length above your mid-chest.
Lower the bar in a controlled manner until it lightly touches your lower chest, keeping elbows tucked at about 30-45 degrees from your torso.
Press the bar back up explosively by driving through your triceps until your elbows lock out.
Re-rack by twisting the bar back into the hooks after completing your set.
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest or flaring your elbows wide.
tips: Grip the bar at shoulder width or slightly narrower
Lower to your lower chest
Press to full lockout
Keep elbows at 30-45° to your torso

name: Close-Grip Barbell Bench Press
description:
**A foundational barbell pressing movement with a narrowed hand placement that prioritizes the triceps as the primary mover.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

It allows heavy loading and is one of the most effective compound exercises for building triceps size and pressing strength simultaneously.

**Pro tip:** Grip the bar at shoulder width — going too narrow stresses the wrists
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the barbell with hands approximately shoulder-width apart — no narrower, to protect the wrists.
Unrack the bar with straight arms, positioning it directly above your lower chest.
Retract your shoulder blades and plant your feet firmly on the floor.
Lower the bar under control to your lower chest, keeping your elbows tucked close to your body at roughly 30 degrees.
Press the bar back up by extending your elbows powerfully, focusing on driving through the triceps.
Lock out completely at the top before beginning the next rep.
Keep your wrists stacked over your elbows throughout the movement.
tips: Grip the bar at shoulder width — going too narrow stresses the wrists
Lower to your lower chest/sternum
Drive through to full lockout
Keep elbows at 30-45° to your torso

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
description:
**The classic close-grip bench press — a heavy compound lift that targets the triceps by narrowing the grip on a standard barbell.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

The reduced grip width shortens the moment arm at the shoulder and increases the demand on elbow extension, making the triceps work harder through every inch of the press.

**Pro tip:** Hands at shoulder width — not too narrow
instructions: Set up on a flat bench with eyes under the bar and grip the barbell at shoulder width.
Arch your upper back slightly, pin your shoulder blades together, and plant your feet.
Unrack the bar and hold it at arm's length over your chest.
Lower the bar slowly to your sternum, keeping elbows tight to your sides throughout the descent.
Touch your chest lightly — no bouncing — then drive the bar upward by extending your elbows.
Exhale as you press through the sticking point and lock out at the top.
Maintain a controlled tempo; avoid excessively fast negatives that reduce triceps time under tension.
tips: Hands at shoulder width — not too narrow
Tuck elbows to 30-45°
Touch your lower chest
Press to full lockout

name: Close-Grip Dumbbell Press
description:
**A dumbbell bench press variation where the dumbbells are held together or very close throughout the movement, shifting the workload from the chest to the triceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

The neutral or semi-pronated grip is generally more wrist-friendly than a barbell close-grip press.

**Pro tip:** Press the dumbbells together throughout the movement
instructions: Sit on a flat bench with a dumbbell in each hand, then lie back, pressing the dumbbells together above your chest with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Keep the dumbbells in contact with each other throughout the entire set.
Lower both dumbbells together toward your mid-chest by bending your elbows, keeping them tucked close to your torso.
Descend until the dumbbells touch your chest or you reach a comfortable depth.
Press the dumbbells back up by extending your elbows, actively squeezing the weights together.
Lock out at the top and hold for a brief contraction.
Focus on pushing the dumbbells inward against each other to increase inner-chest and triceps activation.
tips: Press the dumbbells together throughout the movement
Keep elbows close to your torso
Press to full lockout
Lower under control to your chest

name: Close-Grip EZ-Bar Press
description:
**A close-grip pressing movement using an EZ-bar, whose angled grips reduce wrist strain compared to a straight barbell.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

This makes it an excellent option for lifters who experience discomfort during standard close-grip bench presses while still delivering strong triceps overload.

**Pro tip:** Grip the inner cambers of the EZ bar
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and have a partner hand you an EZ-bar, or unrack it from low J-hooks.
Grip the inner angled portions of the bar so your hands are roughly shoulder-width apart.
Hold the bar at arm's length above your lower chest with elbows locked.
Lower the bar under control to your chest, keeping elbows tucked at approximately 30 degrees from your torso.
Lightly touch your chest, then press the bar back up by powerfully extending your elbows.
Lock out fully at the top before starting the next repetition.
Keep your wrists in a neutral alignment — the EZ-bar's camber should naturally support this.
tips: Grip the inner cambers of the EZ bar
Lower to your chest with elbows tucked
Press to full lockout
Keep your back flat on the bench

name: Close-Grip Push-Up off of a Dumbbell
description:
**A close-grip push-up performed with both hands placed on a single dumbbell standing upright on the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders, abs.

The narrow, elevated hand position intensifies triceps recruitment while the instability of gripping the dumbbell engages the core and forearm stabilizers.

**Pro tip:** Place hands on the dumbbell handle close together
instructions: Place a hexagonal dumbbell upright on the floor in a stable position.
Get into a push-up position with both hands gripping the sides of the dumbbell handle, fingers wrapped around it.
Set your feet at hip width and brace your core so your body forms a rigid plank.
Lower your chest toward the dumbbell by bending your elbows, keeping them tucked close to your ribs.
Descend until your chest nearly touches the dumbbell head.
Push yourself back up by extending your elbows, squeezing the triceps at the top.
Move deliberately — the unstable surface rewards control over speed.
tips: Place hands on the dumbbell handle close together
Keep your body in a straight line
Lower your chest to the dumbbell
Press to full lockout

name: Decline Close Grip Bench Press
description:
**A close-grip bench press performed on a decline bench, which shifts the pressing angle to reduce shoulder involvement and place greater emphasis on the triceps and lower chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

The decline also tends to shorten the range of motion slightly, often allowing heavier loads.

**Pro tip:** Secure your legs under the pads
instructions: Secure your legs under the pads of a decline bench set to approximately 15-30 degrees.
Grip the barbell at shoulder width and unrack it with straight arms.
Position the bar above your lower chest with your shoulder blades retracted.
Lower the bar in a controlled arc to your lower sternum, keeping elbows tucked at your sides.
Touch your chest gently, then drive the bar back up by extending your elbows forcefully.
Lock out at the top and repeat for the prescribed reps.
Always use a spotter or safety pins — the decline position makes self-rescue difficult.
tips: Secure your legs under the pads
Use a shoulder-width grip
Lower to your lower chest
Press to full lockout

name: Decline Close-Grip Bench To Skull Crusher
description:
**A hybrid movement that combines a decline close-grip press with a skull crusher in a single fluid rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

You press the bar up from your chest, then transition into an elbow-only extension at the top, effectively doubling the triceps stimulus per repetition and creating a uniquely demanding training effect.

**Pro tip:** Use a decline bench with a lighter weight than either exercise alone
instructions: Set up on a decline bench with your legs secured and grip a barbell or EZ-bar at shoulder width.
Unrack the bar and hold it at arm's length above your lower chest.
Lower the bar to your chest with elbows tucked, then press it back up to lockout — this is the close-grip press portion.
From the locked-out position, keep your upper arms vertical and bend only at the elbows to lower the bar toward your forehead or just behind it.
Extend your elbows to return the bar to the top — this completes the skull crusher portion.
That entire sequence counts as one rep. Repeat for the prescribed count.
Use a moderate weight — the combined movement is more fatiguing than either exercise alone.
tips: Use a decline bench with a lighter weight than either exercise alone
Lower to your forehead first (skull crusher portion)
Then press from your chest (press portion)
Keep elbows from flaring

name: Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
**A skull-crusher-style triceps extension performed on a decline bench with dumbbells.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The decline angle shifts the resistance curve so that tension remains high through the mid-range, while using dumbbells allows each arm to work independently and move through its natural path.

**Pro tip:** Secure your legs under the pads
instructions: Secure yourself on a decline bench set to 15-30 degrees with a dumbbell in each hand.
Press the dumbbells to arm's length above your shoulders with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Lock your upper arms in place, pointing straight up toward the ceiling.
Bend at the elbows to lower the dumbbells slowly toward the sides of your head.
Descend until you feel a strong stretch in the triceps — roughly when your forearms pass parallel to the floor.
Reverse the motion by extending your elbows, pressing the dumbbells back to the start.
Keep the movement strict — only your forearms should be moving.
tips: Secure your legs under the pads
Keep upper arms vertical
Lower the dumbbells beside your head
Extend to full lockout

name: Decline EZ Bar Triceps Extension
description:
**A decline-bench skull crusher using an EZ-bar, combining the wrist-friendly angled grip with the altered resistance angle of the decline.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

This variation keeps tension on the triceps longer at the top of the rep compared to a flat-bench version, since gravity still pulls the bar toward your face even near lockout.

**Pro tip:** Secure your legs and grip the EZ-bar on the inner cambers
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with your legs secured and grip an EZ-bar on the inner cambers.
Press the bar to arm's length directly above your shoulders.
Keeping your upper arms fixed and perpendicular to the floor, bend your elbows to lower the bar toward your forehead.
Lower under control until the bar is just above or slightly behind your forehead.
Without pausing, extend your elbows to drive the bar back to the starting position.
Squeeze the triceps at lockout before beginning the next rep.
Use a controlled tempo — the decline makes it harder to bail if you lose control of the bar.
tips: Secure your legs and grip the EZ-bar on the inner cambers
Keep upper arms vertical or slightly angled back
Lower the bar toward your forehead
Extend to full lockout

name: Diamond Close Grip Push-Up
description:
**A push-up variation where the hands are placed close together beneath the chest with thumbs and index fingers forming a diamond shape.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

This extremely narrow hand position maximizes triceps involvement and has been shown in EMG studies to be one of the most effective bodyweight triceps exercises available.

**Pro tip:** Form a diamond with your thumbs and index fingers
instructions: Get into a push-up position and place your hands directly under your chest, touching your thumbs and index fingers together to form a diamond shape.
Extend your legs behind you and brace your core so your body is a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward your hands by bending your elbows, keeping them relatively close to your body.
Descend until your chest touches or nearly touches your hands.
Push yourself back up by forcefully extending your elbows until your arms are straight.
Maintain a rigid torso throughout — no sagging hips or piked position.
To increase difficulty, elevate your feet on a bench; to decrease, perform from your knees.
tips: Form a diamond with your thumbs and index fingers
Keep elbows close to your body as you lower
Lower your chest to your hands
Press to full lockout

name: Diamond Push-Ups
description:
**The standard diamond push-up — a bodyweight staple that requires no equipment and delivers exceptional triceps activation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

By bringing the hands together under the sternum, the exercise dramatically reduces chest leverage and forces the triceps to handle the majority of the pressing load.

**Pro tip:** Thumbs and index fingers form a diamond
instructions: Assume a high plank position with your hands together beneath your sternum, forming a diamond with your thumbs and forefingers.
Set your feet hip-width apart and engage your glutes and abs to maintain a flat back.
Inhale as you lower your body toward the floor, allowing your elbows to track slightly outward.
Lower until your chest lightly contacts your hands or you reach full depth.
Exhale and press back up explosively, focusing on squeezing the triceps to straighten your arms.
Fully lock out your elbows at the top of each rep.
Perform each rep with deliberate control — avoid rushing through the eccentric phase.
tips: Thumbs and index fingers form a diamond
Keep your body straight from head to heels
Lower until your chest touches your hands
Press to full lockout

name: Dip Machine
description:
**A machine-assisted dip that replicates the pressing pattern of parallel bar dips with adjustable resistance and a guided movement path.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

It removes the balance and stabilization demands of free dips, letting you isolate the triceps more effectively and train closer to failure safely.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the seat height so handles are at chest level
instructions: Adjust the seat height so that the handles are at roughly chest level when seated.
Sit down, grip the handles with your palms facing inward or downward (depending on machine design), and place your feet flat on the floor.
Press the handles downward by extending your elbows, keeping your torso upright to emphasize the triceps.
Continue pressing until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly allow the handles to return to the starting position, feeling a stretch across the triceps.

name: Dips - Triceps Version

Place your hands on the edge, shoulder-width apart, and walk your feet back until your body is at an angle with arms extended.

Push through your palms to extend your elbows and return to the starting position.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels and avoid bending at the hips.
tips: Use a bar at about head height
Keep arms overhead and lower by bending elbows
Keep your body straight
Press to full extension

name: Parallel Bar Dip
description:
**A foundational upper body compound exercise performed on parallel bars that loads the triceps, chest, and shoulders through a vertical pressing pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

The ascending strength curve means resistance feels heaviest at the bottom stretched position and lightest at lockout, making it a powerful movement for building pressing strength and upper body mass.

**Pro tip:** Keep your torso upright — don't lean forward
instructions: Grip the parallel bars and lift yourself to a straight-arm support position with your arms locked out.
Keep your torso as upright as possible to emphasize the triceps — leaning forward shifts more work to the chest.
Bend your elbows to lower your body until your upper arms are at least parallel to the floor, maintaining control throughout the descent.
Press yourself back up by driving through your palms and extending your elbows to full lockout.
Keep your elbows relatively close to your body and avoid swinging or kipping.
Add weight via a belt or vest once bodyweight becomes manageable for your target rep range.
tips: Keep your torso upright — don't lean forward
Lower to about 90° elbow flexion
Press to full lockout
Add weight with a dip belt for progression

name: Pin Presses
description:
**A partial-range barbell press performed from safety pins set at a specific height in a power rack.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders, forearms.

By eliminating the bottom portion of the lift, pin presses shift the demand heavily onto the triceps during the lockout phase, making them a powerful tool for building pressing strength through sticking points.

**Pro tip:** Set pins at the desired height — typically at or above chest level
instructions: Set the safety pins in a power rack at or just above your typical sticking point on the bench press.
Lie on the bench and position yourself so the barbell rests on the pins at chest or mid-range height.
Grip the bar slightly narrower than your standard bench press width.
Brace your core, drive your feet into the floor, and press the bar explosively off the pins to full lockout.
Lower the bar back to the pins under control, allowing it to come to a complete dead stop before initiating the next rep.
Avoid bouncing the bar off the pins.
tips: Set pins at the desired height — typically at or above chest level
Start each rep from a dead stop on the pins
Press explosively to lockout
Use a close grip to emphasize triceps

name: Push-Ups - Close Triceps Position
description:
**A bodyweight pressing variation where the hands are placed close together beneath the chest, redirecting the majority of the workload onto the triceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

This movement doubles as an effective chest and shoulder builder while requiring zero equipment, making it ideal for home or travel training.

**Pro tip:** Place hands inside shoulder width
instructions: Assume a plank position with your hands placed directly under your chest, thumbs and index fingers nearly touching to form a diamond or narrow triangle shape.
Keep your body in a rigid straight line from head to heels by bracing your glutes and core.
Lower yourself by bending the elbows, keeping them tucked close to your torso rather than flaring outward.
Descend until your chest lightly touches your hands, then press back up forcefully to full arm extension.
If the full version is too challenging, perform the movement from your knees while maintaining a straight torso.
tips: Place hands inside shoulder width
Keep elbows close to your body
Lower your chest to your hands
Press to full lockout

name: Resistance Band Overhead Extension
description:
**An overhead triceps extension performed with a resistance band, providing increasing tension as the arm straightens.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: shoulders.

The ascending resistance profile matches the triceps' natural strength curve during extension, delivering a strong peak contraction at lockout while remaining joint-friendly.

**Pro tip:** Anchor the band under your feet or behind you
instructions: Anchor a resistance band to a low point behind you or step on one end with your foot.
Grasp the other end with both hands and raise your arms overhead so your elbows point toward the ceiling.
Start with your forearms folded behind your head, feeling a stretch in the triceps.
Extend your arms upward by straightening at the elbows until your hands are directly overhead and your arms are fully locked out.
Squeeze the triceps hard at the top, then slowly return to the stretched position.
Keep your elbows stationary and pointed forward throughout the movement.
tips: Anchor the band under your feet or behind you
Keep upper arms by your ears
Extend fully — the band is heaviest here
Control the eccentric as tension drops

name: Resistance Band Pushdown
description:
**A triceps pushdown replicated with a resistance band anchored overhead, offering portable and joint-friendly triceps isolation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The band's ascending resistance delivers progressively greater tension as the arm extends, creating an intense contraction at the bottom of each rep without heavy joint stress.

**Pro tip:** Anchor the band above head height
instructions: Secure a resistance band to a high anchor point such as a pull-up bar or the top of a door frame using a door anchor.
Face the anchor and grasp the band with both hands at roughly chest height, elbows pinned to your sides.
Press your hands downward by extending your elbows until your arms are fully straight.
Hold the bottom position for a brief squeeze, then allow your forearms to rise back to the starting position under control.
Keep your upper arms completely stationary and avoid leaning forward to use body momentum.
tips: Anchor the band above head height
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Extend to full lockout where resistance peaks
Control the return

name: Reverse Band Bench Press
description:
**A bench press variation where heavy resistance bands are looped from the top of a power rack to the barbell, providing the most assistance at the bottom of the lift and progressively less as you press upward. This accommodating resistance lets you overload the lockout while reducing stress on the shoulders and chest in the vulnerable stretched position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: chest. Secondary: triceps, shoulders, forearms.



**Pro tip:** Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
instructions: Attach heavy resistance bands to the top of a power rack on each side and loop them around the ends of the barbell.
Lie on the bench and set up as you would for a standard bench press with a full arch, retracted shoulder blades, and feet firmly planted.
Unrack the bar and lower it to your chest in a controlled manner — the bands will assist you at the bottom.
Press the bar back up explosively, recognizing that the load will feel heavier as the bands lose tension near lockout.
Focus on accelerating through the entire range of motion and locking out forcefully at the top.
tips: Retract and depress your scapulae before unracking to create a stable pressing platform
Lower the bar under control to maximize time under tension at the stretched position
Drive your feet into the floor to maintain full-body tension
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width for optimal pec activation
Avoid bouncing the bar off your chest — pause briefly at the bottom

name: Reverse Grip Triceps Pushdown
description:
**A cable pushdown performed with an underhand (supinated) grip, which shifts emphasis toward the medial head of the triceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The cable's flat resistance curve provides consistent tension throughout the entire range of motion, and the reverse grip allows for a uniquely strong contraction at full elbow extension.

**Pro tip:** Use an underhand grip on a straight bar
instructions: Stand facing a high cable pulley with a straight bar or EZ-bar attachment.
Grasp the bar with an underhand grip, palms facing the ceiling, hands about shoulder-width apart.
Pin your elbows firmly against your sides and start with your forearms roughly parallel to the floor.
Push the bar downward by extending your elbows until your arms are completely straight.
Squeeze the triceps at the bottom, then slowly allow the bar to return to the starting position.
Keep your wrists neutral and avoid curling them during the movement.
tips: Use an underhand grip on a straight bar
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Extend to full lockout
Don't lean forward excessively

name: Reverse Triceps Bench Press
description:
**A bench press performed with a reverse (supinated) grip on the barbell, which shifts the emphasis from the chest to the triceps and upper chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

The underhand grip changes the elbow tracking pattern, forcing the triceps to work harder through the pressing motion while also engaging the anterior deltoids.

**Pro tip:** Use a supinated grip at shoulder width
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grasp the barbell with an underhand grip, hands roughly shoulder-width apart.
It is strongly recommended to use a spotter or Smith machine for safety due to the grip orientation.
Unrack the bar and hold it above your lower chest with arms extended.
Lower the bar slowly to your lower chest, keeping your elbows tucked close to your body.
Press the bar back up to lockout by driving through the triceps.
Maintain a firm grip throughout and avoid letting the bar roll in your hands.
tips: Use a supinated grip at shoulder width
Use a spotter — the grip can feel unstable
Lower to your lower chest
Press to full lockout

name: Ring Dips
description:
**A challenging bodyweight dip performed on gymnastic rings, which introduces instability that forces the triceps, chest, and shoulder stabilizers to work significantly harder than on fixed bars.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

The rings allow a natural movement path and greater range of motion, making this an elite upper-body pressing exercise.

**Pro tip:** Turn the rings out at the top for full lockout
instructions: Grip a pair of gymnastic rings and jump or press yourself up to the support position with arms fully locked out and rings turned outward slightly.
Keep your core tight and body vertical.
Lower yourself by bending your elbows until your upper arms are at least parallel to the floor, or deeper if mobility allows.
The rings will want to drift outward — actively press them inward to maintain control.
Drive back up to full lockout by pressing through your palms and extending your elbows.
Turn the rings out at the top for a full contraction.
tips: Turn the rings out at the top for full lockout
Keep your torso upright for triceps emphasis
Don't go too deep — control the depth
Master parallel bar dips first

name: Rope Pushdown
description:
**A triceps isolation staple performed on a cable machine using a rope attachment, which allows the hands to split apart at the bottom for a more complete contraction.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The cable provides constant tension across the full range of motion, making this one of the most effective and elbow-friendly triceps exercises available.

**Pro tip:** Split the rope apart at the bottom for peak contraction
instructions: Attach a rope handle to a high cable pulley.
Stand facing the machine and grasp each end of the rope with a neutral grip.
Start with your elbows bent at roughly 90 degrees and pinned to your sides.
Push the rope downward by extending your elbows, and as you reach the bottom, actively spread the rope ends apart by pulling your hands away from each other.
Squeeze the triceps hard in this split position, then slowly return to the start.
Keep your torso upright and avoid using momentum from your shoulders or hips.
tips: Split the rope apart at the bottom for peak contraction
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Extend to full lockout before splitting
Don't lean forward to cheat

name: Seated Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
**A single-arm triceps kickback performed from a seated, bent-over position, which stabilizes the torso and reduces momentum cheating.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

This isolation movement targets the triceps in a shortened position, emphasizing the peak contraction and the lateral head of the muscle.

**Pro tip:** Bend forward with your upper arm parallel to the floor
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand and raise your upper arm so it is parallel to the floor, elbow bent at 90 degrees.
Keeping your upper arm locked in place, extend your forearm backward until your arm is completely straight.
Squeeze the triceps at full extension, then slowly lower the dumbbell back to the 90-degree starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
Avoid swinging the weight or dropping your elbow.
tips: Bend forward with your upper arm parallel to the floor
Extend fully to lockout — this is where resistance peaks
Squeeze at full extension
Don't swing the weight

name: Seated Bent-Over Two-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
**A bilateral triceps kickback performed while seated and bent forward, allowing both arms to work simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The seated position limits lower back involvement and momentum, keeping the focus squarely on the triceps through the shortened range where they contract hardest.

**Pro tip:** Bend forward with both upper arms parallel to the floor
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand.
Lean forward from the hips until your chest is close to your thighs, keeping your back flat.
Raise both upper arms so they are parallel to the floor with elbows bent at 90 degrees.
Simultaneously extend both forearms backward until your arms are fully straight and parallel to the ground.
Hold the peak contraction briefly, then lower the dumbbells back under control.
Keep your upper arms stationary throughout and avoid using body momentum to swing the weights.
tips: Bend forward with both upper arms parallel to the floor
Extend both arms to full lockout simultaneously
Squeeze at the top for 1 second
Use lighter weight than single-arm version

name: Seated Triceps Press
description:
**An overhead triceps extension performed seated with a single dumbbell held in both hands, which places the long head of the triceps under a deep stretch at the bottom of each rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The seated position stabilizes the spine and prevents excessive arching, allowing you to focus purely on triceps contraction.

**Pro tip:** Sit upright with back support
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support.
Hold a single dumbbell vertically overhead with both hands cupped around the top end of the weight, arms fully extended.
Keeping your upper arms close to your ears, lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending at the elbows until you feel a deep stretch in the triceps.
Press the weight back overhead by extending your elbows until your arms are straight.
Avoid flaring your elbows excessively outward and keep your core braced to prevent arching your lower back.
tips: Sit upright with back support
Hold a dumbbell overhead with both hands
Lower behind your head by bending at the elbows
Extend fully at the top

name: Single Arm Cable Pushdown
description:
**A unilateral cable pushdown that isolates each triceps independently, helping to identify and correct strength imbalances between arms.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The cable's constant tension profile ensures the triceps are loaded evenly from the stretched to the contracted position on every rep.

**Pro tip:** Use a single handle on a high pulley
instructions: Attach a single handle to a high cable pulley.
Stand facing the machine and grasp the handle with one hand using an overhand grip.
Pin your elbow to your side with your forearm at roughly 90 degrees to start.
Press the handle downward by extending your elbow until your arm is fully straight.
Squeeze at the bottom, then slowly return to the starting position.
Keep your torso still and avoid rotating your body to generate momentum.
tips: Use a single handle on a high pulley
Keep your elbow pinned to your side
Extend to full lockout
Match reps and weight on both arms

name: Single Arm Dumbbell Overhead Extension
description:
**A unilateral overhead extension that stretches and loads the long head of the triceps through a full range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: shoulders.

Working one arm at a time allows you to focus on the mind-muscle connection and address any asymmetries between your left and right triceps.

**Pro tip:** Keep your upper arm close to your ear
instructions: Stand or sit upright and press a dumbbell overhead with one arm, fully extending your elbow.
Keep your upper arm close to your ear and your bicep near your head.
Lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending at the elbow until your forearm is at least parallel to the floor or you feel a full stretch in the triceps.
Press the weight back up to full lockout by extending the elbow.
Use your free hand to stabilize the working arm's elbow if needed.
Avoid leaning to the side or arching your back excessively.
tips: Keep your upper arm close to your ear
Lower the dumbbell behind your head
Extend fully at the top
Use your free hand to stabilize if needed

name: Skull Crusher
description:
**A classic triceps isolation exercise performed lying on a bench with a barbell or EZ-bar, lowering the weight toward the forehead or just behind the head.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

Skull crushers effectively load the triceps through a long range of motion and are one of the most reliable mass-building movements for the upper arms.

**Pro tip:** Keep upper arms vertical or slightly angled back
instructions: Lie face-up on a flat bench holding a barbell or EZ-bar with a narrow overhand grip, arms extended straight above your chest.
Keeping your upper arms vertical and stationary, bend your elbows to lower the bar in an arc toward your forehead or just past the top of your head.
Lower until your forearms are roughly parallel to the floor or you feel a deep stretch in the triceps.
Reverse the motion by extending your elbows to press the bar back to the starting position.
Keep your elbows from flaring outward and control the weight throughout the descent.

Lower to your forehead under control
Press to full lockout
Don't let elbows flare apart

name: Sled Overhead Triceps Extension
description:
**An overhead triceps extension performed using a sled or TRX-style suspension setup, where your body angle creates the resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: shoulders.

This bodyweight variation provides a unique loading angle and constant tension, making it an effective triceps builder that can be easily scaled by adjusting your foot position.

**Pro tip:** Keep arms overhead with elbows close to ears
instructions: Face away from a sled, cable column, or suspension anchor point.
Grasp the handles or straps with both hands and extend your arms overhead.
Lean forward so your body forms a straight diagonal line, with your weight supported through the handles.
Bend your elbows to lower your body forward, allowing your hands to travel behind your head while keeping your upper arms fixed.
Extend your elbows to push yourself back to the starting position.
Adjust difficulty by moving your feet closer to the anchor point for more resistance or farther away for less.
tips: Keep arms overhead with elbows close to ears
Extend your arms as you walk forward
Maintain a steady walking pace
Use light sled weight — this is harder than it looks

name: Smith Machine Close-Grip Bench Press
description:
**A close-grip bench press performed on a Smith machine, which provides a fixed bar path that eliminates the need for lateral stabilization.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: chest, shoulders.

This allows you to focus entirely on pressing through the triceps with heavier loads and reduced injury risk, making it an excellent choice for triceps-focused pressing volume.

**Pro tip:** Grip the bar at shoulder width or slightly narrower
instructions: Position a flat bench inside the Smith machine so the bar tracks over your mid to lower chest.
Lie down and grip the bar with hands about shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
Unrack the bar by rotating the hooks and lower it to your lower chest with your elbows tucked at roughly 45 degrees.
Press the bar back up to full lockout, focusing on driving through the triceps.
Re-rack by twisting the bar back into the hooks at the top.

tips: Grip the bar at shoulder width or slightly narrower
Lower to your lower chest
Press to full lockout
The guided path lets you focus on triceps contraction

name: Speed Band Overhead Triceps
description:
**A fast-tempo overhead triceps extension using a resistance band, designed to develop explosive elbow extension speed and triceps endurance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: shoulders.

The band's ascending resistance rewards aggressive acceleration, making this variation particularly useful for athletes who need rapid arm extension power.

**Pro tip:** Anchor the band behind you
instructions: Anchor a resistance band at a low point behind you or step on it.
Grasp the band with both hands and bring them behind your head with elbows pointing forward and upward.
Explosively extend your elbows to press your hands overhead, fully locking out at the top.
Immediately return to the stretched position and repeat at a brisk, controlled tempo.
Focus on speed during the extension phase while maintaining control on the return.
Keep your elbows pointed forward and your core braced to prevent excessive spinal extension.
tips: Anchor the band behind you
Extend explosively overhead
Control the return
Focus on speed, not just load

name: Standing Bent-Over One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
**A single-arm triceps kickback performed while standing in a hinged-over position, targeting the triceps in their shortened range.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

This exercise emphasizes the peak contraction and is particularly effective for developing the lateral head and improving the visible horseshoe shape of the triceps.

**Pro tip:** Bend at the waist with upper arm parallel to the floor
instructions: Stand with your feet staggered and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor.
You may brace your non-working hand on your knee or a bench for support.
Hold a dumbbell in the working hand with your upper arm raised parallel to the floor and elbow bent at 90 degrees.
Extend your forearm backward until your entire arm is straight and parallel to the ground.
Squeeze the triceps at full lockout, then slowly lower back to the bent position.
Keep your upper arm locked in place and avoid using body swing to move the weight.
tips: Bend at the waist with upper arm parallel to the floor
Extend fully to lockout
Squeeze at full extension for 1 second
Don't swing — strict elbow extension only

name: Standing Bent-Over Two-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
**A bilateral kickback performed standing in a hip-hinge position, working both triceps simultaneously through their shortened range.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

This variation is time-efficient and trains the triceps to contract forcefully at full extension, which has direct carryover to pressing and pushing movements.

**Pro tip:** Bend at the waist with both upper arms parallel to the floor
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, maintaining a flat back.
Raise both upper arms until they are parallel to the ground with elbows bent at 90 degrees.
Extend both forearms simultaneously until your arms are fully straight behind you.
Pause at the top and squeeze the triceps, then lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control.
Avoid jerking the weights or letting your elbows drop during the set.
tips: Bend at the waist with both upper arms parallel to the floor
Extend both arms to full lockout
Squeeze at the top
Keep your back flat

name: Standing Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
**An overhead triceps extension performed standing with a single dumbbell held in both hands, placing the long head of the triceps under a significant stretch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The standing position engages the core for stability and allows for a natural overhead pressing path.

**Pro tip:** Hold a dumbbell with both hands overhead
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hold a single dumbbell vertically overhead with both hands wrapped around the upper end of the weight.
Keep your upper arms close to your ears and your elbows pointing forward.
Lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending at the elbows until you feel a deep stretch in the triceps.
Press the weight back up by extending your elbows to full lockout.
Brace your core throughout to prevent excessive arching of the lower back, and avoid letting your elbows flare wide.
tips: Hold a dumbbell with both hands overhead
Keep upper arms close to your ears
Lower behind your head

name: Standing Low-Pulley One-Arm Triceps Extension
description:
**A unilateral overhead triceps extension performed facing away from a low cable pulley, which provides continuous tension and a unique resistance angle.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The low pulley position creates maximum tension in the stretched position, making this an excellent exercise for emphasizing the long head of the triceps.

**Pro tip:** Face away from a low pulley
instructions: Attach a single handle to a low cable pulley.
Stand facing away from the machine and grasp the handle with one hand.
Step forward to create tension and raise your arm overhead with your elbow bent, hand behind your head.
Keeping your upper arm vertical and close to your ear, extend your elbow to press the handle overhead until your arm is fully straight.
Slowly lower back to the stretched position.
Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning or twisting.

Keep your upper arm by your ear
Extend fully overhead
Control the eccentric

name: Standing One-Arm Dumbbell Triceps Extension
description:
**A unilateral overhead extension performed standing with a single dumbbell, allowing focused work on each triceps independently.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

This exercise provides a deep stretch on the long head and helps identify and correct strength differences between arms.

**Pro tip:** Keep your upper arm close to your ear
instructions: Stand upright and press a dumbbell overhead with one arm until your elbow is fully extended.
Position your upper arm close to your head with your bicep near your ear.
Lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending the elbow, keeping the upper arm stationary, until you feel a full stretch in the triceps.
Extend the elbow to press the weight back to the top.
You can use your opposite hand to lightly support the working elbow for stability.

Lower the dumbbell behind your head
Extend fully
Don't lean to the side

name: Standing Overhead Barbell Triceps Extension
description:
**An overhead triceps extension performed standing with a barbell, which allows heavier loading than dumbbell variations and trains both arms simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: shoulders.

The overhead position places the long head of the triceps under a deep stretch, making this a potent mass builder for the upper arms.

**Pro tip:** Use a shoulder-width or narrower grip
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and press a barbell overhead with a narrow grip, roughly shoulder-width or slightly inside.
With your upper arms vertical and elbows pointing forward, lower the bar behind your head by bending at the elbows.
Descend until your forearms are at least parallel to the floor or you feel a strong stretch in the triceps.
Press the bar back overhead by forcefully extending your elbows.

tips: Use a shoulder-width or narrower grip
Keep upper arms close to your ears
Lower the bar behind your head
Extend fully at the top

name: Standing Towel Triceps Extension
description:
**A bodyweight triceps extension performed using a towel looped over a high anchor point, providing a simple yet effective way to train the triceps with no traditional equipment.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: triceps. Secondary: none.

The towel grip also challenges forearm and grip strength while the body angle determines the resistance level.

**Pro tip:** Loop a towel over a high pulley or bar
instructions: Drape a sturdy towel over a high bar, beam, or pull-up bar so both ends hang evenly.
Grasp one end of the towel in each hand with an overhead grip and face away from the anchor.
Lean forward with arms overhead and elbows bent, allowing your body weight to create resistance.

Grip the towel ends firmly

name: Straight Bar Pushdown
description: **The arm sculptor that never lets tension escape.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. This cable isolation move keeps constant resistance on the back of your arms throughout the full range. The straight bar lets you push heavier loads than rope variations, making it ideal for building thick triceps. **Pro tip:** Lock your elbows at your sides and only move your forearms — if your elbows drift forward, you're letting your shoulders steal the work. **Biomechanics:** The flat cable curve ensures even loading from top to bottom, eliminating dead spots and maximizing time under tension.
instructions: Attach a straight bar to a high cable pulley and select your weight.
Stand facing the machine, grab the bar with an overhand grip at shoulder width, and pin your elbows tight against your ribs.
Push the bar straight down by extending your arms until they are fully locked out, squeezing the back of your arms hard at the bottom.
Slowly let the bar rise back up until your forearms are roughly parallel to the floor, keeping your elbows pinned in place the entire time.
Repeat for your target reps without letting your upper arms move forward or your body lean over the bar.
tips: Use an overhand grip on the straight bar
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Extend to full lockout
Don't lean forward excessively

name: Supine Chest Throw
description: **Explosive power meets chest day in one dynamic launch.** **Muscles worked:** chest, triceps, shoulders. Lying on your back, you explosively push a weight or your body upward, training fast-twitch muscle fibers that normal pressing misses.
