This is a plyometric-style movement that builds real-world pushing power and athletic speed. **Pro tip:** Focus on launching as fast as possible on every rep — speed is the entire point, so keep the movement light and snappy. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve means peak force happens in the middle of the press, and the stretched starting position loads the chest fibers when they're at their longest.
instructions: Lie flat on your back on the floor with your knees bent and feet planted.
Place your hands just outside shoulder width on the ground beside your chest, fingers pointing toward your feet.
Explosively push your upper body off the ground as fast as you can, fully extending your arms.
Land softly by bending your elbows to absorb the impact and immediately lower back to the starting position.
Reset your hand position and repeat, prioritizing speed and power on each rep.
tips: Focus on maximum acceleration through the pressing phase
Absorb the landing or catch with soft elbows
Warm up thoroughly before explosive movements
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity

name: Tate Press
description: **The insider's tricep move that most gym-goers have never tried.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, chest, shoulders. You hold dumbbells over your chest and bend your elbows inward so the weights lower toward the center of your chest, then press them back up. It's a unique angle that hammers the triceps while giving the inner chest a bonus squeeze. **Pro tip:** Use a lighter weight than you think — the unusual angle makes this much harder than it looks, and form breakdown happens fast. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped curve peaks mid-rep, and the inward elbow path shifts emphasis to the medial and lateral tricep heads more than standard pressing movements.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended straight above your chest, palms facing your feet.
Bend your elbows outward and lower the ends of the dumbbells toward the center of your chest, keeping your upper arms relatively still.
Touch the dumbbells lightly to your chest without bouncing.
Press the dumbbells back up by straightening your arms, squeezing your triceps hard at the top.
Keep your elbows flared slightly outward throughout the movement and repeat for your target reps.
tips: Start with dumbbells pressed overhead
Lower by bending elbows inward — dumbbells come toward your chest
Press back up by extending elbows outward
Keep upper arms relatively still

name: Tricep Dips
description: **Your bodyweight becomes the ultimate tricep-building tool.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, chest, shoulders. Using parallel bars or a dip station, you lower and raise your entire body using arm strength alone. This compound movement loads the triceps heavily in the stretched position at the bottom, making it one of the most effective bodyweight exercises for arm size. **Pro tip:** Keep your torso upright and elbows tucked back to keep the focus on triceps — leaning forward shifts the work to your chest. **Biomechanics:** The ascending strength curve means the movement gets easier as you push up, so control the lowering phase slowly to maximize the stretch-position loading where muscle damage is greatest.
instructions: Grip the parallel bars and lift yourself up until your arms are fully straight, body hanging between the bars.
Keep your torso as upright as possible with your elbows pointing behind you, not flared out.
Lower yourself slowly by bending your elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Push yourself back up by straightening your arms completely, squeezing the back of your arms at the top.
Repeat without swinging or using momentum to lift yourself.
tips: Keep torso upright — don't lean forward
Lower to about 90° elbow flexion
Press to full lockout
Add weight for progression

name: Tricep Dumbbell Kickback
description: **The finishing move that torches your triceps when they're already screaming.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. You hinge forward and extend a dumbbell behind you, squeezing the tricep at full lockout. Because the hardest point is at peak contraction, this exercise is perfect for chasing a deep burn and pump at the end of an arm workout. **Pro tip:** Pause for a full second at the top of each rep with your arm completely straight — that's where all the magic happens. **Biomechanics:** The descending strength curve means maximum tension occurs when the tricep is fully shortened, making this ideal for targeting the contracted position that other exercises miss.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in one hand and place your opposite hand and knee on a flat bench for support, keeping your back flat.
Lift your elbow up so your upper arm is parallel to the floor and tucked against your side.
Extend the dumbbell straight back by straightening your arm completely, squeezing the tricep hard at the top.
Slowly bend your elbow to lower the dumbbell back to the starting position without dropping your upper arm.
Complete all reps on one side, then switch to the other arm.
tips: Keep upper arm parallel to the floor
Extend fully to lockout — this is where it counts
Squeeze at full extension for 1 second
Don't swing — strict elbow extension

name: Tricep Side Stretch
description: **A simple reach that melts away tightness hiding in the back of your arm.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, shoulders. This bodyweight stretch targets the long head of the tricep by reaching one arm across your body and gently pulling it closer with the opposite hand. It's perfect after pressing or pushing workouts to restore flexibility and reduce soreness. **Pro tip:** Breathe deeply and hold for at least 20 seconds per side — rushing through stretches robs you of the recovery benefits.
instructions: Stand tall with your feet shoulder-width apart and your shoulders relaxed.
Extend one arm straight across your chest at shoulder height.
Use your opposite hand to gently press the extended arm closer to your body until you feel a stretch in the back of your arm and shoulder.
Hold the stretch for 20 to 30 seconds while breathing deeply and keeping your shoulders down.
Release and repeat on the other side.
tips: Reach one arm overhead and bend the elbow
Use the other hand to gently pull the elbow behind your head
Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
Don't force the stretch

name: Triceps Overhead Extension with Rope
description: **The overhead angle that unlocks tricep growth most people leave on the table.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, shoulders. Facing away from a cable machine, you extend a rope attachment overhead, stretching the long head of the tricep under load. The cable provides smooth, constant tension that free weights can't match in this position. **Pro tip:** Take a staggered stance and lean slightly forward to stay balanced — the cable will try to pull you backward. **Biomechanics:** The flat cable resistance paired with the overhead arm position creates sustained tension through the mid-range, and the stretched overhead position preferentially targets the long head of the tricep.
instructions: Attach a rope to a low cable pulley, grab both ends, and turn to face away from the machine.
Step forward into a staggered stance, lean your torso slightly forward, and bring the rope behind your head with your elbows bent and pointing forward.
Extend both arms overhead by straightening your elbows, spreading the rope ends apart at the top.
Slowly bend your elbows to lower the rope back behind your head, feeling a deep stretch in your triceps.
Keep your upper arms steady and close to your ears throughout every rep.
tips: Face away from a low or high pulley
Keep upper arms by your ears
Split the rope at full extension
Don't let elbows flare

name: Triceps Pushdown
description: **The bread-and-butter arm builder that belongs in every tricep workout.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. Standing at a cable station, you push a handle straight down by locking out your arms, isolating the triceps with zero momentum. It's simple, effective, and the constant cable tension makes every inch of the rep count. **Pro tip:** Squeeze at the bottom for a one-count and resist the weight slowly on the way up — the controlled negative is where growth happens. **Biomechanics:** The flat strength curve from the cable pulley delivers uniform resistance throughout the movement, keeping the triceps loaded evenly from start to finish without any rest points.
instructions: Stand facing a cable machine with a straight or angled bar attached to the high pulley.
Grab the handle with an overhand grip and pull it down until your elbows are pinned at your sides and your forearms are parallel to the floor.
Push the handle straight down by extending your arms fully, squeezing the triceps at the bottom.
Slowly allow the handle to rise back to the starting position, keeping your elbows locked in place.
Repeat without rocking your body or letting your elbows drift forward.
tips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Extend to full lockout on every rep
Don't lean forward to cheat
Control the eccentric

name: Triceps Pushdown - Rope Attachment
description: **Split the rope, split the pump — double the squeeze at the bottom.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. The rope attachment lets you pull the ends apart at lockout, creating an extra contraction that bar attachments can't replicate. This tweak activates the lateral head of the tricep more aggressively, giving you that coveted outer-arm definition. **Pro tip:** Actively pull the rope ends away from each other at the bottom of every rep — if you're not splitting, you're not getting the full benefit. **Biomechanics:** The flat cable tension combined with the ability to rotate and spread at the bottom increases the range of effective motion, recruiting more muscle fibers at peak contraction.
instructions: Attach a rope to the high pulley of a cable machine and select your weight.
Stand facing the machine, grab one end of the rope in each hand with a neutral grip, and tuck your elbows against your sides.
Push the rope downward by straightening your arms, and at the bottom, pull the two ends apart away from each other.
Squeeze the back of your arms hard for a moment at full extension.
Slowly let the rope rise back up until your forearms are parallel to the floor, then repeat.
tips: Split the rope apart at the bottom
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Extend to full lockout before splitting
Don't lean forward

name: Triceps Pushdown - V-Bar Attachment
description: **The neutral grip that lets you push heavier and feel every fiber fire.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. The V-bar's angled handles place your wrists in a natural position, reducing joint stress and letting you load up more weight than straight bar or rope variations. It's the pushdown variation built for chasing strength and size together. **Pro tip:** Keep your chest up and shoulders back — slouching forward turns this into a full-body lean instead of a tricep exercise. **Biomechanics:** The flat cable resistance and neutral wrist position reduce forearm compensations, directing more force through the triceps and allowing heavier progressive overload over time.
instructions: Attach a V-bar to the high pulley of a cable machine and choose your weight.
Stand upright facing the machine, grip both handles of the V-bar with palms facing each other, and lock your elbows at your sides.
Push the V-bar straight down until your arms are fully extended, squeezing your triceps at the bottom.
Slowly return the bar to the starting position where your forearms are about parallel to the floor.
Repeat for your target reps, keeping your upper body still and elbows stationary throughout.
tips: Grip the V-bar with both hands
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Extend to full lockout
The V-bar allows heavier loads than rope

name: Triceps Stretch
description: **The overhead reach that releases every knot hiding behind your arm.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, shoulders. By reaching one hand down behind your head and gently pressing the elbow with the other hand, you deeply stretch the long head of the tricep. This is essential after any pressing or overhead workout to maintain healthy range of motion and prevent stiffness. **Pro tip:** Gently pull your elbow behind your head rather than just pushing it down — this angles the stretch deeper into the long head.
instructions: Stand or sit tall with your spine straight and shoulders relaxed.
Raise one arm overhead and bend the elbow so your hand drops behind your head toward the opposite shoulder blade.
Use your other hand to gently press the bent elbow further back and down until you feel a deep stretch in the back of your upper arm.
Hold for 20 to 30 seconds while breathing steadily, keeping your head upright.
Release and switch arms.
tips: Reach overhead and bend your elbow
Gently pull the elbow with the opposite hand
Hold for 20-30 seconds
Breathe normally throughout

name: V-Bar Pushdown
description: **Lock in, load up, and let the V-bar do what it was designed for — pure tricep punishment.** **Muscles worked:** triceps. This cable isolation exercise uses the V-shaped attachment to place your hands in a comfortable neutral position while you push the weight straight down. The result is heavy, joint-friendly tricep work with constant tension from top to bottom. **Pro tip:** Experiment with a slight forward lean from the hips to increase the range of motion at the bottom — just don't turn it into a crunch. **Biomechanics:** The flat resistance profile of the cable ensures no dead spots, and the neutral grip minimizes wrist and elbow strain, allowing sustained high-volume training.
instructions: Set up at a cable station with a V-bar attached to the high pulley.
Grip the V-bar with both hands, palms facing inward, and stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Pin your elbows to your sides and push the bar down until your arms are completely straight.
Pause briefly at the bottom, squeezing the triceps.
Control the bar back up to the starting position without letting your elbows move forward, and repeat.
tips: Grip the V-bar firmly
Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Extend to full lockout
Don't lean forward excessively

name: Weighted Bench Dip
description: **Add plates to the classic dip and watch your triceps transform.** **Muscles worked:** triceps, chest, shoulders. With your hands on a bench behind you and weight on your lap, you lower and press your body up using raw arm strength. The added load turns a basic bodyweight move into a serious mass builder. **Pro tip:** Don't go too deep — lower until your upper arms are parallel to the floor; going further puts unnecessary stress on your shoulders. **Biomechanics:** The ascending strength curve means the bottom stretched position is hardest, so control the descent to capitalize on the eccentric loading where the most muscle-building stimulus occurs.
instructions: Sit on a bench edge, place your hands beside your hips gripping the edge, and extend your legs onto another bench or the floor.
Have a partner place a weight plate on your upper thighs near your hips.
Slide your hips off the bench and lower your body by bending your elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Press yourself back up by straightening your arms completely, squeezing your triceps at the top.
Repeat for your target reps, keeping your back close to the bench throughout.
tips: Place weight plates on your lap
Keep your back close to the bench
Don't go too deep — protect your shoulders
Press to full lockout

name: Barbell Wrist Curl
description: **The forearm pump station that turns your grip from weak link to weapon.** **Muscles worked:** forearms. Sitting with your forearms resting on your thighs, you curl a barbell upward using only your wrists. It directly targets the wrist flexors on the inside of your forearm, building the grip strength and forearm size that heavy lifts demand. **Pro tip:** Let the bar roll down to your fingertips at the bottom before curling it back up — this extended range of motion recruits more muscle fibers. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve peaks in the mid-range, so focus on controlling the weight through the full arc rather than just squeezing at the top.
instructions: Sit on a bench and rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists hanging just past your knees, palms facing up.
Grip a barbell with both hands at about shoulder width.
Lower the barbell by extending your wrists downward as far as comfortable.
Curl the barbell upward by flexing your wrists, squeezing your forearms at the top.
Lower slowly and repeat, keeping your forearms pressed against your thighs the entire time.
tips: Rest your forearms on a bench with wrists hanging off the edge
Let the bar roll to your fingertips at the bottom for extra range
Curl your wrists up and squeeze at the top
Use a full range of motion

name: Behind Back Wrist Curl
description: **The sneaky forearm builder you can do right off the rack.** **Muscles worked:** forearms. Standing with a barbell held behind your back, you curl the weight using only your wrists. This behind-the-body angle shifts the emphasis slightly compared to standard wrist curls and allows you to handle more weight comfortably. **Pro tip:** Use a Smith machine if balance is an issue — it lets you focus purely on the wrist curl without worrying about the bar drifting. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped curve loads the mid-range most heavily, and the standing behind-back position naturally limits range of motion, making it a focused, high-tension forearm exercise.
instructions: Stand upright and hold a barbell behind your back with an overhand grip, arms straight and the bar resting against your glutes.
Keeping your arms still, lower the barbell by extending your wrists downward.
Curl the barbell upward by flexing your wrists as high as you can.
Squeeze your forearms briefly at the top of the movement.
Lower under control and repeat for your target reps.
tips: Stand with the barbell behind you, palms facing back
Curl your wrists up by flexing them
Let the bar roll to your fingertips at the bottom
Keep your arms still — only wrists move

name: Bottoms-Up Clean From The Hang Position
description: **The ultimate grip-and-go move that demands total body coordination.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, biceps, shoulders, quads, hamstrings, traps, lower back, forearms. You clean a kettlebell from hip height to a bottoms-up position where the bell faces the ceiling, requiring explosive hip drive and an iron grip. This full-body exercise builds raw power, coordination, and bulletproof shoulder stability. **Pro tip:** Crush the handle as hard as you can — the tighter your grip, the more stable the kettlebell stays overhead. **Biomechanics:** The ascending curve demands peak force through the hips, while the inverted kettlebell creates a reflexive stabilization demand through the shoulder and forearm that standard cleans don't provide.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell by the handle in one hand at hip height with the bell hanging down.
Hinge slightly at the hips, then explosively drive your hips forward to swing the kettlebell upward.
As the kettlebell rises, pull your elbow in tight and flip the bell so the bottom faces the ceiling, catching it at shoulder height.
Hold the bottoms-up position for a moment, keeping your grip tight and your core braced.
Lower the kettlebell back to the hang position under control and repeat.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Cable Wrist Curl
description: **Constant tension meets wrist curls — your forearms won't get a single second of rest.** **Muscles worked:** forearms. Using a low cable pulley, you curl the handle upward with your wrists while your forearms stay anchored. The cable eliminates the dead spots that gravity creates with free weights, keeping your forearm flexors under load through every degree of the curl. **Pro tip:** Try single-arm sets to fix any strength imbalance between your dominant and non-dominant hand. **Biomechanics:** The flat resistance curve from the cable maintains uniform tension throughout the entire wrist curl arc, making this superior to barbell or dumbbell versions for sustained time under tension.
instructions: Attach a straight bar or single handle to a low cable pulley.
Kneel or sit facing the machine with your forearms resting on a bench or your thighs, palms facing up, and grip the handle.
Let your wrists extend downward to get a full stretch in your forearms.
Curl the handle upward by flexing your wrists, squeezing at the top.
Slowly lower back to the stretched position and repeat without lifting your forearms off the support.
tips: Attach a straight bar to a low pulley
Rest forearms on a bench with wrists over the edge
Curl your wrists up against the cable
Control the eccentric

name: Dead Hang
description: **Just hang there — it's harder than it sounds and more useful than you'd think.** **Muscles worked:** forearms, lats, shoulders. Gripping a pull-up bar and simply hanging with straight arms builds crushing grip endurance while decompressing your spine and stretching your lats and shoulders. It's the simplest exercise in the gym and one of the most underrated for overall upper body health. **Pro tip:** Time your hangs and try to add 5 seconds each week — progressive overload works for grip just like it works for everything else. **Biomechanics:** The flat loading profile means your forearm flexors and finger muscles sustain a constant isometric contraction, building endurance-oriented grip strength that transfers directly to deadlifts, rows, and pull-ups.
instructions: Reach up and grab a pull-up bar with both hands using an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder width.
Step or jump off the platform and hang with your arms fully extended and your feet off the ground.
Relax your shoulders slightly to let your body decompress, but keep your grip tight.
Hold the position for as long as you can while breathing steadily.
Step down carefully when your grip gives out, rest, and repeat.
tips: Hang with a full grip — thumbs wrapped around the bar
Keep shoulders engaged — don't just hang from ligaments
Build up time gradually
Use chalk if grip is slipping

name: Dumbbell Lying Pronation
description: **The rotational move that builds forearms from an angle nobody trains.** **Muscles worked:** forearms. Lying your forearm on a bench with a dumbbell in hand, you rotate your wrist inward from a palm-up to a palm-down position. This targets the pronator muscles deep inside your forearm that are critical for grip strength and injury prevention. **Pro tip:** Use a very light dumbbell — these are small muscles, and going too heavy leads to sloppy form and wrist strain. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve peaks at mid-rotation, and the lying position isolates the pronators by removing any assistance from the upper arm or shoulder.
instructions: Sit beside a flat bench and rest your forearm on the bench surface with your wrist hanging off the edge, palm facing up, holding a dumbbell by one end.
Slowly rotate your wrist inward, turning your palm from facing up to facing down.
Pause briefly at the fully pronated position.
Slowly rotate back to the starting palm-up position.
Repeat for your target reps, then switch arms.
tips: Rest your forearm on a bench with hand hanging off
Hold the dumbbell at one end for leverage
Rotate from palm-up to palm-down
Use very light weight — these are small muscles

name: Dumbbell Lying Supination
description: **The reverse twist that balances out your forearm training.** **Muscles worked:** forearms. This is the mirror image of pronation — you rotate your wrist outward from palm-down to palm-up while your forearm rests on a bench. It targets the supinator muscles that are essential for turning doorknobs, using tools, and maintaining healthy elbow joints. **Pro tip:** Match your supination volume to your pronation volume — training one without the other creates imbalances that can lead to elbow pain. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped curve loads the supinators most in the mid-range of rotation, and the supported forearm position ensures pure rotational isolation without cheating.
instructions: Sit beside a flat bench and rest your forearm on the surface with your wrist hanging off the edge, palm facing down, holding a dumbbell by one end.
Slowly rotate your wrist outward, turning your palm from facing down to facing up.
Pause briefly at the fully supinated position.
Slowly rotate back to the starting palm-down position.
Repeat for your target reps, then switch arms.
tips: Rest your forearm on a bench with hand hanging off
Hold the dumbbell at one end for leverage
Rotate from palm-down to palm-up
Use very light weight

name: Dumbbell Reverse Wrist Curl
description: **Flip your grip and unlock the top side of your forearms.** **Muscles worked:** forearms. With palms facing down, you curl a dumbbell upward using your wrist extensors — the muscles on the outside of your forearm that most people completely neglect. Strengthening these prevents elbow tendinitis and builds balanced, complete forearm development. **Pro tip:** Go slow and light — the wrist extensors are much weaker than the flexors, so check your ego and focus on feeling the contraction. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve means the mid-range carries the most load, and the overhand dumbbell grip ensures the wrist extensors do all the work without help from the flexors.
instructions: Sit on a bench and rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists hanging past your knees, palms facing down, holding a dumbbell in each hand.
Let your wrists drop downward to get a full stretch on the top of your forearms.
Curl the dumbbells upward by extending your wrists as high as you can.
Squeeze the top of your forearms briefly at the peak.
Lower slowly and repeat, keeping your forearms pressed against your thighs.
tips: Rest your forearm on a bench with palm facing down
Extend your wrist upward against the dumbbell
Use lighter weight than wrist curls — extensors are weaker
Full range of motion

name: Dumbbell Wrist Curl
description: **One arm at a time — the focused forearm builder that fixes imbalances.** **Muscles worked:** forearms. Holding a dumbbell with your forearm supported, you curl the weight using only your wrist. The dumbbell version lets each arm work independently, exposing and correcting any strength differences between your left and right sides. **Pro tip:** Perform your weaker arm first and match the reps with your stronger arm — this is the fastest way to even out grip strength. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped curve peaks at mid-range, and the unilateral dumbbell loading forces each forearm to stabilize independently, building functional grip strength that bilateral barbell curls can mask.
instructions: Sit on a bench and rest one forearm on your thigh with your wrist hanging past your knee, palm facing up, holding a dumbbell.
Let the dumbbell lower by extending your wrist downward for a full stretch.
Curl the dumbbell upward by flexing your wrist, squeezing your inner forearm at the top.
Lower under control and repeat for your target reps.
Switch to the other arm and perform the same number of reps.
tips: Rest your forearm on your thigh or a bench
Let the dumbbell roll to your fingertips at the bottom
Curl your wrist up and squeeze
Use a full range of motion

name: Farmer's Walk (Forearms)
description: **Pick up something heavy and walk — the oldest strength test in the book.** **Muscles worked:** forearms, traps, abs, lower back. Grab a pair of heavy dumbbells and walk with them. That's it. This brutally simple exercise builds an iron grip, rock-solid core stability, and total-body toughness that carries over to every other lift you do. **Pro tip:** Choose a weight that makes your grip fail around 30 to 45 seconds — if you can walk for a minute easily, go heavier. **Biomechanics:** The flat loading profile creates a sustained isometric demand on the forearm flexors and finger muscles, while the walking component adds dynamic core and postural stabilization that static holds don't provide.
instructions: Stand between two heavy dumbbells, hinge at the hips, and grip one in each hand.
Stand up tall with your chest proud, shoulders pulled back, and arms hanging straight at your sides.
Walk forward with controlled, even steps, keeping the dumbbells from swinging.
Maintain an upright posture and tight core throughout the walk.
Walk for your target distance or time, set the dumbbells down carefully, rest, and repeat.
tips: Use the heaviest weight you can hold for the target distance
Keep shoulders back and chest up
Walk with controlled steps
Grip as hard as possible throughout

name: Fat Grip Curl
description: **Thicken the bar and watch your forearms catch fire on every curl.** **Muscles worked:** forearms, biceps. By wrapping fat grips around a barbell (or using a thick bar), you force your hands to work overtime just to hold on. The thicker diameter shifts the training emphasis from your biceps to your forearms and finger flexors, building a grip that won't quit. **Pro tip:** Drop the weight by about 30 percent compared to your normal curl — the thick grip is a humbling equalizer. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve still peaks at mid-curl, but the increased handle diameter recruits significantly more forearm musculature throughout the entire range, turning a standard bicep exercise into a grip-dominant movement.
instructions: Attach fat grips to a barbell or use a thick-handled barbell, and load it with a moderate weight.
Stand upright and grip the bar with an underhand grip at shoulder width, arms fully extended.
Curl the bar upward toward your shoulders by bending your elbows, keeping your upper arms still.
Squeeze at the top, focusing on maintaining your grip on the thick bar.
Lower the bar slowly back to the starting position and repeat.
tips: Attach fat grips to a standard bar or dumbbell
Your grip will fail before your biceps — that's the point
Use lighter weight than standard curls
Squeeze the grips as hard as possible

name: Finger Curls
description: **The micro-movement that builds a vice-like grip from the ground up.** **Muscles worked:** forearms. You let a barbell roll down to your fingertips and then curl it back into your palm using only finger strength. It targets the deep finger flexors that are the true foundation of grip strength — the muscles that determine whether you can hold onto a heavy deadlift or not. **Pro tip:** Do these at the very end of your workout when your forearms are already fatigued — the pump will be unreal. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve loads the finger flexors most in the mid-range of the curl, and the open-hand starting position creates a longer effective range of motion than standard wrist curls.
instructions: Sit on a bench with your forearms resting on your thighs, wrists past your knees, palms up, holding a barbell.
Relax your grip and let the barbell roll down your palms toward your fingertips.
Once the bar is resting on your fingertips, curl your fingers to roll the bar back up into your palms.
Squeeze your grip tight at the top for a moment.
Slowly release and let the bar roll back to your fingertips, then repeat.
tips: Let the bar roll to your fingertips at the bottom
Curl your fingers to bring the bar back into your palm
Use a full range of finger motion
Can be done seated or standing behind the back

name: Gripper Squeeze
description:
**A direct grip-strengthening exercise that targets the finger flexors and forearm musculature by repeatedly crushing a hand gripper or similar resistance device.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

The ascending resistance profile means the exercise becomes hardest at full closure, training the shortened position of the grip muscles.

**Pro tip:** Choose a gripper appropriate for your strength level
instructions: Hold a hand gripper in one hand with the handles resting against your palm and fingers.
Position the gripper so one handle sits in the base of your palm and the other rests across your mid-fingers.
Squeeze the handles together by closing your hand forcefully, contracting the forearm flexors.
Hold the fully closed position for a one-count squeeze at the top.
Slowly allow the gripper to open back to the starting position under control.
Complete all prescribed reps on one hand before switching to the other.
Avoid using your opposite hand to assist or repositioning the gripper between reps.
tips: Choose a gripper appropriate for your strength level
Close the gripper fully — partial reps don't count
Hold the closed position for 1-2 seconds
Progress to heavier grippers over time

name: Hammer Curl Forearm Focus
description:
**A neutral-grip curling variation that shifts emphasis from the biceps onto the brachioradialis and wrist extensors of the forearm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: biceps.

The hammer (palms-facing) grip reduces biceps involvement and forces the forearm muscles to work harder through the mid-range of the curl.

**Pro tip:** Keep wrist neutral — thumbs up throughout
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral (palms-facing-in) grip.
Keep your elbows pinned close to your torso throughout the movement.
Curl the dumbbells upward by flexing at the elbow, maintaining the neutral wrist position without rotating.
Focus on squeezing the forearm muscles rather than driving with the biceps.
Raise the dumbbells until your forearms are just past parallel to the floor.
Lower the weights slowly back to the starting position, resisting gravity on the way down.
Keep your upper arms stationary — avoid swinging or using momentum.
tips: Keep wrist neutral — thumbs up throughout
Focus on the forearm contraction, not the biceps
Use a controlled tempo
Don't swing the weight

name: Kneeling Forearm Stretch
description:
**A bodyweight forearm exercise performed from a kneeling position with the palms placed flat on the floor and fingers pointed back toward the knees.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This loads the wrist flexors in a deep stretch under your own body weight, promoting flexibility and eccentric stimulus in the forearm muscles.

**Pro tip:** Place palms flat on the floor with fingers pointing toward you
instructions: Kneel on the floor and place both palms flat on the ground in front of you with your fingers pointing back toward your knees.
Keep your elbows straight or with a very slight bend.
Slowly shift your body weight backward over your wrists, increasing the stretch through the inner forearms.
Hold the stretched position for the prescribed duration, breathing steadily.
To increase intensity, lean further back; to decrease, shift weight forward.
Release the stretch gently by shifting your weight forward before lifting your hands.
Avoid bouncing or jerking into the stretch — maintain smooth, controlled pressure throughout.
tips: Place palms flat on the floor with fingers pointing toward you
Gently lean back to increase the stretch
Hold for 20-30 seconds
Don't force it — ease into the stretch

name: Palms-Down Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
description:
**An isolation exercise for the wrist extensors performed by resting the forearms on a bench with the palms facing down and curling dumbbells upward through wrist extension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

Using dumbbells allows each forearm to work independently, correcting strength imbalances between sides.

**Pro tip:** Rest forearms on a bench with wrists hanging off
instructions: Kneel beside a flat bench and rest your forearms on the pad with your wrists hanging just over the edge, palms facing down.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (overhand) grip.
Allow the wrists to drop downward into flexion, letting the dumbbells hang toward the floor.
Curl the dumbbells upward by extending the wrists, raising the knuckles toward the ceiling.
Squeeze the wrist extensors briefly at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbells back down slowly under control to the starting position.
Keep your forearms pressed firmly against the bench throughout — only the wrists should move.
tips: Rest forearms on a bench with wrists hanging off
Extend wrists upward against the dumbbells
Use lighter weight than flexion curls
Full range of motion

name: Palms-Down Wrist Curl Over A Bench
description:
**A barbell wrist extension exercise that isolates the forearm extensors by curling the bar upward with the palms facing down while the forearms are braced on a bench.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

The barbell allows heavier loading compared to dumbbells and trains both forearms simultaneously.

**Pro tip:** Rest forearms on a bench with wrists over the edge
instructions: Kneel beside a flat bench and place your forearms flat on the pad with your wrists extending just past the edge, palms facing down.
Grip a barbell with a shoulder-width overhand (pronated) grip.
Let your wrists drop into flexion so the barbell hangs below the bench edge.
Extend your wrists to curl the barbell upward, raising the backs of your hands toward the ceiling.
Pause briefly at the top, squeezing the forearm extensors.
Lower the barbell back down in a controlled manner.
Keep your forearms stationary on the bench — isolate the movement entirely at the wrist joint.
tips: Rest forearms on a bench with wrists over the edge
Extend wrists upward against the barbell
Use lighter weight than wrist curls
Control the full range of motion

name: Palms-Up Barbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
description:
**A classic forearm flexor builder performed by resting the forearms on a bench with palms facing up and curling a barbell through wrist flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This targets the inner forearm muscles responsible for grip strength and wrist curling power, with the barbell enabling progressive overload.

**Pro tip:** Rest forearms on a bench with wrists hanging off
instructions: Kneel beside a flat bench and lay your forearms on the pad with your wrists just past the edge, palms facing up.
Grip a barbell with an underhand (supinated) grip at roughly shoulder width.
Allow your wrists to extend downward so the barbell drops toward the floor.
Curl the barbell upward by flexing your wrists, bringing your palms toward your inner forearms.
Hold the peak contraction for a brief moment at the top.
Lower the bar slowly back to the stretched position.
Keep your forearms glued to the bench — do not lift your elbows or rock your body.
tips: Rest forearms on a bench with wrists hanging off
Let the bar roll to fingertips at the bottom
Curl wrists up and squeeze at the top
Use a full range of motion

name: Palms-Up Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
description:
**A unilateral-friendly wrist flexion exercise performed with dumbbells while the forearms rest on a bench and palms face upward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

Each forearm works independently, making this variation ideal for addressing side-to-side imbalances in the wrist flexor muscles.

**Pro tip:** Rest forearms on a bench with wrists over the edge
instructions: Kneel next to a flat bench and place your forearms on the pad with wrists hanging over the far edge, palms facing up.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a supinated (underhand) grip.
Let your wrists extend downward, lowering the dumbbells toward the floor for a full stretch.
Flex your wrists to curl the dumbbells upward, contracting the inner forearm muscles.
Squeeze at the top of the movement for a one-count hold.
Lower the dumbbells back down under control.
Ensure only your wrists move — your forearms should remain flat on the bench at all times.
tips: Rest forearms on a bench with wrists over the edge
Curl wrists up against the dumbbells
Let dumbbells roll to fingertips at the bottom
Squeeze at the top

name: Plate Pinch
description:
**A grip-focused isometric exercise where you pinch two or more weight plates together using only your fingertips and thumb.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This trains the thumb adductors and finger flexors in a pinch-grip pattern that is often neglected by standard grip exercises, building functional hand strength.

**Pro tip:** Pinch two smooth plates together with fingers on one side, thumb on the other
instructions: Place two smooth-sided weight plates together with the flat sides facing outward.
Stand upright and grip the plates by pinching them between your thumb on one side and your fingers on the other.
Lift the plates off the rack or floor and hold them at your side with your arm straight.
Maintain the pinch grip for the prescribed time, keeping your shoulders level.
Focus on driving your thumb into the plate as hard as possible to prevent slipping.
Set the plates down with control once the hold is complete.
Repeat on the opposite hand. Increase difficulty by adding thinner plates to widen the pinch distance.
tips: Pinch two smooth plates together with fingers on one side, thumb on the other
Hold for time — start with 15-20 seconds
Progress by adding plates or time
Use chalk for better grip

name: Resistance Band Wrist Curl
description:
**A wrist flexion exercise using a resistance band that provides increasing tension as the wrist curls upward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

The ascending resistance profile matches the forearm flexors' strength curve well, making this a joint-friendly alternative to weighted wrist curls that can be performed anywhere.

**Pro tip:** Loop the band under your foot and grip with palm up
instructions: Sit on a chair or bench and rest your forearm on your thigh with your wrist just past your knee, palm facing up.
Loop a resistance band under your foot and hold the other end in your working hand.
Start with your wrist extended downward so the band has light tension.
Curl your wrist upward by flexing the forearm muscles, pulling the band toward you.
Squeeze at the top where band tension is greatest.
Slowly lower your wrist back to the starting position, controlling the band's pull.
Complete all reps on one side before switching. Adjust band tension by shortening or lengthening the loop under your foot.
tips: Loop the band under your foot and grip with palm up
Curl your wrist up against the band
Squeeze at the top where resistance peaks
Use for warm-ups or high-rep finishers

name: Reverse Barbell Curl
description:
**A standing curl performed with an overhand (pronated) grip on a barbell, shifting the primary workload from the biceps to the brachioradialis and wrist extensors.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: biceps.

This is one of the most effective exercises for building the visible outer forearm mass that creates a balanced arm appearance.

**Pro tip:** Grip the bar with palms facing down
instructions: Stand upright and grip a barbell with a shoulder-width overhand (pronated) grip, arms fully extended at your sides.
Pin your elbows to your ribcage and keep your upper arms stationary throughout.
Curl the barbell upward by flexing at the elbows, focusing on driving with the forearms rather than the biceps.
Raise the bar until your forearms are roughly parallel to the floor or slightly above.
Pause briefly at the top, feeling the contraction in the outer forearm.
Lower the barbell slowly back to full arm extension.
Use a controlled tempo — avoid swinging the weight or leaning backward to generate momentum.
tips: Grip the bar with palms facing down
Keep wrists rigid throughout
Keep elbows at your sides
Use lighter weight than supinated curls

name: Reverse Wrist Curl
description:
**An isolation movement for the wrist extensors performed by holding dumbbells with an overhand grip and curling the wrists upward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This directly targets the muscles on the top of the forearm, which are essential for wrist stability and balanced forearm development.

**Pro tip:** Rest forearm on thigh or bench with palm down
instructions: Sit on a bench and rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists extending past your knees, palms facing down.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (overhand) grip.
Allow your wrists to flex downward, letting the dumbbells hang toward the floor.
Extend your wrists to curl the dumbbells upward, raising the backs of your hands toward the ceiling.
Hold the top position briefly, squeezing the wrist extensors.
Lower the dumbbells back down slowly and with control.
Use light to moderate weight — the wrist extensors are small muscles that respond better to higher reps and strict form.
tips: Rest forearm on thigh or bench with palm down
Extend wrist upward
Use lighter weight than wrist curls
Full range of motion

name: Rickshaw Carry
description:
**A loaded carry variation performed using a rickshaw or frame carry apparatus that challenges grip endurance while simultaneously loading the entire posterior chain and core.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: traps, abs, erectors, quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The neutral handle position allows heavier loads than standard farmer's walks, making it an excellent total-body and grip exercise.

**Pro tip:** Grip the handles firmly
instructions: Step inside the rickshaw frame and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Bend at the hips and knees to grip the handles firmly with a neutral grip.
Drive through your legs to stand fully upright, locking out your hips and bracing your core.
Walk forward with controlled, even strides, keeping your torso tall and shoulders pulled back.
Maintain a crushing grip on the handles throughout the carry — do not let the implement drift in your hands.
Walk for the prescribed distance or time, then carefully lower the frame back to the ground by hinging at the hips.
Keep your head neutral and avoid looking down at your feet while walking.
tips: Grip the handles firmly
Keep shoulders back and chest up
Walk with controlled steps
Grip as hard as possible throughout

name: Seated Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
description:
**A seated wrist extension exercise where the forearms rest on the thighs with palms facing down, isolating the wrist extensors with dumbbells.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

The seated position provides stability and eliminates body English, ensuring strict forearm isolation with independent loading per arm.

**Pro tip:** Sit with forearms on your thighs, wrists over your knees
instructions: Sit on a bench with your feet flat on the floor, shoulder-width apart.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand and rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists just past your kneecaps, palms facing down.
Let your wrists flex downward so the dumbbells hang below knee level.
Extend your wrists to curl the dumbbells upward, contracting the top of your forearms.
Pause at the peak contraction for a brief squeeze.
Lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control.
Keep your forearms pressed into your thighs throughout — only the wrists should move.
tips: Sit with forearms on your thighs, wrists over your knees
Extend wrists upward
Use lighter weight than flexion curls
Control the full range

name: Seated Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl
description:
**A seated wrist flexion exercise with dumbbells resting on the thighs and palms facing upward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This isolates the forearm flexors responsible for grip strength and wrist curling power, with the thigh-supported position ensuring strict form and full range of motion at the wrist.

**Pro tip:** Sit with forearms on your thighs, palms up
instructions: Sit on a bench with feet flat on the floor and hold a dumbbell in each hand.
Rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists just past your knees, palms facing up.
Allow your wrists to extend downward, lowering the dumbbells toward the floor.
Curl the dumbbells upward by flexing your wrists, bringing your palms toward your inner forearms.
Squeeze the forearm flexors at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the stretched position.
Keep your forearms firmly on your thighs and avoid rocking your torso to generate momentum.
tips: Sit with forearms on your thighs, palms up
Let dumbbells roll to fingertips at the bottom
Curl wrists up and squeeze
Full range of motion

name: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
description:
**A single-arm wrist extension exercise performed seated with the forearm resting on the thigh and palm facing down.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

Training one arm at a time allows full concentration on the working forearm extensor muscles and helps identify and correct strength differences between sides.

**Pro tip:** Rest your forearm on your thigh with palm down
instructions: Sit on a bench and hold a dumbbell in one hand with a pronated (palm-down) grip.
Rest that forearm on your thigh with your wrist extending just past your knee.
Use your free hand to stabilize your working forearm if needed.
Let your wrist flex downward, lowering the dumbbell toward the floor.
Extend your wrist to curl the dumbbell upward, contracting the top of your forearm.
Hold the top position briefly before lowering back down with control.
Complete all reps on one arm before switching to the other side.
tips: Rest your forearm on your thigh with palm down
Extend your wrist upward
Use your free hand to stabilize if needed
Light weight, full range

name: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Up Wrist Curl
description:
**A unilateral wrist flexion exercise performed one arm at a time while seated, with the palm facing up and the forearm braced on the thigh.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This single-arm approach maximizes mind-muscle connection with the forearm flexors and ensures equal development on both sides.

**Pro tip:** Rest your forearm on your thigh with palm up
instructions: Sit on a bench and hold a dumbbell in one hand with a supinated (palm-up) grip.
Rest your forearm on your thigh with your wrist just past your knee.
Let your wrist extend downward, allowing the dumbbell to drop toward the floor.
Curl the dumbbell upward by flexing your wrist, squeezing the inner forearm at the top.
Lower the dumbbell back down slowly, maintaining tension throughout the range of motion.
Complete all prescribed reps before switching to the opposite arm.
You may use your free hand to lightly brace the working forearm for added stability.
tips: Rest your forearm on your thigh with palm up
Let the dumbbell roll to fingertips
Curl your wrist up and squeeze
Match volume on both sides

name: Seated Palm-Up Barbell Wrist Curl
description:
**A seated barbell wrist curl with palms facing up, targeting the forearm flexors while the forearms rest on the thighs for support.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

The seated thigh-supported position is a practical alternative to the bench-supported version and allows for quick setup with heavier barbell loads.

**Pro tip:** Sit with forearms on thighs, palms up, wrists over knees
instructions: Sit on a bench with feet flat and wider than shoulder-width for stability.
Hold a barbell with an underhand (supinated) grip at shoulder width and rest your forearms on your thighs, wrists past your knees.
Let the barbell roll down your fingers slightly as your wrists extend downward for a deep stretch.
Curl the barbell upward by flexing your wrists and rolling the bar back into your palms.
Squeeze the forearm flexors hard at the top of the movement.
Lower the bar back down in a controlled manner to the fully stretched position.
Keep your torso still and avoid bouncing the weight — let the forearms do all the work.
tips: Sit with forearms on thighs, palms up, wrists over knees
Let the bar roll to fingertips at the bottom
Curl wrists up and squeeze at the top
Full range of motion

name: Seated Palms-Down Barbell Wrist Curl
description:
**A seated barbell wrist extension exercise with palms facing down and forearms braced on the thighs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This targets the wrist extensors on the outer forearm using a barbell for bilateral loading, and the seated thigh-supported setup provides a stable base for strict isolation.

**Pro tip:** Sit with forearms on thighs, palms down, wrists over knees
instructions: Sit on a bench with feet flat on the floor and hold a barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip at shoulder width.
Rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists extending just past your knees, palms facing down.
Allow your wrists to flex downward, letting the barbell hang below knee level.
Extend your wrists to curl the barbell upward, raising the backs of your hands toward the ceiling.
Pause at the top and squeeze the forearm extensors.
Lower the barbell back down slowly under control.
Use lighter weight than palms-up curls — the wrist extensors are weaker than the flexors.
tips: Sit with forearms on thighs, palms down, wrists over knees
Extend wrists upward against the bar
Use lighter weight than palms-up version
Control the full range

name: Seated Two-Arm Palms-Up Low-Pulley Wrist Curl
description:
**A cable-based wrist flexion exercise performed seated with both palms facing up, using a low pulley to provide constant tension throughout the entire range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

The flat resistance curve from the cable eliminates the dead spots found in free-weight wrist curls, delivering an excellent effort-to-results ratio.

**Pro tip:** Sit facing a low pulley with forearms on thighs
instructions: Attach a straight bar to a low cable pulley and sit on a bench facing the machine.
Grip the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip at shoulder width.
Rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists just past your knees, palms facing up.
Allow the cable to pull your wrists into extension, stretching the forearm flexors.
Curl the bar upward by flexing your wrists against the cable resistance.
Squeeze the forearm flexors at the peak contraction.
Lower back slowly, letting the cable pull your wrists into a full stretch before the next rep. Maintain constant tension — do not let the weight stack touch down between reps.
tips: Sit facing a low pulley with forearms on thighs
Curl wrists up against the cable
The cable keeps tension at the top and bottom
Control the eccentric

name: Standing Olympic Plate Hand Squeeze
description:
**A standing grip exercise where you hold an Olympic plate by its hub or rim using a pinch or open-hand grip and squeeze repeatedly.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This trains the finger flexors and thumb strength through a functional gripping pattern, using readily available gym equipment.

**Pro tip:** Pinch the plate between fingers and thumb
instructions: Stand upright and hold an Olympic weight plate at your side by gripping the hub or smooth face with your fingers and thumb.
Keep your arm straight and your shoulder relaxed.
Squeeze the plate as hard as possible, contracting all the muscles of your hand and forearm.
Hold the maximum squeeze for two to three seconds.
Relax the grip slightly without dropping the plate, then squeeze again.
Repeat for the prescribed number of squeezes or hold for time.
Switch hands and repeat. Start with a lighter plate and progress to heavier ones as grip strength improves.
tips: Pinch the plate between fingers and thumb
Hold for time — start with 10-15 seconds
Use chalk for better grip
Progress by using heavier plates

name: Standing Palms-Up Barbell Behind The Back Wrist Curl
description:
**A standing wrist curl performed with a barbell held behind the back in a palms-up position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This behind-the-back angle changes the line of pull on the forearm flexors compared to standard wrist curls and allows the bar to roll into the fingertips for an extended range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Stand with barbell behind you, palms facing back
instructions: Stand upright and hold a barbell behind your back with a supinated (palms-facing-away) grip at roughly shoulder width.
Let the barbell rest against your glutes with your arms straight.
Allow the bar to roll down to your fingertips by extending your wrists and opening your fingers slightly.
Curl the bar back up by flexing your fingers and wrists, rolling the barbell into your palms.
Squeeze the forearm flexors at the top of the curl.
Lower the bar back to the fingertip position under control.
Keep your arms straight and your body still — all movement should come from the wrists and fingers.
tips: Stand with barbell behind you, palms facing back
Curl wrists up by flexing them
Let the bar roll to fingertips at the bottom
Keep arms still — only wrists move

name: Suitcase Carry Forearm
description:
**A unilateral loaded carry performed by holding a heavy dumbbell in one hand at your side and walking, emphasizing grip endurance and anti-lateral flexion of the core.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: abs, traps, erectors.

The single-sided load forces the forearm to maintain a crushing grip while the obliques and spinal erectors work to keep the torso upright.

**Pro tip:** Hold a heavy dumbbell in one hand at your side
instructions: Stand upright and pick up a heavy dumbbell in one hand, holding it at your side with your arm straight.
Brace your core and pull your shoulders back and down — resist the urge to lean toward the loaded side.
Walk forward with controlled, even steps, keeping your torso as vertical as possible.
Maintain a tight grip on the dumbbell throughout the entire carry.
Walk for the prescribed distance or time.
Set the dumbbell down with control, then switch to the opposite hand and repeat.
Keep your strides moderate in length — short, choppy steps help maintain balance and posture.
tips: Hold a heavy dumbbell in one hand at your side
Walk with tall posture — don't lean to the loaded side
Grip as hard as possible
Switch sides for equal work

name: Towel Pull-Up
description:
**A pull-up variation performed by draping towels over a pull-up bar and gripping the towel ends instead of the bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: forearms, biceps, traps, erectors.

The thick, unstable grip surface dramatically increases forearm and grip demands while still training the lats and upper back through a full vertical pulling pattern.

**Pro tip:** Grip will be the limiting factor, not lats
instructions: Drape two towels over a pull-up bar, spaced roughly shoulder-width apart.
Grip one towel in each hand as high as possible, wrapping your fingers tightly around the fabric.
Hang with arms fully extended, engaging your shoulders by pulling your shoulder blades slightly down.
Pull yourself upward by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing the towels as hard as possible.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or your hands reach chest level.
Lower yourself slowly back to a full hang, maintaining your grip on the towels.
If you cannot complete full reps, perform towel dead hangs for time to build the necessary grip strength first.
tips: Grip will be the limiting factor, not lats
Great for grip strength development
Use for grip training, not primary lat work
The lat stimulus is identical to regular pull-ups

name: Wrist Circles
description:
**A simple bodyweight mobility exercise where the wrists are rotated through their full circular range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

This warms up the wrist joint, promotes synovial fluid circulation, and gently activates all the forearm muscles through flexion, extension, and radial and ulnar deviation.

**Pro tip:** Make slow, controlled circles with your wrists
instructions: Extend your arms in front of you at shoulder height or hold them at your sides.
Make fists with both hands or interlace your fingers.
Slowly rotate your wrists in a clockwise direction, making the largest circles you can.
Perform the prescribed number of circles in one direction.
Reverse direction and perform the same number of counterclockwise circles.
Keep the movements smooth and controlled — avoid jerky or rushed rotations.
If you feel any clicking or discomfort, reduce the circle size and move more slowly through that range.
tips: Make slow, controlled circles with your wrists
Go both clockwise and counterclockwise
Can be done with or without light weight
Perform 10-15 circles each direction

name: Wrist Roller
description:
**A forearm exercise using a wrist roller device — a handle with a rope and weight attached — where you roll the weight up and down by rotating the handle with alternating wrist movements.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: shoulders.

This delivers an excellent effort-to-results ratio by keeping the forearm flexors and extensors under continuous tension throughout the set.

**Pro tip:** Hold the roller at arm's length in front of you
instructions: Hold a wrist roller device with both hands at arm's length in front of you, palms facing down, with the weight hanging from the rope.
Stand upright with your arms extended at shoulder height or slightly below.
Roll the weight upward by alternating wrist flexion movements — turn one hand forward, then the other, winding the rope around the handle.
Continue rolling until the weight reaches the handle at the top.
Reverse the motion by extending each wrist alternately, slowly unwinding the rope to lower the weight back down.
One full roll up and back down counts as one rep.
Keep your arms as still as possible — the shoulders will fatigue, but the goal is to isolate the wrist rolling motion.
tips: Hold the roller at arm's length in front of you
Roll the weight up by alternating wrist curls
Roll it back down under control
Keep arms extended — don't let them drop

name: Wrist Rotations with Straight Bar
description:
**An isolation forearm exercise that targets the wrist flexors and extensors through rotational movement against the resistance of a straight barbell.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: forearms. Secondary: none.

The fixed grip width of the bar challenges pronation and supination strength evenly across both wrists simultaneously.

**Pro tip:** Hold a bar with one end weighted
instructions: Stand upright and hold a straight barbell in front of you with both hands at shoulder width, arms extended and elbows slightly bent.
Begin with palms facing down (pronated grip).
Slowly rotate both wrists inward so your palms turn to face upward (supinated), keeping your forearms as still as possible.
Reverse the motion, rotating your wrists back through the starting position and continuing until your palms face down again.
Perform each rotation in a controlled, deliberate manner, pausing briefly at the end ranges.
Avoid using momentum or swinging the bar — all movement should originate from the wrists.
tips: Hold a bar with one end weighted
Rotate between palm-up and palm-down
Use controlled, slow rotations
Light weight — these are small muscles

name: All Fours Quad Stretch
description:
**A bodyweight quad stretch performed from a hands-and-knees position that places the rectus femoris and vastus muscles under a deep passive stretch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

This movement is primarily used for flexibility and recovery rather than hypertrophy, making it an excellent low-fatigue option for improving knee and hip mobility.

**Pro tip:** Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
instructions: Start on all fours with your hands directly under your shoulders and knees under your hips.
Reach one hand back and grasp the ankle or foot of the same-side leg.
Gently pull your heel toward your glute, feeling a deep stretch along the front of your thigh.
Keep your supporting arm locked out and your core braced to maintain balance.
Hold the stretched position for 20-30 seconds, breathing steadily throughout.
Release slowly and repeat on the opposite side.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
Breathe deeply throughout the stretch
Do not bounce or force the stretch

name: Alternate Leg Diagonal Bound
description:
**A plyometric compound movement where you explosively bound forward and laterally, alternating legs with each stride.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, calves, hamstrings.

This exercise develops lower-body power, coordination, and single-leg stability, though its high-impact nature and limited time under tension make it better suited for athletic performance than muscle growth.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Stand on one leg with a slight bend in the knee and your torso leaning slightly forward.
Drive off your planted foot explosively, propelling yourself forward and diagonally to the opposite side.
Land on the opposite foot with a soft, controlled knee bend to absorb impact.
Immediately explode off the landing leg, bounding diagonally in the other direction.
Pump your arms in opposition to your legs to generate momentum and maintain balance.
Continue alternating legs for the prescribed number of bounds, focusing on maximal distance and height with each leap.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Backward Drag
description:
**A full-body compound movement where you walk backward while dragging a weighted sled or resisting an external load.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, forearms, erectors.

The backward locomotion pattern places continuous tension on the quads through a shortened range of motion while also engaging the grip, posterior chain, and core for stabilization.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
instructions: Attach a sled, rope, or resistance source and grasp the handles or rope ends firmly with both hands.
Face the sled and lean back slightly, establishing tension in the line.
Begin walking backward with short, deliberate steps, driving through the balls of your feet.
Keep your chest up, core braced, and arms extended or held at your sides to maintain a strong pulling position.
Focus on pushing the ground away with each step rather than simply shuffling backward.
Maintain a steady pace for the prescribed distance or time, keeping constant tension throughout.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
Keep your torso at a forward lean angle appropriate for the implement
Start with lighter loads and focus on maintaining consistent stride length
Use this as a finisher or conditioning tool after primary quad work

name: Barbell Back Squat
description:
**The foundational barbell squat variation with the bar positioned across the upper traps or rear delts.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs, adductors.

It loads the quads heavily through a full range of motion under significant stretch at the bottom, recruiting nearly every muscle in the lower body and core. Its high growth from the stretched position potential makes it a cornerstone movement for quad development, though systemic fatigue is considerable.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Set the barbell in a squat rack at approximately upper-chest height and step under it, positioning the bar across your upper traps (high bar) or rear delts (low bar).
Grip the bar firmly, retract your shoulder blades, and unrack by standing up and stepping back with two to three short steps.
Set your feet at roughly shoulder width with toes pointed slightly outward.
Initiate the descent by simultaneously bending at the hips and knees, sitting down between your legs.
Lower until your hip crease drops at least to knee level, keeping your chest tall and your weight balanced over your midfoot.
Drive upward by pressing through your full foot, extending your hips and knees together until you reach a standing position.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Barbell Front Squat
description:
**A barbell squat variation with the bar racked across the front deltoids and clavicles.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, abs, erectors, adductors.

The anterior bar position forces a more upright torso, shifting emphasis onto the quads and demanding greater core and upper-back engagement. It offers a superior effort-to-results ratio compared to back squats for quad-focused training.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Set the barbell in a rack at collarbone height and step into it, positioning the bar across your front delts with your elbows driven high.
Use either a clean grip (fingertips under the bar) or a cross-arm grip to secure the bar.
Unrack by standing tall and take two short steps back, setting your feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly flared.
Descend by bending your hips and knees simultaneously, keeping your elbows high and your torso as vertical as possible.
Sink until your hip crease passes below your knees, maintaining pressure through your full foot.
Press upward by driving your elbows up and extending your knees and hips until you are fully standing.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Barbell Full Squat
description:
**A deep barbell squat taken to the fullest range of motion your mobility allows, often referred to as an ass-to-grass squat.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs, adductors.

The extended depth maximizes quad and glute stretch under load, amplifying growth from the stretched position stimulus, though the increased range also elevates systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Set up under the barbell in a squat rack with the bar across your upper back, grip it firmly, and unrack by standing.
Step back and position your feet shoulder-width apart with toes angled outward 15-30 degrees.
Take a deep breath, brace your core, and begin descending by breaking at the hips and knees together.
Continue lowering as deep as your mobility permits — ideally until your hamstrings contact your calves — while keeping your heels planted.
Maintain a neutral spine throughout the entire range, allowing a slight forward lean as needed.
Reverse the movement by driving forcefully through your feet, standing fully upright at the top.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Barbell Hack Squat
description:
**A squat performed with the barbell held behind the legs at arm's length, mimicking a deadlift-squat hybrid.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, forearms, adductors, abs, erectors.

The posterior bar placement shifts the center of gravity backward, forcing the knees further forward and placing exceptional demand on the quads with an excellent effort-to-results profile.

**Pro tip:** Descend until your thighs are at least parallel to the platform for full quad stretch
instructions: Stand in front of a loaded barbell on the floor with your heels close to the bar, feet shoulder-width apart.
Squat down and grasp the bar behind you with a shoulder-width overhand grip.
With your chest up and core braced, drive through your heels to stand, lifting the bar along the back of your legs.
Extend your hips and knees fully at the top, squeezing your quads.
Lower the bar back down under control by bending your knees and sitting back, keeping the bar close to your legs.
Lightly touch the plates to the floor before initiating the next rep.
tips: Descend until your thighs are at least parallel to the platform for full quad stretch
Place your feet lower on the platform to increase quad demand
Keep your back flat against the pad throughout the movement
Use a controlled 2-3 second eccentric to maximize quad time under tension
A narrow stance increases quad emphasis over adductor involvement

name: Barbell Lunge
description:
**A unilateral lower-body compound exercise performed by stepping forward with a barbell across the upper back and lowering into a split stance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors, abs.

The deep knee flexion of the lead leg creates significant quad stretch under load, while the staggered stance challenges balance and recruits stabilizer muscles throughout the hips and core.

**Pro tip:** Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
instructions: Position a barbell across your upper traps in a squat rack, unrack it, and step back to clear the hooks.
Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and core braced.
Take a controlled step forward with one leg, planting your foot flat on the ground.
Lower your body by bending both knees until your rear knee nearly touches the floor and your front thigh is roughly parallel to the ground.
Press through the heel and midfoot of your front leg to drive yourself back to the starting position.
Alternate legs each rep or complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
Avoid letting your front knee collapse inward — track it over your second toe
A shorter step length increases quad demand; a longer step shifts to glutes
Keep your weight centered over the front leg throughout the movement
coaching_cues: Drive up from the front leg

name: Barbell Reverse Lunge
description:
**A lunge variation where you step backward rather than forward while carrying a barbell on your upper back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs.

Stepping rearward reduces shear force on the front knee and allows for a more controlled descent, making it a joint-friendlier alternative that still delivers deep quad stretch and strong glute activation.

**Pro tip:** Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
instructions: Set a barbell across your upper traps, unrack it, and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Step one foot backward approximately two to two-and-a-half feet, landing on the ball of your rear foot.
Lower your hips by bending both knees until your back knee hovers just above the floor.
Keep your front shin relatively vertical and your torso upright throughout the descent.
Drive through the heel of your front foot to return to standing, bringing your rear foot back to the start.
Repeat on the same leg for all prescribed reps or alternate sides each rep.
tips: Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
Maintain an upright torso — forward lean reduces quad stimulus
Drive up through the ball of the front foot for maximum quad engagement
Use a controlled tempo to increase time under tension on the quads

name: Barbell Side Split Squat
description:
**A lateral squat variation performed with a barbell on the back, where you shift your weight to one side and squat down over that leg while the opposite leg stays extended.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs, erectors.

This movement heavily loads the quads and adductors of the working leg through a deep stretch, while also developing lateral hip mobility and single-leg strength.

**Pro tip:** Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
instructions: Position a barbell across your upper back and take a wide stance, roughly 1.5 to 2 times shoulder width, with toes pointed slightly outward.
Shift your weight to one side by bending that knee and hip, sitting down over that foot.
Keep the opposite leg as straight as possible with the foot flat on the floor.
Descend until the working thigh is at or below parallel, maintaining an upright chest.
Drive through the heel of the bent leg to push yourself back to the center starting position.
Repeat on the same side for all reps or alternate sides each repetition.
tips: Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
Lower until your rear knee nearly touches the ground for full quad ROM
Keep your torso upright to maintain quad dominance on the front leg
Push through the front foot to drive back up, focusing on quad contraction
coaching_cues: Drop the back knee straight down

name: Barbell Squat
description:
**The standard barbell squat performed to parallel depth with the bar on the upper back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs, adductors.

It is one of the most effective exercises for building overall lower-body mass and strength, loading the quads through a large range of motion under heavy resistance. The high stretch potential at the bottom position drives significant muscle growth stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
instructions: Set the bar in a rack at mid-chest height, step under it, and position it across your upper traps or rear delts.
Grip the bar outside shoulder width, brace your core, and unrack by standing up.
Walk back two to three steps and set your feet roughly shoulder-width apart with toes turned out slightly.
Inhale deeply, then descend by bending your knees and hips simultaneously until your thighs reach at least parallel to the floor.
Keep your chest lifted, knees tracking over your toes, and weight centered over your midfoot.
Exhale as you drive upward through your feet, fully extending your hips and knees to complete the rep.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow
