name: Barbell Squat To A Bench
description:
**A barbell squat where you descend until your glutes lightly contact a bench behind you, then stand back up.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs, adductors.

The bench serves as a depth gauge to ensure consistent range of motion each rep and can help lifters build confidence at the bottom position. The brief contact reduces stretch-reflex contribution, demanding more concentric strength from the quads.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Place a flat bench or box behind you inside a squat rack and set the barbell at upper-chest height.
Step under the bar, position it on your upper back, and unrack it.
Step back until the bench is directly behind you, feet shoulder-width apart.
Squat down in a controlled manner until your glutes lightly touch the bench surface.
Do not sit down or relax onto the bench — maintain tension in your legs and core throughout.
As soon as you feel the bench, drive upward explosively through your feet to return to standing.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Barbell Step Ups
description:
**A unilateral compound exercise where you step onto an elevated platform with a barbell across your back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The movement primarily challenges the quads and glutes of the stepping leg through a mid-range loading profile, while also developing balance and single-leg coordination under load.

**Pro tip:** Use a box height that creates at least 90 degrees of knee flexion on the working leg
instructions: Set a barbell across your upper back and stand facing a sturdy bench or box at approximately knee height.
Place one foot entirely on top of the platform, ensuring your whole foot is in contact.
Drive through the heel and midfoot of the elevated leg to step up, extending your hip and knee fully at the top.
Bring your trailing leg up briefly without resting it on the box, then lower it back to the floor under control.
Keep your torso upright and avoid pushing off excessively with the trailing leg.
Complete all reps on one side before switching, or alternate legs each rep.
tips: Use a box height that creates at least 90 degrees of knee flexion on the working leg
Drive through the heel and midfoot of the top leg — avoid pushing off the back foot
Keep your torso upright throughout to maintain quad dominance
Control the descent slowly to maximize eccentric quad loading

name: Barbell Walking Lunge
description:
**A dynamic lunge variation where each rep propels you forward into the next stride with a barbell on your back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors, abs.

The continuous forward motion increases time under tension per set and challenges balance and coordination more than stationary lunges, while the deep knee flexion at each step delivers strong quad stretch under load.

**Pro tip:** Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
instructions: Position a barbell across your upper traps, unrack it, and step into an open space with room to walk forward.
Take a large step forward with one leg, planting your foot firmly.
Lower your hips until your rear knee nearly grazes the floor and your front thigh is parallel to the ground.
Drive through the front foot to stand and immediately step forward with the opposite leg into the next lunge.
Maintain an upright torso and keep your core engaged throughout the walking sequence.
Continue alternating legs for the prescribed number of steps or distance.
tips: Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
Avoid letting your front knee collapse inward — track it over your second toe
A shorter step length increases quad demand; a longer step shifts to glutes
Keep your weight centered over the front leg throughout the movement
coaching_cues: Drive up from the front leg

name: Bear Crawl Sled Drags
description:
**A full-body conditioning exercise combining the bear crawl position with sled dragging resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, shoulders, abs.

You move forward on hands and feet while tethered to a weighted sled behind you, creating constant tension through the quads, shoulders, and core. The flat strength curve and sustained effort make this primarily a conditioning and work-capacity tool.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
instructions: Attach a sled harness around your waist or shoulders and connect it to a loaded sled behind you.
Get into a bear crawl position — hands under shoulders, knees hovering just above the ground under your hips.
Begin crawling forward by moving opposite hand and foot simultaneously, dragging the sled with each stride.
Keep your hips low and your back flat, avoiding any excessive rise in your hips.
Take short, controlled steps and maintain steady breathing throughout.
Continue for the prescribed distance or time, keeping a consistent pace against the sled resistance.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
Keep your torso at a forward lean angle appropriate for the implement
Start with lighter loads and focus on maintaining consistent stride length
Use this as a finisher or conditioning tool after primary quad work

name: Belt Squat
description:
**A machine-based squat variation where the load is suspended from a belt around the hips rather than resting on the spine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors.

This eliminates spinal compression entirely, allowing you to train the quads and glutes through a full range of motion with significantly less systemic fatigue and back stress than barbell squats.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Stand on the belt squat platform and attach the loading belt securely around your hips.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart on the platform edges, toes slightly turned out.
Unrack the weight by standing tall, then begin your descent by bending at the hips and knees.
Lower until your thighs reach at least parallel to the platform, keeping your torso upright.
Drive through your full foot to stand back up, extending your hips and knees completely.
Use the handles for balance only — avoid pulling yourself up with your arms.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Bench Jump
description:
**A plyometric exercise where you explosively jump onto a bench or elevated surface from a standing position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

This movement develops lower-body power and rate of force development in the quads, glutes, and calves, though the brief time under tension and high impact make it better suited for athletic performance than hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Stand facing a sturdy flat bench or plyo box at approximately knee to mid-thigh height.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart, roughly one foot from the bench.
Dip into a quarter squat, swinging your arms back to load the jump.
Explode upward by extending your hips, knees, and ankles simultaneously while swinging your arms forward.
Land softly on top of the bench with both feet, absorbing the impact with bent knees.
Stand fully upright on the bench, then step down carefully and reset for the next rep.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Bench Sprint
description:
**A high-intensity plyometric drill where you rapidly alternate driving each foot onto a bench in a sprinting motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

This exercise builds explosive quad power, cardiovascular endurance, and foot speed, functioning primarily as a conditioning and athletic performance tool rather than a hypertrophy stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Stand facing a flat bench or low box with one foot placed on top of it.
Begin by explosively driving off both legs, switching your feet in mid-air so the opposite foot lands on the bench.
Continue alternating feet as rapidly as possible, mimicking a sprinting motion.
Pump your arms in coordination with your legs to maintain rhythm and balance.
Stay on the balls of your feet and keep ground contact time as short as possible.
Perform for the prescribed number of reps per leg or for a set time duration.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Bicycling
description:
**Outdoor cycling that engages the quads, glutes, and hamstrings through repetitive pedaling against variable terrain resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

While it provides excellent cardiovascular conditioning and endurance for the lower body, the limited range of motion and low peak tension make it a poor choice for muscle hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
instructions: Adjust your bicycle seat height so your knee has a slight bend at the bottom of the pedal stroke.
Begin pedaling at a comfortable cadence, engaging your quads to push down through each stroke.
Maintain an upright or slightly forward-leaning posture with a relaxed grip on the handlebars.
Increase resistance by shifting to a harder gear or seeking inclines to challenge the quads further.
Keep a smooth, circular pedaling motion rather than stomping down on each stroke.
Cycle for the prescribed duration or distance, adjusting intensity to match your training goal.
tips: Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
Adjust resistance to a level that challenges the quads without compromising form
Keep your posture upright and avoid hunching forward

name: Bicycling, Stationary
description:
**Indoor cycling on a stationary bike that provides controlled, low-impact cardiovascular training with primary engagement of the quads.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The adjustable resistance allows for varied intensity, but the restricted range of motion and absence of meaningful eccentric loading limit its hypertrophy potential to essentially zero.

**Pro tip:** Use this as active recovery or warm-up rather than a primary quad builder
instructions: Adjust the seat height so your knee maintains a slight bend at the lowest point of the pedal stroke.
Set the handlebar height to a comfortable position that allows a neutral spine.
Begin pedaling at a moderate cadence, pushing through the balls of your feet.
Increase the resistance dial to add challenge to the quads as needed.
Maintain a steady rhythm and avoid rocking your hips side to side.
Continue for the prescribed time, using intervals of higher resistance or speed if desired.
tips: Use this as active recovery or warm-up rather than a primary quad builder
Focus on consistent effort rather than maximum intensity
Stay hydrated and maintain steady breathing throughout

name: Bodyweight Squat
description:
**The fundamental unloaded squat pattern using only your body weight for resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

Despite the lack of external load, the deep stretch position at the bottom still provides meaningful quad stimulus for beginners, and the movement serves as an essential pattern for building squat mechanics, mobility, and muscular endurance.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Extend your arms in front of you for counterbalance or hold them at your chest.
Initiate the squat by pushing your hips back and bending your knees simultaneously.
Lower yourself until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor, or as deep as your mobility allows.
Keep your chest lifted, back neutral, and heels firmly planted throughout.
Press through your full foot to stand back up, squeezing your quads and glutes at the top.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Bodyweight Walking Lunge
description:
**A dynamic unloaded lunge where you continuously walk forward, alternating legs with each stride.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The deep split-stance position stretches the quads of the lead leg under bodyweight, making it an effective movement for building foundational lunge mechanics, muscular endurance, and single-leg stability without external load.

**Pro tip:** Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
instructions: Stand upright with your feet together and hands on your hips or at your sides.
Step forward with one leg, planting your foot flat on the ground.
Lower your hips by bending both knees until your rear knee nearly touches the floor.
Drive through the heel of your front foot to propel yourself forward and upward into the next step.
Swing your rear leg forward into the next lunge without pausing in the middle.
Continue walking forward, alternating legs, for the prescribed number of steps or distance.
tips: Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
Maintain an upright torso — forward lean reduces quad stimulus
Drive up through the ball of the front foot for maximum quad engagement
Use a controlled tempo to increase time under tension on the quads

name: Box Squat
description:
**A barbell squat variation where you sit back onto a box at the bottom of each rep before standing.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, adductors, abs.

The pause on the box eliminates the stretch reflex, forcing the quads and posterior chain to generate force from a dead stop. This builds starting strength and teaches consistent depth, though the reduced stretch-reflex contribution moderates its hypertrophy potential.

**Pro tip:** Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
instructions: Set a box or bench behind you at a height that puts your thighs at or slightly below parallel when seated.
Position a barbell across your upper back, unrack it, and step back to straddle the box.
Push your hips back and descend in a controlled manner until you sit fully on the box.
Pause briefly on the box while maintaining a tight core and upright chest — do not relax your muscles.
Explode upward by driving your feet into the floor and extending your hips and knees simultaneously.
Stand fully upright before beginning the next rep.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Box Squat with Bands
description:
**A box squat performed with resistance bands looped over the barbell and anchored to the base of the rack, adding accommodating resistance that increases as you stand.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs, adductors.

The bands reduce the load at the bottom and increase it at the top, training explosive concentric power and lockout strength while the box eliminates the stretch reflex at the bottom.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Loop resistance bands over each end of the barbell and anchor them to the base of the squat rack or heavy dumbbells on the floor.
Set a box behind you at the appropriate depth and position the barbell across your upper back.
Unrack the bar — you should feel the band tension increase as you stand tall.
Step back over the box, push your hips back, and sit down onto the box under control.
Pause briefly on the box, keeping your core tight and muscles engaged.
Drive explosively off the box, accelerating through the full range of motion as the band tension increases toward lockout.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Box Squat with Chains
description:
**A squat variation where you descend onto a box while accommodating resistance from chains increases the load progressively through the concentric phase.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs, adductors.

The box enforces consistent depth and a brief pause that eliminates the stretch reflex, demanding raw concentric strength from the quads and posterior chain.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Set up a barbell with chains draped evenly on each side so they pile on the floor at the bottom.
Position a box behind you at a height that places your hip crease just below your knees.
Unrack the bar across your upper traps, step back, and set your feet slightly wider than shoulder width.
Inhale, brace your core, and sit back onto the box under control.
Pause briefly on the box while maintaining tension in your trunk, then drive through your full foot to stand explosively.
Lock out your hips and knees at the top before beginning the next rep.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Cable Deadlifts
description:
**A cable-based hip hinge that mimics the deadlift pattern while providing constant tension from the pulley system.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, forearms, lats, traps.

The low cable angle loads the glutes and hamstrings throughout the entire range of motion, making it a joint-friendly alternative to barbell deadlifts for hypertrophy-focused training.

**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Attach a straight bar or rope handle to the lowest pulley setting.
Stand facing away from the cable stack with the handle between your legs, feet hip-width apart.
Hinge at the hips and grasp the attachment with both hands, arms extended.
Brace your core, flatten your back, and drive your hips forward to stand tall, squeezing your glutes hard at lockout.
Reverse the movement by pushing your hips back, letting the cable pull your torso forward while keeping a neutral spine.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings, then repeat.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Cable Hip Adduction
description:
**An isolation exercise targeting the inner thigh adductor muscles using a low cable pulley.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: adductors. Secondary: none.

The constant cable tension provides smooth resistance through the full sweep of the leg, making it effective for strengthening and developing the often-neglected adductor group.

**Pro tip:** Stand sideways to the cable machine
instructions: Attach an ankle cuff to the lowest cable pulley and secure it around the ankle of your working leg.
Stand sideways to the machine so the working leg is closest to the stack.
Hold the frame for balance and stand tall on your outside leg with a slight knee bend.
Allow the cable to pull your working leg out to the side until you feel a stretch in your inner thigh.
Squeeze your adductors to sweep the working leg across your body past the midline.
Control the return to the stretched position and repeat for all reps before switching sides.
tips: Stand sideways to the cable machine
Keep the working leg straight or slightly bent
Control the eccentric — don't let the cable yank your leg out
Lean slightly away from the machine for balance

name: Car Deadlift
description:
**A strongman event where you lift one end of a vehicle chassis using a built-in frame or handles.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, quads, forearms, lats, traps.

The lever-arm mechanics and fixed bar path recruit the entire posterior chain along with the quads, making it a brutal full-body test of raw pulling power and hip drive.

**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Step inside the car deadlift frame and position your feet roughly hip-width apart, centered under the handles.
Bend at the hips and knees to grip the handles firmly with a neutral or overhand grip.
Set your back flat, chest up, and take a deep breath to brace your core.
Drive through your heels, extending your knees and hips simultaneously to lift the frame.
Continue pressing your hips forward until you reach full lockout.
Lower the frame under control by reversing the hip hinge and knee bend, resetting your position before the next rep.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Chair Squat
description:
**A machine-based squat performed on a guided apparatus that supports the back while you press through a seated or reclined position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors.

The fixed path isolates the quads more directly than free-weight squats and allows safe training close to failure without a spotter.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Adjust the seat and back pad so your knees align with the machine's pivot point and your feet sit flat on the platform at shoulder width.
Sit into the machine, press your back firmly against the pad, and grip the side handles.
Unrack the weight by extending your legs, then release the safety catches.
Lower yourself by bending your knees until your thighs reach at least parallel, keeping your lower back pressed into the pad.
Drive through your full foot to press back up to the starting position without fully locking your knees.
Re-engage the safeties after your final rep.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Clean from Blocks
description:
**An Olympic lifting variation where the barbell starts elevated on blocks rather than the floor, shortening the pull and letting you focus on the explosive second pull and catch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, traps, shoulders, erectors, quads, forearms.

It develops hip power, upper-back strength, and rapid triple extension while reducing demand on the lower back.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Set blocks at a height that places the barbell just below your knees.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, grip the bar just outside your legs with a hook grip.
Set your back flat, shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar, and eyes forward.
Initiate the pull by driving through your legs, then explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles in a powerful triple extension.
Shrug and pull yourself under the bar, rotating your elbows forward to catch it in a front rack position at the bottom of a front squat.
Stand up to complete the lift, then return the bar to the blocks.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Clean Pull
description:
**A pulling accessory for the clean that trains the first and second pull without the catch phase.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, traps, forearms, quads.

It overloads the hip extension pattern and upper-back engagement, building the positional strength and explosive power needed for heavier cleans.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Set up over the barbell with feet hip-width apart, toes slightly turned out.
Grip the bar just outside your knees using a hook or double overhand grip.
Drop your hips, set your chest up, and flatten your back.
Push the floor away to lift the bar past your knees, keeping it close to your body.
Once the bar passes mid-thigh, violently extend your hips and rise onto your toes while shrugging the bar upward.
Keep your arms straight throughout and do not bend them to curl the bar.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Conan's Wheel
description:
**A strongman event where you carry a heavy pivoting beam in the crook of your arms and walk in a circle around a central post.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, abs, erectors, forearms, shoulders, calves.

It brutally taxes the quads, core, and grip while demanding cardiovascular endurance and mental toughness under a crushing load.

**Pro tip:** Focus on controlled movement through the full available range of motion
instructions: Step up to the beam and position it in the crook of both elbows, clasping your hands together in front of your chest.
Brace your core hard and lift the beam by extending your legs.
Begin walking forward in a circular path around the pivot post, taking short deliberate steps.
Keep your torso as upright as possible and breathe in short controlled bursts.
Drive through each step with your quads and maintain a tight grip on the beam.
Continue walking the prescribed distance or time, then carefully lower the beam back to its resting position.
tips: Focus on controlled movement through the full available range of motion
Maintain proper alignment of knees over toes throughout
Use a load that allows you to complete all reps with good form
Brace your core to maintain stability throughout the movement

name: Curtsy Lunge
description:
**A lunge variation where the trailing leg crosses behind the front leg in a curtsy-like motion, placing a deep stretch on the glutes and adductors while heavily loading the quads of the front leg.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, adductors, hamstrings, abs.

The cross-body angle challenges hip stability and builds single-leg strength through a unique plane of motion.

**Pro tip:** Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Step your right foot diagonally behind and to the left of your left foot, bending both knees to lower into a lunge.
Descend until your front thigh is roughly parallel to the floor, keeping your front knee tracking over your toes and your torso upright.
Press through the heel of your front foot to drive back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching, or alternate legs each rep.
tips: Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
Lower until your rear knee nearly touches the ground for full quad ROM
Keep your torso upright to maintain quad dominance on the front leg
Push through the front foot to drive back up, focusing on quad contraction
coaching_cues: Drop the back knee straight down

name: Cyclist Squat
description:
**A narrow-stance squat performed with heels elevated on a wedge or plates, shifting the demand heavily onto the quadriceps by allowing a more upright torso and deeper knee flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, adductors, abs, erectors.

Named for the quad-dominant demands similar to cycling, it is one of the most effective barbell movements for isolating quad growth.

**Pro tip:** Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
instructions: Place a pair of small plates or a squat wedge on the floor and position your heels on them with a narrow hip-width stance.
Unrack a barbell across your upper traps and step back onto the wedge.
Keep your torso as vertical as possible, brace your core, and descend by bending your knees forward over your toes.
Sink as deep as your mobility allows, ideally until your hamstrings cover your calves.
Drive straight up through the balls of your feet, maintaining the upright torso throughout.
Lock out at the top and repeat.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Depth Jump Leap
description:
**An advanced plyometric drill where you step off an elevated surface, absorb the landing, and immediately explode into a maximal forward or vertical leap.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The rapid eccentric-to-concentric transition trains the stretch-shortening cycle, developing reactive power and rate of force development in the lower body.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Stand on a sturdy box or platform between 30 and 60 centimeters high.
Step off the edge with one foot, landing on both feet simultaneously with soft knees.
The instant your feet contact the ground, minimize ground contact time by immediately driving your arms up and jumping as high or as far forward as possible.
Land with bent knees to absorb the impact.
Reset fully between reps to maintain maximal intent on every jump.
Keep your chest up and avoid collapsing at the hips on landing.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Double Leg Butt Kick
description:
**A dynamic plyometric exercise where you jump and kick both heels toward your glutes simultaneously in mid-air.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

It develops lower-body explosiveness, coordination, and hip flexor mobility while serving as an effective warm-up or conditioning drill.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and arms relaxed at your sides.
Bend your knees slightly and swing your arms to generate momentum as you jump straight up.
At the peak of the jump, tuck both heels up toward your glutes by rapidly flexing your knees.
Quickly extend your legs back down to land softly on the balls of your feet with knees slightly bent.
Immediately transition into the next rep, maintaining a quick rhythmic tempo.
Keep your torso upright throughout and avoid excessive forward lean.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Dumbbell Lateral Lunge
description:
**A frontal-plane lunge where you step wide to the side while holding dumbbells, deeply loading the quads and adductors of the working leg.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, adductors, hamstrings, abs.

The lateral movement pattern strengthens muscles in a plane often neglected by traditional forward-and-back exercises, improving hip mobility and athletic stability.

**Pro tip:** Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand with feet together.
Take a wide step directly to your right, pushing your hips back and bending your right knee to lower into a deep side lunge.
Keep your left leg straight and your right foot flat on the floor with toes pointing forward.
Descend until your right thigh is parallel to the ground or as deep as mobility allows.
Press through your right foot to push back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side or alternate each rep.
tips: Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
Avoid letting your front knee collapse inward — track it over your second toe
A shorter step length increases quad demand; a longer step shifts to glutes
Keep your weight centered over the front leg throughout the movement
coaching_cues: Drive up from the front leg

name: Dumbbell Lunge
description:
**A classic unilateral lower-body exercise where you step forward into a split stance while holding dumbbells, loading the quads and glutes through a deep range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs.

The free-weight nature demands balance and core stability, making it a highly functional movement for building leg size and single-leg strength.

**Pro tip:** Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging at your sides.
Stand tall with feet hip-width apart.
Step forward with one foot and lower your body by bending both knees until your rear knee nearly touches the floor and your front thigh is parallel to the ground.
Keep your front knee aligned over your ankle and your torso upright.
Push through the heel of your front foot to return to the starting position.
Alternate legs each rep or complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
Maintain an upright torso — forward lean reduces quad stimulus
Drive up through the ball of the front foot for maximum quad engagement
Use a controlled tempo to increase time under tension on the quads

name: Dumbbell Lunges
description:
**Walking lunges performed with dumbbells at your sides, combining forward locomotion with deep knee flexion to create a potent quad and glute builder.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors, abs.

The continuous stepping pattern elevates the metabolic demand compared to stationary lunges, making it effective for both hypertrophy and conditioning.

**Pro tip:** Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
instructions: Grasp a dumbbell in each hand and let them hang at arm's length by your sides.
Stand at one end of an open space with feet together.
Step forward with your lead leg into a deep lunge, lowering until both knees form roughly ninety-degree angles.
Without pausing, drive through the front heel and bring your rear foot forward to step directly into the next lunge.
Continue walking forward, alternating legs with each step.
Maintain an upright torso and controlled pace throughout the set.
tips: Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
Lower until your rear knee nearly touches the ground for full quad ROM
Keep your torso upright to maintain quad dominance on the front leg
Push through the front foot to drive back up, focusing on quad contraction
coaching_cues: Drop the back knee straight down

name: Dumbbell Rear Lunge
description:
**A reverse lunge variation with dumbbells where you step backward rather than forward, reducing shear force on the front knee while still deeply loading the quads and glutes.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs.

The backward step makes it easier to maintain an upright torso and is generally more knee-friendly than forward lunges.

**Pro tip:** Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Step one foot straight back, planting the ball of that foot on the ground behind you.
Bend both knees to lower your body until your front thigh is parallel to the floor and your rear knee hovers just above the ground.
Keep your front shin relatively vertical and your chest tall.
Press through the heel of your front foot to drive yourself back to the starting position.
Alternate legs or complete all reps on one side first.
tips: Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
Avoid letting your front knee collapse inward — track it over your second toe
A shorter step length increases quad demand; a longer step shifts to glutes
Keep your weight centered over the front leg throughout the movement
coaching_cues: Drive up from the front leg

name: Dumbbell Seated Box Jump
description:
**A plyometric exercise where you begin seated on a bench holding light dumbbells and explosively jump onto a box.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

Starting from a dead stop eliminates the stretch-shortening cycle, forcing pure concentric power production from the quads and glutes under added external load.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Sit on a bench or box facing a plyo box set at an appropriate height, holding a light dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Place your feet flat on the floor at shoulder width.
Without rocking backward, explosively drive through both feet and swing the dumbbells forward to propel yourself onto the box.
Land softly on top of the box with both feet, absorbing the impact with bent knees.
Stand fully upright on the box, then step down carefully.
Reset on the bench before the next rep.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Dumbbell Squat
description:
**A fundamental squat pattern performed while holding dumbbells, offering a lower-barrier entry to loaded squatting than a barbell.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs.

The dumbbells at your sides allow a natural center of gravity and encourage an upright torso, making it an excellent quad and glute builder for all experience levels.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing your body.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and toes turned out slightly.
Brace your core, push your hips back, and bend your knees to lower yourself as if sitting into a chair.
Descend until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor, keeping your chest up and weight balanced across your full foot.
Drive through your feet to stand back up, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Dumbbell Squat To A Bench
description:
**A dumbbell squat where you lower yourself until you lightly touch a bench behind you, providing a consistent depth target and a brief pause that removes momentum.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, adductors, abs.

The bench acts as a safety cue and confidence builder, making it ideal for beginners learning squat mechanics or lifters training the pause at the bottom.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Position a flat bench behind you and hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, a few inches in front of the bench.
Brace your core and lower yourself by pushing your hips back and bending your knees until your glutes lightly contact the bench.
Do not fully sit down or relax your muscles.
Pause for a brief moment, then drive through your heels to stand back up to full extension.
Keep your chest lifted and knees tracking over your toes throughout.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Dumbbell Step Up
description:
**A unilateral exercise where you step onto an elevated platform while holding dumbbells, primarily targeting the quads and glutes of the leading leg.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors.

The step-up closely mimics real-world movements like climbing stairs, building functional single-leg strength and addressing muscle imbalances between sides.

**Pro tip:** Focus on the working leg doing all the work — the trail leg should be passive
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand facing a sturdy box or bench at roughly knee height.
Place your entire lead foot flat on the box.
Press through the heel of your lead foot to drive your body upward until you are standing fully on the box with both legs straight.
Avoid pushing off with your trailing foot on the ground.
Step back down under control with the trailing leg first.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other side.
tips: Focus on the working leg doing all the work — the trail leg should be passive
Maintain an upright posture and avoid leaning forward
Use a tempo of 2-1-2 for controlled quad loading through the full range
coaching_cues: All the work from the lead leg

name: Dumbbell Step Ups
description:
**Alternating step-ups performed with dumbbells, where you switch the leading leg each rep to train both sides evenly within the same set.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The alternating pattern adds a light cardiovascular element and keeps both legs working in quick succession for balanced development.

**Pro tip:** Use a box height that creates at least 90 degrees of knee flexion on the working leg
instructions: Stand in front of a stable elevated platform holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Step up with your right foot, pressing through the heel to lift your body onto the platform.
Bring your left foot up to stand fully on top, then step back down leading with your left foot.
On the next rep, lead with your left foot.
Continue alternating legs each repetition.
Keep your torso upright, avoid leaning forward, and control the descent on every rep.
tips: Use a box height that creates at least 90 degrees of knee flexion on the working leg
Drive through the heel and midfoot of the top leg — avoid pushing off the back foot
Keep your torso upright throughout to maintain quad dominance
Control the descent slowly to maximize eccentric quad loading

name: Elevated Back Lunge
description:
**A reverse lunge performed with the front foot elevated on a small platform and a barbell on your back, increasing the range of motion at the bottom to create a deeper stretch on the quads and glutes.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs.

The added depth amplifies the hypertrophy stimulus compared to flat-ground lunges.

**Pro tip:** Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
instructions: Set a low platform or bumper plate on the floor and rack a barbell across your upper back.
Step your front foot onto the platform and position yourself so you have room to step back.
Step your rear foot back and lower your body by bending both knees, descending until your rear knee drops below the level of the platform.
Keep your front shin vertical and your torso upright throughout.
Drive through the heel of your elevated front foot to return to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
Maintain an upright torso — forward lean reduces quad stimulus
Drive up through the ball of the front foot for maximum quad engagement
Use a controlled tempo to increase time under tension on the quads

name: Elliptical Trainer
description:
**A low-impact cardio machine that simulates a walking or running stride in a smooth elliptical path, engaging the quads, glutes, and hamstrings without the joint stress of ground contact.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

It is primarily used for cardiovascular conditioning, active recovery, and calorie expenditure rather than muscle building.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
instructions: Step onto the foot pedals and grasp the moving handles or the stationary center handles.
Begin pedaling in a smooth forward motion, keeping your feet flat on the pedals throughout.
Set the resistance and incline to your desired level using the console.
Maintain an upright posture with your core engaged and avoid leaning heavily on the handles.
Pedal at a steady cadence for the prescribed duration.
To finish, gradually reduce your speed and step off carefully once the pedals have stopped.
tips: Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
Adjust resistance to a level that challenges the quads without compromising form
Keep your posture upright and avoid hunching forward

name: Fast Skipping
description:
**An explosive locomotion drill where you skip forward at high speed, driving your knees high and pushing off forcefully with each stride.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

It develops lower-body power, coordination, and cardiovascular fitness while reinforcing proper running mechanics and ground-contact efficiency.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Begin at one end of an open space.
Drive your right knee up toward your chest while pushing off explosively with your left foot, propelling yourself forward and slightly upward.
Swing your opposite arm forward in sync with the driving knee.
Land on the ball of your left foot and immediately drive your left knee up as you push off with your right foot.
Continue alternating in a rapid skipping rhythm, covering as much ground as possible with each bound.
Stay on the balls of your feet and pump your arms aggressively to maximize height and distance.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Frankenstein Squat
description:
**A front squat variation where you hold the barbell in the front rack position with arms extended straight out in front of you, removing any wrist or lat flexibility requirements.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, abs, erectors, adductors.

The zombie-like arm position forces an extremely upright torso, placing maximum demand on the quads and core to maintain posture through the lift.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Unrack a barbell in a front rack position, then extend both arms straight out in front of you at shoulder height so the bar rests on the front of your deltoids without any hand support.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out.
Brace your core hard to keep the bar from rolling forward.
Descend into a deep squat by bending your knees and sitting straight down, keeping your torso as vertical as possible.
Go as deep as you can while maintaining the bar position, then drive through your feet to stand back up.
Use lighter loads than a standard front squat since the bar position is less secure.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Freehand Jump Squat
description: **Launch yourself skyward and build explosive lower-body power with nothing but your own bodyweight.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, and inner thighs. This plyometric squat variation trains your legs to produce force quickly, making it ideal for athletes and anyone wanting more speed and spring. The rapid stretch-and-contract cycle fires up your fast-twitch muscle fibers. **Pro tip:** Land softly by bending your knees on impact — quiet landings mean safer joints. **Biomechanics:** The triple extension at the hip, knee, and ankle during takeoff maximizes power output, while the eccentric landing phase demands strong deceleration from the quads and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, arms at your sides.
Lower into a squat by bending your knees and pushing your hips back until your thighs are roughly parallel to the floor.
Swing your arms upward and explode off the ground, jumping as high as you can.
Land softly on the balls of your feet, immediately sinking back into the squat position.
Reset briefly and repeat for the desired number of reps.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Frog Hops
description: **Channel your inner amphibian and cover ground with powerful forward leaps.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves. Frog hops combine a deep squat with a horizontal jump, training your legs to push both up and forward. They're a fun, no-equipment way to build endurance and coordination while torching calories. **Pro tip:** Focus on distance rather than height — drive forward through your heels for maximum ground coverage. **Biomechanics:** The deep starting position loads the quads and glutes at a long muscle length, and the forward trajectory shifts emphasis toward hip extension compared to a vertical jump.
instructions: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, toes pointed slightly outward.
Drop into a deep squat, reaching your hands toward the ground between your feet.
Swing your arms forward and leap ahead as far as you can, extending your hips and knees fully.
Land in the deep squat position with soft knees, absorbing the impact.
Immediately transition into the next hop and continue for the desired distance or reps.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Front Barbell Squat
description: **The king of quad builders — a barbell racked across your front shoulders forces you to stay upright and punish your thighs.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, calves, inner thighs, and spinal erectors. Because the weight sits in front of your body, your core works overtime to keep you from folding forward. This makes it one of the best exercises for building both leg size and trunk stability. **Pro tip:** Keep your elbows high throughout — the moment they drop, the bar rolls forward and the lift falls apart. **Biomechanics:** The anterior load shifts your center of gravity forward, requiring greater knee flexion and a more vertical torso, which increases quad demand while reducing shear stress on the lower back.
instructions: Set a barbell at shoulder height on a squat rack and step under it, resting the bar across your front deltoids with elbows high.
Grip the bar with fingertips just outside your shoulders, keeping your elbows pointed forward.
Unrack the bar, step back, and set your feet shoulder-width apart.
Brace your core and squat down by bending your knees and hips until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your whole foot to stand back up, keeping your chest tall and elbows high.
Re-rack the bar once your set is complete.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Front Barbell Squat To A Bench
description: **Add a built-in depth check to your front squat by using a bench as your bottom target.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, calves, inner thighs, and spinal erectors. Squatting to a bench teaches consistent depth and removes the guesswork from every rep. It also lets you pause briefly at the bottom, eliminating momentum and forcing your muscles to do all the work. **Pro tip:** Lightly touch the bench — don't sit and relax, or you'll lose core tension and risk rounding your back. **Biomechanics:** The bench reference point standardizes range of motion, and the brief pause at the bottom reduces the stretch-shortening cycle, increasing concentric strength demands on the quads.
instructions: Place a flat bench behind you inside a squat rack and set the barbell at shoulder height.
Step under the bar, resting it across your front deltoids with elbows high and fingertips gripping the bar.
Unrack and step back so the bench is directly behind you.
Squat down in a controlled manner until your glutes lightly touch the bench.
Without sitting fully, drive through your feet to stand back up, keeping your elbows high and chest tall.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, then re-rack the barbell.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Front Cone Hops (or hurdle hops)
description: **Turn a row of simple cones into an agility drill that sharpens your footwork and reactive power.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves. Hopping over cones trains your body to absorb and redirect force quickly, which translates directly to sports performance. The forward movement pattern also builds coordination and ankle stability. **Pro tip:** Stay on the balls of your feet and minimize ground contact time — pretend the floor is a hot surface. **Biomechanics:** Each landing demands rapid eccentric loading of the quads and calves, followed by an immediate concentric burst, training the stretch-shortening cycle for improved reactive strength.
instructions: Set up a line of cones or small hurdles spaced about two feet apart.
Stand behind the first cone with feet hip-width apart and knees slightly bent.
Jump forward over the first cone, swinging your arms for momentum.
Land softly on both feet and immediately spring over the next cone.
Continue hopping over each cone without pausing between jumps.
Walk back to the start and repeat for the desired number of rounds.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Front Squat (Clean Grip)
description: **The Olympic lifter's squat of choice — a full clean grip keeps the bar locked in place for heavier, more confident front squats.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, calves, traps, spinal erectors, forearms, and inner thighs. The clean grip wraps all four fingers around the bar, providing superior control compared to a crossed-arm hold. This variation demands excellent wrist and shoulder flexibility but rewards you with a rock-solid rack position. **Pro tip:** Stretch your wrists and lats before attempting this — limited mobility is the number-one reason lifters struggle with the clean grip. **Biomechanics:** The full grip engages the upper traps and anterior deltoids isometrically to stabilize the bar, while the upright torso position maximizes knee flexion and quad recruitment throughout the lift.
instructions: Set a barbell at shoulder height in a squat rack.
Step under the bar and place it across your front deltoids, wrapping all four fingers around the bar with elbows driven high.
Unrack the bar and step back into a shoulder-width stance.
Take a deep breath, brace your core, and squat down until your hip crease drops below your knees.
Drive upward through your whole foot, keeping your elbows high and your torso vertical.
Return to standing and repeat.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Front Squats With Two Kettlebells
description: **Double the kettlebells, double the core challenge — rack a pair and feel every stabilizer in your trunk light up.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, calves, and inner thighs. Holding two kettlebells in the front rack position creates an offset, unstable load that forces your core to work harder than a barbell equivalent. It's a fantastic option when barbells aren't available or you want variety. **Pro tip:** Clean the kettlebells to your chest before you start squatting — don't try to muscle them into position mid-set. **Biomechanics:** The independent loading of each arm challenges anti-rotation stability, while the compact front-loaded position encourages a vertical torso and deep knee flexion for maximal quad engagement.
instructions: Clean two kettlebells to the front rack position, resting them against your upper chest with elbows tucked close to your body.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out.
Brace your core and squat down by pushing your hips back and bending your knees until your thighs are parallel or below.
Press through your whole foot to stand back up, keeping the kettlebells stable against your chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Goblet Squat
description: **Hold a single dumbbell like a chalice and unlock the deepest, most comfortable squat of your life.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, calves, and inner thighs. The goblet squat is one of the best squat variations for beginners because the front-loaded weight naturally keeps your torso upright. It also works beautifully as a warm-up or high-rep finisher for experienced lifters. **Pro tip:** Use your elbows to push your knees outward at the bottom — this opens your hips and lets you sink deeper. **Biomechanics:** The anterior load counterbalances your hips, allowing a more upright torso angle that reduces lower-back stress while increasing the range of motion at the knee for greater quad stimulus.
instructions: Hold one dumbbell vertically at chest height, cupping the top end with both hands.
Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and squat down, keeping the dumbbell close to your chest.
Lower until your elbows touch or pass the inside of your knees.
Drive through your feet to stand back up, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Repeat for the desired number of reps.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Hack Squat
description: **Lock into the machine and isolate your quads with heavy loads and zero balance worries.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, and inner thighs. The hack squat machine supports your back on an angled pad, letting you focus entirely on pushing weight with your legs. It's one of the most effective ways to overload the quads safely. **Pro tip:** Place your feet lower on the platform to shift more work onto the quads — higher foot placement emphasizes glutes and hamstrings. **Biomechanics:** The fixed movement path and back support eliminate spinal loading, allowing greater knee flexion under heavy loads and producing a deep quad stretch at the bottom of each rep.
instructions: Step onto the hack squat machine platform and position your back flat against the pad.
Place your feet shoulder-width apart on the platform, roughly in the middle.
Release the safety handles and straighten your legs to unrack the weight.
Lower yourself by bending your knees until your thighs reach at least parallel to the platform.
Press through your feet to drive the sled back up without locking your knees completely.
Re-engage the safety handles when your set is finished.
tips: Push through the balls of your feet to maximize quad activation
Do not lock out fully at the top — keep constant tension on the quads
Lower as deep as your mobility allows for maximum stretch-mediated stimulus
Avoid letting your heels rise off the platform during the descent
coaching_cues: Push the sled away from you

name: Hack Squat Narrow Stance
description: **Bring your feet together on the hack squat and watch your outer quads beg for mercy.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and inner thighs. Narrowing your stance shifts the emphasis toward the outer sweep of the quadriceps, helping build that coveted teardrop shape above the knee. The machine still handles all the stabilization, so you can push hard. **Pro tip:** Keep your knees tracking over your toes — a narrow stance can tempt your knees to cave inward, so stay mindful. **Biomechanics:** The reduced stance width limits hip abduction, placing greater demand on the vastus lateralis and rectus femoris while still allowing a deep range of motion under the machine's guided path.
instructions: Step onto the hack squat machine and press your back firmly against the pad.
Place your feet close together — about hip-width or narrower — in the center of the platform.
Release the safety handles and straighten your legs.
Lower the sled by bending your knees deeply, keeping your knees tracking forward over your toes.
Drive back up through your feet, stopping just short of full lockout.
Lock the safety handles when done.
tips: Descend until your thighs are at least parallel to the platform for full quad stretch
Place your feet lower on the platform to increase quad demand
Keep your back flat against the pad throughout the movement
Use a controlled 2-3 second eccentric to maximize quad time under tension
A narrow stance increases quad emphasis over adductor involvement

name: Hang Clean - Below the Knees
description: **Start from a dead-stop hang below your knees and rip the barbell to your shoulders in one explosive motion.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, traps, spinal erectors, shoulders, forearms, calves, and quads. This hang clean variation forces you to generate power from a disadvantaged position, building tremendous pulling strength and hip drive. It's a staple for athletes who need to be explosive from awkward positions. **Pro tip:** Think about jumping with the bar rather than pulling it with your arms — the power comes from your hips, not your biceps. **Biomechanics:** Initiating below the knees increases the pulling distance and time under tension for the posterior chain, demanding greater rate of force development through the hip and knee extensors to complete the catch.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, gripping a barbell with an overhand grip just outside your thighs.
Hinge at the hips and lower the bar to just below your knees, keeping your back flat and chest up.
Explosively extend your hips and knees, shrugging the bar upward.
As the bar rises, pull yourself under it and catch it across your front shoulders in a partial squat.
Stand up fully to complete the rep.
Lower the bar back to the hang position below your knees and repeat.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Heaving Snatch Balance
description: **Dip, drive, and drop under the bar overhead — this Olympic drill builds lightning-fast reflexes and overhead stability.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, shoulders, triceps, abs, forearms, traps, and spinal erectors. The heaving snatch balance teaches you to receive a barbell in the overhead squat position using a small dip-and-drive to get the bar moving. It bridges the gap between a push press and a full snatch. **Pro tip:** Start light and master the timing before adding weight — speed under the bar matters far more than how much is on it. **Biomechanics:** The initial dip loads the quads eccentrically, the drive phase produces vertical bar momentum, and the rapid descent into an overhead squat demands coordinated shoulder stability and deep hip and knee flexion.
instructions: Place a barbell across your upper back in a wide snatch grip, standing with feet hip-width apart.
Perform a short, quick dip by bending your knees a few inches.
Immediately drive upward with your legs to pop the bar off your shoulders.
As the bar lifts, push yourself underneath it and catch it overhead with locked arms in a deep squat.
Stand up with the bar overhead to complete the rep.
Lower the bar back to your shoulders and repeat.
tips: Focus on speed under the bar — the quads must fire rapidly to catch the weight
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso as you receive the bar in the squat position
Start with light loads to master the timing and coordination

name: Hip Flexion with Band
description: **A simple band around your foot turns a basic knee raise into a targeted hip flexor and quad burner.** **Muscles worked:** quads and abs. This isolation exercise strengthens the often-neglected hip flexors, which are critical for running, kicking, and lifting your knees. The band provides increasing resistance as you raise your leg higher. **Pro tip:** Slow down the lowering phase — fighting the band on the way down doubles the work your hip flexors do. **Biomechanics:** The ascending resistance profile of the band matches the hip flexors' strongest range, providing peak tension at full flexion where the muscles are shortest.
instructions: Anchor a resistance band to a low, sturdy point behind you and loop the other end around one foot.
Stand tall facing away from the anchor, holding onto something for balance if needed.
Lift your banded knee up toward your chest, keeping your standing leg straight.
Pause briefly at the top when your thigh is parallel to the floor.
Lower your leg slowly back to the starting position, resisting the band's pull.
Complete all reps on one side, then switch legs.
tips: Focus on controlled movement through the full available range of motion
Maintain proper alignment of knees over toes throughout
Use a load that allows you to complete all reps with good form
Brace your core to maintain stability throughout the movement

name: Intermediate Hip Flexor and Quad Stretch
description: **Undo hours of sitting with this deep kneeling stretch that opens your hip flexors and lengthens your quads.** **Muscles worked:** quads and hip flexors. This stretch targets the front of your thigh and the muscles that pull your knee toward your chest. It's especially helpful for anyone who sits at a desk all day, since tight hip flexors can contribute to lower-back pain. **Pro tip:** Squeeze the glute of your back leg to deepen the stretch without arching your lower back.
instructions: Kneel on one knee with your front foot flat on the floor and your back knee on a soft surface.
Keep your torso upright and gently shift your hips forward until you feel a stretch in the front of your back thigh.
Reach back with the same-side hand and grab your back foot, pulling your heel toward your glute.
Hold the stretch for 20 to 30 seconds, breathing deeply.
Release your foot, return to the starting position, and switch sides.
tips: Ease into the stretch gradually
Maintain steady breathing
Stop if you feel sharp pain rather than a gentle pull

name: Iron Crosses (stretch)
description: **Lie flat and let gravity twist your lower body into a deep rotational stretch that loosens your hips and spine.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, and abs. Iron crosses use a controlled twisting motion to stretch the front of your thighs, your hip flexors, and the muscles along your trunk. This stretch is great for improving rotational mobility and relieving tightness after heavy leg training. **Pro tip:** Keep both shoulder blades pinned to the floor — if your shoulder lifts, you've rotated too far.
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your arms extended straight out to the sides, forming a T shape.
Lift one leg straight up toward the ceiling.
Slowly lower that leg across your body toward the opposite hand, keeping it as straight as possible.
Hold the stretched position for 15 to 30 seconds while keeping both shoulders on the ground.
Return your leg to the starting position and repeat on the other side.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
Breathe deeply throughout the stretch
Do not bounce or force the stretch

name: Jefferson Squats
description: **Straddle the barbell like you're stepping over a fence and squat — this old-school lift builds legs and grip in one unconventional package.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, spinal erectors, inner thighs, forearms, calves, and abs. The Jefferson squat places the bar between your legs with a staggered stance, creating a unique anti-rotation demand that strengthens your core in ways a regular squat can't. It's a forgotten gem that deserves a comeback. **Pro tip:** Alternate which foot is in front each set to keep both sides balanced. **Biomechanics:** The asymmetric stance generates a rotational force that the core must resist, training anti-rotation stability while the straddle position allows a deep squat with reduced spinal compression compared to a back squat.
instructions: Place a barbell on the floor and stand over it with one foot in front of the bar and one behind, in a staggered stance.
Squat down and grip the bar with one hand in front of you and one behind, using an overhand or mixed grip.
Brace your core and stand up by driving through both feet, lifting the bar between your legs.
Lower yourself back down in a controlled manner until the plates lightly touch the floor.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch your lead foot on the next set.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Jerk Dip Squat
description: **A short, sharp dip with a heavy barbell on your shoulders that trains the exact leg drive you need for a powerful jerk.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, calves, abs, hamstrings, traps, spinal erectors, forearms, and inner thighs. The jerk dip squat is a partial-range movement that builds strength and confidence in the dip portion of the split jerk or push jerk. It lets you handle loads heavier than your best jerk. **Pro tip:** Keep the dip to about four inches — going too deep turns this into a front squat and defeats the purpose. **Biomechanics:** The short range of motion under supramaximal loads trains the quads and connective tissues to handle high forces at the specific joint angles used during the jerk dip, improving rate of force development for the drive phase.
instructions: Set a barbell at shoulder height in a squat rack and position it across your front shoulders in a jerk grip.
Unrack the bar and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Perform a quick, controlled dip by bending your knees about four inches, keeping your torso perfectly vertical.
Pause briefly at the bottom of the dip, then drive back up to standing.
Repeat for the desired number of reps.
Re-rack the barbell when finished.
tips: Focus on speed under the bar — the quads must fire rapidly to catch the weight
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso as you receive the bar in the squat position
Start with light loads to master the timing and coordination

name: Jogging, Treadmill
description: **Hop on the belt and settle into a steady pace — the treadmill takes the guesswork out of your cardio.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves. Treadmill jogging provides a controlled, weather-proof way to build cardiovascular fitness and burn calories. The cushioned surface is easier on your joints than pavement, and you can adjust speed and incline to match your fitness level. **Pro tip:** Set a slight incline of 1 to 2 percent to better mimic outdoor running and reduce the tendency to overstride. **Biomechanics:** The moving belt assists hip extension during the push-off phase, slightly reducing posterior chain demand compared to overground running, which is why a small incline helps replicate natural running mechanics.
instructions: Step onto the treadmill and straddle the belt with your feet on the side rails.
Set the speed to a comfortable jogging pace and the incline to 1 to 2 percent.
Step onto the moving belt and begin jogging with a natural stride.
Keep your posture upright, arms swinging naturally at your sides.
Jog for your target duration, then gradually reduce the speed to a walk before stepping off.
tips: Use this as active recovery or warm-up rather than a primary quad builder
Focus on consistent effort rather than maximum intensity
Stay hydrated and maintain steady breathing throughout

name: Jump Squat
description: **The purest expression of lower-body explosiveness — squat down and launch yourself off the ground with zero equipment.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, and inner thighs. Jump squats develop the fast-twitch muscle fibers responsible for sprinting, jumping, and quick direction changes. They're a staple in athletic training programs for good reason. **Pro tip:** Reset fully between reps if you're training for maximum height — rushing leads to sloppy form and diminishing jumps. **Biomechanics:** The concentric phase demands rapid quad and glute contraction to overcome gravity, while the eccentric landing phase trains the muscles to absorb two to three times your bodyweight in ground reaction forces.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hands by your sides or clasped in front of your chest.
