Lower into a squat until your thighs are about parallel to the floor.
Explode upward, jumping as high as possible and extending your body fully.
Land softly on the balls of your feet, bending your knees to absorb the impact.
Immediately lower into the next squat and repeat.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat
description: **Cradle a kettlebell at your chest and discover why this is the go-to squat for mobility and muscle in one move.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, calves, and inner thighs. The kettlebell's compact shape sits naturally against your torso, making the goblet position feel more comfortable than a dumbbell for many people. It's an excellent tool for grooving a deep, upright squat pattern. **Pro tip:** At the bottom, pause for two seconds and use your elbows to gently pry your knees apart — this doubles as a hip mobility drill. **Biomechanics:** The bell's center of mass sits close to the body's midline, reducing the moment arm on the spine while the front-loaded position encourages anterior core engagement and deep knee flexion.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the horns (sides of the handle) at chest height, close to your body.
Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, toes turned out slightly.
Brace your core and squat down, keeping the kettlebell against your chest.
Lower until your thighs are below parallel, using your elbows to push your knees outward if needed.
Drive through your feet to stand back up and squeeze your glutes at the top.
Repeat for the desired number of reps.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Kettlebell Pistol Squat
description: **Balance on one leg, hold a kettlebell as a counterweight, and prove your single-leg strength is the real deal.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, and abs. The pistol squat is one of the most demanding bodyweight movements, and adding a kettlebell as a counterweight actually makes it easier to balance while increasing the load on your working leg. It exposes and corrects side-to-side imbalances. **Pro tip:** Hold the kettlebell out in front of you with straight arms — the further forward it is, the easier it is to stay balanced. **Biomechanics:** The single-leg stance doubles the load on the working quad and glute compared to a bilateral squat, while the counterweight shifts the center of mass forward to allow a more vertical torso and deeper knee flexion.
instructions: Hold a kettlebell by the horns at chest height with both hands.
Stand on one leg and extend the other leg straight out in front of you.
Slowly squat down on your standing leg, pushing the kettlebell forward for balance as you descend.
Lower until your hamstring touches your calf, keeping your extended leg off the ground.
Drive through your standing foot to rise back up to a full standing position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Kneeling Hip Flexor
description: **Drop to one knee and let gravity gently pull open the front of your hip — the simplest stretch for desk-bound bodies.** **Muscles worked:** quads and hip flexors. This classic kneeling stretch targets the muscles that get chronically shortened from sitting. Performing it regularly can improve your posture, reduce anterior pelvic tilt, and make your squats and deadlifts feel smoother. **Pro tip:** Tuck your tailbone slightly under you — this posterior pelvic tilt intensifies the stretch without needing to lean further forward.
instructions: Kneel on one knee with your front foot flat on the floor, both knees bent at roughly 90 degrees.
Place your hands on your front knee or on your hips for balance.
Gently push your hips forward until you feel a stretch in the front of your back hip and thigh.
Hold for 20 to 30 seconds, breathing steadily.
Return to the starting position and switch sides.
tips: Ease into the stretch gradually
Maintain steady breathing
Stop if you feel sharp pain rather than a gentle pull

name: Landmine Squat
description: **Anchor one end of a barbell in a corner and squat with a natural arc that's easier on your back than almost any other barbell variation.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, inner thighs, and spinal erectors. The landmine squat uses an angled barbell path that guides you into an upright position, making it a joint-friendly alternative to traditional squats. It's perfect for lifters with shoulder or back limitations. **Pro tip:** Interlace your fingers around the end of the bar and keep it tight to your chest — letting it drift forward turns this into a front raise. **Biomechanics:** The fixed pivot point of the barbell creates an arc that naturally shifts the load anteriorly as you descend, promoting an upright torso and reducing spinal compression while still allowing heavy quad loading.
instructions: Wedge one end of a barbell into a landmine attachment or securely into a corner.
Stand facing the bar and hold the free end at chest height with both hands interlaced around it.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart, close to the anchored end.
Squat down by bending your knees and hips, keeping the bar close to your chest.
Lower until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your feet to stand back up and repeat.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Leg Extension
description: **The ultimate quad isolation machine — sit down, extend your legs, and feel every fiber of your thighs working in plain sight.** **Muscles worked:** quads. The leg extension removes every other muscle from the equation and puts 100 percent of the work on your quadriceps. It's ideal for pre-exhausting your quads before squats or finishing them off after compound work. **Pro tip:** Pause for a full second at the top of each rep and squeeze hard — this brief hold dramatically increases the burn and muscle activation. **Biomechanics:** The fixed pivot at the knee joint creates a bell-shaped resistance curve, with peak tension occurring at roughly 90 degrees of knee extension, making the mid-range the most challenging portion of the lift.
instructions: Sit on the leg extension machine with your back flat against the pad and adjust the shin pad so it rests on your lower shins, just above your ankles.
Grip the side handles for stability.
Extend your legs by straightening your knees until your legs are fully straight.
Squeeze your quads hard at the top for one second.
Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position without letting the stack touch down.
Repeat for the desired number of reps.
tips: Extend fully to lockout to achieve peak quad contraction at the top
Control the eccentric phase for 3 seconds to maximize quad tension throughout the ROM
Adjust the pad position so it sits just above your ankles
Point your toes slightly outward to emphasize the vastus medialis
Avoid using momentum — each rep should be controlled and deliberate

name: Leg Extensions
description: **Park yourself in the machine and crank out reps that sculpt a razor-sharp quad separation you can see from across the gym.** **Muscles worked:** quads. Leg extensions let you train your quadriceps through a full range of motion with precise control over the load. They're one of the safest ways to push your quads close to failure without worrying about balance or lower-back fatigue. **Pro tip:** Point your toes slightly outward to emphasize the inner quad (vastus medialis) or slightly inward for the outer sweep — small foot adjustments shift the focus. **Biomechanics:** Because the resistance arm is attached at the ankle, the lever arm is longest when the knee is near full extension, creating peak quad tension in the shortened position and making the lockout the hardest part of each rep.
instructions: Sit on the leg extension machine and adjust the back pad so your knees align with the machine's pivot point.
Position the shin pad comfortably on your lower shins.
Hold the side handles and sit back firmly against the pad.
Straighten your legs fully by contracting your quads.
Lower the weight slowly under control, stopping just before the weight stack rests.
Repeat for the desired number of reps.
tips: Squeeze hard at the top for 1-2 seconds to maximize peak contraction
Use a full range of motion from deep flexion to full extension
Keep your back flat against the seat pad throughout
Lower the weight slowly — the eccentric is where much of the hypertrophy stimulus occurs

name: Leg Press
description:
**A machine-based lower body compound movement where you push a weighted sled away from your torso using your legs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The angled platform and back support eliminate spinal loading, allowing you to train the quads through a deep range of motion with heavy loads while keeping systemic fatigue manageable.

**Pro tip:** Lower the sled until your knees reach at least 90 degrees of flexion for full quad loading
instructions: Sit in the leg press with your back flat against the pad and feet shoulder-width apart on the platform, placed roughly in the center.
Unrack the sled by extending your legs and releasing the safety handles.
Lower the platform by bending your knees toward your chest until your thighs reach at least parallel depth without your lower back rounding off the pad.
Drive through your full foot to press the sled back up, stopping just short of full lockout to maintain constant tension on the quads.
tips: Lower the sled until your knees reach at least 90 degrees of flexion for full quad loading
Place your feet lower on the platform to increase knee flexion demand and quad emphasis
Do not lock out fully at the top — maintain tension on the quads throughout
Use a controlled eccentric of 2-3 seconds to maximize quad time under tension
A narrower foot placement shifts more demand to the quads
coaching_cues: Don't lock out at the top

name: Leg Press Narrow Stance
description:
**A leg press variation performed with feet placed closer together on the platform, shifting greater emphasis onto the outer sweep of the quadriceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors.

The narrow foot position increases knee flexion demands and reduces adductor contribution compared to a standard stance, making it an effective quad-dominant pressing option.

**Pro tip:** Push through the balls of your feet to emphasize quad activation
instructions: Position yourself in the leg press machine with your back firmly against the pad.
Place your feet roughly hip-width apart or narrower in the center of the platform.
Unrack the sled and release the safeties.
Lower the weight under control, allowing your knees to travel forward over your toes as you descend to full depth.
Press back up through the balls of your feet and midfoot, avoiding complete knee lockout at the top.
tips: Push through the balls of your feet to emphasize quad activation
Lower the sled as deep as your hip mobility allows without your lower back rounding
Keep your head and back flat against the pad throughout the movement
Avoid bouncing at the bottom — control the turnaround to protect your knees

name: Leg Press Wide Stance
description:
**A leg press variation using a wider-than-shoulder-width foot placement with toes angled outward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors.

This stance increases adductor and glute involvement while still training the quads as the primary mover. The wider base can feel more comfortable for those with limited ankle mobility.

**Pro tip:** Lower the sled until your knees reach at least 90 degrees of flexion for full quad loading
instructions: Sit in the leg press and set your feet wider than shoulder width on the platform with toes pointed outward at roughly 30 to 45 degrees.
Keep your back pressed into the pad throughout.
Unrack and lower the sled by bending your knees outward in line with your toes until you reach a deep but controlled bottom position.
Push the platform away by driving through your heels and midfoot, maintaining knee tracking over the toes throughout the rep.
tips: Lower the sled until your knees reach at least 90 degrees of flexion for full quad loading
Place your feet lower on the platform to increase knee flexion demand and quad emphasis
Do not lock out fully at the top — maintain tension on the quads throughout
Use a controlled eccentric of 2-3 seconds to maximize quad time under tension
A narrower foot placement shifts more demand to the quads

name: Leverage Deadlift
description:
**A machine-guided deadlift pattern that uses a lever arm system to replicate the hip hinge movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, quads, forearms, traps.

The fixed bar path reduces stabilization demands and grip limitations, letting you focus on loading the glutes and posterior chain. It serves as a solid alternative when barbell deadlifts create excessive systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Stand on the platform with feet hip-width apart and grasp the handles at your sides.
Set your hips back with a slight knee bend, keeping your chest up and spine neutral.
Drive your hips forward and stand tall by squeezing your glutes at the top.
Lower the weight by hinging at the hips first, pushing them back while maintaining a flat back, until the weight stack lightly touches down before initiating the next rep.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Linear Depth Jump
description:
**A plyometric exercise where you step off a raised surface, absorb the landing, and immediately explode forward into a broad jump.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

This movement trains reactive strength and the stretch-shortening cycle of the lower body. It is primarily an athletic performance tool rather than a hypertrophy exercise.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Stand on a box or platform 12 to 24 inches high.
Step off the edge with one foot, landing on both feet simultaneously with soft knees.
Upon ground contact, minimize ground contact time by immediately jumping forward as far as possible, swinging your arms to generate momentum.
Land with bent knees and hips to absorb the impact.
Reset fully between reps to maintain quality.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Looking At Ceiling
description:
**A bodyweight quad stretch performed by kneeling and leaning your torso backward toward the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

This position places the rectus femoris under an intense passive stretch across both the hip and knee joints simultaneously. It is used for flexibility work and cooldown rather than active muscle building.

**Pro tip:** Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
instructions: Kneel on a soft surface with your knees together and the tops of your feet flat on the ground.
Slowly lean your torso backward, placing your hands behind you on the floor for support.
Continue lowering until you feel a deep stretch across the front of your thighs.
Hold the position for 30 to 60 seconds while breathing steadily.
To exit, press through your hands and engage your core to return upright.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
Breathe deeply throughout the stretch
Do not bounce or force the stretch

name: Lunge Sprint
description:
**A high-velocity machine-based lunge variation performed in a rapid alternating fashion, mimicking a sprinting stride under resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

This exercise prioritizes cardiovascular conditioning and muscular endurance over raw hypertrophy. The fast tempo and high rep nature generate significant metabolic stress but limited mechanical tension per rep.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Set up in the machine with your torso upright and hands gripping the handles.
Place one foot forward on the platform and the other behind you.
Begin alternating your legs in a rapid lunging motion, driving through the front heel each stride.
Keep your core braced and torso stable throughout.
Maintain a controlled but fast cadence for the prescribed duration or rep count, ensuring each stride reaches adequate depth.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Lying Machine Squat
description:
**A squat variation performed on a machine where you lie on your back at an angle and press a platform by extending your knees and hips.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The reclined position and guided track remove balance demands and spinal compression, allowing deep knee flexion and heavy quad loading with excellent stretch at the bottom.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Lie back on the machine pad and place your feet shoulder-width apart on the foot platform.
Position your shoulders under the pads if available.
Release the safety mechanism and lower the weight by bending your knees deeply, aiming for your thighs to pass parallel.
Press through your full foot to extend your legs back to the starting position without locking out your knees completely.
Keep your lower back pressed into the pad at all times.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Lying Prone Quadriceps
description:
**A prone quad stretch where you lie face down and pull one or both heels toward your glutes.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

This isolates the rectus femoris and vastus group under a deep passive stretch. It is a flexibility and recovery exercise rather than a strength or hypertrophy movement.

**Pro tip:** Ease into the stretch gradually
instructions: Lie face down on a mat with your legs extended.
Bend one knee and reach back with the same-side hand to grasp your ankle or foot.
Gently pull your heel toward your glute until you feel a strong stretch along the front of your thigh.
Keep your hips pressed into the floor to maximize the stretch on the rectus femoris.
Hold for 30 to 60 seconds per side, breathing deeply throughout.
tips: Ease into the stretch gradually
Maintain steady breathing
Stop if you feel sharp pain rather than a gentle pull

name: Mountain Climbers
description:
**A dynamic bodyweight exercise performed from a push-up position where you rapidly alternate driving your knees toward your chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: abs, glutes, hamstrings, shoulders, chest.

Mountain climbers elevate heart rate quickly and challenge core stability while working the hip flexors and quads through a short range of motion. They are primarily a conditioning tool.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands directly under your shoulders and body forming a straight line from head to heels.
Drive one knee toward your chest, then quickly switch legs in a running motion.
Keep your hips level and avoid letting them pike up or sag down.
Maintain a braced core throughout and breathe rhythmically.
Continue alternating at the desired pace for the prescribed time or rep count.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Narrow Stance Hack Squats
description:
**A hack squat performed with a narrow, hip-width or closer foot placement that maximizes knee flexion and quad recruitment.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The machine back pad and guided track eliminate balance concerns, letting you chase deep ranges of motion safely. This is one of the most effective machine exercises for quad hypertrophy due to its excellent effort-to-results ratio.

**Pro tip:** Push through the balls of your feet to maximize quad activation
instructions: Step into the hack squat machine and position your back against the pad with shoulders under the supports.
Place your feet hip-width apart or narrower on the lower portion of the platform.
Unrack the weight and descend by bending your knees, allowing them to travel well forward over your toes.
Lower until your hamstrings cover your calves or you reach your comfortable end range.
Drive up through your midfoot and the balls of your feet, stopping just before lockout.
tips: Push through the balls of your feet to maximize quad activation
Do not lock out fully at the top — keep constant tension on the quads
Lower as deep as your mobility allows for maximum stretch-mediated stimulus
Avoid letting your heels rise off the platform during the descent
coaching_cues: Push the sled away from you

name: Narrow Stance Leg Press
description:
**A leg press performed with feet placed close together on the sled, biasing the quads by increasing the knee flexion range relative to hip flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

This foot position reduces inner thigh contribution and places greater demand on the vastus lateralis and rectus femoris through a full stretch at the bottom.

**Pro tip:** Push through the balls of your feet to emphasize quad activation
instructions: Sit in the leg press with your back flat against the pad.
Place your feet together or within hip width on the center of the platform.
Release the safeties and lower the sled by bending your knees toward your shoulders, descending as deep as your mobility allows without your lower back lifting.
Press the platform away by extending your knees forcefully, keeping constant tension by not locking out at the top.
tips: Push through the balls of your feet to emphasize quad activation
Lower the sled as deep as your hip mobility allows without your lower back rounding
Keep your head and back flat against the pad throughout the movement
Avoid bouncing at the bottom — control the turnaround to protect your knees
coaching_cues: Don't lock out at the top

name: Narrow Stance Squats
description:
**A barbell back squat performed with a hip-width or narrower stance that increases forward knee travel and quad demand.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.

The narrower base requires greater ankle dorsiflexion and shifts the loading emphasis away from the hips and toward the knee extensors. Spinal loading is still present, contributing to moderate systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Set up under a barbell in a squat rack with the bar resting on your upper traps.
Step back and position your feet at hip width or slightly narrower with toes pointed forward or slightly out.
Brace your core, then descend by breaking at the knees and hips simultaneously, keeping your torso as upright as possible.
Squat to at least parallel depth or deeper.
Drive up through your midfoot, maintaining an upright chest throughout the ascent.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Olympic Squat
description:
**A high-bar back squat performed with an upright torso, full depth, and typically weightlifting shoes to maximize knee flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.

Derived from the squat pattern used by Olympic weightlifters, this variation prioritizes quad development through a long range of motion while still loading the glutes and posterior chain as synergists.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Position the barbell high on your upper traps and grip the bar with a comfortable width.
Unrack and step back into a shoulder-width stance with toes turned out slightly.
Keeping your chest tall and elbows pointed down, descend into a full-depth squat where your hip crease drops well below your knees.
Your knees should track over your toes and travel forward significantly.
Drive out of the hole by pushing through your full foot, maintaining your upright torso angle all the way to the top.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: On Your Side Quad Stretch
description:
**A side-lying quad stretch that targets the rectus femoris and the vastus group by pulling the heel toward the glute while the hip remains in a neutral or slightly extended position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

Lying on your side stabilizes the pelvis and prevents compensatory lumbar extension, making it a controlled and effective flexibility exercise.

**Pro tip:** Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
instructions: Lie on your side with your bottom arm extended under your head for support.
Bend your top knee and reach back with your top hand to grab your ankle.
Pull your heel gently toward your glute while keeping your knees aligned and your hips stacked.
To deepen the stretch, push your top hip slightly forward.
Hold for 30 to 60 seconds, then switch sides.
Avoid arching your lower back.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
Breathe deeply throughout the stretch
Do not bounce or force the stretch

name: On-Your-Back Quad Stretch
description:
**A supine quad stretch performed by tucking one foot beneath your glute while lying on your back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

This position stretches the quadriceps across both the knee and hip joints, particularly targeting the rectus femoris. The floor provides feedback to keep the lower back neutral and the pelvis stable.

**Pro tip:** Ease into the stretch gradually
instructions: Sit on the floor and tuck one foot back beside your hip with the top of the foot on the ground.
Slowly lower yourself onto your back, using your elbows for support as you descend.
Once lying flat, you should feel an intense stretch through the front of the bent leg.
If lying fully flat is too intense, stay propped on your elbows.
Hold for 30 to 60 seconds per side, keeping your lower back pressed toward the floor.
tips: Ease into the stretch gradually
Maintain steady breathing
Stop if you feel sharp pain rather than a gentle pull

name: One Half Locust
description:
**A yoga-derived prone back extension where you lift one leg off the ground while lying face down, engaging the spinal erectors, glutes, and hamstrings unilaterally.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: erectors. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings.

This exercise builds lower back endurance and posterior chain activation with minimal equipment and low injury risk.

**Pro tip:** Lift opposite arm and leg simultaneously
instructions: Lie face down on a mat with your arms at your sides and forehead resting on the floor.
Keeping one leg straight, lift it off the ground as high as comfortably possible by contracting your glute and lower back on that side.
Hold the top position for a brief pause, then lower with control.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
Keep your hips pressed into the mat and avoid rotating your pelvis during the lift.
tips: Lift opposite arm and leg simultaneously
Keep the movement controlled
Hold the top for 1-2 seconds

name: One Leg Barbell Squat
description:
**A unilateral barbell squat where one leg performs the full squatting motion while the other is elevated behind you on a bench or remains off the ground.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs, adductors, erectors.

This exercise addresses bilateral strength imbalances and demands significant quad output per leg. The barbell loading adds spinal compression, increasing systemic fatigue compared to dumbbell alternatives.

**Pro tip:** Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
instructions: Set up a barbell on your upper back as you would for a standard squat.
Stand on one leg with the other foot elevated on a bench behind you or held in the air.
Brace your core and descend by bending your working knee, keeping your torso as upright as possible.
Lower until your working thigh reaches at least parallel.
Drive through your full foot to stand back up.
Complete all reps on one side before switching legs.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: One-Arm Overhead Kettlebell Squats
description:
**A full-depth squat performed while holding a kettlebell locked out overhead with one arm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, shoulders, abs, calves, adductors.

The offset load challenges shoulder stability, thoracic extension, and core anti-lateral flexion while the legs perform a standard squatting pattern. This is as much a stability and mobility exercise as it is a quad builder.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Clean and press a kettlebell overhead with one arm, locking your elbow and stacking the weight directly over your shoulder.
Set your feet at shoulder width with toes slightly turned out.
Keeping the kettlebell stable overhead and your eyes forward, squat down to full depth by bending your knees and hips.
Your free arm can extend to the side for balance.
Stand back up by driving through your feet while keeping the kettlebell locked out.
Maintain a tight core and avoid leaning to either side.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: One-Arm Side Deadlift
description:
**A deadlift variation where you stand beside a barbell and lift it with one hand, creating an asymmetric load that heavily challenges the obliques and lateral core in addition to the posterior chain.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, quads, forearms, traps, abs.

This old-school strongman exercise builds grip strength, hip hinge mechanics, and anti-lateral flexion stability simultaneously.

**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Stand beside a loaded barbell so it is perpendicular to your body at your side.
Hinge at the hips and bend your knees to grasp the center of the bar with one hand using an overhand grip.
Brace your core hard to resist lateral flexion.
Stand up by driving your hips forward and extending your knees, keeping the bar close to your body.
Lower the bar back to the floor under control.
Keep your shoulders as level as possible throughout.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Overhead Squat
description:
**A full-depth squat performed with a barbell held in a wide-grip snatch position overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, shoulders, abs, erectors, triceps, calves, adductors.

This movement demands exceptional shoulder mobility, thoracic extension, ankle flexibility, and total-body stability. While the quads are the primary mover, the overhead position limits loading capacity, making it more of a mobility and skill exercise than a pure hypertrophy tool.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Take a wide snatch grip on the barbell and press or snatch it to a locked-out overhead position.
Your arms should be fully extended with the bar slightly behind your head.
Set your feet at shoulder width with toes turned out.
Descend into a full squat while keeping the bar balanced directly over your midfoot.
Your torso must stay as vertical as possible.
At the bottom, your hips should be well below your knees with the bar still overhead.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Pause Squat
description:
**A barbell back squat with a deliberate pause at the bottom position, eliminating the stretch reflex and forcing the quads and glutes to generate force from a dead stop.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs, adductors.

The pause increases time under tension in the lengthened position, enhancing growth from the stretched position and building strength out of the hole.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Set up as you would for a standard back squat with the bar on your upper back.
Unrack and descend under control to your full squat depth.
At the bottom, hold a completely still pause for 2 to 3 seconds without relaxing your brace or shifting your weight.
After the pause, drive explosively out of the hole through your midfoot.
Use lighter loads than your regular squat to account for the increased difficulty of the dead-stop concentric.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Pendulum Squat
description:
**A machine squat that moves along an arc rather than a linear track, creating a resistance profile that is heaviest in the stretched bottom position where the quads are most lengthened.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors.

This unique loading curve makes it one of the best machines for quad hypertrophy, delivering an outstanding effort-to-results ratio with zero spinal loading.

**Pro tip:** Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
instructions: Step onto the pendulum squat platform and position your shoulders against the pads.
Place your feet on the lower portion of the platform at shoulder width.
Release the safety and descend by bending your knees, allowing the machine to swing through its arc.
Go as deep as your mobility allows, feeling a strong quad stretch at the bottom.
Drive back up by extending your knees, controlling the arc throughout.
Avoid locking out at the top to keep tension on the quads.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Pistol Squat
description:
**A single-leg bodyweight squat performed to full depth with the non-working leg extended straight in front of you.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs.

This advanced movement requires significant quad strength, ankle mobility, hip flexor endurance, and balance. It provides a deep stretch on the working quad at the bottom and is an excellent measure of unilateral lower body capability.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Stand on one leg with your other leg extended straight out in front of you.
Extend your arms forward for counterbalance.
Slowly lower yourself by bending your standing knee, sitting your hips back and down while keeping your extended leg off the ground.
Descend until your hamstring contacts your calf at the bottom.
Drive through your full foot to stand back up without letting your extended leg touch the floor.
Keep your heel planted and torso as upright as possible throughout.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Plie Dumbbell Squat
description:
**A wide-stance squat holding a single dumbbell between your legs, inspired by the ballet plie position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, calves, abs.

The wide foot placement and turned-out toes increase adductor and inner thigh recruitment while the quads remain the primary driver. The dumbbell hangs freely, so loading is limited by grip and the range the dumbbell can travel before hitting the floor.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Stand with your feet significantly wider than shoulder width and toes pointed outward at 45 degrees.
Hold a dumbbell vertically by one end with both hands, letting it hang between your legs.
Keeping your torso upright and core braced, bend your knees outward in line with your toes and lower until your thighs are at or below parallel.
Drive through your heels to return to standing, squeezing your glutes and inner thighs at the top.
Stand on elevated surfaces if you need more range of motion.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Power Jerk
description:
**An explosive Olympic weightlifting derivative where you drive a barbell from the front rack overhead by dipping and re-bending the knees to catch the load at lockout.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, calves, abs, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

Primarily a power and athletic performance movement rather than a hypertrophy tool, it demands full-body coordination and rapid force production through the shoulders, triceps, and lower body.

**Pro tip:** This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
instructions: Unrack the barbell in a front rack position with elbows slightly forward and feet hip-width apart.
Perform a quick, shallow dip by bending the knees a few inches while keeping the torso vertical.
Explosively drive upward through the legs, transferring force into the bar to press it overhead.
As the bar rises, re-bend the knees into a partial squat to catch the barbell at full arm extension.
Stand up to full lockout with the bar directly over your midfoot, shoulders active and elbows locked.
Lower the bar back to the front rack under control and reset before the next rep.
tips: This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
The legs do most of the work — the arms guide and stabilize
Practice the footwork separately before adding heavy loads
Not recommended as a primary deltoid hypertrophy exercise

name: Power Snatch from Blocks
description:
**A partial-range Olympic lift variation where the barbell starts on elevated blocks, allowing you to focus on the explosive second pull and overhead catch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, traps, erectors, shoulders, forearms, triceps, calves, quads.

By eliminating the initial floor pull, this movement isolates hip extension power and upper-back coordination, making it a sport-specific tool for developing rate of force development.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Set blocks so the barbell rests at approximately mid-thigh or just above the knee.
Grip the bar with a wide snatch grip, hinge at the hips, and position your shoulders over or slightly in front of the bar.
Explosively extend the hips and knees, shrugging the bar upward while keeping it close to the body.
As the bar reaches peak height, pull yourself under it and catch it overhead with arms locked and a slight knee bend.
Stabilize overhead, then stand to full extension with the bar balanced over your midfoot.
Lower the bar back to the blocks in a controlled manner and reset your position.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Quad Stretch
description:
**A static flexibility exercise that lengthens the quadriceps by bringing the heel toward the glute.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

This stretch targets the rectus femoris and vastus group, helping to restore range of motion, reduce post-training tightness, and maintain healthy knee and hip function. It produces virtually no fatigue, making it ideal for warm-ups and cooldowns.

**Pro tip:** Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
instructions: Stand upright on one leg, using a wall or sturdy object for balance if needed.
Bend the opposite knee and grasp the top of that foot or ankle with the same-side hand.
Gently pull the heel toward your glute until you feel a comfortable stretch along the front of the thigh.
Keep your knees close together and your standing leg slightly soft.
Hold the stretch for 20–30 seconds while breathing steadily.
Release and repeat on the opposite leg.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
Breathe deeply throughout the stretch
Do not bounce or force the stretch

name: Quadriceps-SMR
description:
**A self-muscle and tissue release technique performed with a foam roller to address tightness and adhesions in the quadriceps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

By applying sustained pressure across the front of the thigh, this modality can improve tissue quality, reduce perceived soreness, and temporarily increase range of motion without generating meaningful fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Ease into the stretch gradually
instructions: Lie face down and place a foam roller under the front of one or both thighs, just above the knee.
Support your upper body on your forearms with your core lightly braced.
Slowly roll from just above the kneecap up toward the hip crease, spending about 1–2 seconds per inch.
When you find a tender spot, pause and apply steady pressure for 15–30 seconds until the discomfort eases.
Continue rolling the full length of the quadriceps for 60–90 seconds per leg.
To increase pressure, stack one leg on top of the other or shift more bodyweight onto the roller.
tips: Ease into the stretch gradually
Maintain steady breathing
Stop if you feel sharp pain rather than a gentle pull

name: Quick Leap
description:
**A plyometric drill emphasizing rapid ground contact and reactive jump ability.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The goal is to minimize time on the ground between each leap, training the stretch-shortening cycle and elastic energy storage in the lower body. This is primarily an athletic performance and conditioning exercise rather than a muscle-building movement.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, arms relaxed at your sides.
Perform a quick countermovement by slightly bending the knees and hinging the hips.
Explode upward, driving through the balls of your feet and swinging your arms overhead.
Land softly on the balls of your feet with knees slightly bent to absorb impact.
Immediately spring into the next leap with minimal ground contact time.
Repeat for the prescribed number of reps, focusing on speed and height rather than distance.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Rear Leg Raises
description:
**A bodyweight glute isolation exercise where you extend one leg behind you against gravity.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

This movement targets the gluteus maximus through hip extension with minimal joint stress and very low systemic fatigue, making it an excellent option for activation work, high-rep burnouts, or rehabilitation protocols.

**Pro tip:** Focus on hip extension, not knee extension — drive the heel back
instructions: Position yourself on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips.
Keeping the knee bent at 90 degrees, lift one leg upward by driving the sole of the foot toward the ceiling.
Squeeze the glute hard at the top when the thigh is roughly parallel to the floor.
Lower the leg back down with control, stopping just before the knee touches the ground.
Complete all prescribed reps on one side before switching legs.
Maintain a neutral spine throughout—avoid arching the lower back to compensate for limited hip extension.
tips: Focus on hip extension, not knee extension — drive the heel back
Keep your core tight to prevent rotation or lumbar extension
Control the eccentric — don't let the weight snap back
Squeeze at the top of each rep for peak glute contraction

name: Recumbent Bike
description:
**A low-impact cardiovascular exercise performed on a seated bike with back support.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The reclined position reduces spinal loading and makes this an accessible option for warm-ups, active recovery, or steady-state cardio. While it engages the quads, glutes, and hamstrings through a pedaling motion, the resistance is insufficient for meaningful hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
instructions: Adjust the seat so that your knee has a slight bend at the furthest pedal position.
Sit back into the seat with your lower back supported by the backrest.
Place your feet on the pedals and grip the side handles lightly.
Begin pedaling at a comfortable cadence, selecting a resistance level that matches your training goal.
Maintain an upright posture and avoid gripping the handles too tightly.
Continue for the desired duration, adjusting resistance or cadence to manage intensity.
tips: Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
Adjust resistance to a level that challenges the quads without compromising form
Keep your posture upright and avoid hunching forward

name: Reverse Band Box Squat
description:
**A squat variation that combines a box target with reverse bands attached overhead to deload the barbell at the bottom and progressively increase resistance as you stand.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.

Sitting to a box enforces consistent depth and breaks the eccentric-concentric chain, while the bands accommodate the ascending strength curve of the squat, allowing heavier loads at lockout.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Attach heavy resistance bands from the top of a power rack to each end of the barbell so they provide assistance at the bottom.
Set a box behind you at a height that places your thighs at or just below parallel when seated.
Unrack the bar across your upper back, step back, and position your feet in a wide stance with toes slightly turned out.
Push your hips back and descend in a controlled manner until you sit fully on the box.
Pause briefly on the box, then drive forcefully through your feet to stand, pressing your hips forward at the top.
Re-rack the bar after completing all reps, ensuring the bands remain evenly loaded throughout.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Reverse Band Power Squat
description:
**A barbell squat performed with reverse bands that hang from the top of the rack and lighten the load at the bottom of the movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.

This setup matches the natural ascending strength curve of the squat, allowing you to overload the top portion while still achieving deep, stretch-loaded positions. The result is high mechanical tension through a full range of motion with strong hypertrophy potential for the quads.

**Pro tip:** Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
instructions: Loop heavy resistance bands over the top of a power rack and attach each end to the barbell sleeves.
Load the bar heavier than your normal squat weight, accounting for the band assistance at the bottom.
Unrack the barbell across your upper traps, step back, and set your feet shoulder-width apart.
Descend into a deep squat by pushing the hips back and bending the knees, feeling the bands assist as you approach the bottom.
Drive out of the hole by pressing through the full foot, maintaining a braced core as the band assistance decreases toward lockout.
Stand to full hip and knee extension, then begin the next rep under control.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Rickshaw Deadlift
description:
**A deadlift variation performed on a rickshaw or trap-bar-style frame that positions the handles at your sides rather than in front of the body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, quads, forearms, traps.

This neutral-grip setup reduces shear forces on the lumbar spine and shifts emphasis toward the glutes and quads while still heavily loading the posterior chain. The side-handle design also makes it more accessible for lifters with limited shoulder mobility.

**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Step inside the rickshaw frame and stand with feet hip-width apart, centered between the handles.
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to grasp the handles with a firm, neutral grip.
Set your back flat, brace your core, and pull your shoulders slightly back and down.
Drive through the floor by extending the hips and knees simultaneously, keeping the frame balanced.
Stand to full lockout with hips extended and shoulders pulled back—avoid hyperextending the lower back.
Reverse the movement by hinging at the hips first, then bending the knees to return the frame to the ground.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Rocket Jump
description:
**A high-intensity plyometric exercise where you squat down and then launch vertically with maximum effort, reaching your arms overhead like a rocket.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

This movement develops explosive lower-body power and cardiovascular conditioning. It is primarily an athletic and metabolic training tool with limited direct hypertrophy benefit.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hands at your sides.
Lower into a half-squat position, bringing your hands together in front of your chest.
Explode upward as high as possible, extending your arms straight overhead.
Fully extend your hips, knees, and ankles at the peak of the jump.
Land softly on the balls of your feet, immediately sinking back into the squat position.
Repeat in a continuous rhythm for the prescribed reps, prioritizing jump height and soft landings.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Rope Jumping
description:
**A classic conditioning exercise that involves continuously jumping over a spinning rope.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: calves. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, shoulders.

It primarily trains the calves through repeated plantar flexion while also challenging coordination, timing, and cardiovascular endurance. As a low-resistance, high-repetition activity, it serves as an effective warm-up or cardio tool but offers negligible muscle-building stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Stay on the balls of your feet
instructions: Hold one handle in each hand with the rope behind your heels, elbows close to your sides.
Swing the rope overhead by rotating your wrists—not your entire arms.
Jump just high enough to clear the rope, landing on the balls of your feet with soft knees.
Keep your torso upright and your core lightly engaged throughout.
Maintain a steady, rhythmic cadence, breathing naturally.
Continue for the target duration or rep count, resting briefly if you trip on the rope.
tips: Stay on the balls of your feet
Keep jumps small and efficient
This is conditioning, not calf training for hypertrophy

name: Rowing, Stationary
description:
**A full-body cardiovascular exercise performed on an indoor rowing ergometer.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: lats. Secondary: biceps, quads, hamstrings, glutes, erectors, forearms, calves.

Each stroke combines a powerful leg drive with a horizontal pull, engaging the lats, upper back, and biceps alongside the quads, glutes, and hamstrings. It offers a favorable effort-to-results ratio for conditioning and can serve as a low-impact alternative to running.

**Pro tip:** This is cardio, not lat hypertrophy training
instructions: Sit on the rower seat and strap your feet into the footplates with the strap across the ball of each foot.
Grab the handle with an overhand grip, arms extended, and slide forward until your shins are nearly vertical.
Initiate the stroke by driving through your heels, extending the knees and hips powerfully.
Once your legs are nearly straight, lean back slightly and pull the handle to your lower ribcage.
Reverse the sequence: extend the arms first, hinge forward at the hips, then bend the knees to slide back to the start.
Maintain a smooth, continuous rhythm at your target stroke rate and resistance setting.
tips: This is cardio, not lat hypertrophy training
The lats assist during the pull phase of each stroke
Drive with the legs first, then pull with the arms
Good for conditioning and warm-ups

name: Running, Treadmill
description:
**Steady-state or interval running performed on a motorized treadmill.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The controlled surface and adjustable speed and incline make it a versatile cardiovascular training tool. While it engages the quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves through repetitive stride cycles, the loading is far too light to drive meaningful hypertrophy, making it purely a conditioning modality.

**Pro tip:** Use this as active recovery or warm-up rather than a primary quad builder
instructions: Step onto the side rails of the treadmill and set your desired speed and incline.
Carefully step onto the moving belt, starting at a walking pace to warm up.
Gradually increase speed to your target running pace over 1–2 minutes.
Run with an upright posture, landing on the midfoot beneath your center of mass.
Swing your arms naturally at your sides, keeping shoulders relaxed and gaze forward.
To finish, gradually reduce speed back to a walk, then step off onto the side rails once the belt has slowed sufficiently.
tips: Use this as active recovery or warm-up rather than a primary quad builder
Focus on consistent effort rather than maximum intensity
Stay hydrated and maintain steady breathing throughout

name: Safety Bar Squat
description:
**A squat variation using a safety squat bar, which features padded shoulder supports and forward-angled handles.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, abs, adductors.

The cambered design shifts the load slightly forward, increasing demand on the quads and upper back compared to a straight bar. The padded yoke eliminates shoulder mobility requirements, making it an excellent option for lifters with upper-body limitations while still delivering high growth from the stretched position potential.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Position the safety squat bar across your upper traps, letting the pads rest on your shoulders and gripping the front handles.
Unrack the bar, step back, and set your feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly turned out.
Brace your core hard and begin the descent by simultaneously bending the hips and knees.
Squat to at least parallel depth, keeping your chest up against the bar's forward pull.
Drive upward by pressing through the full foot, maintaining an upright torso throughout the ascent.
Lock out the hips and knees at the top, then begin the next rep.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Sandbag Load
description:
**A strongman-style exercise where you lift an awkward, shifting sandbag from the ground and load it onto a platform or over a bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, quads, biceps, forearms, traps, shoulders, abs, calves.

The unstable load demands total-body coordination, grip endurance, and brute hip extension power. While it recruits nearly every muscle group, the high systemic fatigue and skill demands make it better suited for functional strength and competition prep than targeted hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Brace hard and drive through the hips
instructions: Straddle the sandbag with a wide stance, hinge at the hips, and dig your fingers underneath it.
Lap the sandbag by pulling it onto your thighs while sitting back slightly.
Wrap your arms around the bag, squeezing it tightly against your torso.
Explosively extend the hips and stand, driving the sandbag upward.
In one continuous motion, heave the bag onto the target platform or over the loading bar.
Reset your position and repeat, focusing on a fast, aggressive pick from the ground each rep.
tips: Brace hard and drive through the hips
Keep the implement close to your body
Explosive hip extension is key — lock out hard at the top

name: Scissors Jump
description:
**A dynamic plyometric lunge variation where you jump and switch your lead leg mid-air on every rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

This exercise develops single-leg power, coordination, and cardiovascular endurance. The rapid alternating pattern challenges balance and reactive strength, making it a staple in athletic conditioning programs.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Begin in a split stance with one foot forward and one foot back, both knees bent at roughly 90 degrees.
Explode upward by driving through both legs, swinging your arms for momentum.
While airborne, switch your leg positions so the back leg comes forward and the front leg goes back.
Land softly in a lunge position with the opposite leg now leading.
Immediately descend into the lunge and spring into the next jump.
Continue alternating for the prescribed reps, keeping your torso upright and landings controlled.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Side Hop-Sprint
description:
**An agility drill that combines lateral hops with a forward sprint, training both frontal-plane reactivity and linear acceleration.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The lateral component engages the adductors and abductors for stabilization while the sprint phase loads the quads, glutes, and hamstrings. This is a sport-performance and conditioning exercise with minimal hypertrophy value.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Stand in an athletic position with knees slightly bent and weight on the balls of your feet.
Perform 2–3 quick lateral hops to one side, staying low and controlled.
On the final hop, plant your outside foot hard and immediately transition into an all-out forward sprint.
Sprint for 10–20 yards, driving the knees high and pumping the arms.
Decelerate gradually, walk back to the start, and repeat hopping to the opposite side.
Focus on a seamless transition from lateral movement to linear speed on each rep.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Side Standing Long Jump
description:
**A lateral plyometric exercise where you jump for maximum distance to one side from a standing position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

It develops frontal-plane power, hip abductor and adductor strength, and single-leg landing stability. This drill is commonly used in athletic testing and sport-specific power development programs.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Stand on one leg or with feet together, perpendicular to the direction you intend to jump.
Load the hips and knees with a quick countermovement, swinging the arms to generate momentum.
Explode laterally, driving off the inside leg and projecting your body as far to the side as possible.
Land on both feet simultaneously with knees bent to absorb the impact.
Stabilize completely before resetting—avoid letting momentum carry you into extra steps.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, alternating direction each set or as programmed.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Side to Side Box Shuffle
description:
**A lateral agility drill performed by shuffling over a low box or step from side to side.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

It trains lateral quickness, hip stability, and cardiovascular conditioning while lightly loading the quads, glutes, and adductors. The continuous movement pattern makes it effective for warm-ups and metabolic circuits.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Stand to one side of a low box or aerobic step in an athletic stance.
Step the near foot onto the box, then quickly follow with the far foot so both feet briefly touch the top.
Step down on the opposite side, leading with the far foot.
Immediately reverse direction and shuffle back over the box.
Keep your hips low, chest up, and arms pumping naturally for balance.
Continue alternating sides at a brisk pace for the prescribed duration or rep count.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Single Leg Butt Kick
description:
**A dynamic warm-up and conditioning drill where you rapidly kick one heel toward your glute while hopping on the standing leg.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: hamstrings, calves, glutes.

It promotes hamstring activation, knee flexion mobility, and light cardiovascular stimulus. This is a preparatory movement best used before sprinting or lower-body training sessions.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Stand tall on one leg with a slight bend in the supporting knee.
Rapidly kick the free leg's heel up toward your glute by flexing the knee.
Simultaneously perform a small hop on the standing leg to maintain rhythm.
Snap the kicking leg back down and immediately repeat.
Complete all reps on one side before switching, or alternate legs each rep as programmed.
Keep your posture upright and your core engaged—avoid leaning forward excessively.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Single Leg Extension
description:
**A unilateral version of the leg extension performed one leg at a time on a standard leg extension machine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

Training each leg independently eliminates strength imbalances and allows you to focus entirely on the quadriceps contraction. The excellent effort-to-results ratio and isolation nature make it a top-tier accessory for quad hypertrophy and rehabilitation.

**Pro tip:** Extend fully to lockout to achieve peak quad contraction at the top
instructions: Adjust the machine so the backrest supports your full back and the ankle pad sits just above your foot.
Sit with one leg hooked under the pad and the other resting to the side or on the floor.
Extend the working knee until the leg is fully straight, squeezing the quad hard at the top.
Hold the peak contraction for a brief count.
Lower the weight slowly under control, stopping just before the weight stack touches down.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other side.
tips: Extend fully to lockout to achieve peak quad contraction at the top
Control the eccentric phase for 3 seconds to maximize quad tension throughout the ROM
Adjust the pad position so it sits just above your ankles
Point your toes slightly outward to emphasize the vastus medialis
Avoid using momentum — each rep should be controlled and deliberate

name: Single Leg Press
description:
**A unilateral leg press performed one leg at a time, which increases range of motion and eliminates bilateral strength discrepancies.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors.

The deep stretch at the bottom combined with the ascending resistance profile creates excellent conditions for quad hypertrophy. The machine's back support removes spinal loading, allowing you to push closer to failure safely.

**Pro tip:** Lower the sled until your knees reach at least 90 degrees of flexion for full quad loading
instructions: Sit in the leg press with your back flat against the pad and place one foot in the center of the platform at shoulder height.
Release the safety catches and lower the sled by bending the knee, allowing a deep stretch in the quad and hip.
Descend until your knee approaches your chest without your lower back rounding off the pad.
Press through the full foot to drive the sled back up, stopping just short of full knee lockout.
Maintain steady control throughout—avoid bouncing out of the bottom.
Complete all reps on one leg, then switch sides and match the volume.
tips: Lower the sled until your knees reach at least 90 degrees of flexion for full quad loading
Place your feet lower on the platform to increase knee flexion demand and quad emphasis
Do not lock out fully at the top — maintain tension on the quads throughout
Use a controlled eccentric of 2-3 seconds to maximize quad time under tension
A narrower foot placement shifts more demand to the quads

name: Single Leg Push-off
description:
**A unilateral plyometric exercise where you drive off one leg from an elevated surface to develop single-leg power and balance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

By isolating each leg, this drill exposes and corrects asymmetries in force production. It is primarily used for athletic development and pre-activity potentiation rather than muscle growth.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Stand facing a sturdy box or step with one foot placed firmly on top.
Drive through the elevated foot to propel your body upward, fully extending the hip and knee.
Swing the opposite knee upward to add momentum and height.
Land softly back on the box with the same foot, absorbing the impact through a slight knee bend.
Reset your balance briefly, then immediately perform the next rep.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other side.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Single-Cone Sprint Drill
description:
**A speed and agility drill built around a single cone used as a directional marker for sprints, cuts, and decelerations.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

It trains acceleration mechanics, change-of-direction ability, and reactive footwork. As a conditioning and sport-performance tool, it generates high cardiovascular demand but minimal hypertrophy stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Place a single cone 10–15 yards away from your starting position.
Begin in a two-point or three-point stance and sprint to the cone at maximum effort.
Decelerate sharply at the cone, planting your outside foot to change direction.
Sprint back to the starting line with the same intensity.
Rest briefly, then repeat for the prescribed number of rounds.
Vary the drill by approaching the cone from different angles or adding a lateral shuffle on the return.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Single-Leg High Box Squat
description:
**A unilateral squat variation performed by lowering yourself onto a high box using one leg.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, abs, adductors.

The elevated surface reduces range-of-motion demands while still training deep knee flexion under load, making it an excellent progression toward full pistol squats and a reliable tool for correcting side-to-side strength imbalances.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Stand on one foot approximately six inches in front of a knee-height box or bench.
Extend the non-working leg forward and hold your arms out for balance.
Push your hips back and bend the working knee to lower yourself in a controlled manner until your glutes lightly touch the box surface.
Pause briefly without fully sitting, then drive through the full foot to stand back up.
Keep your torso as upright as possible and your knee tracking over your toes throughout the movement.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Single-Leg Hop Progression
description:
**A plyometric drill that develops single-leg power, reactive strength, and ankle stability through progressively challenging hopping patterns.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

Primarily used in athletic performance and rehabilitation programs rather than hypertrophy training, it trains the stretch-shortening cycle and improves force production at high velocities.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Begin standing on one foot with a slight bend in the knee and your arms relaxed at your sides.
Start with small, controlled hops in place, focusing on soft landings with the knee bending to absorb impact.
Once stable, progress to forward hops covering short distances, then lateral hops, and finally multi-directional patterns.
Swing your arms naturally to assist each jump.
Land quietly each time, maintaining balance for a full second before the next hop.
Perform all reps on one leg before switching.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Single-Leg Lateral Hop
description:
**A single-leg plyometric exercise performed by hopping side to side, targeting frontal-plane power and stability.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

This movement strengthens the muscles responsible for lateral deceleration and change of direction, making it particularly valuable for athletes in court and field sports as well as for knee injury prevention protocols.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Stand on one foot with a soft knee bend.
Push off laterally, jumping to the side and landing on the same foot.
Absorb the landing by bending at the hip, knee, and ankle simultaneously, holding the balanced position for one to two seconds.
Then hop back to the starting position using the same controlled landing.
Keep your chest up and your core braced throughout.
Use your arms for counterbalance by swinging them in the direction of travel.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
