Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Single-Leg Leg Extension
description:
**A unilateral isolation exercise performed on a leg extension machine using one leg at a time.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: none.

By working each quadriceps independently, this variation exposes and corrects strength asymmetries while delivering a focused stimulus with minimal systemic fatigue. The machine's fixed path allows you to push close to failure safely on each side.

**Pro tip:** Squeeze hard at the top for 1-2 seconds to maximize peak contraction
instructions: Sit in the leg extension machine with your back flat against the pad and adjust the shin pad so it rests just above your ankle.
Select a weight appropriate for single-leg work.
Extend one leg by contracting the quadriceps until the knee is fully straightened, squeezing hard at the top for a one-count.
Lower the weight slowly under control, taking roughly two to three seconds on the eccentric.
Stop just short of the weight stack touching to maintain constant tension.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other.
tips: Squeeze hard at the top for 1-2 seconds to maximize peak contraction
Use a full range of motion from deep flexion to full extension
Keep your back flat against the seat pad throughout
Lower the weight slowly — the eccentric is where much of the hypertrophy stimulus occurs

name: Single-Leg Stride Jump
description:
**An explosive single-leg plyometric that involves driving off one foot to cover maximum forward distance in a bounding stride pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

This exercise develops unilateral power output and hip extension force, making it a staple in sprint training and athletic performance programs rather than traditional muscle-building routines.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Stand on one foot with a slight forward lean and your arms ready at your sides.
Drive forcefully off the ground by extending the hip, knee, and ankle, propelling yourself forward and slightly upward in a long stride.
Swing the opposite knee forward and up to generate momentum.
Land on the same driving foot with a bent knee, absorbing the impact quietly.
Stabilize fully before performing the next rep.
Keep your core tight and your gaze forward throughout the set.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Sissy Squat
description:
**A bodyweight quad isolation movement where you lean your torso backward while bending deeply at the knees, placing extreme stretch on the rectus femoris and vastus muscles.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, abs.

The descending strength curve means the hardest point is the deep position, delivering a powerful lengthened-position stimulus that rivals machine-based quad exercises.

**Pro tip:** Lean your torso back as your knees travel forward to create extreme quad stretch
instructions: Stand upright with your feet hip-width apart and hold onto a sturdy object at your side for light balance support.

Rise onto the balls of your feet, then simultaneously push your knees far forward and lean your torso backward, keeping your hips fully extended so your body forms a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Lower until your knees reach maximum flexion and you feel a deep quad stretch.
Reverse the motion by driving through the balls of your feet and squeezing the quads to return to the starting position.
Avoid sitting back into the hips — the movement should occur almost entirely at the knee joint.
tips: Lean your torso back as your knees travel forward to create extreme quad stretch
Control the descent slowly — this exercise places high stress on the knee joint
Start with bodyweight only until you master the balance and movement pattern
Keep your hips extended throughout — do not sit back into a squat pattern
Use a support for balance if needed, but do not pull yourself up with your arms

name: Sit Squats
description:
**A beginner-friendly squat variation where you lower yourself to a seated position on a chair or bench and then stand back up.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors.

The tactile target removes the fear of falling and teaches proper squat depth, making it an ideal entry point for new trainees or those rehabilitating lower-body injuries.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Stand with your back to a sturdy chair or bench, feet shoulder-width apart and toes turned out slightly.
Extend your arms in front of you for counterbalance.
Push your hips back and bend your knees to lower yourself in a controlled fashion until you are fully seated on the surface.
Pause for a brief moment, then lean your torso slightly forward and drive through your heels to stand back up without using momentum.
Keep your chest lifted and your knees tracking over your toes throughout the entire movement.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Skating
description:
**An endurance and coordination activity that involves continuous lateral pushing and gliding motions, whether on ice, inline skates, or mimicked as a bodyweight drill.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

Skating primarily develops cardiovascular fitness and lower-body muscular endurance rather than hypertrophy, with significant demands on the adductors and hip stabilizers.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
instructions: Assume a low athletic stance with knees bent and your torso leaning slightly forward.
Push off laterally with one foot, extending the hip and knee to propel yourself to the side.
Glide or land on the opposite foot, absorbing the force with a bent knee.
Immediately push off in the other direction, alternating sides in a rhythmic pattern.
Keep your arms swinging naturally for balance and momentum.
Maintain a low center of gravity throughout and focus on powerful lateral pushes rather than upward bouncing.
tips: Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
Adjust resistance to a level that challenges the quads without compromising form
Keep your posture upright and avoid hunching forward

name: Sled Drag - Harness
description:
**A loaded locomotion exercise performed by attaching a harness to your torso and dragging a weighted sled behind you.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The concentric-only nature of the movement produces minimal muscle damage and soreness, making it an excellent tool for building work capacity, improving conditioning, and adding training volume without compromising recovery.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
instructions: Attach a sled harness around your waist or shoulders and connect it to a loaded sled via a strap or chain.
Face away from the sled and lean forward into the harness until the strap is taut.
Drive forward by taking powerful, deliberate steps, pushing through the balls of your feet with each stride.
Keep your torso at roughly a 45-degree forward lean and pump your arms to assist the drive.
Maintain a steady pace for the prescribed distance, focusing on full leg extension with each step.
Walk back to the start to reset for the next set.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
Keep your torso at a forward lean angle appropriate for the implement
Start with lighter loads and focus on maintaining consistent stride length
Use this as a finisher or conditioning tool after primary quad work

name: Smith Machine Leg Press
description:
**A creative leg press alternative performed by lying on the floor beneath a Smith machine and pressing the bar upward with your feet.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

This setup replicates the leg press angle when no dedicated machine is available, providing a deep stretch on the quads and glutes at the bottom while the Smith bar's fixed path ensures stable, repeatable reps.

**Pro tip:** Push through the balls of your feet to emphasize quad activation
instructions: Position a mat on the floor directly beneath the Smith machine bar.
Lie on your back and place both feet flat on the bar at roughly shoulder width.
Unrack the bar by rotating the safety hooks.
Bend your knees and lower the bar toward your chest, allowing your knees to travel past 90 degrees for a deep stretch.
Press the bar back up by extending your hips and knees, stopping just short of full lockout to keep tension on the quads.
Rerack the bar carefully after completing all reps.
tips: Push through the balls of your feet to emphasize quad activation
Lower the sled as deep as your hip mobility allows without your lower back rounding
Keep your head and back flat against the pad throughout the movement
Avoid bouncing at the bottom — control the turnaround to protect your knees

name: Smith Machine Pistol Squat
description:
**A machine-assisted single-leg squat performed in the Smith machine, which provides balance support while preserving the deep unilateral stimulus of a pistol squat.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs.

The guided bar path lets you focus entirely on leg drive and depth without worrying about lateral stability, making it accessible to lifters who lack the mobility or balance for freestanding pistol squats.

**Pro tip:** Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
instructions: Stand inside the Smith machine with the bar resting across your upper traps.
Unrack the bar and lift one foot off the ground, extending that leg forward.
Slowly bend the working knee and push your hips back to descend into a deep single-leg squat, going as low as your mobility allows.
Keep your heel planted and your torso relatively upright by using the bar for balance.
Drive through the working foot to stand back up, squeezing the quad and glute at the top.
Complete all reps on one side before switching legs.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Smith Machine Squat
description:
**A bilateral squat performed on the Smith machine, where the fixed vertical bar path removes the need for horizontal balance and allows you to position your feet slightly forward to emphasize quad loading. This makes it a strong hypertrophy tool for lifters who want to train close to failure on squats without the stability demands of a free barbell.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.



**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Step under the Smith machine bar and position it across your upper back.
Place your feet slightly forward of the bar at shoulder width with toes angled out about 15 degrees.
Unrack the bar by rotating the hooks.
Descend by bending at the hips and knees simultaneously, lowering until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive upward through your full foot, pressing your back into the bar as you extend the hips and knees.
Avoid locking out completely at the top to maintain quad tension.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Smith Single-Leg Split Squat
description:
**A unilateral squat variation performed in the Smith machine with one foot elevated behind you on a bench.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The fixed bar path eliminates balance as a limiting factor, allowing you to load the front leg aggressively and achieve deep knee flexion for a potent quad and glute stimulus with excellent stretch at the bottom.

**Pro tip:** Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
instructions: Set up inside the Smith machine with a flat bench positioned about two feet behind you.
Place the bar across your upper traps and unrack it.
Rest the top of your rear foot on the bench behind you.
Lower yourself by bending the front knee, descending until the rear knee nearly touches the floor and the front thigh is at or below parallel.
Keep your front shin relatively vertical and your torso upright.
Press through the front heel and midfoot to drive back up to the starting position.
tips: Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
Lower until your rear knee nearly touches the ground for full quad ROM
Keep your torso upright to maintain quad dominance on the front leg
Push through the front foot to drive back up, focusing on quad contraction
coaching_cues: Drop the back knee straight down

name: Snatch Balance
description:
**An Olympic weightlifting accessory where you press yourself under a barbell held overhead by rapidly dropping into a full overhead squat.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, shoulders, triceps, abs, calves, traps, erectors, forearms.

This highly technical movement develops speed under the bar, overhead stability, and confidence in the receiving position of the snatch. It is a skill and coordination exercise rather than a hypertrophy tool.

**Pro tip:** Focus on speed under the bar — the quads must fire rapidly to catch the weight
instructions: Stand with the barbell resting on your upper back using a wide snatch grip.
Your feet should be at hip width.

In one explosive motion, dip slightly at the knees and then drive yourself downward under the bar, punching your arms to full lockout overhead while simultaneously jumping your feet out to squat width.
Receive the bar in a deep overhead squat with your arms locked, chest up, and hips below parallel.
Stand up by driving through your full foot while keeping the bar stable overhead.
Reset the bar to your back and repeat.
tips: Focus on speed under the bar — the quads must fire rapidly to catch the weight
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the movement
Maintain an upright torso as you receive the bar in the squat position
Start with light loads to master the timing and coordination

name: Snatch from Blocks
description:
**A snatch variation where the barbell starts on elevated blocks rather than the floor, allowing the lifter to isolate specific phases of the pull.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, traps, erectors, shoulders, forearms, triceps, calves, quads.

By adjusting block height, you can target the second pull or transition phase, making this a valuable positional drill for improving snatch technique, rate of force development, and pulling power.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Set the blocks so the barbell rests at your desired starting height — typically just below or above the knee.
Take a wide snatch grip and set your back flat with shoulders over or slightly in front of the bar.
Initiate the pull by driving your legs into the floor and extending the hips explosively.
As the bar passes the hip crease, shrug and pull yourself under the bar, receiving it overhead in a full squat with locked arms.
Stand to full extension, then lower the bar back to the blocks under control.
Reset your position completely before each rep.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Spanish Squat
description:
**A band-assisted squat variation where a resistance band is looped behind the knees and anchored to a fixed point, pulling the tibia backward and allowing the lifter to sit deeply into knee flexion with a vertical torso. This unique setup isolates the quadriceps by reducing hip-hinge demands and is widely used in patellar tendon rehabilitation and quad-focused hypertrophy work.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, adductors.



**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Anchor a heavy looped resistance band to a sturdy post at knee height.
Step into the band so it sits in the crease behind both knees, then walk backward until there is significant tension pulling your shins back.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Lean your torso back slightly and bend your knees to lower into a deep squat, letting the band support your balance.
Your shins should remain nearly vertical while your knees flex deeply.
Hold the bottom position or perform controlled reps by driving through your quads to stand.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Speed Box Squat
description:
**A dynamic-effort squat variation where you sit onto a box and explode upward with maximal speed using submaximal loads, typically 50-60% of your one-rep max.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.

The brief pause on the box breaks the eccentric-concentric chain, forcing you to generate force from a dead stop and developing explosive power and rate of force development in the squat pattern.

**Pro tip:** Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
instructions: Set a box behind you at a height that puts your hip crease at or just below knee level.
Position a barbell on your upper back and unrack it.
Take a wide stance with toes pointed out.
Sit back and descend in a controlled manner onto the box, keeping your shins vertical.
Once seated, maintain tightness in your core and upper back — do not relax.
After a brief pause, explode upward by driving your feet into the floor and thrusting your hips forward as fast as possible.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Speed Squats
description:
**A dynamic-effort barbell squat performed with submaximal weight and maximum concentric velocity.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.

Typically programmed at 50-70% of one-rep max for multiple low-rep sets, speed squats develop rate of force development, improve nerve-to-muscle efficiency, and teach the body to recruit high-threshold muscle fibers quickly — all of which transfer to heavier maximal-effort squats.

**Pro tip:** Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
instructions: Set up a barbell on your upper back as you would for a standard back squat.
Unrack the bar and take your normal squat stance.
Descend at a controlled but brisk pace to at least parallel depth.
The moment you reach the bottom, reverse direction and drive upward as explosively as possible, aiming to accelerate the bar through the entire concentric phase.
Maintain perfect technique despite the speed — chest up, knees tracking over toes, and core braced.
Perform sets of two to three reps with short rest periods between sets to maintain bar speed quality.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Split Clean
description:
**A clean variation where the lifter receives the barbell in a split stance — one foot forward and one foot back — rather than a full front squat.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, traps, erectors, shoulders, forearms, calves, quads.

This older style of receiving position demands less ankle and hip mobility than a squat clean and is sometimes used as a teaching progression or by athletes whose mobility limits the squat catch position.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet hip-width apart and grip the bar just outside your knees with a clean grip.
Set your back flat and shoulders over the bar.
Pull the bar from the floor by extending the legs and hips.
As the bar reaches hip height, shrug explosively and pull yourself under the bar, splitting your feet into a lunge position — front foot flat and rear foot on the ball.
Catch the bar in the front rack position with elbows high.
Stabilize in the split, then recover by stepping the front foot back and the rear foot forward to bring your feet together.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Split Jerk
description:
**The most common jerk variation in Olympic weightlifting, where the lifter drives a barbell overhead from the front rack and receives it with the legs split into a lunge stance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

This technique allows heavier loads to be locked out overhead compared to a push jerk because the split position lowers the required receiving height. It is a power and skill movement, not a hypertrophy exercise.

**Pro tip:** This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
instructions: Start with the barbell in the front rack position — bar resting on the front deltoids, elbows up, and feet at hip width.
Dip straight down by bending the knees two to three inches while keeping the torso vertical.
Immediately reverse direction and drive the bar upward explosively by extending the legs and hips.
As the bar leaves the shoulders, split your feet — front foot forward about two feet and rear foot back about the same distance.
Punch your arms to full lockout overhead and catch the bar with a stable, slightly bent front knee and rear knee slightly bent.
Recover by stepping the front foot back halfway, then the rear foot forward to bring feet together under the locked-out bar.
tips: This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
The legs do most of the work — the arms guide and stabilize
Practice the footwork separately before adding heavy loads
Not recommended as a primary deltoid hypertrophy exercise

name: Split Jump
description:
**A plyometric lunge variation where you explosively jump from a split stance and switch your leg positions mid-air before landing.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

This exercise builds lower-body reactive power, coordination, and single-leg stability, making it a staple in athletic conditioning and HIIT-style training programs.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Begin in a lunge position with one foot forward and the other behind you, both knees bent to approximately 90 degrees.
Swing your arms and explode upward, jumping as high as possible.
While airborne, switch your leg positions so the front leg moves to the back and the back leg moves to the front.
Land softly in a lunge with both knees bending to absorb the impact.
Immediately descend into the next rep or pause briefly to stabilize before jumping again.
Keep your torso upright and your core engaged throughout the set.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Split Squat with Dumbbells
description:
**A stationary unilateral squat performed with a dumbbell in each hand and one foot positioned forward of the other.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, adductors, abs.

This exercise delivers a deep stretch on the quads and hip flexors of the rear leg while heavily loading the front leg's quadriceps and glutes. The dumbbell loading keeps the center of gravity low, reducing balance demands compared to barbell variations.

**Pro tip:** Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and step one foot forward about two to three feet, keeping the rear foot on its ball.
This is your starting position.
Lower your body by bending both knees until the rear knee nearly touches the floor and the front thigh reaches parallel or below.
Keep your front shin close to vertical and your torso upright with a neutral spine.
Drive through the front foot's heel and midfoot to return to the starting position.
Complete all prescribed reps on one side before switching the forward leg.
tips: Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
Avoid letting your front knee collapse inward — track it over your second toe
A shorter step length increases quad demand; a longer step shifts to glutes
Keep your weight centered over the front leg throughout the movement
coaching_cues: Drive up from the front leg

name: Squat Jerk
description:
**A rare and highly technical jerk variation where the lifter drives the barbell overhead and receives it in a full overhead squat rather than a split stance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: triceps, quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, traps, erectors, forearms.

This demands exceptional overhead mobility, core stability, and timing. While it allows the lifter to receive the bar at the lowest possible height, the difficulty of the catch position makes it uncommon outside of a few elite weightlifters.

**Pro tip:** This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
instructions: Begin with the barbell in the front rack position with feet at hip width.
Perform a short, sharp dip by bending the knees while keeping the torso perfectly vertical.
Explode upward by extending the legs and hips to drive the bar off the shoulders.
Instead of splitting, jump your feet out to squat width and drop into a full overhead squat, punching the arms to lockout as you descend.
Receive the bar with locked elbows, an upright torso, and hips well below parallel.
Stand up from the squat while keeping the bar stable overhead.
tips: This is a skill movement — technique matters more than raw strength
The legs do most of the work — the arms guide and stabilize
Practice the footwork separately before adding heavy loads
Not recommended as a primary deltoid hypertrophy exercise

name: Squat with Bands
description:
**A barbell back squat performed with resistance bands attached from the bar to the floor or rack base, adding accommodating resistance that increases as you stand up.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.

The bands reduce the load at the bottom where you are weakest and add resistance at the top where you are strongest, training you to accelerate through the entire range of motion and improving lockout strength.

**Pro tip:** Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
instructions: Loop resistance bands over each end of the barbell and anchor them to heavy dumbbells on the floor or to the base of the squat rack.
Set up under the bar as you would for a normal back squat — bar on upper back, feet shoulder-width apart.
Unrack the bar and note the added tension from the bands.
Descend to at least parallel depth at a controlled pace.
Drive upward explosively, pushing hard through the sticking point as band tension increases.
Focus on accelerating the bar all the way to the top of the rep.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Squat with Chains
description:
**A barbell back squat with heavy chains draped over the bar sleeves, providing accommodating resistance that changes with your position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, erectors, calves, adductors, abs.

As you descend, chain links pile on the floor and the load decreases; as you stand, the chains lift off the floor and the load increases. This teaches aggressive drive out of the hole and builds strength through the full range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
instructions: Drape chains evenly over each sleeve of the barbell so that roughly half the chain rests on the floor when you are standing at full extension.
Set up under the bar with your normal squat stance and unrack it.
Descend into a full squat — as you lower, chain links will accumulate on the floor, reducing the total load at the bottom.
Drive out of the hole aggressively, knowing the weight will increase as more chain lifts off the ground.
Maintain a braced core, upright chest, and consistent bar path throughout.
The variable loading encourages maximum effort at every point in the range of motion.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Squat with Plate Movers
description: **Turn every rep into a full-body coordination drill by shifting plates mid-set.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs, adductors, and lower back. This barbell squat variation adds plate repositioning between reps, forcing your core to stabilize under shifting loads. The deep squat position stretches your quads hard while the ascending strength curve means lockout gets easier. **Pro tip:** Keep your chest tall during plate transitions — the moment you round forward under fatigue is when injuries happen. **Biomechanics:** The ascending resistance profile means your quads and glutes produce peak force as you drive out of the hole, while plate repositioning adds an anti-rotation demand on your core.
instructions: Set up a barbell on a squat rack at shoulder height and load it with plates.
Unrack the bar across your upper back, step back, and place your feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly turned out.
Lower yourself by bending your knees and hips until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your whole foot to stand back up, then reposition the plate movers as needed before the next rep.
Keep your core braced and chest lifted throughout every rep and transition.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Squats - With Bands
description: **Make the top of your squat brutally hard — where most people coast.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, lower back, and adductors. Looping a resistance band around the setup adds increasing tension as you stand, eliminating the easy lockout most lifters rely on. This teaches you to accelerate through the entire range of motion. **Pro tip:** Start with a light band — even a thin one adds significant resistance at the top and changes the feel dramatically. **Biomechanics:** The band's elastic tension stacks on top of the barbell's gravity-based load, creating accommodating resistance that better matches your ascending strength curve through the squat.
instructions: Loop a resistance band under your feet or around the base of a squat rack, then attach each end to the bar.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly out, and unrack the bar across your upper back.
Lower yourself under control until your thighs reach parallel or below, feeling the band tension decrease at the bottom.
Push hard through your feet to stand up, driving against the increasing band resistance.
Complete all reps with a tight core and controlled tempo.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Stairmaster
description: **The machine that humbles everyone — from beginners to elite athletes.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves. The revolving staircase forces continuous stepping under load, building muscular endurance and cardiovascular fitness simultaneously. Unlike flat cardio, every step demands real quad and glute effort against gravity. **Pro tip:** Stand upright and avoid death-gripping the handrails — light fingertip contact only, so your legs do the real work. **Biomechanics:** Each step cycle moves your knee and hip through a mid-range arc, producing steady tension on the quads and glutes without the joint stress of impact-based cardio.
instructions: Step onto the Stairmaster machine and grip the handrails lightly for balance.
Select your desired speed or program on the console.
Begin stepping at a steady pace, placing your full foot on each stair.
Keep your torso upright and avoid leaning heavily on the rails.
Maintain a consistent rhythm for your target duration, adjusting speed as needed.
tips: Focus on the working leg doing all the work — the trail leg should be passive
Maintain an upright posture and avoid leaning forward
Use a tempo of 2-1-2 for controlled quad loading through the full range
coaching_cues: All the work from the lead leg

name: Standing Elevated Quad Stretch
description: **Unlock tight quads in seconds by using gravity and elevation to deepen the stretch.** **Muscles worked:** quads. This bodyweight stretch places your foot on a raised surface behind you, allowing gravity to gently pull your hip forward and lengthen the entire front of your thigh. It's especially useful after heavy squatting or prolonged sitting. **Pro tip:** Squeeze your glute on the stretching side to tilt your pelvis and intensify the stretch without forcing your knee.
instructions: Stand in front of a bench, step, or raised surface and place the top of one foot on it behind you.
Keep your standing leg slightly bent and your torso tall.
Gently push your hips forward until you feel a deep stretch along the front of your back thigh.
Hold for 20 to 30 seconds, breathing steadily.
Switch legs and repeat.
tips: Hold for 20-30 seconds per side
Breathe deeply throughout the stretch
Do not bounce or force the stretch

name: Standing Hip Flexors
description: **Release the muscles that sitting all day locks up tight.** **Muscles worked:** quads (specifically the hip flexor portion) and abs. This standing stretch targets the front of your hip where your quad crosses the joint, helping restore range of motion lost from desk work. Regular practice can reduce lower back tension caused by chronically shortened hip flexors. **Pro tip:** Think about tucking your tailbone slightly under you — this posterior pelvic tilt is what actually stretches the hip flexor, not just leaning forward.
instructions: Stand tall and step one foot forward into a staggered stance.
Bend your front knee slightly while keeping your back leg straight.
Tuck your tailbone under and gently shift your hips forward until you feel a stretch at the front of your back hip.
Hold for 20 to 30 seconds while breathing deeply.
Switch sides and repeat.
tips: Ease into the stretch gradually
Maintain steady breathing
Stop if you feel sharp pain rather than a gentle pull

name: Standing Long Jump
description: **Measure your raw lower-body power in a single explosive leap.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves. This bodyweight movement demands maximum force production in one coordinated burst, training your muscles to fire together rapidly. It builds athletic power that transfers directly to sprinting, cutting, and sport performance. **Pro tip:** Swing your arms aggressively — they contribute up to 20% of your jump distance by creating upward and forward momentum. **Biomechanics:** The rapid stretch-shortening cycle during the countermovement loads your quads and glutes eccentrically before an explosive concentric push through the mid-range of hip and knee extension.
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart at a marked starting line.
Swing your arms back as you bend your knees and hips into a quarter-squat.
Explode forward and upward by extending your hips, knees, and ankles while swinging your arms forward.
Land softly on both feet with bent knees to absorb the impact.
Mark your distance and reset for the next rep.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Star Jump
description: **Explode off the ground and spread wide like a starfish — pure athletic fun.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, and shoulders. This full-body plyometric move combines a squat jump with a mid-air spread of your arms and legs, building coordination, power, and cardiovascular endurance all at once. It's a staple in bodyweight conditioning circuits. **Pro tip:** Focus on a soft, quiet landing — if your feet are slapping the ground, you're not absorbing force properly. **Biomechanics:** The jump phase loads your quads and glutes concentrically while the mid-air limb spread activates your shoulders and hip abductors, and the landing demands eccentric control from your entire lower body.
instructions: Stand with your feet together and arms at your sides.
Bend your knees into a quarter-squat, then jump as high as you can.
At the peak of your jump, spread your arms and legs wide into a star shape.
Bring your limbs back together before landing softly with bent knees.
Immediately drop into the next rep for continuous movement.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Step Mill
description: **Climb an endless staircase that builds legs of steel and lungs to match.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves. The step mill uses rotating stairs that demand a full stepping motion, making it more challenging than a standard stair stepper. Each step requires you to lift your body weight, building real lower-body strength alongside serious cardio conditioning. **Pro tip:** Skip every other step occasionally to increase the range of motion and shift more work to your glutes. **Biomechanics:** The continuous stepping pattern keeps your quads and glutes under constant mid-range tension while the vertical displacement of your center of mass creates a high metabolic demand.
instructions: Step onto the step mill machine and lightly hold the side rails for balance.
Choose your speed or workout program on the display.
Begin climbing by stepping naturally onto each rotating stair with your full foot.
Stand tall with your core engaged — avoid hunching over the console.
Continue for your target time, adjusting intensity as needed.
tips: Use a box height that creates at least 90 degrees of knee flexion on the working leg
Drive through the heel and midfoot of the top leg — avoid pushing off the back foot
Keep your torso upright throughout to maintain quad dominance
Control the descent slowly to maximize eccentric quad loading

name: Step-Up
description: **One leg, one box, total quad domination — no hiding your weak side.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and adductors. Holding dumbbells while stepping onto a raised platform forces each leg to work independently, exposing and correcting strength imbalances. The movement closely mimics real-life activities like climbing stairs, making it highly functional. **Pro tip:** Push through the heel of your top foot and avoid pushing off with your bottom foot — that's cheating your working leg out of the stimulus. **Biomechanics:** The single-leg stance creates a mid-range loading pattern on the quads and glutes while demanding significant stabilization from the hip adductors and core.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand facing a sturdy box or bench.
Place your entire right foot on top of the box.
Press through your right foot to step up, bringing your left foot to meet it on top.
Step back down with your left foot first, controlling the descent.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other leg.
tips: Focus on the working leg doing all the work — the trail leg should be passive
Maintain an upright posture and avoid leaning forward
Use a tempo of 2-1-2 for controlled quad loading through the full range
coaching_cues: All the work from the lead leg

name: Stride Jump Crossover
description: **Add a lateral twist to your jumps and train the agility most programs ignore.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, and adductors. This bodyweight plyometric combines a forward stride with a crossover landing pattern, challenging your coordination and lateral stability. It builds the multi-directional power athletes need for cutting and change-of-direction sports. **Pro tip:** Start slow and master the footwork pattern before adding speed — sloppy crossovers are an ankle injury waiting to happen. **Biomechanics:** The crossover pattern loads the adductors and lateral stabilizers eccentrically on landing while the jump phase trains concentric quad and glute power through a mid-range arc.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart in an open space.
Jump forward off both feet while crossing one leg in front of the other mid-air.
Land softly with knees bent, absorbing the impact through your legs.
Immediately jump again, crossing the opposite leg in front this time.
Continue alternating crossover jumps for your target rep count.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Suspended Split Squat
description: **Hang your back foot in a suspension strap and discover muscles you didn't know existed.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, and adductors. Elevating your rear foot in a suspension trainer creates instability that forces deep stabilizer engagement while allowing greater range of motion than a standard split squat. The deep stretch at the bottom makes this excellent for quad growth. **Pro tip:** Position yourself far enough from the anchor that the strap hangs at about 45 degrees — too close and you'll fight the straps. **Biomechanics:** The suspended rear foot shifts most load to the front leg while instability recruits additional stabilizers, and the deep bottom position creates high stretch-mediated quad tension.
instructions: Set up a suspension trainer or TRX at about knee height.
Face away from the anchor and place the top of one foot in the strap behind you.
Hop your front foot forward until you have a comfortable split stance.
Lower your back knee toward the floor by bending your front knee, keeping your torso upright.
Drive through your front foot to return to standing.
Complete all reps on one side, then switch legs.
tips: Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
Maintain an upright torso — forward lean reduces quad stimulus
Drive up through the ball of the front foot for maximum quad engagement
Use a controlled tempo to increase time under tension on the quads

name: Tempo Squat
description: **Slow the clock and make every inch of the squat count — no momentum allowed.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, adductors, and lower back. By deliberately slowing the lowering and rising phases of a barbell squat, you dramatically increase time under tension and eliminate any bouncing or cheating. This builds muscular control and hypertrophy with lighter loads than regular squats. **Pro tip:** Use a 3-1-3 tempo (3 seconds down, 1-second pause, 3 seconds up) as your starting point and adjust from there. **Biomechanics:** The extended eccentric phase maximizes muscle fiber recruitment in the quads at their most stretched position, while the pause eliminates the stretch reflex and forces pure concentric strength.
instructions: Set up a barbell on a squat rack and unrack it across your upper back.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and brace your core.
Lower yourself slowly over 3 seconds, counting in your head, until your thighs are parallel or below.
Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing.
Rise back up over 3 seconds with controlled effort, then repeat.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: Tire Flip
description: **Channel your inner strongman and flip something that weighs more than your excuses.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, quads, hamstrings, lower back, lats, forearms, shoulders, triceps, chest, calves, and traps. This full-body power movement starts as a deadlift, transitions into a push, and finishes with an explosive press against a heavy tire. It builds raw functional strength and mental toughness. **Pro tip:** Drive with your legs first — trying to lift the tire with your back is a recipe for injury. Get low, push the ground away, then flip. **Biomechanics:** The initial pull loads the posterior chain through hip extension, then transitions to a horizontal push engaging the chest, shoulders, and triceps as the tire passes the tipping point.
instructions: Stand facing a large tire with your feet hip-width apart and toes close to the tire.
Squat down and wedge your fingers under the bottom edge of the tire with a wide grip.
Drive hard through your legs to lift the tire off the ground, keeping your chest up.
As the tire reaches hip height, shift your hands to a pushing position on the tread.
Push the tire forward and over with an explosive shove, then walk to it and repeat.
tips: Brace hard and drive through the hips
Keep the implement close to your body
Explosive hip extension is key — lock out hard at the top

name: Trail Running/Walking
description: **Ditch the treadmill and let uneven terrain train your legs the way nature intended.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves. Running or walking on trails engages more stabilizer muscles than flat surfaces because every step is slightly different. Hills, roots, and rocks force constant micro-adjustments that build ankle stability, balance, and functional leg endurance. **Pro tip:** Shorten your stride on downhills and lean slightly forward on uphills — fighting the terrain wastes energy and stresses your joints. **Biomechanics:** The variable terrain creates unpredictable loading patterns that recruit stabilizers across the ankle, knee, and hip joints while the incline changes shift emphasis between quads (uphill) and hamstrings/calves (downhill).
instructions: Choose a trail appropriate for your fitness level with varied terrain.
Start at a comfortable walking or jogging pace to warm up.
Maintain a steady effort level rather than a fixed speed — let the terrain dictate your pace.
Keep your eyes a few feet ahead to anticipate footing changes.
Cool down with easy walking for the last 5 minutes of your session.
tips: Use this as active recovery or warm-up rather than a primary quad builder
Focus on consistent effort rather than maximum intensity
Stay hydrated and maintain steady breathing throughout

name: Walking Lunge
description: **Cover ground while crushing your quads — every step is a single-leg squat in disguise.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, adductors, and abs. Holding dumbbells while lunging forward step by step creates continuous tension on your legs with no rest between reps. The deep stretch at the bottom of each lunge makes this one of the best movements for quad growth and hip mobility combined. **Pro tip:** Take slightly longer steps to increase the glute stretch, or shorter steps to keep the focus on your quads. **Biomechanics:** The forward stepping pattern loads the front leg's quads eccentrically during descent and concentrically during the drive-up, while the trailing leg's hip flexors get a dynamic stretch at the bottom of each rep.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand tall with feet together.
Step forward with one leg and lower your back knee toward the ground until both knees form roughly 90-degree angles.
Push off your front foot to bring your back leg forward into the next lunge step.
Continue walking forward, alternating legs with each step.
Keep your torso upright and core tight throughout the entire set.
tips: Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
Lower until your rear knee nearly touches the ground for full quad ROM
Keep your torso upright to maintain quad dominance on the front leg
Push through the front foot to drive back up, focusing on quad contraction
coaching_cues: Drop the back knee straight down

name: Walking, Treadmill
description: **The most underrated recovery and fat-loss tool in any gym — just walk.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, and calves. Treadmill walking provides low-impact cardiovascular exercise that's easy on your joints while still burning calories and promoting blood flow for recovery. Adding an incline transforms it from a casual stroll into a serious glute and quad builder. **Pro tip:** Set the incline to 8-12% and keep your hands off the rails for a dramatically harder workout that mimics hill climbing. **Biomechanics:** The consistent belt speed maintains a steady gait cycle, and the machine's flat surface reduces the stabilization demands compared to outdoor walking, making it ideal for recovery sessions.
instructions: Step onto the treadmill and straddle the belt before starting it.
Set your desired speed (typically 2.5 to 4.0 mph) and optional incline.
Begin walking with a natural stride, letting your arms swing freely.
Maintain an upright posture — avoid holding the handrails unless needed for safety.
Walk for your target duration, then gradually reduce speed to cool down.
tips: Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
Adjust resistance to a level that challenges the quads without compromising form
Keep your posture upright and avoid hunching forward

name: Wall Sit
description: **Press your back against the wall and let your quads burn in a battle against the clock.** **Muscles worked:** quads and glutes. This isometric hold keeps your muscles under constant tension without any joint movement, building muscular endurance and mental grit. It's deceptively simple — most people start shaking within 30 seconds. **Pro tip:** Place your feet far enough from the wall that your shins stay vertical — knees drifting over your toes shifts stress to the knee joint. **Biomechanics:** The static hold at roughly 90 degrees of knee flexion creates sustained mid-range tension on the quads, primarily targeting the vastus medialis and rectus femoris through isometric contraction.
instructions: Stand with your back flat against a smooth wall.
Slide down until your thighs are parallel to the floor and your knees are bent at about 90 degrees.
Keep your back pressed firmly against the wall and your feet flat on the ground.
Hold this position for your target time, breathing steadily.
Slide back up the wall to finish when your time is complete.
tips: Position your thighs parallel to the ground for maximum quad isometric demand
Keep your back flat against the wall and your feet shoulder-width apart
Breathe steadily throughout — do not hold your breath
Aim for holds of 30-60 seconds to build quad muscular endurance

name: Weighted Jump Squat
description: **Add a barbell to your jump squat and turn raw power into your superpower.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, lower back, adductors, and abs. This explosive barbell movement combines a squat with a vertical jump, training your muscles to produce maximum force at high speed. It bridges the gap between pure strength training and athletic performance. **Pro tip:** Use only 20-30% of your back squat max — this is about speed and power, not grinding out heavy reps. **Biomechanics:** The rapid stretch-shortening cycle loads the quads and glutes eccentrically during the dip, then demands explosive concentric force through the mid-range to launch both you and the barbell off the ground.
instructions: Set up a barbell with light weight on a squat rack and unrack it across your upper back.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and brace your core.
Dip into a quarter-squat, then explode upward as powerfully as possible, leaving the ground.
Land softly with bent knees, absorbing the impact before immediately dropping into the next rep.
Keep the bar secure on your back throughout the jump and landing.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Weighted Sissy Squat
description: **Lean back, defy gravity, and isolate your quads like nothing else can.** **Muscles worked:** quads, abs, and lower back. This barbell-loaded variation of the sissy squat has you leaning your torso backward while bending your knees, placing extreme stretch on the quads with minimal glute involvement. It's one of the most targeted quad exercises available for advanced lifters. **Pro tip:** Hold onto a rack with one hand for balance if needed — the limiting factor should be your quads, not your ability to stay upright. **Biomechanics:** The backward lean shifts the resistance curve to peak at the stretched position, creating maximum tension on the rectus femoris and vastus muscles when they're at their longest length — ideal for stretch-mediated growth.
instructions: Hold a barbell across your upper back or front-rack position while standing near a squat rack for balance support.
Rise onto the balls of your feet and begin bending your knees forward.
Lean your torso backward as your knees travel forward, lowering until you feel a deep quad stretch.
Reverse the movement by driving your knees back and bringing your torso upright.
Control every inch of the movement — momentum defeats the purpose.
tips: Lean your torso back as your knees travel forward to create extreme quad stretch
Control the descent slowly — this exercise places high stress on the knee joint
Start with bodyweight only until you master the balance and movement pattern
Keep your hips extended throughout — do not sit back into a squat pattern
Use a support for balance if needed, but do not pull yourself up with your arms

name: Weighted Squat
description: **Grab any heavy object and squat — the oldest strength exercise in human history.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs, and adductors. This bodyweight-category squat uses any available external load — a backpack, sandbag, or weighted vest — to add resistance without a barbell. The deep bottom position provides an excellent quad stretch while building foundational lower-body strength. **Pro tip:** Hug the weight close to your chest to keep your center of gravity stable and reduce lower back strain. **Biomechanics:** The ascending strength curve means the hardest part is the bottom of the squat where your quads are most stretched, making this effective for building strength out of the hole.
instructions: Hold a weight close to your chest or wear a weighted vest, standing with feet shoulder-width apart.
Brace your core and begin lowering by bending your knees and pushing your hips back.
Descend until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor, keeping your heels planted.
Drive through your whole foot to stand back up to the starting position.
Repeat for your target number of reps with controlled form.
tips: Push your knees forward over your toes to increase knee flexion range and quad demand
Maintain a braced core throughout to protect the spine under load
Use a tempo of 3-1-1 to maximize mechanical tension on the quads
A narrower stance shifts more demand to the quads versus the adductors

name: Wide Stance Barbell Squat
description: **Widen your feet and unlock a squat that hammers your inner thighs and glutes.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, adductors, calves, lower back, and abs. Taking a wider-than-shoulder stance shifts emphasis to the adductors and glutes while still heavily loading the quads. The wider base also allows many lifters to squat deeper with a more upright torso. **Pro tip:** Point your toes out at roughly 30-45 degrees and make sure your knees track over your toes throughout the movement. **Biomechanics:** The wide stance increases hip abduction and external rotation demands, recruiting the adductors as primary movers alongside the quads, while the more upright torso reduces shear force on the lower back.
instructions: Set up a barbell on a squat rack and unrack it across your upper back.
Take a wide stance — roughly 1.5 times shoulder width — with toes pointed outward.
Brace your core and lower yourself by bending your knees and hips, pushing your knees out over your toes.
Descend until your thighs are parallel or below, keeping your chest up.
Drive through your feet to stand back up and repeat.
tips: Full depth is non-negotiable for maximal quad growth — the bottom position is where the magic happens
Keep your chest up and elbows under the bar to maintain an upright torso
Breathe and brace before each rep to maintain intra-abdominal pressure
Drive your knees out in line with your toes to maintain proper tracking
coaching_cues: Drive up like the floor is a platform

name: Yoke Walk
description: **Strap a crushing load across your back and walk — total-body strength in its purest form.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs, lower back, and traps. The yoke walk loads your entire body under heavy weight while demanding you move forward with control. It builds core stability, leg strength, and mental fortitude simultaneously, making it a staple in strongman training. **Pro tip:** Take short, quick steps rather than long strides — shorter steps keep the yoke stable and prevent it from swaying. **Biomechanics:** The axial loading compresses the spine and demands constant isometric contraction from the core and erectors, while each step requires the quads and glutes to produce force through a limited range of motion under extreme load.
instructions: Position yourself under the yoke bar so it sits across your upper back and traps.
Stand up with the weight by driving through your legs, bracing your entire core.
Take short, controlled steps forward, keeping the yoke as stable as possible.
Walk the prescribed distance, focusing on staying tight and upright.
Carefully lower the yoke back to the ground at the finish.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
Keep your torso at a forward lean angle appropriate for the implement
Start with lighter loads and focus on maintaining consistent stride length
Use this as a finisher or conditioning tool after primary quad work

name: Zercher Squat
description: **Cradle the barbell in your elbows and discover the most brutally honest squat variation.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, biceps, lower back, and adductors. Holding the bar in the crook of your elbows forces an incredibly upright torso and punishes any core weakness immediately. This front-loaded position creates massive quad and ab engagement while building real-world carrying strength. **Pro tip:** Wrap a towel or pad around the bar where it contacts your elbows — the discomfort shouldn't be the limiting factor. **Biomechanics:** The anterior bar position shifts the center of mass forward, requiring greater quad contribution to maintain an upright torso, while the elbow cradle creates a significant isometric demand on the biceps and anterior core.
instructions: Set a barbell in a squat rack at about waist height.
Scoop your arms under the bar so it rests in the crook of your elbows, clasp your hands together.
Lift the bar off the rack and step back into a shoulder-width stance.
Squat down by bending your knees and hips, keeping your torso as upright as possible.
Drive through your feet to stand back up, squeezing the bar tight in your elbows throughout.
tips: Aim for a knee angle below 90 degrees at the bottom to fully load the quads at length
Use lifting shoes with a raised heel to allow deeper knee flexion
Pause briefly at the bottom to eliminate the stretch reflex and increase quad demand
Avoid excessive forward lean which shifts load from quads to hip extensors
coaching_cues: Screw your feet into the floor
Stand up fast, control down slow

name: Zercher Squats
description: **Hold the bar where it hurts and build the kind of core strength machines can't replicate.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, abs, biceps, lower back, and adductors. This barbell squat variation with the bar cradled in your elbows is a test of total-body toughness. The front-loaded position demands extreme core bracing and rewards you with deep quad engagement and functional strength. **Pro tip:** Experiment with a slightly wider stance to allow your elbows to pass between your knees at the bottom — this unlocks greater depth. **Biomechanics:** The low anterior load creates a long moment arm the core must resist, while the deep squat stretches the quads and adductors under load for stretch-mediated growth.
instructions: Position a barbell at waist height in a squat rack.
Slide your arms under the bar and lock it into the crook of your elbows, interlacing your fingers.
Unrack the bar and step back with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Lower into a deep squat, letting your elbows pass inside your knees if possible.
Push through your whole foot to stand, keeping the bar secure and your chest tall.
Repeat for your target reps, re-bracing your core at the top of each rep.
tips: Descend to full depth where your hip crease passes below the knee — this maximizes quad stretch
Keep your torso as upright as possible to maintain quad dominance over hip extensors
Drive through the full foot, not just the heels, to maintain balance at depth
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension at long muscle lengths
Avoid cutting depth short — partial squats significantly reduce quad hypertrophy stimulus

name: 45 Degree Back Extension
description: **The single best exercise for bulletproofing your lower back without heavy spinal loading.** **Muscles worked:** lower back (erectors), glutes, and hamstrings. Using a 45-degree hyperextension bench, you hinge at the hips against gravity to strengthen the muscles that protect your spine. It's both a warm-up essential and a serious strength builder depending on how you load it. **Pro tip:** Pause for a full second at the top of each rep and squeeze your glutes — this turns a simple back exercise into a posterior chain powerhouse. **Biomechanics:** The 45-degree angle creates a bell-shaped resistance curve where tension peaks at the mid-range of hip extension, effectively loading the erectors and glutes while reducing the shear forces that fully horizontal back extensions can create.
instructions: Set up on a 45-degree back extension bench with your hips just past the top of the pad.
Cross your arms over your chest or place your hands behind your head.
Lower your torso toward the ground by hinging at the hips, keeping your back straight.
Raise your torso back up by squeezing your glutes and lower back until your body forms a straight line.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower again with control.
tips: Pad should sit at hip crease level
Cross arms over chest or hold a plate
Come to neutral at the top — don't hyperextend
Allow slight rounding at the bottom for full erector stretch

name: 90/90 Hamstring
description:
**A supine hamstring stretch and activation drill performed with the hip and knee each bent at 90 degrees.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

The working leg extends toward the ceiling while the opposite leg remains anchored, creating a controlled lengthening stimulus through the posterior thigh with virtually no systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Keep your back tall, don't slouch
instructions: Lie face-up with both hips and knees bent to 90 degrees, feet off the floor.
Slowly straighten one leg toward the ceiling until you feel a deep stretch in the hamstring.
Hold the end position for 2–3 seconds, actively contracting the hamstring against the stretch.
Return to the start and repeat for the prescribed reps before switching legs.
Keep your lower back pressed into the floor throughout.
tips: Keep your back tall, don't slouch
Breathe deeply into the stretch
Hold each position for 30-60 seconds

name: Alternating Hang Clean
description:
**An explosive kettlebell clean performed by alternating hands each rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, biceps, calves.

The movement demands rapid hip extension and full-body coordination as you catch and re-hinge the bell, making it primarily a power and conditioning tool rather than a hypertrophy exercise.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, a kettlebell on the floor between your feet.
Hinge at the hips, grasp the bell with one hand, and explosively drive your hips forward to clean it to the rack position at your shoulder.
Lower the bell back through the hinge and, as it passes between your legs, switch hands at the bottom and clean with the opposite arm.
Maintain a flat back during every hinge and absorb the catch softly at the shoulder.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Ball Leg Curl
description:
**A stability-ball hamstring curl that challenges knee flexion strength while demanding core and hip stabilization.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, calves.

The unstable surface increases muscle fiber activation in the hamstrings and glutes, though the limited loading ceiling keeps its hypertrophy stimulus moderate.

**Pro tip:** Keep hips elevated throughout — don't let them sag between reps
instructions: Lie on your back with your heels placed on top of a stability ball, legs straight, and hips lifted off the ground into a bridge.
Dig your heels into the ball and curl it toward your glutes by bending your knees, keeping your hips elevated throughout.
Pause briefly when your knees reach roughly 90 degrees, then slowly extend your legs back to the start.
If the ball wobbles, narrow your foot placement slightly and brace your core harder.
tips: Keep hips elevated throughout — don't let them sag between reps
Dig heels into the ball
Control the eccentric — don't let the ball roll away from you

name: Band Good Morning
description:
**A hip-hinge movement using a resistance band draped over the neck and anchored under the feet.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors.

The ascending resistance profile loads the hamstrings progressively through the range, with peak tension at lockout, while the deep stretch at the bottom offers strong lengthened-position stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Push hips back, not down
instructions: Stand on the center of a looped resistance band with feet hip-width apart and loop the other end behind your neck across your traps.
With a slight bend in the knees, push your hips back and hinge forward until your torso is near parallel to the floor, feeling a deep hamstring stretch.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Keep your spine neutral and chest proud throughout the entire movement.
tips: Push hips back, not down
Band tension should be moderate — too heavy pulls you forward
Keep knees slightly bent

name: Band Good Morning (Pull Through)
description:
**A band-resisted hip hinge where the band is anchored low behind you and pulled through the legs, mimicking a cable pull-through.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: erectors, glutes.

This setup reduces spinal loading compared to a traditional good morning while still delivering a powerful stretch on the hamstrings and glutes at the bottom of each rep.

**Pro tip:** Step far enough from the anchor to maintain tension at the top
instructions: Anchor a looped band to a low, sturdy point behind you.
Step forward, straddling the band so it passes between your legs, and hold the end at hip height with both hands.
Walk out until there is moderate tension.
Hinge at the hips, letting the band pull your hands back between your thighs, until you feel a strong hamstring stretch.
Explosively extend your hips to stand tall, driving through your glutes.
Keep your arms relaxed—the hips do all the work.
tips: Step far enough from the anchor to maintain tension at the top
Hinge deep — the band should pull you back
Squeeze glutes hard at lockout

name: Banded Good Morning
description:
**A resistance-band good morning that provides accommodating resistance—light at the bottom where the hamstrings are most vulnerable and heaviest at lockout.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors.

The band tension encourages an aggressive hip drive while the deep hinge delivers a significant stretch-mediated stimulus to the posterior chain.

**Pro tip:** Loop band securely under both feet
instructions: Loop a resistance band under both feet and behind your neck, standing with feet roughly hip-width apart.
Soften your knees slightly and push your hips back, lowering your torso toward the floor while keeping your back flat.
Descend until you feel a strong pull through your hamstrings, then reverse the motion by driving your hips forward forcefully.
Squeeze your glutes at the top and avoid hyperextending your lower back.
tips: Loop band securely under both feet
Push hips back to initiate
Don't round the lower back at the bottom

name: Banded Leg Curl
description:
**A prone or standing leg curl using a resistance band anchored in front of you.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

The band's ascending resistance makes the contracted position the most challenging portion, providing a strong peak-contraction stimulus to the hamstrings with minimal joint stress and easy setup.

**Pro tip:** Anchor the band securely at a low point
instructions: Anchor a band to a low fixed point.
Lie face-down or stand facing the anchor and loop the band around one ankle.
Starting with the leg extended toward the anchor, curl your heel toward your glute by bending the knee against the band's resistance.
Squeeze hard at the top for a one-second count, then control the eccentric back to full extension.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
Keep your hips pressed down if lying prone.
tips: Anchor the band securely at a low point
Control the eccentric — don't let the band snap your leg straight
Squeeze hard at peak contraction

name: Barbell Stiff Leg Deadlift
description:
**A barbell hip-hinge performed with minimal knee bend, maximizing the stretch placed on the hamstrings throughout the descent.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms, traps.

Heavy loads can be used, but the significant spinal erector and grip demands mean systemic fatigue is considerable relative to the hamstring stimulus alone.

**Pro tip:** Keep the bar close to your legs throughout
instructions: Stand holding a barbell at hip height with an overhand grip, feet hip-width apart.
Keeping your legs nearly straight with only a very slight knee bend, push your hips back and lower the bar along your shins by hinging at the hips.
Descend until you feel a maximal hamstring stretch—typically when the bar reaches mid-shin or just below the knees.
Reverse the movement by driving your hips forward, returning to a tall standing position.
Maintain a neutral spine and keep the bar close to your body at all times.
tips: Keep the bar close to your legs throughout
Push hips back — the bar path should be nearly vertical
Stop when you feel a deep hamstring stretch, not when the bar touches the floor
Use straps if grip limits your hamstring training

name: Box Jump (Multiple Response)
description:
**A plyometric drill where you perform consecutive box jumps with minimal ground contact time between reps.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The focus is on reactive power and rate of force development rather than muscle growth, making it a sport-performance tool with limited direct hypertrophy value.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Stand facing a plyo box at an appropriate height.
Drop into a quick quarter-squat and immediately explode upward, landing softly on top of the box with both feet.
Step or hop back down and, upon landing, immediately reload and jump again with minimal pause.
Focus on fast ground contact and full hip extension on each jump.
Perform the prescribed number of consecutive reps, prioritizing speed and landing mechanics over height.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Box Skip
description:
**A dynamic plyometric skipping movement performed onto or over a box, emphasizing single-leg power, coordination, and elastic energy return.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

It develops athletic qualities like sprint mechanics and reactive strength but contributes minimally to muscle hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Approach a low plyo box with a skipping rhythm.
Drive one knee up explosively while pushing off the opposite foot to skip onto or over the box.
Land softly on the ball of your foot, absorb the impact through your hips and knees, and immediately transition into the next skip.
Alternate legs with each rep.
Keep your arms pumping in opposition to your legs to maintain balance and momentum.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Cable Pull-Through
description:
**A cable-loaded hip hinge performed facing away from a low pulley, pulling the handle through the legs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors.

The cable's line of pull keeps constant tension on the glutes and hamstrings, with peak loading at the lockout where the glutes are fully shortened—making it an excellent glute-focused finisher with low spinal stress.

**Pro tip:** Hinge at the hips, not the lower back — maintain a neutral spine
instructions: Attach a rope handle to a low cable pulley.
Face away from the machine, reach between your legs, and grab the rope with both hands.
Walk forward a few steps to create tension.
With feet slightly wider than hip-width, hinge at the hips and let the cable pull your hands back between your thighs.
When you feel a stretch in your hamstrings, powerfully thrust your hips forward to a tall standing position, squeezing your glutes hard at lockout.
Your arms stay straight throughout—they are just hooks connecting you to the cable.
tips: Hinge at the hips, not the lower back — maintain a neutral spine
Squeeze the glutes hard at the top to lock out
Keep the cable between your legs and stand far enough from the stack
Soft knee bend — this is a hip hinge, not a squat

name: Chair Leg Extended Stretch
description:
