**A seated hamstring stretch performed by extending one leg onto a chair or elevated surface and leaning the torso forward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This is a pure flexibility and recovery drill that places the hamstrings under a prolonged passive stretch with zero fatigue cost, ideal for warm-ups or cooldowns.

**Pro tip:** Keep the extended leg straight
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench or chair and extend one leg straight out onto another chair or surface at roughly the same height.
Keep the extended leg's knee straight and toes pointed up.
Slowly lean your torso forward from the hips, reaching toward your toes until you feel a comfortable but deep stretch along the back of the thigh.
Hold for 20–30 seconds, breathing steadily.
Switch legs and repeat.
Do not round your lower back—hinge from the hips.
tips: Keep the extended leg straight
Lean forward from the hips, not the upper back
Hold for 30-60 seconds per side

name: Clean
description:
**The full barbell clean is an Olympic weightlifting movement that takes the bar from the floor to the front-rack position in one explosive sequence.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, calves, lats.

It recruits nearly every muscle in the body and develops tremendous power output, but its technical demands and high fatigue cost make it a poor choice for targeted hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Set up over the barbell with feet hip-width apart, hips back, chest up, and hands gripping the bar just outside your knees.
Push through the floor to lift the bar past your knees, then explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles in a powerful triple extension.
As the bar reaches its peak height, pull yourself under it by dropping into a front squat and catching the bar on your front delts with elbows high.
Stand up out of the squat to complete the lift.
Reset and repeat.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Clean Deadlift
description:
**A deadlift variation performed with clean-grip positioning and an upright torso angle, designed to strengthen the pull phase of the clean.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, forearms, lats.

It emphasizes leg drive off the floor and upper-back positioning more than a conventional deadlift, but its primary purpose is Olympic lift assistance rather than hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Position your feet hip-width apart under the barbell with a clean-width grip just outside the knees.
Set your hips slightly lower than a conventional deadlift, chest tall, and lats engaged.
Push the floor away to break the bar from the ground, keeping the bar close to your shins.
As the bar passes the knees, maintain your back angle momentarily before extending the hips and knees together to reach full lockout.
Lower the bar under control to the floor and reset your position for each rep.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Deficit Romanian Deadlift
description:
**A Romanian deadlift performed while standing on a raised platform, increasing the range of motion at the bottom and amplifying the stretch placed on the hamstrings beyond what a standard RDL provides.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms, traps.

This extended range makes it one of the most potent exercises for stretch-mediated hamstring hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Use a 2-4 inch deficit — more is not always better
instructions: Stand on a 2–4 inch platform or bumper plate holding a barbell at hip height with an overhand grip.
With feet hip-width apart and knees slightly bent, push your hips back and lower the bar along your legs.
The deficit allows the bar to travel past your feet, deepening the hamstring stretch.
Descend until you reach your maximum controllable stretch without rounding your back, then drive your hips forward to return to standing.
Keep the bar in contact with your legs throughout.
tips: Use a 2-4 inch deficit — more is not always better
Maintain a neutral spine even at the deepest point
The extra range should come from hip flexion, not lumbar flexion
Use moderate loads — the extended ROM increases injury risk

name: Double Kettlebell Alternating Hang Clean
description:
**A double-kettlebell clean from the hang position where you alternate which bell you clean each rep while the other remains in the hang.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, biceps, calves.

This variation adds an asymmetric loading challenge and demands exceptional hip power and grip endurance, serving primarily as a conditioning and power-development tool.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand holding two kettlebells at arm's length in front of your thighs.
Hinge at the hips to swing both bells back between your legs, then explosively extend your hips.
As you drive up, clean one kettlebell to the rack position at your shoulder while the other returns to the hang.
On the next rep, clean the opposite bell.
Alternate each rep, keeping your core braced and your hinge crisp.
Absorb each catch smoothly to protect your wrists and shoulders.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Dumbbell Good Morning
description:
**A hip-hinge good morning performed holding a dumbbell at the chest or behind the head.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors.

The dumbbell limits the total load compared to a barbell version, making it a more accessible option that still delivers a deep hamstring stretch and solid posterior chain activation with moderate systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Hold dumbbells at shoulders for more erector demand, or at sides for less
instructions: Hold a dumbbell vertically against your upper chest with both hands cupped under the top bell.
Stand with feet hip-width apart and a slight knee bend.
Push your hips back and hinge your torso forward, keeping the dumbbell tight to your chest.
Lower until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor or until you feel a strong hamstring stretch.
Reverse by driving your hips forward and squeezing your glutes at the top.
Maintain a neutral spine throughout.
tips: Hold dumbbells at shoulders for more erector demand, or at sides for less
Push hips back — this is a hinge, not a squat
Keep knees slightly bent

name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
description:
**A Romanian deadlift performed with dumbbells held at the sides, allowing a slightly greater range of motion and more natural hand positioning than the barbell version.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms, traps.

The dumbbells travel alongside the legs rather than in front, which can reduce lower-back stress while still delivering a potent hamstring stretch.

**Pro tip:** Let the dumbbells travel close to your legs
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing your thighs.
With feet hip-width apart and knees slightly bent, push your hips back and slide the dumbbells down along the outside of your legs.
Descend until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings—typically around mid-shin level.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing your glutes at lockout.
Keep your shoulder blades retracted and the dumbbells close to your body.
tips: Let the dumbbells travel close to your legs
Push hips back until you feel a deep hamstring stretch
Keep shoulder blades retracted
Dumbbells allow a neutral grip which is easier on the shoulders

name: Eccentric Nordic Curl
description:
**The eccentric-only phase of a Nordic hamstring curl, where you slowly lower your body from a kneeling position toward the floor using only hamstring resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This exercise produces extreme mechanical tension at long muscle lengths and is one of the most effective movements for hamstring injury prevention and stretch-position hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Use your hands to catch yourself at the bottom and push back up
instructions: Kneel on a pad with your ankles secured under a sturdy object or held by a partner.
Begin upright with your torso tall and arms ready in front of you.
Keeping your hips extended and body in a straight line from knees to head, slowly lower yourself toward the floor by extending at the knees.
Resist gravity with your hamstrings for as long as possible—aim for a 3–5 second descent.
Catch yourself with your hands at the bottom and push back up to the start to reset.
Do not bend at the hips during the lowering phase.
tips: Use your hands to catch yourself at the bottom and push back up
Aim for a 3-5 second lowering phase
Keep hips extended throughout — don't bend at the waist
Start with 3-4 reps — the DOMS will be severe initially

name: Floor Glute-Ham Raise
description:
**A floor-based glute-ham raise performed without a GHD machine, using a partner or anchor to hold the ankles.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, calves, erectors.

It combines knee flexion and hip extension against bodyweight, creating intense hamstring loading especially in the lengthened position. The descending strength curve means the hardest part is near full extension.

**Pro tip:** Anchor your feet securely under something heavy
instructions: Kneel on a padded surface with your ankles pinned down by a partner or heavy object.
Start with your torso upright and hips fully extended.
Lower your body toward the floor by straightening your knees, resisting with your hamstrings as long as possible.
Once you can no longer hold, catch yourself lightly with your hands.
Use a small push from your hands combined with a powerful hamstring contraction and hip extension to pull yourself back to the upright kneeling position.
tips: Anchor your feet securely under something heavy
Keep hips extended — don't pike at the waist
Use your hands to assist on the concentric if needed

name: Front Box Jump
description:
**A forward-facing plyometric box jump that develops explosive lower-body power and vertical force production.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

The movement trains the stretch-shortening cycle of the quads, glutes, and calves but generates minimal hypertrophy stimulus due to the brief time under tension and bodyweight-only loading.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Stand facing a plyo box at a comfortable distance with feet shoulder-width apart.
Drop into a quick countermovement by bending your hips and knees while swinging your arms back.
Explosively jump upward and forward, driving your arms overhead, and land softly on top of the box with both feet flat.
Stand fully upright on the box, then step back down carefully.
Reset your stance and repeat for the prescribed reps.
Focus on a soft, controlled landing each time.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Front Leg Raises
description:
**A standing or hanging leg raise performed to the front, targeting the lower abdominals and hip flexors.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: quads.

The bell-shaped resistance profile means the movement is hardest in the mid-range where the lever arm is longest, providing a solid core training stimulus with moderate fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout
instructions: Stand upright holding onto a support for balance, or hang from a pull-up bar.
Keeping one or both legs straight, raise them directly in front of you by flexing at the hips.
Lift until your legs are parallel to the floor or as high as your mobility allows without rounding your lower back.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower under control back to the starting position.
Avoid swinging or using momentum—each rep should be deliberate and controlled.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Glute Ham Raise
description:
**A GHD-machine exercise that combines knee flexion and hip extension to train the hamstrings through a full range of motion under bodyweight.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, calves, erectors.

The descending strength curve makes the lengthened position extremely challenging, delivering potent growth from the stretched position stimulus with a favorable fatigue profile.

**Pro tip:** Keep hips extended throughout — don't pike
instructions: Set up on a glute-ham developer with your knees on or just behind the pad and your feet secured against the footplate.
Begin with your torso hanging perpendicular to the floor.
Simultaneously extend your hips and flex your knees to pull your body upward until your torso is parallel to the floor or slightly above.
Squeeze your hamstrings and glutes hard at the top, then slowly lower yourself back to the starting position under control.
Keep your core braced and avoid excessive lumbar extension.
tips: Keep hips extended throughout — don't pike
Control the eccentric for at least 2-3 seconds
Push toes into the plate to engage hamstrings more
Add load with a plate on the chest once bodyweight is easy

name: Good Morning
description:
**A barbell hip-hinge performed with the bar on the upper back, bending forward at the hips while maintaining a neutral spine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, abs.

The good morning places the hamstrings under a deep stretch at the bottom and demands significant erector and core stabilization, making it an effective posterior chain builder with moderate systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Push hips back, don't just bend forward
instructions: Position a barbell across your upper traps as you would for a back squat.
Stand with feet hip-to-shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in your knees.
Brace your core, then push your hips back and hinge forward, lowering your torso until it is roughly parallel to the floor or until you feel a strong hamstring stretch.
Drive your hips forward to return to the upright position, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Keep the bar stable on your back and your spine neutral throughout.
tips: Push hips back, don't just bend forward
Keep the bar on your upper traps, not your neck
Stop when you feel a strong hamstring stretch
This exercise has high spinal loading — use moderate weights

name: Good Morning off Pins
description:
**A good morning variation performed from a dead stop with the barbell resting on safety pins set at the bottom of the range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, abs.

Starting from the stretched position eliminates the stretch-shortening cycle, forcing the hamstrings, glutes, and erectors to generate force concentrically without any elastic rebound—building raw strength out of the hole.

**Pro tip:** Set pins at your typical bottom position
instructions: Set the safety pins in a power rack at a height that corresponds to your torso being roughly parallel to the floor.
Position yourself under the bar on the pins with it resting across your upper traps, feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and hips pushed back.
From this dead-stop position, brace hard and drive your hips forward to lift the bar off the pins, standing up to full extension.
Squeeze your glutes at the top, then slowly hinge back down and let the bar settle completely on the pins before the next rep.
Reset your brace each rep.
tips: Set pins at your typical bottom position
Reset completely between reps — no bouncing
Brace hard before each rep
Use moderate loads — no eccentric pre-load means you're weaker

name: Hamstring Stretch
description:
**A static flexibility drill that lengthens the hamstring muscle group by placing the posterior thigh under a sustained passive stretch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

Ideal as a warm-up primer or cool-down recovery tool to restore resting muscle length and reduce post-training stiffness.

**Pro tip:** Hold for 30-60 seconds
instructions: Stand upright and extend one leg forward, placing the heel on a low surface or the ground with the toes pointed toward the ceiling.
Keep the extended leg completely straight and hinge at the hips, lowering your torso toward the outstretched leg.
Maintain a flat back throughout the movement—avoid rounding the spine.
Hold the deepest comfortable position for 20–30 seconds, breathing steadily.
You should feel a deep pull along the back of the thigh and behind the knee.
Release slowly and repeat on the opposite leg.
tips: Hold for 30-60 seconds
Breathe deeply and relax into the stretch
Don't bounce — static holds only

name: Hamstring-SMR
description:
**A self-muscle and tissue release technique that uses a foam roller or lacrosse ball to apply direct pressure to the hamstrings, breaking up adhesions and improving tissue quality.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: none.

This recovery-focused drill enhances blood flow and can restore range of motion between training sessions.

**Pro tip:** Roll slowly — spend 30-60 seconds per leg
instructions: Sit on the floor and place a foam roller beneath one thigh, just above the back of the knee.
Support your bodyweight with your hands positioned behind you on the ground.
Slowly roll the device along the length of the hamstring from just above the knee to just below the glute.
When you encounter a tender or knotted area, pause and apply sustained pressure for 15–30 seconds.
Rotate the leg slightly inward and outward to address the medial and lateral portions of the hamstring.
Continue for 60–90 seconds per leg, then switch sides.
tips: Roll slowly — spend 30-60 seconds per leg
Pause on tender spots for 10-15 seconds
Don't roll aggressively on injured tissue

name: Hang Snatch - Below Knees
description:
**An Olympic weightlifting variation that begins from a dead-hang position just below the knees, demanding explosive hip extension and precise barbell coordination to receive the load overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, calves, abs.

This full-body power exercise develops rate of force development and athletic explosiveness across the entire posterior and anterior chain.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, gripping the barbell with a wide snatch grip.
Hinge at the hips and lower the bar to just below knee height, keeping the back flat and shoulders over the bar.
Explosively drive through the floor, extending the hips and knees simultaneously while shrugging the bar upward.
As the bar reaches its peak height, pull yourself underneath it and lock the arms overhead in a full squat or power position.
Stabilize the bar overhead with active shoulders before standing to full extension.
Lower the bar under control back to the hang position for the next rep.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Hanging Bar Good Morning
description:
**A barbell hip-hinge movement performed with the bar resting across the upper back, emphasizing a deep stretch of the hamstrings under load.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, abs.

The hanging start position increases time under tension in the lengthened range, making it a potent stimulus for hamstring hypertrophy and posterior chain strength.

**Pro tip:** Keep the bar close to your body
instructions: Set up in a power rack with the barbell across your upper traps, as you would for a back squat.
Unrack the bar and stand with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent.
Push the hips back and hinge forward at the waist, lowering your torso toward parallel with the floor.
Keep the barbell tight against your back and maintain a neutral spine throughout the descent.
Descend until you feel a strong stretch in the hamstrings, pausing briefly at the bottom.
Drive the hips forward to return to the upright position, squeezing the glutes at the top.
tips: Keep the bar close to your body
Push hips back — same hinge pattern as a standard good morning
Use straps if grip is a limiter

name: Hurdle Hops
description:
**A plyometric drill that involves consecutive explosive jumps over a series of hurdles, training reactive power and elastic energy utilization in the lower body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

Primarily used for athletic performance development rather than muscle building, this exercise sharpens ground-contact efficiency and vertical force production.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Set up a line of low hurdles spaced evenly apart, roughly hip-width in height.
Stand facing the first hurdle with feet shoulder-width apart and arms at your sides.
Dip into a quarter squat and explosively jump upward and forward, clearing the hurdle.
Land softly on the balls of both feet, immediately loading for the next jump with minimal ground contact time.
Swing the arms aggressively to assist each takeoff.
Continue through all hurdles, focusing on quick, reactive contacts rather than maximum height.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Inchworm
description:
**A dynamic mobility exercise that combines a standing forward fold with a hand walk-out to a plank position, actively lengthening the hamstrings while engaging the core and upper body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: abs, shoulders, chest.

Excellent as a full-body warm-up movement that raises core temperature and improves functional flexibility.

**Pro tip:** Keep legs as straight as possible during the walk-back
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart.
Hinge at the hips and reach your hands toward the floor, keeping the legs as straight as possible.
Walk your hands forward along the ground until you reach a full plank position with shoulders stacked over wrists.
Pause briefly in the plank, bracing the core.
Walk your feet forward toward your hands in small steps, keeping the legs straight to maximize the hamstring stretch.
Return to standing and repeat for the prescribed number of reps.
tips: Keep legs as straight as possible during the walk-back
Move slowly and deliberately
Great as part of a warm-up

name: Intermediate Groin Stretch
description:
**A static flexibility exercise targeting the inner thigh musculature by placing the adductors in a sustained lengthened position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: adductors. Secondary: hamstrings.

This stretch improves hip mobility and reduces tightness that can limit squat depth and lateral movement patterns.

**Pro tip:** Ease into the stretch gradually
instructions: Sit on the floor with the soles of your feet together and knees falling outward in a butterfly position.
Hold your ankles with both hands and sit up tall through the spine.
Gently press the knees toward the floor using your elbows or light hand pressure.
Lean the torso slightly forward from the hips while maintaining a flat back to deepen the stretch.
Hold the position for 20–30 seconds, breathing deeply and relaxing into the stretch with each exhale.
Release slowly and repeat for 2–3 rounds.
tips: Ease into the stretch gradually
Hold 30-60 seconds
Don't bounce

name: Kettlebell Dead Clean
description:
**A kettlebell power movement initiated from a dead stop on the floor, requiring the lifter to generate force from zero momentum to clean the bell into the rack position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, calves.

This variation eliminates the stretch-shortening cycle, building raw starting strength and explosive hip drive.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Place the kettlebell on the floor between your feet, standing with a shoulder-width stance.
Hinge at the hips, bend the knees slightly, and grip the kettlebell handle with one hand.
Brace the core, flatten the back, and explosively extend the hips and knees to lift the bell off the ground.
As the kettlebell rises, keep it close to the body and rotate the wrist so the bell rolls smoothly into the rack position at shoulder height.
The forearm should be vertical and the bell resting against the outer forearm and bicep.
Return the kettlebell to the floor under control, resetting completely before the next rep.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Kettlebell Hang Clean
description:
**A kettlebell clean variation starting from a hang position between the thighs, using a powerful hip snap to propel the bell into the front rack.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, calves, quads.

The hang start allows use of the stretch reflex, making it slightly more rhythmic than the dead clean while still demanding explosive posterior chain engagement.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in one hand at arm's length.
Hike the kettlebell back between the legs by hinging at the hips, loading the hamstrings and glutes.
Explosively snap the hips forward, driving the kettlebell upward.
As the bell reaches chest height, pull the elbow back and rotate the wrist to guide the kettlebell into the rack position.
Absorb the bell softly against the forearm and chest without letting it crash or flip over the hand.
Hike the bell back between the legs to begin the next repetition.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Kettlebell One-Legged Deadlift
description:
**A unilateral hip-hinge exercise performed on one leg while holding a kettlebell, delivering a deep hamstring stretch under load combined with significant balance and stabilization demands.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms.

This movement addresses side-to-side imbalances and builds single-leg posterior chain strength with high growth from the stretched position potential.

**Pro tip:** Let the free leg extend behind you as a counterbalance
instructions: Stand on one leg holding a kettlebell in the opposite hand, with a slight bend in the standing knee.
Hinge at the hip, sending the free leg straight behind you as a counterbalance while lowering the kettlebell toward the floor.
Keep the hips square to the ground—avoid opening or rotating the pelvis.
Lower until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor or until you feel a strong hamstring stretch on the working leg.
Drive through the standing heel and squeeze the glute to return to the upright position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching legs.
tips: Let the free leg extend behind you as a counterbalance
Keep hips square — don't rotate open
The kettlebell should travel straight down
Use a lighter weight than you think — balance is the limiter initially

name: Kettlebell Romanian Deadlift
description:
**A bilateral hip-hinge movement using one or two kettlebells that emphasizes the eccentric stretch of the hamstrings through a controlled descent.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms.

The kettlebell's center of mass sits below the handle, subtly altering the loading mechanics compared to a barbell and providing a strong stimulus for hamstring lengthening and growth.

**Pro tip:** Push hips back until you feel a deep hamstring stretch
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a kettlebell in each hand (or one with both hands) in front of the thighs.
Unlock the knees slightly and push the hips back, lowering the kettlebells along the front of the legs.
Keep the back flat and chest proud as you descend, feeling a progressive stretch through the hamstrings.
Lower until the kettlebells reach mid-shin level or until your hamstring flexibility limits further range.
Reverse the movement by driving the hips forward and contracting the glutes to stand tall.
Maintain a controlled tempo throughout—avoid bouncing at the bottom.
tips: Push hips back until you feel a deep hamstring stretch
Keep kettlebells close to your legs
Slight knee bend — this is not a squat

name: Kettlebell Swing
description:
**A ballistic hip-hinge exercise where a kettlebell is swung between the legs and propelled forward to shoulder height through an explosive hip snap.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, shoulders, abs, forearms.

This foundational kettlebell movement develops posterior chain power, cardiovascular conditioning, and grip endurance in a single efficient drill.

**Pro tip:** Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
instructions: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, kettlebell on the floor about a foot in front of you.
Hinge at the hips, grip the kettlebell with both hands, and hike it back between the legs.
Explosively extend the hips and knees, projecting the kettlebell forward and upward.
At the top of the swing, the arms should be roughly parallel to the floor, with the glutes fully contracted and the body forming a straight line.
Allow the kettlebell to fall back under gravity, guiding it between the legs by hinging at the hips.
Immediately transition into the next rep, maintaining a rhythmic breathing pattern—exhale on the drive, inhale on the backswing.
tips: Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
Hike the bell back between your legs like a football snap
Stand tall and squeeze the glutes hard at the top
Don't squat the swing — it's a hinge pattern

name: Kettlebell Swing Hamstring
description:
**A kettlebell swing variation with an exaggerated hip hinge to shift greater emphasis onto the hamstrings during the eccentric backswing phase.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, abs, forearms.

By sitting deeper into the hinge and maintaining straighter legs, this version increases hamstring loading while preserving the explosive hip-drive mechanics of the standard swing.

**Pro tip:** Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
instructions: Set up as you would for a standard kettlebell swing with feet shoulder-width apart.
Hike the kettlebell back between the legs, but allow a deeper hip hinge with less knee bend than a conventional swing.
You should feel a pronounced stretch in the hamstrings at the bottom of each backswing.
Snap the hips forward explosively to drive the kettlebell to chest or shoulder height.
Keep the arms relaxed—they are simply levers; all force comes from the hip extension.
Focus on the hamstring stretch-to-contraction cycle on every rep, maintaining a flat back throughout.
tips: Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
Hike the bell back between your legs like a football snap
Stand tall and squeeze the glutes hard at the top
Don't squat the swing — it's a hinge pattern

name: Knee Tuck Jump
description:
**A high-intensity plyometric exercise where you jump vertically and tuck the knees toward the chest at the peak of each rep.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs.

This explosive movement develops lower-body power, reactive strength, and hip flexor speed, making it a staple in athletic performance and conditioning programs.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, arms at your sides.
Dip into a quarter squat and swing the arms back to load the jump.
Explode upward, driving through the balls of the feet while swinging the arms overhead.
At the peak of the jump, pull both knees up toward the chest as high as possible.
Release the knees and extend the legs to prepare for a soft landing on the balls of the feet.
Absorb the landing by bending the knees, reset quickly, and repeat for the prescribed reps.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Leg-Up Hamstring Stretch
description:
**A supine hamstring flexibility exercise where one leg is elevated and held in a straightened position to passively lengthen the posterior thigh.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This stretch is gentle on the lower back since the spine remains supported on the floor, making it suitable for all fitness levels.

**Pro tip:** Keep the elevated leg straight
instructions: Lie flat on your back with both legs extended on the floor.
Raise one leg toward the ceiling, keeping it as straight as possible.
Grasp the back of the thigh, calf, or use a strap around the foot to gently pull the leg closer to your torso.
Keep the opposite leg flat on the ground and the hips level.
Hold the stretch for 20–30 seconds, feeling a deep pull along the back of the raised leg.
Lower the leg slowly and repeat on the other side.
tips: Keep the elevated leg straight
Lean forward from the hips
Hold 30-60 seconds per side

name: Linear 3-Part Start Technique
description:
**A sprint mechanics drill that breaks the acceleration phase into three distinct body positions—set, drive, and transition—to develop optimal force application angles during the initial steps of a sprint. This technique drill prioritizes nerve-to-muscle patterning over raw strength development.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.



**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Begin in a staggered stance with one foot forward and the opposite arm forward, torso leaning at approximately 45 degrees.
On the start signal, drive the back leg forward with a powerful knee punch while pushing off the front foot.
Maintain a forward lean during the first 2–3 steps, keeping the shin angles aggressive.
Gradually transition to a more upright posture over the next 3–5 steps as speed builds.
Focus on driving the feet behind the center of mass and maintaining full arm action.
Decelerate after 10–15 meters, walk back, and repeat.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Linear Acceleration Wall Drill
description:
**A wall-supported sprint drill that teaches proper acceleration mechanics by isolating the piston-like leg drive pattern against a fixed surface.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

By removing the need to actually accelerate, athletes can focus purely on knee drive angles, foot strike position, and maintaining a rigid torso.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Place both hands on a wall at shoulder height, lean forward so your body forms a straight line at roughly 45 degrees.
Brace the core and lift one knee to hip height with the foot dorsiflexed.
On command, exchange leg positions rapidly—driving the raised knee down and back while the opposite knee drives up.
Each foot strike should land directly beneath or slightly behind the hips.
Maintain the forward lean and rigid torso throughout—do not let the hips sag or pike.
Perform for 5–10 exchanges per set, focusing on speed and precision of leg action.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Lunge Pass Through
description:
**A dynamic lunge variation where a kettlebell is passed under the front leg at the bottom of each rep, combining lower-body strength with coordination and core anti-rotation demands.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, abs, adductors.

The deep lunge position places the quads and hip flexors under a significant stretch while the pass-through challenges stability.

**Pro tip:** Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
instructions: Step forward into a deep lunge position with the front thigh at or below parallel.
Hold the kettlebell in one hand at your side.
At the bottom of the lunge, pass the kettlebell under the front thigh from one hand to the other.
Keep the torso upright and the core braced to resist rotation during the transfer.
Drive through the front heel to return to standing.
Alternate legs each rep, passing the kettlebell under the new front leg each time.
tips: Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
Avoid letting your front knee collapse inward — track it over your second toe
A shorter step length increases quad demand; a longer step shifts to glutes
Keep your weight centered over the front leg throughout the movement
coaching_cues: Drive up from the front leg

name: Lying Hamstring
description:
**A prone or supine hamstring stretch performed while lying down, allowing gravity and relaxation to progressively lengthen the muscle without spinal loading.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This low-intensity flexibility exercise is ideal for post-workout recovery or daily mobility maintenance.

**Pro tip:** Keep the stretching leg straight
instructions: Lie on your back with both legs flat on the floor.
Bend one knee and draw it toward your chest, then extend the leg upward toward the ceiling.
Straighten the leg as much as your flexibility allows, holding behind the thigh or calf.
Gently pull the leg closer to your body until a comfortable stretch is felt along the back of the thigh.
Keep the opposite leg flat and relaxed on the ground, hips pressed into the floor.
Hold for 20–30 seconds, then switch sides.
tips: Keep the stretching leg straight
Use a strap or towel if you can't reach your foot
Keep the non-stretching leg flat on the ground

name: Lying Leg Curl
description:
**A machine-based isolation exercise performed face-down that targets the hamstrings through knee flexion against a resistance pad.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

The lying position stabilizes the hips and removes momentum, allowing focused and controlled loading of the hamstring muscles through their primary function of bending the knee.

**Pro tip:** Don't let your hips rise off the pad — this shortens the hamstrings
instructions: Lie face-down on the leg curl machine with the ankle pad positioned just above the heels.
Grip the handles and press the hips firmly into the bench to prevent them from rising.
Curl the weight upward by bending the knees, bringing the heels toward the glutes.
Squeeze the hamstrings hard at the top of the movement for a one-second hold.
Lower the weight slowly under control, resisting the eccentric phase for 2–3 seconds.
Stop just short of full knee extension at the bottom to maintain tension on the hamstrings.
tips: Don't let your hips rise off the pad — this shortens the hamstrings
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds
Get a full stretch at the bottom — let the knee fully extend
Point toes away to reduce calf involvement

name: Lying Leg Curls
description:
**A prone hamstring isolation exercise using a lever-arm machine to provide consistent resistance through the knee flexion arc.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This staple leg-day movement allows precise load selection and is highly effective for accumulating training volume on the hamstrings with minimal systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Keep hips pressed into the pad throughout
instructions: Adjust the machine so the ankle pad sits on the lower calves, just above the Achilles tendon.
Lie face-down with knees aligned with the machine's pivot point.
Initiate the curl by contracting the hamstrings to bring the pad toward the glutes.
Avoid jerking or using momentum—perform each rep with a smooth, deliberate tempo.
Pause briefly at peak contraction before lowering the weight in a controlled 2–3 second negative.
Do not slam the weight stack at the bottom; maintain continuous tension throughout the set.
tips: Keep hips pressed into the pad throughout
Full range of motion — extend completely at the bottom
Control the negative
Don't use momentum to swing the weight up

name: Moving Claw Series
description:
**A sprint-specific warm-up drill that emphasizes the clawing or pawing action of the foot striking the ground, reinforcing proper ground-contact mechanics while moving forward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: hamstrings, calves, glutes.

This drill trains the nervous system to produce quick, aggressive foot contacts that translate directly to faster sprinting.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Begin walking or jogging slowly forward with an upright posture.
Lift one knee to hip height, then actively drive the foot down and back toward the ground in a clawing motion.
The foot should strike the ground directly beneath or slightly behind the hip.
Immediately cycle the opposite leg through the same pattern.
Gradually increase speed across the drill distance while maintaining the aggressive claw action.
Perform over 20–30 meters, walk back, and repeat for 3–4 sets.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Muscle Snatch
description:
**An Olympic lifting accessory where the barbell is pulled from the floor to overhead in one continuous motion without dropping under the bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, triceps, forearms.

By eliminating the catch phase, this exercise isolates the pulling mechanics and overhead turnover, building upper-back strength, shoulder stability, and full-body coordination.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Set up with feet hip-width apart, gripping the barbell with a wide snatch grip.
Lift the bar off the floor by extending the knees and hips, keeping the back angle constant.
As the bar passes the knees, accelerate aggressively by extending the hips and shrugging the shoulders.
Continue pulling the bar upward with high elbows, then press and rotate it overhead without rebending the knees.
Lock the bar out overhead with straight arms and active shoulders.
Lower the bar back to the hips under control, then return to the floor for the next rep.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Natural Glute Ham Raise
description:
**An advanced bodyweight posterior chain exercise performed on a GHD bench where the lifter lowers the torso from an upright kneeling position using eccentric hamstring control, then drives back up through powerful knee flexion. This movement loads the hamstrings at long muscle lengths under high tension, making it one of the most effective exercises for hamstring hypertrophy and injury prevention.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, calves, erectors.



**Pro tip:** Keep hips extended — don't pike at the waist
instructions: Secure your ankles under the foot pads of a GHD or have a partner hold them firmly.
Begin in an upright kneeling position with the torso vertical and arms crossed over the chest.
Slowly lower your body forward by extending at the knees, resisting gravity with the hamstrings.
Maintain a straight line from the knees through the hips to the shoulders—do not bend at the waist.
Lower as far as you can control, then forcefully contract the hamstrings to pull yourself back to the starting position.
If you cannot complete the concentric phase, use a light hand push off the floor to assist.
tips: Keep hips extended — don't pike at the waist
Use hands to catch yourself and assist the concentric
Aim for a 3-5 second eccentric
Progress by reducing hand assistance over time

name: Nordic Curl
description:
**A bodyweight knee-flexion exercise where the lifter kneels and slowly lowers the torso toward the ground using only hamstring eccentric strength to control the descent.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

Widely regarded as one of the most effective hamstring exercises for building eccentric strength, promoting muscle growth at long lengths, and reducing hamstring injury risk.

**Pro tip:** Keep hips extended throughout — the most common error is piking at the hips
instructions: Kneel on a pad with your ankles secured under a fixed object or held by a training partner.
Keep the hips fully extended and the torso upright—form a straight line from knees to head.
Slowly lean forward by allowing the knees to extend, lowering your body toward the floor.
Resist the descent as long as possible using hamstring strength, maintaining the rigid body line.
When you can no longer control the fall, catch yourself with your hands and perform a gentle push-up to assist the return.
Pull yourself back to the upright position by contracting the hamstrings, using as little hand assistance as possible.
tips: Keep hips extended throughout — the most common error is piking at the hips
Control the descent for 3-5 seconds
Use hands to assist the concentric initially
Start with 3-4 reps — eccentric DOMS will be significant at first
coaching_cues: Fight gravity all the way down

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Clean
description:
**An explosive single-arm kettlebell movement that drives the bell from below the hips into the front rack position using powerful hip extension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, quads.

Primarily taxes the glutes through a rapid hinge pattern while demanding grip strength and shoulder stability to receive the weight cleanly.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, kettlebell on the floor between your feet.
Hinge at the hips and grasp the handle with one hand using an overhand grip.
Hike the bell back between your legs, then explosively drive your hips forward to propel the kettlebell upward.
As the bell rises past your waist, pull your elbow tight to your ribs and rotate your hand around the bell so it lands softly against your forearm in the rack position.
Pause briefly at the top with the bell resting on your forearm and bicep, elbow tucked.
Reverse the motion by letting the bell drop and guiding it back between your legs for the next rep.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: One-Arm Kettlebell Swings
description:
**A ballistic single-arm hip hinge that propels a kettlebell to chest or overhead height through forceful glute contraction.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, shoulders, forearms, abs, calves.

The unilateral loading introduces an anti-rotation demand on the core and challenges grip endurance more than the two-handed variation.

**Pro tip:** Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
instructions: Place the kettlebell on the floor slightly ahead of you.
Hinge forward, grab the handle with one hand, and hike it back between your legs with a flat back.
Snap your hips forward aggressively, squeezing your glutes at the top to project the bell to roughly chest height.
Let the free arm swing naturally for balance.
As the bell descends, hinge your hips back and guide it between your legs, maintaining a neutral spine.
Avoid rotating your torso—keep your shoulders square throughout.
tips: Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
Hike the bell back between your legs like a football snap
Stand tall and squeeze the glutes hard at the top
Don't squat the swing — it's a hinge pattern

name: One-Arm Open Palm Kettlebell Clean
description:
**A kettlebell clean variation performed with an open palm grip, requiring the lifter to catch and control the bell without wrapping the fingers around the handle during the pull phase.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms.

This builds superior hand-eye coordination and teaches a smooth rack transition while still driving power from the glutes.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Set up as you would for a standard one-arm kettlebell clean with feet shoulder-width apart.
Hike the bell back with an open palm resting against the handle rather than gripping it tightly.
Drive your hips forward explosively.
As the bell travels upward, keep your palm open and guide it into the rack position by letting the handle settle into your fingers at the top.
Close your grip only once the bell is seated against your forearm.
Lower by opening the hand again and allowing the bell to swing back between your legs under control.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Open Palm Kettlebell Clean
description:
**A bilateral open-palm kettlebell clean that develops explosive hip drive and refined bell-handling skill.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms.

By releasing the tight grip during the pull, the lifter is forced to generate clean vertical force from the hips rather than muscling the weight up with the arms.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand over the kettlebell with a hip-width stance.
Hinge down and place your open palm against the handle without wrapping your fingers.
Hike the bell back, then thrust your hips forward powerfully.
Let the momentum carry the bell upward while your open hand guides it.
As the bell reaches chest height, allow the handle to rotate into your fingers and close your grip as it settles into the rack position against your forearm.
Keep your elbow pinned to your torso at the top.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Platform Hamstring Slides
description:
**A bodyweight hamstring exercise performed by sliding the feet along a low-friction surface while lying supine, forcing the hamstrings to control both the eccentric extension and concentric curl of the legs. The platform provides a stable base that makes the movement more consistent than floor-based variations.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes.



**Pro tip:** Keep hips elevated throughout
instructions: Lie face-up on the platform with your knees bent and feet flat on a sliding surface such as sliders or a towel on a smooth floor.
Press your hips up into a bridge position by squeezing your glutes.
Slowly extend both legs outward by sliding your heels away from you, lowering your hips only slightly as you lengthen.
Once your legs are nearly straight, pull your heels back toward your glutes by contracting your hamstrings, driving your hips back up simultaneously.
Keep constant tension on the hamstrings and avoid letting your hips drop to the floor between reps.
Perform the movement slowly to maximize time under tension.
tips: Keep hips elevated throughout
Use sliders, socks on hardwood, or a towel on tile
Control the slide out — don't collapse

name: Power Clean from Blocks
description:
**A power clean variation where the barbell starts on elevated blocks rather than the floor, shortening the pull and allowing the lifter to focus on the explosive second pull and hip extension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, calves.

This is an excellent tool for developing rate of force production in the glutes and posterior chain without the demands of a full pull from the ground.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Set blocks so the barbell rests at approximately mid-shin to just below the knee.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, grip the bar just outside your knees with a hook or double overhand grip.
Set your back flat, chest up, and shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar.
Push through the floor and extend your hips explosively once the bar passes the knees, shrugging hard and pulling your elbows high.
Receive the bar in a quarter-squat front rack position with elbows forward and the bar resting on your front deltoids.
Stand to full extension.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Power Snatch
description:
**A full-body Olympic lift that takes the barbell from the floor to overhead in one continuous motion, caught in a partial squat rather than a full deep squat.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms, calves, triceps.

The movement demands violent hip extension from the glutes and coordinates nearly every muscle in the body through a precise sequence of pulls.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, toes slightly out.
Grip the bar with a wide snatch grip so it sits in the hip crease when standing.
Hinge down with a flat back, shoulders over the bar, and arms straight.
Pull the bar off the floor by pushing through your legs, keeping it close to your shins.
As the bar passes the knees, accelerate by driving your hips forward explosively and shrugging upward.
Pull yourself under the bar and punch your arms to lockout overhead, catching it in a quarter-squat with the bar directly over your midfoot.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Power Stairs
description:
**A dynamic lower-body exercise that involves bounding or stepping explosively up a flight of stairs, heavily loading the quads through repeated knee extension against gravity.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, erectors, shoulders.

The continuous upward movement builds muscular endurance and power while the stair height enforces a deep range of motion at the knee and hip.

**Pro tip:** Use a box height that creates at least 90 degrees of knee flexion on the working leg
instructions: Stand at the base of a staircase with feet hip-width apart.
Drive off one foot and step or bound up the stairs, pushing forcefully through the ball of your foot and extending your knee and hip fully with each stride.
Pump your arms to generate momentum and maintain an upright torso.
Land on the mid-foot of each step and immediately transition into the next stride.
For power development, take two or three stairs at a time.
For endurance, use single steps at a faster cadence.
tips: Use a box height that creates at least 90 degrees of knee flexion on the working leg
Drive through the heel and midfoot of the top leg — avoid pushing off the back foot
Keep your torso upright throughout to maintain quad dominance
Control the descent slowly to maximize eccentric quad loading

name: Prone Manual Hamstring
description:
**A partner-assisted hamstring curl performed face-down where a training partner provides manual resistance against the leg curl motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This allows for variable resistance throughout the range and is especially effective for eccentric overload when the partner pushes harder during the lowering phase.

**Pro tip:** Communicate with your partner about resistance level
instructions: Lie face-down on a flat bench or the floor with your legs straight.
Have a training partner place their hands on the back of your ankles.
Curl your heels toward your glutes while your partner applies steady downward resistance against the movement.
Squeeze your hamstrings hard at the top of the curl, then slowly lower your legs as your partner increases the resistance on the way down for an accentuated eccentric.
Keep your hips pressed into the bench throughout and avoid arching your lower back.
Communicate with your partner to calibrate the resistance so you can complete the target reps with good form.
tips: Communicate with your partner about resistance level
Partner should provide more resistance during the eccentric
Keep hips flat on the ground

name: Prowler Sprint
description:
**A high-intensity pushing exercise where you sprint while driving a weighted sled across a flat surface.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, chest, shoulders.

The constant forward lean and knee drive place enormous demand on the quads, while the lack of an eccentric component makes it surprisingly joint-friendly despite the high effort.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
instructions: Load the prowler sled to an appropriate weight.
Grip the high or low handles and lean into the sled at roughly a 45-degree angle with arms extended.
Drive one knee forward while pushing off the opposite foot, keeping your steps short and powerful.
Stay low with your hips behind your shoulders to maintain forward force.
Sprint for a set distance—typically 20 to 40 meters—then rest and repeat.
Breathe rhythmically and avoid holding your breath.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
Keep your torso at a forward lean angle appropriate for the implement
Start with lighter loads and focus on maintaining consistent stride length
Use this as a finisher or conditioning tool after primary quad work

name: Reverse Band Sumo Deadlift
description:
**A sumo deadlift performed with resistance bands attached overhead that assist the lift off the floor and reduce assistance as you stand up, allowing heavier loads at lockout.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, adductors, forearms, traps, lats.

This accommodating resistance profile overloads the glutes and adductors at the top while reducing stress on the lower back in the most vulnerable bottom position.

**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Attach heavy resistance bands to the top of a power rack and loop them over each end of the barbell.
Set up with a wide sumo stance, toes pointed out 30 to 45 degrees, and grip the bar inside your knees with straight arms.
Drop your hips, brace your core, and push the floor apart with your feet to initiate the pull.
The bands will provide the most assistance at the bottom.
As you rise, the band tension decreases and the load increases—drive your hips through aggressively at the top and squeeze your glutes to lock out.
Lower the bar under control.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Reverse Hyperextension
description:
**A posterior chain exercise performed on a dedicated machine where the torso is fixed and the legs swing through a full arc behind the body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors.

The reverse hyper provides traction on the spine during the eccentric phase, making it both a strengthening and decompression tool for the lower back and glutes.

**Pro tip:** Hinge at the hips, not the lower back
instructions: Lie face-down on the reverse hyperextension machine with your hips at the edge of the pad and your torso flat.
Grip the handles firmly.
Place your ankles behind the roller pad or in the strap attachment.
Let your legs hang straight down to start.
Contract your glutes and hamstrings to swing your legs upward until they are in line with or slightly above your torso.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze your glutes.
tips: Hinge at the hips, not the lower back
Squeeze the glutes to drive the movement — don't hyperextend the spine
Control the descent to maintain tension on the posterior chain
Round your upper back slightly to reduce erector contribution and increase glute demand

name: Romanian Deadlift
description:
**A hip-hinge movement that loads the hamstrings under a deep stretch by lowering a barbell along the legs with minimal knee bend.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms, traps.

The Romanian deadlift is one of the most effective exercises for hamstring hypertrophy due to its emphasis on the lengthened position, where muscle fibers experience the highest mechanical tension.

**Pro tip:** Keep the bar in contact with your legs throughout
instructions: Unrack a barbell from a rack or deadlift it to a standing position with feet hip-width apart and a double overhand or mixed grip.
With a slight bend in your knees that stays fixed throughout, push your hips back and lower the bar along your thighs and shins.
Keep the bar in contact with your legs and your back flat.
Descend until you feel a strong stretch in your hamstrings—typically when the bar reaches mid-shin level.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Do not round your lower back at any point.
tips: Keep the bar in contact with your legs throughout
Push hips back — the bar should travel nearly vertically
Stop when you feel a deep hamstring stretch, not when the bar reaches a certain height
Use straps so grip doesn't limit hamstring training
Slight knee bend — this is not a stiff-leg deadlift
coaching_cues: Feel the stretch at the bottom

name: Romanian Deadlift from Deficit
description:
**A Romanian deadlift performed while standing on an elevated surface such as a plate or low platform, increasing the range of motion at the bottom and placing the hamstrings under an even deeper stretch. This extended range makes it a superior choice for lifters seeking maximum lengthened-position stimulus for hamstring growth.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms, traps.



**Pro tip:** Use a 2-4 inch deficit
instructions: Stand on a stable platform two to four inches high with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with a double overhand or mixed grip.
Lock a slight knee bend and push your hips back, lowering the bar along your legs.
The deficit allows the bar to travel past your feet, deepening the hamstring stretch beyond what a flat-floor RDL permits.
Descend until you reach your maximum stretch without rounding your back—this will be lower than a standard RDL.
Drive your hips forward to stand back up, keeping the bar close to your body.
Use lighter loads than your standard RDL until you adapt to the increased range of motion.
tips: Use a 2-4 inch deficit
The extra range must come from hip flexion, not lumbar rounding
Use moderate loads — the extended ROM is demanding
Keep the bar close to your legs

name: Runner's Stretch
description:
**A static flexibility exercise that targets the hamstrings by extending one leg forward while kneeling on the opposite knee, creating a deep passive stretch along the back of the thigh.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves, quads.

This is a recovery and mobility tool rather than a strength exercise, ideal for improving hip hinge range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Straighten the front leg fully
instructions: Begin in a half-kneeling position with your right knee on the ground and your left foot flat on the floor in front of you.
Shift your weight back and straighten your left leg, placing your heel on the ground with toes pointing up.
Hinge forward at the hips with a flat back and reach toward your left toes until you feel a deep stretch in the back of your left thigh.
Hold the position for 30 to 60 seconds while breathing deeply.
Avoid rounding your spine—the stretch should come from the hip hinge.
Switch sides and repeat.
tips: Straighten the front leg fully
Hinge from the hips toward the front foot
Hold 30-60 seconds per side

name: Seated Band Hamstring Curl
description:
**A seated hamstring curl performed using a resistance band anchored in front of the body, providing increasing tension as the knees flex.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

The seated position keeps the hamstrings in a lengthened state at the hip, which combined with the band's ascending resistance profile creates meaningful stretch-mediated tension for hamstring development.

**Pro tip:** Sit on a bench with the band anchored in front of you
instructions: Sit on a bench or chair with your legs extended.
Loop a resistance band around a sturdy anchor point at floor level in front of you and place the other end behind your ankles.
Start with your legs straight and the band under light tension.
Curl your heels back under the bench by bending your knees, pulling against the band's resistance.
Squeeze your hamstrings at the fully contracted position, then slowly extend your legs back to the start, controlling the band tension on the way out.
Keep your torso upright and avoid rocking backward to generate momentum.
tips: Sit on a bench with the band anchored in front of you
Start with the knee extended to maximize the stretch
Control the eccentric — don't let the band snap your leg straight

name: Seated Floor Hamstring Stretch
description:
**A classic seated forward fold performed on the floor with both legs extended, targeting the hamstrings through a sustained passive stretch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This fundamental flexibility exercise requires no equipment and is effective for improving posterior chain mobility over time.

**Pro tip:** Keep knees straight
instructions: Sit on the floor with both legs extended straight in front of you and your feet together.
Sit tall to establish a neutral spine.
Hinge forward from your hips, reaching your hands toward your toes or shins.
Keep your back as flat as possible rather than rounding your upper back to reach further.
Hold the deepest comfortable position for 30 to 60 seconds, breathing steadily and relaxing into the stretch with each exhale.
For a deeper stretch, dorsiflex your ankles by pulling your toes toward you.
tips: Keep knees straight
Hinge from the hips, not the upper back
Hold for 30-60 seconds

name: Seated Hamstring
description:
**A seated hamstring stretch variation where one or both legs are extended while the torso folds forward, placing the hamstrings under a prolonged passive stretch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This mobility exercise is used for recovery, warm-up preparation, or long-term flexibility development.

**Pro tip:** Keep the stretching leg straight
instructions: Sit on a bench or elevated surface with one leg extended straight on the surface and the other foot on the floor for stability.
Sit upright first, then slowly hinge forward from the hips toward the extended leg.
Reach toward your ankle or foot while maintaining a flat back.
Hold the stretch at the point of moderate tension for 30 to 60 seconds.
Breathe deeply and allow gravity to deepen the stretch gradually.
Switch legs and repeat.
tips: Keep the stretching leg straight
Lean forward from the hips
Hold 30-60 seconds

name: Seated Hamstring and Calf Stretch
description:
**A combined stretch that targets both the hamstrings and calves simultaneously by extending the legs and dorsiflexing the ankles while folding forward from a seated position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This dual-target approach is efficient for improving overall posterior leg flexibility in a single hold.

**Pro tip:** Pull toes toward you to add calf stretch
instructions: Sit on the floor with both legs extended and together.
Loop a towel or resistance band around the balls of your feet and hold the ends in each hand.
Pull your toes toward your shins to engage the calf stretch while simultaneously hinging forward from the hips to stretch the hamstrings.
Keep your knees straight and your back as flat as possible.
Hold for 30 to 60 seconds, using the towel to gently increase the dorsiflexion and forward fold with each exhale.
If no towel is available, simply reach for your toes and actively pull them back.
tips: Pull toes toward you to add calf stretch
Keep knees straight
Hold 30-60 seconds

name: Seated Leg Curl
description:
**A machine-based hamstring isolation exercise performed in a seated position that places the hamstrings under significant stretch at the start of each rep due to hip flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

This stretched starting position makes the seated leg curl one of the most effective machine exercises for hamstring hypertrophy, delivering high stimulus with minimal systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Adjust the pad so your knee joint aligns with the machine's pivot point
instructions: Adjust the machine so the back pad supports your full torso and the shin pad sits just above your ankles.
Set the thigh pad snugly against your lower quads to prevent your hips from rising.
Start with your legs extended and the weight stack engaged.
Curl your heels toward the underside of the seat by contracting your hamstrings forcefully.
Squeeze at the bottom of the curl for a one-second count.
Slowly return to the starting position, fully extending your legs to maximize the stretch on the hamstrings.
tips: Adjust the pad so your knee joint aligns with the machine's pivot point
Get a full stretch at the top — let the knee extend completely
Control the eccentric for 2-3 seconds
Lean slightly forward to increase hip flexion and hamstring stretch

name: Single Leg Curl
description:
**A unilateral leg curl performed one leg at a time on a lying or seated curl machine, allowing you to identify and correct strength imbalances between legs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: calves.

Training each hamstring independently ensures the stronger leg cannot compensate for the weaker one.

**Pro tip:** Match the machine type — seated is superior for hamstring length
instructions: Set up on a lying or seated leg curl machine and position only one leg against the shin pad.
Secure the other leg out of the way or let it rest.
Curl the working leg by bending your knee and pulling the pad toward your glute.
Squeeze the hamstring at peak contraction, then lower the weight slowly back to the start.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
Use a weight that is approximately half of what you would use bilaterally, or slightly less to account for the stabilization demand.
tips: Match the machine type — seated is superior for hamstring length
Focus on the weaker leg first
Control the eccentric on each rep

name: Single Leg Romanian Deadlift
description:
**A unilateral hip hinge performed while balancing on one leg with a dumbbell, delivering a deep hamstring stretch under load while simultaneously challenging balance and hip stability.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms.

The single-leg stance amplifies the stretch on the working hamstring and recruits the glute medius for lateral stability.

**Pro tip:** Let the free leg extend behind you as a counterbalance
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in the hand opposite to your standing leg.
Stand on one foot with a slight knee bend.
Hinge at the hip by pushing your free leg straight back behind you as a counterbalance while lowering the dumbbell toward the floor.
Keep your hips square—avoid opening up to the side.
Descend until you feel a deep stretch in the standing leg's hamstring, typically when your torso is near parallel to the ground.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing the glute at the top.
tips: Let the free leg extend behind you as a counterbalance
Keep hips square — don't rotate open
Use a wall or rack for balance support if needed initially
The limiting factor is often balance, not hamstring strength

name: Single-Leg Deadlift
description:
**A single-leg hip hinge that loads the hamstrings and glutes through a full range of motion while demanding proprioception and ankle stability.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, erectors, forearms.

Holding dumbbells in one or both hands adds load to the stretched position, making this a potent exercise for building resilient, well-balanced hamstrings.

**Pro tip:** Keep the working leg slightly bent
instructions: Stand on your left foot holding a dumbbell in your right hand or one in each hand.
Soften your standing knee slightly.
Hinge forward by sending your right leg straight back and lowering your torso until both are roughly parallel to the floor.
Let the dumbbell travel straight down toward the ground, keeping it close to your standing leg.
Your body should form a straight line from head to trailing heel.
Reverse the movement by contracting your hamstring and glute to pull yourself back to standing.
tips: Keep the working leg slightly bent
Hinge at the hip — don't round the back
Use a kettlebell or dumbbell for easier balance

name: Sliding Leg Curl
description:
**A bodyweight hamstring curl performed by sliding the heels along a smooth surface from a bridge position, using friction-reducing tools like sliders, socks on hardwood, or a towel on tile.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: hamstrings. Secondary: glutes, calves.

This accessible exercise effectively trains knee flexion against bodyweight resistance without any gym equipment.

**Pro tip:** Keep hips elevated throughout the entire set
instructions: Lie on your back on a smooth floor with your knees bent and feet on sliders or a towel.
Lift your hips into a bridge position by squeezing your glutes.
Slowly slide your feet away from your body, extending your legs while keeping your hips elevated as long as possible.
Once your legs are nearly straight, reverse the motion by digging your heels into the sliders and curling them back toward your glutes.
Maintain the bridge throughout the set.
If the full bilateral version is too easy, perform it one leg at a time.
tips: Keep hips elevated throughout the entire set
Use sliders, socks on hardwood, or a towel
Extend fully on each rep for maximum range

name: Smith Machine Hang Power Clean
description:
**A hang power clean performed on a Smith machine, which constrains the bar to a vertical path and removes the need to stabilize the bar laterally.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, traps, shoulders, forearms.

While it sacrifices some of the coordination demands of a free-weight clean, it allows beginners to practice the explosive hip extension pattern in a more controlled environment.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Set the Smith machine bar at mid-thigh height and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip and unhook it.
Lower the bar to just above your knees by hinging at the hips with a flat back.
Explosively extend your hips and shrug upward, pulling the bar along the fixed track.
Rotate your elbows forward and catch the bar in a front rack position at shoulder height while dropping into a quarter squat.
Stand to full extension.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Smith Machine Romanian Deadlift
description: **The guided-rail secret to bulletproof hamstrings.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, lower back, forearms. The Smith machine locks the bar path so you can focus entirely on loading your hamstrings through a deep stretch. This is ideal for lifters who want heavy hip-hinge work without worrying about balance. **Pro tip:** Push your hips back as far as possible rather than just bending forward — the stretch should originate from the hips, not the spine. **Biomechanics:** The fixed bar path removes the stabilization demand, allowing greater loading on the hamstrings at their lengthened position. The ascending strength curve means the lift gets easier as you stand up, so control the lowering phase for maximum growth stimulus.
instructions: Stand inside the Smith machine with the bar across your upper thighs, feet hip-width apart.
Unlock the bar, keep a slight bend in your knees, and push your hips straight back.
Lower the bar along your thighs until you feel a strong stretch in your hamstrings, roughly mid-shin level.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Keep the bar close to your body throughout and avoid rounding your back.
