tips: Stand so the bar travels close to your legs
Push hips back — the fixed bar path means you must position correctly
Use straps for heavier loads

name: Smith Machine Stiff-Legged Deadlift
description: **Straighten up and feel the burn from hip to heel.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, lower back. By keeping your legs nearly straight on the machine, you shift even more tension onto the hamstrings compared to the Romanian variation. The machine handles the balance so you can chase a deeper stretch safely. **Pro tip:** Think about pushing the floor away with your heels on the way up — this cue fires the hamstrings harder than thinking about standing. **Biomechanics:** Straighter legs increase the moment arm at the hip, placing greater demand on the hamstrings at full stretch. The machine's fixed path prevents the bar from drifting, protecting the lower back while you load the lengthened position aggressively.
instructions: Set up in the Smith machine with the bar at thigh height and your feet hip-width apart.
Unlock the bar and keep your legs as straight as comfortable — a very slight knee bend is fine.
Hinge at the hips and lower the bar down your legs until you feel a deep hamstring stretch.
Reverse the movement by driving your hips forward until you're standing tall.
Re-lock the bar at the top of your final rep.
tips: Keep legs nearly straight — only a very slight knee bend
Push hips back to initiate
The fixed path means you must position your feet correctly relative to the bar

name: Snatch Deadlift
description: **The wide-grip pull that builds a back like a barn door.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, lower back, lats, traps, forearms, quads. Using a wider-than-normal grip forces you into a deeper starting position and recruits your upper back heavily. This is a strength builder for Olympic lifting and raw pulling power alike. **Pro tip:** Set your shoulder blades down and back before you pull — losing upper-back tightness with a wide grip is the fastest way to miss the lift. **Biomechanics:** The wide grip lowers the effective arm length, requiring greater hip and knee flexion at the start. This increases range of motion and time under tension for the posterior chain while demanding significant lat engagement to keep the bar close.
instructions: Stand over a loaded barbell with feet hip-width apart and grip the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands near the collars.
Drop your hips, brace your core, and pull your shoulder blades together with a flat back.
Drive through your feet to lift the bar, keeping it as close to your body as possible.
Stand fully upright with hips locked out and shoulders pulled back.
Lower the bar under control back to the floor, resetting your position each rep.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Snatch Pull
description: **Explosive power from the ground up — no catch required.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, lower back, traps, quads, calves, forearms. This Olympic lifting accessory trains the pulling phase of the snatch without the overhead catch, making it accessible for athletes wanting hip explosiveness. You'll build serious trap and glute power. **Pro tip:** Accelerate through the entire pull — the bar should feel weightless at the top. If it doesn't, you're not extending hard enough. **Biomechanics:** Triple extension of ankles, knees, and hips generates maximum force. The wide grip and upward shrug heavily recruit the traps, while explosive hip drive trains the glutes and hamstrings at high velocities.
instructions: Set up over a barbell with a wide snatch-width overhand grip and feet hip-width apart.
With a flat back and hips low, push through the floor to begin the pull.
As the bar passes your knees, explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles while shrugging your shoulders upward.
Rise onto your toes at the peak of the pull, keeping the bar close to your body.
Lower the bar back to the floor with control and reset before the next rep.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Split Snatch
description: **Catch the bar overhead in a lunge and own every plane of movement.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, quads, lower back, traps, shoulders, triceps, forearms, calves. This full-body Olympic lift combines explosive pulling with an overhead catch in a split stance, demanding coordination, power, and shoulder stability. **Pro tip:** Land with your front shin vertical — if your knee shoots forward, you've split too narrow. **Biomechanics:** The split landing trains single-leg stability under load asymmetrically. The overhead lockout requires full shoulder flexion and triceps engagement, while the pull follows triple-extension mechanics.
instructions: Stand over a barbell with a wide snatch grip, feet hip-width apart, back flat.
Pull the bar explosively from the floor by extending your hips, knees, and ankles.
As the bar reaches its peak height, quickly drop into a split stance — one foot forward, one back.
Catch the bar overhead with arms fully locked out while in the split position.
Stand up by bringing your front foot back and rear foot forward to meet in the middle.
Lower the bar under control to your shoulders, then to the floor.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Split Squats
description: **One leg at a time — the great equalizer for lopsided legs.** **Muscles worked:** quads, glutes, hamstrings, calves, inner thighs. Split squats expose and fix strength imbalances between your legs while building serious single-leg stability. No equipment needed, just your body and some floor space. **Pro tip:** Keep about 80% of your weight on the front foot — the back leg is just for balance, not for pushing. **Biomechanics:** The staggered stance increases the range of motion at the front hip and knee, placing the quads under a deep stretch at the bottom. The ascending strength curve means the hardest part is the bottom position, making this excellent for building strength out of the hole.
instructions: Stand tall and take a large step forward so your feet are staggered front to back.
Keep your torso upright and lower your body by bending both knees.
Descend until your back knee nearly touches the floor and your front thigh is roughly parallel to the ground.
Push through your front foot to stand back up to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching legs.
tips: Descend deep enough that the front thigh reaches parallel or below
Maintain an upright torso — forward lean reduces quad stimulus
Drive up through the ball of the front foot for maximum quad engagement
Use a controlled tempo to increase time under tension on the quads

name: Standing Hamstring and Calf Stretch
description: **Two-for-one relief from the ankle to the sit bone.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, calves. This simple standing stretch targets the entire back line of your leg in one position. It's perfect after leg day or long periods of sitting to restore flexibility. **Pro tip:** Flex your toes toward your shin to intensify the calf stretch — you'll feel the pull shift lower down your leg instantly.
instructions: Stand upright and extend one leg straight out in front of you, resting the heel on the ground with toes pointed up.
Keep your standing leg slightly bent for balance.
Hinge forward at the hips with a flat back until you feel a stretch along the back of your extended leg.
Hold the position for 20-30 seconds, breathing deeply.
Switch legs and repeat.
tips: Keep the front leg straight
Pull toes toward you for calf emphasis
Hold 30-60 seconds per side

name: Standing Leg Curl
description: **Isolate one hamstring at a time with machine precision.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings. The standing leg curl machine lets you train each leg independently, making it easy to spot and fix imbalances. The mid-range loading means peak tension hits when your knee is around 90 degrees. **Pro tip:** Squeeze at the top for a full second — most people rush the contraction and leave gains on the table. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped strength curve means resistance is highest in the middle of the range. Standing upright keeps the hamstrings in a slightly shortened position at the hip, which shifts emphasis toward the lower portion of the muscle near the knee.
instructions: Step into the standing leg curl machine and position the pad just above your ankle on one leg.
Hold the handles for support and keep your standing leg firmly planted.
Curl the working leg up by bending your knee, bringing your heel toward your glute.
Squeeze at the top, then slowly lower back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other.
tips: Stand tall — don't lean forward excessively
Get a full stretch at the bottom
Control the eccentric

name: Standing Toe Touches
description: **The simplest stretch you already know — done right, it unlocks everything.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, calves, lower back. Standing toe touches lengthen the entire posterior chain from your heels to your lower back. It's a classic for a reason — quick, effective, and needs zero equipment. **Pro tip:** Bend your knees slightly if you can't reach your toes — forcing straight legs with a rounded back defeats the purpose.
instructions: Stand with your feet together and arms at your sides.
Slowly bend forward at the hips, reaching your hands toward your toes.
Let your head and arms hang relaxed as you feel the stretch along the back of your legs.
Hold for 20-30 seconds, breathing steadily.
Slowly roll back up to standing, stacking one vertebra at a time.
tips: Keep knees straight
Reach for the toes, not the floor
Don't bounce

name: Stiff-Leg Deadlift
description: **Lock your knees, load the stretch, and watch your hamstrings grow.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, lower back, forearms. The stiff-leg deadlift maximizes hamstring stretch by keeping the knees nearly locked throughout. It's one of the most effective barbell movements for building the back of your legs. **Pro tip:** Stop the descent when your back starts to round — going lower with a curved spine adds risk, not muscle. **Biomechanics:** Near-straight legs create a long lever arm from the hip, placing maximum tension on the hamstrings at their most lengthened position. The ascending strength curve means the bottom is the hardest part, which is exactly where the growth stimulus is greatest.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a barbell with an overhand grip at thigh level.
Keep your legs nearly straight with only a very slight bend in the knees.
Hinge at the hips and lower the barbell down the front of your legs.
Descend until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings, typically around mid-shin.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, keeping the bar close to your body.
tips: Keep the bar close to your legs
Push hips back — don't just bend forward
Stop at a deep hamstring stretch, not at a specific bar height
Use straps if grip limits loading

name: Stiff-Legged Barbell Deadlift
description: **The old-school hamstring builder that never stopped working.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, lower back, forearms. This barbell classic keeps your legs rigid to funnel all the work into the hamstrings and glutes. It's a staple in bodybuilding programs for a reason — nothing stretches and loads the hamstrings quite like this. **Pro tip:** Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back, not by bending your torso forward — the hip hinge is what protects your spine. **Biomechanics:** Maintaining stiff legs increases the hip flexion moment, demanding more from the hamstrings to control the descent and power the ascent. The barbell's position in front of the body creates a longer moment arm compared to trap bar variations, increasing lower back involvement.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip in front of your thighs.
Brace your core and keep your legs straight with a minimal knee bend.
Push your hips back and lower the barbell along your legs until you feel a strong hamstring stretch.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then drive your hips forward to stand back up.
Keep the barbell close to your body and your back flat throughout every rep.
tips: Minimal knee bend — legs should be nearly straight
Bar stays close to the body
Descend until you feel a deep hamstring stretch
Don't sacrifice spinal position for extra range

name: Stiff-Legged Dumbbell Deadlift
description: **Dumbbells in hand, freedom to find your perfect groove.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, lower back, forearms. Using dumbbells instead of a barbell lets the weights travel alongside your legs rather than in front, which many people find more comfortable on the lower back. You also get a slightly greater range of motion. **Pro tip:** Let the dumbbells drift slightly in front of your shins rather than straight down — this keeps tension on the hamstrings instead of shifting it to your back. **Biomechanics:** The independent loading of each hand allows natural arm rotation and a path that follows your body's contours. This can reduce lower back shear compared to a fixed barbell while still delivering a deep hamstring stretch at the bottom.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Keep your legs nearly straight with a very slight knee bend.
Hinge at the hips and lower the dumbbells down the front of your legs.
Descend until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing your glutes at the top.
tips: Keep dumbbells close to your legs
Minimal knee bend
Stop at a deep hamstring stretch

name: Sumo Deadlift with Bands
description: **Add rubber resistance and learn what real lockout strength feels like.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, quads, lower back, lats, traps, forearms. Looping bands around the barbell increases resistance as you stand up, forcing you to accelerate through the entire pull. This builds explosive hip power and a rock-solid lockout. **Pro tip:** Set up the bands so there's light tension even at the bottom — if they're slack off the floor, you're not getting the benefit. **Biomechanics:** The bands add accommodating resistance that matches the ascending strength curve of the sumo deadlift. As mechanical advantage increases toward lockout, band tension rises proportionally, eliminating the deceleration phase and training rate of force development.
instructions: Loop resistance bands around the barbell and anchor them under your feet or to the base of a rack.
Stand with a wide sumo stance over the barbell, toes pointed outward.
Grip the bar with hands inside your knees using an overhand or mixed grip.
Brace your core, drop your hips, and drive through the floor to lift the bar.
Stand fully upright, locking out your hips and knees against the band tension.
Lower the bar under control back to the floor.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Sumo Deadlift with Chains
description: **Chain links pile on pounds as you rise — the ultimate progressive overload trick.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, quads, lower back, lats, traps, forearms. Draping chains over the barbell adds weight gradually as more chain lifts off the floor with each inch you rise. This teaches you to drive hard through the entire range. **Pro tip:** Use chains adding roughly 10-20% of bar weight at lockout — too heavy and your floor form suffers. **Biomechanics:** Chains provide linear accommodating resistance — lightest at the bottom where you're weakest, heaviest at the top where leverage is best, smoothing out sticking points.
instructions: Drape chains evenly over each end of a loaded barbell so they pool on the floor.
Set up in a wide sumo stance with toes angled out and hands gripping inside your knees.
Brace hard, flatten your back, and push the floor apart with your feet to initiate the pull.
As you rise, more chain lifts off the floor — keep accelerating through the movement.
Lock out fully at the top with hips and knees extended.
Lower the bar slowly, letting the chains settle back to the floor.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Sumo Romanian Deadlift
description: **Go wide to hit your inner thighs and hamstrings in one devastating pull.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, lower back, inner thighs, forearms. The wide sumo stance adds adductor work to the classic Romanian deadlift, giving you a two-for-one posterior chain and inner thigh builder. The deep stretch at the bottom is where the magic happens. **Pro tip:** Point your toes out at about 45 degrees and track your knees over your toes — this opens the hips for a deeper, safer stretch. **Biomechanics:** The wide stance shortens the effective torso lever arm, reducing lower back stress while increasing the stretch on the adductors and medial hamstrings. The hip-hinge pattern still loads the hamstrings at their longest length, preserving the high stretch-mediated growth potential.
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width, toes pointed outward, holding a barbell at thigh level.
Keep a slight bend in your knees and push your hips straight back.
Lower the barbell between your legs until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings and inner thighs.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing your glutes at the top.
Keep the bar close to your body and your back flat throughout.
tips: Toes pointed out 30-45 degrees
Push hips back — same hinge pattern as conventional RDL
The wide stance may feel more comfortable for taller lifters

name: Swiss Ball Leg Curl
description: **A stability ball, your bodyweight, and hamstrings that won't quit.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes. This deceptively tough exercise uses a stability ball to train your hamstrings through knee flexion while demanding serious core and hip stability. It's a great home or travel workout option. **Pro tip:** If the double-leg version feels easy, try single-leg curls — the difficulty jumps dramatically. **Biomechanics:** The unstable surface forces constant co-contraction of the hamstrings and glutes to maintain hip extension. The bell-shaped strength curve means peak difficulty occurs mid-curl, and the rolling motion trains both the knee-flexion and hip-extension functions of the hamstrings simultaneously.
instructions: Lie on your back with your heels on top of a stability ball, legs straight, arms at your sides.
Lift your hips off the ground so your body forms a straight line from shoulders to feet.
Bend your knees and roll the ball toward your glutes by digging your heels in.
Squeeze your hamstrings at the top, then slowly extend your legs to roll the ball back out.
Keep your hips elevated throughout the entire set.
tips: Keep hips elevated throughout
Dig heels into the ball
Control the ball — don't let it wobble

name: The Straddle
description: **Sit wide, fold forward, and unlock flexibility you didn't know you had.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, inner thighs. The straddle stretch opens up the entire inner leg line while lengthening the hamstrings. It's a foundational flexibility position used in gymnastics, martial arts, and yoga alike. **Pro tip:** Sit on a thin cushion or folded towel if your pelvis tilts backward — elevating your hips makes it much easier to hinge forward with a flat back.
instructions: Sit on the floor with your legs spread wide apart in a V shape.
Keep your toes pointed up and your knees facing the ceiling.
With a tall spine, slowly hinge forward at the hips, reaching your hands out in front of you.
Go only as far as you can while keeping your back relatively flat.
Hold for 30-60 seconds, breathing deeply and relaxing into the stretch.
tips: Sit tall and lean forward from the hips
Keep knees straight
Hold 30-60 seconds

name: Trap Bar Romanian Deadlift
description: **Step inside the bar and give your lower back a break it deserves.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, lower back, forearms. The trap bar positions the load at your sides instead of in front, reducing spinal shear while still delivering a massive hamstring stretch. It's the friendliest Romanian deadlift variation for people with back sensitivity. **Pro tip:** Use the low handles if your trap bar has them — high handles reduce range of motion and cut into the hamstring stretch. **Biomechanics:** The centered load aligns closer to your center of mass, reducing the moment arm on the lumbar spine by up to 20% compared to a straight barbell. This allows heavier loading with less back fatigue while maintaining the deep stretched position that drives hamstring growth.
instructions: Stand inside a trap bar with feet hip-width apart and grip the handles at your sides.
Lift the bar to standing, then push your hips back with a slight knee bend.
Lower the bar by hinging at the hips until you feel a strong hamstring stretch.
Keep your chest up and back flat throughout the descent.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing and squeeze your glutes at the top.
tips: Push hips back — same hinge pattern as barbell RDL
The trap bar handles should travel beside your legs
Use the low handles for more range of motion

name: Upper Back-Leg Grab
description: **Reach back, grab your leg, and melt tension from your hamstrings and spine.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, lower back. This standing stretch combines a hamstring lengthening with a gentle upper-back release. It's a quick reset you can do anywhere — between sets, at your desk, or during a warm-up. **Pro tip:** Pull gently and let gravity do most of the work — forcing the stretch with your arms can strain your back.
instructions: Stand tall with feet together.
Lift one leg slightly behind you and reach back with both hands to grab your ankle or calf.
Gently fold your torso forward while pulling your leg upward to deepen the stretch.
Hold for 20-30 seconds, feeling the stretch in your hamstring and upper back.
Release slowly and repeat on the other side.
tips: Keep the standing leg slightly bent for balance
Pull gently — don't force the range
Hold 30-60 seconds per side

name: Vertical Swing
description: **Swing a dumbbell between your legs and launch it skyward for total-body power.** **Muscles worked:** glutes, hamstrings, quads, shoulders, lower back. The vertical swing is a kettlebell-swing cousin that uses a single dumbbell to build explosive hip drive and shoulder endurance. It's a fantastic conditioning tool that doubles as strength work. **Pro tip:** The power comes from your hips, not your arms — if your shoulders are burning before your glutes, you're lifting the weight instead of swinging it. **Biomechanics:** The hip hinge loads the glutes and hamstrings eccentrically during the backswing, then the explosive hip extension launches the weight upward. The bell-shaped strength curve means peak force occurs at hip level, and the shoulders work isometrically to guide the dumbbell overhead.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding one dumbbell with both hands between your legs.
Hinge at the hips and swing the dumbbell back between your legs.
Explosively drive your hips forward and swing the dumbbell up overhead in one fluid motion.
Control the dumbbell as it swings back down between your legs.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout every swing.
tips: Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
Hike the bell back between your legs like a football snap
Stand tall and squeeze the glutes hard at the top
Don't squat the swing — it's a hinge pattern

name: Wide Stance Stiff Legs
description: **Spread your feet, stiffen your knees, and feel every fiber of your inner hamstrings light up.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, lower back, inner thighs, forearms. This wide-stance variation of the stiff-leg deadlift shifts emphasis toward the inner hamstrings and adductors while still hammering the entire posterior chain. It's a great way to add variety to your hip-hinge training. **Pro tip:** Keep the barbell path straight down rather than letting it drift forward — the wide stance already increases the stretch, so you don't need extra range. **Biomechanics:** The wider foot placement increases hip abduction, which lengthens the adductors and medial hamstrings more than a conventional stance. Combined with stiff legs, this creates an extreme stretch stimulus at the bottom, making it highly effective for stretch-mediated muscle growth.
instructions: Stand with feet well outside shoulder-width, toes slightly pointed out, holding a barbell at thigh level.
Keep your legs straight with only a minimal knee bend.
Hinge at the hips and lower the barbell straight down between your legs.
Descend until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings and inner thighs.
Drive your hips forward to stand back up, keeping the bar close to your body.
tips: Toes pointed out slightly
Keep legs straight
Push hips back and descend until you feel a deep stretch

name: World's Greatest Stretch
description: **It earned the name — one move that opens up your entire body.** **Muscles worked:** hamstrings, glutes, quads, inner thighs, lower back. This dynamic stretch flows through a lunge, a twist, and a hamstring lengthening in one seamless sequence. It's the gold standard warm-up for athletes, lifters, and anyone who sits too much. **Pro tip:** Spend an extra breath in each position rather than rushing through — the stretch compounds when you give your tissues time to relax.
instructions: Step forward into a deep lunge with your right foot, placing both hands on the floor inside your front foot.
Rotate your torso to the right and reach your right arm toward the ceiling, holding for a breath.
Bring your hand back down, then straighten your front leg to stretch your hamstring, keeping your hands on the floor.
Return to the lunge position and step back to standing.
Repeat on the left side, alternating for 5-6 reps per side.
tips: Move through each position deliberately
Straighten the front leg for the hamstring component
Rotate through the thoracic spine, not the lumbar

name: Adductor
description: **Open the gates to your inner thighs and move without restriction.** **Muscles worked:** inner thighs (adductors). This targeted stretch lengthens the adductor muscles that run along your inner thigh. Tight adductors limit squat depth and lateral movement, so this stretch pays dividends across your entire training. **Pro tip:** Ease into the stretch gradually over 30 seconds — your adductors respond better to patient, sustained holds than aggressive pushing.
instructions: Stand with your feet wide apart, toes pointing slightly outward.
Shift your weight to one side, bending that knee while keeping the other leg straight.
Lower your hips until you feel a stretch along the inner thigh of the straight leg.
Hold for 20-30 seconds, breathing deeply.
Slowly return to center and repeat on the other side.
tips: Keep your back straight
Ease into the stretch
Hold 30-60 seconds

name: Adductor/Groin
description: **The seated butterfly's grounded cousin for deep groin relief.** **Muscles worked:** inner thighs (adductors). This groin-focused stretch targets the same muscles as the adductor stretch but from a different angle, reaching the deeper fibers closer to the pelvis. It's essential for anyone who runs, squats, or plays sports with lateral movement. **Pro tip:** Keep your back straight and hinge from the hips — rounding your spine shifts the stretch away from the groin and into your lower back.
instructions: Sit on the floor with the soles of your feet together and knees falling out to the sides.
Hold your feet with both hands and sit up tall.
Gently press your knees toward the floor using your elbows.
Lean forward slightly from the hips to deepen the stretch in your groin.
Hold for 30-60 seconds, breathing steadily and relaxing into the position.
tips: Move slowly into the stretch
Keep breathing throughout
Hold 30-60 seconds per side

name: Ankle On The Knee
description: **Cross one ankle over the opposite knee and unlock your deep glute muscles.** **Muscles worked:** glutes (especially the deep external rotators). This figure-four position targets the piriformis and deep glute muscles that are notoriously tight from sitting. It's one of the best stretches for relieving sciatic-type tightness and improving hip mobility. **Pro tip:** Pull your bottom knee closer to your chest to intensify the stretch — even a small change in angle makes a big difference in the deep glutes.
instructions: Lie on your back with both knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Cross your right ankle over your left knee, creating a figure-four shape.
Reach through and grab behind your left thigh with both hands.
Gently pull your left leg toward your chest until you feel a stretch deep in your right glute.
Hold for 20-30 seconds, then switch sides.
tips: Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
Breathe deeply and relax into the stretch
Don't force range of motion — ease into it progressively
Use this for warm-up or recovery, not as a hypertrophy stimulus

name: Band Hip Abduction
description:
**A resistance band exercise that targets the gluteus medius and minimus by driving the legs apart against band tension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

The ascending resistance profile means the movement becomes hardest at peak abduction, making it effective for building lateral hip strength and stability.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Place a looped resistance band around both legs just above the knees.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and chest upright.
Keeping your torso stable, push one leg outward against the band as far as possible.
Hold the end position for a one-count squeeze, focusing on the outer glute.
Return the leg slowly to the starting position under control.
Complete all reps on one side before switching, or alternate legs each rep.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Band Hip Adductions
description:
**An isolation movement using a resistance band to strengthen the inner thigh muscles by pulling the leg inward against elastic tension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: adductors. Secondary: none.

The ascending resistance curve provides increasing challenge as the adductors contract, offering a joint-friendly way to train this often-neglected muscle group.

**Pro tip:** Anchor the band at ankle height
instructions: Anchor a looped band to a sturdy low point at ankle height.
Stand sideways to the anchor and loop the band around the ankle closest to it.
Step far enough away to create moderate tension with your leg slightly abducted.
Brace your core and pull the banded leg across your body past the midline.
Squeeze the inner thigh at peak contraction for a brief pause.
Return slowly to the start position, resisting the band's pull throughout.
tips: Anchor the band at ankle height
Stand sideways and pull the leg across your body
Control the return — don't let the band snap back

name: Banded Clamshell
description:
**A side-lying glute activation drill that uses a resistance band to load hip external rotation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

By keeping the feet together and rotating the top knee upward against band tension, this exercise isolates the gluteus medius and deep external rotators with minimal joint stress.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Place a resistance band around both legs just above the knees.
Lie on your side with hips stacked, knees bent to roughly 90 degrees, and feet together.
Rest your head on your bottom arm and place your top hand on your hip for stability.
Keeping your feet pressed together, rotate your top knee upward as high as possible without letting your pelvis roll backward.
Squeeze the glute hard at the top for a one-second hold.
Lower the knee back down with control and repeat for all reps before switching sides.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Banded Hip Thrust
description:
**A compound glute builder that uses a resistance band draped across the hips to load the hip thrust pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

The ascending band tension peaks at lockout where the glutes are maximally shortened, creating an excellent effort-to-results ratio with minimal spinal loading.

**Pro tip:** Focus on the lockout — don't just lift the weight, actively contract the glutes at the top
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back resting against a stable bench and loop a resistance band across your hips, anchoring each end under your feet.
Plant your feet flat on the floor roughly shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward.
Drive through your heels and thrust your hips upward until your torso is parallel to the floor.
At the top, squeeze your glutes forcefully and hold for a one-count, keeping your ribs down and chin tucked.
Lower your hips back toward the floor in a controlled two-to-three-second descent.
Reset your brace at the bottom and repeat.
tips: Focus on the lockout — don't just lift the weight, actively contract the glutes at the top
Posterior pelvic tilt at the top maximizes glute activation
Avoid pushing your hips too high — hyperextension shifts load to the lower back
Use a pad or towel on the bar for comfort

name: Banded Lateral Walk
description:
**A dynamic glute activation exercise where you step sideways against the resistance of a looped band.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

This movement trains the gluteus medius through repeated hip abduction under constant tension, making it a staple warm-up and accessory drill for hip stability.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Place a looped resistance band around both legs just above the ankles or below the knees.
Stand in a quarter-squat position with feet hip-width apart and tension already on the band.
Step laterally with the lead leg, pushing against the band to create a wide stance.
Follow with the trailing leg, returning to hip-width stance without letting the band go slack.
Maintain the low athletic position throughout, keeping your weight centered over your midfoot.
Complete all steps in one direction, then reverse to return.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Banded Squat Walk
description:
**A compound movement combining a partial squat hold with forward or backward walking against band resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads.

This exercise challenges the glutes to maintain hip abduction tension while the quads work to sustain the squat position, building functional lower-body endurance.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Loop a resistance band around both legs just above the knees.
Drop into a quarter-to-half squat with your chest up and core braced.
Step forward at a diagonal with one foot, pressing the knee outward against the band.
Follow with the opposite foot, maintaining the squat depth and band tension throughout.
Continue walking forward for the prescribed distance or reps, then reverse direction.
Never let your knees cave inward or allow your torso to rise out of the squat.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Barbell Glute Bridge
description:
**A floor-based hip extension exercise performed with a loaded barbell across the hips.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

Because the back stays on the ground, the range of motion is shorter than a hip thrust, but it allows heavy loading with a straightforward setup and strong glute activation at lockout.

**Pro tip:** Focus on the squeeze at the top — this is where the glutes are strongest
instructions: Sit on the floor with legs extended and roll a padded barbell over your thighs to rest in the hip crease.
Lie back flat, bend your knees, and plant your feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart.
Grip the barbell with both hands to keep it stable.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips upward until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees.
Squeeze the glutes hard at the top for a full second.
Lower the hips back to the floor under control and repeat without bouncing.
tips: Focus on the squeeze at the top — this is where the glutes are strongest
Don't let your ribs flare — maintain a neutral spine
For single-leg variants, keep your hips level throughout

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
description:
**The gold-standard barbell glute exercise, performed with the upper back elevated on a bench to allow a greater range of hip extension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

Heavy loads can be used safely because the spine remains neutral, and the shortened loading position delivers peak tension where the glutes are fully contracted.

**Pro tip:** Focus on the lockout — don't just lift the weight, actively contract the glutes at the top
instructions: Sit on the floor with your shoulder blades resting against the long edge of a flat bench and roll a padded barbell into your hip crease.
Plant your feet flat on the floor, roughly shoulder-width apart, with shins vertical at the top of the movement.
Grip the bar with both hands for stability, tuck your chin, and brace your core.
Drive through your heels to thrust the bar upward until your hips are fully extended and your torso is parallel to the floor.
Hold the lockout for a deliberate squeeze, keeping your ribs down to avoid hyperextending the lower back.
Lower your hips in a controlled manner until they nearly touch the floor, then drive up again.
tips: Focus on the lockout — don't just lift the weight, actively contract the glutes at the top
Posterior pelvic tilt at the top maximizes glute activation
Avoid pushing your hips too high — hyperextension shifts load to the lower back
Use a pad or towel on the bar for comfort

name: Barbell Sumo Squat
description:
**A wide-stance barbell squat variation that shifts emphasis toward the glutes and adductors compared to a conventional squat.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, adductors, hamstrings.

The bell-shaped strength curve means the mid-range is most challenging, and the wider foot placement allows a more upright torso, reducing lower-back demands.

**Pro tip:** Adding bands or chains shifts peak force toward lockout where glutes have better leverage
instructions: Set up under a barbell in a squat rack and position it across your upper traps or rear delts.
Step out and adopt a stance roughly 1.5 times shoulder width with toes turned out 30 to 45 degrees.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades together, and initiate the descent by pushing your hips back and knees outward.
Squat down until your hip crease drops to at least knee level, keeping your knees tracking over your toes.
Drive upward through the full foot, pressing the knees out and squeezing the glutes as you approach lockout.
Stand fully tall at the top, reset your brace, and begin the next rep.
tips: Adding bands or chains shifts peak force toward lockout where glutes have better leverage
Drive your knees out to increase glute recruitment
Focus on the drive out of the hole — glutes contribute most from mid-range to lockout
Note: at deep hip flexion, adductor magnus has the longest moment arm for hip extension, not glutes

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
description:
**A unilateral squat variation with the rear foot elevated on a bench, loading the front leg through a deep range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs.

The stretched position at the bottom creates high mechanical tension on the quads and glutes, giving this exercise strong hypertrophy potential while also challenging balance and core stability.

**Pro tip:** Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand and stand about two feet in front of a knee-height bench.
Reach one foot back and place the top of that foot or your toes on the bench.
Keep your torso upright with your core braced and shoulders back.
Lower your body by bending the front knee until your rear knee nearly touches the floor and your front thigh is at or below parallel.
Drive through the heel and midfoot of the front leg to return to the top position.
Complete all prescribed reps on one leg before switching sides.
tips: Take a step length that allows your front knee to track over your toes at the bottom
Lower until your rear knee nearly touches the ground for full quad ROM
Keep your torso upright to maintain quad dominance on the front leg
Push through the front foot to drive back up, focusing on quad contraction
coaching_cues: Drop the back knee straight down

name: Butt Lift (Bridge)
description:
**A beginner-friendly bodyweight bridge that trains hip extension by lifting the hips off the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

The descending resistance profile means the movement is easiest at the top where the glutes are most active, making it an accessible entry point for glute training and a useful high-rep finisher.

**Pro tip:** Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes, not by pushing through your lower back
instructions: Lie face-up on the floor with your knees bent and feet flat, positioned about hip-width apart.
Place your arms at your sides with palms pressing into the floor for stability.
Push through your heels to lift your hips off the ground until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees.
Squeeze your glutes tightly at the top and hold for one to two seconds.
Lower your hips back to the floor slowly, maintaining tension in the glutes throughout the descent.
Lightly touch the floor and immediately begin the next rep.
tips: Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes, not by pushing through your lower back
Hold the top position for 1-2 seconds to maximize shortened-position tension
Keep your core braced to prevent lumbar hyperextension
Feet flat, knees at roughly 90 degrees at the top

name: Cable Hip Abduction
description:
**A standing cable exercise that isolates the gluteus medius by sweeping the leg away from the body against constant cable tension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

The bell-shaped resistance curve provides meaningful loading through the mid-range, and the cable's line of pull keeps tension on the muscle throughout the entire rep.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Attach an ankle cuff to the low pulley of a cable machine and secure it around the ankle farthest from the machine.
Stand sideways to the cable stack, holding the frame for balance with the near hand.
Start with the working leg crossed slightly in front of the support leg to pre-stretch the glute.
Keeping your torso upright and hips level, sweep the working leg directly out to the side in a controlled arc.
Pause briefly at the top of the range without leaning your torso away.
Return the leg slowly to the starting position, resisting the cable on the way back.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Cable Kickback
description:
**A cable-based glute isolation exercise that extends the hip against a constant load.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

The flat strength curve of the cable provides even resistance throughout the entire range of motion, and the excellent effort-to-results ratio makes it ideal for accumulating quality glute volume without systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Focus on hip extension, not knee extension — drive the heel back
instructions: Attach an ankle cuff to the low pulley and strap it around one ankle.
Face the cable stack and hold the frame with both hands, hinging slightly forward at the hips.
Start with the working leg under your body with a slight knee bend.
Extend the hip to drive your foot straight back and upward, squeezing the glute at full extension.
Keep your lower back neutral throughout — the movement should come entirely from the hip joint.
Return the leg forward under control and repeat for all reps before switching sides.
tips: Focus on hip extension, not knee extension — drive the heel back
Keep your core tight to prevent rotation or lumbar extension
Control the eccentric — don't let the weight snap back
Squeeze at the top of each rep for peak glute contraction

name: Cable Pull Through Glute
description:
**A hip-hinge movement performed facing away from a low cable pulley, pulling the handle through the legs to full hip extension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors.

This exercise teaches proper hinge mechanics while loading the glutes and posterior chain, with the cable providing continuous tension that free weights cannot replicate in this pattern.

**Pro tip:** Hinge at the hips, not the lower back — maintain a neutral spine
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the lowest position and attach a rope handle.
Straddle the cable, facing away from the machine, and grab the rope between your legs with both hands.
Walk forward a few steps to create tension, then stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips by pushing them back, allowing the rope to travel between your legs while keeping your spine neutral and knees softly bent.
Once you feel a deep stretch in the hamstrings and glutes, drive your hips forward explosively to stand tall.
Squeeze the glutes hard at lockout, pause, then hinge back for the next rep.
tips: Hinge at the hips, not the lower back — maintain a neutral spine
Squeeze the glutes hard at the top to lock out
Keep the cable between your legs and stand far enough from the stack
Soft knee bend — this is a hip hinge, not a squat

name: Carioca Quick Step
description:
**A dynamic lateral movement drill that involves crossing one foot over and behind the other in a rapid grapevine pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, calves, abs, adductors.

This exercise develops hip mobility, coordination, and multi-planar glute activation while also serving as an effective warm-up for athletic training sessions.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Stand in an athletic position with feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent.
Begin moving laterally by crossing your trailing foot in front of the lead foot.
Step the lead foot out to continue moving in the same direction.
Now cross the trailing foot behind the lead foot, alternating front and back crosses rapidly.
Keep your hips low, arms relaxed and moving naturally for balance, and stay light on the balls of your feet.
Travel the prescribed distance, then reverse direction and lead with the opposite side.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Curtsy Lunge Glute
description:
**A lunge variation where the rear leg crosses behind the front leg in a curtsy-like motion, placing the glutes under a unique diagonal stretch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, adductors, hamstrings.

This crossover pattern increases gluteus medius and adductor engagement compared to a standard lunge, making it a valuable accessory for well-rounded hip development.

**Pro tip:** Take a long enough step to feel a stretch in the trailing hip flexor
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand with feet hip-width apart.
Step one foot diagonally behind and across the other leg, as if performing a curtsy.
Bend both knees and lower your hips until the front thigh is roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep your torso upright and your front knee tracking over your toes throughout the descent.
Press through the heel of the front foot to drive back up to the starting position.
Alternate legs each rep or complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Take a long enough step to feel a stretch in the trailing hip flexor
Drive up through the front heel to maximize glute engagement
Keep your torso upright or slightly forward-leaning
Control the descent — don't drop into the bottom position

name: Donkey Kick
description:
**A kneeling glute isolation exercise where you drive one bent leg upward toward the ceiling.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

The flat resistance profile of bodyweight keeps tension consistent through the range, and the excellent effort-to-results ratio allows high-volume glute work without taxing the nervous system.

**Pro tip:** Focus on hip extension, not knee extension — drive the heel back
instructions: Start on all fours with your hands directly under your shoulders and knees under your hips.
Keep one knee bent at 90 degrees and lift that leg upward, driving the sole of your foot toward the ceiling.
Raise the leg until your thigh is in line with your torso — avoid arching your lower back beyond this point.
Squeeze the glute hard at the top for a one-second hold.
Lower the knee back toward the floor with control, stopping just before it touches.
Complete all reps on one side, then switch legs.
tips: Focus on hip extension, not knee extension — drive the heel back
Keep your core tight to prevent rotation or lumbar extension
Control the eccentric — don't let the weight snap back
Squeeze at the top of each rep for peak glute contraction

name: Downward Facing Balance
description:
**A stability-focused bodyweight exercise performed in a prone position where one leg is extended and held behind the body while maintaining balance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, abs.

This movement challenges the glutes isometrically and dynamically while demanding core engagement to keep the body aligned.

**Pro tip:** Use a controlled tempo — momentum defeats the purpose of isolation
instructions: Begin on all fours with a flat back, hands under shoulders and knees under hips.
Extend one leg straight back until it is in line with your torso, toes pointed down.
Simultaneously extend the opposite arm forward for counterbalance.
Hold this position, engaging the glute of the extended leg and bracing your core to prevent rotation.
Maintain the hold for the prescribed duration or perform slow, controlled pulses with the extended leg.
Return to all fours and repeat on the opposite side.
tips: Use a controlled tempo — momentum defeats the purpose of isolation
Keep the working leg's knee at a fixed angle throughout
Avoid arching your lower back to get more range of motion

name: Dumbbell Bulgarian Split Squat
description:
**A dumbbell-loaded unilateral squat with the rear foot elevated, combining deep quad and glute stretch at the bottom with ascending resistance as you stand.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, adductors, abs.

The dumbbells lower the center of gravity compared to a barbell version, improving balance while still delivering high growth from the stretched position potential.

**Pro tip:** Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
instructions: Grasp a dumbbell in each hand and stand roughly two feet in front of a bench.
Place the top of your rear foot on the bench behind you, keeping most of your weight on the front leg.
Descend by bending the front knee and hip, lowering until your rear knee nearly grazes the floor.
Keep your front shin relatively vertical and your torso upright throughout the movement.
Push forcefully through the front heel to stand back up, fully extending the hip and knee.
Finish all reps on one leg before switching to the other side.
tips: Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to maximize eccentric quad loading
Avoid letting your front knee collapse inward — track it over your second toe
A shorter step length increases quad demand; a longer step shifts to glutes
Keep your weight centered over the front leg throughout the movement
coaching_cues: Drive up from the front leg

name: Dumbbell Hip Thrust
description:
**A bench-supported hip thrust using a single dumbbell held across the hips for resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

This variation is more accessible than the barbell version for home or crowded gym settings while still delivering excellent glute stimulus at the shortened position with minimal systemic fatigue.

**Pro tip:** Squeeze hard at the top for a full second — this is where glutes have their best leverage
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a stable bench and place a dumbbell vertically on your hip crease, holding it with both hands.
Set your feet flat on the floor about shoulder-width apart with knees bent.
Brace your core and drive your hips upward by pressing through your heels until your torso is parallel to the floor.
At the top, squeeze the glutes maximally for a full second, keeping your chin tucked and ribs down.
Lower your hips in a slow, controlled descent until they hover just above the floor.
Immediately drive back up into the next rep.
tips: Squeeze hard at the top for a full second — this is where glutes have their best leverage
Keep your chin tucked and ribs down to prevent lumbar hyperextension
Drive through your heels, not your toes
Position the bar in your hip crease, not on your stomach
Feet placement: shins roughly vertical at the top position

name: Fire Hydrant
description:
**A kneeling hip abduction and external rotation exercise that isolates the gluteus medius and deep hip rotators.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

Named for its resemblance to a dog at a fire hydrant, this movement is a low-impact, high-activation drill that builds lateral hip strength without any equipment.

**Pro tip:** Focus on hip extension, not knee extension — drive the heel back
instructions: Start on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips, back flat.
Keeping the knee bent at 90 degrees, lift one leg out to the side as high as you can without shifting your hips.
Your thigh should move directly to the side — imagine opening a gate with your knee.
Squeeze the outer glute at the top and hold for a brief pause.
Lower the leg back to the starting position with control, stopping just before the knee touches down.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Focus on hip extension, not knee extension — drive the heel back
Keep your core tight to prevent rotation or lumbar extension
Control the eccentric — don't let the weight snap back
Squeeze at the top of each rep for peak glute contraction

name: Flutter Kicks
description:
**A supine core exercise where the legs alternate in small, rapid up-and-down kicks while held off the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: quads, glutes.

This movement primarily challenges the lower abdominals to maintain pelvic stability while the hip flexors, quads, and glutes work to control the kicking motion.

**Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your legs extended and hands placed under your glutes or at your sides for support.
Press your lower back firmly into the floor by engaging your abs.
Lift both feet a few inches off the ground.
Alternate kicking each leg up and down in a small, controlled range — roughly six to twelve inches of travel.
Keep the movements quick but deliberate, breathing steadily throughout.
Continue for the prescribed time or rep count without letting your feet touch the floor.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Frog Pump
description:
**A glute bridge variation performed with the soles of the feet pressed together and knees flared outward in a butterfly position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

This foot placement externally rotates the hips and reduces hamstring involvement, directing nearly all of the work into the glutes for a powerful isolation pump.

**Pro tip:** Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes, not by pushing through your lower back
instructions: Lie on your back and bring the soles of your feet together, letting your knees fall open to the sides.
Position your heels as close to your glutes as comfortable.
Place your arms flat on the floor at your sides for stability.
Drive your hips upward by squeezing your glutes, pressing through the outer edges of your feet.
Lift until your hips are fully extended and hold the contraction for a beat.
Lower back down with control and repeat at a steady tempo for high reps.
tips: Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes, not by pushing through your lower back
Hold the top position for 1-2 seconds to maximize shortened-position tension
Keep your core braced to prevent lumbar hyperextension
Feet flat, knees at roughly 90 degrees at the top

name: Glute Bridge
description:
**The foundational bodyweight hip extension exercise performed lying on the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

By driving the hips upward into full extension, the glutes contract forcefully at the shortened position. Its simplicity and zero equipment requirement make it a versatile movement for warm-ups, rehab, and high-rep glute finishers.

**Pro tip:** Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes, not by pushing through your lower back
instructions: Lie face-up with knees bent, feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart, and arms resting at your sides.
Engage your core and press your lower back gently into the floor.
Push through your heels to lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees.
Contract the glutes as hard as possible at the top and hold for one to two seconds.
Lower your hips back to the floor in a slow, controlled manner.
Tap the floor lightly and drive into the next rep without fully relaxing at the bottom.
tips: Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes, not by pushing through your lower back
Hold the top position for 1-2 seconds to maximize shortened-position tension
Keep your core braced to prevent lumbar hyperextension
Feet flat, knees at roughly 90 degrees at the top

name: Glute Kickback
description:
**A bodyweight hip extension exercise performed from a kneeling or standing position, driving the leg straight back to engage the gluteus maximus.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

The flat resistance profile provides consistent tension throughout the rep, and the excellent effort-to-results ratio makes it ideal for high-volume glute training without recovery cost.

**Pro tip:** Use a controlled tempo — momentum defeats the purpose of isolation
instructions: Start on all fours with your hands under your shoulders and knees under your hips.
Extend one leg straight back, keeping it in line with your torso.
Lift the extended leg upward by squeezing the glute, raising the foot toward the ceiling.
Keep your hips square to the floor and avoid rotating or arching your lower back.
Hold the top position for a brief squeeze, then lower the leg back to hip height.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other leg.
tips: Use a controlled tempo — momentum defeats the purpose of isolation
Keep the working leg's knee at a fixed angle throughout
Avoid arching your lower back to get more range of motion

name: Groin and Back Stretch
description:
**A seated flexibility drill that simultaneously opens the inner thighs and decompresses the lower back.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: adductors. Secondary: erectors.

By folding forward over spread legs, you place the adductors under a deep passive stretch while gently lengthening the spinal erectors, making it an efficient two-for-one mobility exercise.

**Pro tip:** Move slowly between positions
instructions: Sit on the floor with your legs spread wide apart and toes pointing upward.
Keep your back as straight as possible and slowly hinge forward at the hips, reaching your hands toward the floor in front of you.
Hold the deepest comfortable position for 20-30 seconds, breathing deeply and allowing gravity to deepen the stretch with each exhale.
Avoid rounding your upper back excessively.
Return to the upright position slowly and repeat for the desired number of sets.
tips: Move slowly between positions
Hold each stretch 30-60 seconds
Don't force the range

name: Groiners
description:
**A dynamic bodyweight movement that combines a deep lunge with an adductor stretch, performed from a push-up position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: adductors. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings.

This drill actively mobilizes the groin while engaging the glutes and hamstrings through repeated stepping motions, serving as an effective warm-up or mobility filler.

**Pro tip:** Step the foot all the way to the hand
instructions: Begin in a push-up position with your arms fully extended and body in a straight line.
Step your right foot forward and place it flat on the floor just outside your right hand.
Sink your hips down toward the ground to feel a deep stretch through the left groin and inner thigh.
Hold briefly, then return the right foot to the starting position and repeat on the left side.
Alternate sides in a controlled, rhythmic fashion for the prescribed number of repetitions, keeping your core braced throughout.
tips: Step the foot all the way to the hand
Keep the back leg straight
Alternate sides fluidly

name: Hip Circles (prone)
description:
**A controlled rotational movement performed on all fours that targets the gluteal muscles through circular hip motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

This exercise improves hip joint mobility and activates the deep stabilizers of the hip, making it a staple in warm-up routines and prehabilitation programs.

**Pro tip:** Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
instructions: Position yourself on all fours with your hands directly under your shoulders and knees under your hips.
Keeping your knee bent at 90 degrees, lift your right leg out to the side and begin drawing large circles with your knee.
Move forward, up, back, and down in a smooth circular path.
Perform all prescribed repetitions in one direction, then reverse the circle.
Switch to the left leg and repeat.
Maintain a stable torso and avoid shifting your weight excessively to the supporting side.
tips: Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
Breathe deeply and relax into the stretch
Don't force range of motion — ease into it progressively
Use this for warm-up or recovery, not as a hypertrophy stimulus

name: Hip Extension with Bands
description:
**A standing or quadruped glute isolation exercise using a resistance band to load hip extension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

The ascending resistance profile means tension increases as the glutes approach peak contraction, providing a strong squeeze at the top of each rep while keeping joint stress minimal.

**Pro tip:** Focus on controlled hip extension through the available range
instructions: Anchor a looped resistance band to a low, sturdy object and loop the other end around one ankle.
Stand facing the anchor point and hold onto something for balance.
Keeping your working leg straight or with a slight bend, drive it directly behind you by squeezing your glute.
Pause at full extension for a one-count, then return slowly to the start.
Avoid arching your lower back—focus on moving only at the hip joint.
Complete all reps on one side before switching legs.
tips: Focus on controlled hip extension through the available range
Squeeze the glutes at the point of peak contraction
Maintain a neutral spine throughout

name: Hip Lift with Band
description:
**A supine glute bridge variation with a resistance band draped across the hips to add external load at lockout.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings.

The band's ascending tension profile maximally challenges the glutes in their shortened position, reinforcing a powerful hip lockout and strong glute contraction at the top.

**Pro tip:** Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes, not by pushing through your lower back
instructions: Lie face-up on the floor with your knees bent and feet flat, hip-width apart.
Loop a resistance band over your hips and pin both ends to the ground under your hands or feet.
Drive through your heels and thrust your hips toward the ceiling until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees.
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top and hold for one to two seconds.
Lower your hips back down under control and repeat.
Keep your ribs down and avoid hyperextending your lower back at the top.
tips: Drive your hips up by squeezing your glutes, not by pushing through your lower back
Hold the top position for 1-2 seconds to maximize shortened-position tension
Keep your core braced to prevent lumbar hyperextension
Feet flat, knees at roughly 90 degrees at the top

name: Iliotibial Tract-SMR
description:
**A self-muscle and tissue release technique using a foam roller to address tightness and adhesions along the iliotibial band on the outer thigh.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

By applying sustained pressure and slow rolling, this drill helps restore tissue quality around the lateral hip and knee, reducing discomfort associated with IT band syndrome.

**Pro tip:** Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
instructions: Lie on your side with a foam roller positioned just below your hip bone on the outer thigh.
Stack or stagger your legs and support your upper body with your forearm.
Slowly roll along the outside of your thigh from just below the hip to just above the knee.
Pause on any particularly tender spots for 20-30 seconds, allowing the pressure to release the tissue.
Control the pressure by adjusting how much body weight you place on the roller.
Spend 60-90 seconds per side.
tips: Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
Breathe deeply and relax into the stretch
Don't force range of motion — ease into it progressively
Use this for warm-up or recovery, not as a hypertrophy stimulus

name: IT Band and Glute Stretch
description:
**A standing or seated stretch that targets the iliotibial band and the outer gluteal muscles through cross-body leg positioning.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

This movement alleviates lateral hip tightness and improves frontal-plane mobility, benefiting anyone who sits for extended periods or performs repetitive lower-body movements.

**Pro tip:** Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
instructions: Stand upright and cross your right leg behind your left.
Keeping both feet on the ground, lean your torso to the left while pushing your right hip outward until you feel a stretch along the outer right hip and thigh.
Hold for 20-30 seconds while breathing steadily.
For a deeper variation, sit on the floor, cross one ankle over the opposite knee, and gently press the raised knee away from your body.
Perform on both sides for equal duration.
tips: Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
Breathe deeply and relax into the stretch
Don't force range of motion — ease into it progressively
Use this for warm-up or recovery, not as a hypertrophy stimulus

name: Kettlebell Sumo Deadlift
description:
**A wide-stance deadlift performed with a kettlebell held between the legs, emphasizing the glutes and adductors while recruiting the entire posterior chain.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, erectors, adductors, forearms, traps.

The sumo stance shortens the range of motion compared to conventional pulls and allows a more upright torso, making it accessible for lifters developing hip hinge mechanics.

**Pro tip:** Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
instructions: Stand with your feet significantly wider than shoulder-width and toes turned out 30-45 degrees.
Place a kettlebell on the floor between your feet.
Push your hips back and bend your knees to grip the kettlebell handle with both hands.
Brace your core, flatten your back, and drive through your heels to stand up, extending your hips and knees simultaneously.
Squeeze your glutes at the top.
Reverse the movement by hinging at the hips and bending the knees to return the kettlebell to the floor.
tips: Brace your core hard before each rep — this protects the spine and improves force transfer
Think of pushing the floor away rather than pulling the bar up
Keep the bar close to your body throughout the lift
Lock out with the glutes, not by leaning back
Glutes contribute most from mid-shin to lockout — focus on squeezing through that range

name: Kettlebell Swing Glute
description:
**A ballistic hip-hinge movement using a kettlebell that trains explosive glute contraction through a powerful hip snap.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, erectors, lats, shoulders, forearms.

The bell-shaped resistance profile demands rapid force production at the midpoint while the shortened loading position ensures the glutes are maximally engaged at the top of each swing.

**Pro tip:** Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
instructions: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, kettlebell on the floor about a foot in front of you.
Hinge at the hips, grip the handle with both hands, and hike the bell back between your legs.
Explosively drive your hips forward, squeezing your glutes to propel the kettlebell to chest height.
Let the bell float momentarily at the top, then guide it back between your legs by hinging at the hips.
Keep your arms relaxed—the power comes entirely from the hip snap.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout every rep.
tips: Power comes from the hip snap, not the arms — arms are just along for the ride
Hike the bell back between your legs like a football snap
Stand tall and squeeze the glutes hard at the top
Don't squat the swing — it's a hinge pattern

name: Knee Across The Body
description:
**A supine stretch that targets the deep external rotators of the hip, particularly the piriformis and gluteus medius.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: none.

By drawing one knee across the body, you create a rotational stretch through the outer hip that relieves tightness from prolonged sitting or heavy lower-body training.

**Pro tip:** Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
instructions: Lie flat on your back with both legs extended.
Bend your right knee and use your left hand to gently pull it across your body toward the floor on your left side.
Keep your right shoulder pinned to the ground and extend your right arm out for stability.
Hold the stretch for 20-30 seconds, breathing deeply to allow the hip to release further.
Return to the starting position and repeat on the opposite side.
Avoid forcing the knee down—let gravity and relaxation do the work.
tips: Hold each position for 30-60 seconds for static stretches
Breathe deeply and relax into the stretch
Don't force range of motion — ease into it progressively
Use this for warm-up or recovery, not as a hypertrophy stimulus

name: Kneeling Jump Squat
description:
**An explosive movement starting from a kneeling position where you generate enough force through the glutes and quads to jump onto your feet.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, calves.

When loaded with a barbell, this exercise develops powerful hip extension and rate of force development, making it a potent tool for athletes seeking explosive lower-body strength.

**Pro tip:** Adding bands or chains shifts peak force toward lockout where glutes have better leverage
instructions: Kneel on a padded surface with a barbell across your upper back in a high-bar position.
Sit your hips back onto your heels.
In one explosive motion, drive your hips forward and swing your feet underneath you to land in a squat stance.
Absorb the landing softly with bent knees.
Stand fully, then carefully return to the kneeling position and reset.
Start with light weight or bodyweight only until the movement pattern is comfortable.
tips: Adding bands or chains shifts peak force toward lockout where glutes have better leverage
Drive your knees out to increase glute recruitment
Focus on the drive out of the hole — glutes contribute most from mid-range to lockout
Note: at deep hip flexion, adductor magnus has the longest moment arm for hip extension, not glutes

name: Kneeling Squat
description:
**A barbell squat variation performed entirely from the knees that isolates the hip extensors by removing ankle and knee-dominant contributions.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, abs, erectors.

This forces the glutes to serve as the primary mover through a controlled sit-back-and-drive pattern, making it an effective glute-focused accessory lift.

**Pro tip:** Adding bands or chains shifts peak force toward lockout where glutes have better leverage
instructions: Kneel on a padded mat with a barbell positioned across your upper traps.
Your knees should be hip-width apart and the tops of your feet flat on the floor.
Brace your core and slowly sit your hips back toward your heels, maintaining an upright torso.
Descend until your glutes lightly touch your calves, then drive your hips forward powerfully to return to the tall kneeling position.
Squeeze your glutes at the top and avoid hyperextending your lower back.
Use moderate loads and controlled tempos.
tips: Adding bands or chains shifts peak force toward lockout where glutes have better leverage
Drive your knees out to increase glute recruitment
Focus on the drive out of the hole — glutes contribute most from mid-range to lockout
Note: at deep hip flexion, adductor magnus has the longest moment arm for hip extension, not glutes

name: Lateral Bound
description:
**A plyometric exercise involving powerful single-leg jumps from side to side that develops lateral power, hip stability, and frontal-plane athleticism.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

Each landing demands eccentric control from the glutes and adductors, training deceleration ability alongside explosive push-off strength.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Stand on your right foot with a slight bend in the knee and your left foot off the ground.
Push off laterally to the left, driving through the right glute and leg.
Land softly on your left foot, absorbing the impact by bending the knee and sinking into a quarter squat.
Stabilize briefly, then bound back to the right.
Swing your arms naturally to generate momentum.
Focus on sticking each landing with control before initiating the next bound.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Lateral Box Jump
description:
**A plyometric drill where you jump sideways onto a box, training lateral explosiveness and hip abductor power.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

This movement challenges the glutes to produce force in the frontal plane while the box provides a measurable height target that encourages progressive overload through box selection.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Stand to the side of a sturdy box or platform at a height appropriate for your ability.
Position yourself about arm's length away with feet hip-width apart.
Bend your knees, swing your arms, and jump laterally onto the box, landing with both feet simultaneously.
Land softly with knees slightly bent and hips back.
Stand tall on the box, then step down carefully on the opposite side.
Turn to face the box and repeat in the other direction.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Lateral Cone Hops
description:
**A fast-paced agility drill involving rapid lateral hops over cones that builds reactive strength, coordination, and lateral quickness.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, calves, adductors.

The glutes and adductors work continuously to stabilize and propel the body side to side, making this an excellent conditioning and athletic development exercise.

**Pro tip:** Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
instructions: Set up a line of small cones spaced about two feet apart.
Stand to one side of the first cone with feet together and knees slightly bent.
Hop laterally over each cone in succession, landing lightly on the balls of your feet.
Keep your hops quick and rhythmic, minimizing ground contact time.
Once you reach the end, immediately reverse direction and hop back.
Maintain a low athletic stance with your chest up and arms ready for balance.
tips: Focus on pushing the knee out, not rotating the foot
Keep your pelvis stable — don't let it shift or rotate
Control the return — don't let the weight slam back
Maintain an upright torso for seated variations

name: Leg Lift
description:
