Pause at the top, then lower slowly and repeat for the target rep count.
tips: Lower into full dorsiflexion at the bottom — this is where the gastroc produces meaningful tension
Pause briefly at the bottom stretch before driving up
Keep your knees straight to maintain gastrocnemius length

name: Standing Calf Raise
description:
**The classic standing calf raise performed on a dedicated machine with shoulder pads.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: calves. Secondary: none.

With legs straight, the gastrocnemius is the primary mover, and the machine's guided path lets you push heavy loads safely through a full range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Lower into full dorsiflexion at the bottom — this is where the gastroc produces meaningful tension
instructions: Step onto the machine platform with the balls of your feet on the edge and your shoulders under the pads.
Stand up to release the safety stops, keeping your legs straight and core tight.
Allow your heels to sink below the platform for a complete stretch at the bottom.
Drive upward through the balls of your feet until you reach full plantar flexion.
Squeeze the calves at the peak for a brief hold.
Lower under control and repeat, engaging the safety stops only when the set is finished.
tips: Lower into full dorsiflexion at the bottom — this is where the gastroc produces meaningful tension
Pause briefly at the bottom stretch before driving up
Keep your knees straight to maintain gastrocnemius length

name: Standing Calf Raises
description:
**A machine-based standing calf exercise that isolates the gastrocnemius and soleus through vertical plantar flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: calves. Secondary: none.

The fixed movement path removes balance as a limiting factor, allowing you to train closer to true muscular failure for maximum calf stimulation.

**Pro tip:** Lower into full dorsiflexion at the bottom — this is where the gastroc produces meaningful tension
instructions: Position yourself in the standing calf raise machine with shoulders under the pads and the balls of your feet on the foot plate.
Straighten your legs fully and disengage the safety mechanism.
Drop your heels as low as the platform allows, pausing briefly in the stretched position.
Push explosively through the balls of your feet to rise onto your toes.
Hold the contracted position at the top for one to two seconds.
Lower slowly back to the stretch and continue for the desired number of reps.
tips: Lower into full dorsiflexion at the bottom — this is where the gastroc produces meaningful tension
Pause briefly at the bottom stretch before driving up
Keep your knees straight to maintain gastrocnemius length

name: Standing Dumbbell Calf Raise
description:
**A standing calf raise using dumbbells held at the sides for resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: calves. Secondary: none.

This accessible variation can be performed almost anywhere and allows for easy load adjustments. The standing position with straight legs prioritizes the gastrocnemius muscle.

**Pro tip:** Lower into full dorsiflexion at the bottom — this is where the gastroc produces meaningful tension
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides and stand with the balls of your feet on a raised surface, heels hanging off.
Use a wall or sturdy object nearby for light balance support if needed.
Keep your legs straight and lower your heels below the platform into a deep stretch.
Press through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible.
Contract the calves firmly at the top of the movement.
Lower with a controlled two-to-three-second tempo and repeat.
tips: Lower into full dorsiflexion at the bottom — this is where the gastroc produces meaningful tension
Pause briefly at the bottom stretch before driving up
Keep your knees straight to maintain gastrocnemius length

name: Standing Gastrocnemius Calf Stretch
description:
**A static stretch targeting the gastrocnemius, the larger superficial calf muscle.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: calves. Secondary: none.

Performed with a straight back leg, this stretch improves ankle dorsiflexion range of motion and is valuable for recovery, warm-up preparation, and maintaining healthy calf tissue quality.

**Pro tip:** Hold stretches for 30-60 seconds
instructions: Stand facing a wall and place both hands on it at shoulder height.
Step one foot back about two to three feet, keeping that leg completely straight.
Bend your front knee and lean your hips forward toward the wall.
Keep the rear heel pressed firmly into the ground until you feel a stretch in the upper calf.
Hold the stretch for 20 to 30 seconds while breathing steadily.
Switch legs and repeat on the other side.
tips: Hold stretches for 30-60 seconds
For SMR, spend extra time on tender spots
Perform before or after calf training

name: Standing Soleus And Achilles Stretch
description:
**A standing stretch that targets the soleus and Achilles tendon by using a bent-knee position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: calves. Secondary: none.

Bending the knee slackens the gastrocnemius and shifts the stretch deeper into the soleus and the Achilles complex, promoting flexibility in structures critical for walking, running, and squatting.

**Pro tip:** Hold stretches for 30-60 seconds
instructions: Stand facing a wall with hands placed against it for support.
Step one foot back roughly 12 to 18 inches behind the other.
Bend both knees, sinking your hips slightly downward and forward.
Keep the rear heel flat on the ground and focus the stretch on the lower calf and Achilles area.
Hold for 20 to 30 seconds, breathing deeply and relaxing into the stretch.
Switch sides and repeat.
tips: Hold stretches for 30-60 seconds
For SMR, spend extra time on tender spots
Perform before or after calf training

name: Tibialis Raise
description:
**An exercise that isolates the tibialis anterior, the muscle running along the front of the shin.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: calves. Secondary: none.

Regularly training this muscle builds ankle resilience, reduces shin splint risk, and balances the strength ratio between the front and back of the lower leg.

**Pro tip:** Pull your toes toward your shins with control
instructions: Stand with your back against a wall and your feet about 12 inches in front of you.
Keep your heels on the ground and your legs straight or with a very slight knee bend.
Lift your toes and the balls of your feet off the ground as high as possible by dorsiflexing at the ankles.
Squeeze the muscles on the front of your shins at the top of the movement.
Lower your toes back toward the ground slowly and with control.
Repeat for the target rep range, typically 15 to 25 reps.
tips: Pull your toes toward your shins with control
Use full range of motion
This is prehab work — keep it light and controlled

name: 3/4 Sit-Up
description:
**A partial-range sit-up where you rise approximately three-quarters of the way up rather than completing the full movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

By stopping short of the top, you maintain constant tension on the abs and reduce hip flexor involvement that typically dominates the upper portion of a full sit-up.

**Pro tip:** Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hands placed lightly behind your head or crossed over your chest.
Brace your core and begin curling your torso off the ground, leading with your chest.
Rise until your torso reaches roughly a 45-degree angle with the floor — about three-quarters of a full sit-up.
Pause briefly at this point, keeping the abs engaged.
Lower yourself back down under control without letting your shoulder blades fully relax on the floor between reps.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Ab Crunch Machine
description:
**A machine-based abdominal exercise that provides adjustable, consistent resistance through the crunch pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The guided path isolates the abs effectively and allows for progressive overload with precise weight increments, making it one of the most efficient tools for direct ab hypertrophy.

**Pro tip:** Focus on curling your spine, not hinging at the hips
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the pivot point of the machine aligns roughly with your mid-torso.
Sit down and place your feet under the foot pads and grip the handles or place your chest against the pad, depending on the machine design.
Select an appropriate weight on the stack.
Crunch forward by flexing your spine, drawing your ribcage toward your pelvis.
Squeeze the abs hard at the bottom of the crunch.
Return slowly to the starting position without letting the weight stack touch down, and repeat.
tips: Focus on curling your spine, not hinging at the hips
Adjust the pad so resistance is applied to your upper chest
Control the eccentric — do not let the weight stack slam

name: Ab Roller
description:
**A challenging anti-collapse core exercise using a small wheel with handles.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, lats, triceps.

As you roll forward, the abs must resist spinal extension under increasing leverage, creating intense eccentric loading. The movement also demands significant shoulder and lat engagement to pull the wheel back.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
instructions: Kneel on a mat and grip the ab roller handles with both hands, positioning it on the floor directly beneath your shoulders.
Brace your core and tuck your pelvis slightly to prevent lower back arching.
Slowly roll the wheel forward, extending your arms and lowering your torso toward the ground.
Extend as far as you can while maintaining a rigid torso — do not let your hips sag or lower back hyperextend.
Pull the wheel back toward your knees by contracting your abs and lats.
Return to the starting position and repeat, keeping each rep controlled.
tips: Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
Only roll out as far as you can control without low back extension
Squeeze your glutes to lock your pelvis in position

name: Ab Wheel Rollout
description:
**An advanced anti-collapse movement performed with an ab wheel that places the core under extreme tension at full stretch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, lats, triceps.

The rollout demands coordinated effort from the abs, lats, and shoulders to control the eccentric phase and reverse the motion, making it one of the most effective bodyweight core exercises available.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
instructions: Start on your knees with the ab wheel on the floor in front of you, hands gripping the handles shoulder-width apart.
Engage your core and maintain a posterior pelvic tilt throughout the movement.
Roll the wheel forward in a straight line, allowing your body to extend toward the floor.
Go only as far as you can control without your lower back collapsing into extension.
Reverse the movement by pulling your arms back underneath you, using your abs to drive the return.
Stop when the wheel is back under your shoulders and repeat.
tips: Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
Only roll out as far as you can control without low back extension
Squeeze your glutes to lock your pelvis in position

name: Advanced Kettlebell Windmill
description:
**A complex kettlebell movement that combines lateral flexion, hip hinging, and overhead stability to challenge the obliques, glutes, and shoulders simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, shoulders.

The advanced version uses a heavier load or dual kettlebells and demands excellent hip mobility, thoracic rotation, and shoulder stability.

**Pro tip:** Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
instructions: Press a kettlebell overhead with one arm, locking the elbow and stacking the wrist over the shoulder.
Angle your feet roughly 45 degrees away from the loaded arm.
Push your hip out toward the side of the overhead kettlebell.
Hinge at the hip and slowly lower your free hand toward the floor, keeping your eyes on the kettlebell overhead.
Descend until your hand touches the floor or your hamstring flexibility limits you.
Reverse the movement by driving your hips forward and standing tall, keeping the kettlebell locked out overhead throughout.
tips: Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
Push your hip out to the side of the overhead arm
Maintain a slow, controlled descent

name: Air Bike
description:
**A bodyweight core exercise performed lying on the back, mimicking a pedaling motion while alternating elbow-to-knee contact.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The rotational component targets the obliques alongside the abs, and the continuous movement pattern makes it effective for building muscular endurance in the midsection.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your hands placed lightly behind your head and your legs extended.
Lift your shoulders off the ground and bring one knee toward your chest.
Rotate your torso to bring the opposite elbow toward the incoming knee.
As you straighten that leg back out, simultaneously bring the other knee in and rotate to the opposite side.
Continue alternating in a smooth pedaling motion without pulling on your neck.
Maintain the pace for the prescribed rep count or time duration.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Alternate Heel Touchers
description:
**A supine oblique exercise where you laterally crunch side to side, reaching each hand toward the same-side heel.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The short-range lateral flexion keeps constant tension on the obliques and is beginner-friendly while still providing a solid burn at higher rep ranges.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent, feet flat on the floor about shoulder-width apart, and arms at your sides.
Lift your head and shoulders slightly off the ground to engage the abs.
Reach your right hand toward your right heel by crunching laterally to that side.
Return to center, then immediately reach your left hand toward your left heel.
Continue alternating sides in a controlled rhythm.
Keep your lower back pressed into the floor and avoid lifting your shoulders excessively high.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Barbell Ab Rollout
description:
**An anti-collapse core exercise using a loaded barbell as a rolling implement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: lats, shoulders, triceps.

The wider grip and larger diameter of the barbell plates compared to an ab wheel slightly alter the stability demands, and the ability to add plate weight makes this a scalable progression for advanced trainees.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
instructions: Load a barbell with round plates and place it on the floor in front of you.
Kneel behind the bar and grip it with hands shoulder-width apart.
Brace your core, tuck your pelvis, and begin rolling the barbell forward by extending at the shoulders.
Lower your torso toward the floor while keeping your spine neutral — do not allow your hips to sag.
Roll out as far as you can maintain control, then pull the bar back by contracting your abs and lats.
Return to the kneeling start position and repeat.
tips: Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
Only roll out as far as you can control without low back extension
Squeeze your glutes to lock your pelvis in position

name: Barbell Ab Rollout - On Knees
description:
**The kneeling version of the barbell rollout, which shortens the lever arm compared to the standing variation and makes the movement more accessible.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: lats, shoulders, triceps.

It still delivers intense anti-collapse stimulus to the entire anterior core while building the strength foundation needed for full rollouts.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
instructions: Place a barbell with round plates on the floor and kneel behind it on a pad or mat.
Grip the bar at shoulder width with arms extended.
Engage your abs and maintain a slight posterior pelvic tilt.
Roll the barbell forward slowly, letting your body extend while keeping your core rigid.
Stop when you feel you are at the limit of your ability to control the position without your back arching.
Contract your abs and lats to pull the barbell back to the starting position, exhaling as you return.
tips: Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
Only roll out as far as you can control without low back extension
Squeeze your glutes to lock your pelvis in position

name: Barbell Rollout from Bench
description:
**A barbell rollout variation performed from a kneeling position with the barbell starting on a bench, which reduces the range of motion and makes the exercise more manageable for those building up to floor-level rollouts. The elevated starting point decreases the leverage demand while still training anti-collapse strength.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: lats, shoulders, triceps.



**Pro tip:** Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
instructions: Kneel in front of a flat bench and place a loaded barbell on top of it.
Grip the barbell at shoulder width and position your body so your arms are extended with the bar on the bench.
Brace your core tightly and begin rolling the barbell forward along the bench surface.
Extend your arms and let your torso lower, maintaining a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Roll out as far as you can control, then reverse the motion by pulling with your abs and lats.
Return to the start and repeat for the desired reps.
tips: Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
Only roll out as far as you can control without low back extension
Squeeze your glutes to lock your pelvis in position

name: Barbell Side Bend
description:
**A lateral flexion exercise performed with a barbell across the back, targeting the obliques and quadratus lumborum.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: erectors.

The barbell loading distributes weight symmetrically, requiring the obliques on the opposite side to work eccentrically as you bend and concentrically as you return upright.

**Pro tip:** Only hold weight on one side — the working side is the one being stretched
instructions: Place a barbell across your upper back as you would for a back squat, standing with feet shoulder-width apart.
Keep your hips stationary and your core engaged throughout.
Slowly bend your torso to one side, lowering as far as your mobility allows without rotating.
Pause briefly at the bottom of the lateral bend.
Contract the obliques on the opposite side to return to an upright position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching, or alternate sides each rep.
tips: Only hold weight on one side — the working side is the one being stretched
Avoid rotating — keep the movement strictly in the frontal plane
Control the eccentric to maximize oblique tension
coaching_cues: Feel the stretch on the opposite side

name: Bent Press
description:
**A classic old-time strongman lift where you press a kettlebell overhead by bending your body underneath the weight rather than pressing it directly upward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, glutes, hamstrings, triceps, lats.

This full-body movement demands exceptional shoulder stability, oblique strength, hip mobility, and total-body coordination, making it as much a skill exercise as a strength builder.

**Pro tip:** Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
instructions: Clean a kettlebell to the rack position at your shoulder, elbow tucked tight to your body.
Turn your feet roughly 45 degrees away from the loaded arm.
Begin by pushing your hip out to the side of the kettlebell while keeping your eyes on the weight.
Lean your torso laterally and forward, threading your body under the kettlebell as your arm straightens overhead.
Continue lowering until your free hand reaches the floor or your thigh for support, with the kettlebell locked out above.
Stand back up by driving through your legs and hips while keeping the kettlebell stable overhead.
tips: Keep the kettlebell racked close to your body at the start
The bell sits on the back of your forearm — don't fight it, work with it
Press in a groove that feels natural for your shoulder
Grip hard to improve rotator cuff co-contraction

name: Bent-Knee Hip Raise
description:
**A lower-ab focused exercise performed lying on your back where you lift your hips off the ground by curling your pelvis upward with bent knees.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The bent-knee position shortens the lever arm compared to straight-leg raises, making this a more accessible variation that still effectively targets the lower portion of the abs.

**Pro tip:** Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
instructions: Lie on your back on a mat with your arms at your sides, palms pressing into the floor for stability.
Lift your legs so your hips and knees are both bent at approximately 90 degrees.
Contract your lower abs to curl your hips off the floor, driving your knees toward your chest.
Focus on lifting your pelvis rather than swinging your legs — the movement should come from the abs.
Lower your hips back to the floor slowly and with control.
Repeat without using momentum, keeping each rep deliberate.
tips: Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
Minimize swinging by engaging your lats to stabilize
Control the descent to load the abs eccentrically

name: Bicycle Crunch
description:
**A dynamic floor-based abdominal exercise that combines spinal flexion with rotation, alternating elbow-to-opposite-knee contact in a pedaling motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The simultaneous upper and lower body movement increases abs and oblique activation compared to a standard crunch.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie face-up with your hands lightly behind your head and legs lifted so your thighs are perpendicular to the floor.
Simultaneously curl your right shoulder toward your left knee while extending your right leg straight out, hovering it above the ground.
Reverse the motion fluidly, bringing your left shoulder toward your right knee as you extend the left leg.
Keep your lower back pressed into the floor throughout and avoid pulling on your neck.
Move at a controlled tempo, fully completing each rotation before switching sides.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Bird Dog
description:
**An anti-collapse and anti-rotation core stability exercise performed from a quadruped position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: erectors, glutes.

By extending opposite arm and leg simultaneously, the deep stabilizers of the trunk must fire to resist spinal movement, making it an excellent low-impact option for core development and spinal health.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a straight line from head to heels
instructions: Start on all fours with your hands directly under your shoulders and knees under your hips.
Brace your core to create a neutral spine.
Simultaneously extend your right arm forward and left leg backward until both are parallel to the floor.
Hold the top position for a one-count, keeping your hips square to the ground and resisting any rotation or arching.
Return to the starting position under control and repeat on the opposite side.
Avoid rushing the movement—stability and alignment are the priority.
tips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs as if expecting a punch
Do not let your hips sag or pike up

name: Bosu Ball Cable Crunch With Side Bends
description:
**A cable-loaded abdominal exercise performed on a Bosu ball that combines spinal flexion with lateral flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The unstable surface increases core stabilizer recruitment while the cable provides consistent tension through the entire range of motion, delivering a potent stimulus to both the abs and obliques.

**Pro tip:** Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
instructions: Place a Bosu ball in front of a high cable pulley and kneel on it with the flat side down.
Grasp the rope attachment behind your head with both hands.
Begin by crunching your torso downward, drawing your ribcage toward your pelvis.
At the bottom of the crunch, add a lateral bend to one side, squeezing the obliques.
Return to the upright position and repeat, alternating the side bend direction each rep.
Keep your hips stationary throughout so the movement comes entirely from your trunk.
tips: Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
Lock your arms in position so the movement comes from your spine
Use a controlled tempo to maximize tension on the rectus abdominis

name: Bottoms Up
description:
**A lower-ab-focused bodyweight exercise where you drive your hips off the floor by pressing your feet straight toward the ceiling.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This small but intense movement targets the lower portion of the abs through posterior pelvic tilt under load.

**Pro tip:** Focus on curling your pelvis up, not swinging your legs
instructions: Lie on your back with your arms at your sides, palms pressing into the floor.
Raise your legs so they point straight up at the ceiling with a slight bend in the knees.
Contract your lower abs to lift your hips vertically off the ground, pushing your feet directly upward rather than rolling backward.
Lower your hips back to the floor with control.
Avoid using momentum or swinging your legs—the lift should be a short, deliberate pelvic curl driven entirely by abdominal contraction.
tips: Focus on curling your pelvis up, not swinging your legs
Keep the movement small and controlled
Exhale as you lift your hips

name: Butt-Ups
description:
**A forearm-plank-based abdominal exercise where you pike your hips toward the ceiling and return to plank.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This movement trains the abs through a dynamic shortening contraction while also demanding anti-collapse stability during the plank phase.

**Pro tip:** Focus on curling your pelvis up, not swinging your legs
instructions: Begin in a forearm plank with your elbows under your shoulders and your body in a straight line from head to heels.
Keeping your legs relatively straight, contract your abs to pike your hips upward toward the ceiling until your torso is nearly vertical.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower your hips back to the plank position under control.
Do not let your hips sag below neutral at the bottom.
Focus on initiating the pike from your abdominals rather than pushing through your toes.
tips: Focus on curling your pelvis up, not swinging your legs
Keep the movement small and controlled
Exhale as you lift your hips

name: Cable Crunch
description:
**A kneeling abdominal exercise using a high cable pulley that provides constant tension throughout spinal flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The cable resistance makes it easy to progressively overload, and the flat strength curve ensures the abs are challenged evenly from stretch to contraction.

**Pro tip:** Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
instructions: Kneel facing a high pulley and grasp a rope attachment, holding it at the sides of your head.
Lock your hips in place so they do not move during the set.
Crunch your torso downward by drawing your ribcage toward your pelvis, rounding your upper back as you flex your spine.
Squeeze hard at the bottom, then slowly return to the starting position without letting the weight stack rest.
Keep your arms fixed relative to your head—all movement should come from your trunk curling, not from pulling with your arms.
tips: Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
Lock your arms in position so the movement comes from your spine
Use a controlled tempo to maximize tension on the rectus abdominis

name: Cable Judo Flip
description:
**An explosive rotational cable exercise that mimics a judo throw pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders.

Standing perpendicular to the cable, you pull the handle overhead and slam it diagonally downward, training the obliques, abs, and shoulders through a powerful anti-rotation and rotation sequence.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Stand sideways to a high cable pulley with your feet shoulder-width apart.
Grasp the handle with both hands above the shoulder closest to the machine.
In one powerful motion, rotate your torso and pull the handle diagonally across your body, finishing near the outside of your opposite knee.
Bend your knees slightly as you rotate and allow your back foot to pivot naturally.
Return to the start position under control.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Cable Reverse Crunch
description:
**A cable-loaded reverse crunch that uses a low pulley attached to the ankles to add resistance to hip flexion and posterior pelvic tilt.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The constant cable tension eliminates the dead zone at the bottom of a standard reverse crunch, keeping the abs under load throughout the entire rep.

**Pro tip:** Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
instructions: Attach ankle straps to a low cable pulley and lie face-up on the floor with your head toward the machine.
Bend your knees to roughly 90 degrees.
Curl your knees toward your chest by contracting your lower abs and tilting your pelvis posteriorly, lifting your hips off the ground.
Squeeze at the top, then slowly extend your legs back to the start, resisting the cable's pull.
Do not let your lower back arch excessively at the bottom.
Keep the movement smooth and controlled, focusing on the pelvic curl rather than hip flexor momentum.
tips: Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
Lock your arms in position so the movement comes from your spine
Use a controlled tempo to maximize tension on the rectus abdominis

name: Cable Russian Twists
description:
**A seated rotational core exercise performed with a cable set at mid-height.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders.

The cable provides lateral resistance that challenges the obliques and deep core muscles through trunk rotation while the shoulders work isometrically to maintain arm position.

**Pro tip:** Keep your chest up and rotate through the thoracic spine
instructions: Sit on the floor facing perpendicular to a cable machine with your knees bent and feet flat or slightly elevated.
Grasp the handle with both hands and extend your arms in front of your chest.
Lean back slightly to engage your abs.
Rotate your torso away from the cable, pulling the handle across your body to the opposite side.
Return to center under control, resisting the cable's pull back.
Complete all reps rotating in one direction, then switch sides.
tips: Keep your chest up and rotate through the thoracic spine
Move with control — momentum reduces oblique tension
Exhale as you rotate to each side

name: Cable Seated Crunch
description:
**A seated variation of the cable crunch performed on a bench, which locks the hips in place and isolates spinal flexion against cable resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The seated position reduces hip flexor involvement, directing more tension to the abs.

**Pro tip:** Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
instructions: Sit on a bench with your back to a high cable pulley.
Grasp the rope attachment and hold it behind your head at neck level.
Plant your feet firmly on the floor.
Crunch forward by flexing your spine, drawing your sternum toward your pelvis.
Squeeze your abs hard at the bottom of the movement, then slowly return to the upright position.
Your hips and lower body should remain completely stationary—only your upper trunk moves.
tips: Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
Lock your arms in position so the movement comes from your spine
Use a controlled tempo to maximize tension on the rectus abdominis

name: Cable Woodchop High to Low
description:
**A standing rotational exercise using a high cable pulley where you chop the handle diagonally from above one shoulder down toward the opposite hip.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders.

This movement trains the obliques and entire anterior core through a functional diagonal pattern that mimics real-world and athletic movements.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Stand sideways to a cable machine with the pulley set to its highest position.
Grasp the handle with both hands above your shoulder closest to the machine, arms extended.
With a slight bend in your knees, rotate your torso and pull the handle diagonally across your body toward your opposite hip, pivoting on your back foot.
Keep your arms relatively straight—the power comes from your core rotation, not your arms.
Return to the start slowly, resisting the cable.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Cable Woodchop Low to High
description:
**A standing rotational cable exercise where you drive the handle from a low position near one hip upward across your body to above the opposite shoulder.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders.

This ascending chop pattern emphasizes the obliques and serratus anterior while training rotational power from the ground up.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Stand sideways to a cable machine with the pulley at its lowest setting.
Squat slightly and grasp the handle with both hands near the hip closest to the machine.
Drive upward through your legs while rotating your torso, sweeping the handle diagonally across your body to above the opposite shoulder.
Keep your arms extended but not locked.
Control the return to the starting position, resisting the cable throughout the eccentric.
Pivot your feet naturally with the rotation.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Cocoons
description:
**A full-range bodyweight abdominal exercise where you simultaneously bring your knees to your chest and your arms overhead to your shins, starting from a fully extended position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The long range of motion from full stretch to peak contraction makes this a demanding movement for the entire abs.

**Pro tip:** Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your legs extended and arms stretched overhead on the floor.
In one coordinated motion, tuck your knees toward your chest while simultaneously curling your upper body off the floor and reaching your hands to your shins.
Squeeze your abs tightly at the top when your body is in a tucked ball.
Slowly reverse the movement, extending your legs and arms back to the fully stretched starting position.
Maintain control throughout—do not simply fall back to the floor between reps.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Cross-Body Crunch
description:
**A crunch variation that adds a cross-body rotational element, targeting the obliques alongside the abs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

By driving one elbow toward the opposite knee, you increase the rotational demand compared to a standard crunch.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Place your hands lightly behind your head.
Curl your torso up and rotate, driving your right elbow toward your left knee.
Your left foot stays planted while your right shoulder lifts off the ground.
Lower back to the start with control and repeat on the opposite side.
Avoid pulling on your neck—let your core do the work.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Crunch
description:
**The foundational abdominal exercise involving a short-range spinal flexion movement while lying supine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

Though simple, the crunch effectively isolates the abs when performed with strict form and a deliberate contraction at the top.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent at roughly 90 degrees and feet flat on the floor.
Place your fingertips lightly behind your ears or cross your arms over your chest.
Exhale and curl your shoulders off the ground by flexing your spine, drawing your ribcage toward your pelvis.
Hold the peak contraction for a beat, then lower back down under control.
Your lower back should remain in contact with the floor throughout.
Avoid jerking your head forward or using momentum—the movement is short and controlled.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Crunch - Hands Overhead
description:
**A crunch variation with the arms extended overhead, which lengthens the lever arm and increases the demand on the abs compared to a standard crunch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The overhead position also prevents the common mistake of pulling on the neck.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Extend both arms straight overhead so your biceps are beside your ears, hands clasped or palms together.
Keeping your arms in line with your torso, curl your shoulders and upper back off the floor by contracting your abs.
Reach toward the ceiling as you crunch up.
Pause at the top, then lower slowly.
Your arms stay fixed relative to your head throughout—they move with your torso, not independently.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Crunch - Legs On Exercise Ball
description:
**A crunch performed with the calves resting on a stability ball, which places the hips and knees at approximately 90 degrees and reduces hip flexor involvement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The elevated leg position helps maintain a posterior pelvic tilt, improving abdominal isolation.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back and place your calves on top of a stability ball so your hips and knees form roughly 90-degree angles.
Place your hands lightly behind your head or across your chest.
Curl your upper body off the floor by contracting your abs, lifting your shoulder blades.
Squeeze at the top, then lower back down with control.
Keep the ball stationary—if it rolls, you are using momentum.
Focus on a smooth, deliberate spinal flexion rather than a fast sit-up motion.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Crunches
description:
**The classic abdominal crunch performed for repetitions, targeting the abs through controlled spinal flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This staple movement is effective for building abdominal endurance and mind-muscle connection when executed with proper form and tempo.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie supine with your knees bent, feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart.
Support your head lightly with your fingertips—do not interlace your fingers behind your neck.
Brace your core and exhale as you curl your torso upward, lifting your shoulder blades off the ground.
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribcage and pelvis.
Hold the contraction briefly at the top, then inhale as you lower back down in a controlled manner.
Avoid flattening out completely at the bottom to maintain tension on the abs between reps.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Dead Bug
description:
**An anti-collapse core stability exercise performed lying face-up, where you alternately extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining a braced, neutral spine.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: erectors.

The dead bug trains the deep core stabilizers to resist lumbar extension under changing limb loads, making it excellent for core function and spinal health.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a straight line from head to heels
instructions: Lie on your back with your arms extended toward the ceiling and your hips and knees bent to 90 degrees.
Press your lower back firmly into the floor by engaging your deep core muscles.
Slowly extend your right arm overhead and your left leg straight out, lowering both toward the floor without letting your lower back arch away from the ground.
Return to the starting position and repeat with the opposite arm and leg.
Exhale during the extension phase to help maintain intra-abdominal pressure.
Move slowly—speed is the enemy of this exercise.
tips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs as if expecting a punch
Do not let your hips sag or pike up

name: Decline Crunch
description:
**A crunch performed on a decline bench with the head lower than the hips, which increases the gravitational resistance on the abs compared to a flat crunch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The decline angle extends the effective range of motion and makes the exercise more challenging without added weight.

**Pro tip:** Curl your spine segment by segment — do not sit up with a flat back
instructions: Secure your feet under the pads of a decline bench set to a moderate angle.
Lie back with your hands across your chest or lightly behind your head.
Curl your torso upward by flexing your spine, lifting your shoulder blades off the bench.
Focus on crunching your ribcage toward your pelvis rather than sitting all the way up.
Squeeze at the top, then lower back down under control without fully resting on the bench.
Avoid using momentum to swing yourself up—the movement should be smooth and driven entirely by your abs.
tips: Curl your spine segment by segment — do not sit up with a flat back
Cross your arms over your chest or hold a plate for added resistance
Control the descent to maximize eccentric loading
coaching_cues: Curl up, do not sit up

name: Decline Oblique Crunch
description:
**A decline bench crunch with an added rotational twist at the top, targeting the obliques in addition to the abs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The decline angle increases resistance while the rotation adds a lateral flexion component for more complete midsection development.

**Pro tip:** Curl your spine segment by segment — do not sit up with a flat back
instructions: Secure your feet under the pads of a decline bench.
Lie back and place your hands lightly behind your head.
Curl up and simultaneously rotate your torso, driving one elbow toward the opposite knee.
Squeeze the obliques at the top of the movement, then lower back to the bench with control.
Alternate the direction of rotation each rep, or complete all reps to one side before switching.
Keep the twist controlled—do not jerk or use momentum.
tips: Curl your spine segment by segment — do not sit up with a flat back
Cross your arms over your chest or hold a plate for added resistance
Control the descent to maximize eccentric loading
coaching_cues: Curl up, do not sit up

name: Decline Reverse Crunch
description:
**A reverse crunch performed on a decline bench with the head higher than the hips, gripping the top of the bench for stability.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The decline angle adds gravitational resistance to the hip curl, increasing the challenge on the lower portion of the abs.

**Pro tip:** Curl your spine segment by segment — do not sit up with a flat back
instructions: Lie on a decline bench with your head at the high end.
Reach back and grip the top of the bench or the foot pads for stability.
Bend your knees and lift your legs so your thighs are roughly perpendicular to the bench.
Contract your lower abs to curl your pelvis upward off the bench, bringing your knees toward your chest.
Squeeze at the top, then slowly lower your hips back to the bench.
Do not swing your legs—the movement should be a controlled pelvic curl.
tips: Curl your spine segment by segment — do not sit up with a flat back
Cross your arms over your chest or hold a plate for added resistance
Control the descent to maximize eccentric loading
coaching_cues: Curl up, do not sit up

name: Decline Sit-Up
description:
**A full sit-up performed on a decline bench, combining spinal flexion with hip flexion against increased gravitational resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The decline angle makes this significantly harder than a flat sit-up and allows for progressive overload by adjusting the bench angle or holding a weight plate.

**Pro tip:** Curl your spine segment by segment — do not sit up with a flat back
instructions: Secure your feet under the pads of a decline bench.
Lie all the way back until your upper back and shoulders contact the bench.
Cross your arms over your chest or hold a weight plate against your chest for added resistance.
Sit up by first curling your spine—lifting your shoulders, then your mid-back—and continue until your torso is roughly upright.
Lower yourself back down under control, resisting gravity on the way down.
Avoid dropping back onto the bench.
tips: Curl your spine segment by segment — do not sit up with a flat back
Cross your arms over your chest or hold a plate for added resistance
Control the descent to maximize eccentric loading
coaching_cues: Curl up, do not sit up

name: Double Kettlebell Windmill
description:
**An advanced kettlebell exercise where you hold one bell overhead and one at your side while hinging laterally, training the obliques, shoulders, and posterior chain simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, shoulders, triceps.

The dual load demands exceptional core stability, shoulder control, and hip mobility.

**Pro tip:** Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
instructions: Press one kettlebell overhead with a locked-out arm and hold a second kettlebell in your opposite hand at your side.
Turn your feet out roughly 45 degrees from the overhead arm.
Keeping your eyes on the overhead kettlebell, push your hip out toward the overhead side and slowly hinge at the hip, lowering the bottom kettlebell along your front leg toward the floor.
Your overhead arm stays vertical and locked throughout.
Descend as far as your mobility allows while maintaining a straight overhead arm and neutral spine.
Drive back up by contracting your obliques and glutes.
tips: Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
Push your hip out to the side of the overhead arm
Maintain a slow, controlled descent

name: Dragon Flag
description:
**An advanced bodyweight abdominal exercise popularized by Bruce Lee, where you lower and raise your entire body as a rigid lever from a bench, supported only by your upper back and shoulders.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: erectors.

The extreme lever arm and stretched position make this one of the most demanding ab exercises available.

**Pro tip:** Only lower as far as you can control without your back arching
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the edges or the support behind your head firmly.
Kick your legs up so your body forms a straight line from shoulders to toes, with only your upper back and shoulders in contact with the bench.
Keeping your body rigid as a plank, slowly lower yourself toward the bench in a controlled arc, resisting gravity with your abs.
Lower as far as you can without your hips or lower back touching the bench, then reverse the motion and raise your body back to vertical.
Do not bend at the hips—your body should remain straight throughout.
If you cannot perform the full version, begin with bent knees to shorten the lever.
tips: Only lower as far as you can control without your back arching
Grip the bench firmly behind your head for leverage
Keep your body rigid as a plank throughout

name: Dumbbell Side Bend
description:
**A standing lateral flexion exercise that targets the obliques by bending sideways against the resistance of a dumbbell held at arm's length.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: erectors.

The unilateral load creates a strong demand on the lateral core musculature to control the eccentric descent and power the concentric return.

**Pro tip:** Only hold weight on one side — the working side is the one being stretched
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in one hand at your side with a neutral grip.
Place your free hand behind your head or on your hip.
Keeping your hips stationary and chest tall, slowly lower the dumbbell toward the floor by bending laterally at the waist.
Descend until you feel a deep stretch along the opposite oblique, then contract your obliques to return to the upright position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
Avoid leaning forward or backward throughout the movement.
tips: Only hold weight on one side — the working side is the one being stretched
Avoid rotating — keep the movement strictly in the frontal plane
Control the eccentric to maximize oblique tension
coaching_cues: Feel the stretch on the opposite side

name: Elbow to Knee
description:
**A supine rotational crunch variation where the opposite elbow and knee are driven toward each other, engaging both the abs and the obliques through a cross-body contraction pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The alternating motion provides continuous tension across the entire abdominal wall.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with your hands placed lightly behind your ears and your knees bent at roughly 90 degrees with feet flat on the floor.
Lift your shoulder blades off the ground while simultaneously bringing one knee toward your chest.
Rotate your torso to drive the opposite elbow toward the rising knee.
Squeeze at the point of contact, then lower back under control and repeat on the other side.
Keep your lower back pressed into the floor and avoid pulling on your neck with your hands.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Exercise Ball Crunch
description:
**A crunch performed while lying back over a stability ball, which extends the range of motion beyond what a floor crunch allows.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The unstable surface recruits additional stabilizer muscles while the curvature of the ball permits a greater stretch of the abdominals at the bottom of each rep.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Sit on a stability ball and walk your feet forward until your lower back is fully supported by the ball.
Place your hands behind your head or cross them over your chest.
Allow your torso to drape back over the ball to achieve a full abdominal stretch.
Contract your abs to curl your torso upward, bringing your ribcage toward your pelvis.
Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower back to the stretched position.
Keep your feet firmly planted and hips stable throughout.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Exercise Ball Pull-In
description:
**A dynamic core exercise performed from a push-up position with shins resting on a stability ball.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

By rolling the ball toward the hands through hip flexion and spinal flexion, the lower abdominals and hip flexors work intensely against the instability of the ball.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Begin in a push-up position with your shins resting on top of a stability ball and your hands directly beneath your shoulders.
Brace your core to maintain a straight body line.
Draw your knees toward your chest by rolling the ball forward, allowing your hips to rise slightly as your abs contract.
Hold the tucked position for a beat, then extend your legs back to the starting plank position in a controlled manner.
Avoid letting your hips sag or your lower back arch at any point.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Flat Bench Leg Pull-In
description:
**A seated hip flexion movement performed on the edge of a flat bench that emphasizes the lower portion of the abs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

Gripping the bench for stability allows you to focus entirely on drawing the knees toward the torso against gravity.

**Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench and grip the edges behind you for support.
Lean your torso back slightly and extend your legs out in front of you so they hover just above the bench.
Pull your knees in toward your chest while simultaneously crunching your upper body forward slightly.
Squeeze your abs hard at peak contraction, then slowly extend your legs back out without letting your feet touch the bench.
Maintain a controlled tempo and avoid using momentum to swing your legs.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Flat Bench Lying Leg Raise
description:
**A supine leg raise performed while lying flat on a bench, which allows the legs to drop slightly below the plane of the body for an increased range of motion compared to floor leg raises.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This extended range places greater demand on the lower abdominals through a longer eccentric stretch.

**Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout
instructions: Lie face-up on a flat bench with your legs extended and your hands gripping the bench behind your head or beneath your glutes for stability.
With a slight bend in your knees, raise both legs together until they are perpendicular to the floor.
Lower them back down slowly, allowing them to descend just below bench level to achieve a full stretch.
Reverse the motion by contracting your lower abs.
Keep your lower back pressed firmly against the bench and avoid arching.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Frog Sit-Ups
description:
**A sit-up variation performed with the soles of the feet pressed together and knees splayed outward in a butterfly position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This foot placement neutralizes hip flexor involvement, forcing the abs to perform the majority of the work during the sit-up.

**Pro tip:** Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
instructions: Lie on your back and bring the soles of your feet together, letting your knees fall open to the sides in a butterfly position.
Extend your arms overhead or cross them over your chest.
Engage your abs to curl your torso all the way up to a seated position, reaching your hands toward your feet.
Lower yourself back down under control, allowing your shoulder blades to touch the floor before initiating the next rep.
Focus on using your abdominals rather than momentum to drive the movement.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Gorilla Chin/Crunch
description:
**A demanding compound movement that combines a chin-up with a hanging knee raise performed simultaneously.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: biceps, lats, forearms.

As you pull your body upward, you also drive your knees toward your chest, creating a powerful dual contraction through the lats, biceps, and the entire abdominal wall.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Hang from a chin-up bar with an underhand grip at roughly shoulder width.
From a dead hang, simultaneously pull your body upward while driving your knees toward your chest.
Aim to bring your knees as close to your elbows as possible at the top of the movement.
Lower yourself back to a full hang while extending your legs in a controlled fashion.
Avoid excessive swinging and focus on synchronizing the pull and the crunch into one fluid motion.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Hanging Knee Raise
description:
**A hanging abdominal exercise where the knees are drawn upward toward the chest while suspended from a pull-up bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: forearms.

The hanging position provides a full stretch of the abdominals at the bottom and the bent-knee position makes this more accessible than straight-leg variations while still delivering strong lower ab activation.

**Pro tip:** Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Begin with your legs hanging straight down.
Flex your hips and knees to draw your thighs up toward your chest, curling your pelvis upward at the top to maximize abdominal contraction.
Hold the top position briefly, then lower your legs back to the starting position under control.
Minimize body swing by engaging your lats and keeping the tempo deliberate.
tips: Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
Minimize swinging by engaging your lats to stabilize
Control the descent to load the abs eccentrically

name: Hanging Leg Raise
description:
**An advanced hanging core exercise where the legs are raised with minimal knee bend while suspended from a bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: forearms.

Keeping the legs nearly straight dramatically increases the lever arm, placing significantly greater load on the lower abdominals and hip flexors compared to the knee raise variation.

**Pro tip:** Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip and arms fully extended.
With your legs straight or with only a very slight bend in the knees, raise both legs together in front of you until they reach at least parallel with the floor.
Curl your pelvis slightly at the top for peak abdominal engagement.
Lower your legs back down slowly, resisting gravity throughout the descent.
Keep your torso as still as possible and avoid kipping or swinging to generate momentum.
tips: Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
Minimize swinging by engaging your lats to stabilize
Control the descent to load the abs eccentrically

name: Hanging Pike
description:
**The most advanced hanging leg raise progression, requiring you to lift straight legs all the way up until your toes touch or approach the bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: forearms.

This full-range movement demands exceptional abdominal strength, hip flexor power, and hamstring flexibility, making it a benchmark core exercise for advanced trainees.

**Pro tip:** Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with a firm overhand grip and fully extended arms.
With legs completely straight, raise them in a controlled arc all the way up until your toes approach or touch the bar.
This requires significant posterior pelvic tilt at the top.
Lower your legs back down through the full range of motion with deliberate control.
Avoid any swinging or kipping.
If you cannot reach the bar, raise your legs as high as possible while maintaining straight knees and work toward full range over time.
tips: Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
Minimize swinging by engaging your lats to stabilize
Control the descent to load the abs eccentrically

name: Hollow Body Hold
description:
**A foundational gymnastics isometric hold that trains the entire anterior core to maintain a globally flexed position against gravity.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The body forms a shallow banana shape with arms and legs extended, demanding sustained contraction of the abs and deep stabilizers to prevent the lower back from arching.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a straight line from head to heels
instructions: Lie on your back with your arms extended overhead and legs straight.
Press your lower back firmly into the floor by engaging your abs and tilting your pelvis posteriorly.
Simultaneously lift your arms, head, shoulder blades, and legs a few inches off the ground, forming a shallow curved shape.
Hold this position for the prescribed duration, breathing steadily.
If your lower back lifts off the floor, bend your knees or raise your limbs higher to reduce the lever arm until you build sufficient strength.
tips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs as if expecting a punch
Do not let your hips sag or pike up

name: Jackknife Sit-Up
description:
**A dynamic abdominal exercise where the arms and legs are raised simultaneously from a flat lying position to meet above the midsection, forming a V-shape at the top.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This dual-action movement engages both the upper and lower portions of the abs through a large range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your arms extended overhead and legs straight on the floor.
In one explosive but controlled motion, raise your torso and legs simultaneously, reaching your hands toward your toes at the top so your body forms a V.
Balance briefly on your sit bones at the peak, then lower your arms and legs back to the starting position without letting them rest on the floor between reps.
Keep your legs as straight as possible and avoid rounding your upper back excessively.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Janda Sit-Up
description:
**A specialized sit-up variation designed by Dr.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

Vladimir Janda that minimizes hip flexor contribution through reciprocal inhibition. By actively contracting the hamstrings and glutes during the sit-up, the hip flexors are neurologically inhibited, isolating the abdominals far more effectively than a standard sit-up.

**Pro tip:** Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
instructions: Lie on your back with your knees bent at approximately 90 degrees.
Hook your heels against an immovable object or have a partner hold your ankles.
Actively pull your heels into the ground as if performing a hamstring curl throughout the entire movement.
With your hands across your chest or behind your ears, curl your torso up off the floor one vertebra at a time.
The key is maintaining constant hamstring activation to inhibit the hip flexors.
Lower back down slowly with the same vertebra-by-vertebra control.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Kettlebell Figure 8
description:
**A dynamic full-body movement where a kettlebell is passed in a figure-eight pattern between and around the legs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: hamstrings, glutes, shoulders, forearms.

The continuous hand-to-hand transfer challenges rotational core stability, grip strength, and coordination while the semi-squat stance engages the posterior chain throughout.

**Pro tip:** Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
instructions: Stand with your feet wider than shoulder width and lower into a partial squat with your chest up and back flat.
Hold a kettlebell in one hand in front of you.
Pass the kettlebell between your legs from front to back, transferring it to the opposite hand behind the leg.
Bring that hand around the outside of the leg and pass it through the front again to the other hand, tracing a figure-eight path.
Maintain a stable squat position and keep your core braced throughout.
Move fluidly and avoid rounding your lower back.
tips: Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
Push your hip out to the side of the overhead arm
Maintain a slow, controlled descent

name: Kettlebell Pass Between The Legs
description:
**A standing core stability drill where a kettlebell is circled around the body and passed between the legs in a continuous loop.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, shoulders, forearms.

The shifting center of gravity forces the obliques and deep core stabilizers to fire constantly to prevent the torso from rotating or tilting with the load.

**Pro tip:** Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in your knees.
Hold a kettlebell in one hand at arm's length.
Pass the kettlebell around one leg by handing it off between your legs from front to back, then receiving it with the other hand behind the leg.
Continue the circular path around the leg and repeat.
After the prescribed reps, switch direction.
Keep your torso upright and hips square throughout.
tips: Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
Push your hip out to the side of the overhead arm
Maintain a slow, controlled descent

name: Knee/Hip Raise On Parallel Bars
description:
**A vertical knee or leg raise performed while supported on parallel bars or a captain's chair.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The supported arm position eliminates grip as a limiting factor, allowing you to focus entirely on driving the knees or legs upward through a full range of hip flexion and spinal flexion.

**Pro tip:** Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
instructions: Position yourself on parallel bars or a captain's chair with your forearms resting on the pads and your back against the backrest.
Let your legs hang straight down.
Draw your knees upward toward your chest, curling your pelvis at the top to engage the lower abs fully.
For a more advanced variation, keep your legs straight.
Lower back to the starting position with control, avoiding any swinging.
Exhale forcefully as you raise your knees and inhale on the descent.
tips: Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
Minimize swinging by engaging your lats to stabilize
Control the descent to load the abs eccentrically

name: Kneeling Cable Crunch With Alternating Oblique Twists
description:
**A cable-loaded kneeling crunch that incorporates alternating rotational twists to target both the abs and the obliques.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The cable provides consistent tension throughout the entire range of motion, and the kneeling position anchors the lower body to isolate the trunk musculature effectively.

**Pro tip:** Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
instructions: Kneel beneath a high cable pulley and grasp a rope attachment behind your head.
Begin in an upright kneeling position with tension on the cable.
Crunch downward by flexing your spine, and as you descend, rotate your torso to bring one elbow toward the opposite knee.
Return to the starting position under control, then repeat the crunch with a rotation to the other side.
Alternate sides each rep.
Keep your hips stationary and focus on driving the movement from your abs and obliques, not your arms.
tips: Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
Lock your arms in position so the movement comes from your spine
Use a controlled tempo to maximize tension on the rectus abdominis

name: Landmine 180's
description:
**An explosive rotational exercise using a landmine attachment where the barbell is swung in a 180-degree arc from one hip to the other.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, glutes, erectors.

This movement trains the obliques, deep core muscles, and shoulders through a powerful anti-rotation and rotation pattern that mimics athletic throwing and striking mechanics.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with feet slightly wider than shoulder width.
Grip the end of the barbell with both hands and start with it positioned at one hip.
In a controlled but powerful motion, rotate your torso and pivot your feet to swing the barbell in an arc overhead to the opposite hip.
Your arms should stay relatively straight, acting as levers while the rotation is driven by your core and hips.
Reverse the motion back to the starting side.
Control the deceleration at each end point rather than bouncing.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Landmine Rotation
description:
**A controlled rotational core exercise using a landmine where the barbell is moved in a sweeping arc at a more measured tempo than the explosive 180 variation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders.

This slower, more deliberate approach emphasizes time under tension for the obliques and anti-rotation stabilizers while reducing momentum-based compensation.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight knee bend.
Hold the end of the barbell with both hands at chest height with arms extended.
Rotate your torso to move the barbell to one side in a controlled arc, pivoting on your feet as needed.
Pause briefly, then rotate through center to the opposite side.
Maintain extended arms throughout so the rotation comes from your trunk, not your arms.
Use a deliberate tempo and focus on feeling the obliques control the movement in both directions.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Leg Pull-In
description:
**A floor-based lower abdominal exercise performed by sitting on the ground with legs extended and pulling the knees toward the chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The simultaneous lean-back of the torso and pull-in of the knees creates a strong contraction through the entire abs with emphasis on the lower fibers.

**Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout
instructions: Sit on the floor with your legs extended in front of you and your hands placed on the floor slightly behind your hips for support.
Lean your torso back to about a 45-degree angle while extending your legs out so they hover above the floor.
Simultaneously pull your knees toward your chest and bring your torso slightly forward, creating a crunching motion from both ends.
Extend back out to the starting hover position without letting your feet or shoulders touch the ground.
Maintain steady breathing and a controlled pace.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Leg Raise
description:
**A fundamental floor-based abdominal exercise where both legs are raised from a supine position toward the ceiling.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This simple yet effective movement loads the lower abdominals through hip flexion while the upper abs work isometrically to stabilize the pelvis and prevent the lower back from arching.

**Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your legs straight and your hands placed beneath your glutes or at your sides for support.
Keeping your legs together and as straight as possible, raise them toward the ceiling until your hips are flexed to approximately 90 degrees.
Lower your legs back down slowly, stopping just before they touch the floor to maintain tension.
Press your lower back into the ground throughout the movement.
If you experience lower back discomfort, bend your knees slightly to reduce the lever arm.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Lower Back Curl
description:
**A prone spinal extension exercise that targets the erector spinae muscles by curling the upper body off the floor from a face-down position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: erectors. Secondary: glutes.

This controlled back extension strengthens the posterior chain of the spine and serves as an important counterbalance to the many flexion-based abdominal exercises.

**Pro tip:** Move through spinal flexion and extension only
instructions: Lie face down on the floor with your arms positioned alongside your body or with your hands placed lightly behind your ears.
Keeping your feet on the ground, contract your lower back muscles to lift your chest and upper torso off the floor.
Rise only as high as comfortable without forcing hyperextension.
Hold the top position for a one-second squeeze, then lower back down under control.
Move through a smooth, deliberate range of motion and avoid jerky or rapid movements that could strain the spine.
tips: Move through spinal flexion and extension only
Keep the movement slow and controlled
Use light resistance — the lumbar spine is vulnerable under load

name: Lying Leg Raise
description:
**A supine leg raise variation that can be performed on the floor or a mat, targeting the lower abdominals through controlled hip flexion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The lying position provides back support while the straight-leg raise creates a long lever arm that challenges the abs to control pelvic position throughout the movement.

**Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout
instructions: Lie on your back on a mat with your legs fully extended and arms at your sides or tucked under your lower back for support.
Brace your core and raise both legs together, keeping them straight, until they point toward the ceiling.
Lower them back toward the floor at a slow, controlled tempo, stopping an inch or two above the ground to keep constant tension on the abs.
Avoid letting your lower back peel off the floor at any point.
Exhale as you lift and inhale as you lower.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Machine Crunch
description:
**A seated abdominal crunch performed on a dedicated crunch machine that provides a fixed path of motion and adjustable resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The machine's cam system delivers consistent loading throughout the entire range, making it one of the most efficient ways to progressively overload the abs with minimal technique breakdown.

**Pro tip:** Focus on curling your spine, not hinging at the hips
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the pivot point of the machine aligns with your mid-torso.
