Sit down and position your chest against the pad or grasp the handles, depending on the machine design.
Select an appropriate weight.
Contract your abs to curl your torso downward, driving your ribcage toward your pelvis.
Focus on spinal flexion rather than hip flexion.
Squeeze hard at the bottom of the crunch, then return to the starting position slowly, allowing a full stretch of the abs at the top.
tips: Focus on curling your spine, not hinging at the hips
Adjust the pad so resistance is applied to your upper chest
Control the eccentric — do not let the weight stack slam

name: Medicine Ball Full Twist
description: **Unleash rotational power that carries over to every sport you play.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques) and shoulders. This bodyweight exercise uses a twisting motion to fire up your entire core while challenging your balance and coordination. You'll feel your obliques working overtime as you rotate through the full range of motion. **Pro tip:** Keep your chest tall and initiate the twist from your ribcage, not your arms. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve means peak tension occurs at the midpoint of the twist, making controlled speed essential for maximum muscle engagement.
instructions: Sit on the floor with knees bent, feet flat, and hold an imaginary ball (or clasp your hands) at chest height.
Lean back slightly so your core is engaged and your torso is at roughly a 45-degree angle.
Rotate your upper body fully to the right, bringing your hands past your hip.
Reverse the motion and rotate fully to the left side to complete one rep.
Keep your feet planted and avoid swinging your arms — the twist should come from your midsection.
Perform all reps with a steady, controlled tempo.
tips: Keep your chest up and rotate through the thoracic spine
Move with control — momentum reduces oblique tension
Exhale as you rotate to each side

name: Mountain Climber
description: **Turn a simple floor position into a full-body cardio furnace.** **Muscles worked:** abs, quads, and shoulders. Mountain climbers combine core stability with rapid leg drives, spiking your heart rate while hammering your midsection. This bodyweight move doubles as conditioning and ab work in one efficient package. **Pro tip:** Drive your knees toward your chest rather than just shuffling your feet — the deeper the drive, the harder your abs work. **Biomechanics:** Maintaining a rigid plank throughout creates constant tension on the core, while the alternating leg drives add a dynamic stability challenge that flat crunches can't match.
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands directly under your shoulders and body in a straight line.
Drive your right knee toward your chest while keeping your hips level.
Quickly return your right foot to the start and immediately drive your left knee forward.
Alternate legs at a brisk pace, as if running in place horizontally.
Keep your core tight and avoid letting your hips pike up or sag down.
Continue for the prescribed time or rep count.
tips: Keep your hips level — do not let them pike up or sag
Drive your knees toward your chest with control
Maintain a braced core throughout

name: Oblique Crunches
description: **Carve definition into the sides of your waist with this targeted crunch variation.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques). This bodyweight isolation move zeroes in on the muscles that frame your midsection, helping build that tapered look. The angled crunch path ensures your obliques do the heavy lifting instead of your hip flexors. **Pro tip:** Think about bringing your shoulder toward the opposite hip rather than just lifting your head. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance profile means the obliques are loaded hardest in the middle of the crunch, so squeeze deliberately at the top before lowering.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Place your hands lightly behind your head without pulling on your neck.
Crunch up and rotate your right shoulder toward your left knee.
Lower back down with control, then repeat to the same side for all reps before switching.
Exhale as you crunch up and inhale on the way down.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Oblique Crunches - On The Floor
description: **A grounded twist on the classic crunch that isolates your side abs with zero equipment.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques). Lying fully on the floor removes momentum and forces your obliques to do honest work through every rep. This is a straightforward move that delivers real results when performed with intention. **Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout — if it arches, you've lost core engagement. **Biomechanics:** Because the floor limits your range of motion, each degree of movement is earned purely through oblique contraction, making quality reps far more valuable than quantity.
instructions: Lie flat on the floor with your legs stacked and knees slightly bent, resting on one side.
Place your top hand behind your head and your bottom arm across your torso for stability.
Crunch your upper body sideways, lifting your shoulder off the floor toward your hip.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower slowly.
Complete all reps on one side before switching to the other.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: One-Arm High-Pulley Cable Side Bends
description: **Constant cable tension turns a simple side bend into an oblique-building machine.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques). Using a high-pulley cable keeps resistance on your obliques throughout the entire movement, unlike free-weight side bends where tension drops at the bottom. This isolation exercise is excellent for building side-core strength evenly. **Pro tip:** Stand far enough from the cable stack that you feel a stretch at the top of each rep. **Biomechanics:** The high anchor point creates a diagonal line of pull that matches the natural fiber direction of the obliques, resulting in a more efficient contraction pattern.
instructions: Stand sideways to a cable machine with the pulley set to the highest position.
Grab the handle with the hand closest to the machine, arm extended overhead.
With feet shoulder-width apart, bend your torso away from the cable, crunching your opposite oblique.
Return to the upright position under control, feeling the stretch on the working side.
Keep your hips still — all movement should come from your waist.
Finish all reps on one side, then switch.
tips: Only hold weight on one side — the working side is the one being stretched
Avoid rotating — keep the movement strictly in the frontal plane
Control the eccentric to maximize oblique tension
coaching_cues: Feel the stretch on the opposite side

name: One-Arm Medicine Ball Slam
description: **Channel raw aggression into a single-arm slam that torches your core and lats.** **Muscles worked:** abs, lats, and shoulders. This explosive bodyweight-style movement builds rotational power and core stability while letting you blow off steam. Each slam demands full-body coordination from fingertips to toes. **Pro tip:** Slam through the floor, not at it — think about driving the ball into the ground with your entire torso, not just your arm. **Biomechanics:** The rapid deceleration phase as you slam down creates high peak forces through the obliques and abs, training your core for real-world athletic movements.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball in one hand at shoulder height.
Reach the ball overhead, extending through your torso.
Slam the ball down forcefully, crunching your abs and driving through your core.
Catch or retrieve the ball and reset to the starting position.
Alternate arms each rep or complete all reps on one side before switching.
Keep your knees slightly bent to absorb impact.
tips: Generate force from your core, not just your arms
Use your whole body in a coordinated chain
This is a power exercise — move explosively

name: Otis-Up
description: **Combine a sit-up with an overhead press for a two-in-one core and upper-body burner.** **Muscles worked:** abs, chest, shoulders, and triceps. The Otis-Up challenges your core to stabilize while your arms press weight overhead, making it far more demanding than a standard sit-up. This bodyweight-compatible compound move builds functional pressing strength from a disadvantaged position. **Pro tip:** Lock out your arms at the top of the sit-up, not on the way up — press only when you're fully upright. **Biomechanics:** The shifting center of gravity as you sit up and press forces your abs to work through both spinal flexion and anti-collapse simultaneously.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet anchored, holding a weight (or just your fists) at chest level.
Perform a sit-up, curling your torso off the floor.
As you reach the top of the sit-up, press the weight straight overhead.
Lower the weight back to your chest, then slowly roll back down to the floor.
Keep your feet flat and avoid using momentum to swing up.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Overhead Stretch
description: **Open up your entire front body with one long, satisfying reach toward the sky.** **Muscles worked:** abs, lats, and shoulders. This simple standing stretch lengthens the muscles along your torso, counteracting the tightness from sitting and crunching exercises. Hold it for a few deep breaths and you'll feel your ribcage expand and your posture improve instantly. **Pro tip:** Interlace your fingers and push your palms toward the ceiling while gently leaning to each side for an even deeper stretch.
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart and arms at your sides.
Reach both arms overhead, interlacing your fingers with palms facing up.
Stretch upward as high as you can, lengthening through your entire torso.
Hold the position for 15–30 seconds while breathing deeply.
Gently release and lower your arms back down.
tips: Move slowly through the full range
Breathe deeply into the stretch
Hold end positions for 15-30 seconds

name: Pallof Press
description: **The anti-rotation press that physical therapists and strength coaches both swear by.** **Muscles worked:** abs and shoulders. Instead of moving your spine, you resist the cable trying to twist you — training your core the way it actually works in real life. This cable isolation exercise builds bulletproof stability that protects your back during heavy lifts. **Pro tip:** The farther you press your hands from your chest, the harder your core has to fight the rotation — use that to scale difficulty. **Biomechanics:** The flat resistance curve from the cable provides consistent tension throughout the press, training the deep stabilizers of the core that traditional crunches miss entirely.
instructions: Set a cable machine to chest height and attach a single handle.
Stand sideways to the machine, grasping the handle with both hands at your chest.
Step away until you feel moderate tension on the cable.
Press the handle straight out in front of your chest with arms fully extended.
Hold for 2–3 seconds, resisting the pull that tries to rotate your torso.
Bring the handle back to your chest and repeat. Complete all reps, then switch sides.
tips: Press the handle straight out from your sternum
Resist the pull — do not let your torso rotate
Stand with a slight athletic stance for stability

name: Pallof Press With Rotation
description: **Add a controlled twist to the classic Pallof press and unlock next-level rotational core strength.** **Muscles worked:** abs and shoulders. This cable compound variation layers active rotation on top of anti-rotation, training your core to both resist and produce twisting forces. It bridges the gap between stability work and athletic power. **Pro tip:** Rotate slowly and deliberately — speed kills the effectiveness of this exercise. **Biomechanics:** The cable's flat resistance profile ensures your obliques and deep stabilizers are loaded evenly through both the press and the rotation phases, creating a comprehensive core training stimulus.
instructions: Set a cable machine to chest height and attach a handle.
Stand sideways to the machine, holding the handle at your chest with both hands.
Press the handle out to arm's length in front of you.
With arms extended, rotate your torso away from the cable machine.
Rotate back to center, then return the handle to your chest.
Complete all reps facing one direction, then switch sides.
tips: Press the handle straight out from your sternum
Resist the pull — do not let your torso rotate
Stand with a slight athletic stance for stability

name: Plank
description: **The foundational hold that every strong core is built upon.** **Muscles worked:** abs and shoulders. The plank trains your entire core to resist extension under load, building the endurance and stiffness your spine needs during squats, deadlifts, and daily life. No movement required — just honest effort against gravity. **Pro tip:** Squeeze your glutes and brace like someone is about to poke your stomach — a passive plank is a wasted plank. **Biomechanics:** The flat resistance curve means tension stays constant for the entire hold, making time under tension the primary driver of adaptation in this isometric exercise.
instructions: Place your forearms on the floor with elbows directly under your shoulders.
Extend your legs behind you, resting on your toes, body forming a straight line.
Engage your core by pulling your belly button toward your spine.
Hold this position without letting your hips sag or pike up.
Breathe steadily and maintain the position for the prescribed time.
tips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs as if expecting a punch
Do not let your hips sag or pike up

name: Plank Shoulder Tap
description: **Transform a static plank into a dynamic anti-rotation challenge with one simple tap.** **Muscles worked:** abs and shoulders. Each time you lift a hand to tap the opposite shoulder, your core must fight to keep your hips from rotating. This bodyweight compound move builds stability, coordination, and shoulder endurance simultaneously. **Pro tip:** Widen your feet to make it easier, narrow them to make it brutally hard. **Biomechanics:** Removing one point of contact shifts your center of gravity, forcing the obliques and deep core muscles to fire asymmetrically — a stimulus that standard planks simply cannot provide.
instructions: Start in a high push-up position with hands under shoulders and body in a straight line.
Lift your right hand and tap your left shoulder.
Return your right hand to the floor, then lift your left hand to tap your right shoulder.
Alternate sides while keeping your hips as still as possible.
Avoid rocking side to side — the goal is a quiet, stable torso.
Continue for the prescribed reps or time.
tips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs as if expecting a punch
Do not let your hips sag or pike up

name: Plate Twist
description: **A seated rotation drill that chisels your obliques one twist at a time.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques) and shoulders. Holding a weight plate (or just clasping your hands) while twisting side to side creates sustained tension through your entire midsection. This bodyweight-friendly isolation move is simple to learn but deceptively tough at higher rep counts. **Pro tip:** Touch the plate to the floor on each side to ensure you're getting full rotation. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped loading means your obliques work hardest at the midpoint of each twist, so control the transition between sides rather than bouncing.
instructions: Sit on the floor with knees bent and feet slightly elevated or flat on the ground.
Lean back to about a 45-degree angle, keeping your back straight.
Hold a weight plate or clasp your hands at chest level.
Rotate your torso to the right, bringing the weight toward the floor beside your hip.
Rotate to the left side to complete one rep.
Move with control and keep your core braced throughout.
tips: Keep your chest up and rotate through the thoracic spine
Move with control — momentum reduces oblique tension
Exhale as you rotate to each side

name: Press Sit-Up
description: **Merge a barbell press with a sit-up for a brutally effective core-and-pressing combo.** **Muscles worked:** abs, chest, shoulders, and triceps. This barbell compound exercise forces your abs to stabilize heavy load while your upper body presses, building real-world strength you can't get from machines. It's demanding, rewarding, and efficient. **Pro tip:** Start light — the balance challenge is significant, and your core will fatigue before your arms do. **Biomechanics:** As you sit up, the barbell's center of mass shifts relative to your base of support, requiring your abs to produce flexion force while simultaneously stabilizing against lateral sway.
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent, holding a barbell at chest level with an overhand grip.
Perform a sit-up, curling your torso up off the floor.
At the top of the sit-up, press the barbell overhead to full arm extension.
Lower the barbell back to your chest, then slowly lower your torso back to the floor.
Keep the barbell stable throughout — avoid letting it drift forward or backward.
Use a spotter or light weight until you're comfortable with the movement.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Reverse Crunch
description: **Flip the crunch upside down to finally target your lower abs properly.** **Muscles worked:** abs (lower emphasis). Instead of lifting your shoulders, you curl your hips toward your ribcage, shifting the work to the often-neglected lower portion of your abs. This bodyweight isolation move is gentle on the neck and highly effective. **Pro tip:** Think about tilting your pelvis toward the ceiling rather than swinging your legs — the movement is small but powerful. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped resistance curve peaks when your hips are maximally curled, so pause at the top to capitalize on the strongest contraction point.
instructions: Lie on your back with arms at your sides and knees bent at 90 degrees, feet off the floor.
Contract your lower abs to curl your hips off the floor, bringing your knees toward your chest.
Lift until your lower back just clears the floor — don't roll onto your upper back.
Slowly lower your hips back to the starting position.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid using momentum to swing your legs.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Rope Crunch
description: **The cable crunch variation that top bodybuilders rely on for thick, visible abs.** **Muscles worked:** abs. Using a rope attachment on a cable machine lets you load your abs progressively — something bodyweight crunches can't do. The constant cable tension makes every inch of the movement productive, earning this exercise its excellent effort-to-results rating. **Pro tip:** Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis, not your head toward the floor — the distinction makes or breaks this exercise. **Biomechanics:** The flat resistance curve from the cable ensures your abs are loaded evenly from stretch to contraction, eliminating the dead zones found in gravity-based crunch variations.
instructions: Kneel in front of a cable machine with the pulley set high and a rope attachment connected.
Grab the rope handles and hold them beside your head or at your forehead.
Crunch downward by rounding your spine, bringing your elbows toward your knees.
Squeeze your abs hard at the bottom of the movement.
Slowly return to the starting position, feeling the stretch through your abs.
Keep your hips stationary — only your spine should move.
tips: Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
Lock your arms in position so the movement comes from your spine
Use a controlled tempo to maximize tension on the rectus abdominis

name: Russian Twist
description: **The go-to rotational core exercise that athletes have trusted for decades.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques). Sitting in a V-position and twisting side to side hammers your obliques while challenging your balance and hip flexor endurance. This bodyweight isolation classic is endlessly scalable — add weight when it gets easy. **Pro tip:** Slow down. Most people rush Russian twists and turn them into a momentum exercise — deliberate rotation wins. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped loading profile means your obliques peak in tension at the midpoint of each rotation, so own that middle range instead of bouncing between sides.
instructions: Sit on the floor with knees bent and feet flat or slightly elevated.
Lean back to roughly a 45-degree angle, keeping your spine straight.
Clasp your hands together or hold a weight at chest level.
Rotate your torso to the right, then to the left — that's one rep.
Keep your core tight and move from your ribcage, not your arms.
Maintain the lean-back angle throughout the set.
tips: Keep your chest up and rotate through the thoracic spine
Move with control — momentum reduces oblique tension
Exhale as you rotate to each side

name: Scissor Kick
description: **Rapid alternating leg kicks that set your lower abs on fire without any equipment.** **Muscles worked:** abs (lower emphasis). Scissor kicks keep your abs under constant tension as your legs move in opposite directions just above the floor. This bodyweight isolation exercise is deceptively simple — the burn builds fast and doesn't let up. **Pro tip:** The lower your legs hover, the harder your abs work — but only go as low as you can without your lower back lifting off the floor. **Biomechanics:** The continuous alternating motion prevents your abs from resting between reps, creating an extended time-under-tension stimulus that drives muscular endurance.
instructions: Lie flat on your back with your hands under your hips or at your sides for support.
Lift both legs a few inches off the floor.
Alternately raise one leg up while lowering the other in a scissoring motion.
Keep your legs straight and your core braced throughout.
Continue alternating for the prescribed reps or time.
Press your lower back firmly into the floor the entire time.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Seated Barbell Twist
description: **Park a barbell across your shoulders and twist your way to stronger obliques.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques). Sitting down eliminates lower-body momentum, forcing your obliques to produce every degree of rotation against the barbell's resistance. This barbell isolation exercise is straightforward and effective for building rotational endurance. **Pro tip:** Use a light bar or even a broomstick — this exercise is about range of motion and control, not heavy loading. **Biomechanics:** The barbell resting on your shoulders creates a long lever arm, amplifying rotational resistance at the obliques even with minimal weight, making the bell-shaped tension curve feel more demanding than expected.
instructions: Sit on a bench with feet flat on the floor and a barbell resting across your upper back.
Hold the barbell with a wide overhand grip for stability.
Rotate your torso to the right as far as comfortable.
Reverse and rotate to the left to complete one rep.
Keep your hips facing forward — only your upper body should twist.
Use a smooth, controlled tempo throughout.
tips: Keep your chest up and rotate through the thoracic spine
Move with control — momentum reduces oblique tension
Exhale as you rotate to each side

name: Seated Flat Bench Leg Pull-In
description: **Use a flat bench as your launchpad for a lower-ab exercise that demands real control.** **Muscles worked:** abs (lower emphasis). Sitting on the edge of a bench and pulling your knees toward your chest isolates the lower abs while the bench provides a stable base. This bodyweight isolation move is perfect for those who find floor-based exercises uncomfortable. **Pro tip:** Lean back slightly and grip the bench edges to create a longer lever and a harder contraction. **Biomechanics:** The seated position shifts the resistance curve so your abs must work against gravity through a greater range than lying variations, increasing the effective stimulus per rep.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with your hands gripping the sides for support.
Extend your legs out in front of you, slightly off the floor.
Pull your knees toward your chest by contracting your lower abs.
Slowly extend your legs back out without letting your feet touch the floor.
Keep your upper body relatively still — the movement comes from your hips and abs.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Seated Leg Tucks
description: **Tuck, squeeze, extend — a rhythm that builds lower-ab strength rep by rep.** **Muscles worked:** abs (lower emphasis). This bodyweight isolation exercise has you draw your knees in and push them back out while balancing on your sit bones, creating constant tension through your entire core. It's accessible for beginners yet challenging enough for experienced lifters at higher volumes. **Pro tip:** Exhale forcefully as you tuck your knees in — it helps activate your deep core muscles more completely. **Biomechanics:** Balancing on your sit bones removes ground contact from your legs, meaning your abs must both flex the spine and stabilize your entire body simultaneously.
instructions: Sit on the floor or edge of a bench, leaning back slightly with hands behind you for support.
Extend your legs out in front, hovering them above the floor.
Pull both knees toward your chest in a tucking motion.
Squeeze your abs at the top, then slowly extend your legs back out.
Keep your feet off the floor for the entire set.
Maintain a steady rhythm without rushing.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Seated Overhead Stretch
description: **Decompress your spine and open your side body from the comfort of a seated position.** **Muscles worked:** abs, lats, and shoulders. This seated stretch lengthens the muscles along your torso and sides, relieving tightness from prolonged sitting or intense core training. A few deep breaths here can reset your posture and reduce tension. **Pro tip:** Gently lean to each side while reaching overhead to add a lateral stretch that targets the obliques and lats.
instructions: Sit on the floor or a bench with your back straight and feet flat.
Reach both arms overhead, interlacing your fingers with palms facing up.
Stretch upward, lengthening your spine as tall as possible.
Hold for 15–30 seconds while breathing deeply and evenly.
Slowly lower your arms and relax.
tips: Move slowly through the full range
Breathe deeply into the stretch
Hold end positions for 15-30 seconds

name: Side Bridge
description: **Build a fortress of lateral core strength with this underrated side hold.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques) and shoulders. The side bridge trains your obliques to resist lateral flexion — a critical function for spinal health and athletic performance. Holding this position builds endurance in muscles that most people neglect entirely. **Pro tip:** Stack your feet or stagger them — stacking is harder, staggering gives you a wider base for learning the movement. **Biomechanics:** The bell-shaped loading profile means your obliques work hardest when your hips are at their lowest point, so focus on driving your hips up and maintaining height throughout the hold.
instructions: Lie on your side with your elbow directly under your shoulder and legs stacked.
Lift your hips off the floor until your body forms a straight line from head to feet.
Hold this position, keeping your core tight and hips elevated.
Breathe steadily and avoid letting your hips drop.
Hold for the prescribed time, then switch sides.
tips: Focus on the side crunch — bring your elbow toward the opposite knee
Keep the movement controlled to avoid momentum
Exhale as you contract the obliques

name: Side Jackknife
description: **A lateral crunch that brings your elbow and knee together for a deep oblique squeeze.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques). Lying on your side and crunching up simultaneously with your upper body and legs creates a powerful contraction through the obliques. This bodyweight isolation move is one of the few exercises that truly isolates the side abs through a full range of motion. **Pro tip:** Focus on the squeeze at the top — if your elbow and knee aren't getting close, you're not crunching hard enough. **Biomechanics:** The side-lying position eliminates the abs from dominating, ensuring the obliques bear the primary load through the bell-shaped resistance curve.
instructions: Lie on your side with your bottom arm extended on the floor for balance.
Place your top hand behind your head with your elbow pointing toward the ceiling.
Simultaneously lift your top leg and crunch your upper body sideways, bringing your elbow toward your knee.
Squeeze your obliques at the top of the movement.
Lower back down with control and repeat for all reps before switching sides.
tips: Focus on the side crunch — bring your elbow toward the opposite knee
Keep the movement controlled to avoid momentum
Exhale as you contract the obliques

name: Side Plank
description: **The single best bodyweight exercise for building oblique endurance and lateral stability.** **Muscles worked:** abs (obliques) and shoulders. Holding your body in a straight line on one forearm trains your obliques, glutes, and shoulder stabilizers all at once. This isometric hold is a staple in rehab, performance training, and general fitness for good reason. **Pro tip:** Drive your bottom hip toward the ceiling aggressively — most people sag without realizing it. **Biomechanics:** The flat resistance curve provides constant tension on the obliques for the entire hold duration, making time under tension the key variable for progression in this exercise.
instructions: Lie on your side with your bottom forearm on the floor, elbow directly under your shoulder.
Stack your feet on top of each other or stagger them for more stability.
Lift your hips until your body forms a straight line from head to feet.
Hold this position while keeping your core engaged and hips lifted.
Breathe normally and avoid rotating forward or backward.
Hold for the prescribed time, then switch sides.
tips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs as if expecting a punch
Do not let your hips sag or pike up

name: Sit-Up
description:
**A classic abdominal exercise that trains spinal flexion through a full range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The sit-up involves lifting the entire torso off the ground, engaging the abs and hip flexors to bring the chest toward the knees.

**Pro tip:** Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
instructions: Lie flat on your back with knees bent and feet anchored or flat on the floor.
Place your hands behind your head or across your chest.
Brace your core and curl your torso upward, lifting your lower back off the ground until your chest approaches your thighs.
Lower yourself back down under control to the starting position.
Avoid pulling on your neck and focus on driving the movement with your abdominals.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Sledgehammer Swings
description:
**An explosive full-body movement that challenges rotational core strength and power.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: forearms, lats, shoulders, calves.

Sledgehammer swings involve driving a sledgehammer into a tire using coordinated trunk rotation, hip drive, and shoulder engagement.

**Pro tip:** Generate force from your core, not just your arms
instructions: Stand facing a large tire with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a sledgehammer with a staggered grip.
Raise the hammer overhead, allowing one hand to slide toward the hammer head.
Explosively swing downward, rotating your torso and driving through your hips to strike the tire.
Absorb the rebound, reset your stance, and repeat.
Alternate sides each set to develop balanced rotational power.
tips: Generate force from your core, not just your arms
Use your whole body in a coordinated chain
This is a power exercise — move explosively

name: Smith Machine Hip Raise
description:
**A lower abdominal exercise performed on a Smith machine that uses the fixed bar path to add resistance to a hip raise movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The guided bar allows for progressive overload on the lower abs in a controlled manner.

**Pro tip:** Focus on curling your pelvis up, not swinging your legs
instructions: Lie on a bench positioned inside a Smith machine with the bar set at a low position.
Grip the bar with both hands for stability and extend your legs upward.
Contract your lower abs to lift your hips off the bench, pressing your feet toward the ceiling.
Hold briefly at the top, then lower your hips back down with control.
Keep your legs relatively straight and avoid using momentum.
tips: Focus on curling your pelvis up, not swinging your legs
Keep the movement small and controlled
Exhale as you lift your hips

name: Spell Caster
description:
**A dynamic rotational exercise using dumbbells that targets the obliques and deep core muscles through a sweeping diagonal movement pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: glutes, shoulders.

The spell caster combines hip rotation with weighted arm swings to challenge core stability and power.

**Pro tip:** Generate force from your core, not just your arms
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand at one hip.
Rotate your torso and swing both dumbbells diagonally across your body, finishing with arms extended above the opposite shoulder.
Reverse the motion, swinging back down and across to the starting hip.
Maintain a slight bend in your knees and let your hips rotate naturally with the movement.
Complete all reps in one direction before switching.
tips: Generate force from your core, not just your arms
Use your whole body in a coordinated chain
This is a power exercise — move explosively

name: Spider Crawl
description:
**A dynamic crawling movement performed from a push-up position that challenges core stability while simultaneously working the upper body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: chest, shoulders, triceps.

Each stride requires anti-rotation strength as you bring your knee toward your elbow.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips level — do not let them pike up or sag
instructions: Begin in a push-up position with arms extended and body in a straight line.
Step one hand forward while simultaneously driving the opposite knee toward the same-side elbow.
Alternate sides, crawling forward while keeping your hips low and stable.
Maintain a tight core throughout to prevent your hips from swaying side to side.
Move deliberately and keep your body as close to the ground as possible.
tips: Keep your hips level — do not let them pike up or sag
Drive your knees toward your chest with control
Maintain a braced core throughout

name: Standing Cable Lift
description:
**A diagonal pulling pattern performed on a cable machine that trains the obliques and core through an upward rotational movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, glutes.

The constant cable tension provides consistent resistance throughout the entire range of motion, making it highly effective for functional core strength.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the lowest position and attach a rope or handle.
Stand sideways to the machine with feet wider than shoulder-width.
Grip the handle with both hands near the low-side hip.
Rotate your torso and pull the handle diagonally upward across your body, finishing with arms extended above the opposite shoulder.
Control the return to the starting position.
Keep your hips stable and drive the rotation from your core, not your arms.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Standing Cable Wood Chop
description:
**A rotational core exercise performed on a cable machine using a high-to-low chopping motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, glutes.

The cable provides uniform tension throughout the movement, effectively training the obliques, deep core muscles, and the entire anti-rotation musculature of the trunk.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position and attach a rope or handle.
Stand sideways to the machine with feet shoulder-width apart.
Grasp the handle with both hands above the near shoulder.
In one fluid motion, pull the handle diagonally downward across your body, rotating your torso until your hands reach the opposite hip.
Slowly return to the start.
Pivot on your feet slightly but keep your hips relatively square to generate force from the core.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Standing Lateral Stretch
description:
**A lateral flexion stretch that targets the obliques and intercostal muscles along the side of the torso.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This movement improves lateral mobility and can be used as a warm-up or cooldown exercise to maintain flexibility in the trunk.

**Pro tip:** Move slowly through the full range
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart.
Raise one arm overhead and reach it toward the opposite side, bending your torso laterally.
Keep your hips stationary and avoid leaning forward or backward.
Hold the stretched position for a moment, feeling the lengthening along the side of your ribcage.
Return to upright and repeat on the other side.
Breathe deeply into the stretch and avoid bouncing.
tips: Move slowly through the full range
Breathe deeply into the stretch
Hold end positions for 15-30 seconds

name: Standing Rope Crunch
description:
**A highly effective cable abdominal exercise performed from a standing position using a rope attachment.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The constant cable tension and standing posture allow for heavy loading of the abs with an excellent effort-to-results ratio.

**Pro tip:** Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
instructions: Attach a rope to a high cable pulley.
Stand facing the machine and grasp the rope ends beside your head.
With a slight forward lean, crunch your ribcage downward toward your pelvis by flexing your spine.
Squeeze your abs hard at the bottom of the movement, then slowly extend back to the starting position.
Keep your hips locked in place so the movement comes entirely from spinal flexion, not hip bending.
tips: Crunch your ribcage toward your pelvis — do not just bow at the hips
Lock your arms in position so the movement comes from your spine
Use a controlled tempo to maximize tension on the rectus abdominis

name: Stomach Vacuum
description:
**An isometric exercise that specifically targets the deep core muscles, the deepest layer of abdominal muscle responsible for cinching the waistline.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

Stomach vacuums train the ability to draw the navel inward and hold tension, improving core control and posture.

**Pro tip:** Exhale all air, then pull your navel toward your spine
instructions: Stand upright or position yourself on all fours.
Exhale all the air from your lungs completely.
Without inhaling, draw your belly button inward toward your spine as far as possible, hollowing out your stomach.
Hold this contracted position for 15 to 30 seconds while taking small, shallow breaths through your chest.
Release and repeat.
Progress by increasing hold duration over time.
tips: Exhale all air, then pull your navel toward your spine
Hold the contraction while breathing shallowly
Practice on all fours before progressing to standing

name: Suitcase Carry
description:
**A unilateral loaded carry that challenges the obliques and quadratus lumborum to resist lateral flexion under load.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: forearms, traps.

Holding a heavy weight on one side forces the core to work overtime to keep the torso upright, building functional anti-lateral-flexion strength.

**Pro tip:** Walk tall — do not lean away from the weight
instructions: Pick up a heavy dumbbell or kettlebell in one hand and let it hang at your side.
Stand tall with shoulders level and core braced.
Walk forward with controlled, even steps, resisting the urge to lean toward the loaded side.
Keep your shoulders square and your torso perfectly upright throughout.
Walk for a set distance or time, then switch hands.
Grip the weight firmly and maintain a steady breathing pattern.
tips: Walk tall — do not lean away from the weight
Keep your shoulders level
Brace your core as if expecting a push from the side

name: Supine One-Arm Overhead Throw
description:
**An explosive medicine ball exercise performed from a lying position that develops rotational power and abdominal force production.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: chest, lats, shoulders.

The single-arm variation adds an anti-rotation demand, challenging the obliques asymmetrically.

**Pro tip:** Generate force from your core, not just your arms
instructions: Lie face-up on the floor with knees bent, holding a medicine ball in one hand at chest level.
Brace your core and explosively sit up while simultaneously throwing the ball overhead with one arm toward a wall or partner.
Use the momentum of the sit-up to power the throw.
Catch or retrieve the ball, return to the lying position, and repeat.
Complete all reps on one side before switching arms.
tips: Generate force from your core, not just your arms
Use your whole body in a coordinated chain
This is a power exercise — move explosively

name: Supine Two-Arm Overhead Throw
description:
**A powerful plyometric core exercise where a medicine ball is thrown overhead from a supine position.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: chest, lats, shoulders.

This movement trains the abdominals to generate explosive force through rapid spinal flexion while integrating the lats and shoulders.

**Pro tip:** Generate force from your core, not just your arms
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and hold a medicine ball with both hands extended behind your head.
In one explosive motion, engage your abs to sit up while launching the ball overhead toward a wall or training partner.
Follow through with your arms and torso.
Catch the ball on the return, lower back to the starting position with control, and immediately repeat.
Focus on generating maximum power from the core on each throw.
tips: Generate force from your core, not just your arms
Use your whole body in a coordinated chain
This is a power exercise — move explosively

name: Suspended Fallout
description:
**An advanced anti-collapse core exercise performed using suspension straps that places the abdominals under significant stretch and tension.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: chest, lats, shoulders.

As the arms extend overhead, the lever arm lengthens dramatically, demanding intense core engagement to prevent spinal hyperextension.

**Pro tip:** Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
instructions: Grasp the handles of a suspension trainer and lean forward into a plank position with arms extended in front of you.
Slowly push your hands forward and overhead, allowing your body to extend into a long, straight line.
Go only as far as you can while maintaining a rigid torso without letting your lower back sag.
Reverse the motion by pulling your hands back underneath your shoulders using your abs and lats.
Keep the movement slow and controlled throughout.
tips: Keep your hips from sagging — maintain posterior pelvic tilt throughout
Only roll out as far as you can control without low back extension
Squeeze your glutes to lock your pelvis in position

name: Suspended Reverse Crunch
description:
**A lower abdominal exercise performed with feet suspended in straps, adding instability to a reverse crunch pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The suspension element forces the core to stabilize in multiple planes while the tucking motion targets the lower abs.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Place your feet in the foot cradles of a suspension trainer and assume a push-up position with arms straight.
From this plank position, tuck your knees toward your chest by curling your hips upward and rounding your lower back.
Squeeze your abs at peak contraction, then slowly extend your legs back to the plank position.
Avoid letting your hips sag between reps and keep the movement controlled to maximize abdominal engagement.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Toe Touch
description:
**A supine abdominal exercise where you reach your hands toward your elevated feet, creating a short but intense contraction of the upper abs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The vertical leg position shortens the hip flexor contribution, isolating the abs more effectively than a standard crunch.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back and raise both legs straight up toward the ceiling so they are perpendicular to the floor.
Extend your arms toward your feet.
Contract your abs to lift your shoulder blades off the ground, reaching your fingertips toward your toes.
Hold the top position briefly, then lower your upper back to the floor with control.
Keep your legs stationary throughout and avoid using momentum to swing upward.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Toe Touchers
description:
**A dynamic abdominal exercise combining simultaneous leg and torso movement to create a V-shaped folding motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

Both the upper and lower body move toward each other, demanding coordinated contraction of the entire abs.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie flat on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight on the floor.
Simultaneously raise your legs and torso off the ground, reaching your hands toward your feet at the top.
Your body should form a V-shape at peak contraction.
Lower both your upper and lower body back to the floor in a controlled manner.
Exhale forcefully as you fold upward and maintain tension in your abs throughout the descent.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Torso Rotation
description:
**A seated or standing rotational movement that targets the internal and external obliques through controlled twisting of the trunk.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This exercise develops rotational mobility and muscular endurance in the core without requiring external load.

**Pro tip:** Keep your chest up and rotate through the thoracic spine
instructions: Sit on the floor with knees bent or stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Extend your arms in front of you or hold them at chest level.
Rotate your torso to one side as far as your mobility allows, keeping your hips facing forward.
Pause briefly, then rotate to the opposite side.
Move deliberately and avoid jerking motions.
Focus on initiating the rotation from your obliques rather than swinging your arms to generate momentum.
tips: Keep your chest up and rotate through the thoracic spine
Move with control — momentum reduces oblique tension
Exhale as you rotate to each side

name: Tuck Crunch
description:
**A crunch variation that involves simultaneously drawing the knees toward the chest while crunching the upper body forward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

This dual-action movement engages both the upper and lower portions of the abs in a compact range of motion.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with hands beside your head and feet lifted slightly off the floor.
Simultaneously crunch your upper body forward and pull your knees toward your chest, bringing your elbows and knees close together.
Squeeze your abs tightly at the top of the movement.
Extend back out to the starting position without letting your feet or shoulders touch the ground between reps.
Maintain constant tension on the abdominals throughout the set.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: V-Up
description:
**An advanced bodyweight abdominal exercise that requires simultaneously lifting the legs and torso to form a V-shape at the top.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The V-up demands strong coordination and significant abdominal strength to control both the concentric and eccentric phases.

**Pro tip:** Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
instructions: Lie flat on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight.
In one powerful motion, raise both your legs and upper body simultaneously, reaching your hands toward your shins or toes.
Your body should form a V at the peak.
Lower back down slowly with both your legs and torso returning to the floor at the same time.
Avoid slamming down between reps and keep the movement smooth and controlled.
Exhale as you fold up and inhale on the way down.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Weighted Ball Side Bend
description:
**A lateral flexion exercise performed while holding a medicine ball or weighted ball overhead or at the chest.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The added weight increases the demand on the obliques as you bend sideways, providing a targeted stimulus for the lateral core musculature.

**Pro tip:** Only hold weight on one side — the working side is the one being stretched
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a medicine ball at chest level or overhead with both hands.
Keeping your hips stationary, bend your torso laterally to one side, lowering the ball toward that hip.
Feel a deep stretch on the opposite oblique, then contract the stretched side to return to an upright position.
Perform all reps on one side before switching, or alternate sides each rep.
Avoid rotating your torso and keep the movement strictly in the lateral plane.
tips: Only hold weight on one side — the working side is the one being stretched
Avoid rotating — keep the movement strictly in the frontal plane
Control the eccentric to maximize oblique tension
coaching_cues: Feel the stretch on the opposite side

name: Weighted Crunch
description:
**A loaded variation of the standard crunch that uses a dumbbell held at the chest or behind the head to increase resistance on the abs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

Adding external weight allows for progressive overload, making this a practical choice for building abdominal strength and size.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell against your upper chest with both hands or behind your head.
Curl your shoulders and upper back off the floor by contracting your abs, bringing your ribcage toward your pelvis.
Pause at the top and squeeze, then lower back down under control.
Keep your lower back pressed into the floor and avoid pulling with your arms or neck.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Weighted Crunches
description:
**A resistance-enhanced crunch variation where external load is applied using a weight plate, medicine ball, or similar implement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The added resistance transforms the basic crunch into a strength-building exercise capable of driving abdominal hypertrophy when performed with progressive overload.

**Pro tip:** Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
instructions: Lie face-up with knees bent and feet flat on the ground.
Hold a weight plate or medicine ball against your chest or extend it above your chest with straight arms.
Engage your abs to curl your upper back off the floor, driving your sternum toward your hips.
Hold the peak contraction for a count, then slowly lower back to the starting position.
Keep the weight secure throughout and focus on smooth, controlled reps without jerking upward.
tips: Lift your shoulder blades off the floor — that is the full range for a crunch
Do not pull on your neck with your hands
Focus on shortening the distance between your ribs and pelvis

name: Weighted Hanging Leg Raise
description:
**An advanced hanging leg raise variation with a dumbbell secured between the feet to increase resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: forearms.

This exercise heavily loads the lower abdominals and hip flexors while also demanding significant grip strength to maintain the hang under added weight.

**Pro tip:** Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with a dumbbell secured between your feet or ankles.
Start with legs hanging straight down.
Raise your legs by flexing at the hips and curling your pelvis upward, bringing your feet toward the bar.
Focus on posterior pelvic tilt at the top to maximize abdominal contraction.
Lower your legs slowly back to the starting position, resisting the weight on the way down.
Avoid swinging and keep the movement strict.
tips: Curl your pelvis up at the top — simple leg raising only trains hip flexors
Minimize swinging by engaging your lats to stabilize
Control the descent to load the abs eccentrically

name: Weighted Plank
description:
**A loaded isometric core exercise where a weight plate is placed on the upper back during a standard plank hold.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders.

The additional resistance increases the anti-collapse demand on the abdominals, making the exercise more challenging without changing body position.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a straight line from head to heels
instructions: Assume a forearm plank position with elbows directly under your shoulders and body forming a straight line from head to heels.
Have a training partner place a weight plate on your upper back between the shoulder blades.
Brace your core, squeeze your glutes, and hold the position for the prescribed duration.
Keep your hips level, avoiding any sagging or piking.
Breathe steadily throughout and focus on maintaining total body rigidity.
tips: Maintain a straight line from head to heels
Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs as if expecting a punch
Do not let your hips sag or pike up

name: Weighted Sit-Ups - With Bands
description:
**A banded variation of the classic sit-up that adds accommodating resistance through the range of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The band tension increases as you rise, forcing the abdominals to work harder at peak contraction where they are typically strongest.

**Pro tip:** Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
instructions: Anchor a resistance band behind you at floor level and loop it around your upper back or hold the ends at your chest.
Lie face-up with knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Brace your core and curl your torso upward, rolling through the spine until your chest approaches your thighs.
Control the descent back to the floor, resisting the band's pull throughout the eccentric phase.
Avoid yanking with your neck or using hip flexor momentum to complete the rep.
tips: Initiate by curling your spine, not by pulling with your hip flexors
Anchor your feet for stability if needed
Exhale forcefully as you curl up to maximize rectus abdominis activation

name: Wind Sprints
description:
**A rapid abdominal exercise performed in a seated or reclined position that mimics a sprinting motion with the arms and legs.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: none.

The fast-paced alternating limb movement keeps constant tension on the abs and challenges core stability under speed.

**Pro tip:** Press your lower back into the floor throughout
instructions: Sit on the floor with your torso leaned back at roughly 45 degrees, knees bent, and feet hovering just above the ground.
Rapidly drive one knee toward your chest while simultaneously pumping the opposite arm forward, then switch sides in a sprinting motion.
Keep your core braced and your lower back from rounding excessively.
Maintain a controlled breathing rhythm and perform the movement for the prescribed time or rep count without letting your feet touch the floor.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor throughout
Curl your pelvis at the top to engage the abs, not just the hip flexors
Lower your legs only as far as you can maintain a flat back
coaching_cues: Curl the hips at the top

name: Woodchop
description:
**A diagonal rotational movement performed on a cable machine that trains the obliques and entire core through a chopping pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, glutes.

The cable provides consistent resistance throughout the arc, making it highly effective for developing anti-rotation strength and rotational power.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position and attach a rope or single handle.
Stand sideways to the machine with feet shoulder-width apart.
Grasp the handle with both hands above the shoulder closest to the machine.
In one fluid motion, pull the handle diagonally across your body toward the opposite hip, rotating through your torso while keeping your arms relatively straight.
Your hips and knees should rotate slightly to allow natural movement.
Return to the start under control.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Woodchopper
description:
**A cable-based rotational exercise that can be performed high-to-low or low-to-high, targeting the obliques, deep core muscles, and hip complex.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, glutes.

The constant cable tension trains the core through its primary function of producing and resisting rotation.

**Pro tip:** Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
instructions: Attach a handle to a cable pulley set at either the top or bottom position.
Stand perpendicular to the machine with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Grip the handle with both hands and arms extended.
Rotate your torso powerfully to sweep the handle across your body in a diagonal arc—high to low or low to high depending on pulley placement.
Keep your arms long but not locked, and let your back foot pivot naturally.
Decelerate the movement smoothly at the end range, then reverse the path.
tips: Rotate through your thoracic spine, not your lumbar spine
Keep your arms relatively straight — the power comes from your core
Control the return phase to load the obliques eccentrically
coaching_cues: Arms are levers, core does the work

name: Assault Bike
description:
**A fan-driven stationary bike that uses both arm and leg action to generate resistance.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: hamstrings, glutes, shoulders, calves.

The harder you pedal and push, the greater the air resistance becomes, making it a self-regulating total-body conditioning tool primarily used for cardiovascular training and caloric expenditure.

**Pro tip:** Use this as active recovery or warm-up rather than a primary quad builder
instructions: Adjust the seat height so your knee has a slight bend at the bottom of the pedal stroke.
Grip the handles firmly and begin pedaling while simultaneously pushing and pulling the arms.
Drive through the balls of your feet on the downstroke and actively pull up on the opposite pedal.
Coordinate your arm pushes and pulls with the opposing leg to maintain a smooth rhythm.
Keep your torso relatively upright and avoid excessive rocking.
Use for steady-state cardio at a moderate pace or high-intensity intervals with maximal effort bursts.
tips: Use this as active recovery or warm-up rather than a primary quad builder
Focus on consistent effort rather than maximum intensity
Stay hydrated and maintain steady breathing throughout

name: Barbell Clean
description:
**An Olympic weightlifting movement that explosively pulls a barbell from the floor to the front rack position in one continuous effort.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, traps, shoulders, erectors, forearms.

It develops total-body power, coordination, and rate of force development through the posterior chain and upper back.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, the barbell over mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips and grip the bar just outside your knees with a hook or overhand grip.
Set your back flat, chest up, and shoulders slightly ahead of the bar.
Initiate the pull by driving through the floor with your legs.
As the bar passes the knees, aggressively extend your hips and shrug upward.
Pull yourself under the bar by dropping into a front squat position, catching it across the front delts with elbows high.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Barbell Complex
description:
**A conditioning protocol that chains multiple barbell movements together without releasing the bar.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: quads, glutes, hamstrings, traps, lats, triceps.

Typically combining cleans, presses, rows, squats, and deadlifts in sequence, it creates extreme metabolic demand and challenges muscular endurance across the entire body.

**Pro tip:** Master each component separately before combining them
instructions: Load a barbell with a weight you can press overhead for the prescribed reps—this will be the limiting factor.
Perform each movement in the complex for the designated number of reps before transitioning immediately to the next exercise without setting the bar down.
A common sequence is: deadlift, bent-over row, hang clean, front squat, overhead press.
Maintain solid form on every movement despite accumulating fatigue.
Rest only after completing the full sequence.
Repeat for the prescribed number of rounds.
tips: Master each component separately before combining them
The clean positions the bar for the press — don't rush the transition
These are power and conditioning movements, not hypertrophy exercises
Fatigue accumulates fast — keep reps low

name: Battle Rope Slam
description:
**An explosive conditioning exercise where heavy ropes are raised overhead and slammed forcefully into the ground.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, lats, forearms.

The movement demands rapid shoulder flexion and extension combined with a powerful core crunch, delivering high metabolic stress and upper-body endurance work.

**Pro tip:** These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
instructions: Stand facing the anchor point with feet shoulder-width apart, holding one end of the rope in each hand.
Sink into a quarter squat with a braced core.
Explosively raise both arms overhead, extending onto your toes, then violently slam the ropes downward into the floor while dropping your hips into a squat.
The power should originate from your hips and core, not just your arms.
Immediately reset and repeat in a continuous rhythm.
Keep your chest up and avoid rounding your lower back throughout the set.
tips: These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
The shoulders will fatigue as part of the full-body effort
Keep form consistent even as fatigue accumulates
Useful for work capacity and general fitness

name: Bear Crawl
description:
**A primal locomotion pattern performed on all fours with the knees hovering just above the ground.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, quads, triceps.

It builds shoulder stability, core endurance, and coordination while elevating heart rate, making it a versatile warm-up or conditioning drill.

**Pro tip:** These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
instructions: Start on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips.
Lift your knees one to two inches off the ground so your shins are parallel to the floor.
Move forward by simultaneously advancing the opposite hand and foot—right hand with left foot, then left hand with right foot.
Keep your hips low and level, avoiding excessive side-to-side sway.
Your back should remain flat and your core tight throughout.
Crawl for the prescribed distance or time, then reverse direction if needed.
tips: These are conditioning tools — don't expect targeted deltoid growth
The shoulders will fatigue as part of the full-body effort
Keep form consistent even as fatigue accumulates
Useful for work capacity and general fitness

name: Box Jump
description:
**A plyometric exercise where you explosively jump from the floor onto an elevated platform.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

It develops lower-body power, reactive strength, and fast-twitch muscle fiber recruitment while teaching the body to produce force rapidly.

**Pro tip:** Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
instructions: Stand facing a sturdy box or platform at roughly arm's length distance.
Set your feet hip- to shoulder-width apart.
Swing your arms back as you dip into a quarter squat, then explosively drive upward, swinging your arms forward and tucking your knees to land softly on top of the box.
Land with both feet fully on the surface in a partial squat position with knees tracking over toes.
Stand tall at the top to complete the rep.
Step down carefully—do not jump down repeatedly, as this adds unnecessary impact stress.
tips: Land softly with bent knees to absorb impact forces safely
Focus on explosive intent — maximum effort on each takeoff
Keep your core braced throughout to maintain body control in the air
Start with lower heights or distances and progress gradually
Allow full recovery between sets — these are power exercises, not endurance

name: Broad Jump
description:
**A horizontal plyometric jump that emphasizes forward distance rather than vertical height.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves.

It trains the posterior chain and quads to produce powerful hip extension and develops horizontal force production, which transfers well to sprinting and athletic performance.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart at the edge of a clear landing area.
Swing your arms behind you while hinging at the hips and bending your knees into a loaded position.
Explode forward and upward at roughly a 45-degree angle, driving your arms forward aggressively.
Extend your hips, knees, and ankles fully at takeoff.
In the air, bring your feet forward and prepare to absorb the landing.
Touch down softly with both feet, bending your knees and hips to decelerate.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet for maximum power output
Use your arms to generate momentum and increase jump height
Land with your knees tracking over your toes — avoid valgus collapse
Quality of each rep matters more than quantity — stop when power drops

name: Burpee
description:
**A full-body calisthenic movement that combines a squat thrust, push-up, and vertical jump into one continuous sequence.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: chest, shoulders, triceps, glutes, abs.

It is one of the most metabolically demanding bodyweight exercises, used extensively for conditioning, fat loss, and building work capacity.

**Pro tip:** Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Drop into a squat and place your hands on the floor just in front of your feet.
Kick your feet back to land in a push-up position with a rigid torso.
Perform a push-up by lowering your chest to the floor and pressing back up.
Jump your feet forward toward your hands, returning to the squat position.
Immediately explode upward into a vertical jump, reaching your arms overhead.
tips: Minimize ground contact time to train the stretch-shortening cycle
Keep your chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement
Warm up thoroughly before performing any plyometric exercises
coaching_cues: Drive through the balls of your feet

name: Clean and Press
description:
**A two-part barbell lift that combines an explosive clean from the floor to the front rack with a strict or push press overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: quads, glutes, hamstrings, traps, triceps, erectors, forearms, abs, calves.

It is one of the most complete strength-and-power exercises, demanding coordination across nearly every major muscle group in the body.

**Pro tip:** Master each component separately before combining them
instructions: Set up as you would for a barbell clean: feet hip-width, bar over mid-foot, flat back, chest proud.
Pull the bar from the floor using leg drive and hip extension, catching it in the front rack position across your anterior delts with elbows high.
From here, dip slightly at the knees and drive the bar overhead to full lockout, finishing with the bar directly over your midfoot, biceps by your ears.
Lower the bar back to the front rack, then to the hang or floor position.
Each rep should be one fluid sequence: clean, stabilize, press, lower.
tips: Master each component separately before combining them
The clean positions the bar for the press — don't rush the transition
These are power and conditioning movements, not hypertrophy exercises
Fatigue accumulates fast — keep reps low

name: Devil Press
description:
**A grueling full-body dumbbell movement that merges a burpee with a double dumbbell snatch.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest, glutes, quads, hamstrings, triceps, lats.

It demands explosive hip extension, pressing power, and cardiovascular endurance, making it a staple in high-intensity functional training workouts.

**Pro tip:** Master each component separately before combining them
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Hinge forward and place the dumbbells on the floor, then jump your feet back into a push-up position with hands still on the dumbbells.
Perform a push-up.
Jump your feet forward outside the dumbbells.
In one powerful motion, swing the dumbbells between your legs and use an aggressive hip snap to drive them overhead in a wide arc, finishing with arms locked out above your head.
Lower the dumbbells back to your sides and immediately begin the next rep.
tips: Master each component separately before combining them
The clean positions the bar for the press — don't rush the transition
These are power and conditioning movements, not hypertrophy exercises
Fatigue accumulates fast — keep reps low

name: Dumbbell Clean
description:
**A dumbbell variation of the Olympic clean that pulls the weight from a low position to the front rack in one explosive movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, traps, shoulders, erectors, forearms.

The independent loading of each arm challenges unilateral coordination and grip strength while building total-body power.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in one or both hands at arm's length in front of your thighs.
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees slightly to lower the dumbbells to just below knee height.
Explosively extend your hips and knees, shrugging the weight upward.
As the dumbbell rises, rotate your elbows under and catch it at shoulder height in the front rack position with your elbow pointing forward.
Stand tall to finish the rep.
Lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control and repeat.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Dumbbell Snatch
description:
**A single-arm explosive lift that takes a dumbbell from between the legs to full overhead lockout in one unbroken movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

It builds unilateral power, shoulder stability, and hip explosiveness while demanding significant coordination and timing.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Straddle a dumbbell on the floor or hold it at arm's length between your legs with feet slightly wider than hip-width.
Hinge at the hips with a flat back and grip the dumbbell with one hand.
Drive through your feet, extending your hips and knees explosively.
As the dumbbell accelerates upward, keep it close to your body and punch your hand toward the ceiling, catching it at full arm extension overhead.
Your palm can face forward or inward at lockout.
Stabilize overhead with your bicep near your ear, then lower the weight back to the start.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Dumbbell Thruster
description:
**A seamless combination of a front squat and overhead press performed with dumbbells.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: glutes, triceps, abs, quads.

The momentum generated from the squat drives the press, allowing heavier loads overhead than a strict press alone while creating significant metabolic demand.

**Pro tip:** The squat drives the press — use the momentum from standing up
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing inward or forward, elbows tucked.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Descend into a full front squat, keeping your torso upright and elbows from dropping.
Drive out of the bottom aggressively through your heels.
As you reach full hip and knee extension, use that upward momentum to press both dumbbells overhead to lockout.
Lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height as you simultaneously begin your descent into the next squat.
tips: The squat drives the press — use the momentum from standing up
Keep the bar or dumbbells in a front rack position during the squat
These are metabolically brutal — expect cardiovascular limitation before deltoid failure
Better for conditioning than isolated deltoid hypertrophy

name: Hang Clean
description:
**A clean variation that begins from the hang position—typically at or just above the knees—rather than the floor.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, traps, shoulders, erectors, forearms.

By eliminating the first pull, it isolates the explosive second pull and catch, making it an excellent tool for developing hip power and clean technique.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Unrack or deadlift the barbell to a standing position with an overhand or hook grip just outside the knees.
Hinge at the hips and push them back, lowering the bar to just above knee level while maintaining a flat back and slight knee bend.
From this hang position, explosively extend your hips and knees, shrugging the bar upward.
Pull yourself under the bar and catch it in the front rack position across your anterior delts, dropping into a partial or full front squat.
Stand to complete the rep.
Return the bar to the hang position for the next rep.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Hang Snatch
description:
**An Olympic lifting variation that takes the barbell from the hang position to full overhead lockout in one explosive movement.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

Starting from the hang emphasizes the violent hip extension and overhead receiving position, building speed and positional awareness.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Hold the barbell with a wide snatch grip at arm's length while standing.
