Hinge at the hips to lower the bar to just above the knees, keeping your back flat and shoulders over the bar.
Explosively drive your hips forward and upward, pulling the bar close to your body.
As the bar reaches its peak height, aggressively pull yourself underneath it, punching your arms to full lockout overhead while dropping into an overhead squat.
Stabilize with the bar directly over your midfoot, then stand to complete the lift.
Lower the bar back to the hang position with control.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Kettlebell Clean and Press
description:
**A kettlebell combination lift that cleans the bell to the rack position and then presses it overhead.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: glutes, quads, hamstrings, traps, triceps, erectors, forearms.

The offset center of mass of the kettlebell demands greater grip and wrist stability than barbell or dumbbell equivalents, building functional pressing strength and total-body coordination.

**Pro tip:** Master each component separately before combining them
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, the kettlebell on the floor between your feet.
Hike the kettlebell back between your legs with one hand, then snap your hips forward to clean it to the rack position—bell resting against your forearm, elbow tight to your ribs, wrist straight.
From the rack, brace your core and press the kettlebell overhead by driving your arm straight up to full lockout, rotating your palm forward at the top.
Lower the bell back to the rack, then back to the swing position between your legs.
Repeat for reps, then switch sides.
tips: Master each component separately before combining them
The clean positions the bar for the press — don't rush the transition
These are power and conditioning movements, not hypertrophy exercises
Fatigue accumulates fast — keep reps low

name: Kettlebell Snatch
description:
**A ballistic kettlebell movement that swings the bell from between the legs to a locked-out overhead position in one fluid arc.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

Often called the 'Tsar of kettlebell exercises,' it builds explosive hip power, grip endurance, and shoulder stability simultaneously.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Stand over the kettlebell with feet slightly wider than hip-width.
Hike the bell back between your legs with one hand, keeping your back flat.
Explosively extend your hips to accelerate the kettlebell forward and upward.
As the bell rises past chest height, guide it over your hand by loosening your grip slightly and punching your fist toward the ceiling.
The bell should land softly on the back of your forearm at full lockout overhead.
Avoid letting the bell slam against your wrist by timing the insertion correctly.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Kettlebell Windmill
description:
**A slow, controlled movement that combines a lateral hip hinge with an overhead kettlebell hold.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, glutes, hamstrings.

It deeply challenges oblique stability, hip mobility, and shoulder endurance while providing an excellent stretch through the posterior chain of the lowering side.

**Pro tip:** Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
instructions: Press or clean-and-press a kettlebell overhead with one arm, locking it out completely.
Turn your feet roughly 45 degrees away from the loaded side.
Keeping your eyes on the kettlebell at all times, push your hip out toward the loaded side and slowly hinge laterally, sliding your free hand down your inner leg toward the floor.
Your overhead arm must remain vertical and locked throughout.
Descend as far as your mobility allows while maintaining a straight line from the kettlebell to your grounded foot.
Reverse the movement by driving your hip forward and standing tall.
tips: Keep your eyes on the overhead weight throughout
Push your hip out to the side of the overhead arm
Maintain a slow, controlled descent

name: Man Maker
description:
**One of the most demanding dumbbell complexes, combining a push-up, renegade row on each side, squat clean, and overhead press into a single grueling repetition.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: chest, quads, glutes, triceps, lats, abs.

It taxes virtually every muscle group and drives heart rate through the roof.

**Pro tip:** Master each component separately before combining them
instructions: Begin standing with a dumbbell in each hand.
Place the dumbbells on the floor and jump your feet back into a push-up position, hands gripping the dumbbells.
Perform a push-up.
At the top, row one dumbbell to your hip while stabilizing on the other, then repeat on the opposite side.
Jump your feet forward to the outside of the dumbbells and clean them to shoulder height as you stand.
Immediately press both dumbbells overhead to full lockout.
tips: Master each component separately before combining them
The clean positions the bar for the press — don't rush the transition
These are power and conditioning movements, not hypertrophy exercises
Fatigue accumulates fast — keep reps low

name: Medicine Ball Slam
description:
**An explosive full-body exercise where a medicine ball is lifted overhead and thrown forcefully into the ground.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: abs. Secondary: shoulders, lats.

The slamming action trains the core to produce rapid flexion force while the overhead reach and hip hinge engage the shoulders and posterior chain.

**Pro tip:** Generate force from your core, not just your arms
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a slam ball or dead ball at waist height.
Lift the ball overhead by extending your hips and reaching your arms high, rising onto your toes.
In one aggressive motion, contract your abs and slam the ball into the floor directly in front of you, hinging at the hips and following through with your arms.
Bend your knees to absorb the movement and pick the ball up as it rebounds or settles.
Reset to standing and repeat immediately.
Focus on maximal intent with each slam rather than just going through the motion.
tips: Generate force from your core, not just your arms
Use your whole body in a coordinated chain
This is a power exercise — move explosively

name: Rowing Machine
description:
**A seated cardiovascular machine that simulates the rowing stroke, engaging both the upper and lower body in a cyclical push-pull pattern.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: lats, hamstrings, glutes, biceps, erectors.

It provides low-impact, high-output conditioning that challenges the legs, back, and arms through a full range of motion with every stroke.

**Pro tip:** Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
instructions: Strap your feet into the foot plates and grip the handle with an overhand grip.
Start in the catch position: knees bent, shins roughly vertical, arms extended, and torso leaning slightly forward with a flat back.
Initiate the drive by pressing through your feet, extending your legs first.
As your legs straighten, lean your torso back slightly past vertical and pull the handle to your lower chest.
Reverse the sequence to return: extend your arms, hinge forward at the hips, then bend your knees to slide back to the catch.
Maintain a smooth, connected rhythm and avoid jerking the handle or opening the back too early in the drive.
tips: Maintain a steady cadence that allows sustained effort without excessive fatigue
Adjust resistance to a level that challenges the quads without compromising form
Keep your posture upright and avoid hunching forward

name: Sled Pull
description:
**A full-body pulling movement performed by attaching a harness or handles to a weighted sled and walking backward or pulling it toward you.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: hamstrings, quads, lats, erectors, forearms.

The sled pull delivers constant tension through the posterior chain with minimal eccentric stress, making it a joint-friendly option for building work capacity and glute strength.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the full foot with each step
instructions: Attach a rope or handles to a loaded sled and face the sled.
Grab the handles with both hands at hip height, arms extended.
Lean back slightly to create tension in the line, bracing your core.
Drive through your heels and pull the sled toward you by taking powerful backward steps.
Keep your chest up and hips low throughout the movement.
Maintain a steady pace, fully extending the hips with each stride.
Continue for the prescribed distance or time.
tips: Drive through the full foot with each step
Keep your torso at a consistent angle
Focus on full hip extension with each stride

name: Sled Push
description:
**A lower-body dominant pushing exercise where you drive a weighted sled across a surface.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: quads. Secondary: glutes, hamstrings, calves, shoulders.

The sled push emphasizes the quadriceps through continuous concentric effort, eliminating the eccentric phase and reducing muscle soreness while still delivering a potent training stimulus.

**Pro tip:** Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
instructions: Load the sled with the desired weight and position yourself behind it.
Place both hands on the high or low handles depending on desired body angle.
Set your feet in a staggered stance and lean into the sled at roughly a 45-degree angle.
Drive forcefully through the balls of your feet, extending one leg at a time.
Keep your core tight and spine neutral as you push forward.
Take short, powerful steps, maintaining consistent pressure against the sled.
Push for the prescribed distance, then decelerate gradually.
tips: Drive through the balls of your feet with each step to maximize quad engagement
Keep your torso at a forward lean angle appropriate for the implement
Start with lighter loads and focus on maintaining consistent stride length
Use this as a finisher or conditioning tool after primary quad work

name: Snatch
description:
**An explosive Olympic weightlifting movement that takes the barbell from the floor to overhead in one continuous motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, traps, erectors, forearms.

The snatch demands full-body coordination, hip power, and overhead stability, primarily loading the glutes and posterior chain during the aggressive pull from the ground.

**Pro tip:** Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
instructions: Stand over the barbell with feet hip-width apart, gripping the bar with a wide snatch grip.
Lower your hips, set your back flat, and position your shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar.
Initiate the pull by driving through the floor with your legs while keeping the bar close to your body.
As the bar passes the knees, explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles in a powerful triple extension.
Shrug your shoulders and pull yourself under the bar, rotating your elbows to catch it overhead.
Receive the bar in a deep overhead squat with arms fully locked out.
Stand up to full extension with the barbell stable overhead, then lower it under control.
tips: Speed and timing matter more than raw strength
Full hip extension before pulling under
Keep the bar path close and vertical
Warm up thoroughly — this is a high-velocity movement

name: Thruster
description:
**A high-demand combination lift that merges a front squat with an overhead press in one fluid motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: glutes, triceps, abs, quads.

The thruster uses momentum generated from the squat to drive the barbell overhead, heavily taxing the shoulders at lockout while demanding significant output from the legs and core throughout.

**Pro tip:** The squat drives the press — use the momentum from standing up
instructions: Unrack the barbell in a front rack position with elbows high and the bar resting on your anterior deltoids.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and brace your core.
Descend into a full front squat, keeping your torso upright and elbows elevated.
Drive explosively out of the bottom by pressing through your full foot.
As your hips and knees extend, use the upward momentum to press the barbell overhead.
Lock out the bar directly over your midfoot with arms fully extended.
Lower the bar back to the front rack position and immediately descend into the next rep.
tips: The squat drives the press — use the momentum from standing up
Keep the bar or dumbbells in a front rack position during the squat
These are metabolically brutal — expect cardiovascular limitation before deltoid failure
Better for conditioning than isolated deltoid hypertrophy

name: Turkish Get-Up
description:
**A multi-phase ground-to-standing movement performed while holding a kettlebell locked out overhead with one arm.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: abs, glutes, quads, triceps.

The Turkish get-up builds shoulder stability, core control, and total-body coordination by requiring you to transition through several distinct positions without losing overhead control.

**Pro tip:** Keep your eyes on the weight throughout the entire movement
instructions: Lie on your back and press the kettlebell up with your right hand, arm fully extended toward the ceiling.
Bend your right knee and plant that foot flat on the floor; extend your left arm and leg out at roughly 45 degrees.
Roll onto your left elbow, then press up onto your left hand, keeping your eyes on the kettlebell.
Lift your hips off the ground into a bridge position.
Sweep your left leg underneath your body and place your left knee on the ground in a half-kneeling position.
Remove your left hand from the floor and bring your torso upright.
Stand up from the half-kneeling position, then reverse each step to return to the floor.
tips: Keep your eyes on the weight throughout the entire movement
Each position in the get-up is a checkpoint — pause and stabilize
The shoulder works isometrically — it stabilizes, not presses
Start light and master the pattern before adding load

name: Barbell High Pull
description:
**An explosive pulling movement where the barbell is driven from hip level to upper-chest height using a powerful hip hinge and aggressive shrug.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: shoulders, biceps, forearms, erectors.

The barbell high pull loads the upper traps through a large range of motion under momentum, making it effective for building trap size and upper-body power.

**Pro tip:** Initiate with a powerful hip extension before pulling with the traps
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding the barbell with an overhand grip just outside your thighs.
Hinge at the hips and bend your knees slightly, lowering the bar to just above your knees.
Explosively extend your hips and knees, driving the bar upward.
As the bar rises, shrug your shoulders forcefully and pull with your elbows, guiding the bar toward your upper chest.
Keep your elbows above your wrists at the top of the pull.
Allow the bar to descend under control back to the starting position at your hips.
Reset your hip hinge before initiating the next repetition.
tips: Initiate with a powerful hip extension before pulling with the traps
This is an explosive movement — do not grind reps
Keep the bar close to your body throughout

name: Barbell Shrug
description:
**A straightforward trap isolation exercise performed by elevating the shoulders while holding a loaded barbell at arm's length.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms.

The barbell shrug allows heavy loading and directly targets the upper trapezius through a short but intense range of motion, making it a staple for building trap mass.

**Pro tip:** Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand grip at arm's length in front of your thighs.
Position your feet shoulder-width apart with a slight bend in your knees.
Without bending your elbows, elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible.
Squeeze the contraction at the top for a brief pause.
Lower the bar slowly back to the starting position, allowing your traps to stretch fully at the bottom.
Keep your head neutral and avoid rolling your shoulders forward or backward.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions.
tips: Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
Hold the top position briefly to maximize upper trap activation
Use straps if grip limits your loading

name: Behind The Back Barbell Shrug
description:
**A shrug variation performed with the barbell held behind the body, shifting the line of pull to emphasize the middle and lower traps slightly more than a standard front shrug.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms.

Holding the bar behind you also encourages better posture by pulling the shoulders into slight retraction during the movement.

**Pro tip:** Keep the bar close to your body throughout
instructions: Stand in a Smith machine or rack and grasp the barbell behind you with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Step forward slightly so the bar clears your glutes and hangs at arm's length behind your thighs.
Stand tall with your chest up and shoulders pulled back.
Elevate your shoulders straight up toward your ears, squeezing your traps hard at the top.
Hold the peak contraction briefly before lowering the bar under control.
Allow your shoulders to depress fully at the bottom to achieve a complete stretch.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping your arms straight throughout.
tips: Keep the bar close to your body throughout
Shrug straight up without leaning forward
Use a smith machine if balance is an issue

name: Cable Face Pull
description:
**A cable-based pulling movement that targets the rear deltoids and mid-traps by pulling a rope attachment toward the face with external rotation.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: shoulders. Secondary: traps, biceps.

The cable face pull provides consistent tension throughout the range of motion and is widely regarded as one of the best exercises for shoulder health and postural balance.

**Pro tip:** Pull to your forehead or temples, not your chest
instructions: Set a cable pulley to upper-chest or face height and attach a rope handle.
Grasp each end of the rope with a neutral grip and step back to create tension.
Stand with feet staggered, knees slightly bent, and core braced.
Pull the rope toward your face by driving your elbows back and out to the sides.
As the rope reaches your face, externally rotate your hands so your knuckles point toward the ceiling.
Squeeze your rear delts and mid-traps at the fully contracted position.
Return the rope forward under control, keeping tension on the cable throughout.
tips: Pull to your forehead or temples, not your chest
Externally rotate at the end — hands should finish above elbows
Use a rope attachment for the best range of motion
This is as much a shoulder health exercise as a hypertrophy exercise

name: Cable Shrug
description:
**A trap isolation exercise performed using a low cable pulley, providing smooth and constant resistance throughout the shrugging motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms.

The cable shrug eliminates the momentum issues common with free-weight shrugs and keeps the upper traps under continuous tension from stretch to peak contraction.

**Pro tip:** Stand far enough from the cable to maintain tension at the bottom
instructions: Attach a straight bar or rope handle to a low cable pulley.
Stand facing the machine and grasp the attachment with both hands at arm's length.
Position your feet hip-width apart and stand tall with a neutral spine.
Elevate your shoulders directly upward toward your ears without bending your elbows.
Hold the top position for a one-second squeeze.
Lower your shoulders slowly, letting the cable pull your traps into a full stretch at the bottom.
Repeat in a controlled rhythm for the prescribed reps.
tips: Stand far enough from the cable to maintain tension at the bottom
Shrug straight up with control
Pause at the top contraction

name: Dumbbell High Pull
description:
**An explosive compound pull using dumbbells that combines a hip hinge with an aggressive upward pull to target the traps and shoulders.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: shoulders, biceps, forearms, erectors.

The dumbbell variation allows each arm to move independently, helping correct side-to-side imbalances while still developing upper-body power and trap thickness.

**Pro tip:** Initiate with a powerful hip extension before pulling with the traps
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs with palms facing you.
Hinge at the hips and soften your knees, lowering the dumbbells to just above knee level.
Explosively drive your hips forward and extend your knees.
Use the momentum to shrug and pull the dumbbells upward, leading with your elbows.
Bring the dumbbells to upper-chest height with elbows flared above your wrists.
Control the descent back to the starting position.
Reset your hip hinge and repeat for the target number of reps.
tips: Initiate with a powerful hip extension before pulling with the traps
This is an explosive movement — do not grind reps
Keep the bar close to your body throughout

name: Dumbbell Shrug
description:
**A classic trap-building exercise performed by shrugging heavy dumbbells held at your sides.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms.

The dumbbell shrug offers a natural hand position and unrestricted path of motion compared to a barbell, allowing the shoulders to travel through a slightly greater range and reducing wrist strain.

**Pro tip:** Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing your body.
Set your feet shoulder-width apart and let your arms hang fully extended.
Drive your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as you can.
Pause at the top and contract your upper traps forcefully.
Lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner, fully depressing your shoulders at the bottom.
Avoid using momentum or bending your elbows to cheat the weight up.
Complete all prescribed repetitions with a consistent tempo.
tips: Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
Hold the top position briefly to maximize upper trap activation
Use straps if grip limits your loading

name: Dumbbell Upright Row Traps
description:
**A dumbbell upright row variation performed with a wider grip and emphasis on pulling the elbows high to maximize upper trap recruitment.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: shoulders, biceps, forearms.

Using dumbbells allows a freer wrist angle than a barbell, reducing internal shoulder impingement risk while still delivering a strong stimulus to the traps.

**Pro tip:** Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs with palms facing your body.
Brace your core and maintain an upright posture.
Pull the dumbbells upward by leading with your elbows, keeping the weights close to your torso.
Continue pulling until your elbows are at or above shoulder height and the dumbbells reach upper-chest level.
Focus on driving the elbows up and out rather than curling the wrists.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower the dumbbells under control to the start.
Repeat for the desired rep count without swinging or using body English.
tips: Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
Pull to chest height, not chin height
Lead with your elbows, not your hands

name: Farmer's Walk
description:
**A loaded carry exercise where you walk a set distance holding heavy dumbbells at your sides.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms, abs, erectors, glutes.

The farmer's walk places sustained isometric demand on the upper traps and grip while simultaneously challenging core stability and hip function, making it one of the most functional strength exercises available.

**Pro tip:** Walk tall with your chest up and shoulders packed
instructions: Place two heavy dumbbells on the floor at your sides.
Hinge at the hips, grip each dumbbell firmly, and stand up tall with a braced core.
Pin your shoulders back and down, packing your traps to stabilize the load.
Walk forward with short, controlled steps, keeping your torso completely upright.
Avoid leaning to either side or letting the dumbbells drift away from your body.
Breathe steadily through braced abs for the entire carry.
Walk for the prescribed distance or time, then set the dumbbells down with a controlled hip hinge.
tips: Walk tall with your chest up and shoulders packed
Use heavy loads — the traps respond well to high force isometrics
Keep your steps short and controlled

name: Kettlebell Shrug
description:
**A trap isolation movement using kettlebells held at your sides.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms.

The offset center of mass of the kettlebell slightly increases grip and stabilizer demands compared to dumbbells, while the shrugging motion itself directly targets the upper trapezius through shoulder elevation.

**Pro tip:** Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
instructions: Stand upright with a kettlebell in each hand, arms hanging at your sides with palms facing inward.
Set your feet at hip width and maintain a tall, neutral spine.
Elevate both shoulders toward your ears in a straight vertical line.
Squeeze your upper traps hard at the peak of the movement.
Lower the kettlebells slowly, allowing a full stretch at the bottom.
Keep your arms straight and avoid any jerking or bouncing.
Perform all reps with deliberate control and consistent range of motion.
tips: Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
Hold the top position briefly to maximize upper trap activation
Use straps if grip limits your loading

name: Machine Shrug
description:
**A guided shrug performed on a dedicated shrug or calf raise machine that locks you into a fixed path of motion.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: none.

The machine shrug removes grip as a limiting factor and provides a consistent resistance profile, allowing you to focus entirely on maximally contracting and stretching the upper traps.

**Pro tip:** Focus entirely on the shrug — the machine handles stability
instructions: Position yourself in the machine with the handles or pads resting at your sides at arm's length.
Stand tall with your feet flat and your core engaged.
Elevate your shoulders straight up as high as possible against the resistance.
Hold the top contraction for one to two seconds, focusing on the trap squeeze.
Lower the weight slowly, letting your shoulders depress fully to stretch the traps.
Avoid bending your elbows or using leg drive to assist the movement.
Repeat for the target number of reps with a controlled cadence.
tips: Focus entirely on the shrug — the machine handles stability
Use the full range from depressed to fully elevated
Control the eccentric to maximize time under tension

name: Overhead Shrug
description:
**A shrug variation performed with the barbell held overhead in a wide or snatch-width grip.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: shoulders.

The overhead position shifts emphasis toward the lower and middle traps while also demanding significant shoulder stability, making it an excellent accessory for Olympic lifters and anyone seeking balanced trap development.

**Pro tip:** Use light weight — this is a stability and activation exercise
instructions: Press or snatch a barbell to a locked-out overhead position with a wide grip.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, arms fully extended, and the bar directly over your midfoot.
From this overhead position, actively shrug your shoulders upward toward your ears.
Focus on elevating the scapulae while keeping your elbows locked.
Hold the top position briefly, then lower your shoulders back to the starting position.
Maintain a tight core and avoid arching your lower back throughout.
Use light to moderate weight and perform controlled reps to protect the shoulder joint.
tips: Use light weight — this is a stability and activation exercise
Press the bar overhead first, then shrug up
Keep your core braced to avoid lumbar extension

name: Power Clean
description:
**An Olympic lifting derivative that takes the barbell from the floor to the front rack position in one explosive effort without dropping into a full squat.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: glutes. Secondary: quads, hamstrings, traps, shoulders, erectors, forearms.

The power clean develops hip extension power and total-body athleticism, heavily loading the glutes and posterior chain during the pull while training rapid force production.

**Pro tip:** Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, shins close to the bar, and grip the barbell just outside your knees with an overhand grip.
Set your back flat, chest up, and hips slightly above your knees.
Pull the bar off the floor by extending your legs, keeping the bar tight to your body.
As the bar passes your knees, explosively extend your hips, knees, and ankles.
Shrug your shoulders and pull yourself under the bar, rotating your elbows forward.
Catch the bar in a front rack position with a slight knee bend — no deeper than a quarter squat.
Stand to full extension, then lower the bar back to the floor or hang position.
tips: Explosive hip extension drives the bar — don't arm-pull
Keep the bar close to your body during the pull
Full hip extension before pulling under the bar
Start with lighter loads to master the timing

name: Prone Y Raise
description:
**A face-down isolation exercise where light dumbbells are raised overhead in a Y-shaped path to target the lower and middle trapezius.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: shoulders.

The prone Y raise is a highly effective corrective and hypertrophy exercise for the often-neglected lower traps, improving scapular upward rotation and overhead stability.

**Pro tip:** Use light weight — the lower traps are small and fatigue quickly
instructions: Lie face down on an incline bench set to approximately 30-45 degrees, holding a light dumbbell in each hand.
Let your arms hang straight down toward the floor with thumbs pointing forward or slightly outward.
Raise both arms up and out at roughly a 45-degree angle from your head, forming a Y shape.
Lift until your arms are in line with your torso, squeezing your lower traps at the top.
Hold the peak position for a one-second count.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the hanging start position.
Use light weight and prioritize feeling the lower traps work over lifting heavy.
tips: Use light weight — the lower traps are small and fatigue quickly
Raise your arms in a Y pattern with thumbs pointing up
Focus on the scapular movement, not just arm elevation
coaching_cues: Thumbs up, arms in a Y

name: Rack Pull Shrug
description:
**A hybrid movement combining a partial deadlift from pins with an aggressive shrug at the top.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms, glutes, hamstrings, erectors, lats.

The rack pull shrug allows extremely heavy loading on the traps by using the hips and back to initiate the pull, then finishing with a maximal shoulder elevation that overloads the upper trapezius beyond what a standard shrug permits.

**Pro tip:** Set the pins at or just above knee height
instructions: Set the safety pins in a power rack at just below knee height.
Load the barbell on the pins and grip it with an overhand or mixed grip at shoulder width.
Brace your core, flatten your back, and stand up by extending your hips and knees.
As you reach full lockout, immediately shrug your shoulders as high as possible.
Hold the shrug at the top for a brief squeeze.
Lower your shoulders first, then hinge at the hips to return the bar to the pins.
Reset your position completely before performing the next rep.
tips: Set the pins at or just above knee height
Lock out the hips fully before shrugging
Use straps — grip should not be the limiter

name: Resistance Band Shrug
description:
**A trap isolation exercise performed by standing on a resistance band and shrugging the handles upward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: none.

The band provides accommodating resistance that increases as the traps contract, creating a unique loading profile that maximizes tension at the top of the movement where the traps are most shortened.

**Pro tip:** Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
instructions: Stand on the center of a resistance band with feet hip-width apart.
Grasp each end or handle of the band with your arms at your sides.
Stand tall with a neutral spine and shoulders relaxed downward.
Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears against the band's resistance.
Squeeze hard at the top where the band tension is greatest.
Lower your shoulders slowly back to the starting position.
Perform all reps with smooth, controlled movement and no momentum.
tips: Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
Hold the top position briefly to maximize upper trap activation
Use straps if grip limits your loading

name: Smith Machine Shrug
description:
**A shrug performed on the Smith machine, which locks the bar into a vertical track and eliminates the need to stabilize the load.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms.

This allows you to focus purely on trap contraction with heavy weight, and the fixed bar path makes it easy to train close to failure safely without worrying about balance.

**Pro tip:** Focus entirely on the shrug — the machine handles stability
instructions: Set the Smith machine bar to thigh height and load the desired weight.
Stand centered under the bar and grasp it with an overhand grip at shoulder width.
Unrack the bar by rotating the hooks and stand upright with arms fully extended.
Elevate your shoulders vertically as high as you can, driving them toward your ears.
Pause at the top and squeeze your traps intensely.
Lower the bar under control, letting your shoulders drop fully to stretch the traps.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, then re-rack by rotating the hooks back into the catches.
tips: Focus entirely on the shrug — the machine handles stability
Use the full range from depressed to fully elevated
Control the eccentric to maximize time under tension

name: Snatch Grip High Pull
description:
**An explosive barbell pull performed with a wide snatch-width grip, driving the bar from the hang or floor to upper-chest height.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: shoulders, lats, biceps, forearms, erectors, quads, hamstrings.

The wider grip increases the range of motion and shifts more demand onto the traps and upper back compared to a conventional-width high pull, making it a potent trap and power builder.

**Pro tip:** Initiate with a powerful hip extension before pulling with the traps
instructions: Grip the barbell with a wide snatch grip — hands near the collars or where your wrists align with the rings.
Stand with feet hip-width apart, the bar at arm's length against your thighs.
Hinge at the hips and lower the bar to just above the knees with a flat back.
Explosively extend your hips and knees, driving the bar upward close to your body.
Shrug aggressively and pull with high elbows, bringing the bar to upper-chest or chin height.
Keep the bar close to your torso throughout the pull.
Lower the bar under control back to the hang position and reset before the next rep.
tips: Initiate with a powerful hip extension before pulling with the traps
This is an explosive movement — do not grind reps
Keep the bar close to your body throughout

name: Trap Bar Shrug
description:
**A shrug performed inside a hex or trap bar, which positions the load at your sides rather than in front of your body.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: forearms.

This neutral-grip setup reduces stress on the lower back and wrists while centering the load over your base of support, allowing heavier and more comfortable shrugging for upper trap development.

**Pro tip:** Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
instructions: Step inside a loaded trap bar and grip the handles with a neutral palms-in grip.
Stand up by extending your hips and knees until you are fully upright.
With arms hanging straight, elevate your shoulders directly upward toward your ears.
Contract your upper traps forcefully at the top of the movement.
Lower your shoulders in a controlled manner, achieving a full stretch at the bottom.
Keep your torso vertical and avoid leaning forward or backward.
Complete all reps with a steady tempo before setting the bar back down.
tips: Shrug straight up — do not roll your shoulders
Hold the top position briefly to maximize upper trap activation
Use straps if grip limits your loading

name: Upright Row
description:
**A vertical pulling movement where a barbell is raised from waist level to the upper chest by driving the elbows upward.**

**Muscles worked:** Primary: traps. Secondary: shoulders, biceps, forearms.

The upright row effectively loads both the upper traps and lateral deltoids, and using a slightly wider grip can reduce shoulder impingement risk while maintaining strong trap activation.

**Pro tip:** Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip slightly narrower than shoulder width.
Let the bar hang at arm's length against your upper thighs.
Pull the bar straight up along your body by driving your elbows up and out.
Continue until the bar reaches upper-chest to chin height and your elbows are at or above shoulder level.
Keep the bar close to your torso throughout the entire pull.
Pause briefly at the top, then lower the bar slowly back to the starting position.
Avoid excessive weight that forces you to use momentum or lean backward.
tips: Use a wider-than-shoulder grip to reduce impingement risk
Pull to chest height, not chin height
Lead with your elbows, not your hands
